<b>Preface</b><br>
This textbook, Physical Geography for Secondary Schools is written specifically<br>
for Form Five and Six students in the United Republic of Tanzania. It is prepared<br>
in accordance with the 2010 Geography Syllabus for Advanced Level Secondary<br>
Education, Form V-VI issued by then the Ministry of Education and Vocational<br>
Training.<br>
The textbook consists of six chapters, namely Evolution, composition and<br>
structure of the Earth, Endogenic processes of the Earth, Exogenic processes of<br>
the Earth, Water masses, Soils, and Atmospheric dynamics. Each chapter contains<br>
reading texts, illustrations, activities and exercises. You are encouraged to read all<br>
texts, and attempt all the activities and exercises together with other assignments<br>
provided by the subject teacher. Doing so, will promote the development of the<br>
intended learning competencies.<br>
<b>Tanzania Institute of Education</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>One</b><br>
<b>Evolution, composition and</b><br>
structure of the Earth<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
The origin and evolution of the Earth along its entire solar system has been<br>
researched and debated for hundreds of years. Scientists, astronomers,<br>
astronauts, philosophers and religious scholars have propounded different<br>
theories and arguments on how galaxies, solar system and the planet Earth<br>
evolved and changed overtime. In this chapter, you will learn about the origin<br>
of the universe and the position of the Earth in the solar system. Also, you will<br>
learn about the Earth as a system, the shape of the Earth as well as layers<br>
that form the planet Earth. The competencies developed from this chapter will<br>
enable you to critique the assumptions about the origin of the universe, position<br>
of the solar system and the planet Earth in particular. Furthermore, you will be<br>
able to use the available resources on Earth sustainably.<br>
<b>Origin of the universe</b><br>
<b>The origin of the universe is largely</b><br>
explained by various theories. These<br>
theories have examined processes<br>
through which the universe, galaxies,<br>
stars and planets were formed and the<br>
changes that are still taking place over<br>
time. A number of theories have been<br>
developed to postulate the origin of the<br>
universe from which the planet Earth<br>
is believed to be one of its bodies. The<br>
theories include the Big Bang theory,<br>
<b>Nebula</b><br>
hypothesis,<br>
<b>James</b><br>
<b>Jeans’</b><br>
tidal hypothesis, Inter stellar dust<br>
hypothesis, and the Creation theory.<br>
<b>However, various critiques have been</b><br>
emerged against these theories, though<br>
they are still important in understanding<br>
the evolution of the universe and the<br>
planet Earth in particular.<br>
<b>The Big bang theory</b><br>
<b>This theory was postulated by Lemaitre</b><br>
Georges and Edwin Hubble in 1920s.<br>
<b>The Big bang theory explains that</b><br>
about 15 billion years ago a tremendous<br>
explosion occurred which started<br>
the expansion of the universe. This<br>
explosion is known as the big bang. At<br>
the point of this event all matter and<br>
energy of space were contained at one<br>
point. The explosion pushed matter and<br>
energy exploding in all directions. As<br>
the universe expanded, matter collected<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   1</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
2<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
into clouds that began to condense<br>
and rotate, forming the forerunners of<br>
galaxies. Within galaxies, including<br>
our own Milky Way galaxy, changes<br>
in pressure caused gas and dust to<br>
form distinct clouds. In some of these<br>
clouds, where there was sufficient<br>
mass and the right forces, gravitational<br>
attraction caused the cloud to collapse.<br>
<b>Where the mass of material in the cloud</b><br>
was sufficiently compressed, nuclear<br>
reactions began and a star was born.<br>
<b>Some proportion of stars, including the</b><br>
<b>Sun, formed in the middle of a flattened</b><br>
spinning disk of materials (Figure 1.1).<br>
<b>In case of the Sun, gas and dust within</b><br>
this disk collided and aggregated into<br>
small grains, eventually formed larger<br>
bodies called planetesimals (very small<br>
planets). Some of these planetesimals<br>
or protoplanets reached diameters<br>
of several hundred kilometers. In<br>
successive stages these planetesimals<br>
coalesced into the current eight planets<br>
and their numerous satellites.<br>
<b>Figure 1.1: The big bang</b><br>
<b>Critiques of the Big bang theory</b><br>
While the theory provides some insights on how the universe evolved about 13-<br>
20 billion   years ago, there are arguments put against the theory. Firstly, the<br>
theory propounds that the universe emerged from the enormous atomic nuclear,<br>
it remained silent on how, and through which mechanisms and processes the<br>
atomic nuclear was composed, evolved and developed prior to its accumulation<br>
and explosion. Secondly, based on the theory, it appears that all galaxies emerged<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   2</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
3<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
at the same time and therefore could<br>
properly have at least the same age, but<br>
this has not been the case. In fact, the<br>
current astronomic researchers found<br>
that while other galaxies are too old,<br>
there are many young galaxies around<br>
and far from the Earth.<br>
<b>Nebular hypothesis</b><br>
<b>Nebular hypothesis it is also known</b><br>
as Kant’s gaseous hypothesis was<br>
proposed by Immanuel Kant and<br>
published in his Universal Natural<br>
<b>History and Theory of the Heavens in</b><br>
1755 and modified by Pierre Laplace in<br>
1796. Kant’s nebular hypothesis states<br>
that the solar system was formed from<br>
a cold spinning cloud of gases called<br>
the solar nebular that resulted from<br>
uneven distribution of gases throughout<br>
the universe in the Milky Way Galaxy.<br>
<b>As the gravitational pull began to</b><br>
condense the gas towards the center,<br>
the speed of the rotation increased. This<br>
caused the cloud to flatten, and create<br>
an accretion disk. Matter continued to<br>
collect as the force of gravity toward<br>
the center increased. Eventually, the<br>
gas warmed from increasing pressure.<br>
<b>As the mass further increased, also the</b><br>
gravity increased and the temperature<br>
continued to rise. A ball of hot gas<br>
formed in the center of accretion disk<br>
creating a protostar also known as the<br>
Sun.<br>
<b>Finally, when enough gas gathered in</b><br>
the center of the protostar the pressure<br>
generated enough heat to fuse the atoms<br>
to form a star. Outside the star matter<br>
was forming into clumps of gas, dust<br>
and rocks which created protoplanets.<br>
<b>These protoplanets continued to grow</b><br>
as they trapped materials in their<br>
gravitational fields. As the protoplanets<br>
were formed from the same cloud of<br>
gas and dust, they travelled around the<br>
sun in the same direction and the same<br>
plane. Also, the Nebular hypothesis<br>
explains how the planets are arranged.<br>
<b>The heat and wind caused by the Sun</b><br>
swept the lighter gases further out<br>
developing the solar system. That is<br>
why the rock terrestrial planets such<br>
as Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars are<br>
located closer to the sun while the gas<br>
giants are formed in the cooler outer<br>
region of the solar system.<br>
<b>Critiques on Kant’s Nebular</b><br>
hypothesis<br>
<b>Like many other theories, Kant’s Nebular</b><br>
hypothesis was challenged by Laplace in<br>
1796 in his book entitled ‘Explosion of<br>
the World System’. Laplace showed three<br>
defects of Kant’s nebular hypothesis as<br>
follows: (i) large amount of heat cannot<br>
be generated due to the collision of cold<br>
particles of primordial matter as claimed<br>
by Kant, (ii) mutual collision of particles<br>
cannot generate motion in the primordial<br>
matter and the random motion of the<br>
particles cannot generate circular motion<br>
in the primordial matter and, (iii) the<br>
angular velocity of rotary speed of the<br>
nebular cannot increase due to increase<br>
in size of the nebular as assumed by<br>
Kant.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   3</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Laplace’s nebular hypothesis</b><br>
<b>Pierre Laplace expanded on Kant’s</b><br>
hypothesis. In 1796 Laplace hypothesized<br>
that there was a huge and hot gaseous<br>
nebula in space which was rotating on its<br>
axis. Through continuous circular motion<br>
of the nebular and the continuous cooling<br>
due to loss of heat from its outer surface<br>
through the process of radiation, the<br>
nebular turn into continuous reduction<br>
in its size because of cooling. Thus,<br>
reduction in size and volume of the<br>
nebula increased its circular velocity<br>
(rotary motion). As the size of the nebula<br>
continued to decrease, the velocity of<br>
rotatory motion continued to increase.<br>
<b>Thus, the nebula started spinning at</b><br>
very fast speed and consequently the<br>
centrifugal force became so great that it<br>
exceeded the centripetal force. When this<br>
stage was reached the materials at the<br>
equator of the nebula became weightless.<br>
<b>Consequently, the outer layer was</b><br>
condensed due to excessive cooling and<br>
contracting central nucleus of the nebula,<br>
thus the outer ring (layer) was separated<br>
from the remaining part of the nebula.<br>
<b>This separated ring of material started</b><br>
moving around the nebular. Further,<br>
<b>Laplace maintains that the original ring</b><br>
was divided into nine rings and each<br>
ring moved away from the other ring.<br>
<b>All the materials of each ring condensed</b><br>
at a point or knot in the form of a ‘hot<br>
mass of gases”. Each mass of gases was<br>
later on cooled and condensed to form a<br>
planet. Thus, nine planets were formed<br>
from nine rings and the remaining central<br>
nucleus of the nebula became the sun.<br>
<b>Critiques of Laplace’s nebular</b><br>
hypothesis<br>
<b>To a large extent Laplace’s nebular</b><br>
hypothesis appears to share the common<br>
features of those found in Kant’s gaseous<br>
theory. It is a modified version which<br>
tries to correct the weak points raised<br>
in Kant’s theory. In doing so, the<br>
theory attained popularity and respect<br>
for decades. However, the Laplace’s<br>
nebular theory was later challenged<br>
by the following arguments. (i) Where<br>
did the heat and motion in that nebula<br>
come from? (ii) What was the reason<br>
behind the formation of certain fixed<br>
number of planets from the irregular<br>
ring? (iii) Why did only 9 rings come<br>
out from the irregular ring detached<br>
from the nebula? (iv) Why there were<br>
not more or less than 9 rings? (v) The<br>
small degree of cohesion between the<br>
particles of the nebula would make the<br>
formation continuous, not an intermittent<br>
process? (vi) If Laplace’s hypothesis is<br>
accepted that the planets were formed<br>
from the nebula, then the planets must<br>
have been in liquid state in their initial<br>
stage. But the planets in liquid state<br>
cannot rotate and revolve around the<br>
sun on rings.<br>
<b>James Jeans’ tidal hypothesis</b><br>
Sir James Jeans was a British scientist.<br>
<b>He propounded his ‘tidal hypothesis’</b><br>
to explain the origin of the earth in the<br>
year 1919. Jeans postulated that the solar<br>
system was formed from the sun and<br>
another intruding star. He furthermore<br>
postulated that due to massive<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   4</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
5<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
gravitational force of the intruding star, a<br>
huge amount of matter was ejected from<br>
the primitive sun, which later on became<br>
the building material of future planets. It<br>
is argued that, in the beginning the sun<br>
was a big incandescent gaseous mass<br>
of matter and besides the sun, there was<br>
another star termed as ‘intruding star’<br>
in the universe. This intruding star was<br>
much bigger in size than the primitive<br>
sun. The primitive sun was stationary<br>
and was rotating on its axis while the<br>
‘intruding star’ was moving along such<br>
a path in such a way that it was destined<br>
to come nearer to the primitive sun. Jeans<br>
claims that there was a great impact of<br>
the tidal force of the intruding star on<br>
the surface of the primitive sun.<br>
<b>The tidal hypothesis was modified by</b><br>
<b>Harold Jeffreys, a British scientist in</b><br>
1929, by presenting his concept as<br>
<b>Collision Hypothesis. According to</b><br>
<b>Jeffreys there were three stars in the</b><br>
universe before the origin of our solar<br>
system. One was our primitive sun, the<br>
second one was its ‘companion star’ and<br>
the third was ‘intruding star’ which was<br>
moving towards ‘companion star. Thus,<br>
the intruding star collided against the<br>
‘companion star’. Due to the collision,<br>
the companion star was completely<br>
smashed and shattered. Some shattered<br>
portions were scattered in the sky while<br>
remaining debris started revolving<br>
around the primitive sun. However,<br>
the impact of collision and explosion<br>
enabled the intruding star to clear itself<br>
off from the gravitational attraction of<br>
the primitive sun and gradually vanished<br>
in the universe. The planets of our solar<br>
system were formed from the remaining<br>
debris of the companion star.<br>
<b>Critiques of the tidal hypothesis</b><br>
<b>According to B. Levin the universe is</b><br>
infinite in space and time and the stars<br>
are so distant from each other such<br>
that a close encounter between them is<br>
a remote possibility. James Jeans did<br>
not explain the destiny of the intruding<br>
star. Also, the tidal hypothesis fails to<br>
explain the real distances between the<br>
sun and the planets in our present solar<br>
system.<br>
<b>Inter-Stellar dust hypothesis</b><br>
<b>The Inter-Stellar dust hypothesis was</b><br>
proposed by Otto Schmidt, a Russian<br>
scientist in 1943 to explain the complex<br>
problems of the origin and characteristics<br>
of the solar system and the Earth.<br>
<b>According to the ‘Inter Stellar dust</b><br>
hypothesis’ the Sun during its ‘galactic<br>
revolution’ captured the dark matter of<br>
the universe. The dark matter of gaseous<br>
cloud and dust particles had their own<br>
angular momentum. The dark matter<br>
after being attracted by the sun during<br>
its ‘galactic revolution’ started revolving<br>
around the primitive rotating sun.<br>
<b>Schmidt called these dark matters ‘Inter-</b><br>
<b>Stellar dusts’. The dust particles after</b><br>
being combined and condensed were<br>
changed into a flat disc which started<br>
revolving around the sun. It may be<br>
pointed out that the flat disc of captured<br>
dark matter started revolving around<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   5</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
6<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
the sun under the combined impacts of<br>
three types of motions namely; (i) the<br>
rotational motion of the sun itself, (ii)<br>
gravitational force exerted by the sun<br>
on the disc of dark matter, and (iii) the<br>
angular momentum of dark matter of<br>
the disc.<br>
<b>The particles having larger amount</b><br>
of centrifugal force were thrown out<br>
towards the margins of the rotating<br>
disc of dark matter around the sun.<br>
<b>The particles having small amount</b><br>
of centrifugal force were attracted<br>
towards the band of the disc nearer to<br>
the sun. The intense heat from the sun<br>
dispersed the gaseous particles towards<br>
the margins of the disc. Collision among<br>
the dust particles started the process<br>
of aggregation and accretion around<br>
the bigger particles which became the<br>
essentials of the future planets. With the<br>
passage of time these embryos captured<br>
more and more matter and thus grew in<br>
size to become asteroids. These asteroids<br>
further grew in size due to continuous<br>
acceleration of nearby matter around<br>
them and thus they became planets.<br>
<b>Critiques of the Inter-stellar dust</b><br>
hypothesis<br>
<b>The hypothesis has been criticized that</b><br>
gravitational force of the primitive sun<br>
was incapable of capturing dark matter<br>
scattered in the universe. According<br>
to some astrophysicists ‘meteorites<br>
and asteroids were formed as a result<br>
of the disintegration of some planets<br>
and not the planets were formed out of<br>
meteorites and asteroids. Until now no<br>
trace of remnants of dark matter could be<br>
discovered either in the archaeological<br>
drilling on the earth’s surface or on any<br>
planet.<br>
<b>Creation theory</b><br>
<b>Creation theory (theological theory)</b><br>
is the religious belief that the universe<br>
and life originated from God commands<br>
as written in the Holly Bible (Genesis<br>
1:1-31). The scriptures explain that in<br>
the beginning, God commanded, ‘let be<br>
light’ and then it appeared. He separated<br>
the light and the darkness. The light<br>
was named as ‘Day’ and the darkness<br>
as ‘Night’. He then commanded dome<br>
to divide the water to be in two separate<br>
places that is above and under it. The<br>
water below the sky were commanded<br>
to come together in one place, so that<br>
the land will appear and it was done.<br>
<b>He named the land “Earth”, and the</b><br>
water that comes together as “Sea”. God<br>
commanded, “Let lights appear in the<br>
sky to separate day from night and it will<br>
be the signs of days, years and seasons of<br>
the year. God made the two larger lights,<br>
the sun to rule over the day and the moon<br>
to rule over the night; he also made the<br>
stars. The two lights were placed on the<br>
sky to shine on the Earth. God created the<br>
great sea. God created the great whales<br>
and all kinds of creatures that live in<br>
the water, and all kinds of birds. God<br>
commanded the Earth to produce all<br>
kinds of animal life that is domestic and<br>

<b>Critiques of the creational theory</b><br>
As in the case of other theories,<br>
the creation theory is criticized by<br>
atheists and some scientists for being<br>
incompatible with scientific principles.<br>
<b>Formation of rocky and gaseous</b><br>
planets in the solar system<br>
<b>According to theories of the origin of</b><br>
the universe, it is believed that rocky<br>
planets including Mercury, Venus,<br>
<b>Earth and Mars, were formed near the</b><br>
<b>Sun, and the gaseous planets which</b><br>
include Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and<br>
<b>Neptune were formed in more distant</b><br>
orbits. The temperature of the early<br>
solar system explains why the inner<br>
planets are rocky and the outer ones are<br>
gaseous. As the gases coalesced to form<br>
a protosun, the temperature in the solar<br>
system rised. In the inner solar system<br>
temperatures were as high as 2000K,<br>
while in the outer solar system it was as<br>
cool as 50K. In the inner solar system,<br>
only substances with very high melting<br>
points would have remained solid. All<br>
the rest would have vaporized. So the<br>
inner solar system objects are made<br>
of iron, silicon, magnesium, sulfur,<br>
aluminum, calcium and nickel. Many<br>
of these were present in compounds<br>
with oxygen. There were relatively few<br>
elements of any other kind in a solid<br>
state to form the inner planets.<br>
<b>The inner planets are much smaller than</b><br>
the outer planets, and because of this<br>
they have relatively low gravity and were<br>
not able to attract large amounts of gas<br>
to their atmospheres. In the outer regions<br>
of the solar system where it was cooler,<br>
other elements like water and methane<br>
did not vaporize and were able to form<br>
the giant planets. These planets were<br>
more massive than the inner planets<br>
and were able to attract large amounts<br>
of hydrogen and helium. This explains<br>
why the solar system and the universe<br>
are mainly composed of hydrogen and<br>
helium.<br>
The four rocky planets namely Mercury,<br>
<b>Venus, Earth and Mars are also called</b><br>
terrestrial planets because they are<br>
made up of rocks and metals and have<br>
solid surfaces, and a core which is<br>
mainly made of iron. In many ways,<br>
all rocky planets are similar. They all<br>
have a solid rocky crust, some form of<br>
mantle, and a core. They are the closest<br>
four planets to the Sun. They are much<br>
smaller than the gas planets and rotate<br>
more slowly.<br>

<b>Gas giants are planets that are made</b><br>
of gases. They include Jupiter, Saturn,<br>
<b>Uranus and Neptune. Unlike rocky</b><br>
planets, gas giants do not have a well-<br>
defined surface as there is no clear<br>
boundary between the atmosphere<br>
and the surface. The gas giants have<br>
atmospheres that are mostly hydrogen<br>
and helium. They rotate relatively<br>
rapidly, for instance, while the Earth<br>
spins once on its axis every 24 hours,<br>
<b>Saturn spins once every 10 hours. Gas</b><br>
giants may have a rocky or metallic core<br>
but the majority of their mass is in the<br>
form of gas. Jupiter and Saturn probably<br>
have liquid metallic hydrogen interiors<br>
(liquid hydrogen conducts electricity).<br>
<b>Scientists believe Uranus and Neptune</b><br>
have interiors that contain a mixture<br>
(or layers) of rock, water, methane,<br>
and ammonia. All four gas giants have<br>
rings and moons. Figure 1.2 show the<br>
formation process of planets from the<br>
planetesimals formed in the solar nebular<br>
by collisional coagulation.<br>

<b>Position of the Earth in the solar system</b><br>
The Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the fifth largest planet in the solar<br>
system in terms of size and mass. Its single most outstanding feature is that its<br>
near-surface environment are the only places in the entire universe known to<br>
harbour life. It is about 150 million kilometers from the sun. The earth orbits<br>
around the sun taking 365 days and 5 minutes and 46 seconds. Also, it rotates on<br>

which takes 24 hours to complete its<br>
rotation. This rotation enables the Sun<br>

<b>The Earth is the only planet which is</b><br>
known so far to support life. The Earth<br>
supports life due to presence of water.<br>
Water is found in three states; liquid,<br>
solid and vapour, which is essential<br>
for living things. The Earth has an<br>
atmosphere which contains different<br>
gasses such as oxygen, essential for<br>
life, carbon dioxide which is essential<br>
for photosynthesis in plant, hydrogen<br>
essential for plants, ozone gas which<br>
absorb ultraviolet rays which are the<br>
unwanted rays from the sun. Also, the<br>
<b>Earth has important nutrients found</b><br>
in the soil, air and water which are<br>
essential for living things. Moreover,<br>
the Earth has a magnetic field which<br>
originates from the core of the Earth<br>
which form the magnetic north pole<br>
and magnetic south pole. The magnetic<br>
field acts as the repellant of harmful<br>
radiations from the Sun.<br>
<b>Furthermore, the Earth has suitable</b><br>
climate than other planets, it does not<br>
have extreme temperature like that of<br>
<b>Mercury where temperature goes to</b><br>
4000C and Venus whose temperature is<br>
4800C or Mars whose temperature goes<br>
to -1400C. In these planets life cannot be<br>
possible. According to the origin of the<br>
universe, the solar system is found in<br>
one arm of the milky way galaxy in the<br>
universe. Our solar system is composed<br>
of a set of radically different types of<br>
planets, moons, asteroids, comets,<br>
meteorites, interplanetary dusts and<br>
gases. The planets are arranged from<br>
the gas giants Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus,<br>
and Neptune to the rocky inner planets<br>
<b>Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars. In the</b><br>
solar system, the Earth is an active place.<br>
<b>In the planet Earth, earthquakes rip</b><br>
along plate boundaries, volcanoes eject<br>
fountains of molten lava, and mountain<br>
ranges and seabed are constantly created<br>
and destroyed.<br>
<b>Shape of the Earth</b><br>
<b>The planet Earth is spherical in shape</b><br>
(oblate spheroid). Its diameter from the<br>
<b>North pole through the South pole is</b><br>
about 12722 kilometers while from the<br>
<b>East to West (equator) the diameter is</b><br>
12762 kilometers. The circumference<br>
of the Earth is approximately 40085<br>
kilometers and the Earth rotates<br>
from West to East. The concept of a<br>
spherical earth dates back to ancient<br>
<b>Greek philosophy from around the 6th</b><br>
<b>Century BC, but remained a matter of</b><br>
philosophical speculation until the 3rd<br>
<b>Century BC when Hellenistic astronomy</b><br>
established the spherical shape of the<br>
earth. The Hellenistic paradigm was<br>
gradually adopted throughout the world<br>
during the late Antiquity and in the<br>
middle ages. The concept of spherical<br>
<b>Earth displaced earlier beliefs in a flat</b><br>
earth. Moreover, recent measurements<br>
from satellites suggest that the Earth<br>
is, in fact, slightly sphere-shaped. The<br>
sphere-like shape of the Earth is called<br>
geoid, spheroid or oblate sphere. It is<br>
not a perfect sphere.<br>

<b>Theories on the earth’s shape</b><br>
<b>In the early times most of the people</b><br>
believed that the Earth has a flat shape.<br>
<b>However, as time went on, new arguiment</b><br>
was put forward by Galileo Galilei and<br>
<b>Ferdinand Magellan that the Earth is</b><br>
round in shape.  It was around 500 B.C<br>
that Pythagoras of Samos first proposed<br>
a spherical earth, on aesthetic grounds<br>
rather than on physical evidence.  Like<br>
many Greeks, he believed the sphere was<br>
the most perfect shape. There are several<br>
theories which elaborate on earth’s shape<br>
which include;<br>
(i)	 Plato and Aristotle’s theory of<br>
natural place: This theory relied<br>
on a spherical Earth to explain why<br>
heavy things go down (toward what<br>
<b>Aristotle believed was the center of</b><br>
the Universe), and things like air<br>
and fire go up. In this geocentric<br>
model, the structure of the universe<br>
was believed to be a series of perfect<br>
spheres. Their theory was not easily<br>
accepted until they coupled with<br>
the evidence of the lunar eclipse<br>
and the observations from journeys<br>
around the world.<br>
(ii)	 Heliocentric theory:  It was put<br>
forward by Nicolaus Copernicus in<br>
16th Century. The theory stated that<br>
the Earth was spherical in shape and<br>
it is one of the planets revolving<br>
the Sun.  The theory considered<br>
the Sun to be the centre of the solar<br>
system and perhaps the universe.<br>
<b>Also, he believed that the bodies</b><br>
like planets, comets and asteroids<br>
revolved around it.<br>
(iii)	 Centripetal theory:  The theory was<br>
put forward by Sir Isaac Newton<br>
who used Mathematical evidence<br>
to determine shape of the Earth.<br>
<b>He believed that the Earth is wider</b><br>
along the equator and flattens along<br>
the poles.  According to Sir Isaac,<br>
bulging along the equator is due<br>
to rotation and centrifugal forces<br>
which overcome the gravity hence<br>
pulling of materials away from<br>
the centre. This contribute to the<br>
bulging nature of the Earth along<br>
the equator.<br>
(iv)	 Geocentric theory: The theory was<br>
propounded by Claudius Ptolemy<br>
between 90 -168 AD who argues<br>
that the Earth was spherical in<br>
shape. He believed that the Earth<br>
was the centre of the universe and<br>
stationary.  However, the theory<br>
was partially disproved by modern<br>
science as it was realised that the<br>
Earth and other bodies like stars,<br>
sun, moon and other planets were not<br>
stationary.<br>
<b>Evidences that prove the sphericity</b><br>
of the Earth<br>
<b>There are various evidences that prove</b><br>
on the spherical nature of the Earth,<br>
including the following;<br>
(a)	 Aerial photograph<br>
Numerous photographs have been<br>
taken by satellites at great distances<br>
from the Earth, and all of these<br>
show that the Earth is spherical<br>
(Figure 1.3).<br>

(b)	 Circumnavigation of the earth<br>
The Earth has been circumnavigated many times by land, sea and air. When<br>
navigators sail on the sea or ocean, it has been proven that if they travel from<br>
one part following the same latitude they always come back to the same point<br>
(initial point). If the Earth were flat, this wouldn’t be possible.<br>
(c)	 Lunar eclipse<br>
Lunar eclipse is the eclipse of the moon which is important evidence on the<br>
sphericity of the Earth which was discussed by Aristotle (384-322 BC).  He<br>
cited two convincing arguments for the earths’ sphericity. First is the fact<br>
that during a lunar eclipse, as the moon enters or emerges from the earth’s<br>
shadow, the shape of the shadow seen on the moon is always rounded. Only<br>
a sphere can cast a shadow which is always circular (Figure 1.4).<br>

the Earth. If the Earth were flat, the tops of all poles would lie on the line of<br>
sight (Figure 1.5 (b)).<br>

gravitational pull.  It is as if people are pulling the weight from the centre<br>
as the force tries to balance. It can be related as the people of equal weight<br>

(h)	 The Earth’s curved horizon<br>
The Earth horizon when seen from<br>
a ship, a plane or a high altitude<br>
appears curved. The curved horizon<br>
widens as the observer’s altitude<br>
increases until it becomes circular.<br>
<b>If the earth were not spherical there</b><br>
would be no circular horizon.<br>
<b>Oblateness of the Earth</b><br>
<b>Despite the above evidences which</b><br>
prove the spherical shape of the Earth,<br>
it is argued by Sir Isaac Newton that<br>
the Earth is not exactly sphere, it is<br>
oblate. It is a bit flat at the poles and<br>
bulge at the equator. This is because,<br>
near the equator the Earth rotates at<br>
very high speed compared to the poles.<br>
<b>Due to the high speed the centrifugal</b><br>
force overcomes the gravitational force<br>
which leads to pull the matters away<br>
from the centre. This causes a bulging<br>
of the Earth at the Equator. On the other<br>
hand, the force of gravity is so great at<br>
the poles due to less speed leading to<br>
pulling of matters towards the centre<br>
of the earth. This situation causes a<br>
bulging at the equator and flattening of<br>
the polar region.This shape is caused<br>
by the following factors;<br>
<b>High speed of rotation along the Equator:</b><br>
<b>The earth’s rotational speed along the</b><br>
<b>Equator is 460m/s roughly equivalent</b><br>
to 1000 miles per hour. This high-speed<br>
results into the strong centrifugal force<br>
“radially outward force” to overcome the<br>
earth’s gravitational pull. This results into<br>
maximum equatorial departure “bulge”<br>
as reflected by larger equatorial diameter<br>
12762 km as opposed by polar diameter<br>
12722 km due to stronger gravitational<br>
attraction.<br>
<b>Low speed of rotation at the poles:</b><br>
<b>There is a variation in the gravitational</b><br>
pull at the poles and the equator.<br>
<b>Due to high equatorial departure, the</b><br>
gravitation force at the equator is less<br>
compared to the same at the poles.<br>
<b>This is caused by the fact that points</b><br>
at the equator are far from the center of<br>
the gravity, whereas those around the<br>
poles are relatively near to the center of<br>
gravity and consequently are attracted<br>
more. This makes the earth’s surface<br>
flat because as centrifugal force is less<br>
strong due to low speed of rotation,<br>
while gravitational force is stronger<br>
and overcome the centrifugal force,<br>
consequently pulling the materials<br>
of the Earth towards the centre of the<br>
Earth.<br>
<b>Evidences that prove the oblate</b><br>
spheroid of the Earth<br>
<b>The oblateness of the Earth is justified</b><br>
by five factors namely; inequality of<br>
circumference and diameters between<br>
equator and poles, measurement of<br>
latitude and recent satellite image<br>
data. Larger equatorial diameter of<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   14</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
15<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
12762 km results into larger equatorial<br>
circumference of 40085 km while<br>
smaller polar diameter results into small<br>
circumference of 39955 km. This proves<br>
that the planet Earth is not a perfect<br>
sphere. Variations in the measurements<br>
of latitude are caused by the difference<br>
in the curvature between the equatorial<br>
and polar areas. For example, near the<br>
equator 1o measure the length of 110 569<br>
km while near the poles 1o measure the<br>
length of 111700 km. Recent satellite<br>
data of the Earth suggest that the southern<br>
hemisphere is relatively smaller than the<br>
northern hemisphere which also proves<br>
that the planet earth is not a perfect<br>
sphere (Figure 1.8).<br>

<b>Activity 1.1</b><br>
(a)	 Develop a model of ship visibility at different observable intervals,<br>
and demonstrate it to the class.<br>
(b)	 Use three ranging poles on the area with no obstacles, such as along<br>
the road with at least 500 metres height. Place ranging poles into three<br>
different places but along a straight line then with your fellow student<br>
use two ranging poles to view the one erected at the middle. Study if<br>
the ranging pole erected at the centre is viewed on the same line with<br>
the two. If not, provide concrete reasons.<br>
(c)	 Explain what you have observed and use the information to prove that<br>
the shape of the earth is spherical.<br>

<b>The Earth as a system</b><br>
<b>The Earth is an integrated system, that can</b><br>
be subdivided into five main interrelated<br>
components.  Each component has<br>
its own collection of materials and<br>
processes. These components or parts<br>
of the Earth as a system do not operate<br>
on their own, they all interact in many<br>
ways.  They make up the external and<br>
internal structures of the Earth. These<br>
parts include atmosphere, hydrosphere,<br>
biosphere, geosphere, and cryosphere.<br>
<b>The atmosphere, stands like an envelope</b><br>
of transparent odourless gases held on<br>
the Earth by gravitational attraction. It<br>
extends up from the earth surface for<br>
several hundreds of kilometres. The<br>
biosphere, a life supporting layer which<br>
surrounds the Earth and makes plant<br>
and animal life possible without any<br>
protective device. The geosphere, it<br>
includes the rocks and minerals on Earth<br>
from the molten rock and heavy metals<br>
in the deep interior of the planet to the<br>
sand on beaches and peaks of mountain.<br>
<b>Thus, it includes all minerals, rocks and</b><br>
soils. The Hydrosphere, is the sphere<br>
that covers total amount of water on a<br>
planet Earth.  It includes water available<br>
on the surface of the Earth, underground<br>
and in the air. The cryosphere, is an all-<br>
encompassing term for those portions<br>
of the earth’s surface where water is in<br>
solid form, including sea ice, ice sheets,<br>
lake ice, river ice, snow cover, glaciers,<br>
ice caps, and frozen grounds.<br>
<b>External structure of the Earth</b><br>
<b>The external structure of the Earth</b><br>
consists of atmosphere, hydrosphere,<br>
cryosphere and biosphere.<br>
<b>Atmosphere</b><br>
<b>The word atmosphere is the combination</b><br>
of two Greek words; ‘atmos’ meaning<br>
“vapour” and ‘sphaira’ meaning “sphere”<br>
it is a layer of a gases surrounding a<br>
planet Earth or other material body<br>
of sufficient mass that is held in place<br>
by the gravity of the body. The earth’s<br>
atmosphere is comprised of mixture of<br>
gasses, suspended solids and liquids that<br>
surround the Earth. Thus the atmosphere<br>
is the blanket of air that envelops the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   16</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Earth. It is the zone of mixed odorless</b><br>
gas or air. The earth’s atmosphere is<br>
about 600 to 1000 kilometres thick.<br>
<b>Atmospheric composition</b><br>
<b>The atmosphere is constituted of both</b><br>
living and non-living components.<br>
<b>The living organisms include bacteria</b><br>
in which some are harmful to human<br>
life. Non-living components include<br>
gaseous (Table 1.1), and non-gaseous<br>
components such as dusts and particles.<br>
<b>The atmosphere is composed of various</b><br>
gasses as follows:<br>
(a)	 Nitrogen gas: 78% dilutes oxygen<br>
and prevents rapid burning at the<br>
earth’s surface.  Living things need<br>
it to make proteins.<br>
(b)	 Oxygen gas:  21% used by all living<br>
things, essential for respiration.  It<br>
is necessary for combustion or<br>
burning.<br>
(c)	 Carbon dioxide:  0.03% plants use<br>
it to make oxygen. Acts as a blanket<br>
and prevents the escape of heat into<br>
outer space.<br>
(d)	 Argon: 0.93% used in light bulbs.<br>
(e)	 Water vapor: 0.20 to 4.0% essential<br>
for life processes also prevents heat<br>
loss from the Earth.<br>
(f)	 Trace gases: found only in a very<br>
small amounts, including neon,<br>
helium, krypton and xenon.<br>
<b>The usefulness of atmosphere is highly</b><br>
determined by its composition. For<br>
instance, water vapour facilitates weather<br>
processes, dusts and soot particles act as<br>
condensational nuclei or hydroscopic<br>
nuclei, while gases support life among<br>
others.<br>

<b>Layers of the atmosphere</b><br>
<b>The atmosphere is divided into layers</b><br>
based on its temperature. These layers<br>
are the troposphere, the stratosphere,<br>
the mesosphere and the thermosphere.<br>
<b>The uppermost region, beginning about</b><br>
500 km above the Earth’s surface, is<br>
called the exosphere.<br>

(a)	 Troposphere<br>
The troposphere is the bottom<br>
and, immediate layer from the<br>
earth’s surface. It starts at the<br>
earth’s surface and extends to<br>
an average of 12 kilometers<br>
high being 8 kilometres at the<br>
poles and 17 kilometers at the<br>
equator.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
temperature<br>
in<br>
the troposphere decreases with<br>
increase in altitudes by 0.6o C for<br>
every 100 metres or 6.5o C for<br>
every 1 kilometer (environmental<br>
lapse rate). Pressure falls with<br>
altitude due to decrease of gravity.<br>
<b>The air pressure at the top of the</b><br>
troposphere is only 10% of that at<br>
the sea level (0.1 atmospheres).<br>
The troposphere is where clouds,<br>
precipitation and violent storm<br>
form. Here wind speed increases<br>
with height. About 99% of all<br>
water vapour is concentrated in<br>
this layer. It is by far the wettest<br>
layer of the atmosphere. Also,<br>
it contains three-quarters of the<br>
mass of the entire atmosphere. The<br>
troposphere is where all weather<br>
takes place; it is the region of<br>
rising and falling pockets of<br>
air. There is a thin buffer zone<br>
between the troposphere and the<br>
next layer called the tropopause.<br>
<b>The tropopause lies above the</b><br>
troposphere. It extends from 12<br>
km to 20 km. Here temperature is<br>
constant. The layer is unstable and<br>
contains most of the atmospheres<br>
water vapour, gasses, clouds dust<br>
and pollutants. Troposphere plays<br>
greater role on life including<br>
formation of precipitation,<br>
providing oxygen and hydrogen<br>
and carbon dioxide which is<br>
essential for photosynthesis. It<br>
provides water vapour which<br>
regulates temperature by preventing<br>
the outgoing radiation from the<br>
sun.<br>
(b)	 Stratosphere<br>
This is the layer above the<br>
tropopause. It extends to about 50<br>
km. It is characterized by steady<br>
increase in temperature caused by<br>
concentration of ozone (O3) which<br>
absorbs ultraviolet radiation from<br>
the Sun. It acts as a protective<br>
shield against deteriorates which<br>
usually burn out as they enter<br>
into gravitational field. Winds are<br>
low in the lower troposphere but<br>
increase with height. Air is dry,<br>
and pressure continues to fall with<br>
altitude. There is a recent concern<br>
that man-made fluorocarbon is<br>
depleting the ozone layer. This<br>
has potentially a devastating<br>
consequence to life on Earth. The<br>
boundary separating stratosphere<br>
with the next layer is called the<br>
stratopause. It lies above the<br>
stratosphere and extends from 49<br>
km to 52 km.<br>
The stratosphere contains 90% of<br>
ozone layer primarily between the<br>
top of the troposphere and about<br>
50 km (31 miles) altitude. It is<br>
approximated that about 16–50<br>

km (10–31 miles) above earth’s<br>
surface, ozone forms a thin invisible<br>
shield protecting life from the<br>
sun’s ultraviolet (UV) radiation.<br>
<b>Ozone is a molecule that contains</b><br>
three oxygen atoms. At any<br>
given time, ozone molecules are<br>
constantly formed and destroyed<br>
in the stratosphere. The total<br>
amount has remained relatively<br>
stable during the decades that it<br>
has been measured. Ozone layer<br>
absorbs the harmful ultraviolet<br>
radiation which is emitted from<br>
the sun. Without this layer the life<br>
wouldn’t be possible since it filters<br>
harmful radiation from the sun that<br>
can destroy animal tissues. The<br>
stratosphere is another layer where<br>
the temperature increases with the<br>
increase in height. The stratosphere<br>
is a convenient layer for flying of<br>
plane to avoid turbulence which is<br>
common in the troposphere.<br>

(c)	 Mesosphere<br>
The mesosphere is a layer above<br>
the stratopause. It extends from<br>
52km to 80 km above the surface.<br>
<b>In this layer temperature decreases</b><br>
rapidly with increasing height in<br>
the absence of water vapor, dusts<br>
and clouds to absorb incoming<br>
radiation. This is the region with<br>
lowest temperature which drops<br>
to -900C and the strongest wind<br>
with the speed of 3000 km/hr. The<br>
wind is estimated using temperature<br>
dates which is usually collected by<br>
satellites. Also, this layer contains<br>
fairly high concentrations of ion<br>
and metal atoms. The shooting stars<br>
you observe in the night sky are<br>
meteorites that evaporate as it burns<br>
up in the mesosphere. Gases here<br>
are mixed, not layered by masses,<br>
and air is so thin that you cannot<br>
breathe. Mesosphere has various<br>
importance to human life.<br>
Mesosphere can be seen as a<br>
protective layer of the earth’s<br>
surface because meteorites of<br>
various sizes are burn up in this<br>
layer. It is estimated that meteorites<br>
with 20 meters in size, are burnt<br>
every year. For this case, the<br>
mesosphere is important layer<br>
in protecting us from the falling<br>
meteor shower, with hundreds of<br>
rocks with 20 meters in size that<br>
would have reached on earth’s<br>
surface at supersonic speed. Life<br>
could not be possible on earth.<br>

<b>Also, the mesosphere protects us</b><br>
from solar radiation with varying<br>
wavelengths. At the end of this<br>
layer is the mesopause. Mesopause<br>
lies above mesosphere. This layer<br>
shows no change of temperature<br>
and is characterized by lowest<br>
temperature.<br>
(d)	 Thermosphere<br>
Thermosphere broadly corresponds<br>
with the ionosphere. This layer<br>
lies above mesopause extending<br>
from 80 km into space. In this<br>
layer temperature ranges between<br>
1 0000C and 1 5000C. However,<br>
it cannot be felt because air<br>
molecules are far apart. Also, it<br>
is due to presence of atomic ions<br>
(oxygen) which absorb a very short<br>
wave solar energy by absorption<br>
of oxygen and nitrogen. In this<br>
zone the air is extremely thin with<br>
almost zero amount of air density<br>
and almost without existence of<br>
gravity. The little air present in<br>
the thermosphere mainly consists<br>
of helium, atomic nitrogen, and<br>
atomic oxygen. For this reason<br>
it is not possible to breath in the<br>
thermosphere. The thermosphere<br>
contributes to the protection of<br>
the Earth as it absorbs x-rays and<br>
extreme ultraviolet radiation. Also<br>
it enables space exploration and<br>
easy communication. The charged<br>
particles in the thermosphere<br>
make it easy for long distance<br>
communication through radio, by<br>
which radio signs travel in straight<br>
line and bounces forth between the<br>
earth’s surface and the ionosphere.<br>
<b>It absorbs the sun’s energy and</b><br>
creates moderate temperature.<br>
(e)	 Exosphere<br>
This is the highest and top layer<br>
of the earth’s atmosphere. It<br>
starts at 500 kilometers high and<br>
marks the edge of space. There<br>
are very few molecules layer<br>
mainly composed of extremely<br>
low densities of hydrogen, helium<br>
and several heavier molecules<br>
including nitrogen, oxygen and<br>
carbon dioxide. This layer is the<br>
earth’s first line of defense against<br>
the harmful sun rays. Also, it is<br>
the first layer to come into contact<br>
and protect the Earth from meteors,<br>
asteroids and cosmic rays. Figure<br>
<b>1.9 (a) and (b) show layers of the</b><br>
earth’s atmosphere and its vertical<br>
structure.<br>

<b>The importance of atmosphere in life</b><br>
<b>The atmosphere is shared by every</b><br>
person living on earth. Without the<br>
atmosphere we would find it very hard to<br>
survive. Humans can last weeks without<br>
food, days without water, but only<br>
minutes without air. Our daily lives are<br>
influenced by the atmosphere. Also, we<br>
are protected by the atmosphere. It acts as<br>
a huge blanket, keeping the earth warmer<br>
than it would be without the atmosphere.<br>
This is known as the greenhouse effect,<br>
without the atmosphere, the average<br>
temperature on Earth would be below<br>
freezing.<br>
<b>The atmosphere protects living things on</b><br>
<b>Earth from the sun’s harmful ultraviolet</b><br>
radiation. A thin layer of gas called ozone<br>
high up in the atmosphere at stratosphere<br>
filters out these dangerous rays. Also, the<br>
atmosphere helps to sustain life on Earth.<br>
<b>It provides oxygen for humans and</b><br>
animals to breath, and carbon dioxide for<br>

plants.  Through the hydrological cycle,<br>
plants and animals receive the water<br>
needed to survive. Another importance<br>
of the atmosphere is to protect us from<br>
objects coming towards the Earth from<br>
outer space. Everyday meteoroids enter<br>
the earth’s atmosphere.  These are pieces<br>
of rock that are travelling through space.<br>
<b>When they reach the atmosphere, most</b><br>
of them burn up due to the friction of<br>
the air. The ones that don’t completely<br>
burn up reach the surface of the earth<br>
in a much smaller form.  When they<br>
reach the Earth we call them meteorites.<br>
<b>The atmosphere protects us from space</b><br>
junk that would do damage to us, to<br>
our homes, and to the Earth itself.  It<br>
is because of the heat created by the<br>
friction of the atmosphere that the space<br>
shuttle had to be equipped with a heat<br>
shield to prevent it from burning up as it<br>
re-entered the earth’s atmosphere.  The<br>
heat caused by friction is dangerous to<br>
the astronauts but it is very important to<br>
protect us from things coming in from<br>
outer space.<br>

<b>Hydrosphere</b><br>
<b>Hydrosphere is the area that includes</b><br>
all earth’s water in the state of liquid,<br>
frozen or floating ice in the upper<br>
part or beneath the soil. Most (97%)<br>
of water is found in oceans and seas;<br>
freshwater accounts for 3% only of the<br>
hydrosphere and most of this is found<br>
in glaciers, permanent snow covers at<br>
the poles, lakes, rivers, groundwater,<br>
and in mountainous regions. The rest<br>
is in lakes, rivers, and ground water.<br>
<b>Hydrosphere is referred to as a major</b><br>
setting of the earth’s hydrological cycle.<br>
<b>Freshwater amounts are essentially held</b><br>
at a constant level by the hydrologic<br>
cycle that is driven  by the sun. The<br>
hydrological cycle continuously moves<br>
water around the planet by exchanging<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   22</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
23<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
water molecules from the vegetation<br>
and oceans to the atmosphere and back<br>
around. This movement of water is<br>
through the process of evapotranspiration,<br>
condensation, precipitation, infiltration,<br>
runoff, and subsurface flow  of which are<br>
the wheels in the hydrologic cycle. When<br>
water evaporates, the surroundings are<br>
cooled; as it condenses, water releases<br>
energy and warms its surroundings.<br>
<b>Water is responsible for the formation</b><br>
of landforms through erosion deposition,<br>
and the movement of minerals. It further<br>
hydrates life on the planet and transfers<br>
the energy from terrestrial to aquatic<br>
systems. Generally, without these cycles,<br>
and water itself, life would cease to exist<br>
on the planet Earth.<br>
<b>Biosphere</b><br>
<b>Biosphere refers to the narrow zone</b><br>
of the Earth in which all life forms<br>
exist. Life becomes possible in this<br>
zone due to presence of land, air and<br>
water which support all forms of life.<br>
<b>Biosphere extends vertically into the</b><br>
atmosphere to about 10 kilometers,<br>
downward into the ocean to a depth of<br>
about 10.4 kilometers. There are three<br>
basic components of the biosphere<br>
namely; abiotic, biotic and energy. The<br>
abiotic component consists of all non-<br>
living elements which are essential for<br>
survival of all living organisms. These<br>
elements are lithosphere, atmosphere<br>
and hydrosphere which in turn provide<br>
mineral nutrients, water and certain<br>
gases.<br>
<b>The biotic component is composed</b><br>
of plants and animals include human<br>
beings, plants, micro-organisms and<br>
macro-organisms. Also, energy is<br>
another component which is essential<br>
for generation and reproduction of<br>
all biological lives of this planet.<br>
<b>The biosphere is characterized by the</b><br>
continuous cycling of matter and an<br>
accompanying flow of solar energy<br>
through which certain large molecules<br>
and cells are self-reproducing.<br>
<b>Cryosphere</b><br>
<b>In some places on the Earth water</b><br>
exists in a form of solid ice or snow<br>
(Figure 1.10). These frozen places of<br>
the Earth are called ‘cryosphere.’ The<br>
word ‘cryosphere’ comes from the Greek<br>
word, ‘kryos’ which means ‘cold.’ The<br>
cold regions of our planet influence our<br>
entire world’s climate. The cryosphere<br>
is central to the daily lives of people,<br>
plants, and animals. Also, it has a<br>
great impact on the global cooling and<br>
distribution of cold winds. According<br>
to scientists the cryosphere, are places<br>
where water is in solid form, where very<br>
low temperature freezes water and turns<br>
it into ice. Cryosphere covers most of<br>
the top and bottom of our planet, in the<br>
polar regions. These are areas around<br>
the North Pole, the Arctic and the area<br>
around the South Pole, the Antarctic.<br>
<b>Moreover, it covers many other locations</b><br>
on Earth far away from the cold poles<br>
including high elevations. For example,<br>
the snow on top of Mount Kilimanjaro<br>
found in Tanzania. Frozen soil can be<br>

<b>Internal structure of the Earth</b><br>
The internal structure of the Earth is layered in spherical shells, like an onion.<br>
These layers can be defined by their chemical and geological properties. The Earth<br>
has an outer silicate solid crust, a highly viscous mantle, a liquid outer cover that<br>
is much less viscous than the mantle and a solid inner core (Figure 1.11).<br>

<b>Composition of internal layers of</b><br>
the Earth<br>
<b>The Earth’s interior layers have</b><br>
different densities. This is the result of<br>
heated gas cooled the heavier particles<br>
gathered in the centre of the swilling<br>
mass forming the great core of heavy<br>
liquid metal (Nickel-iron) which is the<br>
centre of the Earth (the core). Also,<br>
around the core, less heavy particles<br>
collected and formed another layer<br>
(iron and magnesium) called mantle.<br>
<b>Moreover, the layer of iron and</b><br>
magnesium (the mantle) formed the<br>
outer layer of silicate and magnesium,<br>
(the SIMA) and the lightest layer of<br>
silicate and aluminium (the SIAL).<br>
Deeper beneath the Earth is hotter.<br>
<b>For every 33 metres depth temperature</b><br>
increase by 10C. But the greater the<br>
pressure on top of something the<br>
more difficult it will make it boil. The<br>
pressure at the centre of the Earth is<br>
high due to many heavy layers on top of<br>
it. Pressure in the core is 3.6x106 Atm.<br>
<b>Thus, because of very high pressure</b><br>
it is believed that the Earth is solid to<br>
the great depth. The structure of Earth<br>
can be defined in two ways, these are;<br>
mechanical and chemical properties.<br>
<b>Mechanical properties can be divided</b><br>
into lithosphere, asthenosphere,<br>
mesospheric mantle, outer core, and<br>
the inner core.  However, chemically<br>
the interior of Earth is divided into<br>
three important layers; the crust which<br>
is divided into two parts namely; SIAL<br>
(made up of Silicate and Aluminium)<br>
at the upper most crust and the SIMA<br>
(made up of Silicate and Magnesia) at<br>
the lower most crust. Second part is<br>
the mantle which is divided into two<br>
parts; the upper mantle and the lower<br>
mantle, and below the mantle is the<br>
core which is also divided into two;<br>
the outer core and the inner core.<br>
<b>The crust</b><br>
<b>The crust ranges from 5-70 km (3-44</b><br>
miles) depth and is the outermost thin<br>
layer of the earth. Usually there are two<br>
kinds of crust: continental crust, and<br>
oceanic crust. First, the thicker crust<br>
is continental crust (SIAL), which is<br>
less dense and is composed of large<br>
proportion of silica and alumina (felsic),<br>
silicate rocks like granite and quartz.<br>
<b>The composition is very important</b><br>
because it makes the continental crust<br>
less dense than the ocean crust. The<br>
ocean crust (SIMA) is thinner, and<br>
predominantly mafic in composition.<br>
<b>Mafic rocks contain minerals with less</b><br>
silica, but more of iron and magnesium.<br>
<b>Mafic rocks (ocean crust) are denser</b><br>
than the felsic rocks of continental<br>
crust. The crust floats on the mantle.<br>
<b>Continental crust floats higher in the</b><br>
mantle than ocean crust because of the<br>
lower density of continental crust. The<br>
rocks of the crust fall into two major<br>
categories namely; SIMA and SIAL.<br>
<b>It is estimated that SIMA starts about</b><br>
11 kilometers below. The upper crust<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   26</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
27<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
(SIAL) and the lower crust (SIMA) are<br>
separated by the border of discontinuity<br>
called Conrad discontinuity. The<br>
boundary between the Earth’s crust<br>
and the mantle is called a Mohorovicic<br>
discontinuity or Moho. This is the surface<br>
of discontinuity between the mantle and<br>
the crust. It was discovered in 1909 by<br>
<b>Andrija Mohorovicic. Here the speed of</b><br>
earthquake waves suddenly accelerates<br>
from 5.0 to 8.1 km/sec. The cause of<br>
the Moho is thought to be a change in<br>
rock composition from rocks containing<br>
plagioclase feldspar (above) to rocks that<br>
contain no feldspars (below). In oceanic<br>
crust, there is a chemical discontinuity<br>
between ultramafic cumulates which has<br>
been observed from deep parts of the<br>
oceanic crust that have been sub-ducted<br>
onto the continental crust and pressed as<br>
ophiolite sequences. Many rocks now<br>
making up the earth’s crust were formed<br>
less than 100 million years ago.<br>
<b>However, the oldest known mineral</b><br>
grains are 4.4 billion years old, indicating<br>
that the earth has had a solid crust for<br>
at least that long. The thin parts of the<br>
crust are the oceanic crust, (SIMA),<br>
which underlies the ocean basins and<br>
below the continental crust (5-10 km),<br>
they are composed of dense (mafic) iron<br>
magnesium silicate igneous rocks, like<br>
basalt.  The thicker crust is continental<br>
crust (SIAL), which is less dense and is<br>
composed of large proportion of silica<br>
and alumina (felsic) silicate rocks, like<br>
granite. It is estimated that sima starts<br>
about 11 kilometres below the Conrad<br>
discontinuity. The upper most mantle<br>
together with the crust constitutes the<br>
lithosphere. There are two different types<br>
of crust, thin oceanic crust that underlies<br>
the ocean basins and thicker continental<br>
crust that underlies the continents.<br>
<b>The thin oceanic crust is composed of</b><br>
primarily of basalt, is heavy in weight<br>
and dark in colour, fine grained and the<br>
thicker continental crust is composed<br>
primarily of granite light in weight, light<br>
in colour, courser grain (granite). The<br>
lithosphere combines the crust and upper<br>
mantle.<br>
<b>Mantle</b><br>
<b>Earth’s mantle is the thickest layer of</b><br>
the Earth. The mantle is also known<br>
as mesosphere. The mantle extends to<br>
about 2890 km where temperature may<br>
reach to about 50000C, with an average<br>
density of about 3.3g/cm3. The most<br>
important characteristic of the mantle<br>
is the presence of a thin layer between<br>
upper mantle and lower mantle. It is a<br>
layer of semi-molten materials acting as<br>
the center of isostatic adjustment, known<br>
as asthenosphere. The lowest part of<br>
the mantle next to the core that make<br>
a mantle boundary is known as the ‘D’<br>
layer (D double prime). The mantle is<br>
ultramafic in composition which means it<br>
is made of iron and magnesium. Although<br>
the mantle has a similar chemical<br>
composition throughout, it has layers<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   27</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
28<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
with different mineral compositions and<br>
different physical properties. It can have<br>
different mineral compositions and still<br>
be the same in chemical composition<br>
because the increasing pressure deeper<br>
in the mantle causes mineral structures<br>
to be reconfigured. This is the surface of<br>
discontinuity between the core and the<br>
mantle called Gutenberg discontinuity,<br>
that was named after a study made by<br>
a German seismologist called Beno<br>
<b>Gurtenberg in 1914. It is about 2 900</b><br>
km depth and temperature is estimated<br>
to be 37000C.<br>
<b>Core</b><br>
This is the innermost part of the Earth,<br>
which is also known as barysphere. It<br>
is made up of iron and nickel. Its size<br>
is that of Mars which is about 6900<br>
km thick. It is divided into two major<br>
parts; outer core which is about 2270<br>
km thick and it is in molten state while<br>
the inner core is about 1216 km radius<br>
which is in solid state. The earth’s core<br>
is composed mainly of iron and nickel<br>
alloy because the average density of<br>
surface material is only around 3000<br>
kg/m3. The core’s temperature is about<br>
62000C. It is hotter than the surface<br>
of the sun (56000C) due to existence<br>
of uranium and nickel elements. The<br>
liquid outer core surrounds the inner<br>
core and is believed to be composed of<br>
iron mixed with nickel and trace amounts<br>
of lighter elements.  Recent speculation<br>
suggests that the innermost part of the<br>
core is enriched in gold, platinum and<br>
other siderophile elements. There is<br>
the boundary which separate the outer<br>
core and inner core which is known as<br>
<b>Lehman discontinuity which occurs</b><br>
at the depth of roughly 5100 km. The<br>
solid inner core is too hot to hold a<br>
permanent magnetic field but probably<br>
acts to stabilize magnetic field at the<br>
surface. Seismic measurements show<br>
that the core is divided into two parts, a<br>
‘solid’ inner core with the densities 16-17<br>
and liquid outer core with the densities<br>
ranging between 9900 and 12200 kg/<br>
m3. The inner core was discovered in<br>
1936 by Inge Lehmann and is generally<br>
believed to be composed primarily of<br>
iron and some nickel. It is not necessarily<br>
solid, but because it is able to deflect<br>
seismic waves and because it is under<br>
high pressure, it is believed to be in solid<br>
form.<br>

<b>Earth’s gravity</b><br>
<b>Every planetary body (including</b><br>
the Earth) is surrounded by its own<br>
gravitational field, which can be<br>
conceptualized with Newton’s law<br>
of universal gravity as exerting an<br>
attractive force on all objects. The rate<br>
of acceleration of falling objects near<br>
the earth’s surface varies very slightly<br>
depending on latitude, surface features<br>
such as mountains and ridges, and<br>
perhaps unusually high or low sub-<br>
surface densities. The strength of gravity<br>
is not the same at all places on the Earth.<br>
<b>Three factors determine the strength of</b><br>
gravity at any given place namely; the<br>
distance from the centre of the earth,<br>
the spin of the earth on its axis, and the<br>
nearby sources of gravity variations,<br>
such as mountains or underground<br>
caverns. Gravity or gravitation is a<br>
natural phenomenon by which all things<br>
with energy are brought towards (or<br>
gravitate towards) one another, including<br>
stars, planets, galaxies and even light<br>
and sub-atomic particles.  Gravity is<br>
responsible for many of the structures<br>
in the universe by creating spheres of<br>
hydrogen where hydrogen fuses under<br>
pressure to form stars and grouping them<br>
into galaxies.  On the Earth, gravity gives<br>
weight to physical objects and causes<br>
the tides.  Gravity has an infinite range<br>
although its effects become increasingly<br>
weaker on further objects. Gravity is a<br>
very important force. Every object in<br>
space exerts a gravitational pull on every<br>
other, and so gravity influences the paths<br>
taken by everything travelling through<br>
space. It is the glue that holds together<br>
entire galaxies.  It keeps planets in orbit.<br>
<b>It makes it possible to use man-made</b><br>
satellites and to go to and return from<br>
the moon. It makes planets habitable<br>
by trapping gases and liquids in an<br>
atmosphere.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Two</b><br>
<b>Endogenic processes of</b><br>
the Earth<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
The earth’s crust might seem fixed and rigid, but evidences show that it is<br>
constantly moving as a result of various forces exerted within it. In this chapter,<br>
you will learn about theories of the earth’s formation which include Isostasy<br>
theory, Continental drift theory, Seafloor spreading theory, and Plate tectonic<br>
theory. Also, you will learn about materials of the Earth’s crust, and internal<br>
geomorphic processes such as earthquakes, vulcanism and diastrophism.<br>
The competencies developed from this chapter will enable you to undertake<br>
various activities on the physical environment with the knowledge of how the<br>
planet Earth is constantly develops and replenishes. Also, the competencies<br>
gained from this chapter, will enable you to critique theories, approaches and<br>
principles about the internal processes of the Earth.<br>
<b>Concept of endogenic processes</b><br>
<b>The earth is constantly shaped by</b><br>
various forces that originate beneath the<br>
earth’s surface. The forces that shape<br>
land can result in folding, faulting,<br>
volcanic eraption and earthquakes. The<br>
driving force for the occurance of these<br>
geomorphic features is derived from<br>
rotation, tidal friction, radio activity<br>
and primordial heat from the origin of<br>
the Earth. The internal processes lead<br>
to the formation of earth’s topographic<br>
features, for example, oceanic basins,<br>
ridges,<br>
and<br>
trenches,<br>
mountain<br>
ranges, rift valleys, folds, faults, and<br>
vulcanic features. Endogenic forces<br>
largely operate within the earth’s crust<br>
mainly into two movements which are<br>
horizontal and vertical movements.<br>
<b>These forces are responsible for the</b><br>
earth’s movements. Hence, the study of<br>
endogenic processes and their resulting<br>
landforms and landscapes remarks<br>
important consideration on the internal<br>
evolution and the surface history of a<br>
geologic body (Figure 2.1).<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   31</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
32<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Endogenic Processes</b><br>
<b>Slow movements</b><br>
(Diastrophism)<br>
<b>Vertical movements</b><br>
(Epeirogenic/<br>
<b>Continental building)</b><br>
<b>Horizontal movements</b><br>
(Orogenic/Mountain<br>
building)<br>
<b>Forces of</b><br>
<b>Compression</b><br>
(i.e. Fold Mountains)<br>
<b>Forces of Tension</b><br>
(i.e. Fault Mountains)<br>
<b>Sudden movements</b><br>
<b>Volcanism</b><br>
<b>Upward</b><br>
movement<br>
<b>Downward</b><br>
movement<br>
<b>Earthquakes</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.1: Endogenic processes affecting the Earth</b><br>
<b>The endogenic processes of the Earth</b><br>
are explained by several theories, the<br>
major ones are described below:<br>
<b>Isostasy theory</b><br>
<b>This theory was proposed by Clarence</b><br>
<b>Dutton who was an American</b><br>
<b>Seismologist and Geologist in 1841-</b><br>
1912.  The term isostasy is derived<br>
from two Greek words “isos” which<br>
means equal and “stasis” which refers<br>
to the state of equilibrium or balance or<br>
standing still. Literally, isostasy refers to<br>
equal stand still. Generally, the theory<br>
explains the tendency of the earth’s crust<br>
to attain an equilibrium state and the<br>
distribution of the materials in the earth’s<br>
crust which conforms to the observed<br>
gravity values.<br>
<b>Assumption of the Isostasy theory</b><br>
<b>It is assumed that the Earth’s crust is</b><br>
floating on the molten rocks of the upper<br>
mantle, like a raft floating in the water<br>
rather than resting on the mantle like a<br>
raft sitting on the ground. The theory<br>
states that the lithosphere which has a<br>
constant density of 2.7 grams per cubic<br>
centimetre, floats in the asthenosphere<br>
which has a constant density of 3.3 grams<br>
per cubic centimetre. The theory explains<br>
that there is a state of gravitational<br>
equilibrium between the earth’s<br>
lithosphere and asthenosphere, in such<br>
a way that the tectonic plates floating at<br>
an elevation depend on their thickness<br>
and density (Figure 2.2). This means<br>
that the weight of continental rock (sial-<br>
silica and aluminium) underlies oceanic<br>
rocks (sima-silica and magnesium) of the<br>
crust, which results to a state of balance.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   32</b><br>
09/03/2022   16:59<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
33<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Crust</b><br>
<b>Crust</b><br>
<b>Lithosphere</b><br>
(strong)<br>
<b>Asthenosphere</b><br>
(weak)<br>
<b>Figure 2.2: Lithosphere floating on asthenosphere</b><br>
<b>The theory further states that where</b><br>
the continental masses (Sial) rise to<br>
form a mountain then the penetration<br>
of Sial downward to Sima is greater to<br>
compensate the excess mass. Moreover,<br>
where the continental mass is thin, the<br>
<b>Sima layer come near to the surface or</b><br>
reach the surface of the earth and form<br>
the ocean floor. The principle of the<br>
theory postulates that, the height of the<br>
mountain is proportional to the depth of<br>
its root. Hence, the deeper the root, the<br>
higher the mountain. Thus, the root of the<br>
mountains sinks to depth proportional to<br>
the height above. This state of balance<br>
between different parts of the earth’s<br>
crust is called Isostasy where equal<br>
mass underlying equal surface area. This<br>
concept helps to explain the existence<br>
of different topographic heights on the<br>
earth’s surface. The theory further states<br>
that any disturbance or restoration in<br>
the earth’s crust will cause sinking and<br>
uplift movement of the earth in order<br>
to balance itself. The disturbance can<br>
either be erosion or melting of the large<br>
accumulated snow while restoration<br>
can be deposition or accumulation<br>
of large snow or a volcanic hill. The<br>
theory therefore, describe the state of<br>
balance achieved between erosion and<br>
deposition.<br>
<b>Isostatic movements</b><br>
<b>There are two main movements of</b><br>
isostasy namely vertical and horizontal<br>
movements. Vertical movement is also<br>
known as the movement of weight while<br>
horizontal movement is referred to as the<br>
movement of heat accumulation.<br>
<b>Vertical movement</b><br>
<b>It involves upward and downward</b><br>
movements of materials which enhance<br>
isostatic adjustment. The process is slow<br>
and thus takes thousands of years in<br>
maintaining the balance between oceanic<br>
mass and continental rocks. Several<br>
evidences to prove the existence of<br>
vertical movements can be indicated in<br>
various parts of the world especially in<br>
the poles in connection to the formation<br>
of ice (ice age) and its melting. Therefore,<br>
it happened that when the great ice caps<br>
was formed, the weight of ice caused<br>
sinking or sagging of the crust, and when<br>
the ice melted, recovery gradually took<br>
place producing considerable changes<br>
in the level of the land relative to<br>
that of the sea. The best examples of<br>

09/03/2022   16:59<br>

34<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>

vertical movement can be evidenced in<br>
different parts of the world including,<br>
the continental shelf of Antarctica which<br>
is deeper than others, its water depth<br>
is about 750 m compared with 180 m<br>
around other continents which may be a<br>
result of weight of the present ice sheet.<br>
<b>Another one is the ice cap of Greenland</b><br>
whose landscape is covered by enormous<br>
weight of ice cap unless otherwise, the<br>
surface of underlying plateaus would be<br>
1100 m higher. Also, the disappearance<br>
of ice sheets in northern Europe due<br>
to changes in weather and climate.<br>
<b>Moreover, there is beach formation in</b><br>
<b>Scandinavia due to uplift of land such</b><br>
as “raised beach”.<br>
<b>As the weight of continental rocks</b><br>
increased due to restoration it results to<br>
extra weight which disrupts the balance in<br>
the area which finally causes sinking. The<br>
extra weight is caused by several reasons<br>
such as volcanic eruption, deposition of<br>
sediments, the accumulation of snow and<br>
ice, the decrease of weight of continental<br>
rock as well as denudation and melting<br>
of ice. Large volcanic eruption: The<br>
outpouring of lava due to huge volcanic<br>
eruption that forms volcanic mountain<br>
may cause extra weight, hence a sinking<br>
movement. Deposition of sediment:<br>
<b>When large amounts of sediments are</b><br>
deposited on a particular region, the<br>
enormous weight of the new sediments<br>
may cause the crust below to sink, for<br>
example submergence of . Accumulation<br>
of snow and ice: The formation of ice<br>
sheets and falling of snow for instance<br>
in the northern hemisphere like Arctic,<br>
<b>Alaska, Greenland, Siberia or Antarctica</b><br>
in the South pole have caused the earth’s<br>
surface to sink. The decrease of weight<br>
of continental rock: If the weight of<br>
continental rocks is decreased because<br>
of erosion and melting of ice in one<br>
area, it results in uplifting of a given<br>
area. Denudation: When large amounts<br>
of materials are eroded away from a<br>
region, the land may rise to compensate<br>
the lost height and weight. Therefore,<br>
as a mountain range is eroded down,<br>
the reduced range rebounds upwards to<br>
a certain extent (Figure 2.3 a, b and c).<br>

<b>Melting of ice. Isostatic post glacial</b><br>
rebound is observed in areas once<br>
covered by ice sheets that have now<br>
melted for example, around the<br>
<b>Baltic-sea and Hudson Bay. As the ice</b><br>
retreats, the load on the lithosphere<br>
and asthenosphere is reduced and they<br>
rebound back towards their equilibrium<br>
levels. In this way it is possible to find<br>
former sea cliffs and associated wave-<br>
cut platforms hundreds of meters above<br>
present day sea level.  The rebound<br>
movements are so slow that the uplift<br>
caused by the ending of the last glacial<br>
period is still continuing.<br>
<b>Horizontal movement</b><br>
<b>This occurs when there is a horizontal</b><br>
flow of mantle from the area of<br>
depressed to the area of uplift for<br>
adjustment. The adjustment involves<br>
horizontal flow or convection current.<br>
It involves accumulation of heat.<br>
It takes place in the simatic layer,<br>
especially in the asthenosphere where<br>
there is maximum plasticity (Figure<br>

2.4). There is continuous radioactive<br>
disintegration within the simatic layer<br>
specifically in the asthenosphere. The<br>
disintegration releases heat which<br>
may be accumulated and prevented<br>
by the overlying more solid layer<br>
from escaping at the earth’s surface by<br>
radiation. The heat therefore makes the<br>
sima mobile resulting to the sinking of<br>
the sialic continents, hence periodic<br>
ocean transgression. Contrastingly, if it<br>
happens that, the accumulated heat is<br>
degenerated due to various processes<br>
such as widespread volcanic activities<br>
thus the sima become less fluid and<br>
continents begin to rise. Therefore, it<br>
produces a regression of the sea, hence<br>
the exposure of former seafloor. These<br>
major transgressions and regressions in<br>
the earth are affected by warping and<br>
tilting of the sialic blocks.<br>

<b>Analogy of Isostasy theory</b><br>
<b>Analogy of Isostasy theory may be made</b><br>
with things like; floated icebergs, floated<br>
pieces of wood, and loaded and unloaded<br>
ship. A floated iceberg: An iceberg or ice<br>
mountain is a large piece of freshwater<br>
ice that has broken off a glacier or an ice<br>
shelf and is floating freely in open water.<br>
<b>It may subsequently become frozen into</b><br>
pack ice (one form of sea ice). An iceberg<br>
always floats with a certain proportion of<br>
its mass below the surface of the water.<br>
<b>The portion below the surface of water</b><br>
is compensation portion (Figure 2.5).<br>
<b>If a layer of ice is somehow sliced off</b><br>
on the top of the iceberg, the remaining<br>
iceberg will rise. Similarly, the earth’s<br>
lithosphere ‘floats’ in the asthenosphere<br>
as the iceberg floats on water.<br>
<b>Figure 2.5: Iceberg floating in water</b><br>
<b>A floated piece of wood: The idea of</b><br>
isostasy may be grasped by considering<br>
a series of wooden blocks of different<br>
heights floating in water. If such woods<br>
are immersed in water a taller one will<br>
remain tall and sink down more than a<br>
shorter one (Figure 2.6). The wooden<br>
blocks emerge by amounts which are<br>
proportional to their respective heights.<br>
<b>The submerged pieces of wood are</b><br>
proportional to their respective emerged<br>
parts.<br>

Loading and unloading ship: Floating ships show how isostasy affects the<br>
behavior of the crust as it floats on the heavy mantle rock. For instance, when<br>
cargo is transferred from ship ‘A’ to ship ‘B’, ship ‘A’ rises in the water and ship<br>
‘B’ sinks (Figure 2.7). Gradually corresponding to the process of loading and<br>
unloading of the cargo.<br>
<b>Ship A</b><br>
<b>Ship B</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.7:   Compensation process</b><br>
The earth maintains its balance in the same way as ships. The process of loading<br>
and unloading the eroded materials of the earth’s surface disturbs the balance on<br>
the earth. Therefore, the process of compensation takes place to restore it. The<br>
restoration process is extremely slow as it takes over thousands of years.<br>
