<b>The evidence of isostatic movement on earth’s surface</b><br>
The evidences of Isostatic movements can be seen in different parts of the world<br>
as explained as follows;<br>
The presence of raised beach in Scandinavia: The raised beach in Scandinavia<br>
which was covered by a vast ice sheet about 10 000 years ago, is still rebounding<br>
isostically at the rate of up to 1m per country.  The result is the formation of raised<br>
beaches which lie between 8-30 m above present beaches. Similarly, the Ballyhillin<br>
beach in Ireland is rebounding isostatically to form raised beaches (Figure 2.8).<br>
<b>Raised beach</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.8: Ballyhillin raised beach in Ireland</b><br>
<b>The evidence of old constructed dock</b><br>
that is Coastal cities in Scandinavia<br>
have been uplifted rapidly enough and<br>
the docks which are constructed several<br>
centuries ago are now far from the shore.<br>
<b>The coastal areas are rebounding to</b><br>
maintain balance due to the areas covered<br>
by vast ice sheet during ice age.<br>
<b>Depression formation: This is another</b><br>
evidence. A depression is a landform<br>
sunken below the surface area.<br>
<b>Depressions may be formed by various</b><br>
mechanisms for example, erosion-<br>
related whereby features like blowouts<br>
are created by wind erosion typically<br>
in either a partially vegetated sand<br>
dune ecosystem or dry soils. Also, area<br>
of subsidence caused by the collapse<br>
of an underlying structure such as<br>
sinkholes in karst terrain. In Greenland<br>
and Antarctica, the surface has been<br>
depressed below sea level by the weight<br>
of glacial ice so as to maintain balance.<br>
<b>Land uplift in Canada: is another</b><br>
evidence in which isostatic rebound has<br>
also occurred indifferent areas such as<br>

<b>Eastern Canada and Southern Ontario</b><br>

<b>During the ice age, the weight of the</b><br>

when the glacier melted because of the<br>

<b>The depth of continental shelf: This is</b><br>
another evidenced where the continental<br>
shelf around Antarctica is covered with<br>
water to a depth of about 2 500 feet<br>
(750 m) compared with 600 feet (180<br>
m) around other continents. This may<br>
be the result of the weight of the present<br>
ice. The present Antarctic ice sheet as a<br>
surface load makes the Earth deform.<br>
<b>This explains why continental shelf of</b><br>
<b>Antarctica is very deep than that of the</b><br>
whole Antarctica continent including the<br>
continental shelf that subsided by the<br>
existence of Antarctic ice sheet as a huge<br>
load on earth’s surface compared with<br>
other continental shelf of the world. The<br>
<b>Antarctic continent is almost isostatic</b><br>
equilibrium.<br>
<b>Existence of mountain ranges: Despite</b><br>
the continuous process of erosion,<br>
several mountains have maintained their<br>
heights. For instance, Mt. Kilimanjaro<br>
has maintained its height of about<br>
5895 m for thousands of years.<br>
<b>Existence of water bodies and depression</b><br>
areas: Despite the continuous deposition<br>
activity which accumulate and deposit<br>
millions of tons of materials, water bodies<br>
like Indian ocean, Lake Victoria, and<br>
Lake Tanganyika have not disappeared.<br>
<b>Also, the emergence of emerged coast</b><br>
formed as the result of land uplift around<br>
the coast of scandinavian countries.<br>
<b>The significance of the Isostasy theory</b><br>
<b>Several implications of the isostasy</b><br>
theory can be provided. However,<br>
the most significant are; the theory<br>
signifies the state of equilibrium in the<br>
earth’s crust with equal mass underline<br>
equal surface area. Therefore, it gives<br>
an understanding on the earth’s crust<br>
dynamic state as it is influenced by<br>
internal and external forces. Also, the<br>
relationship which is made whereby the<br>
crust floats on mantle just like the iceberg<br>
on the ocean or seawater is important<br>
in understanding of plate tectonic and<br>
continental drift theories. Precaution<br>
can be taken depending on nature of<br>
the climatic condition of a given area<br>
especially in the occurrence of ice<br>
sheet and melting of ice. It provides<br>
the basis for predicting the future of<br>
the crustal state at any place on the<br>
surface of the earth and understanding<br>
how different landforms were formed<br>
and the mechanism involved in their<br>
formation.<br>
<b>The effects of isostatic movements</b><br>
<b>Several effects on the earth’s surface</b><br>
may be caused by isostatic movements.<br>
<b>The vertical and horizontal movements</b><br>
of isostasy result into occurrences of<br>
faulting and folding of the earth’s crust.<br>
<b>Faulting and folding have the prolonged</b><br>
impact of causing rift valleys, Mountains<br>

<b>Continental drift theory</b><br>
<b>Continental drift is the second theory</b><br>
proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912<br>
to explain how the continents have<br>
shifted from their original position on<br>
the earth’s surface. The theory was<br>
initially developed by F.B. Taylor who<br>
postulated about the concept of horizontal<br>
displacement of the continents to explain<br>
the distribution of the fold mountains.<br>
<b>Wegener explained that the earth’s</b><br>
continents were once united forming a<br>
single super continent land mass called<br>
<b>Pangaea (Greek word which means all</b><br>
lands together) which was surrounded<br>
by a huge water-mass (ocean) called<br>
<b>Panthalassa about 280 million years</b><br>
ago. Later on about 200 million years<br>
<b>Pangaea broke up to form two land</b><br>
masses. The northern continent which<br>
was known as Laurasia and the southern<br>
continent called Gondwanaland. These<br>
continents were separated by a long<br>
narrow sea called Tethys. About 150<br>
million years later, between the Mid-<br>
Permian and the cretaceous periods,<br>
Laurasia broke up into three continents,<br>
that is Europe, North America and Asia<br>
while Gondwanaland broke up and<br>
formed Africa, South America, Australia,<br>
<b>New Zealand, Antarctica, and Indian</b><br>
<b>Sub-continent as shown in Figure 2.9(a)</b><br>
and 2.9(b).<br>

<b>Figure 2.9:  Continental drift</b><br>
<b>Different scientists explain various</b><br>
causes of continental drift. Among the<br>
scientists include S.B Taylor, A. Holmes<br>
and A. Wegener. The proposed causes<br>
were centrifugal force, tidal attraction<br>
and the force which operate within the<br>
Earth or convection and transfer of heat.<br>
<b>Wegener proposed three main causes</b><br>
of continental drifting which include<br>
centrifugal force, tidal attraction and<br>
convection transfer of heat.<br>
<b>Centrifugal force: The rotation of the</b><br>
earth created a centrifugal force towards<br>
the equator.  He believed that Pangaea<br>
originated near the South Pole and that<br>
the centrifugal force of the planet caused<br>
the proto continent to break apart and the<br>
resultant continents to drift towards the<br>
equator. Wegener called this the pole-<br>
fleeing force. According to Wegener the<br>
continents after breaking away from the<br>
<b>Pangaea moved in two directions namely</b><br>
equator-ward and westward movements.<br>
<b>The equator-ward movement of sialic</b><br>
blocks was caused by gravitational<br>
force and force of buoyancy. Wegener<br>
explained that the lighten sialic materials<br>
were floating without friction on<br>
relatively denser (SIMA) materials.<br>
<b>Equator-ward movement depended</b><br>
on the relation of the centre of gravity<br>
and centre of buoyancy of the floating<br>
continental mass.<br>
<b>Tidal attraction: Wegener presumed that</b><br>
tidal influence of the sun and moon gave<br>
the continent westward motion, a counter<br>
movement of the revolution of the earth.<br>
<b>A. Wegener (1922) complemented</b><br>
<b>F.B Taylor (1910) who had previous</b><br>
proposed that the main driving force<br>
possible for displacement of the continent<br>
was tidal force. With regard to Wegener<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   41</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
42<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
the attractional force resulted from the<br>
sun and the moon which attained its<br>
maximum when the moon was nearest<br>
to the Earth, thus dragged the outer<br>
sialic crust (continental blocks) over<br>
the interior of the earth towards the West.<br>
Convection transfer of heat: In 1929,<br>
<b>Arthur Holmes elaborated one of the</b><br>
<b>Wegener’s hypotheses about the idea that</b><br>
the mantle undergoes thermal convection.<br>
<b>This idea is based on the fact that, when a</b><br>
substance is heated its density decreases<br>
and arises to the surface until is cooled<br>
and sinks again.  The repeated heating<br>
and cooling results in a current which<br>
may be enough to cause such a substance<br>
to slightly move. This is associated<br>
with the movement of continents.<br>
<b>Evidences of the continental drift</b><br>
<b>Ever since, Wegener and other scholars</b><br>
put forward the continental drift theory,<br>
various evidences have been provided to<br>
support the idea. The following are some<br>
of the available evidences to support<br>
the theory.<br>
<b>Jig-saw fit: according to Wegener there</b><br>
is geographical similarity among various<br>
coasts of different continents. This is<br>
known as jig-saw fit. It is obvious that<br>
some coastlines of continent could fit<br>
into one another along the margin if<br>
brought together. For example, the east<br>
coast of South America could fit into the<br>
west coast of Africa (Figure 2.10). The<br>
<b>Jig-saw fit implies that all continents</b><br>
were formed from a single landmass.<br>
<b>South America</b><br>
<b>Africa</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.10: Jig-saw fit of the continents</b><br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
Paleontological evidence: This is a biological evidence which explain the similarity<br>
of plants and animals’ fossils in rocks with similar age which are found on the shores<br>
of the different continents which suggests that the continents were once connected.<br>
For example, fossils of Mosasaurs, a freshwater reptile, have been found both in<br>
Brazil and Western Africa. Also, fossils of the land reptile Lystrosaurus have been<br>
found in rocks of the same age in Africa, India and Antarctica (Figure 2.11). Also,<br>
the distribution of glossopteris flora in India, South Africa, Australia, Antarctica,<br>
Falkland island proves the fact that all landmasses were previously united.<br>

<b>Geological similarities: There are number</b><br>
of large scale geological features which<br>
are matching on different continents.<br>
<b>This evidence uses identical rocks in</b><br>
terms of types, structures and ages<br>
which are found in both sides of Atlantic<br>
<b>Ocean, for example the coastlines of</b><br>
<b>South America and West Africa seem to</b><br>
match up, more particularly the terrains<br>
of separate continents conform as well.<br>
<b>Similarly, the familiar rock strata of the</b><br>
<b>Karroo system of South Africa matched</b><br>
correctly with the Santa Catarina system<br>
in Brazil. Moreover, the mountain ranges<br>
with the same rock types, structure and<br>
age are now found on opposite sides of<br>
the Atlantic Ocean.  For example, the<br>
<b>Appalachian Mountains in Eastern North</b><br>
<b>America are linked with the Eastern</b><br>
Greenland mountain ranges.<br>
<b>Evidence from glaciations: During the</b><br>
latter part of the paregoric era (about 300<br>
million years ago), glaciations occurred<br>

throughout a large part of the continent in<br>
the Southern hemispheres. The deposits<br>
left by these ancient glaciers are real<br>
recognized by striations and grooves<br>
on the underlying rocks showing the<br>
direction in which the ice moved. The<br>
presence of glacial deposited in the<br>
Congo basin where the climate is warm,<br>
used as the evidence that the continents<br>
drifted from regions which were cold to<br>
currently warm regions. For instance,<br>
<b>Africa has been shifted northwards from</b><br>
the south, likewise the presence of coal in<br>
<b>Britain indicates that coal was deposited</b><br>
before shifting of the continents, and the<br>
condition was warm around 300 million<br>
years ago. This is because plants which<br>
led to the formation of coal deposits<br>
cannot exist in areas where there is very<br>
low temperature as the case of today.<br>
<b>Paleomagnetism: The most powerful</b><br>
argument for large scale plate motion are<br>
derived from studies of different aspects<br>
of the earth’s magnetic field. When the<br>
rocks cooled, they were magnetized in<br>
the same direction where magnetic field<br>
(magnetic north) is found.  The paleo<br>
magnetic dating shows that rocks older<br>
than 200 000 years ago from different<br>
parts of the earth have shifted their<br>
relative position, which indicates the<br>
shifting of continents. The shifting of<br>
continents is detected by using a sensitive<br>
instrument called magnetometer which is<br>
capable of determining the direction of<br>
magnetic poles at a given geographical<br>
period.<br>
<b>The formation of rift valley: The presence</b><br>
of rift valleys like the Great East African<br>
rift valley is another major indication<br>
to show that the continents drifted at<br>
a certain geographical period. This rift<br>
valley is formed due to the movement<br>
of plates beneath the continents. The<br>
tensional force of the moving plates<br>
forms cracks which finally form a rift<br>
valley (Figure 2.12).<br>

The red sea evidence: This began as a rift valley 20 million years ago and now<br>
is over 300 km wide. It was formed by divergence between African continent<br>
(plate) and Arabian plate (Figure 2.13).<br>

<b>Coral reefs in the north pole: Recent</b><br>
expedition to the cold north water<br>
especially Arctic have revealed giant<br>
deep sea coral reefs. Normally the coral<br>
reefs develop in the warm water region.<br>
<b>The presence of coral reefs in the cold</b><br>
north water (Arctic) is an evidence that<br>
<b>Laurasia was once located along the</b><br>
tropics.<br>
<b>Climate similarities and conditions: Also</b><br>
known as paleoclimate. Coal formed<br>
under warm wet conditions that were<br>
found beneath the Antarctic-ice cap<br>
were also noticeable in Tropical Brazil<br>
and Central India. Moreover, other<br>
minerals such as coal, sandstone, and<br>
limestone could not have been formed<br>
in Britain having its present climatic<br>
conditions.<br>
<b>Geomorphological evidence: This was</b><br>
based on the nature, age, structure and<br>
chain of Atlas mountain in Africa and<br>
<b>Alps mountain in Europe that seemed</b><br>
to be similar in age, structure and<br>
alignment. It is believed that possibly<br>
the mountains were formed when Africa<br>
moved northward ending up cooling with<br>
European continent.<br>
<b>The consequences of continental drift</b><br>
<b>The consequences of continental drift</b><br>
are highlighted based on the formation<br>
of features, climate change, changes<br>
in oceanic current pattern, evolution<br>
of animals and speciation and crustal<br>
deformation. These consequences<br>
include:<br>

09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
46<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Formation of physical features: Drifting</b><br>
of continents led to the formation of<br>
various features such as mountains,<br>
oceans, seas and the rift valleys. F.B<br>
<b>Taylor (1910) hypothesises that fold</b><br>
mountains were formed as a result of<br>
movement of landmass. According<br>
to Taylor, the wrinkling of crust is<br>
due to the drifting of continents. The<br>
drifting of continents is believed to be<br>
causing the natural catastrophes such<br>
as earthquakes, volcanic eruption and<br>
tsunamis. Drifting up of the landmass<br>
resulted to the formation of oceans and<br>
seas. For example, westward movement<br>
of landmass opened the Atlantic Ocean<br>
while Red Sea was formed after a<br>
divergence between the African plate<br>
and the Arabian plate.<br>
<b>Climate changes: Climate change is a</b><br>
long-term shift in weather conditions<br>
identified by changes in temperature,<br>
precipitation, winds, and other<br>
indicators. Climate change can involve<br>
both changes in average conditions and<br>
changes in variability, including, extreme<br>
weather events. Commonly known as<br>
net pole-ward movement resulted to<br>
same continents shifting to the polar<br>
areas where they are assumed that there<br>
was very low temperature contrary to<br>
those positioned at the equator where<br>
the temperature is high like the African<br>
continent.<br>
<b>Ocean currents pattern change: The</b><br>
ocean currents in some parts of the<br>
world were also affected in terms of<br>
temperature. For example, the waters<br>
around New Zealand’s South Island<br>
cooled from 200C at the beginning of<br>
<b>Eocene period about 56-53 million years</b><br>
ago to 170C during 30–14 million years<br>
ago and then cooling to the present to<br>
<b>120C.</b><br>
<b>Evolution of animals and speciation:</b><br>
<b>The rearrangement and displacement of</b><br>
landmasses helped to create the diversity<br>
of animals which we are seeing today.<br>
<b>Without continental drift, the Earth</b><br>
today would have been very different<br>
in terms of animal diversities available in<br>
different parts of the world. Continental<br>
drift promoted allopatric speciation when<br>
the united landmass separated regions<br>
that were once connected become<br>
geographically isolated. Each separated<br>
region becomes an evolutionary arena<br>
with plants and animals diverged from<br>
those on other continents. For example,<br>
the distribution of the flightless running<br>
birds or ratites in South America, Africa<br>
and Australia is the result of breakdown<br>
of the Pangaea.<br>
<b>Crustal deformation: The drifting of</b><br>
continents is believed to be causing the<br>
natural catastrophes such as earthquakes,<br>
volcanic eruption and tsunamis. When<br>
the landmasses moved they caused<br>
crustal deformation as the applied<br>
force exceeded internal strength of<br>
the contracted rock. The force creates<br>
physical changes and causes fold,<br>
fracture and faults. When energy is<br>
released brittle deformation causes an<br>
earthquake.<br>

<b>Economic activities change: The</b><br>
geographical position changed and<br>
have made some of the possible human<br>
activities which depend on the nature<br>
of the present position of that region.<br>
<b>For example, if Africa remained in the</b><br>
<b>Southern pole, some human activities</b><br>
like agriculture would not be possible.<br>
<b>Also, continental drift resulted into</b><br>
features like sea, ocean, rift valley and<br>
mountains. These features have brought<br>
various human activities such as fishing<br>
and tourist attraction.<br>
<b>Critiques of the theory of continental</b><br>
drift<br>
<b>Despite the explained causes and</b><br>
evidences, continental drift still faces<br>
a number of critiques. Some of the<br>
main critiques are; Wegener has failed<br>
to explain convincingly the causes and<br>
mechanisms of continental drift theory.<br>
<b>For instance, Wegener presumed that tidal</b><br>
influence of the moon gave the continents<br>
westward motion, a counter movement<br>
of the revolution of the earth. Another<br>
opposition emphasized that from Jeffey<br>
(1920) a physicist, tidal friction of such<br>
magnitude which needed to displace the<br>
continents would bring the earth’s rotation<br>
to a half in a matter of few years. On the<br>
other hand, the idea of centrifugal forces<br>
was rejected by scientific community,<br>
primarily because the actual force<br>
generated by the rotation of the earth<br>
were calculated to be insufficient to<br>
move the continents. The use of jig saw<br>
puzzle analogy as one of the evidences<br>
of his theory was not easily accepted.<br>
<b>The argument was that, it was not a</b><br>
perfect fit the fact that not all continents<br>
can fit exactly to each other whereas the<br>
continents true shape was discovered<br>
not to be the shoreline around them, but<br>
the edges of their continental shelves.<br>
<b>Wegener’s explanation of the physical</b><br>
process that separated the continents<br>
was weak and strongly criticized on<br>
valid physical grounds. He proposed<br>
that a continental layer of less dense<br>
rock had moved like a great floating<br>
raft through a sea of denser oceanic<br>
crustal rock.  Geologists could show by<br>
using established principles of physics<br>
that the proposed mechanism was<br>
impossible. Wegener failed to explain<br>
on the development of gracier in the<br>
hot desert such as Iran’s hot desert that<br>
exists to the present.<br>

<b>Seafloor spreading theory</b><br>
<b>Seafloor spreading is one of the modern</b><br>
theories put forward by American<br>
geologist Harry Hess in 1960’s which<br>
supports the idea of continental drift. It<br>
provides explanation on the formation<br>
of the oceanic mountain ranges called<br>
mid-oceanic ridges as fundamental<br>
results of plate tectonic movements<br>
that necessitated continental drifting.<br>
<b>Hess stated that new crustal materials</b><br>
are being formed along the mid oceanic<br>
ridges as a results of earth’s mantle<br>
which behave like a giant convectional<br>
system in which materials are heated<br>
by radioactive elements and rises to<br>
the surface to form new oceanic crust.<br>
<b>As new crustal materials form, new</b><br>
materials push apart the old seafloor on<br>
both side of the ridges and this process<br>
leads to spreading apart of the seafloor<br>
at a rate of 1 – 10 centimetres per year.<br>
<b>A good example is Atlantic Ocean</b><br>
which widens at a rate of 1 to 10 cm<br>
per year.<br>
<b>Seafloor spreading does not lead to</b><br>
enlargement of the crust. This is because<br>
as the new seafloor gains materials at the<br>
mid ridge it loses old material elsewhere<br>
in the deep trenches where the crust of<br>
the new floor is moving downward to<br>
the mantle and then melts and becomes<br>
part of the mantle which then will be<br>
forced out in the form of vulcanicity.<br>
<b>By this process the continents carried</b><br>
above are forced to move along it<br>
(Figure 2.14).<br>
<b>Rocks</b><br>
increasingly<br>
older<br>
<b>Younger</b><br>
rocks<br>

<b>Evidences of Seafloor spreading</b><br>
There are several evidences that support seafloor spreading theory namely presence<br>
of oceanic ridges, rifts, differences in the ages of rocks along the seafloor and<br>
magnetic evidence.<br>

09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
49<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Presence of oceanic ridges: A mid-</b><br>
oceanic ridge is a seafloor mountain<br>
system formed by plate tectonics.<br>
<b>Typically, it has a depth of 2 600 metres</b><br>
and rises about two kilometres above the<br>
deepest portion of an ocean basin. This<br>
feature is where seafloor spreading takes<br>
place along a divergent plate boundary.<br>
<b>Presence of fractures and rifts at the</b><br>
mid oceanic ridge: The center of the mid<br>
ocean is marked by a deep and steeply<br>
walled valley. It is believed that this<br>
is where molten rock comes up from<br>
the mantle.  The molten materials cool<br>
and harden to become part of the ocean<br>
floor. The addition of these materials<br>
creates new crust on each side of the<br>
mid ocean ridge. This is manifested<br>
through the spread of the seafloor of<br>
<b>Atlantic and Indian Oceans. The Atlantic</b><br>
<b>Ocean widens by 5 cm per year. The</b><br>
presence of oceanic trenches which form<br>
as the seafloor buckles in response to<br>
this outward push and evidence from<br>
seismic devices which show that crust<br>
is bending downward in the trenches.<br>
<b>Differences in ages of rocks along</b><br>
the seafloor: Exploration of the mid<br>
ocean ridge has shown that rocks at its<br>
center are younger than the rocks in the<br>
seafloor on either sides. Thus, a move<br>
away from the ridge in either direction<br>
mark the increase of the age of the rocks<br>
of the seafloor.  Also evidences show<br>
that seafloor rocks are younger than<br>
continental rocks. The youngest rocks<br>
along the seafloor are along the rifts of mid<br>
oceanic ridges and older rocks are found<br>
further away from mid-oceanic ridges.<br>
<b>Magnetic evidence: In molten rock</b><br>
iron atoms move about and point to<br>
the magnetic poles of the time, after<br>
solidification iron atoms are locked into<br>
place. These rocks of different ages show<br>
different directions to magnetic poles.<br>
<b>Thus, rocks that hardened in different</b><br>
times show different directions in mid<br>
oceanic ridges. It should be noted that<br>
magnetic poles have been discovered<br>
to be reversing from year to year. That<br>
is, magnetic pole is in the south for a<br>
period of time and then in the north<br>
for a further period of time and so on.<br>
<b>It is claimed that there have been 171</b><br>
reversals over 76 million years. Thus if<br>
basaltic lava formed when the magnetic<br>
pole was in the north new basalt iron<br>
atoms would be aligned to the north,<br>
after the reversal newer lava would be<br>
oriented to the south.<br>
<b>The presence of oceanic trench: This is</b><br>
an indicator that subduction has taken<br>
place after seafloor spreading.<br>
<b>Strength of the Seafloor spreading</b><br>
theory<br>
<b>The Seafloor spreading theory explains</b><br>
the features of the seafloor as it offers<br>
reasons for the presence of oceanic<br>
ridges, rifts along the oceanic ridges,<br>
formation of deep trenches as well as<br>
the reasons for seafloor spreading. Also,<br>
it help us to understand the causes of<br>
movement of lithosphere. However, the<br>
theory fails to explain the mechanism<br>
that would allow continents to move<br>
through ocean basins.<br>

<b>Exercise 2.3</b><br>
1.	 With the aid of diagram,<br>
explain the theory of seafloor<br>
spreading<br>
2.	 How does seafloor spreading<br>
theory connect to previous<br>
theories of the movement of<br>
continents?<br>
3.	 Explain how the theory of<br>
seafloor spreading is related to<br>
the occurrence of earthquakes<br>
and volcanoes.<br>
4.	 Explain the weaknesses of the<br>
Seafloor spreading theory.<br>
<b>Plate tectonic theory</b><br>
<b>Plate tectonics are geological</b><br>
components of study related to the<br>
origin and arrangement of the broad<br>
structural features of the earth’s surface<br>
including not only folds and faults but<br>
also mountain belts, continents and<br>
earthquake belts. The concept of plate<br>
tectonics was brought out in the late<br>
1960’s in attempt to address weaknesses<br>
of two preexisting theories of continental<br>
drift and seafloor spreading. The word<br>
“tectonic” comes from Greek word<br>
“tekton” which means a builder. It is<br>
of wider implication as it includes the<br>
effects of vulcanicity and earthquakes.<br>
<b>The basic idea of plate tectonics is that</b><br>
the lithosphere (Earth’s crust and the<br>
upper rigid part of the mantle) is divided<br>
into a number of separate parts called<br>
tectonic plates which move slowly. At<br>
present it is believed that there are seven<br>
(7) large plates and about thirteen (13)<br>
or more small plates, making a total of<br>
about twenty (20) plates or more.<br>
<b>The larger plates</b><br>
<b>The seven major plates include:</b><br>
(a)	 The North American plate - It<br>
includes surrounding oceanic crust<br>
up to the mid-Atlantic ridge.<br>
(b)	 South American Plate - It includes<br>
surrounding oceanic crust up to the<br>
mid-Atlantic ridge.<br>
(c)	 The Pacific Plate - This plate is the<br>
largest of all covering about one-<br>
fifth of the earth’s surface, hence<br>
occupies the whole of Pacific region<br>
(d)	 The Eurasian plate - It is a<br>
continental plate, thus it occupies<br>
both Europe and Asia continents,<br>
include the surrounding areas.<br>
(e)	 The Antarctic Plate -  It covers<br>
the Antarctica continent and the<br>
surrounding Southern Ocean<br>
(f)	 The African Plate - It takes all of<br>
<b>Africa and the surrounding area to</b><br>
the mid-Atlantic ridge.<br>
(g)	 The Indian plate - This includes<br>
the continental crust of India and<br>
<b>Australia as well as oceanic crust</b><br>
of India and parts of Pacific Ocean.<br>
<b>The small plates</b><br>
<b>The smaller plates are greater in number</b><br>
than major plates ranging to about<br>
13, including Caribbean, Philippine,<br>

<b>Assumptions of the plate tectonic</b><br>
theory<br>
<b>The plate tectonic theory states that ‘the</b><br>
earth’s crust is divided into separate parts<br>
called tectonic plates. These plates are<br>
mobile and float on underlying semi<br>
molten mantle (the asthenosphere) and<br>
are moved by convectional currents.<br>
<b>Generally, the theory can also be</b><br>
explained as the study of the movement of<br>
the plates and their resultant landforms.<br>
<b>The plates are basically divided into two</b><br>
types which are continental plates and<br>
oceanic plates.<br>
<b>Continental plates</b><br>
<b>These are plates which carry the</b><br>
continents above them, hence referred<br>
as continental mass. They are composed<br>
of sialic older, lighter rock of granitic<br>
type. They are dominated by minerals<br>
rich in silica and aluminum (sial). They<br>
have old rocks mainly over 1500 million<br>
years. The continental plate of Africa,<br>
<b>North America, and South America are</b><br>
the good examples of continental plates<br>
with old rocks.<br>
<b>Oceanic plates</b><br>
<b>These are made up of simatic younger</b><br>
and denser rock of basalt composition,<br>
dominated with silica and magnesium<br>
minerals.  They are young mainly<br>
under 200 million years. The boundary<br>
between the crust (continental or<br>
oceanic) and the underlying mantle<br>
is known as Moho or Mohorovicic<br>
discontinuity that was named after a<br>
study conducted by Andrija Mohorovicic<br>
in 1909.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   51</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
52<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Causes of plate movement</b><br>
<b>The causes of plate movement are mantle</b><br>
convectional current, formation of new<br>
oceanic crust, cooling and sinking of<br>
oceanic crust, differences in force of<br>
gravity between the oceanic ridge and<br>
trench and oceanic topography (elevation<br>
of the mid oceanic ridge). Mantle<br>
convectional current: Radioactively<br>
generated heat in the upper mantle<br>
produces convectional currents which<br>
carry plates along them with the<br>
continents as passengers. The heated<br>
material in mantle expands and becomes<br>
less dense and slowly rises, when it<br>
reaches near the crust, it cools slowly<br>
and contrasts hence, become denser and<br>
sinks down again (Figure 2.16). The<br>
new oceanic crust gradually cools and<br>
thickens with age and is pushed downhill<br>
as new magma emerged from the active<br>
zones of divergence behind it. This force<br>
is regarded as a secondary force.<br>
<b>Trench</b><br>
<b>Trench</b><br>
<b>Ridge</b><br>
<b>Mantle</b><br>
700 km<br>
<b>Outer core</b><br>
<b>Inner</b><br>
core<br>
<b>Lithosphere</b><br>
<b>Asthenosphere</b><br>
<b>“SLAB PULL”</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.16: Mantle convectional current</b><br>
<b>Formation of new oceanic crust: New</b><br>
oceanic crust is formed at the mid<br>
oceanic ridge as the magma pushes<br>
the earlier old rocks on either sides of<br>
the seafloor, then cools and solidifies.<br>
This action causes the plates to move.<br>
<b>This is common at mid-Atlantic Ocean</b><br>
where the African plate moves away<br>
from American plates. Cooling and<br>
sinking of oceanic crust: Oceanic<br>
crust is less dense than asthenosphere.<br>
<b>However, it becomes more denser</b><br>
when it is old. When it is denser than<br>
asthenosphere, it sinks into the mantle<br>
thus acts as a driving force of the<br>
plates to move. As a new crust slowly<br>
drifts away from the ridge, it cools and<br>
becomes denser, thus causing the sea<br>
bed to sink at subduction zone. As it<br>
moves down into subduction zone, the<br>
crust is pushed down under another<br>
plate. It bends down and sinks into<br>
the mantle. Thus keeping the plates<br>
moving. Differences in forces of gravity<br>
between the oceanic ridge and oceanic<br>
trench: The existing differences of<br>
gravitational pool between the ridge<br>
and trench causes the ridge to be pulled<br>
to the deep trench hence causing motion<br>
of the plates. Oceanic topography:<br>
<b>Oceanic topography is also known as</b><br>
elevation of the mid oceanic ridges.<br>
<b>The loading and unloading theory</b><br>
applies as elevated parts subjected to<br>
extra weight resulting into sinking<br>
of the materials which in turn lead to<br>
plate motion. As plates move, they<br>
either collide, move apart or slide past<br>
each other resulting to the formation of<br>
various landforms such as mountains<br>
trenches, earthquakes, volcanoes, and<br>
mid oceanic ridges.<br>
<b>Usually, landforms formation occur in</b><br>
plate boundaries, and zones or margins.<br>
<b>However, before accounting for the</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   52</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
53<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
formation of these landforms, several<br>
aspects should be noted;<br>
(a)	 Due to its relatively low density<br>
continental crust does not sink<br>
and so it is permanently floating<br>
on denser oceanic plate.  Oceanic<br>
crust is being formed and destroyed<br>
continuously.<br>
(b)	 Continental plates, such as the<br>
<b>Eurasian plate, may consist of both</b><br>
continental and oceanic crust.<br>
(c)	 Continental crust may extend far<br>
beyond the margins of the land<br>
masses.<br>
(d)	 Plates cannot overlap.  This<br>
means that either they must be<br>
pushed upwards on impact to form<br>
mountains or one plate must be<br>
forced downwards into the mantle<br>
and destroyed.<br>
(e)	 No ‘gap’ may occur on the earth’s<br>
surface, so if two plates are moving<br>
apart, new oceanic crust originating<br>
from the mantle forms new ocean<br>
floor.<br>
(f)	 The earth is neither expanding nor<br>
shrinking in size. Thus when new<br>
oceanic crust is being formed in one<br>
place, older oceanic crust must be<br>
destroyed in another part.<br>
(g)	 Plate movement is slow (though not<br>
in geological terms) and is usually<br>
continuous. Sudden movements are<br>
detected as earthquakes.<br>
(h)	 Most significant landforms (fold<br>
Mountain, volcanoes, island arc,<br>
deep sea trenches and batholith<br>
intrusions) are found at plate<br>
boundaries.  Very little change<br>
occurs in plate centers (shield<br>
lands).<br>
<b>The plate margins</b><br>
<b>These are important regions, zones or</b><br>
boundaries where plates either meet,<br>
slide or move apart from one another.<br>
<b>The margins are also active seismically</b><br>
and tectonically. Plate margins are<br>
characterized by a combination of<br>
tectonic and topographic features:<br>
oceanic ridges, Benioff zones, young<br>
fold mountains, and transform faults.<br>
<b>Plate boundaries can be classified</b><br>
into three main types which includes<br>
divergence, convergence and transform/<br>
conservative/shear boundaries.<br>
<b>Divergence plate boundary</b><br>
<b>This boundary (zone) is also known</b><br>
as constructive zone. This is a zone<br>
whereby two plates move away from<br>
one another. These margins are also<br>
called constructive zones because new<br>
materials from the interior part of the<br>
earth are added to the surface forming<br>
some new features like the mid-ocean<br>
ridge, island arcs, and new oceanic crust.<br>
<b>A divergence zone can be found between</b><br>
oceanic plates, continental plates, and<br>
continental and oceanic plates. When<br>
divergence occurs in the ocean floor no<br>
gap will be left on the ocean floor as new<br>
crustal material from mantle fills them.<br>
<b>Also, when divergence occurs on the</b><br>
continent (Figure 2.17), the continent<br>

will break up and drift apart and the<br>

features.<br>

<b>Formation of rift valley occurs if the</b><br>
divergence boundary is on the continental<br>
land. For example, the East Africa rift<br>
valley. Volcanic features like volcanic<br>
mountain are formed as magma forces<br>
along the fault. Mount Kilimanjaro is<br>
an example of volcanic mountains. A<br>
divergent zone on oceanic to oceanic<br>
boundary, is associated with formation<br>
of oceanic ridges if two oceanic plates<br>
move away from each other. An example<br>
of this is the Mid Atlantic Ridges which<br>
are 1000 km wide and 2500 km high. The<br>
formation of rift valley at the mid-oceanic<br>
ridges happens when the oceanic ridge<br>
breaks at the center. The Atlantic Ocean<br>
is an example of seafloor spreading. It<br>
was formed due to divergence of South<br>
America and African plates. Moreover,<br>
volcanic islands are formed from the<br>
rising submarine volcano at divergence<br>
zone that solidified to form an island.<br>
<b>Volcanicity and shallow earthquakes are</b><br>
also resulting features at divergent zones<br>
on oceanic boundary.<br>
<b>Convergence plate boundary</b><br>
<b>The convergence plate boundary, it is</b><br>
also known as a destructive or consuming<br>
plate margin. This occurs when two plates<br>
move towards each other and result into<br>
formation of trenches such as Marianas,<br>
fold mountains like the Himalaya and<br>
volcanic islands like Japan. Convergence<br>

boundary is divided into three main categories namely; continental-oceanic,<br>
continental-continental and oceanic-oceanic convergence boundaries.<br>
<b>Continental - Oceanic convergence boundary</b><br>
The continental - oceanic convergence boundary forms when oceanic floors (plate)<br>
and continental plate meet. As they meet, the heavier rocks (oceanic floor) will be<br>
forced to sink while the lighter continental rock will be forced to rise. The sinking<br>
oceanic rock will be destroyed in hot mantle and come out in the form of volcano<br>
(Figure 2.18). The subduction zone is active in volcanicity and earthquakes zones.<br>

<b>Continental - Continental convergence boundary</b><br>
Continental - Continental convergence boundary is the zone where two similar<br>
plates (continental and continental) collide such that neither of them sinks below<br>
the other part rather they are forced to bend hence leading to the formation of fold<br>
as well as mountains such as the Himalaya, Alps and Atlas (Figure 2.19). Also, it<br>
is known as destructive zone because the plates tend to lose the materials at the<br>
margins where collision occurs.<br>

<b>Oceanic - Oceanic convergence boundary</b><br>
Oceanic - Oceanic convergence boundary occurs when two oceanic plates converge,<br>
one is subducted beneath the other along an oceanic- oceanic plate boundary<br>
especially when one oceanic plate is slightly denser than the other. An oceanic<br>
trench is formed where one oceanic plate is subducted beneath another (Figure<br>
2.20). On the non-subducted plate, a volcanic island arc forms from the rising<br>
magma generated from the subducting plate. A good example is an island of Japan.<br>

<b>Resulting landforms on convergence</b><br>
plate boundary<br>
<b>Converging boundaries are associated</b><br>
with various landforms. For example,<br>
formation of deep trench due to<br>
downward movement of oceanic rocks<br>
beneath continental rocks or due to<br>
collision of two oceanic plates. Some<br>
examples of these include Java trench and<br>
<b>Mariana trench which were formed when</b><br>
the Philippine plate subducted under the<br>
pacific plate. Fold mountains form when<br>
a continental plate collides with another<br>
continental plate or oceanic plate collides<br>
with a continental plate. Such mountains<br>
include: Atlas Mountains, Himalaya<br>
<b>Mountains, Andes mountain and Rockies</b><br>
mountains. Also, there is formation of an<br>
island arc due to volcanism. Examples<br>
of island arcs include Japan and West<br>
<b>Indies. Formation of volcanoes is due to</b><br>
the rise of destroyed oceanic rocks into<br>
mantle. Moreover, due to descending<br>
of oceanic crust major earthquakes<br>
occur.<br>
<b>Transform boundaries</b><br>
<b>Transform boundaries are also known as</b><br>
conservative tear or shear plate margins.<br>
<b>These occur when two lithospheric plates</b><br>
slide each other along a transform fault<br>
(Figure 2.21). During that process<br>

neither of the plate is created or destructed as a result transform faults which are<br>

boundaries are the San Andreas Fault in California and North Anatolian Fault in<br>
Turkey. These boundaries are associated with faults and earthquakes.<br>

<b>Application of plate tectonic theory</b><br>
<b>The plate tectonic theory provides a</b><br>
global framework that successfully<br>
explains many of the structural and<br>
geophysical phenomena on the earth’s<br>
surface from mountains building and<br>
earthquakes to continental drift. It<br>
explains the formation of island arcs,<br>
ocean trenches, mid oceanic ridge<br>
and rift valleys. Also, it provides the<br>
knowledge that the earth’s lithosphere<br>
is in a constant motion and changes<br>
on the plates are expected. It explains<br>
the occurrence of the oceanic floor<br>
spreading and its consequences to the<br>
lithosphere. It also helps in predicting<br>
future trends of geomorphological state<br>
especially the occurrence of earthquakes<br>
and vulcanicity. Moreover, it gives an<br>
insight on the rock cycle.<br>
<b>Critiques of the plate tectonic</b><br>
theory<br>
<b>Several critiques have been put forward</b><br>
on the plate tectonic theory. According to<br>
the theory, old rocks like pre- Cambrian<br>
rocks cannot be found near the crest of<br>
the mid oceanic ridge. The theory has<br>
not explained about such aspect. Also,<br>
the theory did not explain clearly about<br>
some faults which are common in both<br>
continents and oceans. Moreover, there<br>
is weak evidence on thermal convection<br>
in mantle, which is usually considered to<br>
be one of the driving forces of the plate<br>
motions. Furthermore, the assumption<br>
that the solid lithosphere can be pushed<br>
down into mantle to a depth of 700 km<br>
is highly doubted and is not adequately<br>
sustained.<br>

movements as explained by the<br>

theory explains the formation of<br>
various landforms in East Africa?<br>
<b>Materials of the earth’s crust</b><br>
<b>The Earth’s crust is a thin layer of rock</b><br>
that makes up the outermost solid shell<br>
of our planet. It amounts to less than<br>
half of one percent of the planet’s total<br>
mass but plays a vital role in most of<br>
<b>Earth’s natural cycles. The materials</b><br>
of the earth’s crust include elements,<br>
minerals and rocks. Some elements<br>
are also minerals by themselves for<br>
example, gold and diamond. An<br>
element is a smallest particle of matter<br>
which is combined to form Minerals.<br>
<b>It is a substance which cannot be split</b><br>
into simpler substances by chemical<br>
means. The most abundant elements<br>
on the earth’s crust include; Oxygen<br>
45.2%, Silicon 27.2%, Aluminum 8%,<br>
<b>Iron 5.8%, Calcium 5.1%, Magnesium</b><br>
2.8%, Sodium 2.3%, Potassium 1.7%,<br>
and Titanium 0.9%.<br>

09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
59<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Minerals</b><br>
are<br>
naturally<br>
occurring<br>
inorganic solid substances having a<br>
definite internal structure, chemical<br>
composition, colour and shape. They<br>
are formed due to the combination of<br>
various elements with the exception<br>
of a few which are formed by one<br>
element for example, gold, diamond<br>
and silver (Table 2.1). There are about<br>
2 000 minerals in the earth’s crust but<br>
all minerals are formed from one or<br>
more of eight main elements. Minerals<br>
differ in many ways, for example<br>
in shape, size, colour, hardness,<br>
density and brightness. All minerals,<br>
have several things in common such<br>
as definite chemical composition,<br>
naturally organic and inorganic, and<br>
are crystalline solid. Definite chemical<br>
composition of minerals: This means<br>
that the chemical analysis of any<br>
sample of a given mineral will always<br>
produce the same ratios of elements<br>
for example, quartz has two atoms<br>
of oxygen for every atom of silicon<br>
(SiO2). It means that all occurrence of<br>
minerals has a chemical composition<br>
that all occurrence of that mineral<br>
have a chemical composition that<br>
varies within a specific limited range.<br>
<b>For example, halite “rock salt” had a</b><br>
chemical composition of NaCl. It is<br>
made up of an equal number of atoms<br>
of sodium and chloride. Naturally<br>
organic<br>
and<br>
inorganic<br>
minerals:<br>
<b>Human made crystalline compound are</b><br>
not regarded as minerals nor organic<br>
substances which are compounds of<br>
carbon, oxygen and hydrogen made<br>
by living organisms. Some organisms<br>
build shells while others have parts that<br>
are considered minerals, for example,<br>
<b>Calcite. Crystalline solid: A crystalline</b><br>
substance is one whose atoms are<br>
arranged in a three-dimensions and are<br>
regularly repeating an orderly pattern.<br>
<b>A mineral must be crystalline; most</b><br>
solids are crystalline. However, there<br>
are some substances existing that are<br>
not crystalline but they have the criteria<br>
of being mineral. They are not actually<br>
minerals but mineraloids, for example,<br>
opal.<br>

<b>Complex silicates</b><br>
derived from weathered<br>
minerals i.e feldspars.<br>
<b>Characteristics and classification of</b><br>
minerals<br>
<b>Minerals are characterized and classified</b><br>
by their unique physical and chemical<br>
properties which are hardness, colour,<br>
streak, specific gravity, cleavage fracture,<br>
luster, crystalline structure, tenacity and<br>
reaction with acid. Hardness: Hardness<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   59</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
60<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
refers to mineral resistance to scratching.<br>
<b>For example, diamond is the hardest</b><br>
mineral and cannot easily be scratched or<br>
broken, while talc is the softest mineral<br>
and can easily be broken and scratched<br>
by hard mineral. Hardness of minerals<br>
has been scaled on Moh’s hardness<br>
scale, which ranges from one to ten.<br>
Whereby one for the softer mineral,<br>
ten for the hardest mineral. Colour:<br>
<b>Minerals have distinctive colours</b><br>
which can be used for identification of<br>
mineral types. Colour of the minerals<br>
depends on its chemical composition<br>
for example gold has a yellow colour,<br>
olivine (green), biotite (black). Streak:<br>
<b>This refers to the colour of the mineral</b><br>
in powder form, for example the streak<br>
of metallic mineral tends to appear dark<br>
particles which tend to absorb light,<br>
while most of the nonmetallic minerals<br>
tend to appear lighter because particles<br>
tend to reflect light. Specific gravity:<br>
<b>This refers to the ratio of weight of the</b><br>
mineral in comparison to weight of an<br>
equal amount of water. It is obtained<br>
by finding the difference between the<br>
weights of a mineral in air in comparison<br>
to the weight of mineral in water. Streak<br>
is obtained after scratching rock mineral<br>
on streak plate. Cleavage fracture:<br>
<b>This is obtained when minerals are hit</b><br>
sharply on smooth surface, by this way<br>
minerals will tend to break along the line<br>
of weakness. So different mineral break<br>
in different ways showing different type<br>
of cleavage, for example, mica splits<br>
in one direction and forms thin sheets.<br>
<b>Halite has three cleavage directions</b><br>
and it breaks into tiny cubes.  Diamond<br>
may split in four directions forming a<br>
pyramid.  Other minerals such as quartz<br>
do not split clearly but break into pieces<br>
with irregular shapes. Luster: This is<br>
the physical property of a rock mineral<br>
which indicates how much the surface<br>
of the rock mineral reflect light, hence<br>
shiny or dull. Luster is either metallic<br>
or nonmetallic. Minerals with metallic<br>
luster are generally opaque, chemically<br>
they include most of the native metals<br>
such as the sulfides, and the metallic<br>
oxides. Their refractive indices are 3 or<br>
greater. Minerals with non-metallic luster<br>
are generally transparent or translucent;<br>
chemically, they include minerals of<br>
the other classes such as quartz and<br>
feldspar. Lust of a mineral is affected<br>
by brilliance of light used to mineral<br>
surface. Crystalline structure: This refers<br>
to the shape of rock minerals. Mineral<br>
crystals occur in various shapes and<br>
sizes. Some minerals have a cubic form<br>
like halite (salt), and others are prismatic<br>
like tourmaline. Tenacity: refers to the<br>
toughness or resistance of the mineral to<br>
break or being deformed. Minerals such<br>
as halite, calcite and fluorite are brittle,<br>
as their molecules are held together<br>
by weak ionic bonds. Therefore, these<br>
minerals shatter when they are struck.<br>
<b>Metallic minerals, such as copper and</b><br>
gold, are said to be malleable, as they<br>
are easily hammered into different forms.<br>
<b>Minerals such as talc and gypsum can be</b><br>
cut into thin sheets or sections and are<br>
said to be sectile (like section).  Mica is<br>
an example of a mineral that is elastic, as<br>

it reforms its original shape after a stress<br>
is released. Reaction with acid: Minerals<br>
like carbonate react visibly with acid.<br>
<b>When a drop of dilute hydrochloric acid</b><br>
is applied on a calcite it gives bubbles<br>
releasing carbon dioxide<br>
<b>Rocks</b><br>
<b>Rocks are natural occurring solid</b><br>
cohesive aggregates of one or more<br>
mineral materials. Rocks are made<br>
up of eight chemical elements which<br>
are oxygen, silicon, iron, aluminum,<br>
calcium, potassium, sodium and<br>
magnesium (Table 2.2). The rocks vary<br>
in degree of hardness, coherence, and<br>
permeability.<br>
<b>Table 2.2:  Rocks formed by</b><br>
combination of minerals<br>
<b>Rocks</b><br>
<b>Minerals</b><br>
<b>Limestone</b><br>
Quartz, mica, feldspar,<br>

<b>Granite</b><br>
<b>Felspar, Quartz, mica</b><br>
and some iron ore<br>
<b>Basalt</b><br>
<b>Calcite, dolomite</b><br>
<b>Sandstone</b><br>

and iron ore<br>
<b>Shale</b><br>
Quartz, mica, feldspar,<br>
chlorite, calcite and<br>
dolomite.<br>
<b>The classification of rocks</b><br>
<b>Rocks have varied characteristics.  Some</b><br>
rocks are hard and compact like granite<br>
and sandstone, some are made up of<br>
loose particles for example, gravels and<br>
mud. Similarly, there are rocks made up<br>
of organic matters like limestone and<br>
coal, thus there is a need of classifying<br>
them based on their mode of formation<br>
(origin), chemical composition and age.<br>
<b>Classificatio of rocks according to</b><br>
mode of formation<br>
<b>Based on their mode of formation or</b><br>
origin, rocks can be categorized into<br>
three main types namely Igneous,<br>
sedimentary and metamorphic rocks.<br>
<b>Igneous rocks</b><br>
<b>The term igneous is derived from Latin</b><br>
word “ignis” meaning fire. It is a rock<br>
formed from cooling and solidification<br>
(crystallization) of molten rock materials<br>
(magma). This magma can be derived<br>
from partial melts of pre-existing rocks<br>
in either a planet mantle or crust. The<br>
melting of rocks is influenced with one or<br>
more of three processes that are increase<br>
in temperature, decrease in pressure<br>
as well as change in composition.<br>
<b>Igneous rocks are also called primary</b><br>
rocks because other types of rocks<br>
such as sedimentary and metamorphic<br>
owe their origin from igneous rock.<br>
<b>It forms the basis for soil formation</b><br>
(pedogenesis) and lastly, it constitutes<br>
the largest proportion of the earth’s crust<br>
as it covers almost 99% of all rocks.<br>
<b>Thus, indirectly or directly all other</b><br>
rocks were formed from igneous rocks.<br>
<b>Examples of Igneous rocks includes</b><br>
basalt, granite, gabbros, rhyolite and<br>
dolerite.<br>

09/03/2022   17:00<br>

<b>Characteristics of igneous rocks</b><br>
<b>Igneous rocks consist several</b><br>
characteristics. Some of these<br>
characteristics are as follows: The<br>
rocks are hard and water percolates<br>
with great difficulty along the joints.<br>
<b>Sometimes the rocks can be soft when</b><br>
exposed to environmental factors for<br>
longer time that can be easily dug-out<br>
by spade. Also, igneous rocks consist of<br>
granular or crystalline rocks but there<br>
are much variations in the size, form<br>
and texture of grains because these<br>
properties largely depend upon the rate<br>
and place of cooling and solidification<br>
of magma or lavas. They lack strata like<br>
sedimentary rocks. Lava can cool and<br>
solidify phase after phase to form layers<br>
but these layers are not in strata like<br>
sedimentary rocks. The rocks do not<br>
contain fossils but rather acidic or basic<br>
igneous rocks formed due to cooling<br>
and solidification of magma which did<br>
not contain fossils because there was no<br>
life. Also, the nature of magma is hot,<br>
therefore any fossils can be destroyed.<br>
<b>Igneous rocks are classified into various</b><br>
ways. The classification is based on<br>
various characteristics of the rocks<br>
such as form and texture, size, mode<br>
of formation, chemical composition.<br>
<b>Basing on the mode of formation there</b><br>
are two types of igneous rocks which are<br>
intrusive and extrusive igneous rocks.<br>
<b>Intrusive rocks which are also known</b><br>
as plutonic, occur when magma cools,<br>
solidifies and crystallizes slowly within<br>
the Earth’s crust. A common example<br>
of this type is granite. Intrusive igneous<br>
rocks are also sub-dived into plutonic<br>
and hypabyssal intrusive igneous rocks.<br>
<b>The classification is based on the depth</b><br>
of the place of cooling and solidification<br>
of magma. When magma cools and<br>
solidifies very deep within the earth,<br>
it forms plutonic but when magma is<br>
cooled just below the earth’s surface, the<br>
rocks are hypabyssal. Extrusive igneous<br>
rocks which also are known as volcanic<br>
occurs when magma reaches the earth’s<br>
surface either as lava or fragmental ejecta<br>
forming rocks such as pumice or basalt.<br>
<b>Generally, extrusive igneous rocks are</b><br>
formed mostly by fissure eruption of<br>
volcanoes resulting into flood basalts.<br>
<b>Igneous rocks can be further classified</b><br>
based on their texture and chemical<br>
composition.<br>
<b>Characteristics of igneous rocks based</b><br>
on texture<br>
<b>This refers to the physical characteristics</b><br>
of the minerals such as grain size. It<br>
relates to the cooling and solidification<br>
history of the molten magma from<br>
which it comes. The texture of igneous<br>
rock includes phaneritic, aphanitic,<br>
porphyritic, glassy, vesicular and<br>
fragmented textures. Phaneritic texture:<br>
<b>Rocks are comprised of large crystals</b><br>
of 0.5 mm to several centimeters in<br>
size that are clearly visible to the eye<br>
with or without a hand lens or binocular<br>
microscope and it has no fine matrix<br>
material present. This texture is formed<br>
by slow cooling and solidification of<br>
magma beneath the earth’s surface in the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   62</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
63<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
plutonic environment. Aphanitic texture:<br>
<b>This is comprised of small crystals which</b><br>
are generally less than 0.5 mm and they<br>
cannot be seen by the eye or hand lens.<br>
<b>This texture results from rapid cooling</b><br>
in volcanic or hypabyssal (shallow<br>
subsurface) environments. Porphyritic<br>
texture: This is comprised of at least<br>
two minerals having a visible (large)<br>
difference in grain size. The larger grains<br>
are termed phenocrysts and the finer<br>
grains either matrix or groundmass.<br>
<b>Porphyritic rocks usually have undergone</b><br>
two stages of cooling; one at depth where<br>
the larger phenocrysts formed and the<br>
second near the surface where the matrix<br>
grains crystallized. Glassy texture: This<br>
is non-crystalline whereby the rock<br>
does not contain mineral grains. Glass<br>
results from cooling that is so fast, and<br>
that minerals do not have a chance to<br>
crystallize. This happens when magma or<br>
lava comes into quick contact with much<br>
cooler materials near the earth’s surface.<br>
Pure volcanic glass is known as obsidian.<br>
<b>Vesicular texture: This term refers to</b><br>
vesicles (cracks) within the igneous rock.<br>
<b>Vesicles are the result of gas (bubbles)</b><br>
expansion which often occurs during<br>
volcanic eruptions. Pumice and scoria<br>
are common types of vesicular rocks.<br>
<b>Fragmental (pyroclastic) texture: During</b><br>
intensive volcanic eruption rocks are<br>
usually blown out in the atmosphere.<br>
<b>These rocks are comprised of numerous</b><br>
grains or fragments that have been<br>
joined together by the heat of volcanic<br>
eruption.<br>
<b>Characteristics of igneous rocks based</b><br>
on chemical composition<br>
<b>This refers to the rock’s specific</b><br>
mineralogy and chemical composition.<br>
<b>It is associated with the cooling and</b><br>
solidification history which influences<br>
the composition of that igneous<br>
rocks. Most of the igneous rocks are<br>
extremely compacted in their chemical<br>
composition. They range from Acidic<br>
(Felsic), Basic (Mafic), Ultra basic<br>
(ultra -mafic) and intermediate (Figure<br>
2.20). Acidic igneous rocks are rocks<br>
which have more than 50% of silicate<br>
and basic igneous rocks are those which<br>
have less than 50% of silicate. These type<br>
of rocks based on chemical composition<br>
are acidic, basic and ultra- basic. Acidic<br>
(felsic) igneous rocks: These are types<br>
of igneous rocks which consist of great<br>
amount of silica and feldspar. They have<br>
very little or no iron (metallic minerals),<br>
they are also low in density. For example,<br>
granite has 2.7 g/cm3, and their colour<br>
varies from white to pink. Granite is<br>
good for construction purposes and<br>
building stone and it is acidic in nature.<br>
<b>Basic (mafic) igneous rocks: These are</b><br>
also called Ferro magnesium minerals.<br>
<b>They are igneous rocks that consist of</b><br>
large amount of magnesium, iron and<br>
aluminum. They are rich in iron and<br>
magnesium because of the presence of<br>
metallic minerals for example, basalt,<br>
gabbro and dolerite. They are of high<br>
density of 3.0g/cm3. The world “Ma”<br>
comes from Magnesium and “fic” from<br>
ferric (iron) such as gabbro, basalt and<br>
they are dark in colour. Ultra basic<br>

<b>Sedimentary rocks</b><br>
The word sedimentary comes from the Latin word “sedere” which means ‘sit” or<br>
“settle” referring to the types of rocks which have been formed either from sediments<br>
or the remains of once lived organisms. Sedimentary rocks are rocks formed at<br>
or near the earth’s surface by the accumulation and lithification of sediments<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   64</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
65<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
or by the precipitation from solution<br>
at normal surface temperatures. They<br>
are formed from the remains of former<br>
rocks which have been weathered,<br>
and eroded and the remains have been<br>
transported and deposited elsewhere<br>
by wind, water or ice. The deposited<br>
sediments are consolidated, compacted<br>
and then be cemented in a number<br>
of ways to form sedimentary rocks.<br>
<b>Sediments can be detrital, chemical or</b><br>
organic sediments. Detrital sediments are<br>
mechanically eroded from pre-existing<br>
rocks. Chemical sediments on the other<br>
hand are fluid precipitates or evaporites<br>
deposited in various environments.<br>
<b>Sedimentary rocks are important with</b><br>
respect to resources like limestone<br>
deposits, coal and oil.<br>
<b>Characteristics of sedimentary rocks</b><br>
<b>Sedimentary rocks consist of fossils</b><br>
of plants and animals since they are<br>
formed by sediments derived from old<br>
rocks as well as from plants and animal<br>
remains. They contain several layers or<br>
strata but are seldom crystalline rocks.<br>
<b>Sedimentary rocks are found over the</b><br>
largest surface area of the globe. It is<br>
estimated that about 75% of the surface<br>
cover of the earth is of sedimentary<br>
rocks. The sedimentary rocks are mostly<br>
formed from deposition of sediments of<br>
various types and size which takes place<br>
in sequence and system.  Most of the<br>
sedimentary rocks are soft permeable<br>
and porous, but few are non-porous<br>
and impermeable. They can change to<br>
other types of rock by influence of heat/<br>
pressure or weathering process. They are<br>
formed by accumulation, compaction,<br>
cementation, and lithification of<br>
sediments<br>
<b>Classification of sedimentary rocks</b><br>
<b>Sedimentary rocks are classified into two</b><br>
groups based on the size of the rock or<br>
mineral grain and mode of formation.<br>
Basing on the size of mineral grain,<br>
sedimentary rocks are classified as<br>
clastic. Clastic sedimentary rocks are<br>
formed as bits of weathered rock and<br>
cemented together. Because all kinds<br>
of rocks are subjected to weathering<br>
many different minerals can make up<br>
this group of rocks. Clays and quartz<br>
are the most common.  Classification<br>
of clastic sedimentary rocks is done<br>
according to the size of the sediments<br>
that make up the rock. It consist of rock<br>
and mineral grains, or clasts, of varying<br>
size, ranging from clay-, silt-, and sand-<br>
up to pebble-, cobble-, and boulder-size<br>
materials. These clasts are transported<br>
by gravity, mudflows, running water,<br>
glaciers, and wind and eventually are<br>
deposited in various settings i.e. in desert<br>
dunes, on alluvial fans, across continental<br>
shelves, and in river deltas. Based on<br>
their mode of formation sedimentary<br>
rock is categorized into three types<br>
which are mechanically, organically and<br>
chemically formed sedimentary rocks<br>
(Table 2.3).<br>

<b>Table 2.3: Types of sedimentary rocks and their characteristics</b><br>
<b>Rock name</b><br>
<b>Description</b><br>
<b>Limestone</b><br>
<b>The rock is classified as chemical or organic sediment depending</b><br>
on how it forms. It is composed primarily of calcium carbonate.<br>
<b>The rock can form in two ways either by organically from the</b><br>
accumulation of shell, coral, algal and fecal debris or from<br>
chemically from the precipitation of calcium carbonate from<br>
lake or ocean water. Limestone is used in many ways. Some of<br>
the most common are: production of cement, crushed stone and<br>
acid neutralization.<br>
<b>Conglomerate</b><br>
<b>This is a clastic sedimentary rock that contains large (greater</b><br>
than 2 millimetre diameter) weathered debris. Environments<br>
where large amounts of sand can accumulate include beaches,<br>
deserts, flood plains and deltas.<br>
<b>Sandstone</b><br>
<b>This primarily a clastic sedimentary rock made up mainly</b><br>
of sand-size (1 to 2 millimetre diameter) weathered debris.<br>
<b>Environments where large amounts of sand can accumulate</b><br>
include beaches, deserts, flood plains and deltas.<br>
<b>Coal</b><br>
<b>This purely organic sedimentary rock that forms mainly from</b><br>
plant debris. The plant debris usually accumulates in a swamp<br>
environment. Coal is combustible and is often mined for use as<br>
a fuel.<br>
<b>Mechanically formed sedimentary</b><br>
rocks<br>
<b>Mechanically formed sedimentary</b><br>
(Clastic) rocks are formed from the<br>
mechanical break up of other rocks and<br>
are classified based on particle size.<br>
<b>Closer to the source, the grains will tend</b><br>
to be larger and more angular. It includes<br>
a variety of either coarse or fine texture<br>
rocks formed by lithification, desiccation<br>
and cementation of detrital sediments,<br>
such as sand. Mechanically formed<br>
sedimentary rocks can be classified<br>
into three sub-categories, namely;<br>
argillaceous rocks, arenaceous rocks and<br>
rudaceous rocks. These classifications<br>
are based on transporting agents. The<br>
argillaceous rocks are composed of very<br>
small and fine particles like clay, silt and<br>
mudstone. These can be formed through<br>
wind deposited - loess (mudstone),<br>
river and sea deposited - clays, gravels<br>
and alluvium, and glacier deposited -<br>
<b>Moraines, sand and boulder clay. Its</b><br>
mineral size particles range between<br>
<b>0.02 – 0.003 mm. They are characterized</b><br>
by having visible layers, contain fossils,<br>
those cemented with silicate solution<br>

contents are tough and those cemented<br>
with calcium solution are weak, jointing<br>
may develop at 900 to the bedding<br>
plane to form lines of weakness. The<br>
arenaceous rocks (arenites) include all<br>
clastic sedimentary rocks whose medium<br>
particle sizes range from 2 to 0.06 mm,<br>
or if silt is included, to 0.004 mm.<br>
<b>Some arenites are composed primarily</b><br>
of carbonate particles, in which case<br>
they are called calcarenites and grouped<br>
with the limestones. Rudaceous rocks<br>
consist chiefly large sized particles such<br>
as gravel, pebbles, cobbles, or boulders.<br>
Loose materials of this class are gravels,<br>
pebble-beds, shingle, boulder beds, scree<br>
and talus. When these are cemented, they<br>
form conglomerates and breccias.<br>
<b>Organically formed sedimentary rocks</b><br>
<b>These are the rocks formed from the</b><br>
remains of once lived organisms either<br>
animals or plants and accumulate over<br>
a long period of time. Organically<br>
sedimentary rocks can be further sub-<br>
divided into four (4) sub categories<br>
namely; Calcareous, Siliceous,<br>
Carbonaceous and Ferruginous.<br>
<b>Calcareous rocks. These are rocks</b><br>
formed from the shells and skeletons<br>
of once lived animals for example,<br>
limestone and chalk or coral reefs.<br>
<b>Siliceous rocks. These are rocks formed</b><br>
as a result of remains of organisms like<br>
diatoms and radiolarians whose skeletons<br>
are rich in silica, for example, diatomite<br>
rocks. Carbonaceous rocks. These<br>
are rocks formed from plant remains<br>
buried thousands of years ago, under<br>
heat and overlying pressure resulting<br>
to plants changing into rocks, for<br>
example, coal like lignite, bituminous,<br>
anthracite, and brown coal. Ferruginous<br>
rocks. These are rocks formed from<br>
precipitation of hydrated iron oxide by<br>
some small bacterial organism produced<br>
by decomposing vegetable matter<br>
composed of high concentration of iron<br>
for example, ironstone and ferric rocks.<br>
<b>Chemically formed sedimentary rocks</b><br>
<b>These are kind of rocks that are formed</b><br>
when rocks undergo chemical changes,<br>
whereby the original minerals are<br>
substituted by another type to form<br>
sedimentary rocks. Most of these are<br>
formed from either precipitated or<br>
evaporated salt solution which have<br>
been formed by the dissolving original<br>
minerals. Not all sedimentary rocks are<br>
formed from rock pieces. Chemically<br>
formed igneous rock includes gypsum.<br>
<b>Gypsum and rock salt: These rock</b><br>
form when a body of water which<br>
contains a large amount of dissolved<br>
minerals evaporates, then the dissolved<br>
mineral matter remains as deposit. For<br>
example, if water supply to a lake is<br>
cut-off, therefore, the water in the lake<br>
will eventually disappear and the lake<br>
become Salt Lake. If a body of water<br>
in the lake contains calcium sulphate<br>
dissolved minerals, when dries, usually<br>
form gypsum as one of sedimentary<br>
rock. On the other hand, rock salt will<br>
form if it contains chloride as dissolved<br>
minerals. Carbonation: Occurs when<br>
weak carbonic acid is formed through<br>
dissolving rain water as it falls in from<br>

the atmosphere when it reaches the rocks<br>
rich in calcium carbonate like limestone<br>
that tends to be transformed to calcium<br>
bicarbonate which is easily removed<br>
in solution. Limestone landscape, is<br>
one of the most noticeable feature. It<br>
has very low or almost no vegetation.<br>
<b>The reasons for poor vegetation or</b><br>
absence of vegetation is that the<br>
permeability of limestone permits rain<br>
to soak into it very easily. The rivers<br>
from non-limestone region sometimes<br>
flow to limestone region, and when<br>
this happens, the river, disappears into<br>
vertical holes in the surface and continue<br>
to flow as underground rivers inside the<br>
limestone.<br>
<b>Metamorphic rocks</b><br>
<b>These are rocks formed either from</b><br>
sedimentary or igneous rock or<br>
igneous after being subjected to high<br>
pressure, high temperature below zone<br>
of diagenesis or both. Any rock can<br>
be altered by great heat and pressure<br>
including earlier formed metamorphic<br>
rocks in the process of metamorphism.<br>
<b>In fact, a metamorphic rock means a</b><br>
complete alteration in the appearance<br>
of pre-existing rocks due to change in<br>
mineral composition and texture through<br>
temperature and pressure.<br>
<b>Agents of metamorphism</b><br>
Metamorphism consists of three agents.<br>
These are heat, compression and solution.<br>
<b>Heat is the most important factor for</b><br>
the development of metamorphic rocks<br>
from the pre-existing parent rock. The<br>
heat changes the mineral composition<br>
of the pre-existing rocks. Compression<br>
is another agent. Compression from<br>
convergent horizontal movements<br>
from the endogenic forces put strain<br>
and change the form and composition<br>
of the parent rocks. In this agent when<br>
active chemically hot gases and water<br>
passing through the rocks changes the<br>
chemical composition. For example,<br>
magmatic water and water contained<br>
in the bed of sedimentary rock helps<br>
in introducing chemical changes in the<br>
rocks. Metamorphic rocks are classified<br>
based on their degree of metamorphism<br>
and texture. Metamorphism refers to<br>
the change of minerals or geological<br>
texture of the existing rock to new form<br>
without melting to liquid form (magma).<br>
<b>The change occurs due to pressure, heat</b><br>
or both. Therefore, there are four types<br>
of metamorphism, namely; dynamic<br>
(pressure force), thermal (heat force)<br>
thermos – dynamic (combination of heat<br>
and pressure) and metasomatic.<br>
(a)	 Dynamic metamorphism<br>
This is the type of metamorphism<br>
which is influenced by pressure<br>
caused by compressional forces.<br>
<b>The movement subjects the rocks</b><br>
to great pressure resulting to wide<br>
scale changes of rocks, which<br>
is also referred to as regional<br>
metamorphism. This occurs to rocks<br>
placed under tremendous stress,<br>
for example at a collision plate<br>
boundary like at Alps Mountains,<br>
<b>Himalayan Mountains, and Atlas</b><br>
Mountains.<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

69<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
(b)	 Thermal metamorphism<br>
This involves rocks which are deeply<br>
buried and those which come into<br>
contact with magma. Hence, it is<br>
the type of rock formed only by the<br>
influence of heat. Metamorphism<br>
occurs at temperature of 1000C and<br>
8000C. At these temperature levels<br>
rocks are still solid but softened.<br>
<b>In the softened state minerals</b><br>
may rearrange themselves and<br>
crystals may change their shape<br>
or size. The rocks can change<br>
their appearance and character for<br>
example, sandstone – quartzite,<br>
limestone – marble. Two processes<br>
under this are involved, that is<br>
firstly; by buried metamorphism,<br>
which occurs to deeply buried rocks<br>
where temperature reaches 3000C<br>
to cause metamorphism. Secondly,<br>
by contact metamorphism which<br>
occurs to rocks which are adjacent<br>
to bodies of hot magma intruded<br>
into the cold crust, this is called<br>
aureole metamorphism.<br>
(c)	 Thermo-dynamic metamorphism<br>
Thermo-dynamic metamorphism is<br>
the process which takes place as a<br>
combination of heat and pressure.<br>
<b>It is caused by earth movement</b><br>
on wide scale thus, causing high<br>
pressure and high temperature.<br>
(d)	 Metasomatic metamorphism<br>
This is a process of metamorphism<br>
coupled with the introduction of<br>
ions from an eternal source. The<br>
ions are brought in by water from<br>
outside the immediate environment<br>
and are incorporated into the<br>
newly crystalline minerals. At<br>
the same time hot water may<br>
dissolve minerals that were part<br>
of the rock and carry them away.<br>
<b>For example, during contact</b><br>
metamorphism, metasomatic<br>
may cause schist minerals to<br>
crystallize ions (K+ Na+ Si+4) that<br>
are carried away by the water<br>
and participate in metamorphic<br>
reactions. Metamorphic textures:<br>
<b>This describes the shape and</b><br>
the orientation of grains found<br>
in metamorphic rocks. The<br>
main metamorphic textures are<br>
either foliated, non- foliated or<br>
lineate. Foliated rocks: This is a<br>
metamorphic rock whose minerals<br>
are arranged in parallel layers.<br>
<b>This results when minerals are</b><br>
crystallized or are flattened under<br>
pressure. Also, they occur when<br>
minerals of different densities<br>
separate into layers behaving like<br>
a mixture of oil and water which<br>
result in alternating light and dark<br>
bands. Many foliated rocks break<br>
into thin sheets, for example, slate<br>
and schist. Non-foliated rock. This is<br>
a metamorphic rock without layers<br>
and sometimes they may contain<br>
long crystals. These rocks do not<br>
break up into sheets, for example,<br>
marble and quartzite. Moreover,<br>
metamorphic rocks are very hard<br>
and resistant than the former rocks<br>
mainly igneous and sedimentary<br>
and they are formed under high<br>
pressure and temperature.<br>
