<b>Characteristics of metamorphic rocks</b><br>
<b>Metamorphic rocks are very hard and</b><br>
more resistant to erosion and weathering.<br>
<b>They do not contain fossil and they are</b><br>
formed by metamorphism process. They<br>
can undergo weathering to form sediments<br>
that in turn form sedimentary rock.<br>
Most of them have wavy like structure.<br>
<b>Classification of rocks according to</b><br>
age<br>
<b>Rocks are classified basing on the age</b><br>
which is influenced by time. As time<br>
goes on rocks develop and get matured<br>
or change from one type to another.<br>
<b>This is caused by various factors. The</b><br>
interrelationship between rocks gives<br>
out a physical cycle of the rocks.<br>
<b>The rock cycle</b><br>
<b>Rock cycle is a concept in geology that</b><br>
explains the transition and transformation<br>
among the three main types of rocks<br>
which are igneous, sedimentary, and<br>
metamorphic rocks. Rocks can change<br>
from one type of rock to another. It also<br>
describes the processes and forces behind<br>
that transition. Rocks are constantly<br>
being exposed to various physical and<br>
chemical conditions which change<br>
overtime. The rock cycle is driven by two<br>
forces which are Earth’s internal heat<br>
which causes material to move around in<br>
the core and mantle, and the hydrological<br>
cycle which is movement of water, ice,<br>
and air at the surface. Generally, a rock<br>
cycle can be seen as an endless revisable<br>
process by which rocks change from one<br>
type to another.<br>
<b>Stages of rock cycle formation</b><br>
<b>The first stage of rock cycle is determined</b><br>
by the presence of magma. When magma<br>
is are forced outside via vents, fissures,<br>
and cracks, may cool and solidify to form<br>
igneous rock. The second stage is the<br>
formation of igneous rocks. This occurs<br>
when molten material called magma or<br>
lava cools and solidifies beneath or on<br>
the earth’s surface forming intrusive and<br>
extrusive igneous rocks under the process<br>
known as crystallization. The third<br>
stage is the formation of sedimentary<br>
rock, this occurs when igneous rocks<br>
become exposed to external agents of<br>
weathering and erosion. The weathered<br>
material is transported and deposited<br>
in oceans, lakes basins and lowlands.<br>
<b>The sediments are being compacted by</b><br>
pressure of overlying layers of sediments<br>
to form sedimentary rocks. The process<br>
of sediments to be compacted by the<br>
pressure of overlying layers of sediments<br>
is called lithification or sedimentation.<br>
<b>The fourth stage is the formation of</b><br>
metamorphic rocks. This occurs when<br>
sedimentary rocks which are deeply<br>
buried and rocks at collision zones of<br>
plates are subjected to pressure and heat<br>
turning them into metamorphic rocks<br>
through the process of metamorphism.<br>
<b>The fifth stage is the completion stage of</b><br>
the circle. The cycle loop is completed<br>
by this stage in which the metamorphic<br>
rocks are subject to greater heat and<br>
pressure and become molten and then<br>
be converted into magma. However,<br>
there is no inevitability about the order<br>
of this cycle, for example an igneous<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   70</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>

71<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
rock may change, without becoming<br>
a sedimentary rock, be transformed<br>
into a metamorphic rock by heat and<br>
pressure. Equally, sedimentary and<br>
metamorphic rocks may be converted<br>
to material for new sedimentary rock.<br>
<b>Recently the rock cycle as described</b><br>
above has been related to the theory of<br>
plate tectonics. Where the cycle starts<br>
with the erosion of a continental land,<br>
and the materials from that continent<br>
accumulates on the continental margin<br>
and become compacted by lithification<br>
into sedimentary rock.<br>
<b>If the continental margin is a convergent</b><br>
plate zone, the sedimentary rocks may be<br>
transformed by high pressure to belts of<br>
metamorphic rock. Progressively, those<br>
sediments that were not crumpled up<br>
into mountains may be carried down by<br>
subduction deeper into the crust. They<br>
undergo still greater metamorphism and<br>
eventually temperatures and pressures<br>
may be so great that melting takes place<br>
to generate magma. The magma later on<br>
is converted into igneous rocks which<br>
may eventually appear at the earth’s<br>
surface, either as extrusive igneous rocks<br>
from volcanoes or by the exposure, by<br>
erosion, of intrusive igneous rocks. The<br>
igneous rocks are attacked by weathering<br>
and erosion, then transported to the<br>
continental margin and so the cycle starts<br>
again. Generally speaking, rock cycle has<br>
neither beginning nor end, but however<br>
since the earth’s crust was once made up<br>
of molten material (magma) thus we can<br>
conclude that the first rock to form on<br>
rock cycle is igneous rock (Figure 2.23).<br>

<b>Importance of rock cycle</b><br>
<b>Rock cycle is a good model of</b><br>
explaining the essence source and origin<br>
rocks. It support diagramatically the<br>
transformation of one type of rock to<br>
another. It helps in explain soil formation<br>
(pedology).<br>
<b>Determining age of rocks</b><br>
<b>It is believed that the lowest rocks are the</b><br>
oldest rocks and above ones are younger.<br>
<b>There are two methods of determining</b><br>
age of rocks. One way is to look at any<br>
fossils the rock may contain. If any of the<br>
fossils are unique to one of the geologic<br>
time periods, then the rock was formed<br>
during that particular time period.<br>
<b>Another way is to use the ‘What’s on</b><br>
top?’ rule. When you find layers of rocks<br>
in a cliff or hillside, younger rocks are on<br>
top of older rocks. The age of a rock in<br>
years is called absolute age. Geologists<br>
find absolute ages by measuring the<br>
amount of certain radioactive elements<br>
in the rock. Scientists express the ages<br>

09/03/2022   17:00<br>

72<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
of rocks in the same two ways which are<br>
relative age and absolute age.<br>
(a)	 Relative ages of rocks<br>
Relative ages of rocks are established by<br>
comparing the ages of rocks with each<br>
other.  The determination of the relative<br>
age of a rock is based on the law of<br>
original horizontality of the sediments,<br>
law of superposition, law of original<br>
lateral continuity, law of cross-cutting<br>
relationships, law of inclusions, law of<br>
fossil succession and the lithology of a<br>
rock. The law of original horizontality,<br>
is based on the assumption that most<br>
of the sedimentary rocks are usually<br>
deposited under the action of gravity in<br>
approximately horizontal layers which lie<br>
parallel to the surface to which they are<br>
deposited. The law of superimposition:<br>
<b>It is also, known as the stratigraphy</b><br>
method.  The law holds that the older<br>
layers of the sediments are arranged in<br>
sequence whereas the younger layers are<br>
overlying older layer as influenced with<br>
the force of earth’s gravity. In that case,<br>
the oldest layer will be at the bottom<br>
while the younger layer will be on top.<br>
<b>Generally, the vertical arrangement of the</b><br>
layers downward determines the age of<br>
the rock especially when one knows the<br>
rate at which deposition has been taking<br>
place in forming each layer. The law of<br>
original lateral continuity: is based on<br>
the fact that the layers of sediments will<br>
extend laterally continuous over some<br>
distance until they are interrupted by<br>
the obstacles or become thinner. Law<br>
of cross - cutting relationships: Under<br>
this law the younger features cut across<br>
the older features. Usually faults, dykes,<br>
and erosional angular unconformity,<br>
are considered to be younger than the<br>
materials that is faulted, intruded, or<br>
eroded. For example, the mudstone,<br>
sandstone and shale are cut by the<br>
basalt dyke, it is obvious that mudstone,<br>
sandstone, and shale had to be present<br>
before the intrusion of the basalt dyke.<br>
<b>That makes the dyke to be younger than</b><br>
the mudstone, sandstone, and shale<br>
(Figure 2.24).<br>

<b>The principle of inclusion: occurs</b><br>
when<br>
the<br>
fragmented<br>
rock<br>
is<br>
surrounded with another rock. For<br>
example, if the surrounding rock<br>
is igneous rock then the inclusion<br>
is called xenoliths. Generally, the<br>
inclusion represents the fragments of<br>
the older rock. The principle of fossil<br>
succession: occurs where evolution<br>
has produced a succession of unique<br>
fossils that correlate to the units of<br>
the geologic time scale. By fossil<br>
succession, assemblages of fossils<br>
contained in strata are unique to the<br>
time they lived, and can be used<br>
to correlate rocks of the same age<br>
across a wide geographic distribution.<br>
<b>The principle of lithology of a rock:</b><br>
this is the basis of subdividing rock<br>
sequences into individual units for the<br>
purposes of mapping and correlation<br>
between areas. It is a description of<br>
its physical characteristics visible at<br>
outcrop, in hand or core samples, or<br>
with low magnification microscopy.<br>
<b>In relative age of rocks Relative dating</b><br>
method is used. This method refers<br>
to determining the  age of the rock in<br>
comparison with another. It is mostly<br>
used on classification of sedimentary<br>
rocks. There are three ways to be<br>
used on classification which include<br>
principles of superimposition, mineral<br>
relativity and fossil relativity. The<br>
principle of superimposition: is based<br>
on the law of superposition which<br>
states that “the rock layers are arranged<br>
in a sequence such that young layers<br>
are overlying the older rock layers”.<br>
<b>Therefore, as one goes down the rock</b><br>
layers become older and older.  In other<br>
words, vertical arrangement of layers<br>
downward determines the rock ages.<br>
<b>Basing on such arrangement, it is easy</b><br>
to estimate the relative age of rocks,<br>
especially when one knows the rate<br>
at which deposition has been taking<br>
place in forming each layer. This helps<br>
to know the unconformity which is the<br>
contact between two rock units in which<br>
the upper unit is usually much younger<br>
than the lower unit. Unconformities are<br>
typically buried erosional surfaces that<br>
can represent a break in the geologic<br>
record of hundreds of millions of years<br>
or more. There are three basic types of<br>
unconformities that helps determine<br>
relative ages of rock layers, these are<br>
non- conformity, disconformity and<br>
angular unconformity.<br>
<b>Non-Conformity: This occurs when</b><br>
the sediments are deposited on top of<br>
an eroded volcanic or metamorphic<br>
rock (Figure 2.25). This is the gap that<br>
occurs where the sedimentary rock<br>
over lies the eroded surface of the<br>
rock. Generally, it represents a buried<br>
erosional surface which can represent<br>
a break in the geological records of<br>
hundreds of millions of years.<br>

Disconformity: A disconformity occurs where one layer or several layers<br>
of sedimentary rocks have been removed due to erosion by water moving<br>
underground or as surface run-off. Disconformities are much harder to recognize<br>
in the field because often there is no angular relationship between sets of layers<br>
(Figure 2.26). Disconformities are usually recognized by correlating from one<br>
area to another and finding that some strata is missing in one of the areas.<br>
<b>Layers of</b><br>
sedimentary<br>
rocks being<br>
removed<br>
Figure 2.26: Disconformity.<br>
Angular unconformity: An angular unconformity occurs where horizontally<br>
parallel strata of sedimentary rock are deposited on tilted and eroded layers<br>
producing an angular discordance with the overlying horizontal layers (Figure<br>
2.27). It occurs when the folded rock strata (layers) have been eroded and then<br>
covered by younger horizontal layers of sedimentary rocks.<br>

<b>The mineral relativity: This is based</b><br>
on relativity of mineral rocks. By this<br>
principle any rock is new or old than<br>
the fragments of which is composed.<br>
<b>Fossils index: This method uses fossils</b><br>
found in rocks which lived on definite<br>
order of period. These are remnants or<br>
remains of once lived organisms which<br>
were buried and intermingled with old<br>
rocks. Some fossils found in sedimentary<br>
rocks imply that such living organisms<br>
lived in a definite order of period. For<br>
example, graptolites are normally found<br>
in dark mudstones and shales. They lived<br>
350 to 450 million years ago. Thus rocks<br>
containing these fossils formed on that<br>
period. Trilobites are the earliest marine<br>
organisms that flourished about 500 to<br>
600 million years ago. Thus by relativity<br>
rocks containing graptolites are younger<br>
than those containing Trilobites.<br>
(b) Absolute age of rocks<br>
<b>Absolute dating is the process of</b><br>
determining an age of a rock on a<br>
specified time scale in archaeology<br>
and geology. Some scientists prefer the<br>
terms chronometric or calendar dating,<br>
as use of the word “absolute” implies<br>
an unwarranted certainty of accuracy.<br>
<b>Absolute dating provides a numerical age</b><br>
or range in contrast with relative dating<br>
which places events in order without<br>
any measure of the age between events.<br>
<b>They are methods which determine the</b><br>
actual age of rocks by using scientific<br>
methods. It involves determining the<br>
age of a rock by using the breakdown<br>
of certain elements of nuclei atoms<br>
such as uranium atoms and carbon<br>
14 atoms.  In this method two ways<br>
namely, radioactive dating (radioactive<br>
breakdown of uranium) and radioactive<br>
substance (radio carbon dating or carbon<br>
14) are described.<br>
<b>Radioactive dating: This is also referred</b><br>
to as radioactive breakdown of uranium.<br>
<b>Scientists can measure ages of some</b><br>
rocks in more exact terms. They do this<br>
with the help of radioactive substances<br>
found in some of the rocks.  A radioactive<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   75</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
76<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
substance is one that changes over a<br>
period of time.  All matter is made of<br>
tiny units called atoms. A radioactive<br>
substance has atoms that break apart,<br>
a process called radioactive decay. For<br>
example, uranium is an unstable element<br>
which breaks down in a known rate of<br>
time. Uranium elements break down<br>
gradually into lead which becomes a<br>
stable isotope. The half-life of uranium<br>
decay is 4.5 billion years (Table 2.4).<br>
<b>Suppose a rock had 100 g of uranium</b><br>
half of it, that is 50 g would decay at 4.5<br>
billion years to form lead. At this time<br>
the amount of lead doubles and uranium<br>
decreases by half. Due to this very<br>
long Half-life, Radioactive Uranium is<br>
used to date very old rocks (Table 2.5).<br>
<b>Scientists, carefully, measure the amount</b><br>
of uranium and lead in the rocks.<br>

<b>Usefulness of radioactive dating</b><br>
method<br>
<b>The radioactive dating of meteorites</b><br>
(rock from the space) has helped to<br>
determine the age of the earth because<br>
these meteorites are made of the same<br>
material from which the earth was first<br>
formed about 4.5 billion years.<br>
<b>Radio substance dating: Radiocarbon</b><br>
dating (also referred to as carbon<br>
dating or carbon-14 dating) is a<br>
method for determining the age of an<br>
object containing organic material by<br>
using the properties of radiocarbon<br>
(14C), a radioactive isotope of carbon.<br>
<b>Scientists may be interested in the age</b><br>
of a fossil shell or a piece of wood.<br>
<b>These once-living materials can be</b><br>
dated by measuring radioactive carbon.<br>
<b>All living things contain a radioactive</b><br>
form of carbon. As long as plant or<br>
animal is alive, the amount of Carbon<br>
14 in its body remains the same, but<br>
when the plant or animal dies, the<br>
amount of Carbon-14 starts to decrease<br>
at a known rate. This method is used<br>
to determine the age of younger rocks<br>
of between 60 000 to 70 000 years. For<br>
example, Carbon dioxide has a half-life<br>
of 5 730 years. By noting the amount<br>
of carbon 14 which is still present you<br>
can determine its age in a fossil. The<br>
fossil’s age is found by measuring the<br>
amount of Carbon -14 in the fossil<br>
and comparing to the amount found in<br>
modern living things. For example, the<br>
amount of carbon – 14 in an ancient<br>
piece of wood found to be one half of<br>
the amount of carbn-14 found in wood<br>
from a tree still living, would mean that<br>
one-half life of carbon-14 had passed<br>
since the old wood stopped taking in<br>
the radioactive carbon.<br>
<b>Usefulness of carbon-14 dating</b><br>
method<br>
<b>Carbon-14 is useful in dating fossils</b><br>
in young rocks that are between 1000-<br>
70000 years old. Carbon-14 dating<br>
has provided a record of fairly recent<br>
events in the earth’s history. The<br>
amount of carbon-14 in fossil wood<br>
found in Europe and North America<br>
enables scientists to estimate that these<br>
areas were covered by ice as recently<br>
as 11000 years ago.<br>
<b>Geological time scale (GTS)</b><br>
<b>The geological time scale divides</b><br>
the history of the earth in time unit<br>
and lists these units in the order they<br>
occurred. It is a scheme or chart that<br>
indicates age classification of rocks<br>
and the associated geomorphological<br>
and biological events. GTS is divided<br>
into Eons, Eras, Periods, Epochs and<br>
ages (Table 2.6). Eons consists all that<br>
span of time from which fossil remains<br>
were then known.<br>

<b>Geological eras: This is the smallest time</b><br>
interval that occurs when the eons are<br>
divided. Basically, there are three eras<br>
namely Cenozoic (new life) Mesozoic<br>
(middle life), and Paleozoic (ancient life).<br>
<b>Cenozoic era: Cenozoic means ‘recent</b><br>
life’ popularly known as the age of<br>
mammals. This is the third major era<br>
of the earth’s history which began about<br>
70 million years ago and has not yet<br>
ended.  Many of the today’s mountains<br>
and plateaus were lifted up during this<br>
era. At this era ice affected much of the<br>
land. This era is referred to as the age of<br>
mammals, as mammals were dominant in<br>
life forms. Many mammals resembling<br>
modern form had appeared by the middle<br>
of Cenozoic era, animals like dogs, cats<br>
and horses appeared (Figure 2.28). Most<br>
of human life fossils have been found in<br>
this era. Recently the Cenozoic era is<br>
internationally accepted as the youngest<br>
of the three subdivisions of the fossil<br>
ferrous part of earth’s history. Cenozoic<br>
era is divided into three periods which are<br>
<b>Paleogene (66 million to 23 million years</b><br>
ago, Neogene 23 million years to 2.6<br>
million years ago) and Quaternary (2.6<br>
million years to present). Traditionally<br>
the era is divided into Tertiary and<br>
<b>Quaternary period. Furthermore, periods</b><br>
are divided into epoch, for example, we<br>
are living in Cenozoic Era, Quatenary<br>
period in the Holocene Epoch.<br>
<b>Figure 2.28: Cenozoic era</b><br>
Mesozoic era: Mesozoic means ‘middle life’ also known as the age of reptiles.<br>
These was due to wide spread of reptiles. This is the second era   in earth’s history<br>
which began about 230 million years ago and ended about 70 million years ago.<br>
This era lasted for about 160 million years. At this era continent began to move<br>
to their present configuration and shallow seas began to spread across them. Also,<br>
at this era great changes occurred on plants and animals. The climate of the time<br>
favored the reptiles to flourish and spread. A good example is the giant dinosaurs<br>
meaning ‘terrible lizard’ that were giant (Figure 2.29).  Flowering plants, the<br>
family of maple and oak appeared. Birds and mammals eventually replaced reptiles<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   80</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
81<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
that could not adopt the changed condition. At the end of the period dinosaurs<br>
disappeared. Mesozoic era is divided into Triassic, Jurassic and Cretaceous periods.<br>
<b>Figure 2.29:  Giant reptiles during Mesozoic era</b><br>
The Paleozoic era: Paleozoic means ‘ancient life’. This era began about 600<br>
million year ago and ended about 230 million years ago.  In this era continents<br>
collided, climate changed and different types of life forms appeared. This age is<br>
called the age of invertebrates. Its beginning is marked by sudden appearance<br>
of abundant fossils. The early Paleozoic was a quiet time, continents were close<br>
together many types of Invertebrates involved and multiplied. At the late Paleozoic<br>
era the first forest appeared (Figure 2.30) and (Table 2.6). The bulk of today’s coal<br>
formed at this time, insects, amphibian and reptiles appeared, up to its end life<br>
was established on land.<br>
<b>Figure 2.30: Paleozoic era</b><br>

<b>Pre Cambrian era: This era began with</b><br>
the formation of the Earth and ended<br>
about 600 million years ago. During that<br>
era very few fossils were found in those<br>
rocks and very few of those rocks still<br>
exist as they were worn away or changed<br>
by crustal movement.<br>
<b>How geological time scale (GTS) was</b><br>
named<br>
<b>Some of the names of rocks were given</b><br>
on the basis of their localities where<br>
they were either formed or studied<br>
for example, Cambrian rocks (Wales)<br>
<b>Jurassic (Jura Mountains) Nyanza and</b><br>
<b>Kavirondian (Kenya).  Other names of</b><br>
rocks were given with reference to rock<br>
family for example, cretaceous-chalk<br>
rock.<br>
<b>Importance of the geological time scale</b><br>
<b>The geological time scale depicts ages of</b><br>
rocks by showing the time when and how<br>
certain types of rocks were formed, for<br>
example, some were formed by glacial<br>
deposition and volcanic eruption. Also,<br>
it helps in understanding when and<br>
how different landforms were formed,<br>
for example, mountains of different<br>
types like fold or volcanic mountains.<br>
<b>Moreover, it helps to learn about the</b><br>
different mountain building (orogenic)<br>
like Laurentian Caledonian, Hercynian<br>
and Alpine, Himalaya orogenic, for<br>
example, Alps-formed during Miocene<br>
26 million years ago. The Himalayans<br>
are said to be formed during Oligocene<br>
about 38 million years ago. Furthermore,<br>
it helps to predict the occurrence of<br>
crustal deformation, for example, areas<br>
of old rocks, can be subjected to faulting<br>
anytime in case of disturbance or stress.<br>
<b>Records of the life of plants and animals</b><br>
help in understanding the relationship<br>
existing between living things and the<br>
geological processes. For example,<br>
plants, animals and man emerged when<br>
the conditions became conducive. Plants<br>
emerged when soils had developed and<br>
animals came into existence when plants<br>
already existed and later man emerged.<br>
<b>It helps to show the life record of plants</b><br>
and animals, and depict climatic changes<br>
which had occurred on earth’s surface.<br>
<b>Weaknesses of the methods used to</b><br>
determine the age of rocks<br>
(a)	 Some of the methods used in<br>
determining the age of rocks like<br>
relative methods are largely based<br>
on estimation (extrapolation).<br>
(b)	 Instruments<br>
like<br>
Carbon-14,<br>
cannot be used to determine the<br>
age of rock over 75 000 years ago.<br>

<b>Earthquakes</b><br>
<b>An earthquake is a sudden shake or</b><br>
tremor shaking of the surface of the<br>
<b>Earth, resulting from the sudden release</b><br>
of energy in the earth’s crust stored in<br>
the rocks beneath that creates seismic<br>
waves. Earthquakes can be violent<br>
enough to cause loss of people’s life<br>
and destruction of properties. The<br>
seismicity or seismic activity of an area<br>
refers to the frequency, type and size of<br>
earthquakes experienced over a period<br>
of time.<br>
<b>Classification of earthquakes</b><br>
<b>Earthquake can be classified in various</b><br>
ways including: Classification based<br>
on the causative factors can be either<br>
natural or human induced, based on area<br>
of focus, and rate of casualties. Natural<br>
earthquakes are caused by natural<br>
processes such as vulcanic earthquakes,<br>
caused by vulcanic activity. This involves<br>
both the plutonic earthquakes that occur<br>
deep in the interior of the earth’s crust<br>
and the volcanic earthquakes which<br>
occur near the surface of the earth with<br>
eruptions of both explosive and fissure<br>
types. These types of earthquakes occur<br>
mostly in volcanic areas. For example,<br>
the Mt. Etna volcanic eruption in 1968.<br>
<b>Another type is tectonic earthquakes</b><br>
caused by displacement of tectonic plates<br>
during faulting activity. For example,<br>
in 2001 there was an earthquake of this<br>
kind in Gujarat India. Also, isostatic<br>

earthquake is another natural tremor<br>
which is triggered by sudden disturbance<br>
in isotactic balance at regional scale due<br>
to imbalance in the geological processes.<br>
<b>Artificial or man-made earthquakes</b><br>
are human induced earthquakes which<br>
are caused by human activities such<br>
as mineral and oil extraction using<br>
heavy explosives materials, dam<br>
construction activities using rock<br>
breaking tools like dynamites and water<br>
pumping.<br>
<b>Classification basing on the areas of</b><br>
focus earthquakes are grouped into three<br>
categories which include moderate<br>
earthquakes which refers to the ones<br>
whose focus is set or occurs from about<br>
0-50km deep in the earth’s crust. Also,<br>
the intermediate earthquakes whose<br>
seismic focus is located at about 50-<br>
250 km deep in the earth’s crust. Lastly<br>
is the deep earthquakes, including<br>
those whose seismic focus occurs deep<br>
in the earth’s crust between 250-700<br>
km. From earth’s surface. Moreover,<br>
the classification of earthquake is<br>
done basing on the rate or magnitude<br>
of human casualties. Through this<br>
classification<br>
there<br>
is<br>
moderate<br>
hazardous earthquakes which occur<br>
and cause human death of below 50<br>
000 people. For example, in Kamakura<br>
earthquake in Japan in 1293 recoded<br>
the death of atleast 23 024 people.<br>
<b>Highly hazardous earthquakes occur</b><br>
and cause human death ranging from<br>
51 000 to 100 000 people. For example,<br>
<b>Chimbote, Peru in 1970 at least 67,000</b><br>
people died. The last type is most<br>
hazardous earthquakes causing human<br>
casualties above 100 000 people. For<br>
example, in 1967 Tang-Shan, China<br>
there occurred an earthquake whereby<br>
atleast 7 500 000 people died.<br>
<b>Nature of earthquakes</b><br>
<b>The point at which an earthquake</b><br>
originates is called Focus. Focus refers<br>
to the exact location within the Earth<br>
where an earthquake occurs. It is<br>
point within the earth’s crust where an<br>
earthquake own its origin. It is divided<br>
into three categories namely shallow,<br>
intermediate and deep focus.  Shallow<br>
focus ranges between the earth’s surface<br>
and 70 km depth, while intermediate<br>
and deep ranges between 70 - 300 km<br>
and 300-700 km respectively. Most<br>
of the earthquakes occur at shallow<br>
depth. The point on the earth’s surface<br>
immediately above the focus is called<br>
the epicenter. The epicenter is where<br>
the shock waves first hit the surface, it<br>
is these shock waves which give rise<br>
to an earthquake (Figure 2.31). The<br>
energy generated by an earthquake is<br>
called seismic waves.<br>

<b>Seismic waves</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.31: The occurrence of earthquakes</b><br>
Seismic waves are the waves travelling through the earth’s crust. Also, these waves<br>
are known as shock waves. The seismic waves are divided into two categories<br>
namely, Body waves and Surface waves.<br>
<b>Body waves</b><br>
Body waves are seismic waves that travel in rocks within the earth’s crust. They<br>
occur very far deeper into the interior part of the earth’s crust where the earthquake<br>
originates. Body waves are further divided into two types namely Primary (P)<br>
waves and Secondary (S) waves.<br>
<b>Primary waves</b><br>
Primary waves are also known as P-waves which are type of body wave, that<br>
travel through a continuum and are the first waves from an earthquake to arrive at<br>
a seismograph. Also, it is called compressional wave because the wave compresses<br>
and then stretches the particles of material in the same direction that the wave is<br>
moving (back and forth) (Figure 2.32). They are very fast and can travel through<br>
both liquid and solid materials.<br>
<b>Direction of wave travel</b><br>
<b>Longitudinal or P - wave</b><br>
Figure 2.32: The direction of the P- waves.<br>

<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
86<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Secondary waves</b><br>
<b>Secondary waves are also known as shear</b><br>
waves or S-waves. In this type of waves,<br>
the particle motion is perpendicular to the<br>
direction in which the wave is moving.<br>
<b>The motion is similar to a guitar string</b><br>
which is set in motion by being plucked<br>
in a direction that is perpendicular to<br>
the string (Figure 2.33). The vibrating<br>
molecules perpendicular to the direction<br>
of the wave produces an upward and<br>
downward movement that creates<br>
alternating high points (crests) and the<br>
low points (troughs). The direction of<br>
transverse wave motion moves sideways<br>
to one another as the wave passes. This<br>
motion is called shear movement. They<br>
cannot travel in the liquid materials.<br>
<b>Figure 2.33:  Movements of S- waves</b><br>
<b>Surface waves</b><br>
These are waves travelling through the surface. The surface waves are divided<br>
into love (L) and Rayleigh (R) waves. Love waves cause the surface rocks to<br>
move from side to side at a right angle to the direction of wave movement while<br>
Rayleigh waves cause the surface rocks to have a vertical circular movement<br>
very similar to that of water in a sea wave (Figure 2.34).<br>
(a)  Love waves<br>
(b)  Rayleigh waves<br>
<b>Figure 2.34: Surface waves</b><br>

<b>The causes of earthquakes</b><br>
<b>Earthquakes are caused primarily by</b><br>
lack of equilibrium in any part of the<br>
crust of the earth. There are various<br>
causes of earthquakes, these include<br>
plate movement, volcanic eruptions,<br>
faulting and folding, up-warping and<br>
down-warping, gaseous expansion,<br>
contraction inside the earth and human<br>
activities which causes disequilibrium<br>
of the part of crust of the earth. Various<br>
earthquakes in the earth’s crust are<br>
associated by plate movements.<br>
<b>The surface of the Earth is in continuous</b><br>
slow motion. The slow motion portrays<br>
plate tectonic movement whereby the<br>
motion of immense rigid plates at the<br>
surface of the Earth is in response to<br>
the flow of molten rock material within<br>
the Earth. The plates cover the entire<br>
surface of the globe. Since they are<br>
all moving, they sometimes collide<br>
against each other or sink beneath<br>
the other. Because of that collision of<br>
plates, faults and trenches occur. This<br>
is vividly represented by San Andreas<br>
<b>Fault in California, Peru-Chile trench</b><br>
and Atlantic ridge. At such places the<br>
motion is not smooth, the plates are<br>
stuck together at the edges but the<br>
two plates continuing to move, so the<br>
rocks along the edges are distorted. As<br>
the motion continues, the strain builds<br>
up to the point where the rock cannot<br>
withstand any more bending. With<br>
a lurch, the rock breaks and the two<br>
sides move. The shaking that radiates<br>
out from the breaking rock cause an<br>
earthquake. The earthquakes may<br>
be accelerated by two major factors;<br>
when a tectonic plate sliding over<br>
another plate along fault-line, and the<br>
occurrence of volcanic eruptions which<br>
cause the movement of molten rock<br>
below or on the earth’s crust, which<br>
then cause the movement of plates.<br>
<b>Therefore, the causes of earthquakes</b><br>
are collision between plates. This<br>
occurs when two plates collide and<br>
one layer slide beneath the other thus<br>
the scraps rough surfaces causing the<br>
vibration of the earth. Also, sudden<br>
faulting which occurs when the forces<br>
acting on the crust causing the rocks<br>
to bend and finally breaks suddenly<br>
which in the final analysis will cause<br>
the rocks to vibrate back and forth.<br>
Moreover,<br>
diastrophic<br>
movement<br>
resulting from strains in the rocks<br>
of the crust on divergent boundary<br>
causes shallow earthquakes. Volcanic<br>
eruptions cause earthquakes when<br>
magma moves below the surface with<br>
explosion causing the crust to shake.<br>
<b>Furthermore, there are other causes of</b><br>
earthquakes such as mass movement<br>
and anthropogenic activities such as<br>
quarrying of rocks using explosives<br>
like dynamites, nuclear explosion,<br>
storage of huge volume of water in big<br>
reservoir. Human activities may cause<br>
disturbance that eventually may lead to<br>
an earthquake.<br>
<b>Distribution of earthquakes</b><br>
<b>Distribution of earthquakes is closely</b><br>
related to certain seismic zones of the<br>
<b>Earth.  Earthquakes mostly occur in</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   87</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
88<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
three places which are at convergent<br>
plate margins, divergent plate margins<br>
as well as in the oceanic trenches. In<br>
convergent plate margins, earthquake<br>
occurs when two plates collide from<br>
each other. The impact of the colliding<br>
plates can cause the edges of one or<br>
both plates to buckle up into a mountain<br>
ranges or one of the plates may bend<br>
down into a deep seafloor trench. A<br>
chain of volcanoes often forms parallel<br>
to convergent plate boundaries and<br>
powerful earthquakes are common<br>
along these boundaries. Also, divergent<br>
plate margins occur when two tectonic<br>
plates move away from each other.<br>
<b>Along these boundaries earthquakes</b><br>
occurrence is very common and the<br>
magma will rise from the earth’s mantle<br>
to the surface to make new oceanic<br>
crust. Moreover, it may occur across<br>
oceanic trenches, island arc, and young<br>
fold mountain ranges. These are areas<br>
prone to earthquakes because they lie<br>
in areas of crustal instability. These<br>
are the areas where volcanic activities<br>
frequently take place.<br>
<b>The common regions where earthquakes</b><br>
occur are the circum-pacific belt. This<br>
extends from West Coast of America to<br>
<b>West Indies, the East Coast of Asia from</b><br>
the Aleutian Islands, through Japan to<br>
New Guinea to New Zealand. Also,<br>
they occur in the Alpine-Himalayan<br>
belt (the Mediterranean East Indies<br>
<b>Belt) extending from West to East</b><br>
along the Mediterranean and Himalaya<br>
to join the circum-pacific belt near the<br>
New Guinea. The mid-Atlantic ridge,<br>
is a range of submarine mountains<br>
from Iceland through the Azores,<br>
<b>Ascension Island and Tristan de Cunha</b><br>
and the great African Rift Valley: It<br>
runs along the red-sea and through East<br>
<b>Africa to Beira. Generally, these zones</b><br>
are categorized as the zone of young<br>
fold mountain, zone of faulting and<br>
fracturing, zone of active volcanoes<br>
and along plate boundaries.<br>
<b>Techniques for determining and</b><br>
recording earthquakes<br>
<b>As the shock waves travel out from the</b><br>
focus, they set of vibrations that may be<br>
as high as 200 per minute. The violent<br>
shaking of the surface rocks often<br>
causes great damage to the buildings<br>
and sometimes considerable loss of<br>
life. The earthquake is detected by<br>
using an instrument called seismograph<br>
(seismometer) (Figure 2.35). This<br>
instrument measures, records and<br>
determines earthquake waves.<br>
<b>Base anchores</b><br>
into bedrock<br>
and moves<br>
with it<br>
<b>Support</b><br>
<b>Suspended</b><br>
mass<br>
<b>Rotating</b><br>
drum<br>

<b>Spring</b><br>

<b>Seismograph records earthquake even</b><br>
if its origin is thousands of kilometers<br>
away. The seismograph consists of the<br>
following: heavy suspended weight<br>
that remains motionless while the earth<br>
beneath it moves, a recording device<br>
similar to a pen which is attached to<br>
the weight; and a pen that touches a<br>
strip of paper which is wrapped around<br>
a revolving drum. When seismic<br>
waves vibrate the bedrock, the drum<br>
vibrate along it, but the heavy weight<br>
remains motionless. As the vibrating<br>
drum revolves the motionless, the<br>
pen records a zigzag line on a paper.<br>
<b>This instrument records three distinct</b><br>
types of vibration: P-waves, S-waves,<br>
L- waves.<br>
<b>Measuring earthquakes</b><br>
<b>The severity of an earthquake can be</b><br>
expressed in terms of both intensity<br>
and magnitude. Magnitude is related<br>
to the amount of seismic energy<br>
released at the hypocenter (focus)<br>
of the earthquake whereas intensity<br>
is based on the observed effects of<br>
ground shaking on people, buildings,<br>
and natural features.<br>
<b>Earthquake magnitude: The magnitude</b><br>
is a number that characterises the relative<br>
size of an earthquake. Magnitude is<br>
based on measurement of the maximum<br>
motion recorded by a seismograph.<br>
<b>Several scales are used to measure</b><br>
the magnitude which include local<br>
magnitude (ML), Richter magnitude<br>
(RM), surface-wave magnitude (Ms),<br>
body-wave magnitude (Mb), and<br>
moment magnitude (Mw). All magnitude<br>
scales should yield approximately<br>
the same value for any given<br>
earthquake.<br>
(i)<br>
<b>The moment magnitude scale</b><br>
Moment magnitude scale is used<br>
to provide accurate estimates for<br>
large magnitude earthquakes.<br>
<b>This scale works over a wide</b><br>
range of earthquakes sizes and<br>
is applicable globally. The scale<br>
is based on the total moment<br>
release of the earthquake and is<br>
derived from modeling recording<br>
of earthquake at multiple stations.<br>
<b>Moment magnitude estimates the</b><br>
same as Richter magnitudes for<br>
small to large earthquakes. But<br>
only the moment magnitude scale<br>
is capable of measuring M8 (read<br>
‘magnitude 8’) and greater events<br>
accurately. The moment magnitude<br>
(Mw) scale is uniformly applicable<br>
to all sizes of earthquakes but is<br>
more difficult to compute than the<br>
other types.<br>
(ii)	 Richter scale<br>
One way of measuring the severity<br>
of an earthquake is by its magnitude<br>
on the Richter scale, which ranges<br>
from 0 to over 8.6. Richter scale<br>
was discovered by an American<br>
seismologist in 1935, known as<br>
<b>Charles Richter. The magnitude</b><br>
is a measure of the amount of<br>
energy released in the earthquake,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   89</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:00<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
90<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
as indicated by the amplitude (size)<br>
of the vibrations when they reach<br>
the recording instrument. The<br>
<b>Richter scale determines the rate</b><br>
of an earthquake as insignificant<br>
(less than 4 on the Richter scale),<br>
minor (4-4.9), damaging (5-5.9),<br>
destructive (6-6.9), major (7-7.9),<br>
and great (over 8). For example,<br>
the Hokkaido earthquake of the<br>
year 2003 September measured<br>
8 on the Richter scale in Japan.<br>
<b>The Richter scale does not vary</b><br>
from one place to another thus, it<br>
is uniform.<br>
Earthquake intensity: This is<br>
the degree of damage, which<br>
diminishes on moving away from<br>
the main shock source zone. The<br>
intensity is the measure of shaking<br>
at each location and it varies from<br>
place to place. Mostly it depends<br>
on the distance from the epicenter.<br>
<b>Intensity is expressed in Roman</b><br>
numbers, for example, V-IX based<br>
on the level of observed severity.<br>
<b>Intensity is measured through</b><br>
human observations and reports of<br>
felt shaking, web based form and<br>
recently there are instrumental<br>
data at station location used to<br>
calculate an estimated intensity.<br>
<b>The scale is used to show the</b><br>
intensity of the earthquake and the<br>
effects or damage caused by the<br>
earthquake. The scale ranges from<br>
<b>I to XII. The scale was discovered</b><br>
by Giuseppe Mercalli an Italian<br>
Seismologist in 1902.<br>
<b>Factors determining the degree of</b><br>
damage caused by earthquake<br>
<b>The degree of damage caused by an</b><br>
earthquake depends on several factors<br>
as follows;<br>
(a)	 Location: An earthquake that hits<br>
in a populated area is more likely<br>
to cause damage than the one<br>
that hits unpopulated area or the<br>
middle of the ocean.<br>
(b)	 Magnitude: The more energy<br>
released by an earthquake, the<br>
more destructive it can be.<br>
(c)	 Depth: Earthquakes can happen<br>
anywhere at a depth of about<br>
700 kilometers from the earth’s<br>
surface.<br>
<b>In</b><br>
general,<br>
deeper<br>
earthquakes are less damaging<br>
because their energy dissipates<br>
before it reaches the surface.<br>
(d)	 Distance from the epicenter: The<br>
epicenter is the point at the surface<br>
where the earthquake originates<br>
and is usually the place where<br>
the earthquake’s intensity is the<br>
greatest. Intensity will depend on<br>
how far the epicenter is from the<br>
population.<br>
(e)	 Local geologic conditions: The<br>
nature of the ground at the surface<br>
of an earthquake such as loose,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   90</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
91<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
sandy, soggy soil can have a<br>
profound influence on the level<br>
of damage. That is why buildings<br>
constructed on loose sediments<br>
100 km from an epicenter may<br>
experience more damage than<br>
buildings built on granite.<br>
(f)	 Architecture: Even the strongest<br>
buildings may not survive a bad<br>
earthquake, but architecture plays<br>
a big role in what and who survives<br>
a quake. For example, the January<br>
2010 Haiti earthquake became<br>
worse to poor construction, and<br>
weak to cemented buildings.<br>
<b>Wood</b><br>
frame<br>
building,<br>
for<br>
example, may suffer less damage<br>
than buildings made of materials<br>
that are cemented together.  In<br>
an earthquake, some flexibility<br>
within the individual structures is<br>
desirable.<br>
<b>The consequences of earthquakes</b><br>
<b>The common effects of earthquakes</b><br>
include structural damage to buildings,<br>
fires, damage to bridges and highways,<br>
initiation of slope failures, liquefaction,<br>
tsunami, trauma, and damage to the<br>
life time systems. Usually the types<br>
of effects experienced depend on<br>
where the earthquake occurs. When<br>
it occurs in urban, densely populated<br>
and highly developed areas it will<br>
cause much devastations. Whereas<br>
in rural areas with sparse population<br>
and<br>
underdeveloped<br>
places,<br>
less<br>
devastations will be experienced.<br>
<b>Landslide: Earthquakes can cause</b><br>
landslides especially on slopes that are<br>
already weak. A good example is the<br>
<b>Las Colinas slide in the city of Santa</b><br>
<b>Tecla, El Salvador, which was triggered</b><br>
by a M7.6 offshore earthquakes in<br>
January 2001.<br>
<b>Destruction</b><br>
of<br>
properties<br>
and<br>
resources: In some cases, earthquakes<br>
cause breaking of water and sewage<br>
systems. This threatens lives of<br>
people. For example, the Great San<br>
<b>Francisco earthquake in 1906 most</b><br>
of the damage was caused by fire that<br>
could not be controlled because the<br>
water infrastructure had broken. Also,<br>
<b>Earthquakes may cause destruction of</b><br>
buildings especially when the buildings<br>
are made up of soft sediments, and<br>
multi-stone materials. A good example<br>
is the earthquake of Kagera, Tanzania<br>
in September, 2016 that caused<br>
collapse of various buildings made<br>
of soft sediments (Figure 2.36). The<br>
earthquake measured a magnitude of<br>
<b>5.9 Mw with intensity of VII. Moreover,</b><br>
the destruction of buildings can be<br>
enhanced by liquefaction processes.<br>
<b>Soil liquefaction occurs when saturated</b><br>
or partially saturated soil lose strength<br>
and stiffness in response to an applied<br>
stress from an earthquake.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   91</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
92<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
Figure 2.36: Destructed building by earthquake in Bukoba district, Kagera<br>
region in 2016<br>
<b>Fire outbreak: Fire occurs during</b><br>
ground shake due to the disruption of<br>
gas and electrical lines. For example,<br>
most of the damage in the great 1906<br>
<b>San Francisco earthquake was caused</b><br>
by massive fires in the downtown area<br>
of the city. In that incident, 250 000<br>
buildings were damaged by the fire<br>
which were fueled by gas leaking from<br>
broken pipes.<br>
<b>Tsunami: Tsunami is caused by an</b><br>
earthquake or volcanic eruption on<br>
the seafloor. During an earthquake,<br>
seismic waves can produce powerful<br>
ocean waves. These waves tend to be<br>
very deep, with long distance between<br>
the peaks. In deep water there may be<br>
no noticeable evidence of the tsunami<br>
at the surface. However, when the<br>
wave enters shallow waters, the energy<br>
is forced to the surface and produces a<br>
tall wave that travels at high speed and<br>
moves far inland. Also, earthquakes<br>
cause death to both human and<br>
animal population. For example, on<br>
26th December, 2004, an earthquake<br>
occurred in Sumatra, Banda Aceh in<br>
<b>Indonesia and caused a tsunami which</b><br>
killed over 250 000 people. The affected<br>
areas were Indian Ocean coastline in<br>
which 11 people were reported to have<br>
died in Tanzania.<br>
<b>Stress and panic: During the occurrence</b><br>
of earthquake people usually panic run<br>
away, jump out through windows and<br>
many more unusual behaviour. Although<br>
earthquakes last for a very short<br>
time, a large number of people suffer<br>
psychological traumatic consequences<br>
for many years. Many have lost relatives<br>
and a large number have had to move<br>
away from their homes.  For example,<br>
628 Jurukovski stress had serious<br>
consequences since the population<br>

lost their work capabilities and got<br>
psychological problems.<br>
<b>Damage to transportation and</b><br>
communication systems: Earthquakes<br>
cause damage to transportation and<br>
communication systems such as railways,<br>
highways, telephone lines, electrical<br>
lines, roads and other accompanying<br>
structures which include bridges,<br>
tunnels, embankments, retaining walls,<br>
overpasses and underpasses. These<br>
results to economic loss, breakdown<br>
of timely food supply, medicines, and<br>
equipment which in the final analysis<br>
affect human population.<br>
<b>Damage to industrial and other</b><br>
technological systems: Earthquakes<br>
cause damage to industrial and other<br>
technological systems. Failure of<br>
some technological systems that use<br>
toxic, explosive, and inflammable<br>
materials result into other dangers that<br>
include loss of human lives and serious<br>
environmental damage.<br>
<b>Precautionary measures to avoid</b><br>
damage from earthquakes<br>
<b>Many of the earthquake incidences</b><br>
occur as a result of natural processes,<br>
hence it is difficult to predict their<br>
occurrence. Therefore, it is important to<br>
look at historical records of earthquakes<br>
and conduct geologic field research<br>
that can indicate how recently a fault<br>
has moved. Also, the actual prediction<br>
of earthquakes has to be based<br>
primarily on precursor phenomena<br>
events that precede an earthquake as<br>
well as on the pattern and frequency of<br>
earthquakes in an area. Moreover, there<br>
is a need to construct buildings which<br>
have protective mechanisms from<br>
earthquake disaster.<br>
<b>Earthquake warning signs</b><br>
<b>There are several signs that sometimes</b><br>
are observed before an earthquake occurs.<br>
<b>Most of these signs are associated with</b><br>
earthquakes caused by volcanism. Some<br>
of these signs include; thermal indicator,<br>
water indicator, seismo-electromagnetic<br>
indicator, animal indicator and human<br>
indicator. Thermal indicator: A few<br>
months before the occurrence of an<br>
earthquake the average temperature of<br>
the area keeps increasing. Often on the<br>
day of an earthquake the temperature<br>
of the place may record about 5 to 90C<br>
above the average temperature of that<br>
day. Water indicator: About one or<br>
three days before an earthquake there<br>
is a sudden rise or fall in water level<br>
in wells to as high as one meter. The<br>
water in a well may turn muddy. At times<br>
a fountain appears inside a well or in<br>
the ground (occurs few hours before<br>
the earthquake). Sudden increase or<br>
decrease of water flow in rivers (occur<br>
1 to 2 days before the earthquake).<br>
<b>Seismoelectromagnetic indicator:</b><br>
<b>Before the earthquake the subsurface</b><br>
temperature rises, this reduces the<br>
geomagnetic fields and adversely affects<br>
the propagation of the electromagnetic<br>
waves. This is experienced on the radio,<br>
television (signals are affected), and<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   93</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
94<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
telephone (occurs 10 to 20 hours before<br>
the earthquake). Mobile telephones stop<br>
functioning about 100 to 150 minutes<br>
before the quake. Animal indicator:<br>
<b>Between 10 to 20 hours before the</b><br>
occurrence of the earthquake the entire<br>
animal kingdoms become highly restless.<br>
<b>They move in directionless manner and</b><br>
fear. Birds do not perch on trees but<br>
move about at low height emitting a shrill<br>
noise. Rodents like rats and mongooses<br>
go into panic. Domesticated animals<br>
like cows, dogs struggle against being<br>
tied up and may turn on their owner.<br>
<b>Human indicator: Doctors and nurses</b><br>
have observed that some sensitive<br>
patients in hospital become highly<br>
disturbed before an earthquake. They<br>
exhibit a sudden rise in blood pressure,<br>
heart trouble, headache, migraine and<br>
respiratory disorders. The number of<br>
child deliveries in hospital increases for<br>
about three to five times.<br>
<b>Precautions to be taken during an</b><br>
earthquake<br>
<b>The following are some precautions to</b><br>
be taken to minimize injuries or deaths<br>
of human beings when an earthquake<br>
occurs;<br>
(a)	 Go to a safe place where things<br>
will not fall on you. This may be<br>
away from windows or tall heavy<br>
furniture, such safe places are<br>
known as assembly areas.<br>
(b)	 Wait in your safe place until the<br>
shaking stops, then check if you<br>
are hut, then check the people<br>
around you to help.<br>
(c)	 Move carefully and watch out for<br>
things that have fallen or Brocken,<br>
be ready for additional earthquake<br>
called aftershock.<br>
(d)	 Be on a lookout of fire.<br>
(e)	 In the process of leaving a building<br>
after shaking, use the stairs and<br>
not the elevator<br>
(f)	 If you are outside during the<br>
earthquake, move away from<br>
buildings, trees, and power lines,<br>
crouch down and cover your head.<br>
(g)	 If you are in the elevator stop the<br>
elevator at the nearest floor and<br>
get out immediately<br>
(h)	 If you are driving do not stop<br>
suddenly, turn on your hazard light<br>
to give an alert to other drivers then<br>
slow down slowly.<br>
(i)<br>
In public building, follow the<br>
attendant’s instruction.<br>
(j)<br>
Do not rush to the exit in train,<br>
hold on tightly to the strap or a<br>
handrail.<br>
<b>Tsunami</b><br>
<b>A tsunami is a series of ocean waves</b><br>
generated by any disturbance that<br>
displaces a large water mass.  Such<br>
disturbances include earthquakes,<br>
volcanic eruptions, earth and submarine<br>
landslides which displace large water<br>
masses. The word Tsunami is a Japanese<br>
word meaning ‘harbor wave’. There<br>
are various causes of tsunami include,<br>
earthquakes, volcanic eruptions and<br>
submarine landslides.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   94</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
95<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Earthquakes: This is generated by the</b><br>
movement of plates along the fault<br>
line. Usually Tsunami in this area<br>
occurs when the earthquake magnitude<br>
is 7.5 or greater at a subduction zone<br>
where oceanic plate slide under the<br>
continental plate. There are four<br>
necessary conditions in which an<br>
earthquake can cause tsunami. These<br>
conditions include the occurance of<br>
an earthquake in beneath the ocean<br>
so as to allow materials to slide. The<br>
earthquake magnitude of 7.5 and above<br>
richter scale, which must rupture the<br>
earth’s surface at shallow depth of less<br>
than70 km below the surface and be<br>
caused by the vertical movement of<br>
seafloor.<br>
<b>Volcanic eruption: Violent volcanic</b><br>
eruptions cause disturbances which<br>
displace a great volume of water and<br>
generate extremely destructive tsunami<br>
waves in the source area. Usually<br>
these mechanisms generate waves<br>
by the sudden displacement of water<br>
caused by volcanic explosion. For<br>
example, one of the largest tsunami was<br>
recorded in 1883 after the explosion<br>
and collapse of the Krakatoa Volcano<br>
in Indonesia.<br>
<b>Sub-marine landslides: These are rare</b><br>
events but have been witnessed in<br>
<b>Alaska and elsewhere. The most famous</b><br>
occurrence witnessed on July 9, 1958, in<br>
Lituya bay on Alaska’s southeast coast,<br>
when a nearby earthquake caused 40<br>
million cubic yards of rocks to slide<br>
over 2000 feet equal to 0.6 kilometres<br>
into the narrow inlet of Lituya. Once a<br>
tsunami is generated it can travel across<br>
an entire ocean and cause devastation far<br>
from its source.  In the open sea, tsunami<br>
travels at several hundred kilometers<br>
per hour, and commonly goes unnoticed<br>
as they pass beneath ship because they<br>
are usually less than 1m high and the<br>
distance between wave crest is typically<br>
hundreds of kilometers. In any case,<br>
the tremendous energy possessed by a<br>
tsunami is concentrated on a shoreline<br>
when it hits either as a large breaking<br>
wave or in some cases, as what appears<br>
to be a very rapid rising tide. For<br>
instance, when it occurred in South East<br>
<b>Asia (Malaysian, Singapore, Indonesia</b><br>
and parts of Thailand and India) on<br>
26th December 2004, it is believed that<br>
more than 230 000 people were killed.<br>
<b>A tsunami has various consequences</b><br>
to human beings. It causes high rate<br>
of mortality, illness and injuries, leads<br>
to the spread of infectious diseases<br>
as well as creates physiological<br>
traumas.<br>

about<br>

as cases of study. Guide your study<br>
on the effects of the phenomenon<br>
and ways to overcome it. Present<br>
your work in the class and invite<br>
comments for improvement.<br>

term Tsunami?<br>
<b>Vulcanicity</b><br>
<b>The term vulcanicity is a range of</b><br>
processes by which molten, gaseous,<br>
or solid rock materials are intruded<br>
into the earth’s crust or extruded onto<br>
the earth’s surface. The molten rock<br>
materials before reaching the earth’s<br>
surface are called magma while after<br>
reaching on the earth’s surface they are<br>
called lava. Vulcanicity or vulcanism<br>
includes all processes resulting into the<br>
formation of volcanoes and lava plateaus<br>
(as extrusive vulcanicity), and intrusive<br>
igneous features. A volcano is a hill or<br>
mountain formed by the eruption of<br>
molten materials from a central opening<br>
or vent in the crust. Lava plateau is an<br>
upland with a generally level summit,<br>
and is made of successive layers of lava.<br>
<b>Volcano and lava plateau are extrusive</b><br>
vulcanic features formed onto the<br>
surface of the earth after cooling and<br>
solidifying of the molten materials from<br>
the earth’s mantle. Molten materials or<br>
rocks originate in the upper plastic layer<br>
of earth’s mantle. Although the material<br>
in the mantle has a high temperature, it<br>
is kept in a semi solid state because of<br>
the greater pressure exerted upon them.<br>
<b>However, if this pressure is released</b><br>
locally by folding, faulting or other<br>
movements at plate boundaries, some<br>
of the semi-solid materials become<br>
molten and rise, forcing their way into<br>
weaknesses in the crust or onto the<br>
surface, where they cool and solidify.<br>
<b>Basically the nature of volcanic eruption</b><br>
is of two types which are fissure, and<br>
central eruptions. Fissure eruption is the<br>
type of eruption that concentrates along<br>
narrow fissures whereas lava explored<br>
from the fissure and spread to form<br>
lava plateaus. Many fissure eruptions<br>
are associated with basaltic lava floor.<br>
<b>While, central eruption is the one which</b><br>
concentrates at a central vent and gives<br>
rise to volcanic cones and domes, with or<br>
without summit craters. Volcanoes may<br>
be single, form clusters or be distributed<br>
in chains.  The Mount Kilimanjaro is<br>
an example of an isolated, single, giant<br>
volcano.<br>
<b>Classification of volcanoes</b><br>
<b>Volcanoes can be classified based on</b><br>
mode of eruption and the period of<br>
eruption. Based on the mode of eruption,<br>
there are central eruption and explosive<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   96</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
97<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
eruption or quite eruption. The central<br>
eruption is usually violent and explosive<br>
eruption of lava, volcanic ashes and<br>
fragmental materials through a pipe under<br>
the impact of violent gases. Examples<br>
of central eruption volcanoes are Mount<br>
<b>Kilimanjaro, and Mount Oldonyo Lengai</b><br>
found in Tanzania. Fissure volcanoes<br>
also are quiet, in this case lava flows<br>
quietly through fissure, fracture on fault<br>
due to low pressure. An example of this is<br>
Laki fissure eruption of 1783 in Iceland.<br>
<b>Also, volcanoes are classified according</b><br>
to their activities in which there are three<br>
categories namely active, dormant, and<br>
extinct volcanoes. An active volcano<br>
is the volcano which erupts frequently,<br>
for example mountain Nyiragongo in<br>
<b>Eastern Republic of Congo. A volcano</b><br>
is said to be active only when it has<br>
errupted within five hundred years. A<br>
dormant volcano is a volcano with an<br>
infrequent eruptions.  Also, is known as<br>
sleeping volcano. Example of dormant<br>
volcano are, mountains Kilimanjaro,<br>
<b>Meru and Kenya. Extinct volcano is the</b><br>
one which has ceased erupting for a very<br>
long time in history, for example Mount<br>
Rungwe. Like many other all landforms,<br>
a volcano is also attared by weathering<br>
and erosion particularly at extinct stage<br>
such that with time most of it, if not all<br>
of the volcano may have been removed.<br>
<b>Volcanic materials</b><br>
<b>Volcanic materials ejected during</b><br>
eruption include lava. It is known as lava<br>
when it is ejected on the earth’s surface<br>
accompanied by gases. Other volcanic<br>
materials include vapour, fragmental<br>
materials and ashes. The molten rock<br>
is called magma when it is below the<br>
earth’s surface, and lava when it is on<br>
the earth’s surface. The chemical nature<br>
of the materials can either be acidic or<br>
basic (Table 2.6). Acidic materials are<br>
extremely viscous (sticky) and less<br>
mobile because it contains a lot of<br>
silica. The basic materials are fluid (less<br>
viscous), mobile and flow long distance<br>
before solidifying. Basic materials<br>
contain low melting point and less silica.<br>
<b>Also, lava and magma are classified on</b><br>
the basis of light and dark coloured<br>
minerals into felsic and mafic.<br>

rock type<br>

<b>More than</b><br>
66<br>
Extremely viscous and less mobile.<br>

rapidly<br>
at<br>
high<br>
temperatures over 850o C<br>

<b>Vapour and gases: Volcanic eruption</b><br>
emit vapour and gases. Vapour and<br>
steam constitute about 60% to 90%<br>
of the total gases emitted during<br>
eruption. The vapour includes phreatic<br>
vapour and magmatic vapour. While,<br>
gases emitted include the gaseous<br>
compounds of sulphur, hydrogen and<br>
nitrogen. But most of them are scattered<br>
into the atmosphere. The interaction<br>
between the gases contained in molten<br>
magma generates great heat within<br>
the ascending lavas and so maintains<br>
high temperature within lava pods in<br>
a crater. Also, gas acts as a lubricant<br>
so that loss of much gas from the lava<br>
will increases its viscosity. Pyroclastic<br>
materials: The pyroclastic materials<br>
are also known as solid materials.<br>
<b>When an eruption is accompanied by</b><br>
a series of explosion, solid materials<br>
are ejected as pyro clasts. They may<br>
include fragments of rocks when the<br>
pipe is blown through the crust, angular<br>
fragments, lava cooled in the pipe and<br>
fine materials such as pumice, cinder,<br>
lapilli, ash and dust. Liquid materials:<br>
<b>These are usually the most important</b><br>
product of an eruption which reaches<br>
the earth surface as lava (the molten<br>
magma). The nature of volcanic form,<br>
depends on the nature of eruption and<br>
lava. For example, some lava contains<br>
much silica such as acidic lava with<br>
high melting point. They are very<br>
viscous as they solidify in the vent and<br>
cause recurrent explosive eruption. On<br>
the other side basic lava is poor in silica<br>
since it is rich in iron and magnesium<br>
minerals. It has a lava at melting point<br>
which flows at considerable distance<br>
before solidifying.  Such lavas tend to<br>
produce much flatted zones of great<br>
diameter and the eruption is quite<br>
without much explosive activities, as is<br>
the case in shield volcano.<br>
<b>Features of vulcanicity</b><br>
<b>There are two major types of features or</b><br>
landforms associated with vulcanicity<br>
which are intrusive and extrusive<br>
features.<br>
<b>Intrusive vulcanic features</b><br>
<b>When magma is forced to the surface</b><br>
only a small amount of the mass<br>
actually reaches that level. Most of<br>
the magma is intruded into the crust<br>
where it cools, solidifies, and form a<br>
range of features or landforms. These<br>
are often exposed at the surface by<br>
later erosion. Intrusive or plutonic<br>
features formed within crust, include;<br>
batholith, sill, dyke, laccolith, lopolith<br>
and phacolith.<br>
<b>Batholith: This is a dome or very</b><br>
large mass of igneous rock which is<br>
formed by cooling and solidification of<br>
magma deeply in the heart of mountain<br>
range, in the crust. They have a coarse<br>
grained texture and in most cases they<br>
are granitic in composition. Normally<br>
they form surface features after the<br>
removal of the overlying materials<br>
through denudation. Batholiths have<br>

09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
99<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
irregular  shape  with side walls that<br>
incline steeply against the host rock<br>
(Figure 2.37). The most common<br>
examples<br>
of<br>
batholiths<br>
include<br>
<b>Sierra Nevada Batholith and Idaho</b><br>
<b>Batholith in the United States of</b><br>
<b>America and Aswan Granite Batholith</b><br>
in Egypt and Cape Coast batholith in<br>
Ghana.<br>
<b>Batholith</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.37: Batholith</b><br>
<b>Sill: A sill is a sheet of magma which</b><br>
is formed when igneous materials<br>
solidifies<br>
horizontally<br>
along<br>
the<br>
bedding planes (Figure 2.38). Usually<br>
they are concordant to rock structure<br>
and they can be of any thickness and<br>
extend for many kilometers. Sill is<br>
made up of rock composed of all<br>
types. There are three types of sill<br>
depending on the nature of magma<br>
intrusion which are simple, multiple<br>
and composite sills. Some sills form<br>
ridge like escarpments, when exposed<br>
by erosion which may give rise to<br>
waterfalls and rapids for example,<br>
<b>Kink on Falls - Guinea or Three sisters</b><br>
<b>South Africa. Sills are more important</b><br>
ore deposits and other important<br>
minerals such as gold, platinum and<br>
chromium.<br>
<b>Sill</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.38: Sill</b><br>
<b>Dyke: This is a wall like feature which</b><br>
is formed when magma pushes up<br>
towards the surface through cracks in<br>
the rock, cools and solidifies across the<br>
bending plane (Figure 2.39). Since it is<br>
discordant feature, it does not follow<br>
the nature of plane. Depending on the<br>
attitude of the host rocks, the dyke may<br>
be vertical, horizontal, or inclined, but<br>
most of them are vertical or steeply<br>
inclined. Dykes are categorized into<br>
two which are multiple dykes which<br>
occur due to successive injections of<br>
homogeneous material on the same<br>
fissure and composite dykes which<br>
occur due to successive injection<br>
of different materials into the same<br>
fissure.<br>
<b>Dyke</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.39: Dyke</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   99</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
100<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Laccolith: Is a dome-shaped intrusion</b><br>
feature which is formed within or<br>
between layers of a sedimentary rock<br>
(Figure 2.40). It is formed of viscous<br>
magma which is unable to spread<br>
far and accumulates in a large mass,<br>
bending up the overlying rocks.  With<br>
the continued addition of lava, the<br>
laccolith grows in height and width,<br>
until finally the supply of material or<br>
the propelling force diminishes lava<br>
by congelation in its conduit and the<br>
inflow.  Usually laccoliths are compact<br>
in form.  If the laccolith is more<br>
resistant than the adjacent rocks after<br>
denudation it forms an upland. An<br>
example of this is Univukwe Range in<br>
Harare, Zimbabwe.<br>
<b>Laccolith</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.40: Laccolith</b><br>
<b>Lopolith: This is very large saucer</b><br>
-shaped<br>
intrusion<br>
feature<br>
lying<br>
concordant to the rock strata which<br>
in the end forms a shallow basin. It is<br>
formed as a result of greater weight<br>
of the overlying strata and deposits<br>
(Figure 2.41). Examples of lopolith is<br>
that found in Bushveld Basin in the<br>
Transvaal in South Africa.<br>
<b>Lopolith</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.41: Lopolith</b><br>
<b>Phacolith: This is a concordant intrusion</b><br>
of igneous rocks which is formed after<br>
cooling and solidification of magma<br>
near the crest of an anticline or base<br>
of syncline (Figure 2.42). A phacolith<br>
can form along the sedimentary bed<br>
rock which is concordant by exerting<br>
great thickness along the synclines and<br>
anticlines.<br>
<b>Phacolith</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.42: Phacolith</b><br>
<b>Extrusive vulcanic features</b><br>
<b>When a vulcanic activity takes place</b><br>
above the ground and hot molten<br>
materials<br>
are<br>
released<br>
onto<br>
the<br>
landscape, later cools and solidify, such<br>
activity is called extrusive vulcanic<br>
activity or volcanicity. The materials<br>
thrown<br>
out<br>
include<br>
lava<br>
flows,<br>
pyroclastic debris, volcanic bombs,<br>
ash, dust and gases such as  nitrogen<br>

compounds, sulphur compounds  and<br>
minor<br>
amounts<br>
of<br>
chlorine<br>
and<br>
hydrogen. Extrusive vulcanicity is<br>
responsible for the formation of many<br>
landforms found on the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>Examples of extrusive landforms</b><br>
include volcanic cones, composite<br>
cones, explosive crater, caldera, acidic<br>
lava and shield volcano.<br>
<b>Ash and Cinder cone (Pyroclasts</b><br>
cones) are cone shaped structure which<br>
consist of predominantly fragmented<br>
materials<br>
produced<br>
by<br>
explosive<br>
volcanicity. They are formed when the<br>
lava is blown and ejected violently to a<br>
greater height and fall back to the earth<br>
and build a cone like feature (Figure<br>
2.43). The slopes of the cone are always<br>
concave due the spreading tendency<br>
of lava at the base of the cone while<br>
cinder is steep sided. Examples of ash<br>
and cinder cone are Sarabwe Fileko in<br>
<b>Rungwe in Southern Tanzania and Jos</b><br>
Plateaus in Nigeria.<br>

<b>Ash</b><br>

<b>Composite cone (Strato-Volcano): These</b><br>
are stratified and consist of both lava<br>
flows with concaves sides and beds of<br>
pyroclastic materials. Lava often escapes<br>
from the sides of the cone where it<br>
builds up small conolets (Figure 2.44).<br>
<b>The cone has steep slope and it is the</b><br>
most common volcano. Examples of<br>
composite cones are Kilimanjaro<br>
mountain, Cameroon mountains and<br>
<b>Mountain Nyiragongo in the Democratic</b><br>
Republic of Congo.<br>

<b>Volcanic plug (plug dome): Is a rigid</b><br>
cylindrical plug formed when very<br>
viscous lava is forced out by very<br>
explosive and violent eruptions. The<br>
base of the plug is surrounded by<br>
exploded debris as shown in Figure<br>
2.45. An example of this is Atakor<br>
volcanic area of the Hogger mountain<br>
in Algeria.<br>

Explosion crater: The explosion crater is also known as ring crater. It is a shallow,<br>
flat floored depression surrounded by a low rim of pyro clasts and local rock<br>
(Figure 2.46). Usually less than 50 meters high and often found in groups.  A vent<br>
is blowing through the local rock by a series of gas explosions.<br>
<b>Figure 2.46:  Ring crater</b><br>
<b>Crater: This is a bowl or funnel-shaped</b><br>
depression on top of volcanic cone<br>
after the plug dome is blown off. It has<br>
a diameter of less than 1.6 kilometer.<br>
<b>The depression becomes a crater lake</b><br>
when is filled with water from rainfall<br>
or melting ice (Figure 2.47). Examples<br>
of craters are Embakai, Olmoti and<br>
<b>Ngozi in Tanzania and the Summit of</b><br>
<b>Mountain St. Helens in the United State</b><br>
of America. Craters are formed into two<br>
ways; Firstly, through pouring of lava<br>
during the formation of volcano. During<br>
the formation of a volcano a crater may<br>
form when the outpouring of lava stops.<br>
<b>As the lava cools, the magma in the vent</b><br>
cools as well and contracts at the same<br>
time. This causes it to withdraw into the<br>
vent. In doing so, a depression forms at<br>
the top of the volcano. Craters which have<br>
been formed in this way are found on<br>
the volcanoes of Kilimanjaro, Oldoinyo<br>
<b>Lengai and Ngorongoro in Tanzania; and</b><br>
Marsabit and OlDonyo Nyoike in Kenya.<br>
<b>Other examples include Mahavura in</b><br>
<b>Democratic Republic of Congo; and</b><br>
<b>Versuvius in Italy. Secondly, through</b><br>
big volcanic explosion which blows a<br>
hole in the ground. Craters formed in<br>
this way are known as explosion crater<br>
or Ring crater.<br>
<b>Crater Lake</b><br>
<b>Rim</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.47: Crater</b><br>
<b>Caldera: These are large depression</b><br>
formed when the upper part of the<br>
volcano is either blown away by violent<br>
eruptions or the top of the cone subsides<br>

are Sunda Strait. Collapse calderas are<br>
further subdivided into Krakatau-type<br>
or explosion-collapse calderas and<br>

calderas, for example, Mokuaweo-weo<br>
caldera of Mauna Loa.<br>

<b>Volcanic vent: This is a central opening</b><br>
exposed on the earth’s surface,<br>
underlying the summit crater of the<br>
volcano where volcanic materials<br>
are emitted. This opening, is usually<br>
circular or nearly circular shaped through<br>
which heated materials consisting of<br>
gases, water, liquid lava and fragments<br>
of rocks are ejected from the highly<br>
heated interior to the surface of the earth<br>
(Figure 2.49).<br>

<b>Volcanic vent</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.49: Volcanic vent</b><br>
<b>Acidic Lava (cumulo dome): This is</b><br>
a dome shaped volcano with convex<br>
slopes formed when acidic lava solidifies<br>
around the vent (Figure 2.50).  Lava does<br>
not flow away rather it piles near the vent<br>
since it is very viscous.  The viscosity<br>
of the lava is a result of high content<br>
of silica and its high melting point. An<br>
example of cumulo dome is Ntumbi<br>
dome in Mbeya region, Tanzania.<br>

<b>Acidic lava</b><br>
cone<br>

<b>Shield volcano: This  is broad and</b><br>
extensive domed volcano with gentle<br>
slope sides formed when basic lava<br>
is poured in the surface and spread<br>
to occupy a large area (Figure 2.51).<br>
<b>Lava from the earth’s interior flows out</b><br>
through a vent.<br>

<b>Minor volcanic features</b><br>
<b>There are other minor volcanic features</b><br>
that normally start to take place when<br>
volcanic eruption is approaching the<br>

end. These features include Solfatara,<br>
<b>Fumarole, Mofette, Hot springs, Geyser</b><br>
and mud volcano. Hot springs: This is<br>
the natural outflow of superheated water<br>
from the ground that contain mineral<br>
substances in solution or in suspension<br>
form. They are springs produced by<br>
the geothermal heated groundwater<br>
that emerged onto the surface of the<br>
earth, whereby, shallow magma may<br>
heat groundwater or groundwater may<br>
contact the heated rocks by magma in<br>
active volcanic areas. When the heated<br>
water emerged onto the earth’s surface it<br>
is known as a hot spring. It can influence<br>
the production of geothermal power.<br>
Example of hot springs are Amboni,<br>
<b>Tanga and Nanyala in Songwe region</b><br>
in Tanzania. Geyser: This refers to the<br>
forceful emission of superheated water<br>
from the ground to the high level in the<br>
atmosphere. It is a hot spring which<br>
spouts hot water and vapour from time<br>
to time due to pressure (Figure 2.52).<br>
<b>Geysers are of two types namely pool</b><br>
geyser and nozzle geyser. A pool geyser<br>
spout water from open and relatively<br>
large pool. A nozzle geyser spout water<br>
from very small and contracted vent.<br>
<b>Geyser</b><br>
<b>Strong</b><br>
pressure<br>
<b>Figure 2.52: Gaseous materials being</b><br>
emitted through the vent<br>
<b>Fumarole: A vent through which there</b><br>
is emission of gases and water vapour.<br>
Also, it emits smoke.<br>
<b>Impact of volcanic eruptions on</b><br>
human and the environment<br>
<b>Destructive consequences: Some</b><br>
volcanic eruptions cause great loss of<br>
life. A volcanic eruption in Krakatoa<br>
in 1883, for example, caused great sea<br>
waves which drowned 40 000 people in<br>
neighboring isles. Volcanic eruption may<br>
cause great damage to property, houses,<br>
crops or livestock (Figure 2.53). Gases<br>
emitted during eruption cause pollution<br>
such as air pollution which in turn can<br>
cause soil pollution and affect human<br>
beings. Hot molten materials ejected<br>
on the surface can destroy vegetation<br>
leading to deforestation or loss of<br>
biodiversity.<br>
<b>Figure 2.53: Destruction caused by</b><br>
volcanic eruption<br>
<b>Constructive consequences: The lava</b><br>
formed from volcanic eruption leads<br>
to the formation of fertile soil when<br>
is broken down through weathering<br>
process. A good example is the southern<br>
slopes of Kilimanjaro in Tanzania.<br>
<b>Volcanicity can lead to the formation</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   104</b><br>

of mineral deposits like copper deposits,<br>
diamond of Kimberley in South Africa.<br>
<b>Also, some hot springs are utilized for</b><br>
heating and supplying hot water to<br>
buildings in cold countries like Iceland<br>
and New Zealand. Volcanic eruptions<br>
under hot spring can be used to provide<br>
geothermal power which can be utilized<br>
for electricity generation as the case in<br>
Kenya, Ethiopia and USA. Moreover,<br>
volcanic eruptions can attract tourists<br>
and lead to tourism industry due to the<br>
formation of hot springs and geysers.<br>

the following aspects;<br>

produced both internally and<br>
externally<br>
