<b>Exercise 2.7</b><br>
1.	 Discuss the effects of volcanism<br>
to man and his surroundings.<br>
2.	 Basing on their characteristics,<br>
differentiate between active,<br>
dormant and extinct volcanoes.<br>
3.	 Describe<br>
the<br>
landforms<br>
associated with volcanicity.<br>
4.	 Discuss the importance of<br>
vulcanism.<br>
5.	 With the aid of diagram, explain<br>
six intrusive vulcanic features.<br>
<b>Diastrophism</b><br>
<b>Diastrophism is also known as</b><br>
diastrophic forces. It includes all<br>
processes that move, elevate or build<br>
up portions of the earth’s crust through<br>
orogenic processes. For example,<br>
mountain building through folding and<br>
epirogenic forces involving uplifting<br>
or submergence of large part of the<br>
earth’s crust. The epirogenic processes<br>
include vertical forces responsible for<br>
upward (emergence) and downward<br>
(submergence) movements. The vertical<br>
movement of the earth is either upward<br>
or downward (cymatogenic) along a<br>
radius from the center of the earth to<br>
the surface.  The movement is termed as<br>
epeirogenic process as it occurs at a large<br>
scale.  Upward movement causes uplift<br>
of continental parts or coastal areas.<br>
<b>This is called emergence. Downward</b><br>
movement causes submergence of part of<br>
land mass or coastal area. This is known<br>
as submergence.<br>
<b>The process of orogeny, involves</b><br>
endogenetic forces working in horizontal<br>
manner. Horizontal movement, involves<br>
tensional and compressional forces<br>
that act on the earth’s crust side by<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   105</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
106<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
side tangentially or horizontally which<br>
causes disruption of the horizontal layer<br>
strata. It is known as convergent force<br>
and movement.  Tensional force operates<br>
on opposite directions and is known as a<br>
divergent force and movement. Tensional<br>
forces create rupture, cracks, fracture and<br>
faults in the crustal parts of the earth.<br>
When endogenetic operates face to face,<br>
that is compressional or convergence<br>
force. Compressional force causes crustal<br>
bending leading to the formation of folds<br>
or crustal warping leading to local rise or<br>
subsidence of crustal parts. The process<br>
of crustal warping affect large areas of<br>
the crust either warped (raised) upward<br>
or downward. The upward rise of land<br>
due to convergent compressional force<br>
is called upwarping while the bending<br>
of the crustal part downward due to<br>
convergent compressional force is<br>
known as down-warping. The effects<br>
of earth movement can be categorized<br>
into two groups namely: faulting and<br>
folding.<br>
<b>Faulting</b><br>
<b>Faulting is the process that involves</b><br>
breaking or fracturing of crustal<br>
rocks due to forces resulting from<br>
earth movement to form a faults. The<br>
movement involved in the formation of<br>
a fault may operate vertical or horizontal<br>
or in any direction. In faulting, there is<br>
vertical displacement of rock blocks.<br>
<b>Faulting has various effects on landscape</b><br>
as it causes the occurrence of uplift,<br>
depression, horizontal displacement<br>
of large block of the crust and as the<br>
associated landforms.<br>
<b>Faults</b><br>
<b>These are fractures in the crustal rocks</b><br>
wherein the rocks are displaced along<br>
a plane. Fault plane, is the plane along<br>
which the rock blocks are displaced<br>
by tensional and compressional forces<br>
acting vertically and horizontally to form<br>
a fault. Upthrown side, is the uppermost<br>
block of a fault. Downthrown side, is<br>
the lowermost block of fault. Fault dip,<br>
it is the angle between the fault plane<br>
and horizontal plane. Hanging wall is<br>
the upper wall of a fault. Foot wall, is<br>
the lower wall of a fault. Fault scarp,<br>
is the steep wall-like slope caused<br>
by faulting of the crustal rocks. A<br>
fault can be categorized into normal,<br>
reversed, transform (tear), over thrust<br>
and monocline depending on how it is<br>
formed.<br>
<b>Normal faults: Normal faults are formed</b><br>
due to the displacement of both the rock<br>
blocks in opposite directions due to<br>
fracture consequent upon greatest stress.<br>
<b>This results from tension forces (splitting</b><br>
of rocks) when rocks are pulled apart and<br>
the crust is stretched. It is characterized<br>
by the inclination of the fault plane and<br>
the direction of down throw. Both can<br>
be found in one side either right or left.<br>
<b>They can be vertical or inclined     (Figure</b><br>
2.54). The middle part of the entire fault<br>
tends to rise up, while the hanging wall<br>
sink down.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   106</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
107<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Tensional forces</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.54: Normal Fault</b><br>
<b>Reverse fault: This is a  type of fault</b><br>
formed due to compressional forces<br>
and occurs when the beds of the<br>
rock on one side of the fault plane<br>
are thrust over the other side (Figure<br>
2.55). The fault plane in reverse<br>
fault, is usually inclined at an angle<br>
between 40 degrees and the horizontal<br>
(0 degree).<br>
<b>Compressional forces</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.55: Reverse fault</b><br>
<b>Transform (Tear fault): The transform</b><br>
fault, is also known as strike, slide,<br>
shear, wretch or trans current fault.<br>
<b>It is formed when a vertical fracture</b><br>
displaced parallel in opposing direction<br>
along the line of fault (Figure 2.56),<br>
and a good example is San Andrea’s<br>
fault of California.<br>
<b>Tensional forces</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.56: Transform (Tear fault)</b><br>
<b>Overthrust fault: Is formed when</b><br>
the top part of the fault overrides the<br>
bottom part along a fault plane, because<br>
of compressional forces (Figure 2.57).<br>
<b>Overthrust</b><br>
fault<br>
<b>Figure 2. 57: Overthrust fault</b><br>
<b>Monocline fault: Monocline is a</b><br>
tensional fracture in which the strata<br>
are bending closely related to the<br>
normal fault but the normal fault has<br>
horizontal strata (Figure 2.58).<br>
<b>Monocline fault</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.58: Monocline fault</b><br>

<b>Landforms resulting from faulting</b><br>
<b>The influence of faulting on landscape</b><br>
results into direct and indirect effects.<br>
<b>The indirect effects involve the formation</b><br>
of landforms which are essentially<br>
structured in nature because it is a direct<br>
result of faulting on landscape<br>
<b>Direct landforms</b><br>
<b>Initially faulted landforms appear as</b><br>
abrupt features, but in time erosion will<br>
modify their outlines.  Therefore, these<br>
are faults on the basis of fault pattern.<br>
<b>The following are some of the direct</b><br>
effects of faulting.<br>
<b>Step faulting: Is the process which leads</b><br>
to the formation of several parallel<br>
faults.  Therefore, the faults appear as a<br>
series of steps on either one side of the<br>
rift valley or both sides (Figure 2.59).<br>
<b>Step faults</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.59: Step faulting</b><br>
<b>Fault block: It is also refered as tilted</b><br>
block. This is a block like structure with<br>
inclined strata formed due to faulting<br>
process. Inclination occurs as the result<br>
of one side of the block being uplifted<br>
faster than the other side. It is similar<br>
to monocline fault (Figure 2.60). This<br>
is sometimes known as fault block,<br>
if the block has been titled, then they<br>
are called tilt-block. In USA they are<br>
known as tilt block basin and range<br>
countries. Another example is that of<br>
Afar Triangle in Ethiopia.<br>
<b>Graben</b><br>
<b>Graben</b><br>
<b>Horst</b><br>
<b>Normal faults</b><br>
<b>Figure 2. 60: Fault block</b><br>
<b>Horst mountains: The horst (block)</b><br>
mountain is a table like mountain<br>
formed by faulting process that result<br>
to the rising of the block rock forming<br>
table like structure. Block mountain<br>
is an upland bordered by fault on one<br>
or more sides (Figure 2.61). It occurs<br>
either when the land mass subsided<br>
between two normal faults or a block<br>
remains up stand while on its other side<br>
drops. Example of Block Mountains is<br>
the Drakensberg range in South Africa.<br>
<b>Block mountain formed</b><br>
<b>Compressional force</b><br>
<b>Compressional force</b><br>
<b>Block mountain</b><br>
<b>Figure 2. 61:  Horst mountain</b><br>
<b>Fault scarp landform: It is an escarpment</b><br>
or scarp which is steep on one side and<br>
gentle on the other side (Figure 2.62).<br>

<b>Fault scarp</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.62: Fault scarp</b><br>
<b>Rift valley: It is formed where the</b><br>
<b>Earth’s crust, or outermost layer, is</b><br>
spreading or splitting apart. This kind of<br>
valley is often narrow, with steep sides<br>
and a flat floor. Rift valleys are also<br>
called grabens, which means “ditch”<br>
in German. While there is no official<br>
distinction between a graben and a<br>
rift valley, a graben usually describes<br>
a small rift valley. Rift valleys differ<br>
from river valleys and glacial valleys<br>
because they are created by tectonic<br>
activity and not by the process of<br>
erosion. Rift valleys are created by<br>
plate tectonics which are constantly<br>
in motion shifting against each other<br>
or tear apart from each other. When<br>
plates move apart, the Earth’s crust<br>
separates or rifts to form a rift valley<br>
(Figure 2.63). Many rift valleys have<br>
been found underwater, along the large<br>
ridges that run throughout the ocean<br>
called mid-ocean ridges. Good example<br>
of giant rift valleys include the Baikal<br>
<b>Rift Valley and the East African Rift</b><br>
Valley.<br>
<b>Tensional</b><br>
force<br>
<b>Tensional</b><br>
force<br>
<b>Rift valley</b><br>
<b>Figure 2. 63:  Rift valley</b><br>
<b>The East African Rift System (EARS)</b><br>
forms a narrow (50-150 km wide),<br>
elongate a system of normal faults<br>
that stretches some 3500 km in a sub-<br>
meridian direction, that runs from<br>
northern Jordan Rift Valley in Asia to<br>
Mozambique in South Eastern Africa.<br>
<b>It is connected to the world wide</b><br>
system of oceanic rifts via the Afar<br>
<b>Triangle to the Gulf of Aden and the</b><br>
<b>Red Sea. The EARS is composed of</b><br>
two rift trends called the eastern and<br>
western branches. The eastern branch is<br>
located north and east of Lake Victoria,<br>
which is a volcanic-rich system that<br>
forms the Kenya and Ethiopian Rifts.<br>
<b>In places the rift system was probably</b><br>
initiated in the early Miocene, but<br>
there is also evidence for earlier<br>
<b>Paleogene rift activity in northern</b><br>
<b>Kenya and Ethiopia. The rift branch</b><br>
is well exposed at the surface and is<br>
dotted with relatively small lakes. The<br>
western branch appears to have been<br>
initiated later than the eastern branch,<br>
during the late Miocene. It is composed<br>
of a series of extensive deep and<br>
shallow lakes such as Albert, Edward,<br>
<b>Kivu, Rukwa, Nyasa, and Tanganyika</b><br>
the world’s second deepest lake. These<br>

lakes make much of the rift structure.<br>
<b>The western branch is associated with</b><br>
much less volcanism than that of the<br>
eastern branch although both branches<br>
are seismically and volcanically active<br>
today. The eastern arm, on the other<br>
hand, has popular volcanoes like Mt<br>
<b>Kilimanjaro, Oldonyo Lengai and Mt</b><br>
<b>Meru. The lakes found on the eastern</b><br>
arm are not as deep or as voluminous<br>
like the ones on the western arm. Some<br>
of these lakes are Lake Eyasi, Lake<br>
<b>Natron in Tanzania, and Lake Turkana</b><br>
in Kenya which is the sole large lake in<br>
the eastern branch (Figure 2.57).<br>
<b>Indirect effects of faulting</b><br>
<b>These are the effects resulting from</b><br>
landforms which were formed though<br>
faulting processes. Some of the indirect<br>
effects include waterfalls, river offset,<br>
lake formation, river reverse direction,<br>
occurrence of springs and fault guided<br>
valley.<br>
<b>Waterfalls: Vertical faulting across a</b><br>
river valley may cause a waterfall. A<br>
waterfall (cataracts) is a river or other<br>
body of water’s steep fall over a rocky<br>
or surface. Often, waterfalls form as<br>
streams flow from soft rock to hard rock.<br>
<b>This happens both laterally (as a stream</b><br>
flows across the earth) and vertically (as<br>
the stream drops in a waterfall). In both<br>
cases, the soft rock erodes, leaving a hard<br>
ledge over which the stream falls. A fall<br>
line is the imaginary line along which<br>
parallel rivers plunge as they flow from<br>
uplands to lowlands.<br>
<b>River off set: Tear faulting with a</b><br>
horizontal movement across a river<br>
causes the river to be offset at the point<br>
it crosses the fault.<br>
<b>Lake formation: Rift faulting that forms</b><br>
an enclosed basin may cause a lake if<br>
rivers flow into the basin for example,<br>
Lake Tanganyika, Nyasa and Rudolf.<br>
<b>River flow: Rivers may follow straight</b><br>
natural fault valleys especially in areas of<br>
tilt-block faulting and where differential<br>
movements have raised some parts while<br>
lowering others.<br>
<b>River reverse direction: Crustal tilting</b><br>
or up warping across a river valley will<br>
gradually force the river to reverse its<br>
direction and flow backwards, if it is<br>
unable to maintain its original flow.<br>
<b>For example, Kafu river and Lake</b><br>
<b>Kyoga (Uganda), Katonga river, Lake</b><br>
<b>Victoria, Kagera river and Lake Victoria</b><br>
(Tanzania).<br>
Occurrence of springs: Due to faulting,<br>
springs can be formed because the fault<br>
provides the area of weakness through<br>
which water can came out from the<br>
aquifer. Obsequent fault line scarp:  Is<br>
a scarp facing the opposite direction to<br>
the original fault line scarp.<br>
<b>Fault guided valley: This is a fault which</b><br>
cause rock to be shattered and crushed<br>
to the extent that rocks are more easily<br>
eroded that those further from faulty.<br>
<b>Very common in river valleys, where</b><br>
a valley is guided by a fault line, for<br>
example, Santa River in Guinea.<br>

<b>Folding</b><br>
<b>Folding is the process that involve</b><br>
the wrinkling of the earth’s crust to<br>
foam folds. The folding of rocks, like<br>
faulting, produces a variety of geological<br>
structures such as fold Mountains.  When<br>
the folding is gentle results in simple<br>
up fold (anticlines) as mountains and<br>
down fold (synclines) as valleys.  If<br>
the folding intensity is high results to<br>
the over folding rocks which is very<br>
common. Rock folding is due to the<br>
tremendous compressional forces that<br>
develop as the tectonic plate of the crust<br>
move towards each other to produce<br>
folds. Folds are the wave-like bends that<br>
are formed in the crustal rocks due to<br>
tangential compressive force resulting<br>
from horizontal movement. These<br>
bends are caused by endogenetic forces<br>
originating deep within the earth. The up<br>
folded rock strata which looks like an<br>
arch-like form are called anticlines while<br>
the down folded structure forming trough<br>
like structure is called syncline. The two<br>
sides of a fold are called limbs of the fold.<br>
<b>The plan that bisects the angle between</b><br>
two limbs of the anticline or middle limb<br>
of the syncline is called the axis of fold<br>
or axial plan. To understand fold, it is<br>
important to define the following terms.<br>
<b>Anticlines, is the up folded rock beds</b><br>
due to compressional forces. Syncline<br>
is the down folded rock beds due to<br>
compressive forces. Synclinorium<br>
represents a folded structure which<br>
includes an extensive syncline having<br>
numerous minor anticline and syncline.<br>
<b>When crustal rocks move towards</b><br>
each other they form different features<br>
resulting from folding namely simple<br>
fold, asymmetrical fold, over fold,<br>
recumbent fold, over thrust fold and<br>
geosyncline.<br>
<b>Simple fold:  The simple fold is also</b><br>
refered as symmetrical fold. This is<br>
formed when strata are bending into up<br>
fold (anticline) and down fold (syncline).<br>
The sides of the fold are called the limbs,<br>
caused by compressional forces (Figure<br>
2.64). Usually the limbs are similar in<br>
terms of slope.<br>
<b>Limb</b><br>
<b>Limb</b><br>
<b>Crest</b><br>
<b>Trough</b><br>
<b>Syncline</b><br>

<b>Figure 2.64: Simple fold</b><br>
<b>Asymmetrical fold: Asymmetrical fold is</b><br>
formed when compression continues and<br>
simple fold is changed into asymmetrical<br>
fold. One limb of asymmetrical fold is<br>
steeper than the other (Figure 2.65). A<br>
fold whose limbs are unequal in length,<br>
and axial plane is inclined, dipping in<br>
the same direction as that of the gently<br>
dipping limb. Degree of asymmetry<br>
is given as the ratio of length of short<br>
to that of long limb. Thus, the smaller<br>
the ratio the greater the degree of<br>
asymmetry. Folds whose axial plane is<br>
rotated clockwise from the normal to<br>
their enveloping surfaces are termed Z<br>
folds or dextral or negative asymmetrical<br>
while those in which the axial plane<br>

<b>Over fold: It is also known as</b><br>
overturned fold formed when the axial<br>
plane is inclined so that the fold limbs<br>
dip in the same direction, although not<br>
necessarily by the same amount. One<br>
limb is thus ‘overturned ‘or is pushed<br>
over the other (Figure 2.66).<br>

<b>Compressional force</b><br>

<b>Recumbent fold: This occurs when</b><br>
pressure continues and an over fold is<br>
pushed over another limb to a greater<br>
distance (Figure 2.67). A recumbent<br>
fold has an essentially horizontal axial<br>
plane. When the two limbs of a fold are<br>
essentially parallel to each other and<br>
thus approximately parallel to the axial<br>
plane, the fold is called recumbent.<br>
<b>Compressional force</b><br>
<b>Compressional force</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.67: Recumbent folds</b><br>
<b>Over thrust fold: The over thrust fold is</b><br>
also refered as Nappe fold. It is formed<br>
due to great pressure, the rocks feature<br>
and the limb fold is thrust forward over<br>
the other limb (Figure 2.68). Thrust<br>
faults with a very low angle of dip<br>
and a very large total displacement are<br>
called over thrusts or detachments. They<br>
are often found in intensely deformed<br>
mountain belts. Large thrust faults are<br>
characteristic of compressive tectonic<br>
plate boundaries.<br>
<b>Figure 2.68:  Over thrust fold</b><br>
<b>Geosyncline: This is a very large</b><br>
downfold produced as a result of<br>
subsidence of the earth’s crust (down<br>
warping) caused by the increased weight<br>
of the materials deposited on a particular<br>
place or warping movement of Earth<br>
(Figure 2.69).<br>

<b>Mountain building and classification</b><br>
<b>Mountain building or mountain</b><br>
formation is the geological processes that<br>
underlie formation of mountains. The<br>
responsible forces of mountain building<br>
are endogenic forces such as epirogenic<br>
forces, orogenic forces and volcanism.<br>
<b>These processes lead to formation of</b><br>
various types of mountains. A mountain<br>
is a large natural elevation of the earth’s<br>
surface rising abruptly to a given height<br>
from the surrounding level. For example<br>
<b>Mt. Kilimanjaro is approximately to</b><br>
have the height of about 5895 m.<br>
<b>Mountain building classification is based</b><br>
on several factors including height,<br>
location, mode of origin and period of<br>
origin.  On the basis of height there are<br>
low mountains which range between<br>
700-1000m, rough or middle mountains<br>
which range from 1000 to 1500m, and<br>
high mountains it is from 2000m and<br>
above. Basing on location there are<br>
coastal mountains with examples of<br>
<b>Appalachians, Rockies, and Alps. On</b><br>
the basis of location there is inland<br>
mountains which is formed on the<br>
adjacent land mass. For example, the<br>
<b>Ural mountain in Russia, Vosges and</b><br>
<b>Black forest block mountains. Coastal</b><br>
and inland mountains are also known as<br>
continental mountains.<br>
<b>Also, on the basis of the factor of location</b><br>
there is oceanic mountains most of these<br>
mountains are below water surface or<br>
below sea level such as mid-oceanic<br>
ridges. On the basis of mode of origin,<br>
there are tectonic mountains formed due<br>
to tectonic forces. Also, there are fold<br>
mountains formed due to compressional<br>
forces. They are further divided into<br>
young, mature and old fold mountains.<br>
<b>Another type is block mountains which</b><br>
is formed due to tensional forces.<br>
<b>Moreover, on the mode of origin there is</b><br>
dome mountains originated by magnetic<br>
infusing and upwarping of the crustal<br>
surface, and mountains of accumulation<br>
formed due to accumulation of volcanic<br>
materials. They are also called volcanic<br>
mountains.<br>
Furthermore, on the basis of period,<br>
there are pre-cambrium mountains,<br>
caledonian mountains, Hercynian<br>
mountains and Alpine mountains.<br>
<b>Pre-cambrium mountains include the</b><br>
<b>Laurencin orogeny which took place</b><br>
during the pre-Cambrian era, in the<br>
<b>Archezoic period 3 000 million years</b><br>
ago.  Most of the mountains formed were<br>
eroded. Good examples are the pen plain<br>
of Canadian shield and Akwapin Hills<br>
in Southern Ghana. Also, Caledonian<br>
orogeny took place in the Paleozoic era<br>
between 500-350 million years ago.<br>
<b>Usually it produces extensive mountain</b><br>
ranges which were subsequently eroded<br>
and changed by agents of denudation.<br>

<b>Its remnants include the rugged</b><br>
mountains of the Scottish Highlands<br>
and Scandinavia mountains.<br>
<b>Moreover, hercynian orogeny took</b><br>
place between the late Paleozoic and<br>
<b>Mesozoic era between 350-240 million</b><br>
years ago.  Most of these mountains were<br>
modified due to erosion by ice and water<br>
for example, the cape ranges of South<br>
<b>Africa, the Appalachian Mountains in</b><br>
<b>North America, the Urals Mountains in</b><br>
Russia and Welsh mountains in Britain.<br>
<b>Furthermore, alpine orogeny is the recent</b><br>
built mountain formed during Cenozoic<br>
era 300 million years ago. These are the<br>
highest and most impressive mountains<br>
in the world for example, Atlas Mountain,<br>
<b>Alps, Rockies, Himalaya and Andes</b><br>
mountain.  They developed through the<br>
collision of continental carrying plates.<br>
<b>Theories of mountain building</b><br>
<b>Plate tectonic theory: The theory</b><br>
explains the formation of mountains due<br>
to movement of plates in plate margins.<br>
<b>Most of the mountain formation occurs</b><br>
in plate zones where the plates converge,<br>
diverge or slide, for example, Fold<br>
mountains. Continental drift theory: This<br>
theory explains that when the continents<br>
were forming, the Indian continent<br>
drifted towards the Eurasia, the crust<br>
together with the sediments between<br>
were squeezed to form Fold Mountains,<br>
for example, Himalaya ranges. This is<br>
supported by the presence of Marine<br>
limestone rocks at the summit of Mount<br>
<b>Everest. Contraction theory: This</b><br>
explains that mountains were formed<br>
during the formation of the earth, when<br>
molten rocks were cooling.  The surface<br>
rocks cooled and contracted faster than<br>
those of interior.  Since the interior<br>
was cooling at a lower rate the surface<br>
rocks started to wrinkle in order to fit<br>
on the cooling and contracting rocks<br>
of the interior. Convectional current<br>
theory: The theory states that when the<br>
convectional currents in the mantle move<br>
horizontally, they cause a fractional pull<br>
to the crustal rocks making them move.<br>
<b>When the convectional currents are pulled</b><br>
towards each other hence fold mountains<br>
occur. Geosyncline theory: It is also<br>
known as geosynclinal oregen theory.<br>
<b>The theory was introduce by German</b><br>
geologist Kober. According to him, the<br>
mountain ranges are formed as a result of<br>
compression of geosyncline situated on<br>
the margin of forelands of ancient rigid<br>
land masses. He refered these has border<br>
ranges. Denudation theory: This explains<br>
that, some mountains are formed when<br>
resistant mass of rock remain standing<br>
after denudation has taken away the<br>
surrounding soft rocks.  This explains<br>
the formation of residual mountains like<br>
Sekenke hill in Singida-Tanzania.<br>
<b>Fold Mountains</b><br>
<b>Fold Mountains are the result of folding</b><br>
of the earth’s crust due to the influence<br>
of compressional forces. Fold Mountains<br>
are classified into nature of fold and<br>
period of origin. Based on the nature of<br>
origin, there are simple fold mountains<br>
and complex fold mountains. Simple<br>
fold mountains are characterized by<br>
well-developed system of anticline and<br>
syncline pattern. Complex fold mountains<br>
consist of very complex structure of<br>

folds caused by powerful compressional<br>
forces such as recumbent folds. There<br>
is also Priceline (Centroline) fold. It<br>
is a form of anticline usually of small<br>
dimension in which the limbs have been<br>
forced to pitch (steepen) vertically along<br>
the axis. It is like an elongated dome.<br>
<b>The period of mountain building is</b><br>
distinguished as an orogeny.  Fold<br>
mountain building process as shown<br>
in Figure 2.70 is known as Orogenesis.<br>
<b>There have been several mountains</b><br>

old fold mountains and new folded<br>
mountains. The old folded mountains<br>
originated before tertiary period 3 000<br>
milions years ago such as Caledonian<br>
and Hercynian. The mountains have<br>
been so greatly eroded. Example of these<br>
mountains include Aravallis. The alpine<br>
folded mountains of Tertiary period are<br>
grouped under the category of new folded<br>
mountains formed during Cenozoic era<br>
300 million years ago for example,<br>
Rockies, Andes, Alps, Himalayas.<br>

is forced upwards<br>

<b>Distribution of Fold Mountains</b><br>
The old fold mountains were formed during the Paleozoic and Mesozoic eras.<br>
Most of these are remnants (roots) of old fold mountains. These old fold mountains<br>
are found in North America such as Appalachian mountain and Urals and Cape<br>
Ranges of South Africa.<br>
<b>Distribution of young fold mountains</b><br>
The distribution patterns of Fold Mountains over the globe are generally found<br>
along the margins of the continents either in north-south direction or east west<br>
direction. For example, the alpine chains, lie along the continental margin, formed<br>
during Cenozoic era. Most fold mountains on the earth’s surface are young fold<br>
mountains belt. Young Fold Mountain belts consist of two belts namely; Circum-<br>
Pacific System: Rings the Pacific Ocean (North and South) America based on<br>
the continents for example, Andes, Rockies and Cordilleran ranges.  In Western<br>
part of the Pacific Ocean, the belts take form of Islands for example, Japan and<br>

Philippines. Eurasian – Indonesian Belt: Rings the West of Atlas mountain to<br>
East of Iran and Himalaya ranges then continues to South Eastern Asia and<br>
Indonesia where meet the circum- pacific belt. The belt includes Alps, Caucasus<br>
and Himalayas mountains.<br>

and possible effects of that disaster to the surrounding community.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Three</b><br>
<b>Exogenic processes of</b><br>
the Earth<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Some of the landforms seen on the earth’s surface are the resultant features of<br>
exogenic (external) processes of the Earth which include denudation and deposition.<br>
In this chapter, you will learn about weathering and mass wasting as geographical<br>
processes acting on the earth’s surface and their resultant features. Also, you will<br>
learn about the actions of wind and water in desert, glaciation, river, and waves<br>
along the cost, and their resulting features. The competencies obtained from this<br>
<b>chapter will enable you to undertake various activities that promote environmental</b><br>
conservation and protect the physical environment of the Earth from negative<br>
effects of denudation and deposition. Also, you will be able to use features formed<br>
through denudation and deposition in promoting tourism sector in Tanzania.<br>
<b>The concept of exogenic processes of the Earth</b><br>
The surface of the earth’s crust is under constant sculpturing due to multiple<br>
geomorphic agents which lead into denudation and deposition. The sculpturing<br>
of the earth’s crust entails the process, deformation stages and resultant features.<br>
The mature soil, regolith, and various forms of topographical landscapes seen<br>
today are the results of the geomorphological processes which took place for a<br>
long period of time. These geomorphological processes are termed as exogenic<br>
(external) forces such as weathering, mass wasting, erosion and deposition. Figure<br>
<b>3.1 is a schematic representation of exogenic processes of the Earth.</b><br>

<b>Weathering</b><br>
<b>Weathering refers to the process of</b><br>
weakening, breaking up, disintegration<br>
and decomposition of rocks into small<br>
particles that forms the surface of<br>
the earth. The surface rocks are often<br>
exposed to elements of weather such as<br>
temperature, rainfall, and humidity which<br>
acts as agents of weathering. Weathering<br>
as a geomorphological mechanism and<br>
process, normally changes hard rocks<br>
into fine and soft residuals (the parent<br>
matter of the soil). Weathering does<br>
not produce new landforms because<br>
weathered materials stay in situ as<br>
weathered rock or debris. For this<br>
reason, weathering is often described<br>
as the process of preparation of rock<br>
materials for transportation by the agents<br>
of erosion including water, glacier,<br>
waves and winds. All rocks, whether<br>
soft or hard when exposed on the surface<br>
of the earth, they are subjected to change<br>
over time due to either pressure release,<br>
temperature change or by both. Most of<br>
rocks and minerals within the earth’s<br>
crust are subjected to changes due to<br>
temperature and pressure that greatly<br>
differ to that of the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>This happens because the physical and</b><br>
chemical nature of the materials in the<br>
earth’s surface are not stable with the<br>
conditions on the earth’s surface. Hence<br>
they are easily attacked, decomposed,<br>
and eroded by various physical and<br>
chemical processes. Weathering does<br>
not involve removal of weathered rocks.<br>
<b>Instead the removal of weathered rocks</b><br>
are caused by forces of weather which<br>
include rain action and wind. Weathering<br>
can also be influenced by organisms, a<br>
process known as biological (biotic)<br>
weathering. Weathering is a combination<br>
of processes of destruction and synthesis.<br>
<b>The main mechanisms which often take</b><br>
place simultaneously are mechanical<br>
(physical), chemical and biological<br>
weathering.<br>
<b>Mechanical weathering</b><br>
<b>Mechanical weathering is also known</b><br>
as physical weathering. It refers to<br>
the weakening, breaking down and<br>
disintegration of rocks without any<br>
chemical changes of rock. Usually, it<br>
involves the breaking down of rocks into<br>
smaller fragments. It is common in desert<br>
areas, high mountains and cold regions.<br>
<b>The forces that strain and rupture the</b><br>
rock can either originate within the rock<br>
or applied externally. The following are<br>
different forms of physical weathering.<br>
<b>Thermal expansion: The thermal</b><br>
expansion is also known as insolation.<br>
<b>It is often common in arid  and semi</b><br>
arid areas where there is high daily<br>
range of temperature. When an arid area<br>
experiences high day temperature and<br>
low night temperature, the rocks’ surface<br>
heats and cools alternately. Since rocks<br>
are poor heat conductors, the absorbed<br>
heat is confined to the inner part of the<br>
rocks. Therefore, the outer parts of the<br>
rocks keep expanding during day and<br>
cooling during night. The surface of the<br>
rock expands more than the interior part<br>
and eventually cause cracks to appear<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   118</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:01<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
119<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
extending to a greater depth into the rock. As a result, the outer layer of the rock<br>
pulls away from the layer beneath ending into rupture. The breaking of the outer<br>
layer of the rock is in form of granular disintegration or block disintegration. The<br>
disintegrated rock can peel off as a flat or curved plate of rock from the main rock.<br>
This process is called exfoliation. The exfoliation process occurs in a well-jointed<br>
rock mostly in the desert, semi-desert and monsoon regions. Exfoliation causes<br>
falling of separated plates of rock resulting into the formation of isolated masses of<br>
round-shaped rocks called exfoliation domes (Figure 3.2). Separated fragments of<br>
rocks continue to disintegrate into smaller pieces called talus or screes which often<br>
collects at the base<br>
of exfoliation dome.<br>
<b>The exfoliation dome</b><br>
occurs in desert, semi<br>
desert and monsoon<br>
regions. Examples<br>
of this type of<br>
weathering can be<br>
witnessed in part of<br>
the Sahara Desert in<br>
Egypt, Sinai and Kalahari deserts.<br>
<b>However, when coarse grained rock</b><br>
particles of the granular rocks expand<br>
and contract they may breakdown<br>
into separate smaller rock particles.<br>
<b>The grained rock particles accumulate</b><br>
down around the rock forming a large<br>
mass of coarsely grained igneous rock<br>
which is dome-shaped. This weathering<br>
process is called block disintegration.<br>
<b>The disintegration of rocks that contain</b><br>
a variety of minerals is called granular<br>
disintegration<br>
<b>Frost action: This occurs when water</b><br>
penetrates through cracks and joints of<br>
rocks and freezes. When water freezes<br>
it expands. The increased expansion<br>
of frozen water exerts pressure on<br>
the sides of the cracks in such a way<br>
that the repeated freezing and melting<br>
(thawing) of water keeps widening and<br>
deepening the cracks. Alternate freezing<br>
and thawing processes over time will<br>
slowly cause the rock outcrops to break<br>
down into angular blocks which keep<br>
breaking into smaller fragments (Figure<br>
3.3). Frost action is common in areas<br>
where temperature falls below 0o C and<br>
vegetation cover is limited.  Such action<br>
is most active in temperate regions where<br>
freezing and thawing is frequently taking<br>
place and in tropics which have high<br>
temperature like deserts. Also, it takes<br>
place in the arctic region but on a small<br>
scale because in this region water is<br>
frozen for several months in a year as<br>
compared to the period of melting.<br>
<b>Figure 3.2: Exfoliation dome with mounds of screes</b><br>
screes<br>

in the rock<br>
joints<br>

water cause<br>
expansion<br>
of rock<br>
joints<br>
<b>Figure 3.3: Weathering by frost action</b><br>
<b>Salt crystallization: This is common</b><br>
along the coast and in desert areas. It<br>
occurs when saline water occupies the<br>
pore spaces in the coastal rocks and on<br>
evaporation due to high temperature,<br>
salt crystals are likely to be formed.<br>
<b>Further accumulation and hardening</b><br>
of the salt exerts pressure upon the<br>
rocks causing them to fracture and<br>
disintegrate (Figure 3.4). Basically,<br>
the transformation from liquid to solid<br>
crystalline form produces a volumetric<br>
change, which in turn causes the<br>
necessary<br>
mechanical<br>
action<br>
for<br>
the rupturing of the rock. Also, the<br>
process can occur in desert areas where<br>
capillary action draws water from the<br>
ground to the surface, and where the<br>
rock is sandstone. Individual particles<br>
of sand are broken off by granular<br>
disintegration.<br>
<b>Figure 3.4: Weathered rock by salt crystallization</b><br>

Pressure release: This is also refered as unloading process. Many rocks,<br>
especially intrusive igneous rocks experienced exertion of considerable pressure<br>
for millions of years. The confining pressure strengthens the rocks. On the other<br>
hand, the process of erosion however, removes the overlying rock material, in<br>
such a way that with time the intrusive rocks are exposed and the pressure on<br>
them unloaded paving way to action of other agents and processes. The outer<br>
parts of the rocks then tend to expand (Figure 3.5). Over time, the sheets of rock<br>
break away on the exposed parts along the fractures, a process called exfoliation.<br>
On the other hand, exfoliation due to pressure release is known as ‘sheeting’.<br>
<b>Figure 3.5: Weathering by pressure release</b><br>
<b>Alternate wetting and drying: The</b><br>
absorption of water by surface rocks<br>
cause them to swell. This causes<br>
expansion, and on the other hand,<br>
when they dry out their outer layers<br>
shrink. The contraction of some<br>
rocks following loss of absorbed<br>
water especially in tropical regions<br>
is a rapid process. The repeatedly<br>
wetting and drying weakens the rocks<br>
ending into cracking and breaking<br>
into smaller fragments. For example,<br>
shale crumbles into small-elongated<br>
fragments when dry and when clay<br>
dries, it shrinks and its surface cracks.<br>
<b>This type of weathering occurs in</b><br>
several parts of west Africa particularly<br>
on coastal rocks which are alternately<br>
wetted and dried with the rise and fall<br>
of the tide.<br>
<b>Chemical weathering</b><br>
<b>Chemical weathering is the breakup</b><br>
of rocks by being decomposed by<br>
chemical reaction. The chemical reaction<br>
involves the substances of water, certain<br>
atmospheric gases, and certain minerals<br>
of rocks. When water is charged by<br>
different gases like oxygen and carbon<br>
dioxide that penetrates the rocks and<br>
make contained minerals decomposed to<br>
the ultimate gradual break apart of rocks.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   121</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:02<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
122<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>By chemical weathering; the chemical</b><br>
nature of the rock is transformed from<br>
its original mineral compounds into<br>
secondary (new) mineral compounds<br>
which are susceptible to decomposition.<br>
<b>This type of weathering involves</b><br>
decomposition and transformation of<br>
rock and minerals exposed to water<br>
and air into new chemical products.<br>
<b>A mineral that is crystallized deep</b><br>
underground from a water deficient<br>
zone may eventually be exposed at the<br>
earth’s surface where it can react with the<br>
abundant water to form new mineral. The<br>
newly formed minerals are weathering<br>
products.  The chemical weathering<br>
usually takes place on the exposed<br>
rock surface, but it can also take place<br>
inside the rock in case water can enter<br>
via pores, joints or cracks. Temperature<br>
is a very important factor as it speeds<br>
up the chemical reactions. Also, the<br>
composition and strength of soil water,<br>
minerals dissolved in the soil water and<br>
the soil bacteria are determinants of the<br>
rate of rock disintegration. Chemical<br>
weathering has several effects on rocks<br>
including softening of rocks (minerals)<br>
that makes them more susceptible to<br>
erosive agents than the original. Also,<br>
it produces secondary minerals from<br>
the original minerals that are soluble in<br>
water and which can be removed by<br>
running water. Furthermore, it produces<br>
chemical compounds that can take up<br>
greater volume than the original minerals<br>
hence making them weak and susceptible<br>
to breakage. Depending on the nature<br>
of minerals present in the rocks and<br>
atmospheric gases reacting with water,<br>
chemical weathering takes place through<br>
a number of different processes which<br>
produce different landforms (Figure<br>
3.6). The main five chemical weathering<br>
processes include; carbonation,<br>
hydration, oxidation, hydrolysis, and<br>
solution.<br>
<b>Carbonation: It is a chemical weathering</b><br>
process by which the carbonic acid<br>
formed by the reaction between water<br>
and carbondioxide gas, reacts with<br>
rocks of calcium carbonate minerals.<br>
<b>The reaction makes calcium carbonate</b><br>
converted into calcium bicarbonate,<br>
which is readily removed as it dissolves<br>
in water. The process involves carbonic<br>
acid (a weak solution of carbon dioxide<br>
and water) to change calcium carbonate<br>
into calcium bicarbonate which is easily<br>
removed in solution by ground water. For<br>
example, as rain falls, it dissolves small<br>
amounts of carbon dioxide from the air,<br>
forming a weak carbonic acid that is able<br>
to dissolve many insoluble limestone<br>
minerals into soluble ones. It dissolves<br>
even more carbonates as it seeps through<br>
the soil, hence the exposed surface<br>
rocks that contain calcium carbonate<br>
are readily decomposed when they react<br>
with weak carbonic acid.<br>
<b>CaCO3 + H2O+ CO2</b><br>
<b>Ca (HCO3)2</b><br>
(Calcium<br>
(Calcium<br>
carbonates)<br>
bicarbonate)<br>
<b>Carbonation mostly occurs in areas rich</b><br>
of limestone rocks which is primarily<br>
composed of calcite.<br>

<b>Hydration: Hydration is a process in</b><br>
which certain minerals absorb water and<br>
expand causing fracturing of the rocks.  It<br>
causes volumetric changes in minerals,<br>
which set up strains in the rock, in which<br>
water molecules become attached to<br>
other substances and then the added<br>
water causes the original substance to<br>
swell without any change in its chemical<br>
composition. This swelling process<br>
weakens the rock mass and is usually<br>
sufficient to produce some fracturing.<br>
Some of the rocks, for example,<br>
gypsum also known as calcium sulphate<br>
dehydrated are the results of water that<br>
has been absorbed by calcium sulphate.<br>
<b>The following equation demonstrates the</b><br>
hydration process.<br>
<b>CaSO4       +  2H2O</b><br>
<b>CaSO4.2H2O</b><br>
(Sulphate)      (water)          (gypsum)<br>
<b>Gypsum is used as a fertilizer and the</b><br>
main constituent in many forms of plaster<br>
blackboard and dry wall. Hydration is in<br>
fact a physical-chemical process as the<br>
rocks may swell and exert pressure as<br>
well as change their chemical structure.<br>
<b>This process is most effective in dry and</b><br>
wet seasons.<br>
<b>Oxidation: This is the process which</b><br>
involves a reaction between metallic<br>
ions such as calcium, magnesium or<br>
iron and oxygen to form oxides. Since<br>
oxygen is freely available near the earth’s<br>
surface, it may react with minerals to<br>
change the oxidation state of an ion.<br>
3FesiO3  +  1/2O2<br>
2Fe3O4  +  3SiO2<br>
(Pyroxine)  (Oxygen)  (Magnetic)   (Quartz)<br>
<b>This is more common in iron (Fe)</b><br>
bearing minerals, since iron can have<br>
several oxidation states these are Fe+,<br>
<b>Fe2+ and Fe3+. The new minerals formed</b><br>
by oxidation are easily attacked by other<br>
weathering processes. For example,<br>
oxidation of the iron completely breaks<br>
down rock structures where iron and<br>
silicate are joined.<br>
<b>Hydrolysis: This is the process whereby</b><br>
hydrogen in the water combines with<br>
certain metal ions in a mineral to form<br>
different chemical compounds. It occurs<br>
when acidic water reacts with rock-<br>
forming minerals such as feldspar and<br>
breaks it down to produce clay and salts<br>
that are removed in solution.<br>
4KALSi3O8   +  4H+   + 2H2O   + CO2<br>
4K+   +    Al4Si4O10 (OH)8   +  4SiO2<br>
(Orthoclase)  (Hydrogen  (Water	) (Carbon     (Potasium       (Clay mineral)       (Quartz)<br>
ion)<br>
dioxide)     ion)<br>
The rate of hydrolysis depends on the amount of H+ ions which in turn depends<br>
upon the composition of air and water in the soil, the activity of organisms, the<br>
presence of organic and cation exchange. Hydrolysis is regarded as a physiochemical<br>
process as rocks may swell and exert pressure as well as change chemical structure.<br>
Solution: This is a process in which the minerals in the rocks directly dissolve<br>
in water without their chemical composition being altered, such that it is a direct<br>

chemical change. This is the process in<br>
which some soluble mineral like salt<br>
(rock salt) dissolve in water naturally<br>
leading to the disappearance of the rock.<br>
<b>Biological weathering</b><br>
<b>This refers to the weathering process</b><br>
brought by either animals or plants<br>
action. Biological weathering is also<br>
known as organic or biotic weathering.<br>
<b>Biological weathering is divided into</b><br>
three types namely faunal, flora, and<br>
anthropogenic weathering. Depending<br>
on the means of occurrence it can either<br>
be mechanical or chemical weathering<br>
process.<br>
<b>Flora weathering: This is caused by</b><br>
plants. Mechanical disintegration of<br>
rocks occurs due to the penetration of<br>
roots. As the roots penetrate due to growth<br>
of plants, a powerful force widens the<br>
cracks hence breaking rocks (Figure 3.6).<br>
<b>Decaying vegetation produces organic</b><br>
acids which cause further breaking down<br>
of minerals. Plant roots release chemical<br>
substance like acid which then breaks<br>
down the rocks.<br>
<b>Joints</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.6: Mechanical disintegration of</b><br>
rocks by plants<br>
<b>Faunal weathering: It is caused by</b><br>
animals. Animals such as rabbit and<br>
rats help to loosen and weaken the soil<br>
and rock. Burrowing animals move rock<br>
fragments and sediments on the earth’s<br>
surface hence aid the disintegration of<br>
rocks. Chemical disintegration occurs<br>
due to the presence of bacteria and<br>
burrowing animals in rocks. When<br>
bacteria and burrowing animals excrete<br>
in the rocks or when they die and decay<br>
they release chemical substances in the<br>
rocks hence weaken them and easily<br>
disintegrate.<br>
<b>Anthropogenic weathering: This is caused</b><br>
by human activities. Human activities<br>
such as construction of various structures<br>
like roads, railway lines and buildings<br>
leads to breaking down of rocks.  Also,<br>
agricultural activities such as cultivating<br>
of the land and mining activities break<br>
rocks physically and aid the process<br>
of pressure release. Recently, human<br>
beings have become the most powerful<br>
weathering agent because of further<br>
development in modern technologies.<br>
<b>Human activities also contribute through</b><br>
use of agrochemicals such as fertilizers<br>
and pesticides in agricultural activities<br>
which facilitate chemical disintegration<br>
of the rocks.<br>
<b>The interdependence of mechanical</b><br>
and chemical weathering<br>
<b>The essential difference between chemical</b><br>
and mechanical (physical) weathering is<br>
that, in physical weathering, there is no<br>
actual change involved in the nature of<br>

125<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
rock. The rock is weathered into smaller<br>
fragments without occurrence of any<br>
chemical change. Chemical weathering<br>
reaches greater depths of chemical<br>
change of the rock which result in the<br>
disintegration/weakening of the rock.<br>
<b>However, it should be noted that both</b><br>
types of weathering operate together and<br>
are usually complementary although in a<br>
given area, one may be more dominant<br>
than the other. Chemical decomposition<br>
weakens the rocks, opening the way for<br>
physical disintegration, while fracturing<br>
(physical weathering) allows deeper<br>
penetration of chemical action.<br>

<b>Importance of weathering</b><br>
<b>The process of weathering is a primary</b><br>
source of sediments for erosion and<br>
deposition. A number of sedimentary<br>
rocks are composed of particles that have<br>
been weathered, eroded, transported and<br>
finally deposited. Weathering process<br>
among other things, contributes to the<br>
formation of soils such as clay soil which<br>
is suitable for brick making. Weathering<br>
contributes to the soil formation by<br>
providing mineral particles like sand,<br>
silt and clay. The rocks and mineral<br>
particles from weathering process supply<br>
important nutrients for plant uptake and<br>
growth. Through weathering process<br>
ions from the terrestrial environment are<br>
transported to the sea through leaching<br>
and water run-offs and in turn contribute<br>
to the salinity of seas. Furthermore,<br>
weathering processes loosen the rocks<br>
thereby making rocks and the associated<br>
products readily available for human<br>
exploitations for various developmental<br>
purposes. The breakdown of rocks into<br>
smaller particles contributes to the soil<br>
formation which in turn supports various<br>
life forms on earth. The depth of soil<br>
horizon extends as far the degree to which<br>
the rocks have undergone weathering.<br>
<b>Some of the generated features are useful</b><br>
for tourist activities such as the Bismarck<br>
rock in Mwanza, Tanzania.<br>
<b>Factors affecting weathering</b><br>
Weathering is affected by several factors.<br>
<b>The primary factors are: climate, physical</b><br>
properties, mineral composition and rock<br>
type. Climate determines the kind and<br>

126<br>

the rate of weathering. For example,<br>
climate under low rainfall conditions,<br>
mechanical weathering is more dominant<br>
as compared to chemical weathering<br>
processes. Availability of high moisture<br>
encourages both chemical and physical<br>
weathering thereby generating new<br>
minerals and soluble products. The rate of<br>
weathering is relatively higher in wet and<br>
hot climates as compared to dry climates.<br>
<b>The physical characteristics of rocks</b><br>
in terms of particle size, hardness,<br>
degree of cementation determine the<br>
rate of weathering when subjected to<br>
agents. For example, rocks composed of<br>
coarse fragments (for example, granite)<br>
are easily weathered physically than<br>
chemically. In contrast, rocks containing<br>
fine fragments (for example, basalt) are<br>
susceptible to chemical weathering as<br>
compared to physical weathering. Hard<br>
and highly cemented rocks disintegrate<br>
slowly as compared to weak and non-<br>
cemented rocks. In case of mineral<br>
composition, rocks composed of highly<br>
weatherable minerals are likely to be<br>
weathered more easily compared to<br>
rocks with resistant rock minerals. For<br>
example, biotite-bearing minerals are<br>
weathered more rapidly compared to the<br>
biotite-free rocks like basalt.<br>
<b>With respect to rock type, coarse</b><br>
grained rocks are weathered rapidly<br>
as compared to the fine-grained rocks.<br>
<b>The inter-granular surface increases with</b><br>
decrease in grain size, hence demanding<br>
much more energy to disintegrate the<br>
fine-grained rocks. Whereas, in case<br>
of stratification, the highly stratified<br>
rocks like lignite weather more easily<br>
as compared to the non-stratified rocks<br>
like granite. Differently, the orientation<br>
and degree of cementation of rocks<br>
has an implication on determining<br>
weathering process. For example, the<br>
horizontally, stratified rocks are highly<br>
weathered compared to the vertically<br>
aligned stratification.  For example, the<br>
horizontally stratified shales are less<br>
weathered and will result into shallow<br>
soils than the vertically stratified shales.<br>
<b>Also, sedimentary rocks are weathered</b><br>
more rapidly than igneous rocks. This<br>
is due to the fact that sedimentary rocks<br>
bear a combination of non-clay and clay<br>
minerals, and rocks with a considerable<br>
amount of clay size are likely to break<br>
easily as compared to rocks with low<br>
clay content. Colour of the rock, rock<br>
with dark or black coloured minerals<br>
like olivine heat more quickly hence<br>
vulnerable to rapid rate of disintegration,<br>
wheres rock with light minerals such as<br>
quartz and white coloured minerals like<br>
limestone heat very slowly, hence slow<br>
rate of weathering.<br>
<b>Other factors that influence weathering</b><br>
include gradient, whereby slope in which<br>
the weathered material is situated (that is<br>
steep slopes versus gentle slopes) which<br>
may influence retention of the weathered<br>
material or result into being washed away<br>
of the weathering products. Organisms<br>
influence weathering process based on<br>
their physical and chemical interactions<br>

with rocks. Bedding planes, joints,<br>
and fractures of the rocks are likely to<br>
influence the entrance of water hence<br>
subject the rocks into weathering process.<br>
<b>Both plants and animals are responsible</b><br>
in speeding up the rate of weathering.<br>
<b>Man through different activities such</b><br>
as agriculture, construction and mining<br>
weakens rock structure leading to<br>
weathering. Also, small organisms such<br>
as worms, rabbits and termites which live<br>
and feed in the soil, they release waste<br>
products with chemicals which accelerate<br>
weathering process. Moreover, plants<br>
through penetration of roots accelerate<br>
weathering process leading to weakening<br>
and disintegration of rocks. Importantly,<br>
time is crucial in explaining the rate at<br>
which the rock is weathered. Weathering<br>
can either be rapid or extremely slow.<br>
<b>Thus the time can explain when and at</b><br>
what rate weathering is likely to occur<br>
depending on all the factors covered in<br>
this section.<br>

act as a precursor to soil erosion?<br>

interdependence nature between<br>
physical and chemical weathering.<br>

process be regarded as a life<br>
support on earth?<br>

of a rock towards weathering<br>

weathering process.<br>

weathering processes.<br>
<b>Mass wasting</b><br>
<b>Mass wasting is a movement of rock</b><br>
materials and /or soil down the slope<br>
primarily under the influence of gravity.<br>
<b>It includes creeping, flowing, sliding or</b><br>
falling of rocks and weathered materials<br>
downhill. Although water is not directly<br>
involved in transportation of the mass<br>
wasting materials, it is very important<br>
in this process as it acts as a lubricant<br>
and also it adds weight.<br>
<b>Factors influencing mass wasting</b><br>
<b>There are several factors which play part</b><br>
in triggering mass wasting. Normally,<br>
the factors operate collectively rather<br>
than in isolation. The important factors<br>
include: nature and weight of the material<br>
involved, temperature, rainfall, amount<br>
of water, vegetation cover of an area, the<br>
angle of slope, anthropogenic activities<br>

09/03/2022   17:02<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
128<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
and tectonic movement. Nature and<br>
weight of the materials may in various<br>
ways increase the susceptibility to mass<br>
wasting. For example, the deep or thin<br>
layered weather-able rocks can greatly<br>
result into rapid mass wasting. Likewise,<br>
thin beds increase the chances of mass<br>
wasting as there are more bedding<br>
planes over which movement can occur.<br>
<b>Massive overlying weak rocks like clay</b><br>
or shale can easily slide as compared<br>
to areas covered by sand. In addition,<br>
the large rocks have greater chance to<br>
easily overcome by gravity as compared<br>
to fine weathered materials. The angle<br>
of slope can variably influence mass<br>
wasting. For example, the steeper the<br>
slope, the faster the movement of the<br>
materials. This is because gravity has<br>
much effect with increasing angle of<br>
the slope. Contrary, the effect of gravity<br>
is less active with decreased angle of<br>
slope as the case of gentle slope. Climate<br>
plays a crucialrole, through rainfall<br>
and temperature. Through regarding to<br>
rainfall, mass wasting depends on the<br>
nature and amount of rainfall received.<br>
<b>Areas experiencing heavy rainfall are</b><br>
much more susceptible to massive<br>
landslides over the steep slopes. In<br>
contrary, low rainfall have less effect<br>
on saturating the material and hence<br>
reducing the intensity of landslides. On<br>
the other hand, freezing and thawing<br>
encourages mass wasting as well. In<br>
dry climate, the materials may be loose<br>
but they lack water hence the resultant<br>
movement is likely to be slow.<br>
<b>The amount of water has an effect</b><br>
to mass wasting depending on the<br>
saturation of the materials. The more<br>
saturated the material, the more it is<br>
likely to move fast particularly in a<br>
steep slope. A saturated material moves<br>
more rapidly than the unsaturated<br>
ones. The reason behind is that water<br>
increases the weight of the material and<br>
at the same time reduces the cohesion<br>
between particles of the materials. On<br>
the other hand, water acts as a lubricant<br>
along the bedding plane thus facilitating<br>
the<br>
gravity-initiated<br>
movements.<br>
<b>The availability of vegetation in an</b><br>
area like grasses, shrubs, and large<br>
trees, their roots helps to hold rock<br>
materials together and thus stabilizing<br>
the earth’s surface against movement.<br>
<b>In this case the bare surfaces have a</b><br>
greater chance of encountering mass<br>
wasting as compared to the areas rich<br>
in vegetation. However, presence of<br>
dense vegetation cover in wet regions<br>
fuels the rate of infiltration into the soil<br>
and rocks beneath the surfaces. Due<br>
to this the rate of saturation to rock<br>
materials is fastened hence triggering<br>
mass wasting in an area. Anthropogenic<br>
activities like cultivation, grazing,<br>
mining, clearing vegetation, and road<br>
constructions are greatly affecting<br>
the stability of the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>Vibrations from moving trains and</b><br>
vehicles can cause tremors and<br>
explosion as well can cause shaking<br>
of the ground and ultimately lead to<br>
movement of some unconsolidated<br>

earth material downslope. The tectonic<br>
movements generated by earthquake<br>
and/or volcanic activities normally<br>
generate the wide spread vibrations<br>
which often trigger the movement of<br>
materials such as landslides.  The forces<br>
generated by these tectonic events have<br>
far reaching effects including rock fall<br>
in steep slopes and landslides.<br>
<b>Types of mass wasting</b><br>
<b>There are mainly two categories of mass</b><br>
movement namely slow movement and<br>
rapid movement. These categories are<br>
based on speed of movement, amount<br>
of moisture, angle of slope and nature<br>
of material.<br>
<b>Slow mass wasting</b><br>
<b>This involves a steady movement of</b><br>
earth’s materials downhill. The slow<br>
movement is almost imperceptible<br>
and persists over a long period of<br>
time. The common types of slow mass<br>
movements include; soil creep, talus<br>
creep, solifluction and soil flow.<br>
<b>Soil creep: Soil creep refers to the</b><br>
very slowest downhill movement of<br>
unconsolidated rock fragments and soil<br>
often in very gentle slopes depending<br>
on the repeatedly expansion and<br>
contraction of soil particles in wet and<br>
dry periods (Figure 3.7). It is difficult<br>
to measure soil creep as it takes place<br>
at a rate of less than one centimetre a<br>
year. When wet, soil particles increase<br>
in size and weight, and expand at<br>
right angles. This happens when the<br>
soil dries out, it contracts vertically<br>
resulting to slow movement of the soil<br>
down the slope. The process can also<br>
occur in areas experiencing alternate<br>
freezing<br>
and<br>
thawing<br>
conditions.<br>
<b>Movements of large groups of animals</b><br>
on slopping land and burrowing<br>
animals can facilitate soil creep. Soil<br>
creep can be recognized by observing<br>
bending trunks of trees, broken fences,<br>
tilted electricity and telegraphic poles.<br>
<b>Unconsolidated</b><br>
rocks and soil<br>
<b>Figure 3.7: Soil creep</b><br>
<b>Talus creep: Talus creep involves a</b><br>
very slow downslope movement of the<br>
layers of angular loose rock fragments,<br>
which typically occupies the foot of the<br>
escarpment. Deposits of rock fragments<br>
detached from cliffs or mountain slopes<br>
are piled up at their bases (Figure 3.8).<br>
<b>A talus is a common geologic feature</b><br>
in regions of high cliffs. The angle of<br>
slope of a talus is rarely greater than<br>
40°. The constant weathering to which<br>
a talus is subjected, which breaks the<br>
rock fragments into finer pieces, and<br>
the impact of new material being added<br>
from above give the base of the talus<br>
a tendency to creep and slide. The<br>

term talus is often used to refer to the<br>
fragments themselves.<br>
<b>Scar</b><br>
<b>Talus</b><br>

<b>Solifluction: Solifluction is a slightly</b><br>
faster movement than soil creep. The<br>
process involves movement of frozen<br>
soil together with rock fragments<br>
down the slope. The movement usually<br>
averages between 5 centimetre and<br>
1 metre in a year.  Solifluction often<br>
takes place under periglacial conditions<br>
where vegetation cover is limited (Figure<br>
3.9). During the winter season both the<br>
bedrock and regolith are frozen.  In<br>
summer, the surface layer thaws but the<br>
underlying layer remains frozen and acts<br>
like an impermeable rock.  As the melt<br>
water cannot infiltrate downwards and<br>
the temperature are too low for much<br>
effective evaporation, any top soil will<br>
soon become saturated and will flow<br>
as an active layer over the frozen sub-<br>
soil and rock.  This process results into<br>
solifluction sheets or lobes rounded<br>
tongue like feature reaching up 50 m<br>
in width and had a mixture of sand and<br>
clay formed in valley and at the foot of<br>
sea cliffs.<br>
<b>Solifluction lobe</b><br>
<b>Saturated</b><br>
material<br>
<b>Figure 3.9: Solifluction</b><br>
<b>Soil flow: This is a steady movement</b><br>
of soil in plastic form down the gentle<br>
sloped landscape after being much<br>
saturated with water. It is readily occurs<br>
in clay soil of which readily change into<br>
plasticity especially in wet season.<br>
<b>Rapid mass wasting</b><br>
<b>This is a rapid process of downfall of</b><br>
materials of rock to the lowland. It is a<br>
large scale movement which acts very<br>
suddenly. The movement is basically<br>
gravitational. The steeper the slope,<br>
the more rapid movement will be. The<br>
presence of water is a contributory factor<br>
for rapid mass wasting. If the debris is<br>
dry, it will slide or fall as loose mass<br>
while with water, the movement is in<br>
the nature of flow. The common types of<br>
rapid movements include; landslide. A<br>
rapid movement on a slope downwards<br>
which involves movements like mudflow,<br>
rock slump, rock fall and rock slide, and<br>
avalanche provide basic categories of<br>
rapid mass wasting.<br>

<b>Landslide: Landslide refers to the rapid</b><br>
movement of rock materials in form of<br>
rock slump, rock slide and rock fall It<br>
is one of the natural hazards incidents.<br>
(a)	 Mud flow<br>
<b>Mudflow occurs on a steep slope. It</b><br>
is a more rapid movement of super<br>
saturated sediments exceeding one<br>
kilometer per hour. The movement<br>
occurs after a period of heavy rain.<br>
<b>When both volume and weight are</b><br>
added to the soil giving it a higher water<br>
content when earth flows (Figure 3.10).<br>
<b>It is one among the most fluidly super –</b><br>
saturated of earthly materials consist of<br>
wetted soil combined with fine boulders<br>
and regolith moving down the slope in<br>
speed exceeding 1 km per hour or few<br>
minutes. Depending on the scale, speed<br>
and nature of the topography, mudflow<br>
might be catastrophic. It can bury and<br>
destroy human settlements, hectors of<br>
crop land and causing mass causalities.<br>
<b>Heavy and down pouring of rain in</b><br>
sloped, bare land and less compacted<br>
and porous soil may become the prone<br>
site for mudflow.<br>
<b>Moving mud</b><br>
<b>Original site of mud</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.10: Mud flow</b><br>
(b) 	Rock slump<br>
<b>A slump is a form of mass wasting that</b><br>
occurs when a coherent mass of loosely<br>
consolidated materials or rock layers<br>
moves a short distance down a slope. The<br>
movement is characterized by sliding<br>
along a concave-upward or surface. The<br>
causes of slumping movement include<br>
earthquake shocks, prolonged heavy rain<br>
thorough wetting, freezing and thawing,<br>
undercutting, and loading of the slope.<br>
<b>In some regions, frost action speeds up</b><br>
the process. Frozen soil and sub soil<br>
on steep slope become unstable when<br>
they thaw and the movement of water<br>
down the slope together with the pull<br>
of gravity triggers off a rock slump<br>
(landslide). For example, mass wasting<br>
that occurred in 1955 at Tukuyu-Kandete<br>
area in Tanzania was caused by heavy<br>
rain of about 425 mm fall in one night<br>
that caused saturation of soil materials.<br>
<b>Also, undercutting of a steep slope by</b><br>
river, sea or the steepening of a slope<br>
by human action such as quarrying or<br>
clearing of vegetation from a steep slope<br>
may increase susceptibility of the area<br>
to landslide. Translational slumps occur<br>
when a detached landmass moves along<br>
the surface. Common plane surfaces of<br>
failure include joints or bedding planes,<br>
especially where a permeable layer<br>
overrides an impermeable surface. Block<br>
slumps are types of translational slump<br>
in which one or more related block units<br>
move downslope as a relatively coherent<br>
mass (Figure 3.11).<br>

<b>Rock layers</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.11: Rock slump</b><br>
(c) 	Rock fall<br>
<b>This is a fall of individual rock from</b><br>
a steep cliff.  It is the most rapid<br>
movement of all.  If rock fall goes on<br>
repeatedly for a long time, debris are<br>
collected on the slope to form mound<br>
(talus). These involve very rapidly<br>
falling of huge pieces of boulders and<br>
blocks of rock down the hills, cliffs or<br>
mountains. The varying type of agents<br>
of weathering and steepness of the<br>
land are the major triggering forces<br>
behind the rock fall. In the coastal<br>
areas the waves cut notch contributing<br>
to the occurrence of such falling rocks<br>
or boulders on the raised cliffs. The<br>
enormous pieces of rock materials<br>
which is broken as the results of rock fall<br>
at the base or foot of the mountain or<br>
cliff commonly known as scree or talus<br>
(Figure 3.12). The rapidity of the rocks<br>
downs the hill or mountains depend<br>
on the profile and gradient of the slope<br>
together with extent of availability of<br>
abstraction. This means that the rocks<br>
or boulders rolling or bumping on a<br>
bare mountain or hill may accelerate<br>
into a tremendous speed with possible<br>
devastating impacts on the way down<br>
the hill compared to those rolling on<br>
vegetated slope.<br>
<b>Rock falls</b><br>
<b>Talus</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.12: Rock fall</b><br>
(d)	 Rock slide<br>
<b>A rockslide is a type of landslide</b><br>
caused by rock failure in which part<br>
of the bedding plane of failure passes<br>
through intact rock and material<br>
collapses in masses. While a landslide<br>
occurs when loose sediment falls down<br>
the slope, rockslide occurs only when<br>
solid rocks are transported down slope.<br>
<b>It is also one among the rapid form of</b><br>
mass wasting. For the rocks to slide<br>
down the hill it is required to have the<br>
jointed structure and/or whose bedding<br>
planes roughly parallel to the angle of<br>
the slope. The rock slide may involve<br>
huge size of rock or boulders but in<br>
sliding motion down the gentle slope.<br>
<b>As the case of rock fall, the rockslide</b><br>
may move for considerable distance<br>

<b>Large sized rock</b><br>
or boulders<br>
<b>Figure 3.13: Rock slide</b><br>
<b>Avalanche: Avalanche is a sudden</b><br>
and catastrophic movement of snow,<br>
ice or rock down the slope (Figure<br>
3.14).  It occurs like a landslide when<br>
the mass of material is sufficient to<br>
overcome friction. There are different<br>
types of avalanches grouped on the<br>
basis of nature of movement and<br>
methods of classification. Avalanches<br>
are mainly caused by a heavy snow<br>
fall compressing and adding weight<br>
to earlier falls, deforestation, low<br>
stability and friction on steep slopes<br>
and sudden increase in temperature.<br>
<b>Also, avalanches are caused by heavy</b><br>
rains falling upon a snow, and very<br>
long cold, dry winters followed by<br>
heavy snow falling under this condition<br>
earlier snow fall will turn into ice while<br>
the later will slide.<br>
Accumulation of ice,<br>
snow and rock particles<br>
<b>Fault</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.14: Avalanche</b><br>
<b>Precautionary measures to reduce</b><br>
the impact of mass wasting<br>
<b>In order to reduce the occurance of</b><br>
mass wasting on the environment,<br>
the following are the recommended<br>
measures;<br>
(i)	 Reforestation and afforestation<br>
along the steep slopes so as tree<br>
roots may bind soil together<br>
to reduce possibilities of mass<br>
movement in form of soil creep<br>
or mud flow caused by heavy<br>
rainfall.<br>
(ii)	 Population control along steep<br>
sloped area which would have<br>
cut the slope in order to set up<br>
residential areas there by increasing<br>
the loose material along the slope.<br>
(iii)	 Provision of environmental<br>
education about the causes, effects<br>
and control measures of mass<br>
wasting so that people get to know<br>
how to live in fragile environment.<br>

(iv)	 Adaption of better and modern<br>
farming methods along the steep<br>
slopes like terracing, contour<br>
ploughing and strip cropping. These<br>
reduce surface run off therefore<br>
reducing the possibility for the<br>
materials to flow down slope.<br>
(v)	 Hill slope draining so as to reduce<br>
excess water that encourage<br>
overloading and lubrication of<br>
weathered material sparking off<br>
mass wasting. It involves digging<br>
deep trenches across hills so as to<br>
check access water run-off.<br>

mass wasting.<br>
2.	 Account<br>
for<br>
the<br>
factors<br>
controlling the rate of mass<br>
movement.<br>
3.	 With vivid examples examine<br>
the effects of mass wasting.<br>
4.	 Suggest the measures to curb<br>
down mass wasting.<br>
<b>Wind action and desert</b><br>
landscape<br>
<b>Wind refers to the moving air across</b><br>
the earth’s surface from the region of<br>
high pressure to low pressure. The wind<br>
is caused by uneven heating ranging<br>
from low breezes to high breezes such as<br>
hurricanes and tornadoes. The movement<br>
and patterns of wind are caused by<br>
variations in insolation and earth’s<br>
rotation. Although wind is commonly<br>
moving throughout various regions it is<br>
however noted that it has a significant<br>
effect in the deserts. The wind action<br>
in deserts often generates processes of<br>
erosion, transportation, and deposition.<br>
<b>Ultimately, these processes account</b><br>
for numerous landforms in the deserts.<br>
<b>Essentially, the geographical distribution</b><br>
of deserts is largely controlled by<br>
global and regional atmospheric<br>
circulation patterns. Also, the human<br>
activities such as shifting cultivation,<br>
overgrazing, unsustainable clearing of<br>
forests for different purposes contribute<br>
to desertification. Generally, deserts<br>
cover all arid regions that normally<br>
experience average rainfall of about 250<br>
mm per year. Deserts are persistently<br>
dry, have high rates of evaporation,<br>
poorly developed soils, and are mostly<br>
or completely covered with sparse<br>
vegetation. The deserts experience the<br>
average summer temperature of about<br>
320C to 380C while the daytime winter<br>
temperature records 100 to 180C on<br>
average. Most of the deserts are located<br>
between 150 and 300 North and South of<br>
the equator (Figure 3.15).<br>
Deserts support sparse vegetation cover.<br>
<b>The plants are usually small, widely</b><br>
spaced and experience slow growth.<br>
<b>Most of the plants have wide, shallow</b><br>
root structure adaptive to absorb the<br>
dew. The rate of water loss through<br>
evaporation in deserts is relatively<br>
higher compared to other regions. The<br>
sparse or lack of vegetation cover in<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   134</b><br>

the deserts exposes the ground surface to denudation and deposition processes by<br>
wind. Despite their harsh conditions desert regions support life to a wide range<br>
of terrestrial organisms.<br>
<b>Figure 3.15:  Desert areas in the world</b><br>
<b>Causes of deserts</b><br>
Deserts are caused by several factors,<br>
including rain shadow effects produced<br>
by high mountain ranges and presence<br>
of moist air forced to rise over mountain<br>
ranges which in turn loose its moisture.<br>
<b>Consequently, the dry air comes down</b><br>
on the other side of the mountain,<br>
compressed and warm, bring high<br>
evaporation with little or no rainfall. This<br>
dry region of the mountain or downwind<br>
side of the range is the rain shadow<br>
zone or the leeward side. Also, cold<br>
ocean currents explain the occurrence<br>
of deserts. Usually these currents do<br>
not bring moisture in the areas where<br>
they are passing. Instead they cause high<br>
evaporation or bring very little rains.<br>
<b>This is experienced on the western coasts</b><br>
of continents. On the other hand, long<br>
distance from the sea is also another<br>
cause of deserts whereby most of the<br>
rainfall comes from water evaporation<br>
thus areas far from the ocean receive less<br>
rainfall unlike coastal areas.<br>
<b>Moreover, in sub-tropical high pressure</b><br>
zone where air descends, it is compressed<br>
and warmed, produces an area of<br>
permanent high pressure. As the air<br>
warms it can hold and increase amount<br>
of water vapour which causes the lower<br>
atmosphere to become very dry. The low<br>
relative humidity and little surface water<br>
of the area for evaporation gives a clear<br>
sky resulted to no rain formation. Other<br>
causes of deserts formation include<br>
geographical location of an area, air<br>
circulation patterns, climate, and the<br>
human activites.<br>

<b>Wind action in desert areas</b><br>
As wind blows in desert areas it erodes,<br>
transports and deposits materials from the<br>
land surface. Therefore, wind processes<br>
in the desert areas involve erosion,<br>
transportation and deposition of different<br>
materials. These processes result into<br>
the formation of different features on<br>
the desert area. Transportation by wind<br>
in the desert moves away the materials<br>
into three processes namely; suspension,<br>
saltation and surface creep. Suspension<br>
involves movement of fine materials<br>
by wind which are then raised into a<br>
substantial height and carried to a greater<br>
distance. The movement of dust in the air<br>
to a great distance is sometimes called<br>
dust storm. Saltation involves blowing<br>
the sand particles while bouncing them<br>
on the ground and over one another.<br>
<b>Surface creep involves the process of</b><br>
rolling materials which are heavy and<br>
cannot be lifted by wind. The materials<br>
are rolled from one place to another<br>
depending on the strength and direction<br>
of the wind. The materials which are<br>
carried by wind are transported and<br>
deposited.This materials are generally<br>
reffered as loess.<br>
<b>Transportation</b><br>
or<br>
movement<br>
of<br>
particles depends on several factors. A<br>
wind speed of about 20km/hr is ideal<br>
for movement of sediments. Another<br>
important factor is a constant direction<br>
and steady wind blow for a length<br>
of period of time. Also, the nature of<br>
regolith is important. Materials are<br>
more likely to be moved if there is<br>
no vegetation to bind them together<br>
or to absorb some of the wind energy.<br>
<b>Moreover, dry and unconsolidated</b><br>
or often small enough particles can<br>
easily be transported by wind. Wind<br>
erosion in desert areas, can be effective<br>
anywhere, depending on its strength.<br>
<b>The process of wind erosion in desert</b><br>
areas is carried out into two main<br>
forms namely; deflation and abrasion.<br>
<b>Deflation is the process of removal of</b><br>
the fine material and leaving behind<br>
pebble-strewn<br>
desert<br>
pavements<br>
(Figure 3.16). Deflation causes hollows<br>
or depressions due to the blowing away<br>
of the fine materials and ultimate by<br>
lowering the surface to some depths.<br>
<b>Direction of wind</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.16: Process of deflation</b><br>
