<b>Landforms resulting from wind</b><br>
deflation<br>
<b>The following are features formed</b><br>
by wind deflation. These features are<br>
deflation hollows and depressions.<br>
<b>Deflation hollows are enclosed hollows</b><br>
produced by wind erosion. A deflation<br>
hollow is one of the most graphic results<br>
of deflation that leaves behind a rocky<br>
surface especially that of homogeneous<br>
resistance. Also, deflation forms both<br>
small and large depressions. A good<br>
example of such depressions is found<br>
in the limestone plateau west of the Nile<br>
valley, in Egypt.  The largest depression<br>
is Qattara depression which extends<br>
about 134 m below sea level. Some<br>
of the depressions in desert areas can<br>
extend down filled with water, resulting<br>
to the development of water table. When<br>
a depression is filled with water it is<br>
referred to as oasis. Good examples of<br>
oases are the Ein Gedi in Israel and Al<br>
<b>Farafra in Egypt (Figure 3.17). A number</b><br>
of assumptions account for the formation<br>
of depression.<br>
<b>Water seeps out of aquifer and</b><br>
forms swamps or an oasis<br>
<b>Aquifier</b><br>
<b>Depression</b><br>
produced<br>
by deflation<br>
<b>Prevailing wind</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.17: Deflation hollow</b><br>
<b>Deflation process is affected by the</b><br>
resistance of the rocks. For example,<br>
where the ground surface is made up of<br>
hard rocks the rate of wind erosion will<br>
be slow, but where it is made of soft rock<br>
the rate of erosion will be high and it<br>
will be easy to form a deflation hollow.<br>
<b>The relative ability of wind to erode</b><br>
and carry out loose particles is another<br>
factor influencing the deflation process.<br>
<b>On the dry land wind has the ability to</b><br>
screw and pick sediments upslope as<br>
well as downslope until a watertable<br>
reached. Wind velocity is another factor.<br>
<b>For example, a velocity increase from 2</b><br>
to 6 meters per second causes an eight-<br>
fold increase in wind speed from 2 to<br>
10 meters per second and generates a<br>
125-fold increase in erosional force.<br>
<b>Consequently, fast wind is capable of</b><br>
causing much more erosion than slow<br>
wind.<br>
<b>Abrasion as the second process of wind</b><br>
erosion involves blasting, grinding<br>
and scratching rock surface by sand<br>
particles carried by wind which hurls<br>
these materials against the rock.<br>
<b>This process smoothens, polishes</b><br>
and wears away rocks close to the<br>
ground.<br>
<b>Landforms formed by abrasion</b><br>
<b>Different landforms are formed by</b><br>
abrasion process including ventifacts,<br>
yardangs, rock pedestals, inselberg and<br>
zeugens.<br>

<b>Ventifacts: These are individual pieces of hard</b><br>
rock, broken off by mechanical weathering<br>
but too heavy to be moved by the wind. They<br>
are usually worn away on the windward side<br>
(Figure 3.18). They are either sharpened or<br>
flattened by the action of sand blasted load<br>
passing over them. They are rocks that have<br>
been sculptured by wind-borne particles as<br>
they are worn, faceted, cut, or polished by<br>
abrasive action of windblown sands. Usually<br>
this occurs under arid conditions, in areas<br>
subjected to frequent, high-velocity, and<br>
sand-laden winds.<br>
<b>Ventifacts</b><br>
<b>Soft rock</b><br>
<b>Hard rock</b><br>
<b>Figure 3. 18: Ventifacts</b><br>
Rock pedestals: These are tower-like landforms formed when the weaker strata in<br>
a mass of rocks are shaped by wind abrasion to give structures of various shapes<br>
(Figure 3.19). These features are common in the Tibet Mountain of Central<br>
Sahara, and the Devils Rock of Agadez in Niger. As abrasion goes on attacking<br>
the weaker rocks, the pedestals may break at the base and collapse.<br>
<b>Soft rock</b><br>
<b>Hard rock</b><br>
<b>Direction</b><br>
of wind<br>
<b>Figure 3.19:  Rock pedestals</b><br>
Zeugens: These occur when wind abrasion turns a desert surface which has<br>
horizontal alternate layer of resistant and soft rock into ridges and furrows-like<br>
structure. Furrows are developed by wind along the rock joints by widening soft<br>
rocks until the ridges are left behind protected by their resistant rocks.  Therefore,<br>
zeugens are parallel flat topped ridges of hard rock up to 3 m high to the direction<br>
of wind as (Figure 3.20). Examples of zeugens can be seen in Bahrain (Middle<br>
East).<br>

Yardangs: These are formed when bands of resistant and weak layers of rocks<br>
laid vertically and parallel to the preceding winds in the desert as a result wind<br>
abrasion produces another type of ridge and furrow landscape. They are usually<br>
less than 10 m high and 500 m long but some are more (Figure 3.21). An example<br>
of these is the Silsila gap in Egypt North of Kom Ombo.<br>

Inselberg: Inselberg is a German word which means an island mountain. Inselberg<br>
is a steep sided hill rising abruptly from pediment which extends on all sides<br>
into monotonous plain (Figure 3.22). In some desert regions, water erosion has<br>
removed all the original surface except for isolated pieces which stand up as<br>
round topped masses. Some inselbergs remain as plateau edge after weathering<br>
sheet. Inselbergs are common in Kalahari Desert, parts of Algeria, North West<br>
Nigeria Western Australia and Northern Tanzania in Maasai land. Also, inselberg<br>
can be described as a residual hill or rock mass rising abruptly from the desert<br>

floor. When inselberg is smooth and<br>
round in shape, it is called   Bornhardt<br>
and when it is characterized with joints<br>

together to produce castellated form, it<br>
is called Kopjes.<br>

<b>Millet Seeds: It is also known as rounded</b><br>
sand grains. These are formed due to the<br>
collision of sand grain with or against<br>
one another. The result is to produce a<br>
characteristic of a rounded sand grain (or<br>
millet seed). This forms the dominant<br>
‘end product” of desert erosion.<br>
<b>Wind deposition</b><br>
<b>The load carried by wind depends on</b><br>
the size of material and the strength of<br>
the wind. The size of material carried by<br>
wind, for example, the finest-dust like<br>
grained may be moved right out of the<br>
desert areas while the heavier one may<br>
simply be resorted within the desert.<br>
<b>Features formed due to wind deposition</b><br>
include:<br>
<b>Sand dunes</b><br>
<b>Sand dunes are hills of sand which have</b><br>
been deposited by wind in the desert.<br>
<b>There are different types of sand dunes</b><br>
namely; barchans, attached, transverse,<br>
longitudinal or seif, star and parabolic<br>
dunes.<br>
<b>Seif dunes: These are also called</b><br>
longitudinal dunes. They are long<br>
narrow ridges of sand which lie parallel<br>
to the direction of the prevailing wind.<br>
<b>They are large and extend to about</b><br>
100 km in length and 200 m in height.<br>
<b>A seif dune usually develops from a</b><br>
small sand ridge which as it forms it<br>
slowly moves forward in the direction<br>
of prevailing winds (Figure 3.23).<br>
<b>Good examples of seif dunes occur in</b><br>
the Thar.<br>
<b>Ridges</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.23:   Seif dunes</b><br>
<b>Barchan dunes: Barchans sometime</b><br>
known as barkhans, are crescent<br>
shaped sand dunes which occur either<br>
individually or in groups. A barchan<br>
usually develops from the accumulation<br>
of sand caused by small obstruction<br>
such as a rock or piece of vegetation. As<br>
the mound grows larger, its two edges<br>
are slowly carried forward downwind<br>

and a typical crescent shape develops.<br>
<b>The windward face of the dune is</b><br>
gently sloping but the leeward side is<br>
steep and slightly concave. A barchan’s<br>
height ranges from few meters to 30 m<br>
and is 400 m wide (Figure 3.24). There<br>
are good examples of barchans in the<br>
Sahara desert and Turkestan.<br>
<b>Slip face</b><br>
<b>Horn</b><br>
<b>Wind</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.24: Barchans</b><br>
<b>Attached dunes: Attached dunes are</b><br>
also known as head dunes. These are<br>
sand drift caused by an obstacle of<br>
vegetation or a rock in the path of the<br>
prevailing wind. Sand accumulates in<br>
the ‘dead air space’ around the rock to<br>
form ‘wind line shape’ on the windward<br>
side of the obstacle and formation of<br>
tail dunes on the leeward side.<br>
<b>Transverse dunes: These are long wave-</b><br>
like ridges separated from each other<br>
by flat bottomed troughs. They tend to<br>
form in huge dune fields, but most are<br>
less than 50m high. They are built by<br>
light to moderate winds, blowing from<br>
one direction only. Wind usually blows<br>
perpendicularly to the dune (Figure 3.25).<br>
<b>In the Western Sahara, they are known</b><br>
as ankle.<br>
<b>Direction</b><br>
of wind<br>
<b>Figure 3.25: Transverse dunes</b><br>
<b>Star dunes: These are complex dunes</b><br>
with a star-shape, formed by winds<br>
blowing<br>
from<br>
several<br>
directions<br>
(Figure 3.26).<br>
<b>Figure 3.26: Star dunes</b><br>
<b>Parabolic dunes: A parabolic dune is</b><br>
somehow similar in shape with a barchan<br>
dune, except that it is deeply curved and<br>
is convex in the downwind direction.  The<br>
horns point upwind and are commonly<br>
anchored by vegetation (Figure 3.27).<br>
<b>The parabolic dune requires abundant</b><br>
sand and commonly forms around a<br>
blowout.  As they require abundant sand<br>
and strong winds, parabolic dunes are<br>
typically found inland from an ocean<br>
beach.<br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Direction of wind</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.27: Parabolic dune</b><br>
<b>Ripples</b><br>
<b>These are series of small ridges of more</b><br>
or less parallel produced by saltation of<br>
coarse sand. They often occur on the<br>
slopes of dunes. Ripples indicate the<br>
direction of sand movement.<br>
<b>Loess</b><br>
<b>Loess refers to an accumulation of fine</b><br>
particles of sand that have been carried<br>
and deposited by wind beyond the<br>
desert limits. It occurs extensively in<br>
the loess plateaus of the Northern China<br>
in Hwang-Ho River and Nile river from<br>
<b>Sahara Desert. Loess form the fertile</b><br>
soil, for example, Hwang Ho basin that<br>
attracts agricultural development.<br>
<b>Activity 3.2</b><br>
(a)	In group of five students, visit your<br>
library or use internet sources<br>
to read more on wind action in<br>
deserts and thereafter write a<br>
summary in your exercise book.<br>
(b)	With the assistance of your subject<br>
teacher, make a presentation in<br>
the classroom.<br>
(c)	 During presentation, allow<br>
other students to ask questions<br>
and respond.<br>
(d)	 Improve your work after<br>
presentation then submit for<br>
assessment.<br>
<b>Exercise 3.3</b><br>
1.<br>
Explain<br>
why<br>
wind<br>
is<br>
considered as a major factor of<br>
erosion in the desert areas.<br>
2.<br>
What are the major land forms<br>
associated with mechanical<br>
weathering in the desert areas?<br>
3.<br>
What are the importance of<br>
a desert landscape and its<br>
associated challenges.<br>
<b>Water action in deserts</b><br>
<b>Rainfall is very rare in deserts and when</b><br>
it rains may result into rushing torrents<br>
on steep slopes and cause sheet flood<br>
on gentle slopes. Thus, it is not only<br>
wind that is responsible for shaping<br>
the deserts but also water plays a great<br>
role in eroding the desert surfaces and<br>
depositing the materials leading to a<br>
number of erosional and depositional<br>
features on the topography of a desert.<br>
<b>Water erosional features in the</b><br>
desert area<br>
<b>Water action in the desert area produces</b><br>
erosional features namely rills, gullies,<br>
wadis, pediment, mesas and buttes. The<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   142</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
143<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
formed features are influenced by the<br>
relief, nature of rocks, land surface cover<br>
and amount of rainfall received in the<br>
area. These features are described in the<br>
section that follows.<br>
<b>Rills</b><br>
<b>Rills are small, shallow channels formed</b><br>
by surface runoff on a desert area (Figure<br>
3.28). The desert surface is washed<br>
unevenly by running water through small<br>
channels called rills.<br>
<b>Rills</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.28: Rills</b><br>
<b>Gullies</b><br>
<b>Gullies are deep steep sided troughs</b><br>
produced when erosion in the rills<br>
becomes more concentrated into the<br>
ground. They occur when heavy rainfall<br>
opens and widen the rills into larger<br>
grooves (Figure 3.29).<br>
<b>Gully</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.29: Gully</b><br>
<b>Wadis</b><br>
<b>A wadis is also known as chebka. These</b><br>
are deep cut valleys which have been<br>
formed by exotic river flow across<br>
the desert but have delivered the bulk<br>
of their water from outside of desert<br>
(Figure 3.30). Few wadis hold permanent<br>
streams, and may be flooded during<br>
the rainy storm. Wadis are common in<br>
Saharan desert, for example, Hamra,<br>
Talh Abd el Malik and El Bakht.<br>
<b>Figure 3.30: Wadis</b><br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Pediment</b><br>
<b>This is a gently sloping platform</b><br>
formed when the edges of the desert<br>
and semi-desert highlands get pushed<br>
back by erosion. They are covered with<br>
a thin veil of debris.<br>
<b>Mesas and buttes</b><br>
<b>Mesas are extensive flat-toped residual</b><br>
table lands which are generally capped<br>
with resistant rock stratum (Figure<br>
3.31). Buttes are small residual flat-<br>
topped hills usually capped with<br>
resistant rock stratum which remains<br>
after denudation of mesas or a plateau.<br>
<b>These features are common in Northern</b><br>
areas of Turkana in Kenya.<br>
<b>Mesa</b><br>
<b>Butte</b><br>
<b>Monument</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.31: Mesas and buttes</b><br>
<b>Water depositional features in the</b><br>
desert area<br>
<b>Water action in the desert results to a</b><br>
different depositional features namely<br>
alluvial fans, alluvial cones, bajada,<br>
peripediment and sebka. Figure 3.32 and<br>
<b>3.33 present water depositional features</b><br>
in the desert area. The subsequent<br>
sections explain the varied features<br>
formed by water action in the desert<br>
area.<br>
<b>Alluvial fans</b><br>
Alluvial fans are also known as cone.<br>
<b>These are fan-shaped features formed</b><br>
when large quantities of sediments are<br>
deposited at the gullies. They are formed<br>
where sediments loaded streams exist<br>
steep floor to deposit their load on the<br>
flat desert floor.<br>
<b>Alluvial cones</b><br>
<b>Alluvial cones are formed when large</b><br>
quantities of coarse sediments are<br>
deposited at the foot of steep slopes.<br>
<b>Alluvial cones are similar to fans but</b><br>
differ in the sense that they are composed<br>
of coarser materials and constitute<br>
steeper slopes.<br>
<b>Bajada</b><br>
Bajada are sometimes known as bahada.<br>
Some basins are rimmed by uplands,<br>
and temporary rivers emerging from the<br>
uplands build up alluvial cones (similar<br>
to fans but made of coarse materials) at<br>
the foot of the upland. These eventually<br>
join together to form a continuous feature<br>
known as bajada. They are formed when<br>
several wadis cut through a highland<br>
close to each other.<br>
<b>Peripediment</b><br>
<b>Peripedment is a gentle slope, formed</b><br>
when alluvial deposits overlie the edge<br>
of the pediment surrounding the sebka.<br>
<b>It is the area of accumulated sands and</b><br>
gravels peripheral to a pediment in desert<br>
basin.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   144</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:02<br>

<b>Sebka or playa</b><br>
There is no permanent drainage patterns in deserts, therefore any rain that falls<br>
either evaporates, or infiltrates into the surface, or runs off and drains into basins<br>
(depression). After heavy falls of rain, temporary streams and rivers develop which<br>
may eventually drain into basin where they produce lakes. The lakes soon dry up<br>
through evaporation and turn into salt flats called sebka.<br>

With the aid of diagrams,<br>

features formed by fluvial<br>
action in the desert.<br>

3.<br>
Describe any six characteristics<br>
of a desert landscape.<br>
<b>Water action in the karst</b><br>
region<br>
<b>The term karst is borrowed from</b><br>
<b>Yugoslavia and means features formed</b><br>
in the limestone regions mostly<br>
dominated by limestone rocks. Karst<br>
region is a landscape mainly formed<br>
water soluble limestone or dolomite<br>
to form sinkholes, sinking streams,<br>
caves, springs, and other characteristic<br>
features.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
limestone<br>
region<br>
characterized with materials that can<br>
assume different forms such as calcium<br>
carbonate, marble or gypsum. Calcium<br>
carbonate may exist in various forms<br>
of chalk, Jurassic and carboniferous<br>
limestones consisting mostly of fossils.<br>
<b>A typical karst landscape forms when</b><br>
much of the water falling on the earth’s<br>
surface enters the cracks, fractures,<br>
and holes and eventually dissolves the<br>
bedrock to form solution. The formed<br>
solution can be discharged from<br>
springs that are often cave entrances.<br>
<b>Tanzania is one of places in the world</b><br>
characterised with karst regions. It hosts<br>
significant composition of limestone<br>
rocks in areas such as Amboni and<br>
<b>Nanyala in Tanga and Songwe regions</b><br>
respectively. Also, the karst region is<br>
found in Slovenia, Southern Europe.<br>
<b>Characteristics of the karst region</b><br>
<b>The karst region is characterized with</b><br>
scant vegetation that occur in pocket<br>
such as scrubs and thickets. The region<br>
has rock outcrop with poor soil in<br>
some parts of the region. Rocks in the<br>
region are porous with well jointed<br>
structure that allows percolation of<br>
rain water through the cracks which is<br>
referred to as secondary permeability<br>
of rock. Carbonation is the dominant<br>
weathering process in the region as a<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   146</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
147<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
result the reaction between carbonic<br>
acid and limestone minerals to form<br>
carbonates. Surface drainage is limited<br>
due to the presence of underground<br>
rivers. The region is characterized by<br>
both surface and sub-surface landforms<br>
such as stalagmites, natural pillars, and<br>
helicities associated with evaporation<br>
and precipitation of calcium bicarbonate.<br>
<b>There are residual features like scarps</b><br>
and hills consist of resistant rock.<br>
<b>Gullies, rills, sink hole and grikes are</b><br>
common land forms on the surface of<br>
the entire region. The region largely,<br>
supports the growth of grasses as the<br>
results of poor soils. These grasses are<br>
useful for livestock keeping, as the case<br>
of Yugoslavia in which they are used for<br>
sheep raring.<br>
<b>Features formed in the karst region</b><br>
<b>The features formed in the limestone</b><br>
region are classified based on the place<br>
of their occurrence. They are therefore<br>
classified into two types namely surface<br>
and sub-surface features.<br>
<b>Surface features formed in the karst</b><br>
region<br>
<b>The surface features are landforms</b><br>
formed on the surface of the limestone<br>
region as a result of the influence of<br>
rain water (Figure 3.34). The features<br>
include: Scarps, hills and mountains<br>
which are pieces of hard rock that remain<br>
standing after the other top soil covering<br>
materials are worn away by the running<br>
rain water. Grikes which are depression-<br>
like landforms that are formed as a result<br>
of water eroding the cracks of the surface<br>
of the limestone region allowing water<br>
to disappear underground. Clints are<br>
flat-topped ridge or block like landforms<br>
that separate one grike and another. Their<br>
formation depends on the flow of water<br>
in the fractures of limestone rock.<br>
<b>Swallow hole/sink hole/ponor are</b><br>
vertical holes like landforms formed<br>
as a result of enlargement of the rock<br>
cracks caused by the accumulation of<br>
water on the surface of the limestone<br>
region. Doline which is referred as<br>
a shallow depression with gentle<br>
slopping sides and generally circular<br>
in plain. It is formed as the results<br>
of the coalescing/merging of several<br>
swallow holes. Uvala which is a large<br>
depression formed when several dollies<br>
join together. The diameter of Uvala<br>
can extend to about one kilometer.<br>
<b>Polje which is a very large depression</b><br>
formed when several uvalas collapse<br>
and join together. The collapse may be<br>
due to the dissolving associated with<br>
faulting. Gorge that refers to a steep<br>
sided trough lined by steep rock cliff<br>
formed as the results of the collapse of<br>
the roof of the cave.<br>

<b>Grike</b><br>
(a)<br>

<b>Sub surface/subterranean features</b><br>
formed in the karst region<br>
<b>Sub surface features are also called</b><br>
subterranean features. These are<br>
landforms formed within the limestone<br>
region. Their development is a result of<br>
formation of solution and precipitation<br>
of calcium bicarbonate materials<br>
(Figure 3.35). Sub-surface landforms<br>
include the following: Cave or caverns<br>
these are underground chambers or<br>
cavity like an opening formed as the<br>
results of dissolving of limestone rock<br>
underground. Underground river is a<br>
river stream formed when water sinks<br>
and flow underground through the sink<br>
hole. Also, such river flow is called<br>
subterranean river. Stalagmites these<br>
are finger like structure of rock masses<br>
that grows from the floor of the cave<br>
upward. Their formation is a results of<br>
precipitation of calcium bicarbonate<br>
materials that accumulate on the floor<br>
of the cave. Stalactites these are finger<br>
like structure of calcium bicarbonate<br>
(calcitic) materials that grows from the<br>
roof of the cave down ward. They are<br>
usually hanging vertically from the roof<br>
of the cave. Natural pillar is a pillar<br>
or column like landform formed as the<br>
results of the joining of stalagmite and<br>
stalactite. Also, it can be formed when<br>
stalagmite grows to the roof of the<br>
cave. Anthodites these are flower like<br>
structure that grows from the roof of the<br>
cave often lacks a well-defined shape<br>
as they are very small in size. Helctites<br>
which are crystalline formation that are<br>
of different shapes. They are as thin as<br>
thread arranged spirals, and they can<br>
rejoin the ceiling of the cave.<br>

karst region<br>
<b>Importance of the karst region</b><br>
<b>The karst region has several values or</b><br>
importance, the major ones include:<br>
<b>Supporting livestock keeping as it</b><br>
provides grasses that are used by<br>
goats, sheep or cattle. For example, in<br>
<b>Yugoslavia karst region is used for sheep</b><br>
raring. In some areas, the regions provide<br>
a fertile soil that can be used for crop<br>
production. Such soil includes toreros<br>
soil. The region provides rocks that can<br>
be used for construction activities. Karst<br>

region provides the basis for availability<br>
of raw materials that can be used in<br>

production of cement. Underground<br>
features in the limestone region such<br>
as cave, stalagmite, stalactites, natural<br>
pillars attract tourism which in turns may<br>
provide foreign currencies. For example,<br>
<b>Amboni Tanga in Tanzania. Karst region</b><br>
is a basic sources of water bodies such<br>
as underground river that provides water<br>
for different uses.<br>

<b>Glaciation</b><br>
<b>Glaciation involves both the moving</b><br>
ice (glacier) and periglacial. Glaciation<br>
refers to all processes that take place due<br>
to the influence of the moving mass of<br>
ice (glacier) or the state  of being covered<br>
by glacier or ice sheets while periglacial<br>
refers to near or at the fringe of an ice<br>
sheet, where frost and snow have a major<br>
impact upon the landscape. However, the<br>
term is often used to explain any area<br>
with cold climate such as mountains<br>
in temperate latitudes. Also, it includes<br>
areas which have experienced severe<br>
frost action in the past, for example,<br>
some parts of the Congo basin.<br>
<b>Theories explaining the occurrence</b><br>
of glacial periods<br>
<b>The occurrence of glaciers in different</b><br>
parts of the world has been caused<br>
by climatic changes associated in the<br>
general cooling of the atmospheres.<br>
<b>This can be explained through various</b><br>
theories: Variation of earth distance<br>
from the Sun. This relates to the nature of<br>
the orbit of the Earth and its inclination.<br>
<b>During the greatest distance from the</b><br>
sun (aphelion 152 million km) on<br>
<b>July 4th, the amount of solar radiation</b><br>
is reduced and leads to the formation<br>
of glacier because of cooling of the<br>
atmosphere. The idea was developed by<br>
the former Yugoslavian astronomer and<br>
mathematician, Milutin Milankovitch<br>
(Between 1921 and 1941). Reduction<br>
of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.<br>
<b>When carbon dioxide is excessively</b><br>
reduced in the atmosphere, it leads to<br>
the cooling on the earth’s surface and<br>
the falling of snow occurs. This is due to<br>
the fact that the absence of CO2 causes<br>
the outgoing radiation to escape freely<br>
into the atmosphere through radiation.<br>
<b>Carbon dioxide is a very good absorber</b><br>
of solar radiation. Hence if CO2<br>
content in the atmosphere is reduced<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   149</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
150<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
by a half the temperature go down<br>
by 4º C resulting in the occurrence<br>
of glaciers. The idea was advanced<br>
by Svante Arrhenius, the Swedish<br>
scientist. The increase in the amount<br>
of dust in the atmosphere. When dust,<br>
for example, volcanic dust is present<br>
in a great amount in the atmosphere<br>
it tends to reflect and absorb solar<br>
radiation, which is supposed to reach<br>
the surface. Therefore, substantial<br>
fall in temperature and hence cooling<br>
leading to the occurrence of glacier.<br>
Shifting of the position of the continent.<br>
<b>The shifting of continents during</b><br>
continental drifting also accounts<br>
for the occurrence of glaciers on the<br>
earth’s surface. The landmasses, which<br>
were shifted to extreme polar regions,<br>
assumed very low temperatures that<br>
in turn led to the occurrence of glacier<br>
because the regions were now located<br>
very far from the position of the sun.<br>
<b>Some places like Greenland, Antarctica</b><br>
and Iceland are snow covered with ice<br>
because of being in the polar regions.<br>
<b>Elevation of the land surface. Some</b><br>
places have been elevated as a result<br>
of diastrophic upheavals. Elevation<br>
led to the lowering of temperature<br>
due to the principle of lapse rate thus<br>
temperature decrease with increase<br>
in height, hence formation of glacier.<br>
For example, Mt. Kilimanjaro, Alps,<br>
and Kenya Mountains are covered by<br>
ice and some of them are located in<br>
tropical areas.<br>
<b>Accumulation of ice</b><br>
<b>When the temperature of the air falls</b><br>
below freezing point 0º C, some of its<br>
water vapour condenses and freezes<br>
into snowflakes that fall on the ground<br>
surface as snow. Where snow collects<br>
in hollows it becomes compressed<br>
by the weight of subsequent fall<br>
and gradually develops into a more<br>
compact dense form called neve or firn.<br>
<b>Firn is a consolidated snow which has</b><br>
experienced one winter freezing and<br>
survived one summer melt. When firn<br>
is more compressed it may form more<br>
solid substance called ice. Once ice has<br>
formed it may begin to flow downhill<br>
under the force of gravity as glacier. In<br>
polar and sub-polar regions extensive<br>
areas may be covered by permanent<br>
layers of ice (frozen water) called ice<br>
sheets (ice cap or snow field). The lower<br>
limit of the snow field is called snow-<br>
line. This is a point above which snow<br>
and ice cover the ground throughout<br>
the year. Snowline is the lowest<br>
level on the mountains above which<br>
snow never completely disappear, it<br>
is considered to be permanent level<br>
though it varies with latitude, altitude,<br>
temperature, humidity, precipitation,<br>
aspect and steepness of the slope. There<br>
are two types of snowlines namely<br>
permanent and temporary. Permanent<br>
snowlines are the area where snow<br>
does not melt completely throughout<br>
the year, for example, polar region of<br>
<b>Antarctica and highland areas of very</b><br>
high altitude such as Kilimanjaro and<br>
<b>Alps Mountains. Temporary snowlines</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   150</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
151<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
are the area where snow falls on winter<br>
seasons, for example, in Britain where<br>
snow disappears in August when the<br>
temperature rises.<br>
<b>Movement of ice</b><br>
<b>Ice moves in different forms though its</b><br>
causes to flow is not known exactly. It<br>
is stated that the movement is caused by<br>
freeze-thaw action. The following are<br>
forms of ice movements. Plastic flowage<br>
occurs when ice moves in a massive nature<br>
like a viscous liquid. The movement is<br>
caused by deep accumulation of ice on a<br>
slope which makes the bottom layers to<br>
melt under pressure. The action triggers<br>
off the movement of ice down the slope.<br>
<b>Such mass of moving ice is called a</b><br>
glacier. Basal slip happens when ice<br>
slips and slides over the underlying rock.<br>
<b>It involves the sliding of glacier over</b><br>
its rocky base. Also, internal shearing<br>
is a movement similar to rock faulting<br>
which involves differential sliding<br>
along plains within the ice body caused<br>
by a tremendous pressure exerted by<br>
glaciers.<br>
<b>The gradient and the thickness of the ice</b><br>
affect the speed at which ice flows. The<br>
movement of valley glaciers is greater<br>
than that of ice sheets. Movement on<br>
the surface and in the centre of a valley<br>
glacier is greater than movement at the<br>
sides and bottom. These different rates<br>
of movement lead to the formation of<br>
cracks called crevasses. These cracks<br>
can be transverse crevasses which occur<br>
at right angles to the glacier, marginal<br>
crevasses that occur parallel to its sides<br>
and oblique crevasses which occur<br>
obliquely to its sides.<br>
<b>Types of glaciers</b><br>
<b>Glacier may be classified according to</b><br>
size and shape, the characteristic which<br>
are relatively easy to identify by field<br>
observation. Based on these criteria,<br>
several types of glaciers emerge. Firstly,<br>
ice sheets and ice caps that overspread<br>
continental or plateau regions where the<br>
snow line is low, creeping with a slow<br>
massive movement towards the margins,<br>
for example, Antarctica or Greenland.<br>
<b>Secondly, valley glaciers include large</b><br>
masses of ice which move from either<br>
ice field or cirque basin source. They<br>
usually follow former river courses and<br>
are bounded by steep sides. Thirdly,<br>
piedmont glaciers which consist of<br>
sheets of ice formed by the coalescence<br>
(growing together) of several valley<br>
glaciers which have spread out below<br>
the snowline like lakes of ice-over a low<br>
land areas of wastage for istance, Western<br>
<b>Europe and Alaska. Fourthly, ice shelves</b><br>
that form when the ice sheets reach the<br>
sea and begin to float. Icebergs form<br>
when ice breaks away, a process called<br>
calving. Fifthly, corrie or cirque glaciers<br>
which include small masses of ice<br>
occupying armchair-shaped depressions<br>
on mountain sides. They often overspill<br>
from their hollows to feed valley glaciers.<br>
<b>Sixthly, niche glaciers which are very</b><br>
small and occupy hollows and gullies<br>
on North facing slope in the northern<br>
hemisphere.<br>

<b>Factors affecting the rate of glacial</b><br>
movement.<br>
<b>Glaciers can move from one place to</b><br>
another either fast or slowly due to the<br>
following factors.<br>
(a)	 Nature of gradient: This refers to<br>
the degree of slope. Along steep<br>
slopes glaciers tend to move fast if<br>
not blocked by an obstacle on its<br>
way. This may explain the limited<br>
movement of continental glaciers<br>
along plain areas and gentle slopes.<br>
(b)	 Temperature:<br>
<b>Glaciers</b><br>
move<br>
fast<br>
during<br>
warmer seasons.<br>
<b>This is because the melting rate</b><br>
or breaking rate is very high.<br>
<b>The movement may be in form</b><br>
of<br>
avalanches.<br>
<b>During</b><br>
low<br>
temperature the movement is<br>
limited since the freezing rate is<br>
also high hence making glaciers<br>
to attach to each other.<br>
(c)	 Thickness and weight of glaciers:<br>
<b>The thicker the glacier the fast</b><br>
the movement. This is because<br>
the chance for it to be blocked<br>
on the way is very low. On the<br>
other hand, small glaciers may be<br>
blocked while on their way.<br>
(d)	 Nature of the load: Glaciers are<br>
always associated with loads<br>
transported. The lesser the load the<br>
faster the movement since there<br>
is less friction between bedrock,<br>
glacier and the underlying valley<br>
sides. Therefore, a heavy loaded<br>
glacier will have high friction<br>
hence retarded to move further.<br>
<b>Glacial erosion</b><br>
<b>Ice</b><br>
action<br>
greatly<br>
changes<br>
the<br>
appearance of landforms. Highlands<br>
are subjected to erosion and parts of<br>
lowlands are subjected to both erosion<br>
and deposition. Some of this glacier<br>
is collected in hollows or held back<br>
by glacial deposits, where it forms<br>
lakes, such as, the Great lakes of North<br>
<b>America. Virtually all glacial processes</b><br>
of erosion are physical as the climate is<br>
too cold for chemical reaction to take<br>
place.<br>
<b>Erosion processes of glaciers</b><br>
<b>Glacial erosion which predominates</b><br>
in the highland consists of three<br>
processes. These are sapping which<br>
involves breaking up of rocks by<br>
alternate freezing and thawing of water<br>
at the bottom of cracks between a mass<br>
of ice and the side and floor of a valley<br>
or the side of a mountain. Plucking<br>
is the tearing away of large blocks<br>
of rocks which have become frozen<br>
into the sides or bottom of a glacier.<br>
<b>It involves the removal of blocks of</b><br>
bedrock which have been loosened by<br>
ice. Also, abrasion is the process which<br>
involves the scratching and scraping<br>
of the valley by debris and other<br>
fragments that are frozen or embedded<br>
into the glacier. Therefore, the load is<br>
used as the tool for eroding the surface.<br>
<b>On the other hand, there is another</b><br>
process known as frost shuttering. This<br>
process produces much loose materials<br>
which may fall from valley sides onto<br>
the edges of the glacier to form lateral<br>

moraine. They may be covered later by<br>
snowfall or plunge down crevasses and<br>
be transported as englacial debris.<br>
<b>Landforms of glaciated highlands</b><br>
<b>Features formed on the glaciated</b><br>
highlands<br>
include<br>
the<br>
following;<br>
cirques (corries), arête, pyramidal peak<br>
(horn), U-shaped valley (glaciated<br>
trough), hanging valleys, truncated<br>
spurs, benches, col, fiord and rock basin<br>
(Figure 3.32). They are also known as<br>
Alpine glacier features.<br>
<b>Cirques</b><br>
or<br>
corries:<br>
<b>These</b><br>
are<br>
semi-circular steep sided basins or<br>
depressions cut into the side of a<br>
mountain or at the head of a valley.<br>
<b>They are also known as corries in</b><br>
<b>Scotland and cwm in Wales. During</b><br>
periglacial times, before the last<br>
glaciation, snow collected in hollows<br>
especially on facing slopes. A series<br>
of processes, collectively known as<br>
nivation which included freeze-thaw,<br>
solifluction and possibly chemical<br>
weathering, operated under and around<br>
the snow patch. These processes caused<br>
the underlying rocks to disintegrate the<br>
resultant debris was then removed by<br>
summer melt water streams to leave in<br>
the enlarged hollow. Sometimes a corrie<br>
can be filled with water to form a corrie<br>
lake or tarn, for example, Lake Tana in<br>
<b>Ethiopia. Arête: This is formed when</b><br>
two adjacent cirques erode backwards<br>
or sideways towards each other such<br>
that the previously rounded landscape<br>
is transformed into a narrow-rocky,<br>
steep sided ridge. Pyramidal peak:<br>
<b>This is also known as horn which refers</b><br>
to a steep-sided angular horn formed<br>
by the steepening of the back wall of<br>
corries, when several corries develop<br>
on the sides of the mountain. U-shaped<br>
valley:  A U-shaped valley is also<br>
known as glaciated trough. It is a steep-<br>
sided, flat bottomed, wide valley which<br>
contain features produced by both<br>
glacial erosion and glacial deposition.<br>
<b>Most of the U-shaped valleys were</b><br>
originally river valleys.  The valley is<br>
straightened and any projecting spur<br>
are planed off or truncated.  Thus<br>
the former interlocking spurs are<br>
turned into truncated spurs. Hanging<br>
valleys:<br>
<b>Hanging</b><br>
valleys<br>
resulted<br>
from differential erosion between the<br>
main glacier and its tributary glaciers.<br>
<b>The floor of any tributary glacier is</b><br>
deepened at a slower rate, so that<br>
when glaciers melt it is left hanging<br>
high above the main valley, and its<br>
river has to descend by a single or a<br>
series of waterfalls. Truncated spurs:<br>
<b>A truncated spur is formed due to</b><br>
abrasion by englacial and sub glacial<br>
debris and plucking along the valley<br>
sides which remove the tips of pre-<br>
glacial interlocking spurs leaving cliff-<br>
like structure called truncated spurs.<br>
<b>Also, it may be formed when glacier</b><br>
flows through interlocking spurs with<br>
the ability to straighten the valley by<br>
cutting the projecting spur. Benches:<br>
<b>Benches are also known as alps. These are</b><br>
upper slopes of the river valley, lying<br>
above the glacier surface and after the<br>

glacier melts they form benches. Col: Col is a narrow notch on an arête between<br>
opposite cirques. It is produced by the process called headwall recession due to<br>
plucking. The two cirques meet and create a notch or gap on the arête. Fiord:<br>
Fiord is a submerged glaciated U-shaped valley (sea inlet) when the glacier<br>
eroded its path by undercutting and later plunged into the sea, which then is flooded<br>
by sea water after the rise of the sea level which causes submergence of the<br>
coastal areas. Rock basin: A rock basin is formed by unequal glacial erosion of<br>
the bed rock. This is an irregular depression in the floor of a U-shaped valley. This<br>
feature often develops when the thickness and weight of a glacier increase, such<br>
as, at the junction of two glaciers (Figure 3.36).<br>

<b>Landforms of glaciated lowlands</b><br>
<b>The glacial erosional features in the</b><br>
lowland areas are largely caused by the<br>
influence of ice sheets. An extensive<br>
area covered by an ice sheet smoothen<br>
off the original landforms to give a<br>
rounded topography known as ice-<br>
eroded plain. On these plains there are<br>
roche moutonnee and crag and tails.<br>
<b>Roche moutonnee: A roche moutonnee</b><br>
is an outcrop of resistant rock smoothed<br>
by ice on the upstream end into a gentle<br>
slope and plucked on the downstream<br>
end to give a steep jagged slope (Figure<br>
3.37). Also, the rock moutonnee occurs<br>
in glaciated highlands.<br>

<b>Crag and tail: Crag and tail consists of a</b><br>
larger mass of resistant rock. It is formed<br>
by less resistant rock which protect the<br>
lee-side rocks from erosion, thus forming<br>
a gently sloping tail (Figure 3.38).<br>

<b>Glacial deposition</b><br>
<b>The deposition of materials by ice can</b><br>
be achieved by stratified and unstratified<br>
materials. Stratified materials are sorted<br>
and stratified clays, silt sands or gravel<br>
deposited by melt-water streams while<br>
unstratified materials are unsorted<br>
fluvial-glacial. Such materials consist<br>
of a mixture of rock fragments ranging<br>
in size from clay to boulders and are<br>
deposited directly from ice, without water<br>
transport. Glacial deposition landforms<br>
include boulder clay, erratics, drumlins,<br>
moraine, varves, kames, eskers, kettles<br>
and braided stream.<br>
<b>Boulder clay plain</b><br>
<b>Boulder clay is an extensive area of</b><br>
a monotonous relief. Former valleys<br>
and hills are buried by thick layer of<br>
till deposited beneath the ice sheet and<br>
plastered onto the sub-glacial surface by<br>
the pressure of the ice. The plain contains<br>
other features like erratics, drumlins, and<br>
moraines (Figure 3.39).<br>
<b>Erratics: Are large boulders of varying</b><br>
sizes deposited by glacier or ice sheet as<br>
they melt. Erratic blocks are composed<br>
of materials, which are different from<br>
the materials of the surrounding rocks.<br>
<b>It helps to know the direction of ice</b><br>
movement. These features can be found<br>
in South Wales in Britain.<br>
<b>Drumlins: Are elongated oval shaped low</b><br>
hummock (small glacial hills) made up<br>
of boulder clay and it is about 1km long<br>
and 25-100m high. The upstream sides<br>
are usually steeper than the downstream<br>
sides, and they occur in swarms, usually<br>
oval and shaped like half an egg.<br>
<b>Moraine: A moraine is a boulder clay</b><br>
deposit (till) laid down in the lowlands to<br>
form ridges. It consists of debris mainly<br>
boulders and fragments, transported<br>
and deposited by glacier. There are<br>
different types of moraines (Figure<br>
3.39). The common ones include;<br>
lateral, medial, terminal, recessional,<br>
and ground moraines.  Lateral moraines<br>
are formed when glacier melts and<br>
leaves a ridge of material along the<br>
valley sides. Also, it consists of debris<br>
derived from frost shattering of valley<br>
sides and carried along the edges of<br>
the glacier. Medial moraines refer to<br>
rock fragments deposited in the middle<br>
of the valley, after two lateral moraines<br>
merge. Terminal moraines are formed<br>
when debris is transported at the front of<br>
the glacier and deposited when glacier<br>
melts. It is regularly series of mounds<br>
extending across a valley or lowland<br>
area. Moreover, recessional moraines<br>

refer to a series of roughly parallel terminal moraines that mark the step by step<br>
retreat of the glacier. Furthermore, ground moraine refers to the materials deposited<br>
at the bottom of the glacier after ice melts. They form a fairly thin extensive layer<br>
or blanket of till.<br>

<b>Fluvioglacier landforms</b><br>
<b>These are landforms molded by</b><br>
glacial melt water.  Unsorted material<br>
deposited by ice, melt water transport<br>
and deposited sorted materials of<br>
gravels and sand. Wherever there is<br>
a decrease in discharge a group of<br>
depositional feature are formed.  These<br>
features include the outwash plain,<br>
verves, kames, kame terraces, eskers,<br>
kettles and braided streams.<br>
<b>Outwash plain: Materials such as</b><br>
sand and gravel formerly deposited<br>
by glacier are later picked, sorted and<br>
dropped by running water (melt water)<br>
beyond the maximum extent, of the<br>
ice sheets.  They form a plain of sorted<br>
materials (Figure 3.40). Varves: A varve<br>
is a distinct layer of silt lying on top<br>
of a layer of sand normally deposited<br>
annually in lakes nearby glacial<br>
margins. Two layers of sediments<br>
representing one year’s deposition<br>
in a lake. The light coloured layer<br>
consists of slightly coarser sediments<br>
(silt) deposited during the warmer part<br>
of the year when the nearby glacier<br>
is melting.  The darker layer is finer<br>
sediment (clay), materials that sink<br>
down more slowly during the winter<br>
after the lake surface freezes and the<br>
supply of fresh, coarser sediments stops<br>
due to lack of melt water.  Each band of<br>
light and dark materials represents one<br>
year’s accumulation. The dark colour is<br>
attributed to fine organic matter mixed<br>
with clay.<br>

09/03/2022   17:03<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
157<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Kames and kame terraces: Kames are</b><br>
steep sided hills formed by a sorted sand<br>
and gravel deposited by melt water.<br>
<b>They are deposited unevenly similar to</b><br>
a series of deltas along the front of a<br>
stationary or slowly melting ice sheet.<br>
<b>They vary greatly in size and shape</b><br>
and occur in groups. Kame terraces are<br>
also mounds of sand and gravel along<br>
the valley sides.  They are deposited<br>
by melt water between the glacier and<br>
the valley walls.  Kame terraces are<br>
distinguished from lateral moraine by<br>
their sorted deposits. Eskers: These are<br>
long, sinuous ridge of water deposited<br>
material composed of sorted course<br>
sand and gravel.  Eskers can be up to<br>
50 meters high and several kilometers<br>
long.  They develop in areas of stagnant<br>
ice where rivers maintain sub-glacial<br>
tunnels.  Water flowing through such<br>
tunnels is under hydrostatic pressures<br>
making it impossible to carry heavy or<br>
large load, much of which is deposited<br>
within the tunnel, when the ice melts it<br>
leaves long, winding steep sided ridges.<br>
<b>Kettles: These are formed when the</b><br>
ice block melts to form a depression.<br>
<b>Melting water builds thick deposits</b><br>
of outwash alongside and in from of a<br>
retreating glacier. Blocks of stagnant<br>
ice may be surrounded and buried by<br>
sediments. These depressions often<br>
fill with water to form kettle lakes<br>
(Figure 3.40).<br>

glacial ice<br>

Braided streams: Seasonal variations in discharge make the channels of melt<br>
water to become chocked with coarse materials.  The streams channel themselves<br>
into several minor channels through deposited materials. Such melt water rivers<br>
are termed braided streams.<br>
<b>The importance of glaciated regions</b><br>
Glaciated regions generate ranges of values which include;<br>
(i)<br>
Fertile land for crop production. The landmarks left by the whole process<br>
of glaciation results to the fertile land which is suitable for crop production.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   157</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
158<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Glacial deposit such as till and</b><br>
outwash plains are good areas<br>
for crop production because of<br>
their fertile soil (boulder clay),<br>
for example, East Anglia in<br>
<b>Great Britain, the American and</b><br>
<b>Canadian Pralines producing vast</b><br>
amounts of wheat.<br>
(ii)<br>
Glaciated highlands and lowlands<br>
are good grazing lands.  Cattle<br>
are grazed on the alpine pastures<br>
during summer and are brought<br>
down to their shelter valleys and<br>
cotton postures during winter. This<br>
movement of animals up and down<br>
the mountains periodically, is<br>
called transhumance. It is common<br>
in Switzerland, Norway and other<br>
mountainous countries.<br>
(iii)	 Glaciation produces various types<br>
of lakes such as cirque, ribbon,<br>
moraine dammed and kettle lakes.<br>
<b>Some of these lakes have proved</b><br>
to be excellent route ways across<br>
rugged terrains. For example, the<br>
<b>Hudson Mohawk Gap leads down</b><br>
to New York.  The lakes also are<br>
good sources of water for domestic<br>
as well as for industrial use.<br>
(iv)	 Glaciation produces beautiful<br>
sceneries which attract tourists,<br>
that is a source of national income.<br>
<b>These features include pyramidal</b><br>
peaks, cirque lakes, drumlins<br>
and moraines, for example,<br>
the Kilimanjaro Mountain in<br>
<b>Tanzania.  The peak of Kilimanjaro</b><br>
mountain has an ice cap which is<br>
very attractive to tourists from all<br>
over the world.  Switzerland and<br>
<b>Norway also receive millions of</b><br>
tourists every year because of their<br>
glaciated highlands and lowlands.<br>
(v)<br>
Glacial deposits are good sources<br>
of building materials such as<br>
sand, gravel and pebbles.  These<br>
are used for building houses and<br>
construction of infrastructure such<br>
as roads and railways.<br>
(vi)	 Fiords are glacial troughs which<br>
were flooded after the rise of sea<br>
level. These submerged glacial<br>
valleys are good water ways<br>
which simplify transportation of<br>
goods and passengers in various<br>
countries such as Norway, China,<br>
<b>Switzerland and several other</b><br>
countries in the higher latitudes<br>
areas.<br>
(vii)	 Glaciated mountains are good<br>
for sporting. Every summer and<br>
winter times, people from different<br>
countries visit such places for<br>
sports such as skiing and skating<br>
as well as mountaineering. This is<br>
common in Switzerland, Norway,<br>
<b>Sweden and other European</b><br>
countries that conduct summer<br>
supports competitions.<br>
(viii)	Waterfalls at their junction with<br>
glacial trough are suitable for<br>
development of hydroelectric<br>
power (HEP).  Both Norway,<br>
<b>Sweden and Switzerland produce</b><br>
a large amount of HEP from such<br>
waterfalls.<br>

(ix)	  Ice caps and ice sheets are excellent<br>
sources of rivers.  Thus, they are<br>
storages or reservoirs for fresh<br>
water. For example, rivers flowing<br>
downslope the Kilimanjaro<br>
<b>Mountains are valuable to people</b><br>
living along the slopes and<br>
downslope of the mountain. The<br>
water is for domestic, industrial<br>
and agricultural (irrigation)<br>
use.<br>
<b>Negative influence of Glaciers</b><br>
<b>Glaciers have negative influence on the</b><br>
environment. These include accelerating<br>
floods whereby melting glaciers may<br>
initiate flooding which can be detrimental<br>
to the lives and properties of neighbouring<br>
societies. Moreover, glaciers mass<br>
wasting, frost, and shattering<br>
avalanches may spark off rock falls as it<br>
disintegrates valley sides which may be<br>
disastrous if the rocks tumble reach the<br>
lowlanders.<br>

<b>River action and resulting</b><br>
features<br>
<b>River is the a natural outflow of</b><br>
water from the source to the mouth.<br>
<b>Rivers are among the major agents of</b><br>
denudation and deposition. They involve<br>
land sculpturing as they flow through<br>
erosion, transportation and deposition<br>
of materials. The eroded materials<br>
sometimes move hundreds of kilometres<br>
from its source to the plain stage. The<br>
materials transported by a river are<br>
known as load and can be in the forms<br>
of mud, silt, sand, gravel or suspension.<br>
<b>However, erosion by runing water is</b><br>
known as fluvial processes.<br>
<b>River erosion</b><br>
<b>In the works of the river, erosion is one</b><br>
of the main geological functions of a<br>
river. It involve physical removal of<br>
the soil materials by the influence of<br>
the moving mass of water. Its intensity<br>
varies from place to place depending<br>
on water supply, gradient and nature of<br>
the underlying rock. The erosive work<br>
of the running water along the river bed<br>
and banks performs four main processes<br>
which are; hydraulic action, abrasion<br>
or corrasion, attrition and solution.<br>
<b>Hydraulic action is the force through</b><br>
which water hits river banks, pushes<br>
water into cracks in the river. The air in<br>
the cracks is compressed, leading to high<br>
pressure which weakens and washes out<br>
weak materials thus with time the river<br>
banks collapse. Water itself is used as<br>
a tool to wear away the surface rocks.<br>
<b>Abrasion: Abrasion is also known as</b><br>
corrasion. It refers to the wearing away<br>
of the beds and banks of the river by the<br>
load (rocks or pebbles) carried along by<br>
the river. The action involves grinding<br>
and plucking of loads against bed and<br>
banks of the river. The materials carried<br>
by water are used as a tool to wear<br>
the river banks. This process is most<br>
effective in rain season and is taking place<br>
mostly in the upper course and middle<br>
course of the long profile of the river.<br>
<b>Attrition: Attrition is a rubbing away</b><br>
or wearing down by friction. It occurs<br>
when materials carried downstream are<br>
reduced in size by striking against each<br>
other and against the bed and banks of the<br>
channel. As they continue colliding one<br>
another, angular rocks become smaller<br>
and smaller to fine sizes, while others<br>
are increasingly rounded in appearance<br>
from irregular shape. Solution: Solution<br>
is also known as corrosion. It occurs in<br>
all stages of the river development. It is<br>
independent of the gradient, volume and<br>
amount of loads carried by the river and<br>
the erosive process. It is related to the<br>
chemical composition of the water and<br>
the rock. Acidic water acts as a solvent<br>
in calcareous rock like limestone and<br>
salt rocks. This acid helps to turn many<br>
insoluble minerals into minerals that are<br>
soluble in water, and which can then be<br>
carried away in solution.<br>
<b>Effects of river erosion</b><br>
<b>These four processes of a river erosion</b><br>
in their combinations, enable the river<br>
to erode its channel in three ways,<br>
vertically, laterally and head ward.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   160</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Head ward erosion: Is the</b><br>
erosion which takes place at the<br>
origin of a stream channel, which<br>
causes the origin to move back<br>
away from the direction of the<br>
stream flow. This erosion causes<br>
the stream channel to lengthen. It<br>
occurs when the river cuts back<br>
at its source and so increases its<br>
length (Figure 3.41).<br>
<b>Figure 3.41: Head ward erosion</b><br>
Vertical erosion: Vertical erosion is a geological process where hydraulic action<br>
deepens the channel of a stream or valley by removing materials from the<br>
stream`s bed or the valley floor. This happens more in the upper stage and middle<br>
stage of river development.<br>
<b>Lateral erosion: It</b><br>
erosion is the wearing<br>
away of the sides of a<br>
river channel. This is<br>
mostly effective along<br>
outside meanders<br>
(Figure 3.42). This<br>
form of erosion makes<br>
a river wider and it is<br>
most common in the<br>
middle and lower<br>
stage of a river.<br>
<b>Figure 3.42: River erosion</b><br>
<b>River transportation</b><br>
This process involves transportation of weathered debris from the valley sides,<br>
rock particles eroded from the bottom and sides of the channel from one place<br>
to the other. The larger the river volume is the greater the amount of energy<br>
available for transporting the load. Too much load reduces the erosive power of<br>
the river which ultimately leads to deposition. As the load is carried downstream<br>
the materials are gradually reduced in size by striking against one another or<br>
against the channel sides. In general, the load is transported by four main processes<br>
namely: suspension (grasses, leaves), solution (mud), saltation and traction<br>
<b>Hard rock</b><br>
<b>Soft rock</b><br>
<b>Overhang cliff</b><br>
<b>Steep-sided</b><br>
gorge<br>
<b>Fallen rock particals</b><br>
<b>Plunge pool</b><br>
<b>Erosion</b><br>
<b>Erosion</b><br>
<b>Erosion</b><br>
<b>Deposition</b><br>
<b>Deposition</b><br>
<b>Deposition</b><br>

162<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
(boulders which moves along the bed<br>
of the river). Traction involves the<br>
rolling and dragging of large particles<br>
such as boulders along the bed. The<br>
saltation process, involves bouncing<br>
of small particles along the river<br>
bed whereas suspension refers to the<br>
movement of very fine particles held<br>
up the turbulence of the water. Solution<br>
occurs when dissolved material is<br>
carried by a river. The movement<br>
of materials dissolved in water and<br>
this often happens in areas where the<br>
geology is limestone and is dissolved<br>
by slightly acidic water. Minerals are<br>
dissolved in water and carried along<br>
in solution. This typically occurs in<br>
areas where the underlying bedrock is<br>
limestone.<br>
<b>Factors influencing the rate of river</b><br>
transportation<br>
<b>There are different factors which</b><br>
influence the rate of river transportation.<br>
These include the following;<br>
(i)<br>
The volume of the river; The<br>
energy of the river available for<br>
transporting the load is affected<br>
by the volume of water.  If the<br>
volume of water is high especially<br>
during flooding the river has<br>
higher ability to transport load<br>
than when water volume is low.<br>
(ii)	 The size of the load: the bigger<br>
the size compared to the volume<br>
of water of a river, the lesser is its<br>
ability to transport.<br>
(iii)	 The relief of the river: A river<br>
flowing along steep slopes has<br>
greater ability to transport than<br>
a river flowing on a gentle slope.<br>
<b>This is because along a steep</b><br>
slope the river moves swiftly than<br>
on a plain land.<br>
(iv)	 Climate of the area: Equatorial<br>
region with heavy rainfall will<br>
encourage transportation due to<br>
their greater supply of water than<br>
arid or semiarid regions.<br>
(v)	 Size of the river: the bigger the<br>
size of the river the greater the<br>
energy, hence the higher the<br>
ability to transport load. That<br>
means larger river have greater<br>
energies to transport.<br>
(vi)	 Speed of water (velocity): The<br>
higher the velocity of water flows<br>
the higher the chance to pick up<br>
larger materials, hence the higher<br>
the chances to transport them.<br>
<b>Therefore, rivers with high speed</b><br>
can transport more than low<br>
flowing rivers.<br>
<b>River deposition</b><br>
<b>Deposition occurs when a stream’s</b><br>
energy is insufficient to carry the<br>
entire load. Also, deposition is the<br>
function of river which is determined<br>
by the failure of transportation. Large<br>
boulders and other heavy particles are<br>
deposited first, followed later by finer<br>
debris. Material deposited by a river is<br>

09/03/2022   17:03<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

163<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
given the general name of alluvium. Deposition is greater in the lower course,<br>
especially during floods, for example, inside meanders.  The channel is normally<br>
too small to hold all the loads hence flood discharges, that is water spreads out on<br>
the adjacent flood plain (Figure 3.43). Thus together with limited gradient, speed<br>
is reduced and the river is no longer competent to carry all its load, so deposition<br>
takes place.<br>
<b>Outside curve erosion</b><br>
(increased velocity)<br>
<b>Inside curve deposition</b><br>
(increased velocity)<br>
<b>Figure 3.43: River deposition</b><br>
<b>Deposition is contributed by decrease</b><br>
in water volume, velocity, presence of<br>
obstacles and stagnation of water when<br>
it enters a lake or sea. Deposition occurs<br>
locally whenever speed is reduced,<br>
for example, on the side of meander<br>
or where there is sudden decrease in<br>
gradient or where a narrow valley<br>
suddenly opens into a broad plain.<br>
<b>Cross profile of a river</b><br>
<b>This refers to the width of a river</b><br>
from one side of a bank to another.  It<br>
usually varies from its source to the<br>
mouth as it is narrow in the youth stage<br>
where V-shaped valleys are formed<br>
and widens at the middle because of<br>
lateral erosion and become wider at the<br>
lower course. Usually it is a function of<br>
lateral erosion.<br>
<b>Long profile of a river</b><br>
<b>This covers the total length of a river</b><br>
from the source to its mouth, forming<br>
a slope because of variation in vertical<br>
and lateral erosion. Like in cross profile<br>
also long profile is sub divided into<br>
three sections whereby it is steep at the<br>
youth stage, gentle in the middle and<br>
almost plain at the old stage (mouth) of<br>
the river (Figure 3.44).<br>

<b>Matured stage</b><br>
<b>Lower course/</b><br>

<b>Figure 3.44: Long profile and cross profile of the river</b><br>
<b>Erosion and deposition in the river</b><br>
valley result to degradation and<br>
aggradations of materials respectively.<br>
<b>As the process of erosion and</b><br>
deposition continue it reaches a point<br>
where the rate of erosion balance with<br>
the rate of deposition. This means that<br>
the amount of loads eroded by a river<br>
will be equal to the amount of loads<br>
deposited. This concept is known as<br>
graded river profile.<br>
<b>Graded river profile</b><br>
The concept of graded river profile,<br>
explains the profile of a river which has<br>
attained a state of dynamic equilibrium<br>
between erosion and deposition rate.<br>
<b>A graded river profile has a gently</b><br>
sloping land profile with the gradient<br>
decreasing towards its mouth.  The<br>
graded profile has a concave shape due<br>
to the reason that, the rate of erosion<br>
is higher in the middle than upper and<br>
lower stage.<br>
<b>Critiques against the concept of the</b><br>
graded river profile<br>
<b>The concept has faced many challenges</b><br>
regarding its validity.  Several critiques<br>
have been put forward to disprove<br>
the concept. Climatic variation: The<br>
climate which affects the amount of<br>
rainfall and temperature can affect the<br>
rate of erosion and deposition as the<br>
river crosses different climatic regions.<br>
<b>There is a period of heavy rainfall within</b><br>
a river basin. As the volume of water<br>
raises even the velocity, erosion and<br>
load of the river increase. Ultimately,<br>
extra load carried by the river leads<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   164</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
165<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
to extra deposition further down the<br>
valley or out at sea. Grade is mostly<br>
affected by changes in precipitation<br>
and glaciations therefore cannot affect<br>
both erosion and deposition at the same<br>
time. Nature of rocks: The type of rocks<br>
where a river flows is not uniform<br>
as soft rock can easily be eroded<br>
while hard rock resists. Therefore,<br>
erosion and deposition rate will differ<br>
especially to the areas with minimal<br>
amount of rainfall with alternate layers<br>
of hard rock and soft rock. In hard<br>
rock making adjustment is difficult as<br>
erosion is not effective to produce load<br>
for adjustment. Vegetation: Vegetation<br>
can reduce or increase the rate of<br>
erosion and deposition depending on<br>
the nature of vegetation in a given<br>
region. Thus, adjustment is not obvious<br>
in any place. River rejuvenation: Also,<br>
the power of a river to renew its erosive<br>
power can affect the adjustment due<br>
to the fact that instead of undergoing<br>
deposition to balance the rate of<br>
erosion and deposition, a river starts<br>
eroding again hence the balance<br>
becomes impossible. Mostly this is<br>
affected by sea level changes (sea level<br>
fall), faulting and abrupt increase in<br>
the amount of rainfall. Thus, the state<br>
of dynamic equilibrium is not easily<br>
attained. Mass wasting: The supply<br>
of materials down the slope by the<br>
gravitational pull like landslide may<br>
affect the equilibrium especially on the<br>
rate of erosion and deposition. This is<br>
in the sense that as deposition exceeds<br>
erosion no point of balance may be<br>
attained.<br>
<b>Stages of the development of a river</b><br>
valley<br>
<b>A river, like an animal or a plant, has a</b><br>
life cycle.  In the beginning when it is<br>
in the youth stage, it flows turbulently<br>
in a narrow, steep sided valley, but as it<br>
moves down slope denudation widens<br>
the valley and lowers its floor.  As it<br>
continues moving down, the slope<br>
is reduced and the erosive energy is<br>
reduced too, eventually it portrays in<br>
the stage of maturity.  At this stage the<br>
valley is opened out more and more,<br>
the gradient is further reduced and<br>
deposition becomes active.  Layers of<br>
sediments are dropped by the river to<br>
the entire floor where a gently sloping<br>
plain is built, known as flood plain.<br>
The river is now in the old stage.<br>
<b>Deposition within the mouth of an old</b><br>
river sometimes builds up a triangular<br>
shaped piece of land called a delta.<br>
<b>Youth stage is often called Torrent</b><br>
stage; mature stage is valley stage<br>
whereby old stage is referred to as plain<br>
stage.  Erosion by running water is<br>
referred to as fluvial erosion. The river<br>
is subdivided into three main stages,<br>
namely the upper, middle and lower<br>
stage. These stages are characterized<br>
by different features depending on the<br>
rate of erosion and deposition processes<br>
(Figure 3.45).<br>

<b>Upper course of a river</b><br>
<b>In the upper course, the cross profile</b><br>
of most river valleys are narrow and<br>
<b>V-shaped while the gradient of the</b><br>
valley is usually steep and interrupted by<br>
waterfalls and rapids. Features formed<br>
in the upper course of a river valley<br>
are; pot holes, waterfalls, plunge poor,<br>
interlocking spurs, gorges, rapids and<br>
V-shaped valleys.<br>
<b>Pot holes: Are circular depressions</b><br>
(holes) formed on the bed of the river<br>
when pebbles carried by fast flowing<br>
river swirl and cut the bed of a river.<br>
<b>These depressions gradually get deeper</b><br>
and deeper and wider respectively<br>
(Figure 3.46).<br>
<b>Pot holes</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.46: Pot holes</b><br>
<b>Waterfalls: Is a place where water flows</b><br>
over a vertical drop in the course of<br>
the river. Waterfalls can be formed in<br>
different ways, particularly when hard<br>
and soft rock stand opposite one another<br>
in a crossing river. Also, waterfalls can be<br>
formed when hard rock, lies horizontally<br>

<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
167<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
over the soft rock, it can also occurs<br>
where a dyke has cuts across the bending<br>
plane. Moreover, waterfalls can occur<br>
when  hard rock dips gently upstream,<br>
and when hard rock stands vertically.<br>
<b>Areas associated with waterfalls are</b><br>
along the edges of steep sided plateau,<br>
along faults scarp or cliffs, along an<br>
overhanging valley and at the knick point<br>
of a rejuvenated river (see Figure 3.47).<br>

<b>Plunge pools: It is a depression formed at</b><br>
the base of waterfall created by the erosion<br>
force of falling water (Figure 3.43).<br>
<b>Interlocking spurs: Interlocking spurs</b><br>
are also known as overlapping spurs.<br>
<b>They are projecting ridges that extend</b><br>
alternatively from the opposite sides<br>
of the wall of a young V-shaped valley<br>
down the river with a winding course<br>
flows. They are formed when river<br>
moves down slope through easiest path<br>
that is soft rocks. This often results in<br>
the river taking a winding course which<br>
undercut concave banks that often stand<br>
up as river cliffs (Figure 3.48).<br>
<b>Interlocking spurs</b><br>
<b>Spurs</b><br>
<b>Skip off slope</b><br>
(concave bank)<br>
<b>Undercut bank</b><br>
(concave bank)<br>
<b>Figure 3.48: Interlocking spurs</b><br>
<b>Gorge or canyon: Gorge is a valley with</b><br>
narrow and deep walls often with a stream<br>
flowing through it. A gorge develops if a<br>
river follows a line of weakness such as<br>
a fault. The speed of water in the upper<br>
section, cause the vertical erosion to be<br>
active while lateral erosion to be inactive<br>
because of resistant rocks (Figure 3.49).<br>
<b>Gorge</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.49: Gorge</b><br>
<b>A canyon is a deep and wider gorge with</b><br>
steep sided valley. The Yarlung Zangbo<br>
<b>Grand Canyon in Tibet is the deepest in</b><br>
the world which extent to about 5300<br>
meters deep. It is a gorge that looks more<br>
impressive in appearance such that it can<br>
even attract tourists (Figure 3.50).<br>

<b>Canyon</b><br>

<b>V-shaped valley: This is the narrow</b><br>
steep sided valley with a V shape<br>
formed in the upper section of a river<br>
due to vertical erosion. The valley<br>
is formed when the river completely<br>
covers the whole floor of the valley in<br>
the upper course. The valley is deepened<br>
by vertical abrasion and widened by<br>
lateral erosion causing it to become<br>
<b>V-shaped. The widening of a valley</b><br>
in the upper course is usually not as<br>
greater as the deepening (Figure 3.51).<br>
<b>V-shaped valley</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.51: V-shaped valley</b><br>
Rapids:  These are formed when the water flows on the steep slope area. The<br>
rapids develop if the face of the rock is steep but not vertical (Figure 3.52). It is<br>
the sudden descend of water without forming waterfalls.<br>
<b>Figure 3. 52: Rapids</b><br>

<b>Middle course of a river</b><br>
<b>At this stage lateral erosion is more</b><br>
active than vertical erosion, hence<br>
the valley has developed an open<br>
<b>U-shaped valley. The volume of water</b><br>
increases as more tributaries join the<br>
main river. Also, the load carried by<br>
a river also increases, as gradient is<br>
gentler than in young valley minimal<br>
deposition starts taking place. Also,<br>
river bends (meanders) are pronounced<br>
and spurs are removed to form<br>
bluffs.<br>
<b>Bluffs: These are features formed</b><br>
when interlocking spurs are removed<br>
by erosion. This often happens at the<br>
bed of a river where water is pushed<br>
against the outside bank leaving high<br>
steep bank.<br>
<b>Riffle and pool: Natural streams are</b><br>
never absolutely straight, even where<br>
they have steep gradients. They tend<br>
to develop bends. Erosion is greater<br>
in the outsides of these bends, and the<br>
eroded material is carried downstream,<br>
where it is deposited as bars in the<br>
insides of the bends. These bars force<br>
the stream against the opposite bank,<br>
causing more erosion and deepening of<br>
the channel to form bluff. Eventually,<br>
the bends become pools and the gravel<br>
bars along the river to form “riffles,”<br>
and the stream assumes an undulating<br>
profile (Figure 3.53). In straight<br>
streams beds of several sizes of course<br>
materials, the riffle-pool sequence<br>
commonly occurs at intervals. On the<br>
average, pools tend to be longer than<br>
riffles, and the bed materials in riffles<br>
are noticeably coarser than in pools.<br>
<b>For a stream that is in equilibrium, the</b><br>
gravel bars remain in the same location<br>
for years known as riffle, although the<br>
materials composing them may change<br>
from year to year. Therefore, a riffle is<br>
a rock area in shallow water or a ripple<br>
on water, caused by bars of sediment<br>
on the river bed. A pool is an area of<br>
deeper side banks of a river at slower<br>
flowing water.<br>
<b>Pool</b><br>
<b>Riffle</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.53: Rocks along the river (riffle) and pool aside deep bank</b><br>

09/03/2022   17:03<br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>River cliff: This is a steep face of</b><br>
river bank of considerable height.<br>
<b>Sometimes it is referred to as undercut</b><br>
concave banks often stand up as river<br>
cliff. It is formed on the outside of a<br>
meander bend by the erosive effect of<br>
fast flowing water.<br>
<b>Lower coarse of a river</b><br>
<b>At this stage of a river valley, the river</b><br>
is now closer to the sea and deposition<br>
becomes the dominant process. Lateral<br>
erosion continues eroding (push) back<br>
the banks of a river. Vertical erosion<br>
is almost negligible, but river volume<br>
is now at its maximum level because<br>
rivers are joined by many small rivers<br>
or stream known as tributaries. The<br>
main characteristics feature of the<br>
lower coarse of the river is that its<br>
gradient is lower than in middle coarse<br>
and the speed of a river decreases, thus<br>
causing deposition to take place across<br>
the whole valley floor. The heavy<br>
material is dropped first while the very<br>
fine particle (silt) is transported until<br>
it reaches the mouth of the river. The<br>
deposited materials on the river valley<br>
sometimes cause the distributaries to<br>
spread into several channels called<br>
braided river. The gently plain (valley<br>
floor) where materials are deposited is<br>
called flood plain.<br>
<b>The features which are common on the</b><br>
plain stage or old stage are pronounced<br>
meanders,<br>
flood<br>
plains,<br>
braided<br>
channels, ox-bow lake, levees, deferred<br>
tributaries and deltas.<br>
<b>Meander: Is a winding path of water</b><br>
channel with curved bends of a river<br>
bank which occur in the flood plain<br>
when the speed of a river becomes very<br>
slow (Figure 3.54).<br>
<b>Flood plain</b><br>

<b>Flood plain: This is a wide flat plain of</b><br>
alluvium on the floor of a river channel<br>
where the river flows in a meandering<br>
or braided channel.  Most of the flood<br>
plains are marshy with several swamps<br>
and small lakes (Figure 3.55).<br>

<b>Braided river: Is a river stream which</b><br>
split into several channels, rejoin and split<br>
again as it flow from one point to another.<br>

<b>Braid channel are separated by sand bars</b><br>

<b>Ox bow lake: This is the crescent</b><br>
(curved) shaped lake formed on a river<br>
floodplain in an abandoned meander<br>
channel. It is a lake formed as the<br>
results of the cut-off of the loops of the<br>
neck of the river meanders. Sometimes<br>
is referred to as horse shoe shaped final<br>
section of a once very pronounced<br>
meander which is separated from the<br>
main stream (Figure 3.57).<br>
<b>Stages in the formations of ox-bow</b><br>
lake<br>
<b>Stage 1: When a river flows over flat</b><br>
land it develops large bends called<br>
meanders. At this stage the river is now<br>
eroding sideways into its banks rather<br>
than downwards.<br>
<b>Stage 2: Due to the erosion taking place</b><br>
on the outside of the bend (meander)<br>
and deposition on the inside, the gap<br>
between two arms (neck of meanders)<br>
are narrowed by erosion.<br>
<b>Stage 3: At this stage the river takes</b><br>
the new shorter route. That is cut<br>
right through the neck and the ends<br>
of meanders that are then sealed by<br>
deposition.<br>
Stage 4:	 The meander now becomes an ox-bow lake.<br>

Levees: These are embankment (ridges) built alongside the river channel.  Most of the<br>
levees are not more than one or two meters high, but some are much bigger. Levees<br>
are formed through successive flooding and deposition of sediments by the river.<br>

<b>Deferred tributaries: A deferred stream</b><br>
is also known as a yazoo stream. It refers<br>
to a river or stream that is forced to flow<br>
alongside the main river for a long<br>
distance before joining the main river.<br>
<b>The point at which the deferred stream</b><br>
joins the main river is called the deferred<br>
junction. This is caused by a levee across<br>
the original confluence of the two rivers<br>
which act as a barrier (Figure 3.58).<br>
