lake and leeves<br>
<b>Delta: Is a large flat, low-lying plain of</b><br>
river deposits laid down where a river<br>
flows into the lake, sea and ocean or<br>
another river. The slowing velocity of<br>
the river and the build-up of sediment<br>
allows the river to break from its single<br>
channel as it reaches its mouth. Most of<br>
delta have a triangular shape and project<br>
beyond the shoreline. The mouth of the<br>
river flowing through delta is dominated<br>
by several streams called  distributaries.<br>
<b>Under the right conditions, a river forms</b><br>
a deltaic lobe. A mature deltaic lobe<br>
includes a distributary network which<br>
is a series of smaller, shallower channels,<br>
called distributaries that branch off<br>
from the mainstream of the river. In a<br>
deltaic lobe, heavier, coarser material<br>
settles first. Smaller, finer sediments<br>
like alluvium or silt is carried farther<br>
downstream and deposited beyond the<br>
river’s mouth. A delta is divided into two<br>
parts, the subaqueous and subaerial. The<br>
subaqueous part of a delta is underwater.<br>
<b>It is the most steeply sloping part of the</b><br>
delta, and contains the finest silt. The<br>
newest part of the subaqueous delta which<br>
is furthest from the mouth of the river is<br>
called the prodelta. The subaerial part of<br>
a delta is above water. This part which<br>
is known as lower delta is influenced<br>
by waves and tides while the upper<br>
delta is influenced by the river’s flow.<br>
<b>The condition necessary for the</b><br>
formation of delta<br>
<b>There should be active erosion in the</b><br>
upper and middle course of the river<br>
so that the river can attain large load.<br>
<b>Also, the velocity of a river should be</b><br>
sufficiently low to allow most of its load<br>
such as sediments and solid materials<br>
carried downstream by currents to be<br>
deposited in the river mouth. Moreover,<br>
the river’s load must be deposited faster<br>
than it can be removed by waves, tides<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   172</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
173<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
and currents. There should be a gently<br>
sloping land along the coast where the<br>
materials can be deposited.<br>
<b>Stages for the development of delta</b><br>
<b>There are four stages for the development</b><br>
of a delta, these include the following;<br>
<b>Firstly, accumulation of alluvium</b><br>
deposits. This occurs at the mouth of<br>
the river at the continental shelf that<br>
facilitates the formation of a delta.<br>
<b>Secondly, the formation of the</b><br>
distributaries. These are small channels<br>
that divide the river leading their ways<br>
into the lake, sea or ocean that facilitate<br>
the formation of the delta.  Spits and bars<br>
arise and lagoons are formed in this stage.<br>
<b>The levées of the river extend into the</b><br>
sea via the distributaries (Figure 3.59).<br>
<b>River Directon</b><br>
<b>Distributary</b><br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Sea</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.59: Formation of the distributaries</b><br>
<b>Thirdly, formation of lagoons. These</b><br>
are areas with shallow or surrounded<br>
by shallow water, formed when<br>
areas of water are surrounded by<br>
tributaries as well as their associated<br>
deposits. The lagoons begin to get<br>
filled in with sediments and become<br>
swampy. The delta begins to assume<br>
a more solid appearance (Figure 3.60).<br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.60: Formation of lagoons</b><br>
<b>Fourthly, extension of a delta after a</b><br>
lagoon is covered with alluvium deposits<br>
(silts). The filling of lagoon with silts<br>
cause further division of distributaries<br>
into smaller one and completely being<br>
covered with vegetation. This stage is<br>
considered as old part of the delta which<br>
is colonized by plants and its height<br>
is slowly raised. Swamps gradually<br>
disappear and this part of the delta<br>
becomes dry land (Figure 3.61).<br>
<b>Main river</b><br>
stream<br>
<b>Delta</b><br>

<b>Types of delta</b><br>
There are four main types of delta,<br>
namely arcuate, estuarine, digitate delta<br>
(bird’s foot) and cuspate delta.<br>
<b>Arcuate delta: This type of delta consists</b><br>
of both coarse and fine sediments with<br>
the shape of an inverted cone. It is<br>
crossed by numerous distributaries<br>

(Figure 3.62). Such delta usually has a<br>
shape of triangle-like structure. A good<br>
example includes the Rufiji delta in<br>
<b>Tanzania, the Nile delta in Egypt, delta</b><br>
of Niger, Ganges, Indus, Irrawaddy and<br>
Hwang Ho rivers.<br>

<b>Digitate delta: Is a type of delta which</b><br>
looks like the foot of the bird with its<br>
claws, it is sometime called bird’s foot<br>
delta. This delta consists of very fine<br>
materials called silt and it has a few long<br>
distributaries bordered by levees that cut<br>
out from the shore. It is usually formed<br>
when the power of waves and currents is<br>
low. A good example can be observed in<br>
the Mississippi delta in USA (Figure 3.63).<br>

formed from materials deposited in the<br>
submerged mouth of a river.  It takes<br>
the shape of an estuary. A good example<br>
is the estuarine delta of river Volta in<br>
<b>Ghana, river Congo in the Democratic</b><br>
Republic of Congo, river Seine in France,<br>
<b>Vistula in Poland and river Ob in Russia</b><br>
(Figure 3.64).<br>

<b>Cuspate delta:  Is a delta with a tooth-like</b><br>
shape.  It is formed where a river reaches<br>
a straight coastline along which wave<br>
action is strong. The materials spread<br>
equally on either side of a river’s mouth,<br>
and lagoon is absent (Figure 3.65). This<br>
feature is found in Ebro River in Spain<br>
and Tiber in Italy.<br>

<b>Importance of delta</b><br>
<b>Deltas are incredibly diverse and</b><br>
ecologically important ecosystems.<br>
<b>Deltas absorb runoff from both floods</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   174</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
175<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
(from rivers) and storms (from lakes<br>
or the ocean). Also, deltas filter water<br>
as it slowly makes its way through the<br>
delta’s distributary network. This can<br>
reduce the impact of pollution flowing<br>
from upstream. It is home to many<br>
species of plants and wildlife as well<br>
as many species of fish. Plants such as<br>
lilies and hibiscus grow in deltas, as well<br>
as herbs such as wort, which are used<br>
in traditional medicines are habited in<br>
deltas. Many animals are indigenous to<br>
the shallow, shifting waters of a delta.<br>
Fish, crustaceans such as oysters, birds,<br>
insects, and even apex predators such<br>
as tigers and bears can be part of a<br>
delta’s ecosystem. Deltas have a rich<br>
accumulation of silt, so they are usually<br>
contain fertile agricultural land that<br>
provides food for worldwide distribution.<br>
<b>Crops such as rice and tea are the leading</b><br>
agricultural products of the delta. It is a<br>
world-class recreational destination that<br>
attracts millions visitors per year. Deltas<br>
are beneficial to the economy due to their<br>
well sorted sands and gravels. Sands and<br>
gravels are often quarried from these<br>
deltas and used in concrete for highways,<br>
buildings, sidewalks, and landscaping.<br>
<b>Urban areas and human habitation tends</b><br>
to locate in lowlands near water access<br>
for transportation and sanitation. This<br>
make deltas a common location for<br>
civilizations to flourish due to access<br>
to flat land for farming, freshwater for<br>
sanitation and sea access for trade.<br>
<b>River rejuvenation</b><br>
<b>River rejuvenation refers to renewed</b><br>
erosive activity in a river valley and<br>
usually it results from a negative<br>
movement of base level, caused either<br>
by a fall in sea level or a regional<br>
uplift of the land. River rejuvenation<br>
is caused by several factors such as<br>
fall in sea level (Eustatic change) due<br>
to diastrophic forces for example,<br>
earth movements. Another cause is the<br>
land uplift and subsidence (Isotactic<br>
change), which occurs when the<br>
land is uplifted in the upper section<br>
or subsides in the lower section<br>
resulting to new base level formation.<br>
<b>Moreover, river rejuvenation is caused</b><br>
by increase in the river volume;<br>
which may be resulted from heavy<br>
rainfall.<br>
<b>Types of river rejuvenation</b><br>
<b>There are three types of river</b><br>
rejuvenation<br>
namely;<br>
dynamic,<br>
static and eustatic. Dynamic river<br>
rejuvenation occurs following the<br>
change of water flow gradient of<br>
landscape with river uplifted by tectonic<br>
activities. Static river rejuvenation<br>
occurs without significant change of<br>
water flow gradient it occurs due to<br>
the increase in water volume. The<br>
increase in the volume of water can be<br>
contributed by heavy rains, ice melting<br>
and river capture. Eustatic river<br>
rejuvenation is the renewal of erosive<br>
activity following the fall of lake or sea<br>
level due to diastrophic forces.<br>
<b>Effects of river rejuvenation</b><br>
<b>The effects of river rejuvenation may</b><br>
lead to the following landforms.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   175</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Knick point (rejuvenation head):  Is a</b><br>
break of slope in the long profile of the<br>
river valley, the point where the river bed<br>
falls sharply hence leads to the formation<br>
of a waterfall or a rapid. Examples can<br>
be seen in Congo River and Charlotte<br>
fall in Sierra Leone (Figure 3.66).<br>
<b>Knick point</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.66: Knick point</b><br>
<b>River terraces:  River terraces are steps</b><br>
or bench like features on either side of<br>
the river valley.  They are produced if the<br>
flood plain especially in the lower course<br>
is rejuvenated.  They are considered to be<br>
paired river terraces if they are equal on<br>
both sides of the river valley. If they are<br>
not equal, they are termed as unpaired<br>
river terraces (Figure 3.67).<br>
<b>Flood plain</b><br>
<b>Terrace</b><br>
<b>River stream</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.67: Paired terraces</b><br>
<b>Incised meanders: Are steep sided curved</b><br>
bends of a river valley produced as a<br>
result of undercutting of river bed during<br>
the process of rejuvenation.  There are<br>
two types of incised meanders namely<br>
in-grown meanders and entrenched<br>
meanders: In-grown meanders are the<br>
ones with an asymmetrical valley across<br>
a profile that has alternate steep sides<br>
with large slip off slopes.  They usually<br>
develop on resistant rocks (Figure 3.68).<br>
<b>An example of this feature is found on</b><br>
the river Mkomazi between Mgwasi<br>
and Kifungiro in Usambara mountain<br>
in Lushoto, Tanzania.<br>
<b>Figure 3.68: In-grown incised meanders</b><br>
<b>Entrenched meanders: Are meanders</b><br>
with a steep sided symmetrical cross<br>
profile.  This type of meanders develops<br>
on weak rocks and where the base<br>
level fall quickly causing rapid vertical<br>
incisions (Figure 3.69). An example of<br>
this feature is found on River Tyne in<br>
Northern England.<br>

<b>Valley within a valley:  Is a steep sided</b><br>
rejuvenation gorge formed within the<br>
former valley due to fall in base level<br>
which leads to river rejuvenation.<br>
<b>Waterfalls: Usually formed due to water</b><br>
in the river valley dropping sharply from<br>
the knick point.<br>
<b>Canyon and Gorge:  These are steep sided</b><br>
troughs formed when the undercutting<br>
becomes concentrated into the river bed.<br>
<b>River capture</b><br>
River capture is also called river piracy.<br>
<b>This is the diversion of part of a river</b><br>
course into the system of an adjacent<br>
powerful river that is able to erode its<br>
valley more rapidly than its weaker<br>
neighbor. The necessary conditions for<br>
river capture are; the capturing river<br>
should be stronger or must have greater<br>
energy for the vertical and head ward<br>
erosion. Also, it should be flowing<br>
at a lower level than its victim and<br>
should have greater power of headward<br>
erosion. There should be a layer of less<br>
resistant rock between the two rivers<br>
that allow easy erosion to take place<br>
<b>It should be able to erode its valley</b><br>
more rapidly than its weaker neighbor<br>
(Figure 3.70).<br>
<b>Pirate stream</b><br>
<b>Pirate</b><br>
stream<br>

<b>Features formed due to river capture</b><br>
Possible evidence of river capture can be seen due to several features formed such<br>
as: An elbow of capture which is the sharp change in direction of a river course<br>
at the point of capture. Not all right angled bends in rivers are due to capture,<br>
others are results of structural control. A wind gap is the valley of the beheaded<br>
stream below the point of capture. It may be left as a dry valley. A misfit: is<br>
the beheaded stream having lost its headwaters.  It may be reduced in volume<br>
causing it to appear too small, sometimes described as under fit stream. Incision<br>
of pirate stream:  This occurs when erosive power increases from its enlarged<br>
headwaters, the pirate stream may be rejuvenated and incise its valley. Several<br>
examples can be cited in relation to the river capture like Tiva river capture in<br>
Kenya and Cunene river capture in West-central Angola.<br>

<b>Waves action and landforms</b><br>
<b>Waves (wind waves) are the undulations</b><br>
that occur on the free surface of bodies<br>
of water, such as sea, ocean, lakes, and<br>
rivers. It should be noted that waves are<br>
actually the energy and not the water<br>
as such, which moves across the free<br>
surface of a water body. Wind driven<br>
waves (surface waves) are created<br>
by the friction between wind and the<br>
surface of the ocean. Therefore, wind<br>
acts as the source of energy for waves.<br>
<b>Waves are created by energy passing</b><br>
through water, causing it to move in<br>
circular motion. An area of the sea or<br>
ocean over which the wind blows is<br>
called fetch. The length of the fetch and<br>
the speed of wind determines the size<br>
of the waves generated.<br>
<b>Elements of waves</b><br>
<b>As wind travels across the surface of</b><br>
the ocean, the continuous disturbance<br>
creates a wave crest. A wave crest is<br>
thus the highest water level of the wave<br>
while the lowest water level is known as<br>
the wave trough. The vertical distance<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   178</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
179<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
between the bottom of the trough and the<br>
top of the nearest crest is the wave height<br>
(H). Also, wave amplitude the one-half<br>
of the wave height. The distance between<br>
two crests or troughs is the wavelength<br>
(L). It explains the horizontal distance<br>
between two successive crests or troughs<br>
(Figure 3.71). The time it takes for the<br>
two crests or troughs to pass the same<br>
point is the wave period (T). This implies<br>
that longer waves move faster than<br>
shorter waves. Moreover, wave is also<br>
characterised by wave speed which is<br>
concerned with the rate at which the<br>
wave moves through the water, and is<br>
measured in knots. Lastly, there is wave<br>
frequency which is the number of waves<br>
passing through a given point during a<br>
one-second time interval.<br>
<b>The size and shape of a wave reveals</b><br>
its origin. Steep waves are young and<br>
are probably formed by local winds.<br>
<b>Slow and steady waves originate from</b><br>
distant places, possibly from another<br>
hemisphere. The maximum wave height<br>
is determined by the strength of the wind,<br>
that is, how long it blows and the area<br>
over which it blows in a single direction.<br>
<b>As waves move from deep to shallow</b><br>
water, they get closer together and slow<br>
down. Also, waves get higher as they<br>
travel into shallower water, making<br>
a process known as shoaling. They<br>
also change their shape from a more<br>
symmetrical wave shape to a shape with<br>
more peaked crests and flatter troughs.<br>
<b>Slows and bends makes a process known</b><br>
as wave refraction which occur as the<br>
wave moves close to the shore. This is<br>
when the section of the wave in shallower<br>
water travels slower due to friction on<br>
the bed than the section of the wave in<br>
deeper water.<br>
<b>Waves travel because wind pushes the</b><br>
water body in its course while gravity<br>
pulls the crests of the waves downward.<br>
<b>The falling water pushes the former</b><br>
troughs upward, and the wave moves<br>
to a new position. The actual motion of<br>
the water beneath the waves is circular.<br>
<b>It indicates that things are carried up and</b><br>
forward as the wave approaches, and<br>
down and back as it passes.<br>
1 cycle<br>
<b>Trough</b><br>
<b>Wavelength</b><br>

<b>Swash and backwash movements</b><br>
<b>There are two different movements</b><br>
associated with waves. These movements<br>
are swash and backwash (Figure 3.72).<br>
<b>Swash (Send) is the mass of broken</b><br>
water which is thrown or rushed to<br>
the beach. This happens when waves<br>
pass into shallow water. Backwash is<br>
the returning water to the sea from the<br>
beaches or coast.<br>

<b>Types of Waves</b><br>
<b>Basically, waves can be categorized</b><br>
as constructive waves and destructive<br>
waves.<br>
<b>Constructive wave</b><br>
<b>The constructive wave is the one whose</b><br>
swash is stronger than backwash.<br>
<b>When the swash is stronger than the</b><br>
backwash deposition takes place leading<br>
to the formation of different marine<br>
depositional features. The formation<br>
or building up of such features due to<br>
deposition has made this wave to be<br>
referred to as a constructive wave.<br>
<b>Destructive wave</b><br>
<b>It is the waves whose backwash is</b><br>
stronger than the swash. When the<br>
backwash is stronger than the swash<br>
the coastal area is eroded leading to the<br>
formation of different erosion features.<br>
<b>The tendency of eroding or destroying</b><br>
the shore has made this wave to be<br>
referred to as a destructive wave.<br>
<b>Causes of waves</b><br>
<b>For a wave to be created on the surface</b><br>
of the ocean, there must be a disturbing<br>
force. The type of force would determine<br>
the type of waves created, so that: wind<br>
stress over the oceanic surface which<br>
produces surface waves (wind waves).<br>
<b>The main forces that cause waves are</b><br>
gravitation pull of the moon and the sun<br>
on the Earth accompanied by centrifugal<br>
forces which produce tidal waves; and<br>
large earthquakes and underwater<br>
landslides which produce Tsunami.<br>
<b>Factors that determine height and</b><br>
power of the wave<br>
<b>There are several factors that determine</b><br>
the height and power of the waves. The<br>
factors include; wind velocity or strength<br>
and its duration of operation. The<br>
distance of open water (fetch) over which<br>
the wind can blow determines the height<br>
and power of the wave.  That is where the<br>
fetch is long the waves is large and strong<br>
since it has enough time to develop into<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   180</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
181<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
maturity and vice versa. Moreover, depth<br>
of water is another factor such that when<br>
wave passes into shallow water, its crest<br>
steepens and then breaks, and mass of<br>
broken water rushes to the beach as a<br>
swash.  Therefore, shallow water reduces<br>
velocity of waves. In deep water there is<br>
a great possibility of having waves with<br>
high height and powerful depending on<br>
the strength of wind.<br>
<b>Waves are created by transfer of wind</b><br>
energy blowing over the surface of the sea<br>
water. The waves play a triple function<br>
namely; erosion, transportation and<br>
deposition. The coastal lines of different<br>
regions of the world are dynamic. They<br>
are always under constant changes<br>
at different scales and magnitudes.<br>
<b>Shorelines are being modified due to the</b><br>
action of waves which are determined<br>
by the speed, direction and consistency<br>
of the wind patterns. Waves are the<br>
major driving forces that break down<br>
or build up the line. As the waves move<br>
and strike the adjacent and bottom of<br>
the oceanic rocks they tend to break,<br>
transport and deposit rock and send<br>
materials of different scale and quantity<br>
into and off beaches or shorelines. The<br>
nature of the oceanic and coastal rocks,<br>
climatic conditions, the configuration of<br>
the coasts and fetch also determine the<br>
speed of erosion and types of features<br>
formed at a particular coastal region.<br>
<b>Wave erosion</b><br>
<b>Waves are energy passing through the</b><br>
water, causing it to move in a circular<br>
motion. In other word, waves are<br>
the outward manifestation of kinetic<br>
energy propagating through seawater.<br>
<b>In reality, the water in waves doesn’t</b><br>
travel much at all. The only thing<br>
waves do is transmission of energy<br>
across the sea. The rate and extent of<br>
wave erosion depends on factors that<br>
control the evolution of the coast. The<br>
operational mechanisms of the waves<br>
and their resulting features involves the<br>
following processes; hydraulic action,<br>
abrasion, attrition and solution.<br>
<b>Hydraulic action or hydraulic</b><br>
pressure<br>
<b>As waves move forward, hits against</b><br>
the cliff directly and loosens the interior<br>
of joints and bedding planes. The<br>
compressed air expands with explosive<br>
violence. Such action if repeated again<br>
and again enlarges the cracks and rock<br>
fragments break apart. The striking<br>
waves exert enormous pressure on the air<br>
trapped in crevices, cracks, hollows and<br>
joined rocks. Such alternate compression<br>
and decompression resulting from<br>
forewash and backwash of the breaking<br>
waves cause tremendous pressure which<br>
weaken the compaction of the rocks and<br>
eventually the rocks are shattered and<br>
break into huge fragments at the bottom<br>
of the cliffs or shoreline. Depending on<br>
the velocity of the waves and the nature<br>
of the rocks, the energy of the waves<br>
may dislodge huge load of rock materials<br>
weighting several tones.<br>
<b>Abrasion (corrasion)</b><br>
<b>This occurs when the waves which</b><br>
contain large fragments hit and erode<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   181</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
182<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
the cliff or the shoreline.  It is the most<br>
effective method of erosion and is most<br>
rapid on coasts exposed to storm waves.<br>
<b>The eroded materials may be carried</b><br>
outwards by the backwash or caught by<br>
next breaker and hurled against the shore.<br>
These materials are tools for abrasion.<br>
<b>The amount of the fragmented materials</b><br>
(pebbles and sand) and speed at which<br>
those materials are hurled against the base<br>
of the cliff are the determining factors<br>
for erosive scale on the base of a cliff.<br>
<b>Attrition</b><br>
<b>This is the process whereby the carried</b><br>
materials such as sand and pebbles<br>
collide against each other and break into<br>
smaller particles carried by waves. The<br>
load and materials carried in the waves<br>
keep dashing against each other and<br>
crush as they are transported toward or<br>
away from the shoreline. The materials<br>
extracted from one geographical<br>
location may be transported hundreds<br>
of kilometers away from their original<br>
sources. In the process such transported<br>
load is reduced in size and structure,<br>
and may be deposited at other sides<br>
of the shoreline, at the bottom of the<br>
sea or dissolved through the process of<br>
solution.<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
<b>This is the process through which the</b><br>
soluble rock such as limestone’s or basalt<br>
rock dissolve in water mostly carbonic<br>
acid, and are removed from one point<br>
to another especially on cliff forming<br>
beaches. The global shorelines and<br>
continental are formed or characterized<br>
with different forms of rocks (soft<br>
and harder, easily dissolved and<br>
impermeable). When the seawater reacts<br>
with soluble rocks tends to change the<br>
chemical composition of those oceanic<br>
rocks and eventually, the contacted<br>
rocks is weakened or dissolved forming<br>
different shorelines’ landscapes such as<br>
beaches and cliffs.<br>
<b>Factors affecting the rate of wave</b><br>
erosion<br>
<b>The breaking point of the wave that</b><br>
occurs when waves break just after<br>
hitting the foot of a cliff and release<br>
most of its energy and causing maximum<br>
erosion. But waves which break before<br>
hitting the cliff have minimum erosive<br>
effects. Also, depth of the sea affects<br>
wave erosion because the deeper sea<br>
levels along the coast the higher and<br>
stronger are the waves than where the<br>
coastal sea levels are shallow. Moreover,<br>
the length of the fetch affects wave<br>
strength. When the fetch is long the<br>
wave tends to be strong since it had<br>
enough duration to collect energy from<br>
the prevailing wind. The strength and<br>
direction of prevailing wind are other<br>
factors that affects wave erosion in which<br>
the stronger the wind the more powerful<br>
the wave is, and the weaker the wind the<br>
less powerful wave is. Also, where the<br>
winds blow perpendicular to the coast,<br>
wave erosion becomes more effective<br>
than when the wind blows parallel to the<br>
coast alignment.  Furthermore, the nature<br>
of rocks affects wave erosion. When the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   182</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
183<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
rock is hard the rate of wave erosion is<br>
low but where the rock is soft the rate<br>
of erosion is high. Also, the rocks which<br>
are well-jointed or have been subjected<br>
to faulting undergo erosion at a faster<br>
rate due to an increased vulnerability<br>
to erosion. Moreover, human activities<br>
along the coast such as building on cliff<br>
tops and removal of the beach material<br>
contribute to more rapid coastal erosion.<br>
<b>Human beings can reduce the rate of</b><br>
erosion by constructing walls (groyne)<br>
and planting trees. Groynes are walls<br>
built perpendicular to the coast line to<br>
prevent erosion by the influence of sea<br>
waves.<br>
<b>Wave erosion features</b><br>
Features produced by wave erosion are;<br>
cliff and wave- cut platform, cave, arch,<br>
stack, stump, blow hole, geo, headland<br>
and bay (Figure 3.73 and 3.74).<br>
<b>Cliff</b><br>
<b>The first stage in the development of a</b><br>
cliff is the cutting of a notch at about<br>
high tide level. As erosion proceeds<br>
the notch is further developed and a<br>
defined cliff appears. The cliff steepens<br>
as its base is attacked by wave action.<br>
<b>Therefore, a cliff is the steep face of rock</b><br>
of considerable height along the coast<br>
(Figure 3.73).<br>
<b>Current cliff location</b><br>
<b>Original position of</b><br>
cliff<br>
<b>Cliff retreats</b><br>
<b>Cliff is undercut by</b><br>
waves and collapses<br>

<b>Wave-cut platform</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.73: Cliff erosion and wave-cut platforms</b><br>
<b>Categories of cliffs</b><br>
Cliff can be categorized into vertical cliff in which the wall is steep/vertical, and<br>
overhanging cliff which is formed in rocks whose bedding planes dip towards<br>
the sea (Figure 3.73). Another category is straight cliff which is formed along<br>
the coast composed of rocks with uniform resistance. Also, there is an indented<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   183</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:03<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
cliff composed of headland and bay<br>
as a result of the presence of alternate<br>
bands of hard rocks and soft rocks.<br>
<b>Wave cut platform</b><br>
<b>Wave cut platform is the narrow flat</b><br>
area often found at the base of a sea cliff<br>
or along the shoreline of a lake, bay or<br>
sea that was created through erosion by<br>
waves (Figure 3.73). Normally, waves<br>
cut platforms are formed as the results<br>
of excessive erosion of the surface<br>
of cliffs such that bulk deposition of<br>
sediments and rock debris accumulated<br>
at the base of the cliff. The wave cut<br>
platform may range from few to several<br>
meters depending on the configuration of<br>
the coast and the amount of the eroded<br>
materials to be deposited. The size and<br>
bulkiness of wave cut platform may<br>
reduce the depth of the coastal water<br>
as it makes the water shallow hence<br>
increasing the speed and breaking<br>
power of the waves. This is due to the<br>
fact that as the waves approach on the<br>
coast (where the water is shallow) they<br>
are steepened and have more colliding<br>
power to splash the adjacent and surface<br>
of the cliffs.<br>
<b>Caves</b><br>
<b>These are holes in the cliff face that</b><br>
develop from waves enlarging an<br>
initial weakness in the rock, especially<br>
along joints and faults. Abrasion and<br>
hydraulic force are both vital to cave<br>
formation. Caves are tunnel-like opening<br>
which may develop at the headland or<br>
cliff. A cave may be wide, narrow or<br>
rounded depending on the nature of<br>
the rocks, direction of the current, and<br>
the magnitude of erosive power. Caves<br>
may be developed into two sides of the<br>
headland and when two caves meet a<br>
feature called arch is formed. The size<br>
of the arch depends on the length of the<br>
headland and width of the caves. Also, it<br>
may extend from few to several meters,<br>
and as it gets enlarged by abrasives<br>
wave and the gravitational forces lead<br>
into collapse of the arch forming stack.<br>
<b>When a cave is developed above the</b><br>
arch or headland as a result of alternate<br>
compression and expansion of the air in<br>
the cave is known as blowhole.<br>
<b>Arch</b><br>
<b>Arch is the feature formed when two caves</b><br>
approach one another from either side<br>
of a headland and unite to form an arch.<br>
<b>Stack</b><br>
<b>This is an isolated portion of headland</b><br>
sometimes can be formed from an isolate<br>
portion of the cliff.  Some stacks are very<br>
large and could as well be called islands.<br>
<b>It is an isolated mass of standing rock</b><br>
closer to the cliff. The size of a stack may<br>
range from a square meter to hundreds<br>
square meters. In fact, some of the stacks<br>
could be established as archipelago or<br>
islands. When this huge or minor stack<br>
is further attacked by waves and reduced<br>
into remnant is called a stump.<br>

<b>Stump</b><br>
<b>Refers to the remains of eroded stack or</b><br>
rock pillars.<br>
<b>Blow hole</b><br>
<b>This is the hole formed at the top of the</b><br>
roof of a cave. This is the result of an<br>
alternate compression and expansion<br>
of air in the cave, in time the top of the<br>
roof breaks and produces a blow hole.<br>
<b>Geo</b><br>
<b>Geo is a long narrow in-let formed after</b><br>
the collapse of the roof of a cave.<br>
<b>Headland</b><br>
<b>Headland is the hard part of coast land</b><br>
extending into the ocean. The extension<br>
of the headland may be influenced by<br>
different rate of erosion and configuration<br>
of the coast itself. Some of the headlands<br>
are long and narrow while others are<br>
wider and extensive. When two or more<br>
separated headlands lay parallel to the<br>
coast they produce the inland water<br>
called bay.<br>
<b>Bay</b><br>
<b>A bay is the part of water extending</b><br>
into the land. Also, it can be described<br>
as an outward coastal land which is<br>
extended into the sea. The extension of<br>
the headland may be influenced by the<br>
different rate of erosion and configuration<br>
of the coast itself. The bay is a kind of<br>
recessed coastal body of the water when<br>
it extended further is called a gulf which<br>
can extend hundreds of kilometers.<br>

<b>Wave deposition features</b><br>

tombolo and offshore bar.<br>

<b>Beach</b><br>
A beach is the wider strip of sand or shingle deposits with water along the sea<br>
coast. It is the land bordering the sea coast in between high and low tide. A beach<br>
is formed when materials carried by waves are accumulated along the coast line<br>
(Figure 3.75). Materials on the beach vary in size from fine sand, to pebbles. During<br>
deposition finer sand (silt) are deposited near the sea and the coarser materials<br>
further inland. A good example of beaches along East African coast includes Coco<br>
beach, Bagamoyo and Mikadini, Nungwi beaches in Tanzania.<br>

<b>Barrier beach</b><br>
Barrier beach is a bar parallel to the shore, whose crest rises above high water, or<br>
it is the long ridge of sand, parallel to the coast and separated from it by a lagoon<br>
(Figure 3.76).<br>

<b>Spit</b><br>
Spit is a long narrow ridge of pebbles or sand joined to the land (main land or<br>
island) at one end with the other end pointing in the seaward side. As a spit grows,<br>
it might become stable and fertile and even support habitation (Figure 3.77).<br>

<b>Bar</b><br>
Bar is a ridge of material usually sand which lies parallel or almost parallel to the<br>
coast or across the estuary or bay commonly occur along the coast of northern<br>
Poland.<br>
<b>Tombolo</b><br>
This is a feature formed as a result of a bar joining an island to the mainland. A<br>
good example is Checil beach which joins the Isle of Portland to the south of<br>
mainland Britain (Figure 3.78).<br>

bay.  Good example is that one occurring<br>
along the coast of Poland. Bay bar is<br>
associated with the occurrence of lagoon.<br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Lagoon is a shallow body of water</b><br>
separated from the main body of water by<br>
a sand bar. Lagoon can either be coastal<br>
lagoon or atoll lagoon (Figure 3.79).<br>

<b>Offshore bar or long-shore bar</b><br>
<b>Is the bar which develops along the very</b><br>
gently sloping coast and runs parallel to<br>
it. An example of this feature can be seen<br>
in Northern America along the Southern<br>
part of the Atlantic Coast.<br>
<b>Mudflats</b><br>
<b>Are coastal wetland saturated with mud</b><br>
deposited by tides or rivers.<br>
<b>Cuspate foreland</b><br>
<b>A cuspate foreland is a triangular</b><br>
extension of the shore line. The formation<br>
of cuspate foreland is called long-shore<br>
drift. When waves approach the coastline<br>
at an angle, this cause the movement of<br>
sand or sediments in the same direction<br>
toward the sea to the right or left as seen<br>
in quatsap point near Ducka bush river<br>
in Jefferson.<br>
<b>The importance of wave action and</b><br>
associated features<br>
(a)	 Waves are potential for production<br>
of tidal power which is also a safer<br>
and environmentally friendly<br>
energy as compared to thermal or<br>
nuclear energy sources;<br>
(b)	 Most of the erosive and depositional<br>
features produce different marine to<br>
graphical features ideal for tourism<br>
and other developmental projects;<br>
(c)	 The coastal areas and shallow<br>
lagoons become the ideal places<br>
for fish breeding ground which<br>
in turn enhance the economy and<br>
the livelihoods of the millions of<br>
human population;<br>
(d)	 The coastal areas offer natural<br>
harbors and creation of attractive<br>
features such as bays, headland,<br>
stack and stump, beaches, cliffs,<br>
and carves which can actract tourist<br>
to visit and enhance economic<br>
development of a country;<br>
(e)	 The coastal areas are now among<br>
the most populated geographical<br>
locations in the world because of<br>
partly, their access, supporting<br>
resources and natural beauty; and<br>
(f)	 They help in the distribution of<br>
nutrients in the ocean.<br>
<b>Tides</b><br>
<b>The periodical rise and fall of the sea</b><br>
level, once or twice a day, mainly due to<br>
the attraction of the sun and the moon,<br>
is called a tide. Tides (tidal waves) are<br>
caused by the gravitational pull of the<br>
moon and the sun on the Earth. Tides<br>
have a predictable daily and monthly<br>
cycle. The Earth and moon exert a<br>
gravitational pull on each other, which<br>
is counterbalanced by forces associated<br>
with the earth’s orbital rotation. Thus,<br>
while surface waves obtain their energy<br>
from wind, tidal waves derive their<br>
energy from the gravitational attraction<br>
of the moon and the sun towards the<br>
<b>Earth. Besides the gravitational pull by</b><br>
the moon and the sun, centrifugal forces<br>
that act to balance the gravitational<br>
force also contribute to tidal formation.<br>
<b>Gravitational pull by the moon and the</b><br>
sun and the centrifugal force together act<br>
to create the two major tidal bulges on<br>
the Earth. A tidal current occurs when a<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   188</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
189<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
tide rises and falls against the coastline,<br>
producing a flow of water.<br>
<b>However, movement of water caused</b><br>
by meteorological effects (winds and<br>
atmospheric pressure changes) are called<br>
surges. Surges are not regular like tides.<br>
<b>Types of tides</b><br>
<b>Usually tides vary according to</b><br>
frequency, magnitude and height. Tides<br>
may be grouped into various types based<br>
on their frequency of occurrence in a<br>
day (24 hours) or based on their height.<br>
<b>Based on frequency</b><br>
Based on frequency of occurance,<br>
tides are further subdivided into three<br>
categories. First of all, there is semi-<br>
diurnal tide which is the most common<br>
tidal pattern, featuring two high tides and<br>
two low tides each day.  The successive<br>
high or low tides are approximately of<br>
the same height. The second category<br>
is diurnal tide which has only one high<br>
tide and one low tide during each day.<br>
<b>The successive high and low tides are</b><br>
approximately of the same height. The<br>
third category is mixed tide that explain<br>
tides having variations in height. These<br>
tides generally occur along the west<br>
coast of North America and on many<br>
islands of the Pacific Ocean.<br>
<b>Based on height</b><br>
Based on height or degree of tidal range,<br>
spring tides form when the moon, the<br>
<b>Earth and the Sun are aligned in a</b><br>
straight line, causing the height of the<br>
tide to be higher. Spring tides occur twice<br>
a month, one on full moon period and<br>
another during new moon period. The<br>
moon takes about 28 days to revolve<br>
around the Earth. Usually, the position of<br>
the moon relative to the sun changes in<br>
a lunar month. Therefore, in spring tide,<br>
the sun and the moon align in the same<br>
direction, causing the oceanic surface<br>
to bulge further, resulting in tides with<br>
higher magnitudes. Neap tides occur<br>
when the sun and the moon are at right<br>
angles to each other and the forces of<br>
the sun and moon tend to counteract one<br>
another. The moon’s attraction, though<br>
more than twice as strong as the sun’s, is<br>
diminished by the counteracting force of<br>
the sun’s gravitational pull. This results<br>
in subdued tidal bulges (neap tides). The<br>
time between the high tide and low tide,<br>
when the water level is falling, is called<br>
the ebb. The time between the low tide<br>
and high tide, when the tide is rising, is<br>
called the flow or flood.<br>
<b>Importance of tides</b><br>
<b>Tides are mainly useful for navigation</b><br>
purposes. The high tides are beneficial<br>
in raising water in shallow shores that<br>
often constrain harbouring or anchoring<br>
of the ships at the harbour. Therefore,<br>
due to heights of tides at the entrances<br>
enable easily arrival of boats and ships.<br>
<b>Ebb- dominant estuaries tend to flush</b><br>
sediments seawards and by so doing<br>
provide more stable environments for<br>
shipping. Tides help in desilting the<br>
sediment and removing polluted water<br>
from estuaries. Also, tides are used for<br>
generation of electricity and this is found<br>
in Canada, France, Russia and China.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   189</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Furthermore, high tides are good for</b><br>
bringing fish at the shore enabling the<br>
fishermen get plenty of fish.<br>
<b>Exercise 4.8</b><br>
1.	 Basing on their modes of<br>
formation, explain the types<br>
of ocean movements, waves,<br>
tides and currents.<br>
2.	 Examine<br>
the<br>
causes<br>
and<br>
importance<br>
of<br>
ocean<br>
movements.<br>
3.	 Discuss the importance of<br>
tidal waves to the livelihoods<br>
of communities living along<br>
the coasts of East Africa.<br>
<b>Coast</b><br>
<b>A coast is a narrow zone where the land</b><br>
and the sea or ocean meet or overlap and<br>
directly interact. In studying the coast<br>
two concepts need to be understood.<br>
<b>First is coast line, which is the border</b><br>
between the land and Sea.  It can be a<br>
cliff line or gentle slopping. The second<br>
is shore, this is the land between high<br>
tidal water level and low tidal water<br>
level. The shore line is the line where<br>
the shore and the sea meet.<br>
<b>Factors influencing the shape of the</b><br>
coast<br>
<b>The coast keep on changing due to the</b><br>
following factors:<br>
<b>Marine deposition rate: It leads to the</b><br>
formation of coral reef and beaches.<br>
<b>Swash and backwash: Essentially, swash</b><br>
has deposition impact to the coastal<br>
landscape. Backwash is useful in clearing<br>
(erosion) the coast from deposited<br>
material, coral reef and formation of<br>
beaches.<br>
<b>Rock type and structure: In terms of rock</b><br>
type and structure (geology), hard rock<br>
resist erosion, while soft rock is easily<br>
eroded hence influence changes to the<br>
coast line.<br>
<b>Topography: This is important since the</b><br>
gently sloping shoreline can influence<br>
deposition features while steep sloping<br>
shorelines can influence erosion features.<br>
<b>Geology: In areas whose geology appears</b><br>
in a form of rock with joints are vulnerable<br>
to erosion which may lead to the formation<br>
of caves, wave cut platform, and arch.<br>
<b>Plate movements and volcanic activities:</b><br>
<b>These can lead to the upward or</b><br>
downward movement of the landmass<br>
along the coastline which may result<br>
from volcanic eruption.<br>
<b>Weathering through wave action:</b><br>
<b>Weathering through wave action can lead</b><br>
to the formation of caves, bay, headland,<br>
and arch.<br>
<b>Solution: This process can dissolve some</b><br>
salt and limestone rocks along the coast.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   190</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
191<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Human factor: These involves building</b><br>
of walls/groynes to protect erosion and<br>
flood and through the extraction of stones<br>
and sand for construction of buildings,<br>
recreation centres thus affecting coastline<br>
in one way or another.<br>
<b>Global warming: It is another factor that</b><br>
leads to the melting of ice hence result<br>
into the formation of barrier beach and<br>
delta.<br>
<b>Glaciation: Glaciation influences the</b><br>
shape of the coast such that during the<br>
glacial era, when much of the oceans’<br>
water were frozen, the sea level slowly<br>
decreased as some water was stored on<br>
land as ice sheet, ice caps and ice bags.<br>
<b>During the last ice age, the level of the</b><br>
sea was lower than it is today because<br>
large quantities of water were locked<br>
up in the ice masses which covered<br>
extensive parts of Europe and North<br>
<b>America. Gradual return to warmer</b><br>
conditions melted ice sheets and their<br>
waters returned to the sea.  This caused<br>
the level of the sea to rise and some<br>
coastal regions were submerged.<br>
<b>Earth movements: Earth movements</b><br>
can cause uplift or depression of the<br>
land, such that coastal regions becoming<br>
depressed and others becoming uplifted.<br>
<b>Organisms: Organisms especially coral</b><br>
polyps influence the change of the coast<br>
by being developed by coral reefs like<br>
that of fringing reef, barrier reef and<br>
atoll.<br>
<b>Increased estuarine: This has an</b><br>
implication to the raising of tides that<br>
in turn reduces the drainage and prolong<br>
inundation of the low-lying coastal areas<br>
impacting agriculture, urban and natural<br>
coastal systems.<br>
<b>Eustatic change: This is concerned with</b><br>
the change in sea level by either increase<br>
or decrease of the amount of water in the<br>
oceans.  This is a result of either melting<br>
of ice or freezing of water (Figure 3.80).<br>

<b>Evidences of sea level changes</b><br>
<b>The main evidence of the sea level</b><br>
changes include: the formation of<br>
new features by increased erosion or<br>
deposition; balance between erosion<br>
and deposition at the river mouth;<br>
destruction of crops and vegetation<br>
buildings along the coast due to the rise<br>
of sea level; the formation of fiords,<br>
estuaries, ria and dalmatian coasts; the<br>
raised beaches along the coastal area;<br>
exposure of mud flats and the wave-cut<br>
platforms.<br>
<b>Sea level rise</b><br>
<b>Sea level rise is the functional role of</b><br>
the ocean surface, which is determined<br>
by the volume of ocean water, volume<br>
of ocean basins and the distribution of<br>
the ocean water. Globally, sea level has<br>
risen to approximately 120 m ever since<br>
past glacial maximum approximately<br>
20 000 years ago. It reached a near<br>
standstill about 2 000 to 3 000 years<br>
ago when the rate of sea level rise<br>
slowed to 0.1 to 0.2 per year. However,<br>
from 1 901 to 2 010 the global mean<br>
sea level has risen to about 1.5 to 1.9<br>
mm per year. The change in sea level<br>
can be attributed to numerous factors<br>
namely glacial formation (freezing of<br>
water) leading to the fall of sea level;<br>
melting of ice as a result of the rise<br>
in temperature results into rising of<br>
sea level; and the rising and falling of<br>
coastal land, when land uplifts the sea<br>
level falls while when land sinks the<br>
sea level rises.<br>
<b>Other factors associated to the change in</b><br>
sea level are isostatic adjustment which<br>
refers to a local change in sea level caused<br>
by the change in the level of the land<br>
due to isostatic movement. The ever-<br>
increasing global warming is the primary<br>
cause of the current sea level rise. This is<br>
explained by the ever increasing human<br>
activities such as burning of coal, oil,<br>
forest and cutting down tropical forest<br>
have that have resulted to increased<br>
atmospheric concentrations of heat-<br>
trapping gases that in turn results into<br>
rising of temperature, which leads to the<br>
melting of ice. Melting ice from highland<br>
areas ends up to the seas and oceans<br>
leading to the rise of sea level. The<br>
rise of sea level is associated with the<br>
formation of fiords, ria and estuaries. On<br>
the other hand rising temperature warms<br>
(heats) ocean water, as water gets heated<br>
expands leading to the sea level rise.<br>
<b>Classification of coasts</b><br>
<b>The length of the coastline is the main</b><br>
underlying reason for classifying the<br>
coasts. Coasts are divided into four<br>
categories based on sea level changes.<br>
<b>These are emergent, submergence, stable</b><br>
or neutral, and compound coasts. Coasts<br>
can also be classified based on the nature,<br>
whereby there are indented coasts made<br>
of alternative layer of soft and hard<br>
rocks, and smooth coast which are made<br>
up of uniform rock structure. Besides,<br>
great variety of coastal classification that<br>
exist the coast can be divided into two<br>
basic types namely emerged coast and<br>
submerged coast.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   192</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
193<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Emerged Coast</b><br>
<b>These result from the fall in the sea level</b><br>
or uplift of the land. The emerged coast is<br>
also divided into two subtypes following<br>
their mode of formation and resultant<br>
features. These are emerged highland<br>
coast and emerged lowland coast.<br>
<b>Emerged highland coast: These are</b><br>
formed where the sea level has dropped<br>
or the land near the coastline has risen<br>
because of the uplifting of fractured<br>
land along the coast or folding of coastal<br>
land. The emerged coast therefore, is<br>
divided into highland coast and low<br>
land coast. The emerged coasts can<br>
also be a result of fall of the sea level<br>
caused by some water being locked up<br>
in the ice masses or ice sheets or due<br>
to the high evaporation rate. There are<br>
four main features associated with these<br>
coasts. First feature is a raised beach<br>
which is the most prominent/dominant<br>
feature of raised beach is beyond the<br>
present shore line. It may still possess<br>
arches, stacks, caves and other coastal<br>
features<br>
which<br>
includes,<br>
notches<br>
and wave-cut platforms. The second<br>
feature is notch that is the V-shaped cut<br>
formed as a result of undercutting of a<br>
cliff by wave erosion during the high<br>
tides. An example of this feature is<br>
seen at Kilindini harbour’s entrance in<br>
<b>Mombasa, Kenya. The third feature is</b><br>
wave-cut platform which are platform<br>
like features standing above the present<br>
shoreline. The fourth feature is old cliff<br>
which refers to the cliff standing above<br>
the new cliff (Figure 3.81).<br>
<b>Old cliff with waves</b><br>
<b>Wave-cut platform</b><br>
<b>New cliffs</b><br>
<b>New beach</b><br>

<b>Figure 3.81: Emerged highland coastline</b><br>
<b>Emerged lowland coast: These are</b><br>
emerged continental shelves which<br>
become exposed as a result of relative<br>
fall in sea level along the lowland coast<br>
or rise of the lowland due to land uplift.<br>
<b>The distinguishing feature include wave</b><br>
action in the shallow offshore water do<br>
deposit materials, thus resulting into<br>
the formation of features including<br>
beaches, mudflats, offshore bar and<br>
barrier beaches, various emergence of<br>
old coastline, river rejuvenation, and<br>
emergence of old beaches. Example of<br>
emerged lowland coast feature can be<br>
seen in the coastal plain of Mozambique,<br>
along the coast of the Gulf of Mexico,<br>
<b>Tokoradi in Ghana and along the coast</b><br>
of Cameroon.<br>
<b>Submerged Coasts</b><br>
<b>These result from the rise of sea-level</b><br>
or the fall in the land surface along the<br>
coast.  Mostly characterized by indented<br>
coastline such as rias and fiords. The<br>
submerged coast is divided into upland<br>
coast and lowland coast. The submerged<br>
upland coasts are the coastlines formed<br>

due to the fall of the land or a rise in<br>
the sea level.  They are less common<br>
and mostly characterized by peninsula<br>
(land almost completely surrounded by<br>
water) representing former upland also<br>
have inlets indicating the former valley,<br>
mostly separated by narrow headlands.<br>
<b>Valleys near coastal areas that had been</b><br>
covered out by rivers become estuaries<br>
or arms of the sea that extend inland to<br>
meet the mouth of river. This can be seen<br>
along the coast of South west England,<br>
drowning many river valleys around the<br>
coast of Ireland, Devon and Corn wall<br>
and creating rias and dalmatian coast. In<br>
<b>Scotland glacial valleys were drowned</b><br>
to create fiord and estuary.<br>
<b>Ria coasts: Ria coasts refer to the coast</b><br>
formed when the highland area with<br>
river valley is submerged and the lower<br>
part of its river valley becomes flooded<br>
by water from the sea. Therefore, the<br>
submerged river valley is called a ria.<br>
<b>Ria coasts are characteristics of upland</b><br>
coastal regions where mountains run at<br>
right angle to the sea that is discordant<br>
to the coast. The rise in the sea level<br>
submerges or drawns the lower parts<br>
of the valley to form long narrow<br>
branching inlets separated by narrow<br>
headlands (Figure 3.82). They are not<br>
glaciated, that is the river does not<br>
originate from the glacial area and<br>
depth increases seaward. Example of<br>
<b>Ria coast can be seen north-west France</b><br>
north-west Spain and South-West<br>
<b>Ireland. Ria are useful in supporting</b><br>
few large commercial ports, used for<br>
setting fishing port.<br>
<b>Figure 3.82:  Ria coastline</b><br>
<b>Dalmatian cost: The Dalmatian coast</b><br>
also called longitudinal coast refers to<br>
a coast formed where mountains or hills<br>
and valleys run parallel to the coast.<br>
<b>The valleys become swallowed with</b><br>
water to give narrow inlet and the hills<br>
form islands.  Example of this type of<br>
coast occurs in Yugoslavia and North and<br>
<b>South America. They are characterized</b><br>
by mountain coastline, deep and lack<br>
distinguished ports. It is good for the<br>
allocation of ports. A good example is<br>
<b>San Francisco Port in California (Figure</b><br>
3.83).<br>
<b>Figure 3.83: Dalmatian coast</b><br>

<b>Fiord Coast: This is the coast formed</b><br>
when<br>
<b>U-shaped</b><br>
glacial<br>
valleys<br>
are flooded with water. They are<br>
characterized by a valley with steep<br>
walls with several tributaries joining<br>
the main valley, that tend to have long<br>
valleys due to erosion and extends<br>
further inland. Good examples of this<br>
feature can be seen in Norway, Chile<br>
and New Zealand (Figure 3.84).<br>
<b>Figure 3.84: Fiord coast</b><br>
<b>Estuary Coasts: This is a coast formed</b><br>
when the mouths of rivers along low<br>
lying coast are drowned with water.<br>
<b>The result of this is the formation of</b><br>
funnel-shaped estuaries (Figure 3.85).<br>
<b>Estuary</b><br>
<b>Land</b><br>
<b>Sea</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.85: Estuarine coast</b><br>
<b>Stable coast: Stable or neutral coasts</b><br>
are coasts showing no signs of changes<br>
in sea-level or in the land but influence<br>
coastal land slowly. In these coasts<br>
the incidental erosion process is a<br>
reversible process representing the<br>
condition in which the coastal profile<br>
is restored.<br>
<b>Compound coast: These are coasts</b><br>
produced due to the influence of a<br>
mixture of several emergences and<br>
submergences as well as other effects<br>
like accumulation of materials.<br>
<b>Coral coast</b><br>
<b>These</b><br>
are<br>
coasts<br>
formed<br>
or<br>
characterized by coral reefs. Coral reef<br>
is a limestone rock made up of skeletons<br>
of very small marine organisms called<br>
coral polyps. Coral polyps are the<br>
small marine organisms created with<br>
hard skeleton. Coral is a general name<br>
for any of a large group of marine<br>
organisms with calcareous skeleton.<br>
<b>Coral coasts differ from most other</b><br>
coasts since they are largely organic<br>
in origin. They originate from coral<br>
polyps, which tend to live in colonies.<br>
When polyps die, their skeletons,<br>
which are made of calcium carbonate,<br>
accumulate with other organisms to<br>
form coral limestone under favourable<br>
conditions.  In this way, large banks of<br>
rock called coral reefs are gradually<br>
built up along the coast to form a coral<br>
coast.<br>

09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Conditions necessary for the growth</b><br>
of coral polyps<br>
<b>Sea temperature which ranges between</b><br>
20ºC and 30ºC is a necessary condition<br>
for growth of coral polyps. Sunlight that<br>
penetrates to a depth of at least 50 m is<br>
another requirement as beyond this depth,<br>
sunlight is too weak for photosynthesis<br>
to take place. This is essential for the<br>
survival of the microscopic algae on<br>
which the coral polyps depend. Coral<br>
reefs require salt water which is free<br>
from sediment. They do not occur<br>
at river mouths where the salinity is<br>
neutralized. It requires plentiful supply<br>
of plant food (plankton) and enough<br>
oxygen.<br>
<b>Distribution of coral reefs</b><br>
<b>The above conditions have encouraged</b><br>
the formation of coral reefs along the<br>
coast. Most of the coral reefs are found<br>
in the eastern sides between 20º and<br>
30º North and South with warm and<br>
clear salt water, the tropical area with<br>
plentiful supply of sunlight. Also, the<br>
<b>Eastern coasts of the continents favour</b><br>
coral formation because they are washed<br>
by warm ocean currents. Coral coasts<br>
are widespread in East African coasts,<br>
especially in Zanzibar and Bagamoyo<br>
coasts. They do not develop on the<br>
west coast of land masses because of<br>
the cool currents which flow along<br>
these areas.<br>
<b>Types of coral reefs</b><br>
There are three types of coral reefs,<br>
namely fringing reefs, barrier reefs,<br>
and atolls.<br>
<b>Fringing reef</b><br>
<b>This is a coral reef attached to the</b><br>
coast with shallow narrow lagoon<br>
separating it from the mainland. The<br>
platform surface is usually flat up to<br>
one kilometre wide and its outer edge<br>
falls steeply to the seafloor (Figure<br>
3.86). The Lagoon may disappear at<br>
low water. Good examples of fringing<br>
reefs are observed along the coast<br>
of East Africa, specifically in Kenya<br>
and Tanzania.<br>
<b>Land</b><br>
<b>Shallow lagoon</b><br>
<b>Sea</b><br>
<b>Fringing reef</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.86: A fringing reef</b><br>

<b>Barrier reef</b><br>
A barrier reef is similar to a fringing reef except that the barrier is located far away<br>
from the coast and is therefore separated from the mainland by deep water lagoon<br>
which is wider (Figure 3.87). Barrier reefs occur along parts of the east coast of<br>
Africa, notably around Mayotte, an island between Madagascar and Mozambique.<br>
They are also found along the coast of Queensland, Australia.<br>
<b>Land</b><br>
<b>Deep lagoon</b><br>
<b>Sea</b><br>
<b>Barrier reef</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.87: Barrier reef</b><br>
<b>Atoll reef</b><br>
This type of coral reef is circular in shape, enclosing a fairly deep lagoon without<br>
any land in the centre. The encircling ring is usually broken in a few places to<br>
allow the free flow of water (Figure 3.88). On the inside of the reefs, sand and<br>
limestone debris collect, and palm trees may grow. Also in calm water atolls are<br>
useful for fish breeding. The best example of atoll reef in Africa is the Aldabra<br>
Atoll that lies between Zanzibar and Madagascar.<br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Toll reef</b><br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Sea</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.88: Atoll reef</b><br>

<b>Formation of coral reefs</b><br>
<b>The origin of the coral reefs especially</b><br>
barrier reefs and atoll has been<br>
debatable for more than half a century.<br>
<b>Several theories have been suggested</b><br>
to explain formation of coral reefs<br>
but none is self- satisfactory and<br>
universally accepted. The most widely<br>
accepted theory was put forward by the<br>
great scientist Charles Darwin after his<br>
voyage to the Pacific Island in 1842.<br>
<b>Darwin’s theory</b><br>
<b>Darwin’s theory also known as subsidence</b><br>
theory relies on the subsidence of land<br>
masses. According to this theory, all<br>
coral reefs began as fringing reefs around<br>
an island. Due to a general down warping<br>
of the earth’s crust, the coral continues<br>
to grow upwards to keep pace with the<br>
subsidence. It then forms a barrier reef.<br>
<b>Eventually, when the land is completely</b><br>
submerged, the atoll is formed (Figure<br>
3.89). Thus, according to Darwin, it is<br>
the subsidence of the land mass that<br>
caused the deepening and widening of<br>
lagoon, hence the foundation of a barrier<br>
reef and an atoll.<br>
<b>Coral reef</b><br>
<b>Barrier reef</b><br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Atoll</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.89: Illustration of Darwin’s</b><br>
theory on the origin of fringing reef,<br>
barrier reef and atoll<br>
<b>Daly’s theory</b><br>
<b>This is also known as glacial control</b><br>
theory. The theory is based on the<br>
changing level of the sea during and<br>
after the ice age. This theory holds that<br>
the formation of barrier reefs and atolls<br>
was due to the rise in the sea level caused<br>
by the melting of ice (Figure 3.90).<br>
<b>Coral reef</b><br>
<b>Lagoon</b><br>
<b>Atoll</b><br>
former sea level<br>
<b>Figure 3.90: Illustration of Daly’s theory</b><br>
on the origin of fringing reef, barrier reef<br>
and atoll<br>

<b>Murray’s theory</b><br>
This is also known as stand still theory.<br>
<b>According to Murray’s theory, the</b><br>
formation of barrier reefs does not<br>
involve subsidence. Murray argues<br>
that coral reefs might have grown as a<br>
result of deposition of the coral debris<br>
on the seaward side of the fringing reef.<br>
<b>The deposited materials eventually</b><br>
form base for further growth of<br>
polyps.<br>
<b>The Famous coral reef areas of the</b><br>
world<br>
The most famous coral reef areas of the<br>
world include the following:<br>
<b>Firstly, the Great Barrier Reef: This is</b><br>
the largest coral reef system commonly<br>
found the Queensland and parts of the<br>
coast of Australia. Secondly, the Belize<br>
<b>Barrier Reef: This is the second largest</b><br>
coral reef of the world that covers<br>
along the western Atlantic coast such<br>
as Bermuda, the Bahamas, Florida and<br>
<b>Caribbean islands. Thirdly, the Andros</b><br>
<b>Bahamas Barrier Reef: This is the third</b><br>
largest coral reef of the world that<br>
covers the east coast of Andros Island,<br>
and the Bahamas in Burma. Fourtly,<br>
the New Caledonia Barrier Reef: is<br>
another largest dominant coral reef of<br>
the world that covers almost the length<br>
of 1500 km. Lastly, the Indo Pacific<br>
coral reefs: These coral reefs cover<br>
vast area from the coast of east Africa<br>
to the Persian Gulf through the Indian<br>
and Pacific Oceans.<br>
<b>Importance of coral reefs</b><br>
Coral reefs are important in many ways.<br>
<b>They are used as raw materials in the</b><br>
manufacturing of cement; they provide<br>
stones which are used in building<br>
houses, bridges and other structures;<br>
and are also useful in protecting the<br>
coast against strong waves which can<br>
cause coastal land degradation and<br>
destruction of property. Furthermore,<br>
coral reefs attract tourists and study<br>
tours, which lead to the earning of both<br>
domestic and foreign currency. They<br>
also, provide good breeding places for<br>
fish and encourage the development of<br>
the fishing industry; and are a source of<br>
income. For example, stones selling to<br>
contractors. Finally, they act as honey<br>
pots that attract tourists to visit oceans<br>
and seas.<br>
<b>Problems associated with coral reefs</b><br>
in the world<br>
<b>Although coral reefs are useful in</b><br>
many ways as earlier explained they<br>
still have bottlenecks. Coral reefs are a<br>
great barrier to navigation. This is due<br>
to the fact that ships or canoes cannot<br>
pass through them; and also, deposition<br>
of coral reefs acts as a barrier to the<br>
formation of attractive beaches as they<br>
block fine sediments to be deposited<br>
along the coast line. Furthermore, coral<br>
masses discourage swimming in the sea<br>
or oceans. This is due to the fact that<br>
diving in the sea whose coast is made<br>
up of coral masses is dangerous. On the<br>
other hand, the caves under the coral<br>
reefs can host dangerous organisms<br>

like sharks and whales that can attack<br>
fishermen.<br>
<b>Problems facing the development and</b><br>
growth of coral reefs<br>
<b>Due to the benefits provided by the</b><br>
coral reefs and their geomorphic<br>
importance, it is vital to explore the<br>
problems facing their development<br>
and growth. The main problems are:<br>
strong winds lead to the formation of<br>
strong and destructive waves resulting<br>
from strong waves, and so hinder<br>
the development of coral reefs; oil<br>
spills, acid rain, poor fishing methods<br>
(using chemicals) in the ocean lead to<br>
destruction of coral rock and death of<br>
coral polyps; and breaking coral rock<br>
for the sake of manufacturing cement<br>
and getting stones for construction<br>
poses a great challenge to the growth<br>
of corals. Furthermore, deposition of<br>
mud along the coast brought by rivers<br>
hinders growth of coral reefs; and poor<br>
or absence of coastal management<br>
policies and laws hinder the growth<br>
and development of coral coasts as<br>
people conduct different activities,<br>
like extracting coral for domestic or<br>
constructional purposes. Also, fall of<br>
the sea level affects the development<br>
of coral reefs; and improper conduct<br>
tourist<br>
activities<br>
endanger<br>
the<br>
existence of coral reefs. Furthermore,<br>
an increase of temperature due to<br>
global warming is attributed to coral<br>
bleaching.<br>
<b>Significance of coastal features</b><br>
<b>The coastal features are valuable in</b><br>
numerous ways. The significance of<br>
coastal features are: firstly, deep, well-<br>
sheltered harbours have been developed<br>
from submerged coastlines examples<br>
include Kilindini in Mombasa, Kenya,<br>
and Lagos in Nigeria. Both of these<br>
harbours have been formed along<br>
submerged coasts. Secondly, coastal<br>
features like cliffs, caves, beaches and<br>
beach hotels are tourist attractions.<br>
Thirdly,<br>
the<br>
submerged<br>
glaciated<br>
coasts (fiord coasts) are good breeding<br>
areas for fish. Fourthly, depositional<br>
features like mudflats and silt/sand<br>
provide fertile soil which is very good<br>
for agricultural activities. Lastly, coral<br>
reefs feature protects the coast against<br>
strong waves which can cause coastal<br>
land degradation and destruction of<br>
property.<br>
<b>Problems facing coastal areas</b><br>
<b>Coastal areas are subjected to countless</b><br>
problems, the main problems are:<br>
water pollution caused by disposal of<br>
untreated waters like sewage and water<br>
containing chemicals from industries<br>
and agricultural areas; oil spills from<br>
tanks; excavation of sand and rock;<br>
as well as poor fishing methods<br>
(dynamites fishing). Land degradation<br>
caused by wave erosion, which attacks<br>
the coastal lands as well as erosion by<br>
rejuvenated river discourage formation<br>
of coral reefs. Floods caused by the<br>
occurrence of storms, tsunamis and the<br>
change in sea level due to melting of<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   200</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
201<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
ice or land subsidence may properties<br>
and livelihood activities. Unpleasant<br>
smell from the sea due to water<br>
pollution makes some coastal areas<br>
unfriendly to users, as it is in Kivukoni<br>
area in Dar es Salaam. Occurrence of<br>
volcanic activities along the coast due<br>
to earth movements may alter existing<br>
coastal features, destroy properties and<br>
pollute water. Also, deforestation may<br>
be caused by excessive cutting of trees<br>
as well as soil degradation when people<br>
establish settlements or cultivation.<br>
<b>Coast management</b><br>
<b>The experienced coastal degradation</b><br>
due to pollution and erosion can be<br>
overcome by numerous measures such<br>
as: Construction of groynes (low walls)<br>
can help minimize beaches, roads<br>
passing near the coast, and stabilizing<br>
cliff from being degraded by severe<br>
wave erosion. Planting of vegetation<br>
especially mangrove trees and grass<br>
which provide a uniform stabilizing<br>
cover that protects the surface along<br>
the coast; prohibiting people from<br>
establishing settlements and cultivating<br>
in the coastal areas, while establishing<br>
settlements and farms far from the<br>
coast; formulating coastal management<br>
policies and educating the people in<br>
the coastal areas on the importance<br>
and ways of conserving coastal areas;<br>
discouraging waste disposal in the<br>
oceans, encouraging treatment of waste<br>
before being disposed into the oceans<br>
or seas; recycling of waste should be<br>
encouraged so as to reduce the rate<br>
of dumping in the ocean (especially<br>
solid waste). Lastly, the government<br>
should open up opportunities for<br>
different economic activities rather<br>
than depending much on the fishing<br>
industry and marine tourism so as to<br>
reduce pressure on the coastal areas.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Four</b><br>
<b>Water masses</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Water bodies are vital resources on the planet Earth, as they support life.  In this<br>
<b>chapter you will learn about groundwater, inland drainage, wetlands and swamps,</b><br>
seas and oceans, and coral coast, and their management. The competencies<br>
developed  from this chapter will enable you to sustainably use water as a<br>
resource for various economic activities. Also, the competencies gained from this<br>
chapter, will enable you to plan for economic development of drainage basins in<br>
Tanzania. Moreover, you will be able to use water for healthy life, and properly<br>
engage in managing it.<br>
<b>The concept of water and water</b><br>
masses<br>
<b>Water is a transparent colourless fluid</b><br>
which forms the earth’s streams, lakes,<br>
seas, oceans and rain. It is the major<br>
constituent of the fluid part of living<br>
things. Chemically, a water molecule<br>
contains two hydrogen atoms and<br>
one oxygen atom (H2O) that are held<br>
together by covalent bonds. Water is a<br>
liquid at standard ambient temperature<br>
and pressure, but it often exists on Earth<br>
with its solid state (ice or snow) and<br>
gaseous state (steam or water vapour).<br>
<b>When water is in liquid or solid state</b><br>
it appears either within the ground or<br>
on the earth’s surface forming a water<br>
mass.<br>
<b>A water mass is an identifiable body</b><br>
of water with a common formation<br>
history which has physical properties<br>
distinct from the surrounding water.<br>
<b>Such</b><br>
properties<br>
include<br>
density,<br>
temperature, salinity, chemical-isotopic<br>
ratios and other physical quantities.<br>
<b>Also, water masses are distinguished</b><br>
by their location and vertical position,<br>
resulting into surface, intermediate and<br>
deep water masses.<br>
<b>Water covers about 71 per cent of the</b><br>
earth’s surface. The seas and oceans<br>
make about 97 per cent of the water on<br>
the surface of the Earth, while the fresh<br>
water found in glaciers, lakes, rivers,<br>
streams, wetlands, ponds, groundwater<br>
and polar ice caps forms about 3 per<br>
cent (Figure 4.1).<br>
