<b>Groundwater</b><br>
<b>Groundwater also known as phreatic</b><br>
water or subterranean water is the water<br>
below the ground surface in the cracks<br>
and spaces in the soil and rocks. Also it<br>
may be referred to as the water present<br>
beneath earth’s surface in rock and<br>
soil pore spaces, and in the fractures<br>
of rock formations. The main sources<br>
of groundwater are rainwater and<br>
melt water which infiltrate downward<br>
through the pore spaces of surficial<br>
materials and collects in large quantity<br>
in aquifers of varying sizes and locations.<br>
<b>Groundwater is the most vital of all</b><br>
natural resource, which forms the core<br>
of the ecological system. It is the major<br>
source of water supply for domestic<br>
uses such as drinking, cooking, washing<br>
and bathing, agricultural, industrial,<br>
and  recreational purposes. Also,<br>
groundwater serves various chemical,<br>
physical, and biological water needs in<br>
the environment.<br>
<b>The properties of groundwater</b><br>
<b>Groundwater is the definitive and</b><br>
most fit freshwater resource with<br>
trace concentration of salts for<br>
human consumption. Groundwater is<br>
differentiated from surface water and<br>
other forms of water masses through its<br>
properties which include;<br>
<b>Composition: The geological nature</b><br>
of the soil determines the chemical<br>
composition of groundwater. Water is<br>
constantly in contact with the ground in<br>
which it stagnates or circulates. Hence,<br>
equilibrium develops between the<br>
composition of the soil and that of the<br>
water in such a way that water circulating<br>
in a sandy or granitic substratum is acidic<br>
and has few minerals while water that<br>
passes limestone containing bicarbonates<br>
during circulation becomes alkaline.<br>
<b>Some of the most typical characteristics</b><br>
of groundwater include weak turbidity,<br>
constant temperature, chemical<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   204</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
205<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
composition, pure microbiogical, and<br>
almost absence of oxygen. Circulating<br>
groundwater can have extreme variations<br>
in the composition with the appearance<br>
of pollutants and various contaminants.<br>
<b>Movement: Groundwater is in constant</b><br>
motion. However, the rate at which<br>
it moves is normally slower than it<br>
would move in a stream as it must<br>
pass through intricate passage between<br>
free spaces in the rock. Groundwater<br>
moves in two ways which are; vertical<br>
(upward and downward) and horizontal<br>
(lateral) movement. Groundwater<br>
moves downwards due to the pull of<br>
gravity while upwards moment of water<br>
depends on pressure that is flowing from<br>
higher pressure to lower pressure areas.<br>
<b>Horizontal movement of groundwater is</b><br>
either due to the influence of gradient or<br>
difference in soil moisture between two<br>
adjacent soils. Groundwater moves from<br>
highly moist soil to dry soil. The rate of<br>
groundwater flow is controlled by two<br>
properties of rock which are porosity<br>
and permeability.<br>
<b>Types of groundwater</b><br>
<b>Groundwater can be grouped into four</b><br>
types based on its origin. Firstly, connate<br>
water which is a small amount of<br>
subterranean water which may have been<br>
retained in sedimentary rocks since the<br>
time of their formation. Most of connate<br>
water is saline which is normally found<br>
in arid and semi-arid areas. Secondly,<br>
juvenile or magnetic water which is a type<br>
of groundwater brought to the surface<br>
due to volcanic activities, usually has<br>
high mineral content. Thirdly, oceanic<br>
water which is the type of groundwater<br>
seeped horizontally through rocks<br>
from the ocean or sea waters into the<br>
ground and is common in coastal areas.<br>
<b>Fourthly, meteoric water which is a type</b><br>
of water directly derived from rainfall or<br>
snowmelt. Meteoric water accounts for<br>
a large percent of groundwater.<br>
<b>Groundwater recharge and</b><br>
discharge<br>
Recharge occurs when surface water,<br>
either from direct precipitation or<br>
from rivers, lakes and snow melting<br>
percolates downwards through the<br>
microscopic spaces in the soil and rock<br>
profile. Eventually, the infiltrated water<br>
may make its way into an underground<br>
water-bearing rock formation known<br>
as an aquifer. Discharge is the outflow<br>
of groundwater from aquifers. This<br>
occurs diffusely across the landscape<br>
such as natural springs, rivers, and<br>
lakes. Recharge and discharge rate of<br>
groundwater depends on the permeability<br>
of the rock and the hydraulic gradient.<br>
<b>Groundwater is dependent on other</b><br>
features constituting a water mass, these<br>
include rock permeability, rock porosity,<br>
water table, zone of saturation, and non-<br>
saturated zone.<br>
<b>Rock permeability is the ability of rocks</b><br>
to allow water or other liquid to pass<br>
through them. Therefore, a rock can<br>
either be permeable or impermeable.<br>
<b>Permeable rocks are rocks which allow</b><br>
water or other liquid to pass through<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   205</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:04<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
206<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
freely. Permeability is achieved by the<br>
rock which is either porous or pervious.<br>
<b>Impermeable rock are rocks which do</b><br>
not allow water to pass through them<br>
because there are no cracks and the<br>
pores are too small. Examples of these<br>
rocks include shales, unfractured igneous<br>
and metamorphic rocks (Figure 4.2).<br>
<b>Moreover, the permeability can be</b><br>
classified as Primary and secondary<br>
permeability. Primary permeability<br>
results from the open texture, coarse<br>
constituents and loose cementation of the<br>
rocks with pores of a certain minimum<br>
size, notably sand, sandstone, gravel<br>
and limestone. Secondary permeability<br>
results from the presence of joint cracks<br>
and fissures through which water can<br>
flow, notably in crystalline limestone,<br>
chalk, quartzite and jointed granite.<br>
<b>Rock Porosity is the physical state of</b><br>
a rock possessing cavities between the<br>
mineral grains making up the rock,<br>
which can contain liquid. Therefore, rock<br>
porosity can be described into four states<br>
(Figure 4.2): Porous rocks are rocks<br>
with pores or holes (however small), in<br>
which water may be held, for example<br>
sandstone. Non-porous rocks have<br>
interlocking grains so that water cannot<br>
get into it. Examples of non-porous rocks<br>
are granite, slate and marble. It should<br>
be noted that igneous and metamorphic<br>
rocks are usually non-porous. Pervious<br>
rocks are rocks through which water may<br>
pass because of either cracks or large<br>
pores, such as sandstones and granite<br>
which are jointed. Impervious rocks are<br>
rocks through which water cannot pass<br>
through because there are no cracks and<br>
the pores are too small such as clay rock.<br>
(a)  Porous rock<br>
(b)  Non-porous rock<br>
(c)  Pervious rock<br>
(d)  Impervious rock<br>
<b>Figure 4.2: Rock porous</b><br>

<b>Water table is the upper surface of the</b><br>
zone of saturation. It is the level below<br>
which the ground is saturated with water.<br>
<b>It occurs when water enters the ground</b><br>
and reach a point where it cannot go<br>
down any further due to presence of<br>
impermeable rock.<br>
<b>As rainfall varies with the seasons of</b><br>
the year, the level of the water table<br>
also rises and falls. The presence and<br>
level of the saturated zone depends upon<br>
several factors, including the amount<br>
of rainfall, the elevation and shape of<br>
the land, nature of the soils and rocks.<br>
<b>Hence, there can be a temporary water</b><br>
table which refers to the highest level<br>
to the surface reached during the rainy<br>
season. It can be intermittent water<br>
table which is the zone where spring<br>
occurs in wet season only. Also, it can be<br>
permanent water table showing the level<br>
which is reached during the dry season.<br>
<b>Therefore, the water table rises after</b><br>
heavy rainfall and melting of snow, and<br>
falls during the dry season. Moreover,<br>
there is a perched water table or perched<br>
aquifer which occurs above the regional<br>
water table. It occurs when there is an<br>
impermeable layer of rock or sediment<br>
or relatively impermeable layer above<br>
the main water table but below the land<br>
surface (Figure 4.3).<br>
<b>River</b><br>
<b>Lens of</b><br>
impermeable clay<br>
<b>Saturated zone</b><br>
<b>Water table</b><br>
<b>Impermeable rock</b><br>
<b>Unsaturated zone</b><br>
<b>Perched water table</b><br>
<b>Figure 4.3: Water table</b><br>
Zones of saturation is the area beneath the water table where all pore spaces<br>
are completely filled with water. In this zone  a solid rock or soil hold a great<br>
amount of water in undisturbed zone under specific conditions. This zone is<br>
also referred to as aquifer or phreatic zone. The saturation zone of groundwater<br>
is further divided into two namely zone of intermittent saturation and zone of<br>
permanent saturation. Zone of intermittent saturation is the zone which extends<br>
from the highest level reached by groundwater after a period of prolonged wet<br>
weather, down to the lowest level to which the water table recedes after drought.<br>
Zone of permanent saturation is the zone where the pore spaces in a rock are<br>
always filled with water.  The upper surface on this layer is called the water-table<br>

<b>Aquifer</b><br>
<b>It refers to the body of saturated rock</b><br>
through which water can easily move<br>
and from which usable volumes of<br>
groundwater are stored. Aquifer is both<br>
pervious and porous. It is composed<br>
of rock types such as sandstone,<br>
conglomerate, fractured limestone<br>
and consolidated sands and gravels<br>
as well as fractured volcanic rocks<br>
such as columnar basalts. The rubble<br>
zones between volcanic flows are<br>
generally porous permeable, and they<br>
make excellent aquifers (Figure 4.5).<br>
<b>An aquifer can be divided into two</b><br>
types which are confined aquifer and<br>
unconfined aquifer.<br>
<b>Confined aquifer: Is an aquifer that lies</b><br>
between two layers of impermiable<br>
rocks. The reacharge of confined aquifer<br>
occurs at a place where it exposes to the<br>
ground surface.<br>
<b>Unconfined aquifer: Is an aquifer</b><br>
which is overlain by a permiable rock.<br>
<b>Unconfined aquifers are reacharged by</b><br>
the infiltration of precipitation from the<br>
ground surface.<br>
<b>An aquitard: Is a zone within the Earth</b><br>
that restricts the flow of groundwater<br>
from one aquifer to another. Aquitards<br>
are composed of layers of either<br>
clay<br>
(aquiclude)<br>
and<br>
non-porous<br>
(Figure 4.4). The zone of permanent saturation is also composed of saturated<br>
rock called aquifer.<br>
Zone of non-saturation is a zone which is never completely filled by water but acts<br>
as an intermediate zone for water to percolate on its way to the underlying zones.<br>
On the process of percolation, some amount of water is retained by the soil, and<br>
used by plant through plant roots. This zone is also referred to as aeration zone<br>
or vadose zone; the term vadose is applied to wandering water found beneath the<br>
surface but above the water table.<br>
<b>Zone of intermittent</b><br>
saturation<br>
<b>Zone of non-saturation</b><br>

<b>Lake</b><br>

permanent<br>
saturation<br>

rock (aquifuge). An aquiclude is a layer which is impermiable to the flow<br>
of water. It contains a large amount of water in it but does not permit water<br>
throught it. An aquifuge is an impermiable layer which is neither porous nor<br>
permiable. It cannot store water in it and at the sametime cannot permit water<br>
through it.<br>

<b>Activity 4.1</b><br>
<b>In a group of five students, develop</b><br>
a model of water table and zone of<br>
saturation for the groundwater. The<br>
model should be designed in such a<br>
way that it is mechanical and can be<br>
practically operated to visualize three<br>
levels of water table and zones of<br>
saturation in the ground. The model<br>
can be either movable or stationary.<br>
<b>Groundwater occurrence</b><br>
<b>Groundwater can emerge on the earth’s</b><br>
surface as springs, wells, artesian<br>
basins, artesian wells or wetlands:<br>
<b>Springs</b><br>
<b>A spring is a natural outflow of water</b><br>
from the ground to the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>Springs are sometimes referred to as</b><br>
outlets of groundwater. They may flow<br>
strongly and ooze or they may seep<br>
out. Springs are categorized into two<br>
groups; namely permanent spring with<br>
permanent water table and temporary<br>
spring with temporary water table.<br>
<b>When the level of a water-table rises</b><br>
after heavy rain the spring formed<br>
may be permanent, and it falls during<br>
periods of dry seasons. When a spring<br>
disappears during dry season, that is<br>
known as temporary spring. Springs can<br>
occur singly or in a series forming a<br>

line of springs called spring lines. A spring may develop along the line where the<br>
water-table meets the surface and it can develop into either a stream, a swamp or<br>
a pond (Figure 4.6).<br>

<b>Occurance of spring</b><br>
The geological structures responsible for the occurrence of springs are extremely<br>
varied. Springs may occur in the following ways:<br>
When a permeable rock lies on top of an impermeable rock: A line of spring often<br>
develops where an overlying permeable rock meets a layer of impermeable rock<br>
beneath it. Figure 4.7 below shows two lines of springs: one is permanent and the<br>
other is intermittent.<br>

When well-jointed rocks form hilly landscape: When well-jointed rocks form<br>
hilly landscape, water enters the rocks through the joints, causing springs to<br>
occur where the water-table meets the surface (Figure 4.8).<br>

the damming effect of a dyke, and is termed as dyke spring (Figure 4.9).<br>

<b>When a spring develops along a fault:</b><br>
<b>The spring can also occur along a fault</b><br>
where a permeable rock layer has been<br>
brought against an impermeable rock<br>
layer (Figure 4.12). Such a spring is<br>
known as a fault spring.<br>

along a fault<br>
<b>When the water table is cut by the surface</b><br>
profile of the depression: Springs can<br>
occur in the depression where the water<br>
table is cut by the surface profile of the<br>
depression (Figure 4.13). Such spring<br>
is known as a depression spring.<br>

<b>Wells</b><br>
<b>A well is a deep hole sunk into the ground</b><br>
to the position of the water table. In most<br>
of the wells, the water-level rises and falls<br>
in the same way that springs flow strongly<br>
or weakly or even cease altogether. A<br>
well is categorized into two that is as<br>
a deep/permanent or shallow well. A<br>
permanent well is the one that sinks to<br>
the zone of a permanent water table,<br>
while a shallow well sinks to the zone<br>
of intermittent water table. Some wells<br>
are sunk into non-saturation zone, thus<br>
are referred to as dry wells (Figure 4.14).<br>

<b>Artesian basins</b><br>
<b>An artesian basin is formed when a</b><br>
layer of permeable rocks lies between<br>
two layers of impermeable rocks such<br>
that they form a shallow syncline with<br>
one or both ends of the permeable rock<br>
layer which becomes exposed on the<br>
surface (Figure 4.15). In the artesian<br>
basin, groundwater is confined under<br>
pressure from surrounding layers of<br>
rocks.<br>
<b>Artesian wells</b><br>
<b>An artesian well is a hole dug to tap</b><br>
water from an artesian basin. When<br>
the well is sunk into the artesian basin,<br>
the hydrostatic pressure in the ground<br>
forces water to come out like a fountain<br>
(Figure 4.15). Moreover, when pressure<br>
surface lies above the ground surface,<br>
groundwater will be under pressure<br>
hence water tapped by a well rises<br>
above the level at which it may flow out<br>
at ground level. On the contrary, if the<br>
pressure surface is below the ground,<br>
the well will be artesian but water may<br>
not flow anymore and therefore it will<br>
require a pump to bring water to the<br>
surface. Such a well is known as semi-<br>
artesian well if hydrostatic pressure<br>
cannot force water to reach the surface<br>
of the Earth.<br>
<b>Water table</b><br>
<b>Rain</b><br>
<b>Permiable rock</b><br>
<b>Artesian</b><br>
well<br>
<b>Impermeable</b><br>
rock<br>
<b>Rain</b><br>
<b>Artesian</b><br>
basin<br>
<b>Figure 4.15: Artesian basin and artesian well</b><br>
<b>Conditions for the location of artesian well and artesian basin</b><br>
The location of the artesian well demand three main conditions that are; firstly,<br>
presence of a permeable rock layer lying between two impermeable rock layers;<br>
secondly, the permeable rock layer must be exposed in an area of sufficient<br>
precipitation; and thirdly, the basin must be dip towards a region where the land<br>
surface is lower than it is at the exposed end of the previous formation and there<br>
must be partial or total blockage of exposed ends of the permeable rock that<br>
allows water to sink and saturate the layer (Figure 4.16).<br>

<b>Factors affecting the occurrence of</b><br>
groundwater<br>
<b>The occurrence of groundwater is</b><br>
affected by many factors. The most<br>
important factors are precipitation and<br>
evaporation, rock permeability, gradient<br>
and vegetation cover.<br>
<b>Precipitation and evaporation</b><br>
<b>Low precipitation and high evaporation</b><br>
rate reduce the amount of groundwater,<br>
in turn lower the water table. On other<br>
hand, when the amount of precipitation<br>
is high with low evaporation, the water<br>
table becomes saturated.<br>
<b>Rock permeability</b><br>
<b>Groundwater recharge depends on the</b><br>
permeability of rocks. Where there are<br>
permeable rocks characterized by open<br>
texture, course-grained constituents and<br>
rocks with pores of a certain minimum<br>
size notably sandstones, gravel and<br>
limestone, it constantly allows water to<br>
pass through until it becomes saturated,<br>
forming a zone of aquifer (Figure 4.17).<br>
<b>However, impermeable rocks, mostly</b><br>
igneous and metamorphic rocks, such<br>
as granite, basalt, and schist may result<br>
into dry water table since they do not<br>
allow water to pass through.<br>
<b>Water table</b><br>
<b>Permiable rock</b><br>
<b>Acquiclude</b><br>
<b>Acquifer</b><br>
<b>Impermiable rock</b><br>
<b>Figure 4.17: Zone of aquifer</b><br>
<b>Gradient</b><br>
<b>The gradient also known as slope can</b><br>
affect water percolation. The nature of<br>
gradient greatly influences the rate of<br>
water percolation into the ground. On<br>
steep slope infiltration is low because<br>
water runs faster, but on a gentle slope<br>
surface water infiltrates at high rate<br>
since water runs slowly or is stationary.<br>
<b>Therefore, groundwater can be recharged</b><br>
easily in a gentle slope than in a steep<br>
slope.<br>

09/03/2022   17:05<br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
215<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Vegetation cover</b><br>
<b>Usually water tends to sink down in</b><br>
areas of dense vegetation cover but in<br>
bare rocks (surface) water runs freely<br>
down the slope such that very little<br>
amount infiltrates into the ground to<br>
form groundwater.<br>
<b>Human activities</b><br>
<b>Several activities undertaken by man</b><br>
like irrigation contribute to the amount<br>
of water that percolates into the ground<br>
and forms groundwater. Therefore,<br>
the more the activities of this nature,<br>
the larger the amount of groundwater<br>
collected underground.<br>

<b>Using the knowledge gained on the</b><br>
emergence of groundwater onto<br>
the surface, improvise a model of<br>
artesian basin showing an artesian<br>
well. Demonstrate the model to the<br>
class. Respond to questions asked<br>
(if any) by fellow students.<br>
<b>Hydrological cycle</b><br>
<b>Hydrological cycle is the continuous</b><br>
movement of water in the air, on the<br>
surface and below the earth’s surface,<br>
to the ocean and back to the air. The<br>
driving force of the hydrologic cycle<br>
is the Sun, which provides the energy<br>
needed for evaporation. The hydrologic<br>
cycle consist of physical processes that<br>
create a continuous water movement on<br>
the Earth that has neither a beginning<br>
nor an end, it is an incessant process.<br>
<b>The water cycle processes in the Earth</b><br>
system (atmosphere, hydrosphere,<br>
lithosphere, and biosphere) involves<br>
evaporation, transpiration, condensation,<br>
precipitation, infiltration, runoff, and<br>
groundwater flow. Although the total<br>
amount of water within the cycle<br>
remains essentially constant, its<br>
distribution among the various pathways<br>
is continually changing. Just because<br>
<b>Antarctica is frozen, it does not mean</b><br>
that evaporation is not taking place,<br>
ice can turn directly to water vapor by<br>
sublimation. Again, it should not be<br>
taken for granted that in deserts like<br>
<b>Sahara often lack precipitation, rather</b><br>
it rains but it evaporates before making<br>
it to the ground.<br>
<b>Essentially, the unendless natural process</b><br>
of moving water can be explained by<br>
water which evaporates from the water<br>
bodies, plant leaves (vegetation) and<br>
the land surface into the atmosphere<br>
in form of water vapour and thereafter<br>
water vapor condenses to form clouds<br>
and precipitates in the forms of rain,<br>
snow, sleet, or hail back to the earth’s<br>
surface. The precipitation falls on open<br>
bodies of water, land surface and some<br>
precipitation is intercepted by vegetation.<br>
<b>Precipitation on the land surface</b><br>
forms surface runoff and/or recharges<br>
groundwater. Water that infiltrates into<br>
the ground through percolation becomes<br>
part of groundwater which eventually<br>
reappear as streamflow or can be mixed<br>
with saline groundwater in coastal zones.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   215</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Finally, water re-enters rivers, lakes</b><br>
and oceans in which there is ongoing<br>
evaporation process (Figure 4.18).<br>
<b>Processes of the hydrological cycle</b><br>
<b>The hydrological cycle can be studied</b><br>
by starting at any of the following<br>
processes;<br>
<b>Evaporation is the process whereby</b><br>
liquid water is converted to water<br>
vapour (vaporization) and removed from<br>
the surface. Water evaporates from a<br>
variety of surfaces such as rivers, lakes,<br>
pavements, soils and wet vegetation.<br>
<b>It occurs when radiant energy from</b><br>
the sun heats water, causing the water<br>
molecules to become so active such that<br>
some of them rise into the atmosphere<br>
as vapour (Figure 4.18). Transpiration<br>
is a process through which water is<br>
transported through a plant from its<br>
roots to tiny pores on the underside of<br>
its leaves (stomata) where it evaporates<br>
into the atmosphere as a water vapour.<br>
<b>It occurs as a result of water being taken</b><br>
by plants through the roots and released<br>
into atmosphere through the leaves.<br>
<b>Evaporation and transpiration occur</b><br>
simultaneously, and there is no easy<br>
way of distinguishing between the two<br>
processes. The simultaneous process of<br>
evaporation and transpiration is termed as<br>
evapotranspiration. Evapotranspiration<br>
is the combination effects of two separate<br>
processes whereby water is lost on<br>
one hand from the land surface by<br>
evaporation and on the other hand from<br>
the crop or vegetation by transpiration.<br>
<b>Condensation is the process of changing</b><br>
water vapour to liquid water droplets<br>
in the air creating clouds and fog.<br>
<b>Water vapour in the air rises mostly</b><br>
by convection. This means that warm,<br>
humid air will rise, while cooler air<br>
will flow downward. As the warmer<br>
air rises, the water vapour will lose<br>
energy, causing its temperature to<br>
drop. Then water vapour will change<br>
its state into liquid or ice. Precipitation<br>
is deposition of moisture released from<br>
clouds as rain, sleet, snow, or hail falling<br>
on the earth’s surface. Precipitation is<br>
formed when water vapor, which has<br>
condensed in the atmosphere, becomes<br>
too heavy to be held in the atmospheric<br>
air currents and eventually falls on the<br>
earth’s surface. The warm air rises,<br>
cools and condenses to form clouds.<br>
<b>When precipitation reaches the Earth’s</b><br>
surface, it adds to the surface water in<br>
streams and lakes, or infiltrate the soil<br>
to become groundwater. A portion of<br>
precipitation that reaches the earth’s<br>
surface seeps into the ground through the<br>
process called infiltration. The amount<br>
of water that infiltrates the soil varies<br>
with the degree of land slope, forms and<br>
amount of precipitation, the amount and<br>
types of vegetation, soil and rock types,<br>
and initial soil water content. The more<br>
openings in the surface (cracks, pores,<br>
joints), the more infiltration occurs.<br>
<b>Precipitation that reaches the surface of</b><br>
the Earth but does not infiltrate the soil<br>
is called runoff. Runoff can also come<br>
from melted snow and ice. When there<br>
is a lot of precipitation, soils become<br>
saturated, thus rainwater can no longer<br>
enter it. Runoff will eventually drain into<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   216</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
217<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
creeks, streams, and rivers, lakes and oceans. Along the way, a portion of runoff<br>
evaporates, percolates into the ground, or is used for agricultural, residential, or<br>
industrial purposes. Generally, once water reaches the ground one of the three<br>
processes may occur; some of the water may evaporate back into the atmosphere,<br>
runs on the surface (runoff) or infiltrate and percolate through the soil into the rock<br>
to form groundwater or phreatic water. Groundwater either seeps to make its way<br>
to the oceans, lakes, rivers and streams, or is released back into the atmosphere<br>
through transpiration and evaporation. The balance of water that remains on<br>
the earth’s surface is runoff, which enters into lakes, oceans, rivers and streams<br>
and is carried back to the oceans, where there is ongoing processes of the cycle<br>
as well.<br>

<b>Moreover, once water enters the ground</b><br>
or rock it keeps percolating until it<br>
reaches the water table and the zone<br>
of saturation. The above circulation of<br>
water in the Earth system confirms the<br>
two ways in which water can enter in the<br>
ground or rocks which are groundwater in<br>
karst region and groundwater pollution.<br>
<b>Groundwater in a karst region</b><br>
<b>A karst region is an area characterised</b><br>
by developed zones of enhanced<br>
porosity within carbonate rocks that<br>
create a highly heterogeneous aquifer<br>
system with rapid rates of ground-water<br>
movement and recharge. Karst regions<br>
are pervious but not porous, meaning that<br>
water can pass along bedding planes and<br>
downward through joints but not through<br>
the rock itself. Karst area is formed by the<br>
dissolving rocks that are characterized<br>
by sinkholes, sinking streams, closed<br>
depressions, subterranean drainage and<br>
caves which are underlined by limestone<br>
or dolomite.  Its topography is chiefly<br>
formed by the dissolving of rock. Kast<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   217</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
218<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
areas have a wide range of closed<br>
surface depressions, well-developed<br>
underground drainage systems and<br>
strong interaction between circulation<br>
of surface water and groundwater.<br>
<b>Aquifers and groundwater system</b><br>
in a karst region<br>
<b>A karst aquifer is a body of soluble</b><br>
rock that conducts water principally<br>
through enhanced porosity formed by<br>
dissolution of the rock. Aquifers are<br>
commonly structured as a branching<br>
network of tributary channels, which<br>
connect together to drain a groundwater<br>
basin and discharge to a perennial<br>
spring. The drainage patterns of karst<br>
channels (which flow underground)<br>
resemble the branching pattern formed<br>
by streams flowing above the ground<br>
across insoluble rocks.<br>
<b>In karst aquifers, the channels and</b><br>
caves drain the pore space between the<br>
limestone rock and the fractures formed<br>
by joints, bedding planes and faults.<br>
<b>The water tables in karst areas can be</b><br>
highly irregular in elevation. However,<br>
because water-carrying channels can<br>
develop at various elevations, water<br>
may also be encountered in perched<br>
aquifers. Karst aquifers are divided into<br>
three types which are Kast aquifers with<br>
large conduits, narrow joints and highly<br>
developed with interconnected large<br>
karsts and narrow karst fissures.<br>
<b>Karst springs</b><br>
<b>A karst spring occurs when the</b><br>
groundwater table intersects with the<br>
earth’s surface or the groundwater<br>
rises to the surface through rock faults,<br>
fractures or depression. In most cases<br>
water surfacing from the karst springs<br>
is groundwater to a large scale. Karst<br>
springs occur where the groundwater<br>
discharges from channels or caves.<br>
<b>Karst springs or “cave springs” can have</b><br>
large openings which discharge very<br>
large volumes of water. The sinkholes<br>
and sinking streams that drain to a<br>
large karst spring can be many miles<br>
away from the spring.  Frequently,<br>
groundwater rises to the surface from a<br>
completely water filled stream.  A karst<br>
spring receives drainage from all the<br>
sinkholes and sinking streams within<br>
its groundwater basin, equivalent to a<br>
watershed on the surface. The channels<br>
carrying water from each point where<br>
water sinks join together underground<br>
to form successively larger openings<br>
with ever-increasing flow, which<br>
eventually discharges at a spring. Karst<br>
springs can be perennial (permanent)<br>
or intermittent (temporary, ephemeral<br>
or seasonal). From a perennial spring,<br>
stream flows above land throughout the<br>
year, while the intermittent ones’ flows at<br>
irregular intervals depending on seasonal<br>
variations in rainfall. Sometimes the<br>
intermittent spring can dry several<br>
times or most of the year. Seasonal<br>
springs flow only during a certain<br>
(wet or rainy) season while ephemeral<br>
springs are active only for a short time<br>
as a results of intensive precipitation.<br>
<b>Intermittent springs are very often in</b><br>
karst area.<br>

09/03/2022   17:05<br>

<b>Groundwater Pollution</b><br>
<b>Pollutants found in groundwater can</b><br>
be classified into two broad groups:<br>
biological and chemical pollutants.<br>
<b>Chemical pollutants can be further</b><br>
sub divided into two groups, namely;<br>
inorganic and organic pollutants.<br>
<b>Inorganic pollutants include heavy</b><br>
metals (cations), such as cadmium (Cd),<br>
chromium (Cr), lead (Pb), manganese<br>
(Mn), mercury (Hg), and nickel (Ni)<br>
naturally occurring in the soil. These<br>
highly toxic chemicals may reach<br>
groundwater after mineral dissolution<br>
with acidic waters (from mining or<br>
industrial activities) or after industrial<br>
emissions. Organic pollutants are<br>
those chemicals that contain carbon<br>
as a molecular backbone. Chemicals<br>
that most frequently leach into<br>
groundwater include trihalomethanes<br>
(chloroform, bromodi-chloromethane),<br>
solvents<br>
(tetrachloroethylene),<br>
gasoline components (benzene,<br>
toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene),<br>
and pharmaceuticals and personal care<br>
products such as caffeine, carbamazepine<br>
and sulfamethoxazole. Biological<br>
pollutants include bacteria, viruses,<br>
and parasites that are responsible for<br>
waterborne diseases, such as typhoid<br>
fever, cholera, dysentery, polio,<br>
hepatitis, and schistosomiasis. The<br>
presence of Coliform bacteria is<br>
indicator of recent fecal pollution. This<br>
type of contamination is exclusively<br>
attributed to human and animal<br>
waste.<br>
<b>Sources of Groundwater Pollution</b><br>
<b>Groundwater pollution may originate</b><br>
from point sources and nonpoint<br>
sources. Point sources can be well<br>
identified in space, for example, landfills,<br>
underground storage tanks, or septic<br>
systems. Nonpoint sources are dispersed<br>
over large areas, for example, pesticide<br>
application in agriculture. The latter<br>
is more difficult to identify, measure,<br>
and control than the former. Generally,<br>
the following are the main sources of<br>
groundwater pollution.<br>
<b>Onsite sewage facilities like septic tanks</b><br>
systems. A septic system failure causes<br>
untreated wastewater to leak to the surface<br>
or to seep into the soils. These systems<br>
are known to be ineffective in nutrient<br>
(nitrates and phosphates) removal, and<br>
therefore, nutrient migration into source<br>
of water is directly related to the retention<br>
capacity of the soil surrounding the<br>
leaching field of the septic system. The<br>
chemicals used to clean these systems<br>
can create additional pollutants that may<br>
eventually end up in the groundwater.<br>
<b>Landfills generate leachates that represent</b><br>
a major threat to groundwater quality.<br>
<b>The leachates result from the liquid</b><br>
waste dumped in the landfill and the<br>
decomposition of solid waste aided by<br>
precipitation and surface runoff events.<br>
<b>Improper disposal of untreated industrial</b><br>
and hazardous chemicals into municipal<br>
waste landfills increases the presence<br>
of toxic and hazardous chemicals<br>
in the leachates which contaminate<br>
groundwater. Surface impoundments<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   219</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
220<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
are shallow lagoons or reservoirs<br>
commonly used to temporarily store<br>
or treat hazardous liquid waste. These<br>
sites are protected with several liners<br>
(plastic/clay) at the bottom in order to<br>
prevent leaks. However, the liquid waste<br>
confined in them may potentially enter<br>
the subsoil because these liners are not<br>
leak-free.<br>
<b>Land application of pesticides and</b><br>
fertilizers, especially, once released into<br>
the environment, may have different<br>
behaviors and fates. Some of them may<br>
break down or degrade by biotic and<br>
abiotic processes while others remain<br>
unchanged for a long period of time,<br>
and are called persistent environmental<br>
pollutants. As a consequence, they freely<br>
move with water and become part of<br>
groundwater pollutants.<br>
<b>The use of underground and above ground</b><br>
storage tanks to store chemicals (for<br>
industrial processes), fuels (petroleum<br>
solvents, motor fuels, heating oil), and<br>
wastes (used oil and industrial hazardous<br>
wastes) when handled inappropriately,<br>
may cause groundwater pollution. Spills<br>
out of these tanks may occur when<br>
they are overfilled. Therefore, the tank<br>
content can seep out, move within the<br>
subsoil, and eventually contaminate<br>
groundwater. Storage tanks are also<br>
used for the transportation of chemicals<br>
by trucks and trains. Accidental spills<br>
occurring during transportation and<br>
transfer operations constitute a relevant<br>
source of hazardous chemicals into<br>
groundwater.<br>
<b>Pipelines are used to transport any</b><br>
chemical stable substance (liquid or<br>
gas), such as industrial chemicals and<br>
oil brines. They have been also set for the<br>
transport of wastes, for example, sewer<br>
pipelines. Regardless of pipe material,<br>
when leaks eventually appear in pipeline<br>
systems, lead to a risk of groundwater<br>
contamination. In this regard, a high risk<br>
of biological and chemical pollution is<br>
associated with sewer pipelines.<br>
<b>Mining activities have a big impact on</b><br>
the quality of water resources, thus pose a<br>
threat to groundwater. After precipitation<br>
events, the acid mine drainages, which<br>
consist of leachates containing metals<br>
and minerals generated from mine<br>
wastes for hundreds of years, can move<br>
through the unsaturated zone down to the<br>
saturated zone. Moreover, groundwater<br>
can also be contaminated after spill or<br>
leak of the chemicals used to separate a<br>
target mineral substance from the ore,<br>
for example, cyanide or sulfuric acid.<br>
<b>Application of de-icing chemicals</b><br>
during de-icing of roadways, airports,<br>
and aircraft surfaces is a common<br>
maintenance activity in countries<br>
with severe winters. This is usually<br>
accomplished with chemicals such as<br>
inorganic salts like Sodium Chloride<br>
(NaCl), Magnesium Chloride (MgCl2),<br>
<b>Calcium Chloride (CaCl2), or Potassium</b><br>
<b>Chloride (KCl), and alcohols and glycols</b><br>
(propylene glycol and ethylene glycol).<br>
<b>The brines formed as the salt dissolves</b><br>
and after application of the de-icing<br>
formulation can be introduced into the<br>

subsurface with precipitation events and<br>
alter groundwater quality.<br>
<b>The importance of groundwater in</b><br>
life<br>
<b>Ground water is beneficial in varying</b><br>
ways, mainly: spring influences sites for<br>
settlement in many regions of the world.<br>
<b>For example, spring-line settlement in</b><br>
<b>London basin, Durban and spring line</b><br>
village along the Atlas Mountain in<br>
<b>North Africa. Springs are also a source</b><br>
of domestic water, for example Shiri<br>
spring located in Hai district is the main<br>
source of domestic water to the town<br>
of Moshi in Kilimanjaro. Also, water<br>
from wells is used for irrigation in the<br>
dry areas for example agriculture is<br>
practiced around the oases on Sahara<br>
<b>Desert, in which commonly grown crops</b><br>
include dates and banana. Besides this,<br>
ground water forms a major source of<br>
water for domestic and industrial use. It<br>
acts as a source of rivers for example in<br>
<b>Kenya Highlands and those of Southern</b><br>
<b>Tanzania. Furthermore, in volcanic</b><br>
region, it may form hot spring and<br>
geysers which sometimes are used for<br>
generating geothermal power. A good<br>
example is the Great Rift valley of Kenya.<br>
<b>Moreover, the artesian wells raise water</b><br>
from deep levels in the ground where<br>
pumping could be used. In Kansas, USA,<br>
such water is used in cattle ranching.<br>
<b>Lastly, underground streams are useful in</b><br>
keeping some lakes and oceans fresh due<br>
to the groundwater seeps to the oceans,<br>
lakes, rivers and streams making water<br>
suitable for industrial and home use.<br>
<b>Lake Naivasha, for example, has an</b><br>
underground seep to the Indian Ocean.<br>

<b>Inland drainage</b><br>
<b>Inland drainage refers to a drainage in</b><br>
which rivers do not reach an ocean or<br>
sea instead empty their water in lakes<br>
or an inland sea. It is a drainage system<br>
or pattern which is fed by rainwater and<br>
sometimes by melting ice and snow.<br>
<b>The volume of water in the streams and</b><br>
rivers varies considerably because of<br>
variation in the amount of rainfall and<br>
melting of ice or snow. Inland drainage<br>
is characterized by different types of<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   221</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
222<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
drainages systems which is accordant<br>
and discordant drainage systems.<br>
<b>Accordant drainage system in the one</b><br>
whose pattern of flow corresponds to<br>
the structure and relief on the landscape<br>
over which it flows. Whereas, discordant<br>
drainage system describe the drainage<br>
pattern that cuts across the structural gain<br>
of an area. Besides this, a drainage basin<br>
or catchment is any area of land where<br>
precipitation collects and drains off into a<br>
common outlet such as river, bay or other<br>
bodies of water. In the drainage basin or<br>
catchment all surface water from a river<br>
and all its tributaries intersect at a single<br>
point of lower elevation. Its boundary is<br>
marked by highland which makes any<br>
precipitation drain into adjacent basins.<br>
This boundary is called a watershed.<br>
<b>The main characteristics of inland</b><br>
drainage are: drainage streams join lakes<br>
or inland seas; they are fed by rainwater;<br>
during rains, they experience flash floods;<br>
they dry up during the dry season; and<br>
also, they are less than ten percent of the<br>
internal drainage formed by the inland<br>
drainage along arid and semi-arid areas.<br>
<b>On the whole, inland drainage should not</b><br>
be confused with inland water. Inland<br>
waters are water bodies located within<br>
land boundaries such as ponds, lakes,<br>
streams, and flood plains. Inland water<br>
can be found along coastal areas as well.<br>
<b>Drainage system</b><br>
<b>Drainage system refers to the origin and</b><br>
development of streams through time.<br>
<b>The origin and subsequent evolution</b><br>
of any drainage system in a region are<br>
determined and controlled by two factors<br>
namely; nature of initial surface and<br>
slope and geological structure such as<br>
folds, faults, fractures, joint, dips and<br>
strikes of rock beds and types of rocks.<br>
<b>Drainage systems are divided into two</b><br>
broad categories on the basis of the<br>
adjustment of the stream to the initial<br>
surface and geological structure. The<br>
two categories are sequent streams or<br>
accordant or concordant drainage system<br>
and insequent streams or discordant<br>
drainage system.<br>
<b>Sequent drainage system</b><br>
<b>This is also known as accordant drainage</b><br>
system which follow the regional slope<br>
and is well adjusted to the geological<br>
structure. Sequent streams are further<br>
divided into consequent streams,<br>
obsequent streams subsequent streams,<br>
and resequent streams.<br>
(i)<br>
<b>Consequent streams: Consequent</b><br>
streams which are also called dip streams<br>
are the first streams to be originated<br>
in a particular region. These streams<br>
have their courses in accordance with<br>
the initial slope of land surface (Figure<br>
4.19). In other words, consequent streams<br>
follow the regional slope. In a region<br>
of folded structure in which the crustal<br>
rocks are fold due to lateral compressive<br>
forces into parallel anticlines and<br>
synclines the consequent streams are<br>
formed in the synclinal troughs. Such<br>
consequent streams are called synclinal<br>
consequent streams, which become the<br>
master consequent streams of trellis<br>
drainage pattern at much later date. The<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   222</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
223<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
first streams to be initiated on a newly<br>
emerged coastal plain are consequent<br>
streams which are parallel to each<br>
other and thus form parallel drainage<br>
pattern. The longest stream of the whole<br>
system of consequent streams is called<br>
master consequent. River Pangani<br>
that flows from Mt Kilimanjaro and<br>
<b>Meru is an example of consequent</b><br>
river (stream). Most of the streams<br>
draining the coastal plains of India are<br>
examples of consequent streams. The<br>
most ideal landscapes for the origin and<br>
development of consequent drainage<br>
system are domes and volcanic cones.<br>
<b>Consequent streams are divided in two</b><br>
types which are longitudinal consequent<br>
streams which follow the axis of the<br>
depression or syncline in a folded<br>
structure and lateral consequent streams<br>
which follow the sides of the depressions<br>
or the sides of the anticlines.<br>
(ii)	 Subsequent streams: These streams<br>
originate after the master consequent<br>
stream following the axis of the anticlines<br>
or ridges and the strikes of beds (Figure<br>
4.19). are called subsequent streams.<br>
<b>The subsequent streams originate on</b><br>
the flanks of the anticlines and join the<br>
master consequent at almost right angle<br>
while others maintain their flow parallel<br>
to the master consequent. Subsequent<br>
streams start as gullies on the sides of<br>
the primary consequent valleys, discover<br>
and explore belts of structural weakness,<br>
due to softer strata, fault, or joint-planes,<br>
and shatter zones. The Asan river, a<br>
tributary of the Yamuna river and the<br>
<b>Song river, a tributary of the Ganga river</b><br>
in the Dehra Dun valley in India are good<br>
examples of subsequent streams.<br>
(iii)	 Obsequent streams: These streams<br>
flow in opposite direction to the master<br>
consequent (Figure 4.19). In fact,<br>
obsequent streams are also consequents<br>
because they also follow the slopes of<br>
the ranges. The streams originating from<br>
the northern slopes of the West-east<br>
stretching ranges of the Himalayas flow<br>
northward to meet the East-west flowing<br>
tributaries (subsequent streams) of the<br>
southward draining master consequent<br>
streams. For example, the Mahabharat<br>
<b>Range of the Lesser Himalaya has issued</b><br>
several streams from its northern slopes.<br>
<b>These northward flowing streams join</b><br>
the subsequent stream. Similarly, several<br>
streams originating from the northern<br>
slopes of the Siwalik Range drain<br>
northward to join East-west subsequent<br>
streams of the southward flowing master<br>
consequents of the Ganga and the Yamuna,<br>
north of the Someshwar Range in India.<br>
(iv)	 Resequent streams: The tributary<br>
streams flowing in the direction of<br>
the master consequents are called<br>
resequents. These are originated at<br>
much later date in comparison to the<br>
master resequents. Since they are of<br>
recent origin, and hence they are called<br>
resequent. The resequent streams are<br>
originated during the initiation of the<br>
second cycle erosion in a folded structure.<br>
<b>The gradual demotion of folded as the</b><br>
invention of rolled in the anticlinal<br>
ridges and synclinal valley are converted<br>
into anticlinal valleys and synclinal<br>

<b>Insequent drainage system</b><br>
Insequent streams also known as discordant drainage system do not follow the<br>
regional slope and are not adjusted to geological structure as they drain across<br>
the geological structures. Insequent streams are further divided into antecedent<br>
streams and superimposed streams.<br>
(i)	 Antecedent drainage system: This is a type of drainage in which a river<br>
maintains its course by eroding vertically the rising land at the rate which is fast<br>
enough to keep pace with the land uplift. Good examples are River Ruaha, Niger<br>
river, Indus and Ganges rivers and other Himalayan rivers that are older than<br>
Himalayas themselves. The antecedent rivers are older than the existing landform<br>
(Figure 4.20).<br>

(ii)	 Superimposed drainage: Is the opposite of antecedent drainage whereby a<br>
river has maintained its course into a newly exposed rock layer without following<br>
the structure of the new rock (Figure 4.21).  Such rivers are younger than the<br>
rocks they cut across, for example Zambezi River.<br>

through older rocks regardless<br>
of topography<br>

<b>Furthermore, insequent drainage system</b><br>
can be Ante-position drainage system:<br>
<b>This is the system which has developed</b><br>
as a result of a combination of both<br>
antecedence and superimposition.<br>
<b>This indicates that the river course is</b><br>
superimposed into the new rock layer<br>
having a different structure while the<br>
land is being uplifted, for example the<br>
Colourado River in USA.<br>
<b>Drainage patterns</b><br>
<b>A drainage pattern is the layout or plan</b><br>
made by rivers and their tributaries<br>
on the landscape. The main drainage<br>
patterns are differentiated according to<br>
their geological structure. It considers<br>
relationship with the slope of the land,<br>
differences in rock hardness, and<br>
the rock structure. This is a spatial<br>
arrangement and form of drainage<br>
system<br>
in<br>
terms<br>
of<br>
geometrical<br>
shapes, geological structure, climatic<br>
conditions and denudation history.<br>
<b>In many areas drainage patterns and</b><br>
individual streams often show a<br>
strong relationship with the geological<br>
structure. Therefore, drainage can be<br>
classified whether is related or not<br>
related to rock structure. Streams<br>
naturally flow along those lines where<br>
they find erosion easy, and for this<br>
reason they follow areas of weak rock<br>
to avoid resistant outcrops. Fault-<br>
guided valleys are common in several<br>
regions while fault lines and joints may<br>
be reflected in drainage patterns. In<br>
zones of folded rock, the initial valleys<br>
will be determined by slope and will<br>
tend to follow the synclines.  Later, due<br>
to variations in rock hardness, erosion<br>
may carve valleys along the anticlines,<br>
leaving the syncline and anticline<br>
valleys to form various patterns. The<br>
following are some of the common<br>
types of drainage patterns.<br>

<b>Dendritic pattern</b><br>
<b>This type of pattern possesses a shape like</b><br>
the truck and branches of a tree. Usually<br>
the tributaries converging on the main<br>
stream from many directions joining<br>
at acute angles.  It occurs in regions<br>
underlain by homogeneous rocks. It is<br>
the most common type and is related<br>
to rock structure or differences in rock<br>
hardness (Figure 4.22). The dendritic<br>
pattern is associated with the areas of<br>
homogeneous lithologies, horizontal or<br>
valley greatly dropping strata, flat and<br>
rolling extensive topographic surface<br>
having extremely relief.<br>

<b>Trellis pattern</b><br>
<b>It is a drainage pattern which forms</b><br>
between ridge lines in deformed<br>
(typically sedimentary) rock with<br>
the chief tributaries joining the main<br>
stream approximately at right angles.<br>
<b>Usually minor tributaries join the chief</b><br>
tributaries at right angles and flow more<br>
or less parallel to the main stream. The<br>
pattern is strongly related to structure<br>
or differences in rock hardness and is<br>
commonly found in folded sedimentary<br>
rocks, and metamorphic rocks.  The<br>
chief tributaries are usually aligned<br>
along down folds or parallel zones of<br>
weak rock separated by resistant uplands<br>
(Figure 4.23).<br>

<b>Radial pattern</b><br>
<b>This is an arrangement of streams</b><br>
flowing outwards down the flanks of a<br>
dome or cone shaped upland such as a<br>
large volcano. It is common in volcanic<br>
regime and summit of mountain peak<br>
and is controlled by slope of the land<br>
(Figure 4.24).<br>

<b>Annular pattern</b><br>
This is also known as circular pattern.<br>
<b>In the annular pattern streams often</b><br>
joining the main stream at sharp angles<br>
but arranged in a series of curves which<br>
dissected a dome basin or crater area<br>
(Figure 4.25). It is formed when the<br>
tributaries of master consequent streams<br>
are developed in the form a circle. In a<br>
dissected dome with alternating bands<br>
of hard and soft rocks, the pattern may<br>
appear as having several concentric<br>
curves.  It is common in volcanic region.<br>
<b>Figure 4.25: Annular pattern</b><br>
<b>Rectangular pattern</b><br>
<b>A pattern is similar in plan to the</b><br>
trellis, with tributaries joining each<br>
other at right angles.  It also tends to<br>
have individual streams taking sharp<br>
angular bends along their course. It<br>
is a result of structural control, with<br>
streams following joints or fault lines<br>
in the rock.  It is common in volcanic<br>
regions and granitic rocks (Figure<br>
4.26). Generally, a rectangular pattern<br>
is developed in the regions where the<br>
rock joints form a rectangular  pattern.<br>
<b>Figure 4.26: Rectangular pattern</b><br>
<b>Centripetal pattern</b><br>
<b>This pattern is formed by a series of</b><br>
streams which after emerging from<br>
surrounding uplands converge in a central<br>
low land which may be a depression, or<br>
a basin or a crater lake. This is opposite<br>
of the radial pattern as in this case<br>
streams flow toward central depression<br>
such as craters, eroded structural<br>
domes with weak cores, and enclosed<br>
desert depression (Figure 4.27). Good<br>
examples are the Kathmandu valley of<br>
<b>Nepal where the tributary streams of the</b><br>
<b>Baghmati converge in the tectonically</b><br>
formed circular basin, the depression<br>
formed at the top of Raigarh Dome in<br>
the Lower Chambal Basin and streams<br>
of Tibet distributaries in Nepal.<br>
<b>Figure 4.27: Centripetal pattern</b><br>

09/03/2022   17:05<br>

<b>Braided pattern</b><br>
<b>It is common on broad flood plains</b><br>
with low gradients often due to back<br>
tilting. The pattern formed by the rivers<br>
which split into several channels rejoin<br>
and split again (Figure 4.28). Braided<br>
channel are separated by sand bars or<br>
island of alluvium.<br>

<b>Anastomotic pattern</b><br>

the reconnection of two streams that<br>

<b>Exercise 4.2</b><br>
1.	 Using<br>
examples<br>
from<br>
<b>Africa, describe the common</b><br>
drainage patterns.<br>
2.	 Nature of the surface and<br>
geological structure are the<br>
main factors that control the<br>
drainage systems of an area.<br>
Discuss.<br>
3.	 How can drainage systems<br>
be<br>
distinguished<br>
from<br>
drainage pattern?<br>
<b>River</b><br>
<b>It is a natural flow of water in a</b><br>
valley from the source to the mouth.<br>
<b>It is a mass flow of water over the</b><br>
surface in a particular channel from<br>
its source mostly in the highland<br>
areas, to its mouth, such as lake, sea<br>
or ocean. A long profile of a river is a<br>
representation of a river from it source<br>
(upper course) through middle cause<br>
to its mouth (lower course) and shows<br>
how the river changes over its course.<br>
<b>All rivers are joined by small rivers</b><br>
or streams called tributaries.  A river<br>
branches off into small streams when it<br>
is about to enter into the sea or ocean.<br>
<b>These small streams at the mouth are</b><br>
called distributaries. The area drained<br>
by a river and its tributaries is called<br>
a river basin and the boundary which<br>
separates river basins is known as a<br>
watershed or divide (Figure 4.30).<br>

The upper course (headwater), streams swiftly flow down steep mountain slopes<br>
and cut deep, v-shaped valleys. Waterfalls and rapids occur in this zone. The middle<br>
course (lower-elevation), streams merge to flow down gentle slopes. Valleys broaden<br>
as coalescing rivers start to meander. The lower course (lowest elevations), a river<br>
meanders across a broad, nearly flat valley and floodplain.At a river’s mouth, it<br>
may divide into separate channels as it flows across a delta extending out to sea.<br>
The coastal plain and delta are made of river sediments.<br>

<b>Middle course</b><br>
<b>Lower course</b><br>
(a) A long profile of river<br>

<b>Types of rivers</b><br>
Perennial Rivers: These are permanent rivers which flow throughout the year.<br>
Most of these rivers are situated in the equatorial regions, commonly found in<br>
Amazon and Congo rivers (Figure 4.31). Other rivers originate in areas with<br>
either permanent ice melting or lakes. Good examples are, Rhine river in Western<br>
Europe and Nile river in Africa.<br>
<b>Figure 4.31: Part of Congo River</b><br>
Intermittent rivers: These are types of rivers which flow in rain season when the<br>
rain is abundant. Most of the tropical rivers belong to this type. They are also<br>
known as seasonal rivers. Rivers like St. Lawrence in USA, Orange Vaal in South<br>
Africa and Rufiji river in Tanzania (Figure 4.32) are examples of intermittent rivers.<br>
<b>Figure 4.32: Part of Rufiji River in Tanzania</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   230</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
231<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
Ephemeral rivers: Most of these rivers are found in desert. Usually they emerge<br>
when torrential rainfall occurs and tends to disappear in a short period of time and<br>
sometimes after the rain has stopped. These rivers include Kuiseb river in central<br>
Namibia (Figure 4.33), Luni and Son in India.<br>
<b>Figure 4.33: Ephemeral River Kuiseb in central Namibia</b><br>
<b>River Regime</b><br>
<b>River regime is a seasonal variation or</b><br>
fluctuation in the volume of water in the<br>
river. The change of the volume of water<br>
is a result of interplay of factors which<br>
include climate, gradient, vegetation,<br>
nature of underlying rock and human<br>
activities.<br>
<b>Types of river regimes</b><br>
<b>Simple river regime: It is common in</b><br>
areas marked by two seasons in the year,<br>
dry and wet seasons especially in the<br>
tropics. During rainy season the volume<br>
of water increases and decreases during<br>
dry seasons. Example of rivers under<br>
simple regime include River Ruvuma,<br>
Malagarasi and Ruvu in Tanzania,<br>
<b>Tana and Galana in Kenya. Most of the</b><br>
rivers found in Africa experience simple<br>
regime.<br>
<b>Double river regime: This occurs when</b><br>
a river experiences two maximum water<br>
levels and two minimum water levels.<br>
<b>They are common in the equatorial</b><br>
regions where there are double maximum<br>
rainy seasons which experience two<br>
major seasons of high rainfall in a year.<br>
A good example is the Congo river.<br>
<b>Complex river regime: Complex or</b><br>
composite river regime is a type of river<br>
regime characterized by constant high<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   231</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
232<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
volume of water in the river through out<br>
the year. This type of regime belongs<br>
to big and longest rivers whose length<br>
cut across several climatic regions. A<br>
good example is river Nile, Amazon<br>
and Mississippi.<br>
<b>River basins</b><br>
<b>River basin is defined as an area of land</b><br>
drained by a river and its tributaries. It is<br>
the unit of water resources management.<br>
<b>River basins are primarily identified</b><br>
by hydrological networks which are<br>
determined by nature of morphology of<br>
particular area. The basin may consist<br>
several small basins which are known<br>
as watershed. The area that demarcates<br>
one basin from the other is known as<br>
watershed or divide. Besides, river<br>
basins being recognise by hydrological<br>
networks, there are boards which have<br>
been intrusted in the management of<br>
the river basins.<br>
<b>River basins in China</b><br>
<b>China is the richest country in rivers</b><br>
and lakes. It hosts over 1500 rivers of<br>
varying sizes and river basin as well.<br>
<b>The prominent rivers basins found in</b><br>
<b>China are Hai River Basin, Huai River</b><br>
<b>Basin, Liao River Basin, Pearl River</b><br>
<b>Basin, Songhua River Basin, Yellow</b><br>
<b>River Basin and Yangtze River Basin</b><br>
(Figure 4.34).<br>
<b>Figure 4.34: River basins in China</b><br>

<b>Yangtze River Basin</b><br>
<b>The Yangtze (Changjing) River is</b><br>
<b>China’s longest river, and the world’s</b><br>
third longest river with about 6300<br>
km (Figure 4.35). The river is fed by<br>
the snow and its waterway starts in<br>
the Kunlun Mountains of the Tibetan<br>
<b>Plateau in Qinghai Province and flows</b><br>
<b>South East and later empties into the</b><br>
<b>East Chinese Sea about 20 km North</b><br>
of Shanghai. The Yangtze River is fed<br>
by about 700 tributaries and it is the<br>
largest river system in China with a<br>
basin covering an area close to 19% of<br>
<b>China’s total land. The river provides</b><br>
fresh water resources to about 400<br>
million people, which is about 30%<br>
of the Chinese total population. The<br>
total water availability per year in the<br>
basin amounts to 996 billion m3 on a<br>
long term mean basis, which is about<br>
36.5% of China’s total waters. The<br>
Yangtze Basin area is rich in lakes,<br>
covering an area of more than 15 200<br>
km2 with most of them being situated<br>
in the middle and lower reaches.<br>
<b>The area includes the two largest</b><br>
fresh water lakes in China which<br>
are the Poyang and Dongting lakes.<br>
<b>The Yangtze basin covers about 19</b><br>
provinces, autonomous regions, and<br>
municipalities. The river functions<br>
as the most important transportation<br>
network through the heart of some<br>
of the most densely populated and<br>
economically important areas in China,<br>
known as the Yangtze River Economic<br>
<b>Belt (YREB). The Yangtze is also the</b><br>
major inland waterway with navigation<br>
channels of about 57 000 km (of<br>
which 2837 km is mainstream), which<br>
accounts for about 52.5% of the nation’s<br>
total inland navigation channels. The<br>
shipping capacity of the Yangtze River<br>
is equivalent to about four (4) to six (6)<br>
railways each of the same length. The<br>
<b>Yangtze River Basin (YRB) is a major</b><br>
resource for irrigation, agriculture and<br>
hydro-electric power (H.E.P) with<br>
a technical potential exploitation of<br>
about 256270 MW and an estimated<br>
annual power output of more than 100<br>
<b>TWh, equivalent to 48% and 49% of</b><br>
the total power generated by the nation<br>
respectively. The river currently holds<br>
about 17 hydroelectric dams, including<br>
the largest three gorge dams. Agriculture<br>
along the basin produces almost a half<br>
of the country’s total crop-harvest and<br>
contributes about a third of the national<br>
grain and GDP. The Yangtze River<br>
basin is recognized internationally as<br>
an important ecosystem which is rich<br>
in biodiversity and is listed with its six<br>
wetlands in the Ramsar Convention on<br>
wetlands of international importance.<br>
<b>The Yangtze River basin is under</b><br>
custody of the Changjiang Water<br>
<b>Resources</b><br>
<b>Commission</b><br>
<b>(CWRC),</b><br>
which is one of the seven river basin<br>
management<br>
commissions<br>
under<br>
the Ministry of Water Resources of<br>
<b>China. Together with the prefectural</b><br>
and local branches of the Ministry of<br>
<b>Environmental Protection (MEP), they</b><br>
are responsible for the water quality<br>
and quantity of the river basin. Also,<br>
other ministries under the state council<br>
have the responsibility related to<br>
management of water system. Regional<br>

09/03/2022   17:05<br>

authorities, including provinces, cities and public and private organizations (like<br>

<b>Drainage basins in Tanzania</b><br>
<b>Tanzania is a home to the highest water</b><br>
tower in Africa Mt. Kilimanjaro as<br>
well as the deepest fresh water lake<br>
in Africa Lake Tanganyika. Tanzania<br>
has a total of nine (9) drainage<br>
basins (Figure 4.36), out of those<br>
seven (7) are transboundary basins<br>
and the remaining two (2) are not<br>
transboundary basins. A transboundary<br>
basin is the one which crosses at least<br>
one political border within a state or an<br>
international boundary.  Transboundary<br>
basins include Lake Victoria, Lake<br>
Tanganyika, Lake Nyasa, Pangani,<br>
<b>Ruvuma and Southern Coast, internal</b><br>
drainage basin and Lake Rukwa basin.<br>
<b>It is only Rufiji and Wami-Ruvu Basins</b><br>
which are not transboundary basin.<br>
<b>Tanzania has 14 transboundary fresh</b><br>
water bodies which includes rivers<br>

Songwe, Kagera, Mara, Malagarasi, Momba, Mwiruzi, Umba, and Ruvuma,<br>
and transboundary Lakes such as Nyasa, Victoria, Chala, Jipe, Tanganyika (the<br>

<b>The rivers that are found in the basins</b><br>
in the country are important in feeding<br>
various lakes in Tanzania. This include<br>
great lakes in East Africa which are<br>
Victoria,<br>
<b>Tanganyika</b><br>
and<br>
Nyasa.<br>
Besides the trans-boundary rivers,<br>
<b>Tanzania has many large and small</b><br>
inland rivers which flow towards lakes<br>
or the Indian Ocean. These include<br>
the Ruaha, Kilombero, Wami, Ruvu,<br>
and Ruhuhu rivers.  Lakes alone cover<br>
about 7% of Tanzania’s land surface.<br>
Some of these lakes are Lake Victoria,<br>
<b>Lake Tanganyika, Lake Nyasa (boarder</b><br>
<b>Lakes), Lake Rukwa, Lake Eyasi, Lake</b><br>
<b>Manyara, Lake Singidani, and Lake</b><br>
Natron (inland lakes).<br>
<b>Tanzania is divided into nine (9) water</b><br>
management units corresponding to<br>
the nine major drainage basins. In each<br>
basin, there is a basin water officer who<br>
is responsible for regulating and planning<br>
the use of water resources, based on<br>
the Water Resources Management<br>
<b>Act No. 11 of 2009. The Act provides</b><br>
for institutional and legal framework<br>
for sustainable management and<br>
development of water resources. The<br>
major focus of the Act is to outline the<br>
principles of water resource management,<br>
to provide for the prevention and control<br>
of water pollution, and to provide for<br>
the participation of stakeholders and the<br>
general public in implementation of the<br>
national water policy.<br>

<b>The water resources management</b><br>
section of the water sector development<br>
programme requires the Water Basin<br>
<b>Offices to conduct their activities in</b><br>
line with the principles of Integrated<br>
<b>Water Resource Management (IWRM)</b><br>
approach. The Water Resources Division<br>
under the Ministry of Water and<br>
<b>Irrigation (MWI) is responsible for the</b><br>
quantitative and qualitative assessment<br>
and monitoring of water resources. Also,<br>
it deals with dams’ safety monitoring.<br>
<b>In this regard, the division collects</b><br>
and manages hydrological and water<br>
use data, develops the hydro-geologic<br>
maps, issues water use permits and<br>
inspects the existing water abstraction<br>
systems. The division is also responsible<br>
for water resources planning and<br>
research, regulation, enforcement and<br>
environmental issues associated with<br>
water resources.<br>

<b>Drainage basins and water use</b><br>
conflicts<br>
<b>Water use conflict is a term describing</b><br>
a dispute or disagreement between<br>
countries, states or groups of people over<br>
ownership, access to, and use of water<br>
resources. Opposing interests of water<br>
users is one among potential indicators<br>
for conflicts occurance.<br>
<b>Cases of water conflicts in Tanzania</b><br>
<b>The Pangani river basin</b><br>
<b>The Pangani River Basin is about 43,650</b><br>
km2, with about 5% of its area in Kenya,<br>
and the remainder distributed across<br>
the Arusha, Manyara, Kilimanjaro and<br>
<b>Tanga regions in Tanzania. The Pangani</b><br>
<b>River system drains the southern and</b><br>
eastern sides of Africa’s highest peak,<br>
Mt. Kilimanjaro (5,985 m) as well as Mt.<br>
<b>Meru (4,566 m). It then passes through</b><br>
the arid Masai Steppe, draining the<br>
<b>Pare and Usambara Mountains before</b><br>
reaching the coastal town of Pangani,<br>
making its estuary with the Indian Ocean.<br>
<b>The Pangani Basin is one of Tanzania’s</b><br>
most productive areas, with nationally<br>
important agricultural outputs and<br>
hydropower production. The basin has<br>
forest and biodiversity resources that<br>
are important globally. The basin hosts<br>
millions of people, who rely directly or<br>
indirectly on irrigated agriculture for<br>
their livelihoods. Water within the basin<br>
serves human needs such as drinking,<br>
cooking, washing and sanitation. The<br>
water in the basin also allows arid<br>
land to become productive through<br>
irrigation; provides a habitat for plants,<br>
fish, and wildlife; supplies urban and<br>
industrial uses; generates electricity<br>
through hydroelectric power; and<br>
supports many recreational activities.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   236</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
237<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Water conflicts in the Pangani basin</b><br>
<b>The frequently recorded conflicts in</b><br>
<b>Pangani river basin have been linked to</b><br>
water users of different sizes and power<br>
in the basin. The basin supports both<br>
small scale and large scale agricultural<br>
activities that relie on water from the<br>
basin. In addition, water is used by the<br>
<b>H.E.P plant. Similarly, the three urban</b><br>
centres of Arusha, Moshi and Tanga in<br>
the basin have been demanding much<br>
more water to cater for their expanding<br>
socio-economic needs. This eventually,<br>
necessitated the emergence of conflicts<br>
among the three users who are the city,<br>
municipality and farming communities.<br>
<b>The dominant water use conflicts in the</b><br>
basin are related to water allocation,<br>
quantity and water quality in space and<br>
time. Therefore, water conflicts may<br>
be categorized into four main groups,<br>
namely water tenure conflicts, conflict of<br>
allocation, peasants against pastoralists<br>
conflicts, and peasants against peasants<br>
conflicts.<br>
<b>Conflict of water right: Water right refers</b><br>
to the legal right of a water user to utilize<br>
a given water source, such as a river,<br>
a groundwater source, a dam, a lake<br>
and a pond. The water user rights are<br>
secured by paying the user fees. Many<br>
small-scale users in the Pangani Basin<br>
are reluctant to apply and pay for water<br>
rights, with a notion that water is a ‘gift<br>
from God’, as a result the communities<br>
have been undermining government<br>
efforts to manage water resources, to<br>
an extend of destroying water control<br>
gates and structures thus, leading to the<br>
water use conflict.<br>
<b>Conflict of water allocation: Despite</b><br>
the role played by the Pangani Basin<br>
<b>Office and its Board of managing and</b><br>
allocating water resources in the basin<br>
and ultimately balancing stakeholders’<br>
demands, still the dissatisfaction<br>
among the users have been fueling<br>
on the modalities of using water, and<br>
consequently results into conflicts<br>
between the authority and peasants.<br>
<b>Pastoralists against farmers: Conflicts</b><br>
may occur when individual water users<br>
have divergent interests. For example, in<br>
the past decade, the Maasai pastoralists<br>
from the northern areas of Moshi and<br>
<b>Arusha have been streaming towards</b><br>
the basin, mostly at Kilindi with tens of<br>
thousands of their livestock in search<br>
for water and grazing pasture. In the<br>
process of feeding large numbers of<br>
livestock, they have been unlawfully<br>
entering into farmers’ fields, destroying,<br>
crops and irrigation infrastructures, and<br>
thus causing serious conflicts.<br>
<b>Conflict resolution</b><br>
<b>Some of the necessary steps to resolve</b><br>
these conflicts include the following:<br>
raising communities’ awareness about<br>
water sector reforms and the likely<br>
effects of climate change on water<br>
availability; organizing stakeholders’<br>
workshops, which will bring together<br>
representatives from communities, local<br>
and central government officials, and<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   237</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
238<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
technical experts to discuss the situation<br>
and propose a way forward; recognizing<br>
the efficacy;  and, to some extent,<br>
reinstituting traditional systems whereby<br>
water is managed by hydrological<br>
boundaries and not administrative<br>
boundaries. These measures will make<br>
it easy to negotiate agreements between<br>
upstream and downstream users.<br>
<b>Cases of water use conflict in North-</b><br>
<b>East Africa</b><br>
<b>The Nile River Basin</b><br>
<b>The Nile River Basin is an international</b><br>
water basin which covers eleven<br>
different sovereign nations, namely<br>
the Democratic Republic of Congo,<br>
Rwanda, Burundi, Tanzania, Kenya,<br>
Uganda, Eritrea, South Sudan, Sudan,<br>
<b>Ethiopia and Egypt. The basin is</b><br>
named after the Nile river which is<br>
the longest river in the world covering<br>
about 6 853 km (4258 miles). The Nile<br>
is the only significant source of water<br>
in North Africa supporting about 40%<br>
of Africa’s population living in the<br>
<b>Nile River Basin. The Nile has two</b><br>
major tributaries namely White Nile<br>
and the Blue Nile whereby the former<br>
is longer than the latter. The White<br>
<b>Nile starts from the the Lake Victoria</b><br>
basin which is located in Tanzania,<br>
<b>Uganda, and Kenya, and it flows via</b><br>
<b>Uganda, Southern Sudan. The Blue</b><br>
<b>Nile originates from Ethiopia’s Lake</b><br>
<b>Tana and flows into Sudan from the</b><br>
<b>Southeast. In addition to being a source</b><br>
of internal conflict within nations,<br>
water from the Nile has caused external<br>
tensions between sovereign states.<br>
<b>While Egypt consumes 99% of the</b><br>
<b>Nile’s water supply, it is only a small</b><br>
amount of Nile’s water that originates<br>
from within Egypt’s sovereign borders.<br>
<b>Higher water demands of a lower</b><br>
riparian have often fuelled regional<br>
conflicts. For instance, the construction<br>
of a large man made dam in Ethiopia<br>
and the use of water in Lake Victoria<br>
for irrigation have been among the<br>
causes of misunderstanding among the<br>
countries which have shared uses of<br>
basin.<br>
<b>However, Egypt has been for long time</b><br>
taking control of the Nile over the years.<br>
<b>The intesification of the conflicts over</b><br>
the basin have been recorded ever since<br>
early 20th century mostly fueled by the<br>
rapid increase in population (which<br>
doubled from 25 to 50 million between<br>
1960 and 1987) and the accompanying<br>
demand for food that led to the building<br>
of the Aswan High Dam (opened in<br>
1971) and several new schemes to<br>
irrigate the desert near Cairo (late<br>
1980s). The main purpose of the High<br>
<b>Dam has been to hold back the annual</b><br>
flood water generated by the summer<br>
rains in the Ethiopian Highlands.<br>
<b>Some water is released throughout</b><br>
the year, allowing an extra crop to be<br>
grown, and every surplus is saved as<br>
an insurance against a failure of the<br>
rains. The river regime below Aswan<br>
is now more constant, allowing trade<br>
and navigation throughout the year.<br>

power station which provides Egypt<br>
