<b>Water use conflicts in the Nile basin</b><br>
<b>Egypt and Sudan hold absolute rights</b><br>
to use 100 percent of the Nile water<br>
under agreements reached in 1929<br>
between Egypt and Britain (which<br>
was then the colonial power in Kenya,<br>
<b>Sudan, Tanzania, and Uganda) and in</b><br>
1959 between Egypt and Sudan. In<br>
such agreement, Egypt was assigned<br>
mandate to issue consent to other<br>
nations’ use of the Nile’s water, due to<br>
this most of other basin countries have<br>
been passive in developing projects<br>
that extensively utilize water of the<br>
<b>Nile river.  For many years, economic</b><br>
constraints, external pressures and<br>
internal strife have precluded upstream<br>
countries of the Nile basin from<br>
developing their water resources,<br>
allowing Egypt to take full advantage<br>
of downstream water flow. However,<br>
over the years, due to considerable<br>
population<br>
growth,<br>
economic<br>
development, political consolidation<br>
and improved opportunities to harness<br>
their water resources and increased<br>
demand for energy and arable surfaces,<br>
other basin countries have contested the<br>
validity of these treaties and demanded<br>
their revocation to make way for a more<br>
equitable system of water use along the<br>
Nile valley and its tributaries.<br>
<b>Out of all countries draining water</b><br>
to Nile river, Ethiopia is the most<br>
important, as its Ethiopian highlands<br>
provide nearly 86% of the Nile’s water.<br>
<b>Along with this role, it has recently</b><br>
been developing its largely untapped<br>
potential<br>
for<br>
hydro-energy<br>
and<br>
irrigation. Such water development<br>
plans in Ethiopia and other upstream<br>
countries will require only a small<br>
portion of the Nile’s water, the prospects<br>
for increased water utilization in the<br>
near future raise serious concerns<br>
among the downstream countries of<br>
<b>Egypt and Sudan. Due to this, Egypt</b><br>
which receives only very limited<br>
amounts of rain that makes it largely<br>
dependent on irrigation in the Nile<br>
<b>River for sustaining its agricultural</b><br>
production, raises much concerned with<br>
ever emerging uses in upstreams. This<br>
has been among the reason for Egypt<br>
to discourage any upstream projects<br>
that could reduce downstream river<br>
flow. Eventhough, the construction<br>
of projects such as the hydro-power<br>
facilities on the Nile tributaries will<br>
not necessarily lower the downstream<br>
flows, yet Egypt has been worried<br>
of upstream damming projects. This<br>
has frequently been causing tensions<br>
and fear among the riparian countries<br>
in the basin. For example, the 2011<br>
tension that was recorded has a result<br>
of Ethiopia’s move on the construction<br>
of a 6000 MW hydroelectric dam (the<br>
<b>Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam)</b><br>
on the Blue Nile-the main tributary of<br>
the Nile basin in the absence of any<br>

\<br>
<b>Lakes</b><br>
<b>Lakes are bodies of either fresh or</b><br>
saline water in natural depressions on<br>
the surface of the Earth. The term lake<br>
includes a wide range of water bodies<br>
such as ponds, marshes and swamps<br>
with standing water. The sizes of lakes<br>
range from ponds to larger ones. Some<br>
larger lakes are called seas, such as<br>
the Caspian Sea, the Dead Sea and the<br>
<b>Aral Sea.  Although most of the lakes</b><br>
are permanent yet some contain water<br>
in the wet season only while lakes in<br>
basins of inland drainage (which are<br>
usually semi-arid) may contain water<br>
for a few months in several years.<br>
Lakes receive water from streams,<br>
overland flow, and groundwater, hence<br>
form part of drainage systems.  Many<br>
lakes lose water at an outlet through<br>
outflowing<br>
streams,<br>
where<br>
water<br>
drains over a dam. For example, Lake<br>
<b>Victoria loses water through River</b><br>
<b>Nile, while Lake Nyasa loses water to</b><br>
the Zambezi Basin through Shire River,<br>
lakes Albert and in case of Kyoga loses<br>
water to the White Nile, meanwhile<br>
<b>Lake Tana loses water to the Blue</b><br>
<b>Nile. Lakes contain fresh water four</b><br>
times more compared to the rivers. If<br>
it could not be continuously supplied<br>
with fresh water, they can disappear<br>
through desiccation or accumulation<br>
of sediments. Basically, there are ten<br>
largest lakes in the word as shown in<br>
Table 4.1.<br>

<b>Classification of lakes</b><br>
<b>Lakes are classified according to their</b><br>
modes of formation and the quality<br>
of the water they contain, that is, can<br>
either be freshwater or salt water lakes.<br>
<b>Lakes are divided into two major types:</b><br>
those occupying basins formed by the<br>
earth’s movements, volcanic processes<br>
and erosion; and those formed by the<br>
damming effect of materials deposited<br>
by ice, water or lava. Other lakes are<br>
formed directly from solution, barrier<br>
and human influence.<br>
<b>Lakes formed due to earth’s</b><br>
movements<br>
These also are called tectonic lakes.<br>
<b>They are formed due to movements of</b><br>
the earth’s crust which cause crustal<br>
warping leading to the formation of the<br>
largest lake. They include;<br>
<b>Crustal warping lakes: These lakes are</b><br>
formed when water occupies basins<br>
formed due subsidence of the earth’s<br>
crust as a result of tectonic movements<br>
(Figure 4.37). Good examples of lakes<br>
formed in this way are Lake Victoria<br>
in East Africa and Lake Chad in Chad.<br>
<b>Other examples of lake caused by crustal</b><br>
warping are the Caspian Sea in Russia,<br>
<b>Lake Eyre in Australia and Lake Titicaca</b><br>
in Peru. The Caspian Sea is located in<br>
a depression between the Caucasus<br>
<b>Mountain and Central Asia and its</b><br>
surface is still varying, millions of years<br>
after its emergence. This is an indication<br>
of persistent earth’s movement.<br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Up-warped</b><br>
<b>Ruwenzori</b><br>
<b>Mau</b><br>

<b>Up-warped</b><br>

<b>Figure 4.37: Lakes formed through subsidence of the earth’s crust</b><br>
Rift valley lakes: A rift valley lake is formed when water occupies a deep steep<br>
sided and elongated depression formed due to the divergence of crusted blocks<br>
which cause the central part to down-warp (Figure 4.38). The lake occupies a<br>
basin on the floor of a rift valley. Rift valley lakes are characteristically long and<br>
rectangular in shape, with a fairly uniform width of about 30 to 50 kilometres.<br>
They are usually bordered on both sides but sometimes just on one side by fault<br>
escarpments and highlands. Some of the best examples of rift valley lakes are<br>
Albert, Edward, Tanganyika, Nyasa, Turkana, and Rukwa which are found in the<br>
Great East Africa Rift Valley (in Africa from north to south).<br>
<b>Rift valley</b><br>
<b>Fault blocks</b><br>
<b>River</b><br>
<b>Seawater</b><br>
<b>Figure 4.38: Lakes formed through divergent of the crusted blocks</b><br>
<b>Lakes formed due to volcanicity</b><br>
<b>These are lakes formed due to volcanic</b><br>
activity. They include crater, caldera, and<br>
lava-dammed lakes. They are volcanic<br>
lakes; the most common form being<br>
those filling the caldera of a shut-off<br>
volcano.<br>
<b>Crater and Caldera lakes: These lakes</b><br>
form as a result of violent volcanic<br>
eruptions whereby the top of a volcano<br>
is blown-off, leaving a large crater<br>
which may be filled with water later<br>
on to form a crater lake. Also a crater<br>
may be enlarged by subsidence to form<br>
a caldera which may be filled with<br>
water later on to form a caldera lake<br>
(Figure 4.39). Examples of crater lakes<br>
are Lake Oregon in USA, Lake Toba<br>
in northern Sumatra (Indonesia), Lake<br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Magadi and Empakaai (Ngorongoro</b><br>
<b>Conservation Area, Tanzania), Lake</b><br>
<b>Chala (Tanzania/Kenya), and Lake</b><br>
<b>Ngozi (Mbeya, Tanzania). Examples of</b><br>
caldera lakes are Lake Shela in Ethiopia<br>
and Lake Bosumtwi in Ghana. Crater<br>
lakes are usually not very large, being<br>
confined to the crater of the volcano.<br>
<b>They are circular in shape and up to</b><br>
about 500 metres across.  Although crater<br>
lakes may be formed during volcanic<br>
eruptions, most of them do not last long<br>
because of erosion on the steep shapes<br>
of the mountain cuts through the crater<br>
and drains the lake. Caldera lakes are<br>
usually much larger because they fill<br>
the depression originally occupied by<br>
the mass of volcano.<br>

<b>Figure 4.39: Caldera lake</b><br>
<b>Lava-dammed lakes: These are lakes</b><br>
that form when lava flows from volcano<br>
towards a river or a stream valley.  After<br>
cooling and solidification, the lava blocks<br>
the river from flowing longitudinally, and<br>
water collects in the upper stream to form<br>
a dam called lava-dammed lake (Figure<br>
4.40). Examples of lava dammed lakes<br>
are Lake Belera in Rwanda, Lake Tana<br>
in Ethiopia, Lake Kivu and Lake Edward<br>
in Uganda, and the Sea of Galilee.<br>
<b>Lava dammed</b><br>
lake<br>
<b>Lava barrier</b><br>
<b>Dry river in</b><br>
front of the<br>
barrier<br>
<b>Tributaries</b><br>
<b>Figure 4.40: Lava-dammed lake</b><br>
<b>Lakes formed due to erosion</b><br>
<b>These are lakes formed due to glacier or</b><br>
wind erosion.  Glacial lakes are formed<br>
as a result of the scouring action of ice<br>
leading to over-deepening of rock surface<br>
to form two types of lakes namely Cirque<br>
and Trough (Figure 4.41).<br>
<b>Cirque lake: Refers to depressions that</b><br>
occupy armchair-like depression called<br>
a cirque once ice and glaciers retreat.<br>
<b>It occurs in mountains and valley</b><br>
sidewalls at high elevations. A cirque<br>
lake is also called a tarn. These lakes<br>
sometimes feed a mountain river. Tarns<br>
can be found on the sides of Mount<br>
Kenya, for example the Teleki Tarn.<br>
<b>Trough lake: This type of lake occupies</b><br>
an elongated depression excavated by<br>
ice at the bottom of a U-shaped valley.<br>
It is sometimes called a Ribbon lake.<br>
<b>A good example of ribbon lake is Lake</b><br>
<b>Michaelson found in the Gorges valley</b><br>
near Mount Kenya.<br>

<b>Rock basin lake: This is formed by</b><br>
scouring action of an ice sheet or valley<br>
glacier.  These lakes are common on<br>
the Baltic and Canadian Shields.<br>

<b>Wind erosion lakes</b><br>
These lakes are common in desert areas.<br>
<b>When dry desert wind blows across the</b><br>
surface, it easily picks up loose particles<br>
of dust and sand. Gradually, the surface<br>
is deflated (blown away) until the water<br>
table is reached in some places. Wind<br>
deflation produces depressions which are<br>
formed by the action of eddy currents.<br>
<b>The lakes formed in these depressions</b><br>
are not always true lakes; they may be<br>
nothing more than muddy swamps. Good<br>
examples of these lakes are Qattara<br>
and Siwa depressions in Egypt. More<br>
permanent desert lakes develop when<br>
an aquifer is exposed. These lakes are<br>
called oases. Some desert lakes dry up<br>
due to excessive evaporation and what<br>
remains is a lake bed of salt. This is<br>
called playa or Salt Lake.<br>
<b>Lakes formed due to deposition</b><br>
<b>These are lakes formed due to deposition</b><br>
by rivers, ice and sea or oceans.<br>
<b>Sometimes these lakes are known as</b><br>
barrier lakes.<br>
<b>Lakes formed due to river deposition</b><br>
<b>Ox-bow lakes: An ox-bow lake is formed</b><br>
in a flood plain when a meander of a<br>
river is so acute that only a narrow neck<br>
of land separates the two ends of the<br>
meander. The narrow neck is cut-off<br>
when the river floods. The cut ends of<br>
the meander are sealed by deposition<br>
and the meander becomes an ox-bow<br>
lake (Figure 4.42). Several ox-bow<br>
lakes are found along the River Galma<br>
in Nigeria. Ox-bow lakes may disappear<br>
when vegetation and sediments fill them.<br>
<b>Oxbow lake</b><br>
<b>Deposited</b><br>
material<br>
<b>Figure 4.42: An Ox-bow lake</b><br>
<b>Delta lakes: These lakes are formed</b><br>
at a delta by the movement of silt and<br>
sand, building up barriers behind from<br>
which water is trapped (Figure 4.43).<br>
<b>Delta lakes are found in the Nile Delta</b><br>
in Egypt and Rhône Delta, where Etang<br>
de Vaccares Delta lake is found.<br>

<b>Flood plain lakes: These lakes are</b><br>
formed in the flood plain when natural<br>
levée formed off the river banks prevent<br>
water from returning to the river during<br>
flood. A good example is Lake Matohi<br>
on the Congo basin.<br>
<b>Lakes formed due to glacial deposition</b><br>
<b>Moraine-dammed lake: This type of</b><br>
lake is formed when a terminal moraine<br>
is deposited across a valley, building<br>
up a barrier behind which river water<br>
collects (Figure 4.44). Examples of lakes<br>
formed through this process include<br>
<b>Lake Garda in Italy and Lake Mahoma</b><br>
in the Ruwenzori Mountain ranges.<br>
<b>Lateral</b><br>
moraine<br>
<b>Outwash plain</b><br>
<b>Glacial period</b><br>
<b>Postglacial period</b><br>
<b>Terminal moraine</b><br>
<b>Moraine</b><br>
lake<br>
<b>Figure 4.44: Moraine-dammed lake</b><br>
<b>Boulder clay lakes: These lakes form</b><br>
when boulder clay materials are<br>
deposited on the landscape in which<br>
the drainage is highly irregular, hence<br>
forming many small lakes. Lakes of<br>
this type are common in the Canadian<br>
Shield and in Scandinavia.<br>
<b>Lakes formed due to marine</b><br>
deposition<br>
<b>Haffs lake: Coastal lagoons are lakes</b><br>
that are formed when a sand bar extends<br>
along a coast or when a barrier beach<br>
extends across the mouth of a river.<br>
<b>Such lakes are very common along the</b><br>
<b>West African coast. Good  examples</b><br>
are Keta lagoon in Ghana and Lagos<br>
lagoon in Nigeria. Also many small<br>
lagoons are found along the Mozambican<br>
coast.<br>
<b>Solution lakes: These lakes are formed</b><br>
in the Karst Region where rainwater<br>
or groundwater dissolves limestone<br>
rocks. Solution of limestone rocks form<br>
a cave and when the roof of the cave<br>
collapses, a solution lake is formed.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   245</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
246<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Examples of solution lakes are Lake</b><br>
<b>Scutari in Yugoslavia and Lake Ojikoto</b><br>
in Namibia.<br>
<b>Lakes dammed by landslides and</b><br>
other waste debris<br>
<b>These are small lakes formed when</b><br>
mass movement materials block a river<br>
valley. These lakes, however, are only<br>
temporary. A good example is Lake<br>
<b>Agulu in Nigeria, which was formed</b><br>
by the deposition of materials brought<br>
down from the gullies of the Awka<br>
Highlands.<br>
<b>Man-made Lakes: These are artificial</b><br>
lakes which are often called reservoirs.<br>
<b>They are deliberately formed by</b><br>
construction of dams across a narrow,<br>
steep-sided section of a river valley.<br>
<b>These lakes are made for the purpose</b><br>
of storing water for irrigation or for<br>
production of hydroelectric power or<br>
both. Some man-made lakes are formed<br>
due to excavation of a piece of land for<br>
the purpose of trapping water for local<br>
inhabitants’ usage.  Examples of man-<br>
made lakes are; Akosombo Dam on<br>
the Volta River in Ghana, Lake Nasser<br>
on the River Nile, Lake Kariba on the<br>
<b>River Zambezi, and the Mtera and</b><br>
Nyumba ya Mungu dams in Tanzania.<br>
<b>The economic importance of lakes</b><br>
<b>Both natural and man-made lakes</b><br>
are useful in a varied way. The main<br>
importance of lakes is:<br>
<b>Firstly, lakes are useful for fishing</b><br>
purposes. Fishing is a widespread<br>
traditional activity in the lakes throughout<br>
the world. It involves extraction of fish<br>
and other aquatic animals for commercial<br>
and domestic purposes. There is a need<br>
to conduct more research on water<br>
bodies in order to understand optimal<br>
exploitation of resources supplied by<br>
lakes. Secondly, lakes are useful habitats<br>
for aquatic organisms. Lakes provide<br>
a conducive environment for aquatic<br>
organisms’ habitation. Fish and other<br>
aquatic creatures find their home in lakes.<br>
<b>Thirdly, lakes are used for navigation</b><br>
because water transport take place in<br>
lakes. Ferry transport services on lakes<br>
<b>Victoria, Tanganyika and Nyasa are of</b><br>
vital importance today. Similarly, in<br>
<b>North America, the Great Lakes provide</b><br>
easy access into and through the heart<br>
of the continent. Fourthly, lakes are<br>
useful for irrigation purposes such that<br>
areas surrounding some lakes foster<br>
agricultural activities through irrigation.<br>
<b>For example, the plains surrounding</b><br>
<b>Lake Chad are suitable for irrigation</b><br>
which supports about 22 000 hectares.<br>
<b>Furthermore, wheat, rice and cotton are</b><br>
grown in these plains. Likewise, the low-<br>
lying areas are suitable for plantations.<br>
<b>For example, areas close to Lake Kariba</b><br>
in Zimbabwe supports bananas’ irrigation<br>
farming. Fifthly, lakes supply water for<br>
domestic and industrial use. Water from<br>
lakes may be harnessed for domestic<br>
and industrial use.  Throughout Africa,<br>
farmers build their own small dams to<br>
provide water for their cattle, small scale<br>
irrigation and domestic use on one hand.<br>
On the other hand, lakes such as Victoria,<br>
<b>Tanganyika and Nyasa provide water for</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   246</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:05<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
247<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
the cities and towns in the interlacustrine<br>
regions. The Gaborone dam in Botswana,<br>
supplies water for the capital city.<br>
<b>Sixthly, lakes are used for hydro-electric</b><br>
power (H.E.P) production. All the large<br>
man-made lakes in Africa have been<br>
constructed primarily as reservoirs for<br>
H.E.P generating stations.  In Tanzania,<br>
for example, the Kidatu, Mtera, Kihansi,<br>
and Nyumba ya Mungu dams are used<br>
for H.E.P production. Seventhly, lakes<br>
are important for weather and climate<br>
moderation. The cool on shore breeze<br>
which develops in the afternoon<br>
alongsides the large lakes is an attraction<br>
in tropical areas. The large size of Lake<br>
<b>Victoria results in higher rainfall totals</b><br>
on the western and northern shores,<br>
which have thus become centres of<br>
intensive agriculture and population<br>
settlement. Eighthly, lakes are sources of<br>
minerals. In this case valuable minerals<br>
are extracted from lakes. For example,<br>
<b>Lake Magadi in Kenya, Lake Natron in</b><br>
<b>Tanzania and Lake Katwe in Uganda are</b><br>
inland drainage lakes that are naturally<br>
sarline. Due to this, soda-ash is produced<br>
commercially from Lake Magadi, and<br>
salt from Lake Katwe. Potash, Phosphate<br>
and Lime are extracted from Lake<br>
<b>Manyara and are used as raw materials</b><br>
for the Minjingu Fertilizer Industry.<br>
<b>Lastly, lakes are useful recreational</b><br>
destinations. Lakes are potential for<br>
development of recreational activities.<br>
<b>Lake Nakuru in Kenya, for example, is</b><br>
a game park because of the abundance<br>
of birds which attract many tourists.<br>
<b>Lake Nyasa provides an ideal recreation</b><br>
resort with facilities for sailing,<br>
swimming, snorkelling, diving and<br>
wind surfing.  Similarly, Lake Natron<br>
attracts migratory flamingos which<br>
attract tourists.<br>
<b>Problems facing lakes</b><br>
<b>Lakes encounter numerous threats</b><br>
that impair their usefulness. The main<br>
problems are:<br>
Firstly,<br>
pollution<br>
whereby<br>
the<br>
disposal/discharge<br>
of<br>
unwanted<br>
materials into lakes through industrial<br>
and domestic wastes disposal, oil<br>
spillage and poisonous fishing leading<br>
to lake pollution, thus endangers aquatic<br>
organisms. Secondly, excessive water<br>
exploitation due to population growth,<br>
whereby water is used more excessively<br>
than it can be renewed, results into<br>
lowering of water levels in some lakes.<br>
<b>Thirdly, human encroachment through</b><br>
establishment of human activities such<br>
as farming, animal keeping, mining,<br>
and constructions near lakes and<br>
reclamation threatens the existence<br>
of<br>
lakes.<br>
Fourthly,<br>
deforestation,<br>
particularly at catchment and river<br>
sources limits inlet discharge, causing a<br>
fall in the water levels of lakes. Fifthly,<br>
water weed and hyacinth invasion have<br>
an effect on limiting navigation and<br>
fishing activities in lakes, and threatens<br>
the existence of small lakes. Lastly, is<br>
the evaporation and siltation processes<br>
such that excessive evaporation and<br>
addition of silt on the lake floor cause<br>
a decrease in water level and hence<br>
threaten the existence of some lakes.<br>

1.	 Describe types of lakes based<br>
on their mode of formation.<br>

in Africa.<br>
<b>Wetland and swamps</b><br>
Wetlands are areas of marsh, fen,<br>
peatland or water, whether natural or<br>
artificial, permanent or temporary, with<br>
water that is static or flowing, fresh,<br>
blackish or salt including areas of marine<br>
water with the depth of which at low tides<br>
does not exceed six metres. Wetlands are<br>
land areas that are completely, partly<br>
or temporary inundated. These include<br>
flood plains, swamps and marshes,<br>
peatlands, lakes, mangrove forests, and<br>
river deltas. Wetlands exist in every<br>
country and in every climatic zone, from<br>
the Polar regions to the tropics. Wetlands<br>
are distributed around the world and<br>
cover an area of about 13.079 million<br>
km2. Africa has about 131 million<br>
hectares (equivalent to 1.31 million<br>
km2) of wetlands varying in types from<br>
saline coastal lagoon in West Africa to<br>
fresh and brackish water in East Africa<br>
(Figure 4.45). Examples of wetlands<br>
include the Okavango Delta in Upper<br>
Nile, Lake Victoria basin, Chad basin,<br>
<b>Niger and Zambezi, and the mangrove</b><br>
forests of East Africa stretching from<br>
<b>Kisimayu in Somalia to Maputo in</b><br>
<b>Mozambique. In Tanzania, wetlands</b><br>
include the Rufiji Delta, the Kilombero<br>
<b>Valley, Lake Natron, and Malagarasi-</b><br>
Moyowosi.<br>
<b>Figure 4.45: Wetland</b><br>

<b>Characteristics of wetlands</b><br>
<b>Wetlands are distinguished from</b><br>
landforms or water bodies due to their<br>
unique characteristics. A wetland is a<br>
land area that is saturated with water<br>
either permanently or seasonally such<br>
that it takes on characteristics of a<br>
distinct ecosystem. Wetlands contain<br>
vegetation of aquatic plants adapted to<br>
a unique hydric-soil (water – soil with<br>
specific features). Unlike other water<br>
bodies or landforms, wetlands play a<br>
number of roles in the environment,<br>
through water purification, flood control,<br>
carbon sinking, and shoreline stability.<br>
<b>Wetlands are considered as the most</b><br>
biologically diverse of all ecosystems,<br>
serving as home to a wide range of plants<br>
and animals. Water found in wetlands<br>
can be fresh water, brackish water or salt<br>
water. Thus, wetlands have unique water<br>
levels and types of plants that live within<br>
them. Wetlands have a water table that<br>
stands at or near the land surface for a<br>
long period each year to support aquatic<br>
plants; and also, wetlands provide a<br>
transition between dry land and water<br>
bodies. This transition is referred to as<br>
an ecotone.<br>
<b>Classification of wetlands</b><br>
<b>Wetlands are unique ecosystems that</b><br>
arise when inundation by water produces<br>
soils dominated by anaerobic processes<br>
which in turn force the biota, particularly<br>
root plants to adapt to flooding. Due to<br>
their complex and unique ecosystem,<br>
wetlands can be classified according to<br>
their location and soil types as follows:<br>
<b>Classification based on location</b><br>
<b>Based on location, wetlands are</b><br>
categorized as coastal and inland<br>
wetlands.<br>
<b>Coastal wetlands (tidal wetland) are</b><br>
found along the oceans and closely<br>
linked to estuaries and oceans where<br>
sea water and freshwater mix. They<br>
are dominated by grass, bushes and<br>
tidal salt marshes. Tidal marshes can<br>
be found along protected coastlines in<br>
middle and high latitudes worldwide.<br>
Some are freshwater marshes, delta,<br>
and mangrove swamps while others are<br>
salty, but they are all influenced by the<br>
motion of ocean tides. Tidal marshes are<br>
normally categorized into two distinct<br>
zones: the lower or intertidal marsh, and<br>
the upper or high marsh. Examples of<br>
coastal wetlands are mangrove wetlands<br>
along the East African coast stretching<br>
from Kisimayu in Somalia to Maputo<br>
in Mozambique.<br>
<b>Inland wetlands (non-tidal wetland)</b><br>
are frequently found along streams in<br>
poorly drained depressions and in the<br>
shallow water along the boundaries<br>
of lakes, ponds and rivers. Non-tidal<br>
marshes are the most prevalent and<br>
widely distributed wetlands. Water levels<br>
in these wetlands generally vary from<br>
a few inches to two or three feet, and<br>
some marshes, like prairie pot holes,<br>
may periodically dry out completely.<br>
<b>Inland wetlands include marshes and</b><br>
wet meadows dominated by herbaceous<br>
plants and swamps dominated by shrubs<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   249</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
250<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
and trees. Highly organic, mineral rich<br>
soils of sand, silt, and clay underlie<br>
these wetlands. Reeds and bulrushes<br>
provide excellent habitat for water fowls<br>
and other small mammals, such as red-<br>
winged blackbirds, great blue herons,<br>
otters and muskrats. Examples of inland<br>
wetlands include the Ihefu plains, the<br>
<b>Malagarasi-Moyowosi, Kilombero, and</b><br>
<b>Ruvu wetlands, Liwale swamp, Usangu</b><br>
flats, and Bahi swamp.<br>
<b>Classification according to soil types</b><br>
<b>In this category wetlands are categorized</b><br>
into marshes, swamps, bogs and fens.<br>
<b>Marshes are areas with shallow fresh</b><br>
water or salt water that are mostly<br>
grasslands and characterised by soil with<br>
low mineral content. The amount of water<br>
in a marsh can change with seasons, and<br>
in the case of salt marshes, they can also<br>
change with tides. Freshwater marshes<br>
have soft stemmed and herbaceous plants<br>
like grass, shrubs and wild flowers, and<br>
tend to occur at the edge of lakes and<br>
rivers (Figure 4.46). Salt water marshes<br>
include reeds, grass and different types<br>
of shrubs such as rushes, sedges and<br>
saltbush. They also tend to occur on<br>
coastlines inlets and estuaries where<br>
they are affected by tides and fresh<br>
water. Marshes are home to a variety of<br>
animals, including beavers, alligators,<br>
newts, shrimp and turtles.<br>
<b>Figure 4.46: Marshes</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   250</b><br>

Swamps are wetland ecosystems characterized by mineral soils with poor drainage.<br>
Swamps are found throughout the world, most often in low lying regions with<br>
poor drainage next to rivers. The nearby rivers supply water to these swamps.<br>
Some swamps develop from marshes that slowly fill in, allowing trees and woody<br>
shrubs to grow. The major difference between swamps and marshes however, is<br>
based on the type of vegetation dominating in each of them. While marshes are<br>
dominated by grasses, swamps are dominated by trees. Swamps are characterized<br>
by saturated soils during the growing season and standing water during certain<br>
times of the year. The highly organic soils of swamps form a thick black, nutrient<br>
rich environment for growing of water tolerant trees such as cypress, and shrubs<br>
like button bush. Also swamps provide habitat for specific plants, birds, fish and<br>
vertebrates such as fresh water shrimps, crayfish and clams. Both marshes and<br>
swamps may be fresh water or saltwater. Based on the types of vegetation available,<br>
swamps may be divided into two classes which are shrubs swamps and forested<br>
(woodland) swamps (Figure 4.47).<br>
<b>Figure 4.47: Swamps</b><br>
Bog is a type of wetland ecosystem characterized by wet, spongy, poorly drained<br>
peaty soil, dominated by the growth of bog, mosses, sphagnum and heaths,<br>
particularly Chamaedaphne calyculata. Bogs are usually acid areas, frequently<br>

surrounding a body of open water. Bogs receive water exclusively from rainfall.<br>
Few animals found in bogs include red deer, dragon-flies and birds such as<br>
grouse and plovers. Fen is a type of wetland ecosystem characterized by peaty<br>
soil, dominated by grass like plants, grass, sedges and reeds. Fens are usually<br>
alkaline areas, receiving water mostly from surface and groundwater sources<br>
(Figure 4.48).<br>

<b>Importance of wetlands</b><br>
<b>Wetlands are the link between land and</b><br>
water and are one of the most productive<br>
ecosystems in the World. There are<br>
various important functions of wetlands,<br>
these includes;<br>
<b>Provision of habitat for a wide variety</b><br>
of wildlife and plants: More than one<br>
third of the world’s threatened and<br>
endangered species live only in wetlands<br>
and nearly a half use wetland in their<br>
lives. Estuarine marine fish and shellfish,<br>
coastal birds and many other mammals<br>
depend on coastal wetlands to survive.<br>
<b>Most commercial and game fish use</b><br>
coastal wetlands as their nurseries for<br>
food, shelter and protection of their<br>
young ones. Also, wetlands are vital for<br>
breeding and migratory bird populations.<br>
<b>Purification and storage of water: As</b><br>
surface runoff passes through a healthy<br>
natural wetland, it acts as a filter retaining<br>
excessive nutrients and breaking down<br>
some pollutants before it reaches open<br>
water. Therefore, wetlands maintain high<br>
surface water quality. In addition, most<br>
wetlands act as a sponge, absorbing<br>
water when it is available in excess and<br>
releasing it into the groundwater supply<br>
slowly. They have positive impact on<br>
the hydrology as they ensure adequate<br>
stream flow throughout the year.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   252</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
253<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Floods control: In riparian systems</b><br>
of wetlands and rivers, the sponge-<br>
like action of wetland plants prevents<br>
floods. Preserving and restoring damaged<br>
wetlands can lead to flood control, which<br>
would require very expensive dredging<br>
operations and levee construction.<br>
<b>Protection of shoreline erosion:</b><br>
<b>Wetlands act as wind and tidal forces</b><br>
breaker, which could otherwise cause<br>
shoreline erosion. Coastal areas are<br>
subjected to tremendous erosion when<br>
exposed to storm surges. Wetlands<br>
hold soil with their roots, absorb the<br>
energy of waves and break up the<br>
flow of stream or river currents. Also,<br>
wetlands at the margins of lakes, rivers,<br>
bays and ocean protect shorelines and<br>
stream banks against erosion. Erosion<br>
control is very important today as many<br>
countries spend considerable resources<br>
to protect sensitive areas along rivers<br>
and coasts.<br>
<b>Provision of places for recreational</b><br>
activities: Wetlands have recreational,<br>
historical, scientific and cultural values.<br>
<b>For instance, in the USA more than half</b><br>
of the adult population hunt, fish, bird-<br>
watch or take photographs of wildlife in<br>
wetlands. Also school field trips often<br>
involve the study of wetlands. Painters<br>
and writers often find inspiration to<br>
create their art in wetlands.<br>
<b>Natural products: Some of the fishing</b><br>
industries, like shell fishing industries<br>
harvest wetland-dependent species.<br>
<b>Several plants found on wetlands are</b><br>
valuable such as blue barriers, timber<br>
and wild rice.<br>
<b>Agricultural activities: Wetlands have</b><br>
abundant supply of fresh water, and<br>
fertile soils. They serve as good areas<br>
for agricultural activities where crops<br>
like paddy, potatoes, and vegetables<br>
are grown.  Water from wetlands can<br>
be used for irrigation purposes.<br>
<b>Water supply for domestic uses: Water</b><br>
from wetlands is used for domestic<br>
purposes in Africa. Most African<br>
countries outside of the rainforest zone<br>
receive little rain for most of the year.<br>
<b>Hence water from temporary wetlands</b><br>
is used in households to serve domestic<br>
needs.<br>
<b>Control of climate change: Wetlands</b><br>
have mitigation effect through their<br>
ability to sink carbon. Wetlands store<br>
approximately 44.6 million tons of<br>
carbon per year globally which could<br>
otherwise be available in the atmosphere<br>
and contribute to the greenhouse effect.<br>
<b>Wetland loss and degradation</b><br>
<b>The life supporting importance of</b><br>
wetlands was largely undermined in<br>
the past. As a result, they were drained,<br>
dredged, dammed, and channelled. In<br>
some cases, they were eliminated and<br>
converted into dry-land for cropland,<br>
pasture, mining the underlying resource,<br>
and distorted their life support. Moreover,<br>
in some areas, they were filled with sand<br>
for road beds, flooded them for open<br>
water lakes, or use them as dumping<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   253</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
254<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
sites for water and sewage. Generally,<br>
wetlands are degraded through the<br>
following human activities:<br>
<b>Draining: Over many years, wetland</b><br>
areas have become one among the<br>
populated areas with human beings<br>
carry out various socio-economic<br>
and developmental activities. As a<br>
result, wetlands are drained for the<br>
sake of constructing  roads, railways,<br>
industries, housing, and cities. Most<br>
often crop farming and  for controlling<br>
mosquitoes.<br>
<b>Diverting: Wetland waters are also</b><br>
diverted via man made canals to other<br>
areas for agricultural use. They are also<br>
being directed to cities, for various<br>
uses including making the cities green.<br>
<b>Converting:</b><br>
<b>Some</b><br>
wetlands<br>
are<br>
converted to aquaculture for controlled<br>
growing of fish, shellfish, molluscs<br>
(oysters, mussels, clams), crustaceous<br>
(lobsters, shrimp, crabs) and seaweed.<br>
<b>Destruction of habitat and plant</b><br>
species: Trees and shrubs in mangrove<br>
swamps are cut down, and whole<br>
ecosystems is disturbed. In some areas,<br>
mangrove forests have been completely<br>
cleared and totally destroyed for urban<br>
development.<br>
<b>Pollution: Apart from the destruction</b><br>
of<br>
wetlands<br>
through<br>
different<br>
economic activities. Some of the<br>
human activities have been associated<br>
with<br>
contaminating<br>
the<br>
wetlands<br>
with pesticides and chemicals. With<br>
time, such contamination threaten<br>
the existence of wildlife and bird<br>
populations due to the increase pollution<br>
mostly<br>
caused<br>
by<br>
anthropogenic<br>
activities.<br>
<b>The Effects of wetlands destruction</b><br>
<b>The destruction of wetlands has</b><br>
resulted in serious impacts including<br>
the following. First, decline of water<br>
sources and wetlands functions.  Critical<br>
water changes occur when wetlands are<br>
altered causing decline of water table,<br>
disappearance of wetland vegetation,<br>
and water becomes inadequate to<br>
purify the wastes. Second, changes<br>
of ecosystems and weather. In some<br>
areas, wetlands have significantly<br>
changed. As the wetlands are being<br>
converted to cater for various human<br>
needs, thus leading to drastic change<br>
or disappearance of natural wetland<br>
ecosystems. Third, decline and loss of<br>
native and migratory species. Some of<br>
the living organisms, such as nesting<br>
birds,<br>
amphibian<br>
insects,<br>
reptiles<br>
and other organisms which inhabit<br>
wetlands have been lost or seriously<br>
affected due to shrinking of wetlands.<br>
<b>Migrating birds in great numbers are</b><br>
forced to fly further and for long time<br>
looking for other wetlands since their<br>
food sources have been degraded. For<br>
example, flamingos have been known<br>
to fly up to 375 miles (600 km) each<br>
night in search for conducive wetlands.<br>
Some species, such as crawling insects,<br>
plants, flowers and young wildlife<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   254</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
255<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
are perishing due to degradation of<br>
wetlands which no longer provide life<br>
support.<br>
<b>Fourth, the ecosystems and species</b><br>
in the wetlands have become at risk,<br>
in various ways. As natural wetlands<br>
are destroyed or degraded by human<br>
actions, species and ecosystems in<br>
these areas are highly affected. Most<br>
species which cannot adapt to new harsh<br>
environment become vulnerable and<br>
are likely to became extinct. Through<br>
human activities in the natural wetlands,<br>
the natural capacity for protection of<br>
these wetlands against erosion and<br>
degradation becomes minimal, making<br>
the area inhabitable. Many species have<br>
gone extinct and others have migrated<br>
due to loss of wetlands which formally<br>
habitated them. It should be noted that<br>
many species in wetland ecosystem are<br>
uniquely adapted in those physically<br>
isolated areas from other aquatic areas.<br>
<b>Therefore, destroying such wetlands</b><br>
could lead to loss of the entire ecosystem.<br>
<b>Wetland management</b><br>
<b>The wetlands can properly be managed</b><br>
in various ways. However, the following<br>
are the main management measures:<br>
formulation of policies, enactment of<br>
laws and regulations which are specific<br>
to wetland management; provision of<br>
education on the value and importance<br>
of wetlands to both human life and<br>
the ecosystem. There should also be<br>
provision of wetland management<br>
and conservation skills at all levels to<br>
enhance accountability; encouragement<br>
of construction of artificial wetlands in<br>
order to preserve the available natural<br>
ones so that they may not perish but<br>
flourish; promotion and encouragement<br>
of controlled farming in wetlands,<br>
such as controlled irrigation system to<br>
maintain water levels in wetlands. This<br>
should go hand in hand with avoidance<br>
of bushfire and shifting cultivation, as<br>
well as overstocking and overgrazing in<br>
wetlands; prevention of encroachment<br>
and poaching in wetlands, and control of<br>
settlement expansion towards wetlands.<br>
<b>Furthermore, discouraging overfishing</b><br>
and avoiding illegal fishing that uses<br>
dynamite explosions and poison;<br>
avoiding over-draining of water from<br>
wetlands for both domestic and industrial<br>
use.<br>
<b>Exercise 4.7</b><br>

Explain the importance of<br>
wetlands.<br>
2.<br>
Using a case of Tanzania,<br>
analyse the threat faced by<br>
wetlands and suggest remedial<br>
measures.<br>
<b>Oceans and seas</b><br>
<b>An ocean is a large body-mass of saline</b><br>
water that covers the most part of the<br>
earth’s surface. A sea is also a large<br>
body-mass of water which is smaller<br>
than an ocean but larger than a lake<br>

covering a part of the earth’s surface.<br>
Seas are partly enclosed by land masses.<br>
<b>Oceans and seas together cover almost</b><br>
97% of the earth’s surface while fresh<br>
water (including lakes, glaciers, ice,<br>
groundwater and rivers) covers 3%. The<br>
special importance of these large masses<br>
of water is that they influence climate,<br>
and weather elements through variations<br>
of such elements as temperature, rainfall,<br>
humidity and wind.<br>
Oceans of the world include the Pacific,<br>
the Atlantic, the Indian, the Arctic and<br>
the Southern oceans as shown in Table<br>
4.2. Seas include the Mediterranean<br>
<b>Sea, the Caribbean Sea, the South</b><br>
<b>China Sea, the East China Sea, the Gulf</b><br>
of Mexico, the Baring Sea, the Red Sea<br>
and the Baltic Sea (Figure 4.49).<br>

<b>Physical features of the ocean floor</b><br>
<b>The floor of the ocean has complex</b><br>
structures caused by horizontal and<br>
vertical tectonic motion, break in<br>
the earth’s crust, volcanic activities,<br>
sedimentation, and other factors. The<br>
diversity of underwater relief forms<br>
is classified in the following main<br>
categories including large areas of the<br>
ocean, continental margins, transition<br>
zones, ocean floor and mid ocean ridges.<br>
<b>Continental margins are areas where</b><br>
a transition from continent to ocean<br>
occurs. They include continental shelf,<br>
slope, and foot. The continental shelf<br>
originated from time, when flat, coastal<br>
areas became inundated due to increase<br>
in the height of the sea level from<br>
previous time. The width of the shelf<br>
varies from tens to hundreds kilometers<br>
and its depth can vary from 80 to 500 m.<br>
<b>The continental slope adjoins the shelf</b><br>
framing the continental borderland. The<br>
slope varies from 1 to more than 30 m.<br>
<b>The bottom of the slope reaches depth</b><br>
of 3 000 to 3 500 m where it changes<br>
to a very low grade of flatness at the<br>
continental foot. The continental foot<br>
is characterized by existence of great<br>
thickness of sediment accumulations.<br>
<b>The transition zone is characterized by</b><br>
change from continental crust to oceanic<br>
crusts. Island arcs, deep water troughs<br>
(trenches) and fringing basins are formed<br>
in the transition zone. The bottoms of<br>
the basins are frequently flat, caused<br>
by the expanse of great thickness of the<br>
sediments. However, they contain rises,<br>
ridges and seamounts. The island arcs<br>
separate fringing basins from the ocean,<br>
and are formed where oceanic plates<br>
collide (Figure 4.50).<br>
<b>Continental shelf</b><br>
<b>Continental slope</b><br>
<b>Trench</b><br>
<b>Ridge</b><br>
<b>Floor</b><br>

of the Ocean Floor<br>
<b>Chemical composition of the ocean</b><br>
water and sea<br>
<b>Ocean and sea water contains a number</b><br>
of mineral substances (mineral salts) in<br>
solution form. The important minerals<br>
are sodium chloride, calcium carbonate,<br>
magnesium and potassium. Generally,<br>
oceans contain traces of every mineral,<br>
whether in solution or held in suspension<br>
as solids. Mineral salts are brought into<br>
the seas and oceans through rivers<br>
and volcanic activities taking place<br>
on the seafloor. Although freshwater<br>
is continuously supplied in seas and<br>
oceans yet its effect is insignificant in<br>
diluting the salts concentration due to<br>
the simultaneously evaporation process<br>
in those large water bodies which make<br>
them concentrated ever since they were<br>
formed. Moreover, the free movement<br>
of water in the oceans results in the<br>
proportion of salts remaining in the<br>
oceans, but the amount of salts in a<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   257</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
258<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
given volume of the water varies from<br>
one part of the ocean or sea to another.<br>
<b>The concentration of mineral salts or</b><br>
salinity is measured or expressed as the<br>
number of grains of salts in every 1 000<br>
kg of water and its average is 35 kg. This<br>
means that in every 1 000 kg of water<br>
there will be 35 kg of minerals salts.<br>
<b>Salinity of an ocean and sea</b><br>
<b>Salinity refers to the concentration of</b><br>
salts in the water or soil. Ocean salinity<br>
is the concentration of salts in the ocean<br>
or sea water. It is classified as primary<br>
salinity, secondary salinity and tertiary<br>
salinity. The following are the basic<br>
factors affecting salinity of an ocean<br>
and sea:<br>
<b>Temperature: High temperature results</b><br>
in high evaporation rate, which in turn<br>
increases the concentration of salt.<br>
<b>Therefore, seas enclosed in tropical</b><br>
and sub-tropical areas experience higher<br>
concentrations of salts. On the other<br>
hand, in the oceans and seas located<br>
in Polar regions, the concentration of<br>
mineral salts is below average because<br>
there is low temperature, low evaporation<br>
rate, and the snow and ice supply large<br>
quantities of fresh water during spring<br>
and summer in those regions.<br>
<b>Amount of fresh water from the rivers:</b><br>
<b>High salinity occurs in inland seas such</b><br>
as the Dead Sea in Jordan, the Great Salt<br>
<b>Lake in North America and the Aral Sea</b><br>
in the former USSR. This is due to the<br>
fact that evaporation is relatively high in<br>
these seas and very little fresh water is<br>
brought into them by rivers. Likewise,<br>
salinity in the Mediterranean and Red<br>
seas is high. Contrary to that, the Baltic<br>
<b>Sea has relatively low salinity because</b><br>
several large rivers discharge their waters<br>
into it. In the equatorial region, ocean<br>
and sea water tend to have high salinity<br>
because of high evaporation rate.<br>
<b>Ocean current: An ocean current is</b><br>
the movement of ocean water either<br>
horizontally or vertically. There is a<br>
constant flow of surface water of the<br>
ocean due to prevailing winds, rotation of<br>
the earth and differences in temperature<br>
intensity. There are two types of ocean<br>
currents, namely cold ocean currents,<br>
and warm ocean currents. Cold ocean<br>
currents are large masses of cold water<br>
that move towards the equator. They<br>
absorb the near heat they receive in the<br>
tropics, thereby cooling the air above<br>
them. While warm ocean currents are<br>
large masses of water moving away from<br>
the equator with high temperature. They<br>
form when salty cold water becomes<br>
heavy and sinks in the process forcing<br>
warm and lighter water to move in the<br>
opposite direction. Ocean currents flow<br>
for long distances that create the global<br>
conveyor belt which plays a dominant<br>
role in determining the climate of many<br>
of the Earth’s regions. More specifically,<br>
ocean currents influence the temperature<br>
of the regions through which they travel.<br>
<b>For example, warm currents traveling</b><br>
along more temperate coasts increase the<br>
temperature of the area by warming the<br>
sea breezes that blow over them.<br>

<b>Water movements in the oceans</b><br>
<b>There are two types of water movements</b><br>
in the ocean namely horizontal and<br>
vertical movements. Horizontal<br>
movement of water gives rise to ocean<br>
currents while vertical movement<br>
results in the rising of bottom water<br>
and sinking of surface water. These<br>
movements result from the combined<br>
action of water density and winds. Wind<br>
is the principal cause of ocean currents.<br>
<b>On the other hand earth’s rotation and</b><br>
the shape of land masses influence the<br>
direction of the ocean currents. Examples<br>
of horizontal movements include: the<br>
westward flow of the equatorial currents<br>
which is influenced by the trade winds<br>
and the slow eastward movement of<br>
ocean current which is influenced by<br>
westerly wind known as the west-wind<br>
drift. The west-wind drift covers belts<br>
between 35o and 45o in the Northern<br>
hemisphere and between 30o and 60o in<br>
the Southern hemisphere and approaches<br>
the western sides of the continents. It is<br>
deflected towards the equator along the<br>
coast. The ocean current flow towards<br>
the equator are cool currents. They are<br>
often accompanied by upwelling along<br>
continental margins. In this process,<br>
cooler water from greater depths<br>
rises to the surface, making vertical<br>
movement of ocean current. Examples<br>
of cool currents with upwelling are the<br>
<b>Humboldt (Peru) current, the Chile</b><br>
current, the Benguela current and the<br>
<b>California current. On the Arctic and</b><br>
<b>Antarctic regions in the North-eastern</b><br>
<b>Atlantic Ocean, West- wind drift forms a</b><br>
relatively warm current to the Norwegian<br>
coast and British Isles. This makes the<br>
<b>Russia port of Murmansk on the Arctic</b><br>
Circle free from ice throughout the year.<br>
<b>While in the northern hemisphere, where</b><br>
the polar sea is largely landlocked, cold<br>
currents flow towards the equator along<br>
the east side of the continents. Examples<br>
are the Kamchatka current along the<br>
Asian Coast, and the Labrador Current,<br>
which flows between Labrador and the<br>
Greenland Coast.<br>
<b>The vertical movement are caused by</b><br>
density variations of water in the seas and<br>
oceans which is governed by temperature<br>
and salinity. When the temperature of<br>
water decreases, its density increases.<br>
<b>For example, polar surface waters are</b><br>
cold and their density is high, whereas<br>
equatorial surface waters are warm and<br>
their density is low. In general, polar<br>
waters sink and move towards the<br>
equator, while equatorial waters move<br>
as surface currents towards the poles.<br>
<b>If a body of cold water meets a body of</b><br>
warm water, the cold water sinks below<br>
the warm water.<br>
<b>When the salinity of water rises, the</b><br>
density rises as well. Likewise, if the<br>
salinity falls, the density falls. Therefore,<br>
when a body of water with high salinity<br>
meets another with low salinity, the more<br>
saline water sinks below the less saline<br>
water. A good example of this is the<br>
<b>Amazon River whose fresh water pushes</b><br>
into the Atlantic as a surface current for<br>
about 500 km because its salinity is much<br>
lower than that of the Atlantic.<br>

09/03/2022   17:06<br>

<b>Ocean water circulation</b><br>
<b>The interaction between the ocean and</b><br>
the atmosphere plays a major role in<br>
controlling the climate and weather at the<br>
surface. There are four primary factors<br>
that control ocean circulation.<br>
<b>Water salinity: Chemical weathering</b><br>
of rocks on land produces dissolved<br>
materials that enter the oceans, some<br>
of these dissolved chemicals undergo<br>
reactions within the ocean. For example,<br>
sea creatures extract dissolved calcium<br>
from the water to build their shells and<br>
when they die this calcium drifts onto<br>
the ocean floor.<br>
<b>Water temperature: The surface of the</b><br>
ocean absorbs the solar energy and<br>
gains heat. More heat is gained than<br>
lost in the low latitude and more is<br>
lost than gained in the high latitude.<br>
<b>This leads to polarward movement of</b><br>
warm surface water. This transport of<br>
heat from the equator towards higher<br>
latitudes provides good regulation of<br>
the earth’s climate system, otherwise<br>
the poles would be even colder and<br>
the tropics even hotter than they are at<br>
present.<br>
<b>Surface wind: The surface currents of the</b><br>
ocean are driven by the surface winds.<br>
<b>For example the trade winds drive the</b><br>
northern and southern equatorial currents<br>
moving in westerly direction parallel to<br>
the equator.<br>
<b>Coriolis effect: The surface currents</b><br>
are deflected by the continents and<br>
the Coriolis effect to the right in the<br>
northern hemisphere and to the left in<br>
the southern hemisphere. This creates<br>
warm currents along the eastern coasts<br>
of Americas, Australia, Asia and Africa.<br>
<b>In the north Atlantic this warm current</b><br>
is called the Gulf Stream which brings<br>
warm conditions to North - West Europe.<br>

<b>Water use</b><br>
<b>Water use describe the total amount of</b><br>
water withdrawn from its source to be<br>
used. Water can be used for direct and<br>
indirect purposes. Direct purpose of<br>
water use includes bathing, drinking, and<br>
cooking, while the indirect purposes of<br>
use includes use of water in processing<br>
wood to make paper and in producing<br>
steel for automobiles. The bulk of the<br>
world water use is for agriculture,<br>
industry and electricity.<br>

<b>Water use management</b><br>
<b>Water use management refers to the</b><br>
whole process of optimum use, planning,<br>
developing, and distributing water<br>
resources, which is essential for all<br>
forms of life. Water management is the<br>
control of movement of water resources<br>
to minimize damage to life and property<br>
as to maximize its beneficial use. Water<br>
is one of the most important resources<br>
not only to human beings but also to<br>
plants and animals. Only 3% of the water<br>
available in the world is fresh, of which<br>
only 1% is freely available to human<br>
beings. There are several sources of<br>
water namely, rainfall, wells, springs,<br>
rivers, lakes, seas and oceans.<br>
<b>Water harvesting practices</b><br>
<b>Water harvesting is the means of capturing</b><br>
or trapping rain as it falls or as runoff and<br>
properly manage by avoiding activities<br>
that may distort water quality and<br>
quantity. Water can be harvested to<br>
supplement the ever-increasing water<br>
shortages in some areas and therefore,<br>
relieve community’s unmet need of water<br>
for drinking and irrigation by increasing<br>
groundwater, reducing storm-water<br>
discharge, and reducing water along the<br>
coastal areas which could have caused<br>
side effects to flora and fauna. Broadly<br>
speaking there are four techniques of<br>
harvesting water, namely; capturing<br>
rainwater from rooftops, capturing<br>
runoff from local catchments, capturing<br>
seasonal flood waters from local streams<br>
and conserving water through watershed<br>
management.<br>
<b>Rainwater harvesting methods</b><br>
<b>Rainwater harvesting is the collection</b><br>
and storage of rainwater for reuse on site<br>
rather than allowing it to spread on the<br>
land surface. The stored water can be used<br>
for various purposes, such as gardening,<br>
domestic uses, and irrigation. There are<br>
two ways of harvesting rainwater. These<br>
include roof top rainwater harvesting,<br>
and surface runoff harvesting.<br>
<b>Roof-top rainwater harvesting: This</b><br>
is a system of tapping rainwater as<br>
it falls. Under this method, the roof<br>
becomes the catchment from which<br>
rain water is collected. Such water can<br>
either be stored in a tank or diverted to<br>
artificial recharge system. The method<br>
is less expensive and very effective.<br>
Moreover, if it is implemented properly,<br>
it can help to increase the groundwater<br>
level of the area.<br>
<b>Surface runoff harvesting: Through this</b><br>
method, rainwater in form of surface<br>
runoff can be trapped and used for<br>
recharging aquifer. This method can be<br>
most relevant in rural areas where there<br>
is acute water shortage due to inadequate<br>
water supply and poor distribution.<br>
<b>The importance of rainwater</b><br>
harvesting<br>
<b>Rainwater harvesting increases water</b><br>
availability for both domestic, industrial<br>
and agricultural use. Meanwhile,<br>
environmental degradation has increased<br>
surface runoff so that rain falling on<br>
surface flows away rapidly thus affecting<br>
the rate of groundwater recharge. It is<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   261</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
262<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
clear that, water sources are insufficient<br>
to meet the demands of water supply in<br>
both urban and rural areas on one hand.<br>
<b>On the other hand, the over exploitation</b><br>
of groundwater resources has led to a<br>
decrease in groundwater levels and<br>
quality. Therefore, to minimize the<br>
challanges it is important to tap rainwater<br>
by either storing it for direct use or by<br>
recharging it to groundwater aquifers.<br>
<b>Rainwater harvesting, if properly</b><br>
managed, has the following advantages:<br>
<b>It can help meet the increasing demand</b><br>
for water in the urban and rural areas. It<br>
can reduce runoff and prevents flooding<br>
of roads. The polluted groundwater can<br>
supplement domestic water requirements<br>
during drought conditions. Rooftop<br>
rainwater harvesting is easy to construct,<br>
operate and maintain. It can reduce soil<br>
erosion in urban and rural areas.<br>

<b>Water conservation</b><br>
<b>Water conservation refers to the act</b><br>
of protecting water from destructive<br>
influences. It is the task of not only<br>
protecting but also maintaining water<br>
as a precious resource. Water must be<br>
conserved and protected from all sources<br>
of pollution and degradation that may<br>
compromise its quality and quantity for<br>
unlimited uses.<br>
<b>Water pollution</b><br>
Water pollution is the release of chemical,<br>
physical or biological substances into<br>
bodies of water that makes water unsafe<br>
for diverse use. Water pollution occur<br>
when the composition of water is altered<br>
to make it less suitable for purposes<br>
it would otherwise be suitable in its<br>
natural state. There are several types<br>
of water pollution such as groundwater<br>
pollution, chemical pollution, nutrients<br>
pollution, oxygen depletion pollution,<br>
microbiological pollution, suspended<br>
matter pollution, and surface water<br>
pollution. Hence, polluted water contains<br>
numerous impurities that are not only<br>
poisonous to the aquatic organisms but<br>
also unsafe for human use. The sources<br>
of water pollution include domestic,<br>
industrial and effluent wastes, as well<br>
as massive construction.<br>

<b>Types of water pollution</b><br>
<b>Water Pollution can be classified</b><br>
using several criteria in this case water<br>
pollution is classified based on types of<br>
water pollutants and the source of water<br>
pollutants.<br>
<b>Types of water pollution based on type</b><br>
of water pollutants<br>
<b>In this criteria water pollutants can be</b><br>
classified into three categories namely<br>
chemical, radioactive and biological.<br>
<b>Chemical pollutants: This is the</b><br>
contamination of water with various<br>
chemical pollutants. The most common<br>
water pollutants include crude oil and<br>
various petroleum products with a range<br>
of chemicals such as gasoline, diesel, and<br>
motor lubricants. These compounds are<br>
lighter than water, due to this they will<br>
remain on the top of water. However,<br>
some of these, even in trace amount<br>
may be harmful and unfortunately,<br>
may remain un unnoticed into water.<br>
<b>Another is fertilizers (example, nitrate</b><br>
and phosphate) which are beneficial to<br>
algae, micro-organisms and plant growth.<br>
<b>However, their higher amounts in water</b><br>
are harmful to human and aquatic life.<br>
<b>Also, chlorinated solvents which include</b><br>
carbon tetra chloride and freons is<br>
another chemical pollutant of water.<br>
<b>Since these chemicals are denser than</b><br>
water have a tendency of sinking into<br>
water and are persistent and toxic in<br>
nature. Another water pollutants are<br>
petroleum solvents (include benzene,<br>
toluene, xylenes). These are harmful<br>
chemicals to human being and the<br>
environment when discharged and find<br>
their way into water bodies. Moreover,<br>
antibiotics and other pharmaceutical<br>
products once accumulate into water<br>
they contaminate it to an extent of<br>
making water no longer useful not<br>
only to human being but also to micro-<br>
organism and other life forms. Other<br>
forms of chemical pollutants include<br>
metal and their compounds. Organo-<br>
metal compounds forms following the<br>
reaction between metals and organic<br>
compounds that exist into water. The<br>
resultant pollutants into water bodies<br>
are harmful to human and aquatic life.<br>
<b>Radioactive pollutants: This pollutant is</b><br>
also known as radioactive contamination.<br>
<b>It is another source of water pollution</b><br>
whereby the improper dispose of these<br>
pollutants seriously harm life and the<br>
environment. Biological pollutants:<br>
<b>This includes various types of micro-</b><br>
organisms such as bacterial, worms and<br>
viruses of which their presence in large<br>
quantities in water bodies account for<br>
water pollution, hence unsuitable for<br>
human consumption.<br>
<b>Types of water pollution based on</b><br>
sources of water pollutants<br>
<b>The classification of water pollutants</b><br>
based on sources of pollution results<br>
from an accidental disposal of chemical<br>
substances from various sources. These<br>
sources can include pharmaceutical,<br>
industrial, construction sites, household,<br>
agro-chemicals disposal and accidental<br>
oil spills. These contaminants pollute<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   263</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
264<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
water in different ways and degrees as<br>
describe below.<br>
<b>Domestic wastes: Despite increasing</b><br>
demand of water for domestic uses still<br>
different sources of water are polluted<br>
by various human activities.  As a result,<br>
rivers become loaded by domestic wastes<br>
such as sewage, washing water, dead<br>
bodies, food wastes, plastic containers,<br>
old tyres, old metals and glass bottles.<br>
<b>These wastes are of concern and</b><br>
dangerous to life as most of them<br>
cannot easily decompose. Moreover,<br>
wastes like mercury and lead are<br>
carcinogenic. Untreated wastes of this<br>
nature are hazardous to human beings<br>
and other life forms, and some of them<br>
act as sources of water borne diseases<br>
such as dysentery, cholera and typhoid.<br>
<b>Sometimes chemicals from pesticides</b><br>
and fertilizers are drained to sources<br>
of water.<br>
<b>Industrial Wastes: Industrial wastes may</b><br>
include petroleum, detergents, heavy<br>
metals, and radioactive materials which<br>
often find their way into rivers and other<br>
sources of water. Rivers in turn pour<br>
their water into oceans, seas and lakes<br>
thereby adding or intensifying pollution<br>
of those large water bodies. Based on this<br>
chain, oceans are being overloaded with<br>
unbearable wastes. Some of the wastes<br>
received by the oceans particularly,<br>
mercury and lead can accidentally be<br>
taken by aquatic organisms such as<br>
fish. These chemical often accumulate<br>
in their bodies without causing them<br>
harm. However, human consumption<br>
of those contaminated food stuff can<br>
increase the chances of getting diseases<br>
such as cancer.<br>
<b>Massive construction: The increasing</b><br>
constructions near water catchments<br>
and sources, such as construction of<br>
bridges; encroachments of settlements<br>
and establishment of industries near<br>
water bodies significantly contribute<br>
to pollution of those water bodies. The<br>
pollutants include remaining metals,<br>
cement, pieces of wood, paint, and<br>
pieces of ceiling boards. Upstream<br>
development such as road construction<br>
and agricultural activities often increase<br>
soil erosion, resulting in increased<br>
sedimentation. This leads to flooding in<br>
mid-stream areas and reduction of water<br>
flows downstream. Overuse of limited<br>
water resources is influenced by humans<br>
interference the natural water cycle.<br>
<b>Overexploitation of water resources: The</b><br>
increased demand and uses of water vary<br>
from one place to another depending<br>
on the level of social, economic and<br>
technological advancement which often<br>
lead to a mismatch between availability<br>
and supply of water. The major consumers<br>
of water are rich industrialized countries<br>
while the minor consumers are the least<br>
developed countries. The amount of<br>
water that Africa uses for agricultural<br>
purposes is equivalent to about<br>
0.1% of the water that industrialized<br>
countries use for the same purpose.<br>
<b>This amount is also equivalent to 3%</b><br>
of the amount of water that is used in<br>
North America.<br>

<b>Misuse of water: Overuse of water in</b><br>
one area deprive uses in other areas.<br>
<b>This is likely to compromise agricultural</b><br>
production and its effects can be extended<br>
to food security, loss of income, and loss<br>
of sources of wages. Misuse of water<br>
occurs where clean water is taken or used<br>
and returned to the water system in an<br>
unusable state. Sometimes, the water is<br>
not returned to the system at all due to<br>
unsupportive infrastructure.<br>
<b>Agricultural chemicals: The agricultural</b><br>
chemicals used in irrigated farming<br>
often contaminate surface water and<br>
groundwater. Potassium and nitrogen<br>
from fertilizer applications on both<br>
rain fed and irrigated land, may be<br>
washed into groundwater or surface<br>
water where they can lead to algal<br>
blooms and eutrophication of surface<br>
water. Irrigation can also concentrate<br>
naturally occurring salt minerals through<br>
application of fertilizer which in turn<br>
flows back to groundwater or surface<br>
streams and rivers. Irrigation in arid<br>
regions can also lead to leaching of<br>
naturally-occurring toxic elements, such<br>
as selenium from soils into surface and<br>
groundwater. Over-irrigation can lead<br>
to water logging, which reduces yields<br>
substantially. Industry and urban centres<br>
also return contaminated water to both<br>
surface and groundwater resources.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Five</b><br>
<b>Soils</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Soils are living systems that provide the most fundamental functions needed<br>
for life. In this chapter, you will learn about concept of soils, components of<br>
soil, soil properties, soil profile, soil formation, soil classification, soil fertility,<br>
and soil conservation and management practices. The competencies developed<br>
from this chapter will enable you to use soil resources for various economic<br>
activities like agriculture, building and construction, and tourism. Also, you will<br>
be able to participate in soil conservation and management practices for self<br>
and national development.<br>
<b>Concept of soil</b><br>
<b>Soil is defined as the naturally</b><br>
occurring, unconsolidated upper layer<br>
of the Earth made of the mixture of<br>
minerals, organic matter, gases, liquids<br>
and organisms that together support life<br>
on earth. It consists of unconsolidated<br>
materials of the soil which form a<br>
thin surface layer of the earth above<br>
bedrock refered to as regolith. Soil<br>
is an outcome of interrelationships<br>
and interactions between physical,<br>
chemical and biological processes with<br>
varying natural environment. Soil is<br>
a natural body known as pedosphere<br>
which performs different functions,<br>
such as acting as a medium for plant<br>
growth and a means of water storage,<br>
supply and purification. The scientific<br>
study of all aspects of soil such as<br>
origin, formation, characteristics and<br>
ultilization is called pedology. Soil is<br>
one of the important natural resources<br>
that supports life and ecosystem of an<br>
area. Soil provides nutrients, water,<br>
oxygen and heat to natural land area.<br>
<b>It is important to understand the</b><br>
ability and capacity of soil for proper<br>
management of land.<br>
<b>Components of soil</b><br>
Soil consists of four major components,<br>
which are mineral or inorganic matter,<br>
organic matter, water, and air. These<br>
components exist mostly in a fine<br>
state of subdivision and are intimately<br>
mixed to the extent that a complete<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
268<br>

separation is difficult. Soil contains<br>
about 50% pore space (air 25% and<br>
water 25%), 45% mineral matter, and<br>
5% organic matter (Figure 5.1). The<br>
proportion of air and water in the soil<br>
is subject to great fluctuations due to<br>
natural conditions such as soil texture<br>
and structure, rate of evaporation<br>
and precipitation of an area. It is the<br>
proportion of air and water in the soil<br>
that determine the soil suitability for<br>
plant growth.<br>

<b>Figure 5.1: Soil components</b><br>
<b>Mineral matter</b><br>
<b>The largest component of soil is</b><br>
the mineral matter, also known as<br>
inorganic matter, which makes up<br>
approximately 45% of the total volume<br>
of soil. Soil minerals are derived from<br>
two principal mineral types: primary<br>
minerals and secondary minerals.<br>
<b>Primary minerals are those which</b><br>
have not been chemically altered since<br>
their crystallization from molten lava,<br>
commonly found in sand and silt. They<br>
are similar to the parent rock materials<br>
from which they are formed. They<br>
are often round or irregular in shape.<br>
<b>Secondary minerals result from the</b><br>
weathering of the primary minerals,<br>
which release important ions and<br>
forms more stable minerals, such as<br>
silicate clay. Clays have a large surface<br>
area, which is important for soil<br>
chemistry and water holding capacity.<br>
<b>Additionally, positive and negative</b><br>
charges found around soil minerals<br>
influence the suitability to retain<br>
important nutrients. Mineral matter of<br>
the soil is significant in adding fertility<br>
to the soil, which determines the types<br>
of crops suitable to be grown in the<br>
given soil. Additionally, mineral matter<br>
helps to determine the types of parent<br>
rock. In general, inorganic matter<br>
determines soil properties, rate of soil<br>
formation and textural categories of<br>
soils.<br>
<b>Organic matter</b><br>
<b>Soil organic matter entails the dead</b><br>
decomposed<br>
plant<br>
and<br>
animal<br>
remains or humas as well as living<br>
soil microorganisms such as bacteria,<br>
fungi and earthworms. Organic matter<br>
constitutes 5% of the total volume of<br>
soil. The decomposed organic matter is<br>
renewed in the soil through addition of<br>
dead plants and animal remains and the<br>
waste products from living organisms<br>
to form humus. There are two sources<br>
of humus in the soil: primary source<br>
is from plant remains and secondary<br>
source is from animal remains. Some of<br>
the humus from those sources are more<br>
resistant to further decomposition and<br>
are called stable soil organic matter,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   268</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
269<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
and actually it is what is called humus.<br>
<b>Those which are rapidly decomposed</b><br>
and get completely lost are called<br>
labile organic matter. Soil organisms<br>
such as earth worms, insects, fungi<br>
and microscopic bacteria play the role<br>
of breaking down dead plants and<br>
animals, mixing them with the mineral<br>
soils near the surface. As a result,<br>
the upper most horizon of the soil is<br>
generally rich in organic matter. This<br>
horizon is darker than the lower mineral<br>
horizons. The amount of organic<br>
matter in the soil represents a balance<br>
between the addition of plant residues<br>
and the destructive decomposition by<br>
soil micro-organisms. Generally, the<br>
organic matter content in the top soil<br>
is about 3-5% by weight and influences<br>
most of the physical and chemical<br>
properties. Despite its small content,<br>
organic matter has great influence on<br>
both physical and chemical properties<br>
of soil. Amount of organic matter<br>
varies with space due to differences in<br>
drainage, relief, vegetation and climate.<br>
<b>Soil water</b><br>
<b>Water is one of the basic components</b><br>
of soil which mainly comes from the<br>
atmosphere through precipitation and<br>
underground water. Water makes up to<br>
25% of the total volume of soil. Water is<br>
important for transporting nutrients to<br>
growing plants, soil organisms and for<br>
facilitating both biological and chemical<br>
decomposition. Amount of soil water<br>
(soil moisture) normally varies from<br>
soil to soil due to the number of factors<br>
including soil structure, texture, nature<br>
of underlaying rocks, relief and climate.<br>
<b>Soil water availability (available water</b><br>
capacity) is the capacity of a particular<br>
soil to hold water for plant use. The<br>
amount of soil moisture that is held<br>
after excess water has drained away and<br>
after the rate of downward movement<br>
has decreased (usually 2-3 days after<br>
rain or irrigation) in pervious soil of<br>
uniform texture and structure is known<br>
as field capacity. This exists in soil as<br>
hygroscopic water which is the type of<br>
soil water mostly present in the soil,<br>
unless the soil becomes completely<br>
dry. Water that is attracted to and forms<br>
film around the hygroscopic water with<br>
lower cohesive strength is known as<br>
capillary water. Another type of soil<br>
water is gravitational water which is<br>
found only when the soil is wet but<br>
unavailable as excess water drains<br>
away. The smaller the particles in soil,<br>
the higher the water holding capacity.<br>
<b>The capacity of soil to hold water is</b><br>
largely dependent on soil texture. Thus,<br>
clay soil which has very small particles<br>
has higher water holding capacity<br>
compared to sand which has large sized<br>
particles. The point where water is held<br>
microscopically with too much energy<br>
for a plant to extract is called the wilting<br>
coefficient or permanent wilting point.<br>
<b>When water is bound so tightly to soil</b><br>
particles, it becomes difficult for most<br>
of the plants to access it. This limits<br>
the amount of water available for plant<br>
use. If the soil water content decreases<br>
to the permanent wilting point or any<br>
lower point, most plants can no longer<br>
retrieve soil water for transpiration, thus<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   269</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
270<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
wilt. Moderate or medium pore spaces<br>
allow the flow of required moisture<br>
(water) in the soil, which in a long run<br>
support living organisms present in the<br>
soil to effectively perform bioactivities<br>
and ensure supply of humus and plant<br>
growth.<br>
<b>Soil air</b><br>
<b>Gas or air is the basic component of the</b><br>
soil. Air occupies the same proportion of<br>
pore spaces as that of water (25%) in the<br>
total volume of the soil. Air fills the pore<br>
spaces left unoccupied by soil moisture.<br>
<b>Compared to the atmospheric air, the air</b><br>
in the soil contains more carbon dioxide<br>
released by plant and soil biota, and more<br>
water vapour but less oxygen. Despite<br>
its trace proportional in the soil, still<br>
<b>Oxygen is essential for root and microbe</b><br>
respiration which helps to support plant<br>
growth. Carbon dioxide and nitrogen<br>
are also important for underground<br>
plant functions, such as nitrogen fixing<br>
bacteria. If soils remain waterlogged a<br>
situation by which gas is displaced by<br>
excess water, it can prevent root gas<br>
exchange and may often lead to death<br>
of plants. This is a common concern in<br>
floods and water-logged areas. Amount<br>
of soil air is mainly determined by<br>
climate of the area, season, relief, soil<br>
structure and texture.<br>
<b>Soil properties</b><br>
<b>Soil properties include physical and</b><br>
chemical characteristics of the soil.<br>
Physical properties include texture,<br>
structure, profile, colour, temperature,<br>
porosity and density. Chemical<br>
properties include soil pH, anion and<br>
cation exchange.<br>
<b>Physical properties of the soil</b><br>
<b>These are properties related to physical</b><br>
characteristic and processes in the soil<br>
namely; soil texture, soil structure, soil<br>
colour, soil temperature, soil porosity<br>
and soil density.<br>
<b>Soil texture</b><br>
<b>Soil texture refers to the degree of</b><br>
coarseness or fineness of soil particles.<br>
These soil particles range from coarse,<br>
medium to fine that form sand, silt and<br>
clay soil respectively. The classes are<br>
distinguished in the field by textural<br>
feel which can be further clarified by<br>
separating the relative proportions of<br>
sand, silt and clay by using grading<br>
sieves, based on the particle size<br>
distribution. The classes can be used<br>
to determine crop suitability and<br>
approximate the soil’s responses to<br>
various environmental and management<br>
conditions, such as drought and<br>
calcium (lime) requirements. In soil<br>
science, individual particles of soil are<br>
given names according to their specific<br>
diameters. Table 5.1 presents soil<br>
texture classification as per the United<br>
<b>States Department of Agriculture-</b><br>
<b>USDA against the World Reference</b><br>
<b>Base for soil Resources-WRB (Formed</b><br>
by International Union of Soil Science<br>
<b>- IUSS).</b><br>
