<b>Soil texture analysis</b><br>
<b>Soil texture is determined by the</b><br>
proportions of sand, silt and clay<br>
particles. In the field, it is possible to<br>
decide whether a soil sample is mainly<br>
sand, silt, or clay by feeling it. Sand soil<br>
feels gritty and lacks cohesion, silt is<br>
smoother, soap like feel and is having<br>
a considerable cohesion. Clay is sticky,<br>
plastic and is high cohesive in nature. The<br>
more precise method for determining the<br>
proportion of particles in a given soil is<br>
through laboratory measurements such<br>
as sieving and sedimentation methods.<br>
<b>Sieving method involves the use of sieve</b><br>
that consist different meshes that allows<br>
some of the texture to pass and filter<br>
some of it (Figure 5.2). Clay textures<br>
accumulate at the bottom, while stones<br>
remain at the top. Course, sand and silt<br>
are filtered separately between top and<br>
bottom to their size of the texture while<br>
stones (gravels) are filtered at the top.<br>
<b>Figure 5.2: Sieving method</b><br>
<b>Sedimentation is generally a process</b><br>
that allows particles in suspension in<br>
water to settle out of the suspension<br>
under the effect of gravity. The method<br>
may involve placing the soil sample<br>
into a beaker of water, then, the beaker<br>
is shaken thoroughly and left to settle.<br>
<b>The course texture settles at the bottom</b><br>
quickly and clay suspends at the top as<br>
seen in Figure 5. 3.<br>

<b>Water</b><br>
<b>Clay</b><br>
<b>Silt</b><br>
<b>Fine sand</b><br>
<b>Thick sand</b><br>
<b>Gravel</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.3: Sedimentation method</b><br>

The results obtained are either plotted as a pie chart or as a triangular graph.<br>
Since the proportion of sand, silt and clay vary considerably, it is traditional to<br>
have 12 texture categories as shown in the soil texture triangle (Figure 5.4). The<br>
soil texture triangle is used to convert particle size distribution into recognized<br>
texture class based on the relative amounts or proportions of sand, silts and clay<br>
in various types of soil as a percentage.<br>

<b>Textural classification of soil</b><br>
<b>Sandy soils</b><br>
These are coarse grained soil particles with a gritty feel and lack of cohesion.<br>
Sandy soils are light soils consisting mainly of sand particles with diameters<br>
between 0.05mm and 2mm. Sandy soils contain about 70% or more of sand, it<br>
allows water to drain rapidly and retains little proportion of moisture. Moreover,<br>
it is well aerated and suitable for the growth of ground nut, peas and horticulture.<br>
However, it is more vulnerable to leaching, drought and erosion.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   272</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
273<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Silt soil</b><br>
<b>Silt is a granular material with small</b><br>
size between sand and clay, whose<br>
mineral origin is quartz and feldspar.<br>
<b>Silt may occur as soil or as sediment</b><br>
mixed in suspension with water in a<br>
body of water such as river. It may also<br>
exist as soil deposited at the bottom of a<br>
water body. Silt is typically non-sticky.<br>
<b>Silt particles range from 0.002mm to</b><br>
0.05mm in diameter. It is associated<br>
with cementation which subjects them<br>
to sheet erosion during heavy rains.<br>
<b>Clay soil</b><br>
<b>This is a fine-grained soil with sticky</b><br>
feel. It is elastic when wet being very<br>
cohesive and may be rolled into various<br>
shapes. Clay soil consists of fine<br>
particles of less than 0.002 mm. It holds<br>
little air but much water, and becomes<br>
impermeable after absorbing water. It<br>
is rich in plants nutrients and it is often<br>
fertile. As its name suggests, clay soil<br>
contains more than 35% clay. The soil<br>
supports perennial crops and is suitable<br>
for growing paddy and other cereal<br>
crops. These soils are characterized by<br>
tillage difficulties due to their sticking<br>
tendency in wet seasons and hardness<br>
in dry seasons.<br>
<b>Loam soil</b><br>
Loam soil is composed of sand (40%),<br>
silt (40%) and clay (20%). The soil<br>
prevents water logging capacity but it<br>
allows moisture holding capacity and<br>
retaining of nutrients. Top quality soil<br>
has a loose and open structure that makes<br>
it fast draining but also retains enough<br>
water so that plants are not vulnerable to<br>
drought stress. Loam soils are easier to<br>
till than clay soils. It is potential soil for<br>
agriculture as it is composed of enough<br>
proportion of clay component to hold<br>
and retain nutrients; sufficient sand to<br>
prevent water logging and to improve<br>
drainage; as well as enough silt to bind<br>
sand and clay together.<br>
<b>The importance of soil texture</b><br>
<b>Soil texture influences plants growth</b><br>
in varying ways. It influences soil<br>
permeability, structure and retention<br>
capacity.<br>
<b>Texture</b><br>
controls<br>
the<br>
availability and retentions of nutrients<br>
for plant growth within the soil. For<br>
example, clay soils tend to hold more<br>
water and are less well drained and<br>
aerated than sandy soils. Nutrients<br>
are absorbed into clay particles thus,<br>
become less leached by infiltration<br>
than in sandy soil. Also, it influences<br>
plant growth and roots penetration.<br>
<b>For example, plant roots can penetrate</b><br>
coarse soils more easily than finer soils<br>
and lighter sandy soils are easier to<br>
plough for arable farming than heavier<br>
clay. Moreover, soil texture influences<br>
soil resistance against erosion. For<br>
example, light soils are more prone<br>
to soil erosion than heavy soils. Also,<br>
it determines the type of crop to be<br>
grown, since different types of crops<br>
have different water requirements for<br>
their growth.<br>

<b>Activity 5.1</b><br>
<b>In a group of five students, walk</b><br>
around the school premises or<br>
neighbourhood,<br>
then<br>
do<br>
the<br>
following;<br>
(a)	 Dig different pit holes at<br>
different points and fill them<br>
with water,<br>
(b)	 Leave those holes with water<br>
for several hours, for water<br>
to infiltrate. Based on your<br>
observation, identify the type<br>
of those soils basing on the<br>
rate of infiltration, and<br>
(c)	 If water does not infiltrate, then<br>
leave it for a day. Investigate<br>
the type of soil.<br>
(d)	 Discuss the findings with your<br>
fellow students and write a<br>
summary in your exercise book.<br>
(e)	 Present the findings before<br>
the class and address all the<br>
emerging questions.<br>
<b>Soil structure</b><br>
<b>Soil structure refers to the arrangement</b><br>
of individual soil particles into larger<br>
particles called aggregates. Aggregate<br>
is a group of soil particles held together<br>
by electrostatic forces. These aggregates<br>
form different shapes known as peds.<br>
<b>Soil structure is easily determined by</b><br>
taking a sample of soil that has not<br>
been disturbed and observe it closely to<br>
determine its shape. A number of factors<br>
influence the stability of soil structure.<br>
<b>The primary factor is soil organic matter</b><br>
whose breakdown products create<br>
a cementing effect holding mineral<br>
particles together. Other factors include<br>
soil colloids, tillage, and freezing and<br>
thawing cycles.  Soil structure can be<br>
categorized as single grained structure<br>
and massive structure. Single grained<br>
structure is when the soil is broken into<br>
individual particles that do not stick<br>
together. It always accompanied by a<br>
loose consistence, commonly found in<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   274</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
275<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
sandy soils. Massive structure is the type of structure, where individual particles<br>
of soils are cemented to one another into different shapes of different sizes, such<br>
as prismatic, platy, blocky, crumb, columnar and granular shape.<br>
<b>Crumb structure</b><br>
In this structure, the aggregates are almost round with axis of the same size<br>
(1-5mm). The particles are similar to breadcrumbs and are porous in nature<br>
(Figure 5.5). This type of soil is found in the A horizon and in loam soil. The soils<br>
in this structure are very productive, well aerated, drained, and have good roots<br>
penetration, hence influence considerable plant growth.<br>
<b>Clay</b><br>
<b>Silt</b><br>
<b>Sand</b><br>
<b>Pore space</b><br>
<b>Crumb</b><br>
<b>Relative sizes of particles</b><br>
<b>Enlarged view of soil structure</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.5: Crumby soil structure</b><br>
<b>Granular structure</b><br>
<b>This structure contains small individual</b><br>
particles which are usually non porous.<br>
<b>Its size ranges from 1 to 5 mm. It is found</b><br>
in A-horizon, and compacted in clay soil.<br>
<b>Soil mineral particles are both separated</b><br>
and bridged by organic matter, broken<br>
down products and soil biota which<br>
exudates organic compounds making the<br>
soil easy for cultivation. This soil is fairly<br>
productive and has a problem of drainage<br>
and aeration. This structure allows for<br>
good porosity and easy movement of air<br>
and water (Figure 5.6).<br>
<b>Figure 5.6: Granular soil structure</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   275</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
276<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Platy structure</b><br>
<b>In a plate-like structure, individual</b><br>
particles of soils are arranged on top of<br>
one another horizontally like plates and<br>
bound in compacted soil (Figure 5.7).<br>
<b>Its size is between 1mm and 10mm and</b><br>
it is found in B-horizon. It is usually<br>
found in compacted soil of clay and silt<br>
and they are stretched more horizontally<br>
than vertically. Moreover, it allows<br>
moderate water penetration, aeration<br>
and drainage. It is less productive and<br>
restricts roots penetration.<br>
<b>Figure 5.7: Plate-like soil structure</b><br>
<b>Blocky structure</b><br>
<b>This is a soil structure which has a</b><br>
shape like angular blocks and having<br>
more or less edge. Its particles are<br>
irregular in shape, with horizontal and<br>
vertical axes of about equal length. The<br>
particles may be rounded or angular but<br>
closely fitting (Figure 5.8). They are<br>
commonly found in B-horizon where<br>
clay-loam soils have accumulated.<br>
<b>The soil is productive, well drained</b><br>
and aerated. Its size is between 10 mm<br>
and 75mm its formation is associated<br>
with wetting, drying and thawing<br>
processes.<br>
<b>Figure 5.8: Blocky soil structure</b><br>
<b>Prismatic structure</b><br>
<b>It is a massive type of soil structure</b><br>
containing<br>
particles<br>
arranged<br>
in<br>
vertical columns of soil. Its sizes range<br>
from 20 mm to 100 mm long. It is very<br>
common in the lower part of subsoil B<br>
or in the transitional horizon C often in<br>
areas with limestone and clay soils. Its<br>
vertical axis is larger than horizontal<br>
axis (Figure 5.9). Usually it is quite<br>
productive, formed by wetting and<br>
drying it has adequate water movement<br>
and root development.<br>
<b>Figure 5.9: Prismatic soil structure</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   276</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
277<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Columnar structure</b><br>
<b>This is a soil structure in which the soil</b><br>
particles are arranged with vertical axis<br>
much larger than horizontal rounded caps<br>
and sides of column (Figure 5.10). Its<br>
size is between 20mm and 100mm. It<br>
is commonly found in horizon B and<br>
C, form in alkaline and desert soils,<br>
associated with accumulation of sodium.<br>
<b>It is very dense and difficult for plant</b><br>
root to penetrate. It is quite productive<br>
if water is available<br>
<b>Figure 5.10: Columnar soil structure</b><br>
<b>The importance of soil structure</b><br>
<b>Soil structure influences almost all</b><br>
plants growth factors, such as water<br>
supply,<br>
aeration,<br>
availability<br>
of<br>
nutrients, microbial activities, seed<br>
germination, and root penetration.<br>
<b>Moreover, it modifies the influence</b><br>
of texture with regard to moisture<br>
and air relationship. For example, a<br>
platy structure normally hinders free<br>
drainage and root penetration because<br>
it has very fine particles (Table 5.2).<br>
A crumb structure is well aerated,<br>
drained and good for plant growth. It<br>
is an indicator of soil fertility. It is also,<br>
suitable for various activities including<br>
settlement and construction. Good<br>
soil structure facilitates the activities<br>
of microorganisms, and determines<br>
temperature and moisture of the soil.<br>

1.	 With the aid of diagrams,<br>
describe the soil structure.<br>
2.	 Explain the significance of soil<br>

4.	 With examples, explain factors<br>
affecting soil structure.<br>
<b>Soil colour</b><br>
<b>Soil colour gives an indication of unique</b><br>
characteristics and various processes<br>
going on in the soil. Also, it can suggest<br>
soil fertility and the type of existing<br>
minerals in the soils. For example, the<br>
red colour in the soil is suggests the<br>
abundance of iron oxide under oxidized<br>
conditions (well-drainage) in the soil.<br>
<b>Dark colour is generally associated</b><br>
with the accumulation of highly<br>
decayed organic matter, yellow colour<br>
is suggests the hydrated iron oxides and<br>
hydroxide, and black modules suggests<br>
the presence of manganese oxides.<br>
<b>Mottling and glorying are associated</b><br>
with poor drainage and/or high-water<br>
table. Abundant pale-yellow mottles<br>
coupled with very low pH indicate<br>
possible acid sulphate soils. Colours of<br>
the soil matrix and mottles are indicative<br>
of water and drainage conditions in the<br>
soil, hence suitability of the soil for<br>
agriculture. Low oxygen soils usually<br>
appear grey or greenish due to the<br>
presence of reduced ferrous iron oxide.<br>
<b>The presence of other minerals can</b><br>
also influence soil colour, for example,<br>
manganese oxide makes the soil black<br>
coloured; gluconate  makes the soil<br>
green; and  calcite  can make soil in<br>
arid  regions appear white. Though,<br>
soil colour does not have direct impact<br>
to farming, yet dark coloured soil is<br>
believed to be rich in organic matter,<br>
hence fertile.<br>
<b>Soil colour is described by the</b><br>
parameters called hue, value and<br>
chroma. Hue represents the dominant<br>
wave length or colour of the light<br>
(these are red, yellow, and blue). Value<br>
refers to the lightness or darkness of<br>
the colour. Chroma deals with relative<br>
purity (paleness) or strength of the<br>
colour. The colour of the soil in terms<br>
of the above parameters could be<br>
quickly determined by comparison of<br>
the sample with a standard set of colour<br>
chips mounted in a note-book called<br>
<b>Munsell Soil Colour Charts (MSCC)</b><br>
(Figure 5.11). In these charts, the right-<br>
hand top corner represents the hue,<br>
the vertical axis represents the value,<br>
and the horizontal axis represent the<br>
chroma.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   278</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
279<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Importance of soil colour</b><br>
<b>The soil colour can give information</b><br>
on its uses. For example, the darker the<br>
soil, the higher the relative productivity<br>
often contributed by its richness in<br>
organic matter content. Generally, dark<br>
colour indicates the relative amount of<br>
organic matter present in the soil. In<br>
contrast, light colour is an indication<br>
of non-fertile soils. In young soils, the<br>
light colour is an indicator of parent<br>
rock materials while in mature soils<br>
it is an indication of climate in which<br>
the soil had developed. Warm climates<br>
generally results to the formation of red<br>
coloured soil. Light colour results from<br>
leaching of iron minerals. Mottling or<br>
patches (spotty colours) is the result<br>
of intermittent reduction and oxidation<br>
reaction, thus solution and removal of<br>
some soil components. Mottled colours<br>
indicate temporary excess of water and<br>
lack of aeration.<br>

on their mineral content and<br>
environment. Explain.<br>

influence the soil colour.<br>
3.	 Soil organic matter are not the<br>
only colouring pigments in the<br>
soil. Discuss.<br>

<b>Soil temperature</b><br>
<b>Soil temperature is the warmth or</b><br>
coldness of the soil as a result of<br>
incoming solar radiation being absorbed,<br>
diffused, reflected or scattered by the<br>
earth’s surface. There are several<br>
factors which affect soil temperature<br>
such as climate, slope of the land and<br>
vegetation. Soil temperature vary from<br>
one place to another due to variation in<br>
the climatic condition. In polar latitude<br>
for example, the soil is cold because of<br>
the small amount of insolation received.<br>
<b>In tropical areas soils are warm due to</b><br>
high intensity of insolation reaching the<br>
surface. A warm moist soil will have<br>
greater biota activity, giving a more<br>
rapid break down of organic matter on<br>
one hand, and on the other favour the<br>
chemical weathering of the parent rock<br>
materials. The warm moist soil will not<br>
only contribute to the availability of<br>
soil nutrients but also will boost rapid<br>
seeds germination as compared to cold<br>
and dry soil. The ideal soil temperature<br>
that support the growth of most of the<br>
plants is between 18 ºC and 24 ºC (65<br>
ºF-75 ºF). Soil temperature depends<br>
on soil texture. Sandy soils warm up<br>
quicker than clay or fine-grained soils.<br>
<b>Fine-grained soils carry a large amount</b><br>
of water; therefore, warm slowly. Soil<br>
moisture is the most vital controlling<br>
factor in soil temperature. Soil structure<br>
affects soil temperature by controlling<br>
the pore spaces. Soils with well drained<br>
structures (granular or crumb) warm up<br>
rapidly because of limited water-logging.<br>
<b>Besides this, it should be noted that the</b><br>
soil is composed partly of mineral matter<br>
and partly organic matter. The specific<br>
heat of mineral substances is less than<br>
that of organic materials. Specific heat<br>
of iron is 0.11, whereas of humus is<br>
0.44. Hence, mineral soils get heated<br>
more readily than organic soils. Also,<br>
soil temperature depends on soil colour.<br>
<b>Dark-coloured soils usually warm up</b><br>
more easily than light-coloured soils.<br>
<b>This is because they possess a greater</b><br>
capacity for absorbing the sun’s heat.<br>
<b>Also, soil temperature depends on</b><br>
soil moisture; temperature of water is<br>
higher than temperature of soil. Moist<br>
soils get heated more slowly and have<br>
higher specific heat capacity than<br>
dry soils. Consequently, they have<br>
lower temperature than dry soil. The<br>
evaporation of water rather helps to lower<br>
the temperature of a moist soil. Other<br>
factors that determine soil temperature<br>
include slope of the land (topography)<br>
and vegetation cover. Soil temperature<br>
decreases with increase in elevation. In<br>
terms of aspect, north-facing slopes may<br>
differ from adjoining south-facing slope<br>
because they receive different amount<br>
of incoming solar radiation. Soils that<br>
are covered with vegetation absorb less<br>
heat than those of bare soil. Vegetation<br>
acts as an interceptor and retards the<br>
warming of the soil surface. On the other<br>
hand, plant cover prevents the soil from<br>
getting cool as it retards the loss of heat.<br>
<b>Hence, soils carrying a dense plant cover</b><br>
are cooler in summer and warmer in<br>
winter than unprotected soils. Bare soils<br>
cool off rapidly than those covered with<br>
vegetation. Temperature determines the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   280</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
281<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
rate of decomposition of organic matter<br>
and consequently soil fertility. Also it<br>
influences seed germination, chemical<br>
reactions and life of microorganisms.<br>
<b>Soil porosity</b><br>
<b>Soil porosity refers to the amount of</b><br>
pore spaces present in the soil. It is<br>
abbreviated as ‘P’ referring to the ratio<br>
of the volume of voids (Vv) to the total<br>
volume of soils (Vt). Pores or pore<br>
spaces form when plant roots, insects<br>
and earth worms move through the soil.<br>
<b>A more porous soil allows much water</b><br>
and air circulations in large quantity than<br>
those with low porosity. The porosity of<br>
soil depends on several factors including,<br>
packing density, the breadth of the size<br>
distribution, cementing of the structure<br>
and the shape of particles. For example,<br>
clay and sandy soils have high porosity<br>
because they have angular grains of sandy<br>
which are loosely packed, while the clay<br>
soils have very small grain and numerous<br>
pore spaces (Figure 5.12). As porosity<br>
increases, water retention decreases<br>
and drainage increases, accordingly.<br>
<b>The formula below illustrates how soil</b><br>
porosity is calculated.<br>
<b>Porosity (P)  =  volume of voids (Vv)</b><br>
total volume of soil (Vt)<br>
<b>Soil porosity is usually expressed as a</b><br>
percentage or decimal fraction. It is a<br>
measure of soil capacity to hold water<br>
and air.<br>
<b>Sand</b><br>
<b>Silt</b><br>
<b>Clay</b><br>
<b>Porosity</b><br>
<b>Grains</b><br>
<b>Combination of</b><br>
sand, silt and clay<br>
<b>Water Retention = WR</b><br>
<b>Drainage = D</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.12:  Soil porosity of various soil grains</b><br>
Soil porosity determines the permeability of soil. Soil permeability is the measure<br>
of how easily water flows through soil. Soil permeability is defined as the proper­<br>
ty of a porous soil to transmit water or to allow seepage of water and air through<br>
its interconnecting voids. A soil having continuous voids is called permeable.<br>
Gravels are highly permeable while stiff clay is the least permeable, and hence<br>

such a clay may be termed imperme­<br>
able in practical purposes. It depends<br>
on the size of the pore spaces and how<br>
well are connected to one another.<br>

<b>Soil density</b><br>
<b>The relation between mass and volume</b><br>
of a dry soil is referred to as soil density.<br>
<b>The volume is determined with the use</b><br>
of diameter and height value of the soil<br>
sample. Soil density is expressed in two<br>
accepted concepts which are particle<br>
density (absolute specific gravity) and<br>
bulk density (apparent specific gravity).<br>
<b>Particle density or bulky density is</b><br>
commonly expressed in g/cm3 or g/<br>
cc but it can also be expressed as g/<br>
m3 or mg/cm3. The particle density is<br>
the weight per unit volume of solid<br>
portion of soil (accompanied by water<br>
and air). Generally, the particle density<br>
of normal soils is 2.65-2.75 grams per<br>
cubic centimetre (2.65-2.75 g/cm3). The<br>
particle density is higher if large amounts<br>
of heavy minerals such as magnetite,<br>
limonite, and haematite are present in<br>
the soil. But when soil is rich in organic<br>
matter, the particle density decreases.<br>
<b>The particle density of organic soil range</b><br>
from 1.2-1.7 gm/cm3.  Also, the particle<br>
density is termed as true density. Table<br>
<b>5.3 shows the particle density of different</b><br>
soil textural class. Particle density is<br>
expressed by the following formula;<br>
<b>P. D  =  MS</b><br>
<b>VS</b><br>
Where;<br>
<b>P.D = Particle density</b><br>
<b>MS = Mass of solid portion of soil</b><br>
<b>VS = Volume of solid portion of soil</b><br>
<b>Table 5.3: Particle density of different</b><br>
soil textural classes<br>

<b>Bulky density is the weight per unit</b><br>
volume of dry soil, inclusive of pore<br>
spaces. The bulk density of mineral<br>
soil ranges from 1.0-1.8 g/cm3 while<br>
the bulk density of organic soil is about<br>
<b>0.5 g/cm3. The bulk density of the soil</b><br>
is always smaller than its particles<br>
density and normally decreases as the<br>
minerals become finer in texture. The<br>
bulk density decreases with increase in<br>
total space in the soil and it is a good<br>
estimate of the porosity of the soil (Table<br>
5.4). Bulk density is of more importance<br>
than particle density in understanding<br>

<b>Chemical properties of the soil</b><br>
<b>These are properties relating to the</b><br>
chemical processes taking place in the<br>
soil. The chemical reactions taking<br>
place between solid and liquid phase<br>
in soil control soil chemical properties.<br>
<b>The chemical properties affect the</b><br>
relationship between soil and plants.<br>
<b>They can make nutrients available for</b><br>
plants and soil organisms. The bulk of a<br>
chemical and mineralogical composition<br>
of the soil is derived from rocks resulting<br>
from various chemical processes. The<br>
important chemical processes in the soil<br>
include soil colloids, anion exchange,<br>
cation exchange capacity, and soil pH.<br>
<b>Thus, soil chemical and mineralogical</b><br>
composition is related to that of the<br>
rock’s origin.<br>
<b>Soil colloids</b><br>
<b>Soils possess charged tiny particles called</b><br>
colloids. A colloid is a substance which,<br>
when apparently dissolved, cannot pass<br>
through a membrane since it is smaller<br>
than 0.1 micrometer in diameter. A<br>
colloidal system consists of dispersed<br>
medium that is the phase forming<br>
particles and the dispersion medium that<br>
is the medium with which particles are<br>
distributed. The colloid particles in the<br>
soil are the determinants of chemical<br>
activities in the particular soil. There are<br>
two types of colloids, which are organic<br>
and inorganic or mineral colloids.<br>
<b>Organic colloids are humus particles</b><br>
which make an appreciable portion of<br>
soil colloids. Inorganic colloids are clay<br>
minerals or separates of less than one<br>
micron or hydrous oxides of iron and<br>
aluminium. Colloidal particles, being<br>
very small in size, have large surface<br>
areas for chemical reaction and water<br>
retention. They possess charges which<br>
are either net positive or negative, and<br>
thus they have capacity to hold and<br>
exchange ions on their surface. Both<br>
organic and inorganic colloids are<br>
generated as a result of decomposition<br>
of organic and inorganic matter.<br>
<b>Ion exchange</b><br>
<b>Ion exchange is a reversible process</b><br>
through which ions interchange between<br>
solid and liquid phases. Ion exchange<br>
may take place between solid particles<br>
lying adjacent to liquid. Therefore,<br>
ion exchange is defined as a simple<br>
reversible process by which ions are<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   283</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
284<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
exchanged between a solid and a liquid<br>
phase. When the cations (positively<br>
charged) are involved, the process is<br>
called cation exchange, but when the<br>
anion (negatively charged) are involved,<br>
the process is called anion exchange,<br>
though the latter is less common.<br>
<b>Cation exchange</b><br>
<b>This refers to the interchange between</b><br>
cations in solution and another cation<br>
on the surface of any negatively charged<br>
material such as clay colloid or organic<br>
colloid. Negatively charged soil colloids<br>
have the tendency of adsorbing positively<br>
charged ions released in the soil solution<br>
from weathering of the parent rock<br>
materials, the charge of each depends<br>
on the valency of individual element.<br>
<b>The commonly exchanged cations in</b><br>
the soil include Mg2+, K+, NH4<br>
+, Ca2+,<br>
<b>Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+. Cations are</b><br>
absorbed by plant roots and move along<br>
the concentration gradient. The excess<br>
uptake of cations by plant roots releases<br>
hydrogen ions into the soil solution, in<br>
order to maintain electric neutrality<br>
between the roots and soil solution. The<br>
release of hydrogen ions into the soil<br>
solution increases acidic condition of<br>
the soil solution, which in turn promotes<br>
weathering of parent rock materials to<br>
replace the up taken cations by plants.<br>
<b>The energy of absorption of the specific</b><br>
cation is normally related to valence<br>
and degree of hydration. Therefore,<br>
monovalent cations with a large hydrated<br>
radius like sodium Na+ is weakly<br>
absorbed making it more freely available<br>
ion in the soil solution which accounts<br>
for its vulnerability to leaching. On the<br>
other hand, divalent cations with small<br>
hydrated radius like calcium Ca2+ in<br>
particular are strongly absorbed, and<br>
this defines their resistance to leaching<br>
and therefore forming a calcified soil in<br>
semi-arid climate. Generally, absorption<br>
sequence decreases rightwards thus,<br>
<b>Ca2+>Mg2+>K+>Na+.  Hence, the</b><br>
combination of colloids and attached<br>
cations is called the colloidal complex,<br>
and it is a delicate mechanism. If it<br>
holds the nutrients strongly, they will<br>
not be leached away, yet if the bond is<br>
too strong they cannot be absorbed by<br>
plants. Thus, a fertile soil is likely to be<br>
one in which the cation-colloid attraction<br>
is in a state of balance  (Figure 5.13).<br>
<b>Root hair</b><br>
<b>Soil particle</b><br>
<b>K+</b><br>
–<br>
– –<br>
– –<br>
<b>K+</b><br>
<b>Ca2+</b><br>
<b>Mg2+</b><br>

<b>H+</b><br>
<b>H+</b><br>
<b>Figure 5. 13: Cation exchange</b><br>
<b>Advantages of cation exchange</b><br>
<b>Cation exchange helps plants to absorb</b><br>
both macro and micro nutrients from<br>
the soil for their growth and general life<br>
processes. It results into the formation<br>
of acidic environments in soil solution<br>
due to replacement of metallic cations<br>
<b>Mg2+, K+, NH4</b><br>
+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   284</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
285<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
and Cu2+ by acidic cations particularly<br>
of hydrogen ions H+ which tends to<br>
promote weathering of the parent rock<br>
materials. An acidic condition resulting<br>
from cation exchange on the other hand<br>
creates a suitable medium of growth<br>
for acid loving plants. Cation exchange<br>
depends on the number of factors such as<br>
amount of organic matter which provides<br>
organic colloids with large quantity of<br>
negative charges. Also, it depends on the<br>
types of clay minerals present in the soil.<br>
<b>For instance, soils with large quantity of</b><br>
smectite clay have high cation exchange<br>
capacity than those with kaolinite clay<br>
minerals. In addition, it depends on the<br>
soil reaction pH, as capacity of absorbing<br>
cations increase with the increase of<br>
basicity and vice versa.<br>
<b>Cation exchange capacity</b><br>
Since colloidal particles are charged,<br>
the negatively charged colloids are able<br>
to hold various positively charged ions<br>
known as cations, such as Calcium<br>
(Ca2+), Magnesium (Mg2+), Sodium<br>
(Na+), Hydrogen (H+), and Aluminium<br>
(Al3+). The total amount of cations held<br>
in the soil are   given by an expression<br>
known as cation exchange capacity. The<br>
cation exchange capacity (CEC) is a<br>
measure of the ability of a soil to retain<br>
cations for plant use.  Soils with a low<br>
<b>CEC, such as sands, are less capable of</b><br>
keeping essential plant nutrients than<br>
those with a high CEC, like clays and<br>
humus; consequently, they are less fertile.<br>
<b>Also, soils with high organic matter have</b><br>
high cation exchange capacity.<br>
<b>Anion exchange</b><br>
<b>This is an exchange of anions between</b><br>
soil solution and soil colloids or<br>
between soil colloids and plant roots.<br>
<b>During the exchange process, anions</b><br>
absorbed by a positively charged<br>
colloids and the hydroxyl groups<br>
from plant roots tend to replace each<br>
other causing anion to move along the<br>
concentration gradients to the roots,<br>
and the hydroxyl groups OH- migrate<br>
to the soil solution. The commonly<br>
involved anions include Cl-, NO-<br>
3,<br>
<b>SO4</b><br>
<b>2-, PO4</b><br>
2-. The excessive uptaking<br>
of the anions release hydroxyl groups<br>
OH- that maintain electric neutrality.<br>
<b>Like the cation exchange, the process</b><br>
enables plants to obtain nutrients. It<br>
creates alkaline medium in the soil<br>
solution due to the released basic<br>
anions. The release of hydroxyl<br>
groups into the soil solution creates<br>
the alkalinity condition which may<br>
favour some plants that grow in acidic<br>
condition and determines the type of<br>
fertilizers to be added. Anion exchange<br>
is the soil reaction dependant, amount<br>
of organic matter and type of clay<br>
minerals present in the soil. Anion<br>
exchange capacity is the sum of all<br>
negative charges of all absorbed<br>
anions i.e. Cl-, NO-<br>
<b>3 , SO2</b><br>
4<br>
<b>- PO2</b><br>
4<br>
- each<br>
of them contributes a negative charge<br>
equivalent to its valence. Like in<br>
cation exchange absorption of anions<br>
depends on the valence and degree of<br>
hydration. PO4<br>

<b>Soil pH</b><br>
<b>Soil pH is a chemical soil property</b><br>
which expresses the degree of acidity<br>
and alkalinity in the soil. Also, soil pH<br>
can be defined as the logarithm of the<br>
reciprocal or the negative logarithm<br>
of the hydrogen (H+) ion activity or<br>
concentration in the soil.<br>
<b>It is a measure of acidity or alkalinity of</b><br>
the soil. It is a measure of hydrogen ion<br>
concentration in an aqueous solution<br>
and it is measured by a pH scale that<br>
ranges in values from 1 to 14 (acidic to<br>
basic). However, practically the ideal<br>
soil pH ranges from 4.5 to 6.8 and<br>
<b>7.0 to 9.5 as pH values beyond those</b><br>
extremes are toxic to life forms. A soil<br>
pH of 7 is termed as neutral, above 7 pH<br>
are alkaline and below 7 pH are acidic<br>
(Figure 5.15). Soil pH depends on a<br>
number of factors including climate.<br>
<b>Heavy rainfall removes basic cations</b><br>
especially Calcium (Ca2+), Magnesium<br>
(Mg2+), and Sodium (Na+), through<br>
leaching.<br>
<b>Heavy</b><br>
rainfall<br>
replaces<br>
them by acidic cations especially of<br>
<b>Hydrogen ions (H+) and Aluminium</b><br>
ions (Al3+) resulting into formation of<br>
acidic soils. In regions with little amount<br>
of rainfall, particularly semi-deserts,<br>
alkaline soils are likely to be formed<br>
due to salinization and calcification<br>
leading to the formation of saline soils<br>
especially at the topsoil. Also, parent<br>
rock materials determine soil pH,<br>
whereby acidic soils result from the<br>
acidic parent rock materials particularly<br>
granite rocks as opposed to limestone<br>
rocks which forms alkaline soils. Other<br>
factors include the decomposition of<br>
organic matter, acidic rain, the use of<br>
industrial fertilizers such as ammonia<br>
and ammonium as well as urea. Also,<br>
respiration of soil organisms may alter<br>
soil and the exchange of ions.<br>
<b>Alkalinity</b><br>
<b>Acidity</b><br>
<b>Increasing acidity</b><br>
0<br>
1<br>
2<br>

<b>Neutral</b><br>
<b>Increasing alkalinity</b><br>

Soil pH is measured in a suitable soil water suspension with a pH meter. Since<br>
soil pH influences the amount of exchangeable cations, the soil reaction can be<br>
deduced from the amount of cation exchange capacity present in the soil. When<br>
the percentage base saturation is above 50%, the soil is said to be alkaline while<br>
below 50% the soil is acidic.<br>
Levels of soil pH required to grow specific crops in the soil varies among crop<br>
variety. The ideal soil pH for most of the plants ranges from 6-7.5, though can<br>
tolerate to pH ranging from 5.5-8.5. The tolerance of plants and animals to extreme<br>
soil pH varies with varying plant and animal species (Table 5.5). Some of the crops<br>
grow well in relatively high acidic soil while others grow well in alkaline soil.<br>
Basically, the most favourable soil for crop productivity is neutral soil.<br>
<b>Table 5.5:  Crop species and their various soil pH tolerance</b><br>
pH<br>
<b>Description</b><br>
<b>Crop type which can grow</b><br>
<b>Below 4.8</b><br>
<b>Extremely Acid</b><br>
<b>No normal crop grown</b><br>
<b>4.8 to 5.2</b><br>
<b>Strongly Acid</b><br>
<b>Oat, potatoes, Kale, rye, field lupin</b><br>
<b>5.3 to 5.7</b><br>
<b>Moderately Acid</b><br>
Those above, plus wheat, beans, swede,<br>
turnips, maize, cabbage<br>
<b>5.8 to 6.4</b><br>
<b>Slightly Acid</b><br>
<b>All normal crops</b><br>
<b>6.5 to 6.9</b><br>
<b>Very Slightly Acid</b><br>
<b>All normal crops</b><br>
<b>Above 6.9</b><br>
<b>No Acidity</b><br>
<b>All normal crops</b><br>
<b>It is possible to manipulate soil acidity</b><br>
to suit particular crops. Some of the<br>
ways to modify soil acidity include:<br>
adding plenty of decomposed organic<br>
matter (compost) during planting of<br>
crops. The added organic matter in<br>
the soil during crop sowing increases<br>
the soil acidity gradually to a suitable<br>
concentration<br>
for<br>
plant<br>
growth.<br>
<b>Meanwhile, adding aluminium sulphate</b><br>
and sulphur will change soil instantly<br>
as aluminium increases soil acidity as<br>
soon as it dissolves in the soil, while<br>
adding lime in the soil raises the soil<br>
pH level of a particular soil.<br>
<b>Importance of soil pH</b><br>
<b>It helps to determine the suitability of a</b><br>
given soil as a medium for plant growth<br>
and microbial activities. The suitable<br>
ranges for most crops are slightly acidic<br>
to slightly alkaline. Acidic condition is<br>
desireable in preventing diseases to some<br>
plants such as, cotton mosaic. Soil pH<br>
affects the availability of nutrients such as<br>
nitrogen, calcium, phosphorus, potassium<br>
and magnesium. These nutrients<br>
are mostly available at pH 6.5 – 7.5.<br>
<b>Acidic conditions increase availability of</b><br>
<b>Iron, Copper and Zinc, but they tend to</b><br>
decrease the availability of molybdenum,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   287</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
288<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
which is available at pH ranging from<br>
0-6. Organic matter decomposition is<br>
slowed down under extreme acidic or<br>
alkaline conditions due to slow microbial<br>
activities. Soil pH being an indicator of<br>
the degree of acidity or alkalinity, give<br>
an estimate of the modifications needed<br>
(for acidity cation or liming) to achieve<br>
the favourable soil conditions for plant<br>
growth and microbial activities.<br>

or farm can be maintained.<br>

soil pH.<br>
<b>Soil profile</b><br>
<b>Soil profile is the vertical section of a</b><br>
soil showing the succession of horizons<br>
or layers and the underlying parent rock<br>
materials. The different soil colour and<br>
texture help in dividing the soil profile<br>
into layers or horizons. There are four<br>
major soil horizons, which may further<br>
be divided into smaller horizons, which<br>
are shown by O or A, B, C and D letters<br>
to indicate their genetic origin. However,<br>
<b>O-horizon is not common in all soil</b><br>
types, due to absence of accumulation<br>
of organic matter in some of the soils.<br>
<b>The variations within the horizons are</b><br>
indicated by subscripts, like A1 and<br>
<b>A2, or, B1, B2, and B3. Organic-matter</b><br>
deposits on top of the mineral soil are<br>
often labeled as Ao, F, H (Figure 5.16).<br>
<b>A soil profile helps to determine the best</b><br>
soil suitable for growing a certain type<br>
of crops and identifies the part where<br>
plant nutrients can be found. Note that<br>
the top soil is the richest part of the soil<br>
in nutrients. It helps to determine water<br>
holding capacity, nature of the parent<br>
rock materials and the type of mineral<br>
salts available to plants. There are a<br>
number of processes that are involved<br>
in the development of a soil profile such<br>
as decomposition of organic matter,<br>
weathering of the parent rocks, leaching,<br>
among others.<br>
<b>Horizons</b><br>
<b>Regolith</b><br>
<b>Non-soil</b><br>
<b>Soil</b><br>
<b>O</b><br>
<b>A</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>
<b>C</b><br>
<b>D or R</b><br>
<b>Figure 5. 16: Cross-section of soil profile</b><br>
<b>A - Horizon</b><br>
<b>This is the upper layer where biological</b><br>
activities take place, and is rich in humus.<br>
<b>Organic matter forms Ao (surface</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   288</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
289<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
horizon) at this zone.  It forms organic<br>
matter both fresh popularly known as “L”<br>
and “F” and decaying, makes up most<br>
of the volume. The A-horizon results<br>
essentially from litter derived from dead<br>
plants and animals. The A-horizon is<br>
most affected by the leaching of soluble<br>
materials and by downward movement<br>
of clay particles. The washing out of<br>
materials, that is, the removal of organic<br>
and mineral matter from the A-horizon<br>
is known as eluviation. The A-horizon<br>
is therefore termed as the zone of<br>
eluviation. The upper part of this horizon<br>
is dark coloured due to down–washed<br>
humus from Ao. On the other hand, the<br>
lower part of the horizon has lighter<br>
colour due to removal of clay and iron.<br>
<b>A1 forms the top soil, it is a mineral</b><br>
layer that contains enough partially<br>
decomposed organic matter to give<br>
soil dark colour. A2 is normally light<br>
in colour than O and A1 sublayers. This<br>
layer marks the transitional layer from<br>
O-horizon, A-horizon to B-horizon.<br>
<b>B – Horizon</b><br>
This is the zone beneath the A-Horizon.<br>
It is known as the zone of accumulation,<br>
or illuviation, where clays and other<br>
materials that are removed from the<br>
<b>A-horizon are re-deposited. Illuviation</b><br>
is the process of in-washing- the re-<br>
deposition of organic and mineral matter<br>
in the B–horizon. It is brighter in colour<br>
due to deposition of iron and aluminium.<br>
<b>The A and B horizons together make</b><br>
up the true soil or solum. Horizon<br>
<b>B is divided into B1., B2 and B3. It is</b><br>
popularly known to form subsoils. The<br>
layer is a collection zone of clay, Iron<br>
and Aluminium. B1 is usually a layer<br>
of great, heavier texture, great density<br>
and relatively great clay content than A.<br>
<b>B2 is a darker layer in colour. It is the</b><br>
layer of accumulation of colloids. B3 is<br>
a transitional layer from B-horizon to<br>
C-horizon.<br>
<b>C – Horizon</b><br>
<b>This zone consists mainly of semi</b><br>
weathered parent rock materials (in-situ)<br>
resting on the bedrock. The layer consists<br>
of unconsolidated and semi weathered<br>
parent rock materials, mostly altered by<br>
weathering-subsoils. It lacks the content<br>
of organic matter. The horizons O, A, B<br>
and C form regolith.<br>
<b>D – Horizon</b><br>
<b>Beneath the C-horizon is the D or R –</b><br>
horizon which consists of unaltered or<br>
un weathered bedrock.<br>

<b>Soil formation</b><br>
<b>The transformation of rocks into soil</b><br>
is designated as soil formation. Soil<br>
formation is the genesis of the soil<br>
from parent rock materials (rocks),<br>
adopting both its physical and chemical<br>
characteristics. Soil formation or<br>
pedogenesis is principally initiated<br>
by weathering of the parent rock. Soil<br>
formation is the accumulation of a<br>
layer of loose, broken, unconsolidated<br>
parent rock materials known as regolith.<br>
<b>The Regolith not only originates from</b><br>
weathering but may also be derived from<br>
the deposition of alluvium, drift, loess<br>
and volcanic materials. There are five<br>
main factors that affect the process of soil<br>
formation. These factors includes parent<br>
rock materials, topography and age of the<br>
land (time), climate and organisms. Since<br>
these factors are closely interconnected<br>
and interdependent, their relationship<br>
may be summarized as follows;<br>
<b>Soil = f (parent rock material + climate</b><br>
+ topography + organisms +<br>
time)<br>
<b>Where:</b><br>
f = function of<br>
<b>Parent rock materials</b><br>
<b>The mineral material from which soil</b><br>
forms is called parent rock material or<br>
soil materials. Also, the parent rock<br>
known as geological factor, is a source<br>
of rock fragments which make up soil.<br>
<b>The parent rock materials are chemically</b><br>
and physically weathered, transported,<br>
deposited and precipitated, at the end is<br>
transformed into soil. A rock, whether<br>
igneous, sedimentary or metamorphic,<br>
is the source of all soil mineral materials<br>
and the origin of all plant nutrients with<br>
the exceptions of nitrogen, hydrogen and<br>
carbon. There is greater relationship<br>
between soil formed with parent rock<br>
materials for example, soils formed from<br>
mafic rocks are usually more fertile, with<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   290</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
291<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
higher contents of calcium, magnesium,<br>
and phosphorus than soils formed from<br>
felsic rocks which contain more clay<br>
and have a more reddish-brown colour.<br>
<b>Quartzite, a metamorphic rock of pure</b><br>
quartz (SiO2) rock, is essentially infertile<br>
and resistant to soil formation, and most<br>
soils formed are shallow. Granite gneiss<br>
rocks average about 25% quartz, 65%<br>
feldspar (dominantly alkali feldspar),<br>
with lesser amounts of mica (muscovite<br>
and biotite), and small amounts of<br>
hornblende. Weathering patterns follow<br>
rock fabric, from isotropic in granite<br>
to banded in gneiss, but resulting<br>
soils are very similar. In well leached<br>
environments, the soils formed are acid,<br>
have low contents of base cations and<br>
low mineral nutrient reserves. Kaolinite<br>
and hydroxy interlayered minerals (HIM)<br>
are abundant clay minerals formed in<br>
surface and subsoil horizons.<br>
<b>Sandstones (quartzose sandstones</b><br>
and orthoquartzites) contain more<br>
than 50  percent sand-size particles<br>
predominantly of quartz, the rest being<br>
impurities such as feldspar and mica,<br>
and cementing material usually silica,<br>
iron, or carbonates. In well-leached<br>
environments, most of the soils derived<br>
from such rocks are coarse-textured and<br>
deep. Limestone and dolomites are by<br>
definition composed of more than 50<br>
percent carbonates, the remainder being<br>
‘contaminants’ deposited on the sea<br>
bottom, including silt, clay, and particles<br>
of quartz and iron glacial deposits.<br>
<b>Glacial deposits are unique among the</b><br>
many parent rock materials. They are<br>
the only parent rock materials which are<br>
physically pulverized into small particles<br>
and then abruptly deposited on the land<br>
surface in the zone of soil formation.<br>
These previously unweathered materials,<br>
except where sedimentary rocks have<br>
been incorporated, become in situ sites<br>
of mineralogical alteration and release<br>
of ions in soils formed as the glaciers<br>
retreated. As parent rock materials, the<br>
physically pulverized and previously<br>
unweathered materials differ from the<br>
weathered saprolite materials formed<br>
over crystalline bedrock.<br>
<b>Lacustrine deposits consist of thin</b><br>
alternating layers, usually of silt and clay-<br>
sized particles. Soils formed in lacustrine<br>
deposits are often quite impermeable in<br>
the saturated conditions. They often have<br>
acquired and preserved thick organic<br>
matter residues, thus forming organic<br>
soils called histosols.<br>
<b>The way in which volcanic ash material</b><br>
enters soil as periodic deposits on the<br>
surface of existing soil is unique. This<br>
combination of periodic additions of<br>
rapidly weathering minerals to the soil<br>
surface has given rise to fertile soils. In<br>
areas with favorable temperature and<br>
moisture conditions support the growth<br>
of food crops resulting in high human<br>
population densities. The good examples<br>
are in Java, Japan, Central America, and<br>
the rift valley in East Africa, specifically<br>
Rungwe and Kilimanjaro Mountains.<br>
<b>Most of the soils formed from volcanic</b><br>
ash are now classified as Andisols.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   291</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Climate</b><br>
<b>Climate is the dominant factor as it</b><br>
operates through elements such as<br>
temperature, humidity, precipitation,<br>
wind, and clouds in soil formation, and<br>
shows the distinctive characteristic of the<br>
climate zones in which they form. The<br>
principal climatic variables influencing<br>
soil formation are precipitation and<br>
temperature, which affect the rates<br>
of chemical, physical and biological<br>
processes. The temperature and moisture<br>
both influence the organic matter<br>
content of soil through their effects<br>
on the balance between plant growth<br>
and microbial decomposition. Water is<br>
essential for the entire major chemical<br>
weathering reactions, thus the greater the<br>
depth of water penetration, the greater<br>
the depth of weathering of the soil and its<br>
development. Excess water percolating<br>
through the soil profile transports soluble<br>
and suspended materials from the upper<br>
to the lower layers, the process known<br>
as leaching. Moreover, in desert, semi<br>
desert and tropics, temperature variation<br>
has a significant role of disintegrating<br>
rocks into screes and even very fine<br>
particles which are eventually transported<br>
by the influence of gravity or wind<br>
action. Therefore, climate influences<br>
directly and indirectly the soil formation<br>
process.<br>
<b>Topography</b><br>
<b>Topography or relief is characterized</b><br>
by inclination (slope), elevation, and<br>
orientation of the terrain (Figure 5.17).<br>
<b>Topography influences the rate of</b><br>
precipitation, runoff erosion and the rate<br>
of formation of the surface soil profile.<br>
<b>Steep slopes encourage rapid soil loss by</b><br>
erosion and mass wasting on one hand,<br>
and on the other allow less rainwater to<br>
enter the soil before changing into runoff,<br>
hence little mineral deposition in lower<br>
profiles. Therefore, soils on steep slope<br>
are likely to be thin, poorly developed<br>
and relatively dry. The gentle slope, slows<br>
the lateral movement of water as surface<br>
runoff instead increase the likelihood<br>
of water logging and formation of peat.<br>
<b>In gentle slope there is low rate of soil</b><br>
erosion but the high rate of deposition,<br>
due to the receipt of material moved<br>
downward slope tend to produce deep<br>
soils. In semi-arid regions, low rainfall<br>
on steep slopes results in incomplete<br>
vegetation cover. Therefore, in such<br>
areas, there is little plant contribution<br>
to soil formation.<br>
<b>Shedding</b><br>
zone<br>
<b>Soil</b><br>
depth<br>
<b>Surface run-off</b><br>
<b>Through flow</b><br>
<b>Transfer zone</b><br>
<b>Receiving zone</b><br>
<b>Figure. 5.17 Topography soil formation</b><br>
<b>Living organisms</b><br>
<b>Living organisms or biota as a factor of</b><br>
soil formation includes both plants and<br>
animals ranging from macro- organisms<br>
to micro-organisms. The biological factor<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   292</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
293<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
in particular gives life to the soil, when<br>
plants die, leaves fall onto the soil surface<br>
where microorganisms can act upon<br>
and decay plant tissues. The   organic<br>
matter   is used as an energy source<br>
for microorganisms, increasing their<br>
population in the soil. These organisms<br>
utilize easily digestible materials like<br>
simple sugars and carbohydrates found in<br>
the plant material, leaving more resistant<br>
materials such as fats and waxes behind.<br>
<b>The material left behind is not easily</b><br>
decomposed; it comprises the humus<br>
found in soil.  Humus acts as a gluing<br>
agent, essentially holding primary soil<br>
particles (sand, silt and clay) together<br>
to form secondary aggregates or peds.<br>
<b>The organisms and the humus help to</b><br>
create aid in the soil development and<br>
the formation of soil horizons.<br>
<b>Human activities widely influence soil</b><br>
formation through addition of organic<br>
manure, liming and tilling by using<br>
tractors, draining or irrigation, addition<br>
of fertilizers and the deliberately control<br>
of soil erosion. These activities help to<br>
improve soil stability and development.<br>
Large animals such as gophers, moles,<br>
and prairie dogs bore into the lower<br>
soil horizons, bringing materials to the<br>
surface. Their tunnels are open to the<br>
surface, encouraging the movement<br>
of water and air into the subsurface<br>
layers. In their localized areas, they<br>
enhance mixing of the lower and upper<br>
horizons by creating and later refilling<br>
underground tunnels.<br>
<b>Time</b><br>
<b>Time is a factor in the interaction of</b><br>
other soil forming factors. With time,<br>
soil evolves features that depend on the<br>
interplay of the soil forming factors<br>
listed earlier. It takes several millions of<br>
years for soil to develop a profile. Such<br>
period depends strongly on climate,<br>
parent rock materials, relief and biotic<br>
activity. Over time, the soil develops a<br>
profile that depends on the functions of<br>
biota and climate.  Soil forming factors<br>
continue to affect soils during their<br>
existence, even on stable landscapes<br>
that are long-enduring, for millions of<br>
years. Materials are deposited on top or<br>
are blown or washed from the surface.<br>
Soil usually take a long time to form,<br>
perhaps up to 400 years for 10 mm and<br>
under extreme conditions up to 1000<br>
years for 1 mm. It can take 3000 to<br>
12 000 years to produce a sufficient<br>
depth of mature soil for farming. The<br>
estimation of relative age or degree of<br>
maturity of soils is universally based<br>
on horizon differentiation. In practice,<br>
it is generally maintained that the larger<br>
the number of horizons and the greater<br>
their thickness and intensity the more<br>
mature is the soil. Soil forming process<br>
is a generally very slow and long period<br>
of time is required for a thin layer<br>
of soil to form on a newly exposed<br>
surface. Warm moist environment is<br>
conducive to soil development than<br>
cold and dry environment. For instance,<br>
soil develops relatively quickly from<br>
sediments as compared to that develop<br>
relatively slow on bedrocks.<br>

<b>Activity 5.3</b><br>
In a group of five students;<br>
(a)	 Read books and internet<br>
sources about factors affecting<br>
soil formation,<br>
(b)	 Discuss the findings you have<br>
collected from the sources<br>
you read,<br>
(c)	 Summarize the read materials<br>
and present the findings to the<br>
class.<br>
(d)	 Incorporate all the comments.<br>
<b>Processes of soil formation</b><br>
<b>The process of soil formation is also</b><br>
known as pedogenesis. Numerous<br>
processes are involved in the formation<br>
of soil and eventually, the creation of<br>
the soil profiles, structure, and texture.<br>
<b>There are four basic processes occurring</b><br>
in soil formation namely addition,<br>
losing, translocation and transformation.<br>
<b>The addition process involves the</b><br>
accumulation of materials either from<br>
deposition or rising from below with<br>
raising ground such as addition of leaves.<br>
<b>Losses occur when the materials are</b><br>
moved from the soil, for instance, when<br>
materials are removed due to soil erosion.<br>
<b>Translocation occurs when materials</b><br>
are not removed from the soil, instead<br>
they move from one location to another.<br>
<b>Examples of translocation are eluviation</b><br>
and illuviation. Transformation process<br>
involves changes of soil materials. A<br>
good example of transformation is<br>
weathering process, and when leaves<br>
decompose to form humus. The four<br>
soil forming processes can be grouped<br>
into two major two categories, namely;<br>
simple processes and complex processes.<br>
<b>Simple processes</b><br>
These include processes like weathering,<br>
leaching, eluviation, illuviation,<br>
humification, cheluviation, organic<br>
sorting and mineralization.<br>
<b>Weathering</b><br>
<b>This is the processes by which rocks</b><br>
are decomposed or disintegrated by<br>
chemical, biological or mechanical<br>
means, respectively, due to exposure at<br>
or near the earth’s surface. Weathering<br>
leaves primary minerals as residues<br>
and produces secondary minerals.<br>
<b>Also it determines the rates of release</b><br>
of nutrients, the soil depth, texture and<br>
drainage. In the system, this means that<br>
minerals are released as inputs into soil<br>
system from bedrock store and transfer<br>
into soil store.<br>
<b>Leaching</b><br>
<b>This is the removal of soluble materials</b><br>
in solution from top soil to the sub-soil.<br>
<b>This is common where precipitation</b><br>
exceeds evapotranspiration and there<br>
is soil drainage, rainwater containing<br>
oxygen, carbonic acid and organic<br>
acids collected as it passes through<br>
the surface vegetation, causes chemical<br>
weathering leading to the breakdown of<br>
clays and the dissolving of soluble salts<br>
(bases). Calcium and magnesium are<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   294</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

eluviated from the A-horizon, making<br>
them increasingly acidic as they are<br>
replaced by hydrogen ions. These<br>
are subsequently illuviated to the<br>
underlying B- Horizon, or are leached<br>
out of the system (Figure 5.18).<br>
<b>Organic soil horizon</b><br>
release minerals to the<br>
soil through the process<br>
of decomposition by<br>
soil microorganisms<br>
<b>Rainfall</b><br>
<b>Minerals dissolve into</b><br>
the soil water between<br>
individual soil crumbs<br>
<b>In porous soils dissolved</b><br>
minerals move down<br>
the soil column as the<br>
soil drains<br>
<b>Minerals can be</b><br>
translocated to depth<br>
in the soil column as<br>
they precipitate out of<br>
solution making them<br>
unavailable to plants<br>
<b>Figure 5.18: Leaching in soils</b><br>
<b>Eluviation</b><br>
<b>Eluviation is the downward movement</b><br>
of soil material that is organic and<br>
mineral matter from the A-horizon<br>
to the lower horizon in solution or in<br>
colloidal suspension. It differs from<br>
leaching in that it involves the total loss<br>
of materials and can either be vertical<br>
or lateral. Another difference is that<br>
eluviation involves the movement of<br>
colloids while leaching involves the<br>
movement of materials in solution<br>
form only.<br>
<b>Illuviation</b><br>
<b>Illuviation is the process of washing and</b><br>
re-deposition of organic and mineral<br>
matter in the B-horizon. Illuviation can<br>
lead to the formation of a layer in the<br>
<b>B-horizon called hard pan. B horizons</b><br>
often go through significant change<br>
through the deposition of clays, organics,<br>
carbonates, and other materials. The<br>
particles that are deposited by illuviation<br>
include clay minerals, iron, humus, and<br>
calcium carbonate which are carried<br>
by percolating fluid, usually from<br>
precipitation.<br>
<b>Humification</b><br>
<b>This is the process through which</b><br>
organic matter is decomposed to<br>
form humus. Humus may be mixed<br>
throughout the depth of the soil or it<br>
may form a distinct layer. The process<br>
of humification is the most active of<br>
all in the A-horizon of the soil profile.<br>
<b>Humus is incorporated within the soil</b><br>
to give a crumbly, black, nutrient-rich<br>
layer known as mull. In places where<br>
humus is slow to decompose, such as<br>
in cold, and wet upland areas, humus<br>
produces a fibrous acidic and a nutrient<br>
deficient surface horizon known as mor.<br>
<b>Cheluviation</b><br>
<b>This is a downward movement of</b><br>
soluble chelates through the soil profile.<br>
<b>Cheluviation occurs through the influence</b><br>
of organic agents. When organic matter<br>
decomposes, it releases nutrients and<br>
organic acids. These nutrients and acids<br>
are known as chelating agents which<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   295</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
296<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
attack clays and other minerals, mainly<br>
in the A-horizon. The chelating agents<br>
then combine with the cations of the<br>
iron and aluminium to form organic-<br>
metal compounds known as chelates,<br>
which are readily transported downward<br>
through the soil profile.<br>
<b>Organic sorting</b><br>
<b>Several</b><br>
processes<br>
operate<br>
within<br>
the soil to re-organize mineral and<br>
organic matter into horizons, and<br>
contribute to the aggregation of<br>
particles and the formation of peds.<br>
<b>Earth worm activity is a significant</b><br>
factor in sorting materials into different<br>
particles.<br>
<b>Mineralization</b><br>
<b>This is the conversion of the elements</b><br>
from organic compounds to other<br>
available form as a result of microbial<br>
activities in the soil. It is a process<br>
whereby the organic matters are further<br>
decomposed into mineral compounds<br>
forming important nutrients for the<br>
plant growth.<br>
<b>Complex processes</b><br>
<b>These processes involve a combination</b><br>
of several simple processes involved<br>
in soil formation. They include,<br>
podsolization, calcification, salinization,<br>
gleying and laterization.<br>
<b>Podsolization</b><br>
<b>This is a more intense form of</b><br>
leaching. This is a soil forming process<br>
which normally takes place in cool<br>
climates where precipitation is greatly<br>
in excess of evapotranspiration and<br>
where soils are well drained or sandy.<br>
<b>Podsolization is defined as the removal</b><br>
of iron and aluminium oxides together<br>
with humus. As the surface vegetation<br>
is often coniferous forest, more<br>
rain percolating through it becomes<br>
progressively more acidic and may<br>
reach a pH of 5.0 or less. This in turn<br>
dissolves an increasing amount of bases<br>
such as calcium, magnesium, sodium<br>
and potassium silica and ultimately the<br>
sesquioxides of iron and aluminium.<br>
<b>The resultant podsol soil therefore has</b><br>
two distinct horizons: the bleached<br>
<b>A-horizon drained of coloured minerals</b><br>
by leaching, and the reddish-brown<br>
<b>B-horizon where the sesquioxides</b><br>
have been illuviated. Often the iron<br>
deposits form an iron pan which is a<br>
characteristic of a podsol.<br>
<b>Laterization</b><br>
<b>This is a soil forming process taking</b><br>
place in tropical humid and sub-tropical<br>
regions. Latosols are the typical products<br>
of the process. They are characterized<br>
by the presence of little or no humus,<br>
the removal of soluble and finest soil<br>
components, and heavy accumulation<br>
of iron and aluminium. The type of soil<br>
formed is called laterite soil, which is<br>
sometimes impervious.<br>
<b>Calcification</b><br>
<b>This is a soil formation process in</b><br>
which the surface soil is supplied<br>
with calcium in such a way that the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   296</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
297<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
soil colloids are always close to<br>
saturation. It is a soil forming process<br>
of sub humid and semi-arid climates.<br>
Although there may be some leaching,<br>
it is insufficient to remove all the<br>
calcium which then accumulates in<br>
relatively small amounts, in the B-<br>
horizon. Soils formed through this<br>
process include chernozems, prairies<br>
and sierozems. The collective name<br>
for these calcium accumulating soils is<br>
pedocals.<br>
<b>Salinization</b><br>
<b>This</b><br>
occurs<br>
when<br>
potential<br>
evapotranspiration is greater than<br>
precipitation in places where the water<br>
table is near to the surface. It is therefore<br>
found locally in dry climates, and it<br>
is a characteristic of desert soils. As<br>
moisture evaporates from the surface,<br>
salts are drawn upwards in solution by<br>
capillary action. Further evaporation<br>
results in the deposition of salt as a<br>
hard crust, thus making salinization<br>
to become a critical problem in many<br>
irrigated areas.<br>
<b>Greying</b><br>
<b>This is a soil forming process taking</b><br>
place in poorly drained areas in<br>
cold, and wet climates. Due to poor<br>
drainage condition, there is incomplete<br>
decomposition of organic matter giving<br>
anaerobic or water-logged condition.<br>
<b>This is most likely to occur on gentle</b><br>
slopes, in depressions where the<br>
underlying rock is impermeable, where<br>
the water table is high enough to enter<br>
the soil profile, and in areas with very<br>
heavy rainfall and poor drainage. The<br>
reddish coloured oxidised iron, iron<br>
<b>III (Fe+++ or ferric iron), is chemically</b><br>
reduced to form iron II ( Fe++ or ferrous<br>
iron) which is grey-blue in colour.<br>
<b>Soil catena</b><br>
<b>This refers to the variation on the soil</b><br>
type in terms of maturity and soil<br>
temperature according to the variation<br>
in slope (Figure 5.19). Soil is sometimes<br>
formed in a series from the top down<br>
the slope of the relief with different<br>
characteristics because of different<br>
parent rock materials. This phenomenon<br>
is called soil catena. A catena is a<br>
sequence of soil types down a slope<br>
where each soil type (facet) is different<br>
from, but linked to its adjacent facets.<br>
Soil catena is a function of topography.<br>
<b>The variation of soil type from top to</b><br>
the bottom of the slope is influenced<br>
by leaching, erosion and deposition. At<br>
the top, eluviation or input takes place<br>
while at the middle translocation takes<br>
place.  At the bottom zone, illuviation<br>
or output takes place. Therefore, soil<br>
is thick at the top and bottom, but thin<br>
at the middle. Temperature also varies<br>
along the slope, resulting in differential<br>
rate of decomposition of organic matter.<br>
<b>Also, along the slope soil pH is likely to</b><br>
differ as the top and middle soils may<br>
be subjected more to leaching than the<br>
bottom soils. Soils at the bottom of<br>
the hill is likely to be poorly drained<br>
especially when the parent rocks are<br>
impermeable.<br>

<b>Soil as a system</b><br>
<b>A system usually is made up by a number</b><br>
of inter-related components that works<br>
together as a whole. The idea of soil as a<br>
system is derived from a system’s theory<br>
that was propounded by a biologist called<br>
<b>Ludwig von Bertalanffy (1968). The soil</b><br>
is an open system because the materials<br>
and energy resources are gained and<br>
lost at its boundaries. A soil system<br>
comprises inputs or store, processes,<br>
outputs and recycling or feedback<br>
loops. The interaction amongst soil<br>
components is a network that recycles<br>
every aspect through lose and gain loops.<br>
<b>Inputs or store refers to all ingredients</b><br>
that are added in the soil in order to<br>
make a better soil. The inputs include<br>
such components like water from the<br>
atmosphere or though run-off that adds<br>
moisture contents which is essential for<br>
transportation of soil nutrients. There<br>
are mineral nutrients from weathered<br>
materials that are needed as food for<br>
plants. Other inputs are gasses from<br>
the atmosphere, and respiration of soil<br>
organism, organic matter and nutrients<br>
from decaying plants and animals, and<br>
solar energy and heat that is necessary<br>
for rock disintegration. The processes<br>
involve the mechanisms responsible<br>
for the formation of soil with simple or<br>
complex processes such as weathering,<br>
leaching, eluviation, illuviation,<br>
humification, mineralization, organic<br>
sorting, cheluviation, salinization,<br>
calcification, and podsolization. The<br>
process is responsible for the formation<br>
and development of the soil profile. The<br>
outputs include the end results obtained<br>
after the interaction of various processes.<br>
<b>It exits as the results of what is stored</b><br>
in the process, thus, it is the resultant<br>
effect from the inputs and process.<br>
<b>The components of output include</b><br>
water loss to the atmosphere through<br>
evapotranspiration resulting to the loss<br>
of moisture in the soil that can also affect<br>
soil productivity. Loss of soil nutrients<br>
through leaching process as well as<br>
through surface run-off. Also, loss of soil<br>
nutrients results from decreased amount<br>
of nutrients in the soil, for example<br>
harvesting by uprooting of crops, and<br>
loss of soil particles through soil creeping<br>
and soil erosion. Recycling or feedback<br>
loops is a self-enriching mode of soil<br>
entity that inform the repetitive nature of<br>
soil forming chain. It gives the summary<br>
of the three processes of inputs, process<br>
and outputs. A soil recycles by releasing<br>
the stored nutrients allowing them to<br>
return to the soil ready for future use<br>
through a process known as nutrients/<br>
humus cycle.<br>

<b>Soil classification</b><br>
<b>Soil classification is the grouping of</b><br>
soils with a similar range of properties<br>
(chemical, physical and biological) into<br>
units that can be geo-referenced and<br>
mapped.  Soils contain all naturally<br>
occurring chemical elements and<br>
combine simultaneously solid, liquid and<br>
gaseous states.  Moreover, the number<br>
of physical, chemical and biological<br>
characteristics and their combinations<br>
are nearly endless. Hence many different<br>
approaches have been proposed to come<br>
to sensible grouping of different soils.<br>
<b>Criteria of Soil classification</b><br>
<b>There are many criteria applied in soil</b><br>
classification. These include classifying<br>
soils in terms of maturing, whereby<br>
zonal soil stand for mature soil. Azonal<br>
soil stands for immature soils such<br>
as alluvial, till, sands, gravels, salt<br>
marshes and volcanic soils. Intrazonal<br>
soil is a soil of more recent origin such<br>
as calcimorphic, hydromorphic and<br>
holomorphic soils. This classification<br>
is based on time and stage, that is,<br>
whether the soil profile has been well<br>
developed or not. Another criteria is<br>
based on climatic disparities in which<br>
two soil categories emerge, that is: soils<br>
of humid tropics for example, laterites<br>
and soils of cold regions for example,<br>
tundra soils. Another soil classification<br>
is based on soil forming factors such<br>
as parent rock materials and time of<br>
development. Soils are also classified<br>
based on soil formation processes such<br>
as gleying that produces peat, and<br>
podsolization that produces podsols.<br>
<b>Again, morphological characteristics</b><br>
such as texture, structure, colour,<br>
depth, and porosity are used to classify<br>
soils. Finally, presence or absence of<br>
diagnostic horizons or features that<br>
reflect soil forming processes is used<br>
as one of the classification criteria.<br>

<b>Approaches in Soil classification</b><br>
<b>The existing various soil classification</b><br>
systems originate from different<br>
approaches, attributed to differences<br>
in soil cover and space that lead to<br>
distinguishing different architypes.<br>
<b>However, lack of uniformity in soil</b><br>
classification might be attributed to the<br>
fact that, soil science was distributed<br>
across the world in a rudimentary<br>
state and was generally developed<br>
independently in different countries.<br>
<b>There are many   approaches that are</b><br>
used in soil classification. These include;<br>
morphological, which classify soils on<br>
the basis of morphological properties<br>
of the soil profile; genetic approach<br>
that bases on genetic characteristics;<br>
and integrated which also is called<br>
morpho-genetic approach. Basing on<br>
these approaches among other factors,<br>
different soil classification systems have<br>
been developed for different purposes.<br>
<b>These are USDA Soil Taxonomy which</b><br>
was developed in order to interpret soil<br>
survey; the FAO Legend which was<br>
developed for representing the global<br>
distribution and geography of soils;<br>
and World Reference Base (WRB)<br>
of 1980 which is the international<br>
effort to provide a reference base<br>
that could incorporate numerous soil<br>
classification systems into a system<br>
of names that would be universally<br>
recognized ever since in 1980. Among<br>
the basic principles and criteria adopted<br>
by this system is an attempt to define<br>
and use diagnostic soil characteristics<br>
related to soil-forming processes and<br>
diagnostic features of significance to<br>
soil management. As a basic principle,<br>
these classifications have excluded such<br>
as climatic parameters, soil temperature<br>
and soil moisture regimes of Soil<br>
<b>Taxonomy as criteria for classifying</b><br>
soils. Development of soil classification<br>
systems in the history of soil science have<br>
passed through different steps from the<br>
ancient societies which can be generally<br>
divided into two main phases namely:<br>
first phase that is early soil classification<br>
and the second phase that is modern soil<br>
classification.<br>
<b>Early soil classification</b><br>
<b>The ancient civilization of human</b><br>
society all over the world started<br>
with a cross bond that human beings<br>
developed with soil, and through which<br>
soil differences were established. From<br>
that knowledge a traditional system of<br>
soil categorization emerged. However,<br>
the earliest formal attempt to classify<br>
soils can be found in the Chinese<br>
book “Yugong” which shows the way<br>
<b>Chinese soil was classified into three</b><br>
categories and nine classes basing on<br>
soil texture, colour, and hydrological<br>
features (drainage) that were used as<br>
the basis of land value determination<br>
for tax assessment. This signified the<br>
earliest introduction of morphological<br>
classification system.<br>
<b>Modern soil classification 1882 - 1900</b><br>
<b>The modern soil classification system</b><br>
was initiated by the Russian geologist<br>
<b>Vasily V. Dokuchaeiv who introduced</b><br>

09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
