<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
301<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
the scientific theory of soil classification<br>
on “Russian chernozem” in 1883.<br>
<b>Dokuchaeiv introduced zonation of the</b><br>
global soils temperature and precipitation<br>
distribution and vegetation gradients. He<br>
proposed that soil properties reflect a<br>
combined global effect of genetic factors<br>
of soil formation, and consequently he<br>
categorized the global soils into three<br>
main types namely; normal soils,<br>
transitional soils and abnormal soils<br>
which were later developed by his<br>
successors such as C. F. Marbut and<br>
others, as Zonal, Intrazonal and Azonal<br>
soils, respectively. Moreover, the<br>
broadening of the modern soil science in<br>
<b>USA between 1920s and 1930s built on</b><br>
the Dokuchaeiv`s ideas and C.F. Marbut<br>
borrowed and modified the Russian<br>
pedological views and ultimately<br>
developed a comprehensive system<br>
of soil classification. Progressively,<br>
the Great Soil Groups was evolved<br>
into soil orders and sub-orders such<br>
as Zonal soils, Intrazonal soils and<br>
Azonal soils.<br>
<b>Soil classification according to age</b><br>
<b>Zonal soils</b><br>
<b>Zonal soils are mature soils. They</b><br>
result from the maximum effects of<br>
climate and living matter (vegetation)<br>
upon parent rock in areas where there<br>
are no extremes of weathering, relief<br>
or drainage and where the landscape<br>
and climate have been stable for a<br>
long time. Zonal soils have had time<br>
to develop distinctive profile and have<br>
clear horizons.<br>
<b>Classification of zonal soils</b><br>
<b>Zonal soils are classified into four</b><br>
classes based on the zonation of<br>
climates and vegetation. These classes<br>
are the arid and semi-arid soils, cold<br>
climate soils, tropical climates soils,<br>
and lateritic soils.<br>
<b>The arid and semi-arid soils are</b><br>
soils that develop in arid areas of the<br>
tropics. Because of scarcity of rainfall,<br>
the desert soils are light coloured and<br>
sandy in some areas they may be saline<br>
due to salinization process caused<br>
by high temperature, semi-arid, sub-<br>
humid and humid grassland. They are<br>
dark-coloured soils, such as chestnut<br>
soils, chernozem and prairie soils.<br>
<b>Cold climate soils are tundra soils</b><br>
which develop in areas of very low<br>
temperature with permanently frozen<br>
sub soils, a low rate of evaporation<br>
and a short period of plant growth.<br>
<b>Deep soils cannot develop under</b><br>
such conditions due to frozen subsoil<br>
which limits chemical weathering and<br>
microbial activities.<br>
<b>Tropical climate soils are soils of</b><br>
tropical climates. They include latosols,<br>
black and dark grey soils. Latosols are<br>
the soils which are formed in the tropical<br>
regions which experience high rainfall<br>
and high temperatures with definite<br>
dry season. The high rainfall causes<br>
heavy leaching to take place in these<br>
soils. The soils include laterites soils,<br>
black and dark grey soils, and soils of<br>
tropical humid regions where there are<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   301</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
302<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
basaltic rocks. The soils may be sticky<br>
when wet but dry and crack during<br>
the dry season. They are very rich in<br>
humus in tropical rainforest, which<br>
gives them a black colour as observed<br>
in black cotton soil. Lateritic soils are<br>
soils of forested warm-temperate and<br>
tropical regions. They include reddish-<br>
brown lateritic soils, yellowish-brown<br>
lateritic soils and laterite soils.<br>
<b>Azonal soils</b><br>
<b>Azonal soils are young soils which</b><br>
occur in areas where the soil forming<br>
processes have inadequate time to<br>
operate on the components that form the<br>
soil. Azonal soils exhibit characteristics<br>
of their parent rock, material resulting<br>
from weathering or deposition, and so<br>
they have not developed complete soil<br>
profiles. Also, they are not associated<br>
with specific climatic and vegetation<br>
zones.<br>
<b>Classification of azonal soils</b><br>
<b>Azonal soils are classified into six</b><br>
classes which are mountainous soils,<br>
alluvial soils, glacial soils, windblown<br>
soil, marine soils, and volcanic soils.<br>
<b>Mountainous soils or scree soils are</b><br>
the result from weathering, especially<br>
on mountains. They are composed<br>
of screes, which are small loose rock<br>
particles that accumulate at the foot of a<br>
mountain. Alluvial or fluvial soils result<br>
from deposition of clay, silt and sand<br>
left by flowing water in a river valley<br>
or delta populary known as river borne<br>
soil. Such soils develop mostly in flood<br>
plains where the river deposits most of<br>
its load. They develop very fertile soils<br>
due to composition of various materials.<br>
<b>Glacial soils (till soils) are boulder clays</b><br>
or other unstratified sediments deposited<br>
by glaciers in glaciated lowlands.<br>
<b>They are formed from moraines. The</b><br>
composition of till soils reflects the<br>
character of the rocks over which the<br>
flakier has passed.<br>
<b>Windblown soils or loess soils are</b><br>
immature soils that result from deposition<br>
of materials by wind at the edges of a<br>
hot desert or along the coast also known<br>
as aeolian soil. They are composed of<br>
sand and clay. Good examples are sand<br>
dunes, loess and sand sheets. Marine<br>
soils are azonal soils which are made<br>
up of materials derived from the sea<br>
which built up along the coast in form of<br>
mud banks, sand banks, and dunes. The<br>
formation of marine soil is a function of<br>
natural processes like wave erosion and<br>
deposition. Good examples include salt<br>
marsh soils, mud-flat soils and marine<br>
clays. Volcanic or tectonic soils are<br>
soils formed from volcanic eruptional<br>
materials like lava. They are often very<br>
rich in nutrients, and they hold water well<br>
because of their volcanic ash content.<br>
<b>These soils are called andisols and</b><br>
they are often very young (immature)<br>
and acidic, depending on which type<br>
of the volcano they are derived from.<br>
<b>Volcanic soils around the equator can</b><br>
be very well weathered, and can lose<br>
some of their nutrients unless there is<br>
another eruption. These materials can<br>
be very dark in colour. When mixed with<br>
organic matter they become more fertile.<br>

<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

303<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Example of these soils are recent lava</b><br>
and ash soils.<br>
<b>Intrazonal soil</b><br>
<b>This is a type of soil whose occurrence</b><br>
and development reflects the dominance<br>
of one local factor such as relief, parent<br>
rock materials, or extremes in slopes,<br>
drainage and vegetation. Examples of<br>
intrazonal soils are those which occur<br>
in a forest which is locally influenced<br>
by the forest conditions. Major<br>
characteristics of intrazonal soil are:<br>
they occur under the influence of one<br>
local factor like relief or parent rock<br>
materials; and have well developed<br>
soil profiles which display the horizons<br>
clearly. They are not found in zones<br>
and can be subdivided into three major<br>
categories: calcimorphic, hydromorphic<br>
and halomorphic soils. Calcimorphic<br>
soils are also called calcareous soils.<br>
<b>They develop on chalk and limestone</b><br>
areas. They are poor soils because they<br>
are porous and have no humus. These<br>
include the rendzna and terra-rosa (red<br>
soils) in the Mediterranean countries.<br>
<b>Hydromorphic soils have high water</b><br>
content. Also, peat or bog soils occur in<br>
water logged areas like on coastal lands<br>
by gleying. They can be reclaimed by<br>
artificial drainage. They are common<br>
in the coast of Africa mangroves, the<br>
<b>Atlantic Coast of USA, and the Gulf</b><br>
coast. Halomorphic soils are soils that<br>
have high level of soluble salts which<br>
render them saline and have developed<br>
through the process of salinization. They<br>
mostly occur in hot, dry climate.<br>
<b>The USDA soil taxonomy</b><br>
<b>The soil taxonomy is a set of classes</b><br>
that is defined approximately at<br>
the same level of generalization or<br>
abstraction and that includes all soils.<br>
<b>The USDA soil taxonomy was first</b><br>
published in 1975 after several phases<br>
of modification. This classification<br>
relies on horizon diagnostic properties<br>
comprised of 12 orders (Figure 5.20),<br>
64 sub-orders, and 300 great groups<br>
summarize the orders of the soil<br>
<b>Taxonomy according to the USDA 7th</b><br>
Approximation system.<br>
<b>Alfisols</b><br>
<b>These have a combination of an ochric</b><br>
or umbric epipedon, an argillic or natric<br>
horizon.  They serve as a medium to<br>
high supply of bases in the soil. Also,<br>
they have water available to mesophytic<br>
plants for more than a half of the year<br>
or more than three consecutive months<br>
during a warm season. Presence of<br>
water and bases make them intensively<br>
used. They are extensively common in<br>
low and middle latitudes. Also, these<br>
soils have high content of clay.<br>
<b>Andisols</b><br>
<b>These are characterised by a dominance</b><br>
of short–range-order minerals or Al-<br>
humus complexes that result from<br>
weathering and mineral transformation<br>
with a minimum of translocation. They<br>
have a high phosphorus retention,<br>
available water capacity, and cation-<br>
exchange capacity. They are formed<br>

09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
304<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
from volcanic ejecta or volcaniclastic<br>
materials. They can form in almost<br>
any environment as long as suitable<br>
temperature and adequate moisture<br>
are available to permit weathering<br>
and the formation of short-range<br>
order minerals. The soils can have any<br>
diagnostic epipedon or surface horizon<br>
as long as the unique property of<br>
andisols is in 60% of the upper 60 cm<br>
of the soils, disregarding O–horizons<br>
that have 25% or more organic carbon.<br>
<b>Prior to 1989, the current classified</b><br>
soils as andisols were included in<br>
inceptisols.<br>
<b>Aridisols</b><br>
<b>The unique properties common to</b><br>
aridisols are a combination of lack of<br>
water available to mesophytic plants<br>
for much extended periods, one or<br>
more pedogenic horizons, a surface<br>
horizon or horizons not significantly<br>
darkened by humus and absence<br>
of deep, wide cracks and andic soil<br>
properties.  Aridisols lack water in<br>
most of the time, causing the soils to be<br>
warm enough for plant growth (warmer<br>
than 5oC) however, they can lack water<br>
for about 90 days consecutively when<br>
the soil temperature is above 8oC.<br>
<b>Aridisols are primarily soils of arid</b><br>
areas. They are found in areas that<br>
preclude much entry of water into the<br>
soils at present, either under extremely<br>
scanty rainfall or under slight rainfall<br>
that for one reason or another does not<br>
enter the soils.  The vegetation in many<br>
areas consists of scattered ephemeral<br>
grass and forbs, as well as xerophytic<br>
shrubs.  Some Aridisols furnish limited<br>
grazing.  If irrigated, many of them can<br>
be suitable for a wide variety of crops.<br>
<b>Entisols</b><br>
<b>Entisols are soils mainly characterised</b><br>
by dominance of mineral soil materials<br>
and absence of distinct pedogenic<br>
horizons. Also, they are distinguished<br>
by the absence of features of any major<br>
set of soil forming processes. Entisols<br>
are soils in the sense that they support<br>
plants, but they may be in any climate<br>
and under any vegetation. The absence<br>
of pedogenic horizon may be the result<br>
of an inert parent rock materials, such<br>
as quartz sand, of which horizons do<br>
not readily form; and slowly soluble<br>
hard rock, such as limestone, which<br>
leaves little residue. It may also result<br>
from insufficient time for horizons to<br>
form, as in recent deposit of ash or<br>
alluvium; they occur on slopes where<br>
the rate of erosion exceeds the rate of<br>
formation of pedogenic horizons; they<br>
form from the late mixing of horizons<br>
by animals or by ploughing to a depth<br>
of 1 or 2 m; or the spoils from deep<br>
excavations.<br>
<b>Gelisols</b><br>
<b>The unique property of gelisols is</b><br>
the presence of permafrost and soil<br>
features and properties associated with<br>
freezing and thawing. These features<br>
include irregular or broken horizons<br>
and incorporation of organic materials<br>
in the lower horizons, especially along<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   304</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
305<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
the top of the permafrost table. Freezing<br>
and thawing produce granular, platy,<br>
and vesicular structures in surface and<br>
subsurface horizons. The increase in<br>
soil volume on freezing is considered a<br>
major soil forming process in gelisols.<br>
<b>These soils are confined to higher</b><br>
latitudes or high elevations, but they<br>
form about 13% of the soils in the<br>
world, second to Aridisols.<br>
<b>Histosols</b><br>
<b>These have a very high content of</b><br>
organic matter in the upper 80 cm of the<br>
soils and they do not have permafrost.<br>
<b>The amount of organic matter is at</b><br>
least 20 to 30 percent in more than a<br>
half of this thickness, or the horizon<br>
that is rich in organic matter resting on<br>
rock. Most of the histosols are peats or<br>
mucks, which consist of more or less<br>
decomposed plant remains that have<br>
been accumulated in water. However,<br>
some of them are formed from litter or<br>
moss, or both and are freely drained.<br>
<b>Inceptisols</b><br>
<b>These have a wide range of</b><br>
characteristics and they occur in a wide<br>
variety of climates. They can form in<br>
almost any environment, except in an<br>
arid environment, and the comparable<br>
differences in vegetation are great.<br>
They occur on a variety of landforms.<br>
<b>The unique properties of inceptisols</b><br>
are a combination of water available<br>
to plants for more than half a year or<br>
more than three consecutive months<br>
during a warm season and one or more<br>
pedogenic horizons of alteration or<br>
concentration with little accumulation<br>
of translocated materials other than<br>
carbonates or amorphous silica. These<br>
soils lack one or more of the unique<br>
properties of mollisols, which are thick,<br>
dark surface horizon and with high<br>
calcium supply. Also, they do not have<br>
the unique property of andisols, which<br>
is the dominance of short-range-order<br>
minerals or Al-humus complexes.<br>
<b>Mollisols</b><br>
<b>These are characterised by a combination</b><br>
of very dark brown and black surface<br>
horizons (mollic epipedon) that make<br>
up more than one-third of the combined<br>
thickness of the A and B horizons, or<br>
that is more than 25 cm thick and that<br>
has structure or is not hard or very hard<br>
when dry. They are also characterised<br>
by a dominance of calcium among<br>
the extractable cations in the A and B<br>
horizons; a dominance of crystalline<br>
clay minerals of moderate or high cation<br>
exchange capacity; and presence of less<br>
than 30% clay in some horizons, above<br>
50 cm if the soils have deep, wide cracks<br>
(1 cm or more wide) above this depth at<br>
some season. Characteristically, mollisols<br>
form under grass in climates that have a<br>
moderate pronounced seasonal moisture<br>
deficit. Some mollisols, however, are<br>
formed under a forest ecosystem, and a<br>
few are formed in marshes or in marls in<br>
humid climate. Mollisols are extensive<br>
soils on the steppes of Europe, Asia,<br>
North America and South America.<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

306<br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Oxisols</b><br>
<b>The unique properties of oxisols</b><br>
include extreme weathering of most<br>
minerals other than quartz to kaolin<br>
and free oxides, very low activity<br>
of the clay fraction, and a loamy or<br>
clay texture (sandy loam or finer).<br>
<b>They are commonly found in tropical</b><br>
or subtropical regions, and on land<br>
surfaces that have been stable for a<br>
long time. Generally, the surfaces<br>
are early Pleistocene or much older<br>
but oxisols can occur on relatively<br>
young surfaces when weathered soil<br>
material is deposited. Oxisols develop<br>
in a humid climate. However, due to<br>
climate changes, some are now in an<br>
arid environment.<br>
<b>Spodosols</b><br>
<b>Spodosols have markers in at least an</b><br>
upper sequum of dominant processes<br>
that translocate humus and aluminium<br>
or humus, and iron amorphous materials.<br>
<b>The B-horizon in spodosols consists of</b><br>
an accumulation of black or reddish<br>
amorphous materials that have a high<br>
cation exchange capacity.  This horizon<br>
is the spodic horizon. The spodic<br>
horizon has a course texture, high pH-<br>
dependent change, and few bases. The<br>
cation exchange capacity is related to<br>
the amount of organic carbon rather than<br>
to the clay.<br>
<b>Ultisols</b><br>
<b>Like alfisols, ultisols have markers</b><br>
of clay translocation. However, they<br>
have markers of intensive leaching<br>
that are absent in alfisols. Moreover,<br>
they have an argillic horizon and<br>
a low supply of bases, particularly<br>
in the lower horizons. The cation<br>
exchange is in most cases moderately<br>
low. The decrease in base saturation<br>
with the increase in depth reflects<br>
cycling of bases by plants or addition<br>
in fertilizers. They have water but in<br>
a small amount. They can be used for<br>
shifting cultivation without application<br>
of fertilizers. However, they can be<br>
made highly productive if fertilizer is<br>
applied. They are commonly warm and<br>
moist.<br>
<b>Vertisols</b><br>
<b>Vertisols have markers of processes</b><br>
related to the failure of soil materials<br>
along shear planes (slickensides). They<br>
have a high bulk density when the soils<br>
are dry, and they have low or very low<br>
hydraulic conductivity when the soils<br>
are moist. Also, they are characterised<br>
by having a high content of clay,<br>
pronounced changes in volume with<br>
change in moisture, and cracks that open<br>
and close periodically. Moreover, they<br>
contain evidence of soil movement in<br>
the form of slickensides, and evidence<br>
of wedge-shaped structural aggregates<br>
that are tilted at an angle from the<br>
horizontal.<br>

<b>Development</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.20:  Types of soils according to USDA taxonomy</b><br>
<b>Soils of Tanzania</b><br>
<b>Tanzania has adopted the World Reference Base (WRB) for soil resource</b><br>
classification system in identifying the soils in the country. The WRB as the<br>
system of classification and correlation recognizes 19 dominant soil groups whose<br>
development and distribution corresponds largely to the geological, hydrological<br>
and meteorological variations across the country. Out of 19 identified soil groups,<br>
only 11 are covered in this section.<br>
<b>Histosols</b><br>
Histosols are commonly developed mainly from incompletely decomposed plants<br>
and animal remains, normally with more or less admixture of sand silt or clay.<br>
Poorly drained environments in which they are formed may to the large extent<br>
account for their incomplete decomposition. As they contain high amount of<br>
organic matter, histosols area also called organic soils. In Tanzania histosols are<br>
common in Kagera, Kigoma, Shinyanga and Kilimanjaro. They are useful in<br>
forestry, horticulture, and some arable farming.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   307</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
308<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Andosols</b><br>
<b>Andosols are soils which commonly</b><br>
develop in volcanic parent rock materials<br>
ejected during volcanic processes, such<br>
as cinder, pumice and ashes in relief<br>
ranging from undulating to mountainous<br>
tropical regions. In Tanzania, andosols<br>
are common in Kilimanjaro, Arusha<br>
and Mara, and support a wide range of<br>
crops including tea, coffee, vegetables,<br>
sweat potatoes and wheat among others<br>
due to the high amount of nutrients they<br>
contain.<br>
<b>Arenosols</b><br>
<b>Arenosols are commonly developed</b><br>
from unconsolidated parent rock<br>
materials particularly residual Sandstone<br>
and siliceous weathered rocks or after<br>
weathering of quartz-rich soil material.<br>
<b>Arenosols normally have low nutrient</b><br>
and water storage capacity and may<br>
develop in arid humid regions. Normally,<br>
they are liable to leaching and erosion<br>
which result into mineral depletion. They<br>
are commonly found in Tabora, Iringa,<br>
<b>Mbeya and Rukwa regions. Despite of</b><br>
their susceptibility to leaching, arenosols<br>
are agricultural productive as they<br>
support cereal crops, ground nuts and<br>
Bambara ground nuts.<br>
<b>Vertosols</b><br>
<b>Vertosols develop from clay mineral-</b><br>
rich parent rock materials, particularly<br>
smectite clay in depression, lowly<br>
elevated and undulating tropical<br>
semi-arid and Mediterranean regions.<br>
<b>They have a changing tendency with</b><br>
water regime by alternate shrinking<br>
and expanding in dry and wet seasons<br>
respectively. In wet seasons vertosols<br>
tend to absorb water, expand and become<br>
sticky whereas in dry seasons, they tend<br>
to lose water, shrink and develop deep<br>
cracks. Normally they are associated<br>
with tillage difficult due to high degree<br>
of hardness during dry seasons and<br>
plasticity in wet seasons. However,<br>
they support annual crops such as rice,<br>
maize, cotton, sugarcane and vegetables.<br>
They are commonly found in Mwanza,<br>
<b>Tabora, Kigoma, Mara, Lindi and</b><br>
Coastal region.<br>
<b>Fluvisols</b><br>
<b>Fluvisols develop in areas which</b><br>
experience periodic flooding such as<br>
alluvial plains and river valleys in all<br>
climates. Because of the geological and<br>
geomorphological setting in which they<br>
are formed, fluvisols are usually rich<br>
in organic matter and water content,<br>
and sometimes saline. They are found<br>
in important and major river plains in<br>
Tanzania such as the Rufiji, Ruaha,<br>
<b>Ruvu, and Kilombero basins among</b><br>
others. They are economically potential<br>
soils as are highly supporting annual<br>
crops and grazing. Saline tidal lands<br>
support mangrove plants and other salt<br>
supported species.<br>
<b>Gleysols</b><br>
<b>Gleysols develop from the unconsolidated</b><br>
rock material resulting from fluvial,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   308</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
309<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
marine and lacustrine sediments with<br>
poor drainage. These are the parent rock<br>
materials from which gleysols develop.<br>
Due to poorly drained environments,<br>
which they are found, gleysols are<br>
normally associated with the formation<br>
of peats, especially in wetlands. When<br>
drained, they can support a wide range<br>
of crops, dairy farming and horticulture.<br>
<b>In Tanzania, Gleysols are commonly</b><br>
found in Mbeya, Rukwa and Morogoro.<br>
<b>Leptosols</b><br>
<b>Leptosols develop mainly from</b><br>
mechanical weathering in mountaneous<br>
landscapes with steep slopes, which may<br>
account for their shallow soil profile,<br>
low water content and vulnerability to<br>
erosion. They are useful however for<br>
forestry and nature conservation. They<br>
are common in Dodoma, Lindi, Mbeya<br>
and Iringa.<br>
<b>Ferralsols</b><br>
<b>Ferralsols are strongly weathered soils</b><br>
ranging in colour from red to yellow<br>
developed commonly in warm humid<br>
tropics. They are normally with a deep<br>
well drained profiles rich in sesquioxides<br>
and kaolinite clay minerals. They are<br>
common in Kigoma, Morogoro, Tanga<br>
<b>Kilimanjaro Mtwara and Dar es salaam</b><br>
regions.<br>
<b>Acrisols</b><br>
<b>Acrisols develop mainly from the old</b><br>
land surface with undulating or hilly<br>
topography in acidic weathered clay<br>
parent rock materials in subtropical or<br>
monsoonal warm temperate climate.<br>
They are commonly found in Tanga,<br>
<b>Kilimanjaro, singida, Morogoro, Lindi</b><br>
Mtwara Dodoma, Mbeya, and Ruvuma.<br>
<b>Solonetz soils</b><br>
<b>Solonetz soils develop in areas with</b><br>
flat land and hot dry summer climates<br>
characterized by fine textured sediment<br>
parent rock materials that are likely to<br>
develop solonetz with high amount of<br>
sodium or magnesium ions. Most of them<br>
have a much shallow surface horizons<br>
and are strongly alkaline. Solonetz soils<br>
are commonly found in Arusha, Rukwa,<br>
<b>Dodoma, Iringa, Morogoro and Mbeya</b><br>
regions, Tanzania.<br>
<b>Luvisols</b><br>
<b>Luvisols are dominantly characterized by</b><br>
marked textural differentiations in their<br>
profile developed from the wide range<br>
of unconsolidated material especially<br>
glacial drifts, wind deposited, material<br>
as well as alluvial and colluvial parent<br>
rock materials, in cool to warm climates.<br>
<b>Therefore, areas with flat or gentle</b><br>
sloping land are best sites of luvisols,<br>
which may account for their potentiality.<br>
<b>In Tanzania they commonly occur in</b><br>
Dodoma, Arusha, Manyara, Kilimanjaro,<br>
Tanga and Ruvuma regions.<br>

are presented in Table 5.6 whose<br>
information has been extracted from<br>
Figure 5.21 of Map of Tanzania.<br>

<b>Advantages of soil classification</b><br>
<b>Soil</b><br>
classification<br>
facilitates<br>
communication among the world<br>
soil scientists which brings common<br>
understanding in classifying the global<br>
soils. For instance, the world reference<br>
base for soil resource WRB acts as<br>
the reference system that unifies the<br>
world soil scientists in decision making<br>
among other things when dealing with<br>
the global pedological issues. Soil<br>
classification is the basis of land use<br>
planning in any country. It is through<br>
soil classification that much of the<br>
details about soils are learned and by<br>
which quality of soil both physical<br>
and chemical properties of the soil are<br>
identified. This then becomes the basis<br>
of allocating soils for different uses.<br>
<b>For instance, poorly drained soils are</b><br>
likely to discourage settlements but can<br>
be used for forestry.<br>
<b>Soil classification provides knowledge</b><br>
on how to deal with a particular soil.<br>
<b>Through soil classification, it is possible</b><br>
to know how susceptible a particular soil<br>
is in terms of its erodibility, leaching,<br>
drainage, and water retaining capacity<br>
among other factors. This draws<br>
attention on the sustainable use of the<br>
soil by applying appropriate techniques<br>
including planting trees, mulching and<br>
contour farming depending on the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   311</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
312<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
circumstances. It helps to understand<br>
the existing natural relationship between<br>
pedology and other disciplines such<br>
as geology, meteorology, plantology,<br>
anthropology and hydrology, in the sense<br>
that soil results from the interaction<br>
of environmental variables which<br>
determines most of its properties.<br>
<b>Variation of soils with space and</b><br>
time is reflected by the varied soil<br>
characteristics. Also, soil classification<br>
provides the basis for estimating the<br>
value of the soil and its productivity.<br>
<b>Limitations of soil classification</b><br>
<b>Soil classification is a very expensive</b><br>
process as it requires high financial<br>
support and qualified soil specialists.<br>
<b>Soil has no absolute sharp boundary</b><br>
where one soil type starts and end,<br>
this results in complexity of the<br>
process. Most of the soil classification<br>
systems are from foreign countries<br>
and were developed in circumstances<br>
that do not accurately match with our<br>
local situations. Furthermore, soil<br>
classifications consider too many criteria<br>
to handle which may sometimes bring<br>
confusion.<br>

teacher, present the findings<br>
before the class and address<br>
all emerging questions, and<br>
(c)	 Write notes in your exercise<br>
book and submit to subject<br>
teacher for assessment.<br>

<b>Soil fertility</b><br>
<b>Soil fertility refers to the ability of a soil</b><br>
to provide or supply necessary nutrients<br>
for the optimum growth of plants.  The<br>
ability to produce high or low yields<br>
depends upon the nutrient content,<br>
structure, texture, drainage, acidity<br>
and organic content of a particular soil.<br>
<b>Also, it depends on the relief, climate</b><br>
and farming techniques. For ideal<br>
plant growth, it must have access to<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   312</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
313<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
nine primary and secondary mineral<br>
and several trace elements. The four<br>
primary elements which are needed<br>
in considerable quantities includes;<br>
carbon (C), hydrogen (H2), nitrogen<br>
(N2), and Oxygen (O2). Besides,<br>
there are five secondary elements<br>
which are calcium (Ca), magnesium<br>
(Mg), potassium (K), sulphur (S),<br>
and phosphorous (P). These elements<br>
are needed in smaller amount and<br>
several trace amount elements like<br>
molybdenum (Mo), Copper (Cu),<br>
<b>Boron (B) and Zinc (Zn) are required</b><br>
in minute amount. Calcium helps<br>
the growth of roots and new roots,<br>
while magnesium is a compound of<br>
chlorophyll. Potassium and nitrogen<br>
help in the formation of starches and<br>
oils; molybdenum activates enzymes.<br>
Among these elements, nitrogen,<br>
potassium and calcium are most<br>
likely to become deficient in areas of<br>
cultivation.<br>
<b>Soil</b><br>
nutrients<br>
may<br>
be<br>
obtained<br>
in different ways such as organic<br>
matter decomposition, precipitation,<br>
biological nitrogen fixation, inorganic<br>
fertilizer application and weathering of<br>
soil rocks and minerals. Under normal<br>
recycling these nutrients will be<br>
returned into the soil as vegetation dies<br>
and decomposes. Plants, animals and<br>
microorganisms cannot use nitrogen<br>
in its inert form. Nitrogen must be<br>
converted into its usable form like<br>
ammonia which can be used by plants<br>
and other organisms. All organisms<br>
use ammonia to form Amino acids,<br>
proteins and other nitrogen containing<br>
compounds.<br>
<b>Biological</b><br>
nitrogen<br>
fixation is a process which converts<br>
nitrogen into ammonia which can be<br>
utilized by the plants. When bacteria<br>
die in soil, they release nitrogen<br>
containing compounds which can be<br>
used by plants for their growth and<br>
development. The production of fixed<br>
nitrogen from di-nitrogen for chemical<br>
fertilizer accounts for about 25% of the<br>
<b>Earth’s newly fixed N2, and biological</b><br>
processes account for about 60%.<br>
<b>As nutrients are taken out in the soil</b><br>
system and not replaced, there will be<br>
shortage of macro- nutrients. Soil need<br>
to be managed carefully if they are to<br>
produce maximum agricultural yield<br>
and cause least environmental damage.<br>
<b>Soil productivity</b><br>
<b>Soil productivity refers to the capacity</b><br>
of the soil in its normal environment<br>
to support plant growth to ensure the<br>
attainment of reasonable yields. Soil<br>
productivity is reflected in the growth<br>
of forest, agriculture, and vegetation<br>
or the volume of organic matter<br>
produced onsite. Soil productivity<br>
is largely determined by its ability to<br>
provide water and nutrients to allow<br>
deep rooting of agricultural plants. The<br>
measure of soil productivity is usually<br>
crop yield per millimetre of available<br>
water.  It is an inherent capacity of<br>
soil to support the growth of specified<br>
plants, communities, or a sequence of<br>
plant communities.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   313</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
314<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
Fertile soil has the following properties;<br>
it is rich in nutrients necessary for<br>
basic<br>
plant<br>
nutrition,<br>
including<br>
nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium.<br>
<b>It contains sufficient minerals (trace</b><br>
elements for plant nutrition, including<br>
boron, chlorine, cobalt, copper, iron,<br>
manganese,<br>
molybdenum,<br>
sulphur<br>
and zinc). It contains soil organic<br>
matter that improves soil structure<br>
and moisture retention. Soil pH is<br>
in the range between 4.0 and 9.0 for<br>
most plants but some prefer acidic<br>
or the alkaline condition. A good soil<br>
structure creates a well-drained soil,<br>
but some soils are watery (suitable for<br>
producing rice) while others are moist<br>
(for producing a variety of crops or<br>
plants). Fertile soil contains a wide<br>
range of microorganisms that support<br>
plant growth through nitrogen fixation<br>
and decomposition of organic matter.<br>
<b>It often contains large amounts of top</b><br>
soil which is optimal for deep rooted<br>
crops. Therefore, the fertility of the soil<br>
is influenced by some of the following<br>
aspects: texture of the soil, structure of<br>
the soil, climate of the area, soil pH,<br>
depth of the soil, nature of the parent<br>
rock<br>
materials,<br>
vegetation<br>
cover,<br>
human activities, and relief.<br>
<b>Loss of soil fertility</b><br>
<b>Loss of soil fertility and productivity</b><br>
refers to inability of the soil to provide<br>
the required nutrients for plant growth.<br>
<b>Loss of soil fertility occurs when the</b><br>
quantities of nutrients removed from<br>
the soil in a harvested farm exceed the<br>
quantities of nutrients being applied.<br>
<b>In this situation, nutrient requirements</b><br>
for the crops are met from soil reserves<br>
until these reserves cannot meet crop<br>
demands. This results in a reduction of<br>
plant growth and yield. The soil loses<br>
its fertility and productivity through the<br>
process called soil degradation. Soil<br>
degradation as a phenomenon refers<br>
to a decrease in soil productivity and<br>
quality, often due to human activities<br>
through either its misuse or overuse.<br>
<b>It leads into reduction of soil quality</b><br>
and decrease in a future potential for<br>
the survival of living organism. Soil<br>
degradation consists of soil erosion,<br>
salinization and soil pollution (soil<br>
contamination). The degradation or<br>
deterioration of a soil may be caused<br>
by chemical, physical and biological<br>
factors.<br>
<b>Causes of soil degradation</b><br>
<b>Soil degradation arises from both</b><br>
natural and anthropogenic causes.<br>
<b>The major causes of soil degradation</b><br>
include overpopulation, climate change,<br>
land use and urbanization. However,<br>
the most recognized causes of soil<br>
degradation are deforestation, poor<br>
methods of farming, shifting cultivation,<br>
overcropping, monoculture, the use of<br>
agrochemicals and overgrazing.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   314</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
315<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Chemical factors that cause soil</b><br>
degradation<br>
<b>Salinization</b><br>
<b>Salinization or development of salt, is one</b><br>
of the processes of soil degradation. The<br>
salt accumulation may lead to a partial<br>
or complete loss of the soil capacity to<br>
provide the required amount of water<br>
to plants, and therefore, a change of<br>
fertile lands to deserts. Frequently, the<br>
salinization processes create a practically<br>
irreversible chemical or physical<br>
internal soil degradation. The salinity<br>
problems appear as a consequence of<br>
salt accumulation in zones and depths<br>
where lie soil moisture regime is<br>
characterized by strong losses of water<br>
by evaporations and transpiration, and by<br>
reduced leaching of the remaining salts.<br>
<b>Although these processes of salinization</b><br>
may progressively develop under natural<br>
conditions where a combination of aridity<br>
and restricted drainage exists. This<br>
may only be accelerated when the soil<br>
moisture regime is drastically changed<br>
with the introduction of irrigation and<br>
without appropriate drainage conditions.<br>
<b>Soil acidification</b><br>
<b>Soil acidity is potentially serious soil</b><br>
degradation issue. It occurs when the<br>
concentration of hydrogen ions in the<br>
soil increases. Soil acidification is a<br>
natural process, but can be increased by<br>
some agricultural practices. Acidification<br>
occurs in agricultural soils as a result of<br>
the removal of plant and animal products,<br>
leaching of excess nitrate, addition of<br>
some nitrogen based fertilizers, build-up<br>
in mostly plant-based organic matter.<br>
<b>When soil becomes too acidic can</b><br>
decrease the availability of essential<br>
nutrients, such as phosphorous, calcium,<br>
magnesium, and molybdenum. The<br>
increase of the impact of toxic elements,<br>
decrease plant production and water use.<br>
<b>Also, can affect essential soil biological</b><br>
functions like nitrogen fixation, making<br>
the subject soil vulnerable to  decline<br>
of soil structure and increased erosion.<br>
<b>Without treatment, soil acidification</b><br>
can impact agricultural productivity<br>
and sustainability of farming systems.<br>
<b>Also, acidification can extend into</b><br>
subsoil layers, and impair plant root<br>
development.<br>
<b>Water logging</b><br>
<b>This occurs when most of  the pore spaces</b><br>
in the soil are filled up with water. In this<br>
case almost all the air is driven out of the<br>
soil. This causes the plant roots to suffer<br>
from insufficient air and finally die. Also,<br>
waterlogged organic matter breaks down<br>
very slowly because micro-organisms<br>
necessary for decomposition are inactive<br>
in areas with insufficient oxygen. Water<br>
logging result to the formation of a soil<br>
hardpan called duricrust, which make<br>
soils difficult to till.<br>
<b>Leaching</b><br>
<b>Leaching occurs when nutrients are</b><br>
dissolved in water and then carried<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   315</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
316<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
away downward in the soil profile from<br>
plant roots zone. In this case, the plant<br>
roots suffers from shortage of nutrients<br>
from deeper soil, this weakens plant<br>
growth.<br>
<b>Soil erosion</b><br>
<b>Soil erosion is among the processes of</b><br>
soil degradation. It involves physical<br>
removal of soil materials by the influence<br>
of the moving agents such as water, wind,<br>
ice or wave, and other natural forces.<br>
<b>It is the major contributor to nutrients</b><br>
loss because most of the soil nutrients<br>
are in the top layer (5 to 10 cm) of the<br>
soil. Soil erosion is mainly a naturally<br>
occurring process that affects all<br>
landforms, however, in many developing<br>
countries soil erosion is caused by<br>
anthropogenic activities. Largely from<br>
poor crop farming practices such as tillage<br>
and unsustainable livestock grazing.<br>
<b>Types of soil erosion</b><br>
<b>There is no universal classification of</b><br>
soil erosion. However, the following are<br>
some of the categorise of soil erosion.<br>
<b>Natural or geological or normal soil</b><br>
erosion: This takes place steadily, but<br>
long time slowly and develop features<br>
like valley, channels and plains.<br>
<b>Accelerated soil erosion: This kind</b><br>
of erosion results from disturbances<br>
of natural equilibrium due to human<br>
and animal’s activities. These activities<br>
include deforestation, overgrazing,<br>
and poor methods of farming. These<br>
activities may cause the removal of top<br>
soil.<br>
<b>Soil erosion due to agencies: This type</b><br>
of soil erosion is caused by agencies<br>
like water, wind and biotic factors. In<br>
these agencies, water is more adverse<br>
as it contributes largely on eroding soil.<br>
<b>Soil erosion caused by water particularly</b><br>
rainfall is influenced by energy from two<br>
different sources which are falling of rain<br>
drops and surface flow. The two energies<br>
detach soil particles and remove the<br>
top soil of the bare land. The following<br>
are the forms of soil erosion caused by<br>
runoff of rainwater.<br>
<b>Splash erosion: This occurs when</b><br>
raindrops hit bare soil. The explosive<br>
impact breaks up soil aggregates so that<br>
individual soil particles are splashed onto<br>
the soil surface. It is a type of erosion<br>
that is influenced by rain droplets.<br>
<b>Sheet erosion: This is the gradual</b><br>
uniform removal of the earth’s surface<br>
by water without the formation of rill<br>
or gullies. It is a general removal of<br>
the surface soil over a large area as it<br>
slips gradually down the slope (Figure<br>
5.15). It results in loss of the finest<br>
soil particles that contain most of the<br>
available nutrients and organic matter<br>
in the soil. Soil loss is so gradual that<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   316</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
317<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
the erosion usually goes unnoticed,<br>
but the cumulative impact accounts<br>
for large soil losses. Overgrazed and<br>
cultivated soils where there is little<br>
vegetation to protect and hold the soil<br>
are most vulnerable to sheet erosion.<br>
<b>Early signs of sheet erosion include</b><br>
bare areas, water puddling as soon as<br>
rain falls, visible grass and tree roots,<br>
and exposed subsoil or stony soils.<br>
<b>Rill erosion: This occurs when surface</b><br>
water runoff concentrates, forming small<br>
yet well-defined channels (Figure 5.22).<br>
<b>These distinct channels where the soil has</b><br>
been washed away are called rills when<br>
they are small enough such that they<br>
cannot interfere with field machinery<br>
operations. In many cases, rills are filled<br>
in each year as part of tillage operations.<br>
<b>When rills develop or grow, they form</b><br>
gullies.<br>
<b>Gully erosion: Gully erosion is an</b><br>
advanced stage of rill erosion. Gullies<br>
are formed when surface channels are<br>
eroded to the point where they become<br>
a nuisance factor in normal tillage<br>
operations. Surface water runoff<br>
causes gully formation or the enlarging<br>
of existing gullies. This is a result of<br>
improper outlet design for local surface<br>
and sub-surface drainage systems. The<br>
soil instability of gully banks, usually<br>
associated with seepage of groundwater,<br>
leads to sloughing and slumping of bank<br>
slopes. Gully formations are difficult<br>
to control if corrective measures are<br>
not designed and properly constructed.<br>
<b>Control measures must consider the</b><br>
cause of the increased flow of water<br>
across the landscape and must be capable<br>
of directing the runoff to a proper outlet.<br>

<b>Causes of soil erosion</b><br>
<b>Soil erosion can be a slow process</b><br>
that continues relatively unnoticed, or<br>
can occur at an alarming rate, causing<br>
serious loss of top soil. Other factors<br>
that accelerate soil erosion include soil<br>
de-compaction, low organic matter, loss<br>
of soil structure, poor internal drainage,<br>
salinization and soil acidity.<br>
<b>Deforestation</b><br>
<b>Deforestation is the conversion of</b><br>
forest to an alternative permanent non-<br>
forested land use such as agriculture,<br>
mining, grazing or urban development.<br>
<b>Destruction of natural vegetation cover</b><br>
is a major factor that subject soil to<br>
agencies of erosion mostly water and<br>
wind. Forests play a significant role of<br>
holding the soil firmly and stabilize it<br>
against surface runoff and rain drops that<br>
affect the top layer of soil. Absence of<br>
vegetation, the rain splash easily loosens<br>
the topsoil, and later easily removed by<br>
sheet wash or wind.<br>
<b>Poor methods of farming</b><br>
<b>Under poor methods of farming there are</b><br>
several sub causes of soil erosion such<br>
as poor cultivation methods. Method<br>
like deep ploughing destroys the soil<br>
structure by breaking up compacted<br>
soil, making it easy to be eroded by<br>
wind and water. Also, poor cultivation<br>
methods, especially on steep slopes<br>
increase the rate of surface runoff<br>
and leads to either rill, sheet or gully<br>
erosion. Shifting cultivation. This is an<br>
old method of farming where a slash<br>
and burn technique is used to clean<br>
land followed by a long fallow period.<br>
<b>The fallow period is important for the</b><br>
restoration of soil fertility. Slash and<br>
burn has harmful effect on the soil<br>
because it reduces soil nutrients and<br>
increase susceptibility to soil erosion.<br>
<b>Monoculture practices and over</b><br>
cropping. Monoculture is the practice<br>
of growing same type of crop on the same<br>
piece of land year after year. Continuous<br>
monoculture leads to the fastest depletion<br>
of soil nutrient, compared to other method<br>
of farming. It leads to decrease in soil<br>
organic matter, affects soil structure and<br>
increases need for fertilizers as well as<br>
more water contamination compared to<br>
crop rotation thus, soil becomes exhausted<br>
and leads to poor plant growth.<br>
<b>Overgrazing</b><br>
<b>Soil erosion from overgrazing is the most</b><br>
direct cause of grassland degradation. If<br>
there are too many animals that exceed<br>
the carrying capacity of pasture, it results<br>
to weak recovery of vegitation. Due to<br>
this, land surface can easily be eroded<br>
by rain and wind. Overgrazing is more<br>
common in drier areas of the tropics<br>
where pasture degraded by overgrazing<br>
are subjected to soil erosion.<br>
<b>Population increase</b><br>
<b>Population increase in a given locality</b><br>
leads to massive population pressure on<br>
land in terms of cultivation, firewood,<br>
and timber. These activities lead to the<br>
removal of the vegetation cover and<br>
consequently expose the soil to the<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   318</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

319<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
agents of soil erosion such as water and<br>
wind.<br>
<b>Effects of soil erosion</b><br>
<b>On-site effects</b><br>
<b>The implications of soil erosion by water</b><br>
extend beyond the removal of valuable<br>
top soil. Crop germination, growth and<br>
yield are directly affected by the loss of<br>
manure nutrients and applied fertilizers.<br>
<b>Seeds and plants can be disturbed</b><br>
or completely removed by erosion.<br>
<b>Organic matter from the soil, residues</b><br>
and any applied manure can be relatively<br>
or completely removed. Soil quality,<br>
structure, stability and texture can be<br>
affected by the loss of soil. Textural<br>
changes can in turn affect the water<br>
holding capacity of the soil. Moreover,<br>
on-site effects of soil erosion include<br>
destruction of infrastructures such as<br>
road and buildings due to removal of<br>
the top soil.<br>
<b>Off-site effects</b><br>
<b>The off-site impacts of soil erosion</b><br>
by water are not always apparent as<br>
the case of onsite effects. Eroded soil<br>
deposited down slope, inhibits or delays<br>
the germination of seeds and buries<br>
small seedlings. Also, sediments can<br>
accumulate on down slope properties and<br>
contribute to road damage. Sediments<br>
that reach streams or water course<br>
can accelerate bank erosion, obstruct<br>
stream and drainage channels, fill in<br>
reservoirs, damage fish habitat, and<br>
degrade downstream water quality.<br>
<b>Pesticides and fertilizers, frequently</b><br>
transported together with the eroded<br>
soil, contaminate or pollute downstream<br>
water sources, wetlands and lakes.<br>
<b>Soil pollution</b><br>
<b>Soil pollution refers to the presence of</b><br>
the pollutants into the soil, that impair the<br>
quality of the soil. It is one of the forms<br>
of soil degradation. Soil is polluted in the<br>
same way as groundwater. The sources of<br>
soil pollution include Industrial wastes<br>
results from most industrial wastes<br>
containing poison which kills both macro<br>
and micro-organism found in the soil.<br>
<b>Agricultural chemicals and domestic</b><br>
wastes occurs when these wastes and<br>
chemicals are deposited in the soil as a<br>
result lead to soil pollution. Agricultural<br>
activities through application of<br>
industrial fertilizers and pesticides are<br>
not beneficial if applied repeatedly over<br>
long periods. Mining activities may also<br>
cause soil pollution, the extraction of<br>
petroleum for example, may cause soil<br>
pollution due to oil spillage. Moreover,<br>
oil spill may occur in the process of<br>
transporting petroleum through pipes and<br>
tracks, thus causing soil pollution. The<br>
chemicals in the mineral oil increase the<br>
soil pH level and reduce the phosphorous<br>
concentration in soil.  Both solid and<br>
sewage wastes from homesteads may<br>
cause soil pollution if not well managed.<br>
<b>Acid rain occurs where the atmosphere</b><br>
is highly polluted by harmful gaseous<br>
impurities emitted from the industries<br>
such as Sulphur, Ammonia and Carbonic<br>
gases. When harmful gases introduced<br>
in the atmosphere are washed back to<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   319</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>

the surface and a combined with water<br>
vapour pollute the soil and retard the<br>
overall soil quality.<br>
<b>Impacts of soil pollution</b><br>
<b>Pollution of soil exerts high damage</b><br>
to the soils, and results into numerous<br>
effects which include; death of vital<br>
soil organisms which in turn retards<br>
decomposition of organic matter.Also,<br>
it can result to deterioration of both<br>
physical and chemical properties of soil<br>
which are important for determining<br>
soil productivity. Furthermore, it can<br>
cause loss of soil fertility due to leaching<br>
caused by excessive application of<br>
fertilizers and pesticides.<br>
<b>Biological factors that cause soil</b><br>
degradation<br>
<b>Biological factors are affecting the</b><br>
micro-flora and reduce the microbial<br>
activities of the soil which reduces the<br>
yield. This includes loss of biodiversity,<br>
decline in soil organic matter, loss of<br>
carbon sink, loss of vegetation cover,<br>
increased soil pathogens, and loss of<br>
resistance to diseases.<br>
<b>Natural factors that cause of soil</b><br>
degradation<br>
<b>Climate change may cause land</b><br>
degradation in different ways including<br>
drought, and heavy rainfall. Drought<br>
leads to the disappearance of vegetation<br>
cover which exposes soil to agents of<br>
erosion. Heavy rainfall leads to increase<br>
in surface runoff which eventually<br>
speeds up the rate of erosion, leading<br>
to the development of rills and gullies.<br>
<b>Soil conservation and</b><br>
management<br>
<b>Generally, soil is considered as a</b><br>
living entity as it supports life of living<br>
organisms. It is made from parent rock<br>
(mother rock) which gives soil nature<br>
and point of originality. Through the<br>
process of soil formation, soil can be<br>
created and destroyed. For instance,<br>
through bad methods of farming soil can<br>
be easily destroyed just like any other<br>
living organisms. Soil passes through<br>
different simple and complex processes<br>
in its formation, just like living organism.<br>
<b>The formation of soils needs time, the</b><br>
longer the time the mature the soil. Soil<br>
water takes 25% of the total soil volume,<br>
which comes from either rain, rivers or<br>
ocean. This is similar to living organisms<br>
such as people whose bodies are made<br>
up of 75% of water. Moreover, soil is<br>
made up of soil temperature, and soil<br>
air which comes from atmosphere and<br>
organisms. Therefore, all these life living<br>
facts justify why soils are so important<br>
to all living organisms. It covers and<br>
supports life of many living entities,<br>
hence conservation and management of<br>
soils is of paramount importance.<br>
<b>Soil conservation is the mechanical</b><br>
protection process of maintaining and<br>
improving of soil resource by involving<br>
all aspects of land use planning,<br>
development, and management. This<br>
is the process of preserving, protecting<br>
or restoring the soil and its resources,<br>
especially for future use. It involves<br>
prevention of soil erosion in order to<br>
improve its quality and productivity.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   320</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
321<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Also, it involves introduction of sound</b><br>
methods of farming which not only<br>
prevent erosion but also keep the soil<br>
moist and preserve its healthy state. The<br>
conservation of soil includes prevention<br>
of top most layer of the soil from erosion.<br>
<b>Soil management is the application of</b><br>
operation, practices and treatments to<br>
protect soil and enhance its performance.<br>
<b>Methods of soil conservation</b><br>
<b>Since people are so dependent on the soil</b><br>
for the supply of food, fuel and fibre, it is<br>
vital that great care is taken to conserve,<br>
protect, renew and maintain soil fertility.<br>
<b>The proper farming practices for soil</b><br>
conservation are crucial to increase<br>
infiltration. Some of the important<br>
methods for preserving soils include<br>
the following:<br>
<b>Contour farming</b><br>
<b>In contour ploughing the furrows in</b><br>
which the crops are planted follow<br>
the contour lines (Figure 5.23). These<br>
contour lines create a water break which<br>
reduces the formation of rills and gullies<br>
during times of heavy precipitation,<br>
and allow more time for the water to<br>
settle into the soil. This practice can<br>
increase crop yield through soil moisture<br>
retentions in arid and semi-arid regions.<br>
<b>In contour ploughing, the ruts made by</b><br>
the plough run perpendicular rather than<br>
parallel to the slopes, generally furrows<br>
curve around the land. This method is<br>
most important for preventing erosion.<br>
<b>Figure 5.23: Contour farming</b><br>
<b>Terracing</b><br>
Terrace is one of the oldest techniques for conserving water and soil. Terracing<br>
is common in hilly and mountainous regions. Terraces are built along contour<br>
lines to increase the arable surface area and conserve soil water and soil on<br>
hillslopes. Terraced fields can be of different shapes and sizes and consist of a<br>
flat section and a near vertical riser protected by a wall of dry stones, soil, grass,<br>
or trees. The height of the riser or wall can be from several decimetres to a few<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   321</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
322<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
meters, with a continuous or intermittent<br>
structure comprised of single walls or a<br>
complex series of walls. The flat surface<br>
created by terracing is generally used for<br>
cultivation (Figure 5.24). The creation<br>
of terraces on sloping land can reduce<br>
slope steepness by dividing the slope into<br>
smaller, gently sloping sections resulting<br>
in reduced soil and water loss.<br>
<b>Terracing with correct field management</b><br>
can reduce both runoff and sediment<br>
loss on average by over 41.9% and<br>
52%, respectively.  For example, in<br>
the hills of East Usambara Mountain<br>
in Tanzania, the fanya juu and fanya<br>
chini terraces have reduced both runoff<br>
and sediments loss. Though terraces are<br>
considered as advisable conservation<br>
practices to avoid soil and water loss the<br>
construction and management of terraces<br>
often disturb  carbon flux  between<br>
the atmosphere and soil via shifting<br>
land-use type and topography, which<br>
potentially alter soil organic carbon<br>
(SOC) dynamics. Proper land use, for<br>
examples, terraced reforestation and<br>
afforestation could markedly increase<br>
<b>SOC accumulation. For example, in</b><br>
<b>China’s landscapes terraces increased</b><br>
<b>SOC sequestration by 32.4% compared</b><br>
to that of the sloping lands on average.<br>
<b>Among the ecosystem services provided</b><br>
by terracing are the increase in crop<br>
productivity, expansion of land for<br>
cultivation and creation of aesthetically<br>
pleasing landscapes.<br>
<b>Figure 5.24 Terracing</b><br>
<b>Field strip cropping</b><br>
Field strip cropping is the production of the regular farm crop in more or less<br>
uniform parallel strips laid out cross wise of the general slope but not parallel<br>
to the contour strip cropping (Figure 5.25). Field strip is applicable to uniform<br>
gradual slopes on soil which are resistant to erosion. This conservation method<br>
is simple and can be used in connection with grassed water ways where the true<br>
contour cannot easily be followed. Also, the method prevents soil erosion by wind.<br>

09/03/2022   17:07<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
It works even better when high-growing crops are intensified in the side where<br>
wind blow most frequently than low-growing crops.<br>
<b>Figure. 5.25 Stripping method</b><br>
<b>Crop rotation</b><br>
<b>This is the process of alternating different</b><br>
types of crops on a piece of land in a<br>
year or each growing season. In a four<br>
years’ cycle, for example, maize, grass,<br>
beans and cotton may be grown. When<br>
crops with shallow rooted system are<br>
rotated with deep rooted crops, water and<br>
nutrients are utilized fully. Plants with<br>
deep roots absorb water and nutrients<br>
from the lower layer of the soil profile.<br>
<b>Also, crop rotation is used in controlling</b><br>
weeds. Some weeds grow well when<br>
a certain crop is grown in a field, but<br>
when another crop is grown such weeds<br>
grow with difficulty. Crop rotation is<br>
a good way of controlling plant pests<br>
and diseases. Plants of the same family<br>
are usually attacked by similar pests<br>
and diseases. If a crop of a different<br>
family is grown a pest or disease may<br>
disappear as it may not attack the crop.<br>
<b>Some plants take in a lot of nutrients</b><br>
from the soil. Such plants are called<br>
heavy feeders, others take in only little<br>
and they are called light feeders. By<br>
doing crop rotation of such plants on the<br>
field, the level of nutrients in the soil is<br>
maintained at a reasonable level.<br>
<b>Mulching</b><br>
<b>Mulching is the placement of any organic</b><br>
or inorganic material over the top of a soil<br>
surface to retain soil moisture and protect<br>
it from erosion. The practice can involve<br>
covering the soil with a layer of grass,<br>
straw, plant remains or even polythene<br>
sheets. Cover crops should be grown in<br>
empty space in between rows of plants<br>
specifically to cover such space.  It is used<br>
mainly in tree crops, vegetable gardens<br>
and pineapple plantations. When straws<br>
or grass are used, it is advisable to make<br>
sure that they are free from seeds which<br>
may cause weeds to develop. Mulching<br>
helps to preserve moisture in the soil<br>
by preventing or reducing evaporation,<br>
reduce loss of soil by erosion, control<br>
weeds, prevent use of weed killers and<br>
insecticides, which could be hermful to<br>
other life forms (Figure 5.26).<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   323</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
324<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Moreover, there are other methods of</b><br>
soil conservation (Figure 5.27) like wind<br>
breakers which is a conservation practice<br>
used to reduce the power of wind and<br>
its destructive effects on soil. It involves<br>
planting of trees or maintaining bushes<br>
to shelter crops. Cover crops is a soil<br>
conservation technique used to protect<br>
bare land or soil prone to erosion by<br>
planting cover crops as secondary species<br>
to control agents of erosion such as wind,<br>
and water. In agriculture, cover crops<br>
are plants that are planted to cover the<br>
soil rather than for the purpose of being<br>
harvested. Buffer strips is a technique of<br>
controlling soil erosion which involves<br>
planting of trees and bushes along<br>
the banks of water bodies to prevent<br>
water wash offs. Their roots fix the<br>
soil and prevent slumping and erosion.<br>
<b>Afforestation and reforestation methods</b><br>
ensure soil catchments and prevent soil<br>
erosion. The former refers to process of<br>
planting trees where none has existed<br>
before, while the latter is a process of<br>
planting trees where they once existed.<br>
<b>Building of gabions involves construction</b><br>
of ripraps encased in galvanised steel<br>
wiremesh cages or cylinder. They are<br>
used to stabilize slopes, shorelines, and<br>
stream banks. Ripraps is the process<br>
of pilling off rock pieces to create a<br>
structure known as rip-rap. The pilled<br>
rocks consist of different rock types<br>
of different size to protect shoreline,<br>
stream beds, and bridge abutments.<br>
<b>Reno mattresses are rectangular baskets</b><br>
made of heavenly galvanized, double<br>
twisted steel woven wire mesh. They<br>
are used for retaining walls, channel<br>
lining, slope stabilization, and dams.<br>
<b>Drop structures are also known as grade</b><br>
control, sill or weir. They are man-made<br>
small structures, usually built on minor<br>
streams or as a part of a dam’s spill way,<br>
to pass water to a lower elevation. Drop<br>
structures control the energy and velocity<br>
of water as it passes over, thus reduces<br>
soil erosion. Chute spillway are open<br>
channels like structures constructed<br>
on steep slopes of gullies face with a<br>
suitable inlet and outlet. It consists of an<br>
inlet, vertical curve section, steep-sloped<br>
channel and outlet. The major part of<br>
<b>Figure 5. 26: Mulching method to control soil erosion</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

the drop in water surface takes place in<br>
a channel. Water flow passes through<br>
the inlet and down the paved channel to<br>
the floor of the outlet. Chute spillway is<br>
used to control gully head and convey<br>
the runoff from upstream into the gully.<br>
<b>A check dam is a small barrier that is</b><br>
placed across a river or channel to slow<br>
the movement of water and sediments<br>
and to reduce channel erosion. These are<br>
tiny, sometimes temporary dams built<br>
across a drainage ditch or channel to<br>
lower the velocity of flow of water. The<br>
slowed runoff velocity reduces erosion<br>
in the channel. A check dam can be built<br>
by stones, sand bags filled with gravels<br>
or logs.<br>

<b>The role of institutions in</b><br>
conserving and managing soils<br>
<b>Tanzania is institutionally committed to</b><br>
ensuring that it effectively implements<br>
soil conservation. In fulfilling this<br>
obligation through the responsible<br>
ministry for agriculture and associated<br>
agencies and departments it has<br>
formulated policies and implements<br>
them, creates enabling environments<br>
for implementation of soil conservation<br>
plans, programs and strategies. It also<br>
collaborates with local and international<br>
institutions in implementation of soil<br>
conservation.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   326</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
327<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>The government of Tanzania works</b><br>
with the World Wide Fund (WWF) for<br>
nature. The WWF is an international<br>
non- governmental organization founded<br>
in 1961 that collaborate with the country<br>
in wilderness preservation and reduction<br>
of human impact on the environment.<br>
<b>Another organization is the African</b><br>
<b>Wildlife Foundation (AWF) which is the</b><br>
international conservation organisation<br>
which in collaboration with the country<br>
focuses on Africa’s wildlife and wild<br>
lands. The AWF’s programs and<br>
conservation strategies are designed to<br>
protect the wildlife and wild land of<br>
<b>Africa and ensure a more sustainable</b><br>
soil and water conservation strategies<br>
in Africa.<br>
<b>Again, the International Union for</b><br>
<b>Conservation of Nature (IUCN) is an</b><br>
organisation which collaborates with<br>
the country in nature conservation and<br>
sustainable use of natural resource. It is<br>
involved in data gathering and analysis,<br>
research, field projects, advocacy and<br>
education. A good example can be drawn<br>
from East Usambara mountains, since<br>
1980s, the government of Tanzania in<br>
collaboration with Non-Governmental<br>
<b>Organisations (NGOs) and various</b><br>
donors have been addressing land<br>
degradation problem in East Usambara<br>
mountains.<br>
<b>In 1987 the European Economic</b><br>
<b>Community (EEC) and the International</b><br>
<b>Union for Conservation of Nature</b><br>
(IUCN) in collaboration with the<br>
government of Tanzania established<br>
the East Usambara Agricultural<br>
<b>Development and Environment</b><br>
Conservation Programme (EUADECP).<br>
<b>The programme aimed at conserving</b><br>
forest catchments and agricultural land<br>
by introducing contour strips planted<br>
with guatemala grass (Tripsacum<br>
laxum) and agro-forestry. In 1991 the<br>
government of Tanzania in collaboration<br>
with the government of Finland through<br>
<b>Finland International Development</b><br>
<b>Agency (FINIDA) introduced the East</b><br>
<b>Usambara Forest Conservation Project</b><br>
(EUFCP). The aim of the project was to<br>
conserve forests and control erosion on<br>
arable land by planting contour strips<br>
and provide tree seedlings to the farmers<br>
in order to reduce pressure on forest<br>
resources.<br>
<b>In the year 2000, Muheza District</b><br>
<b>Development Programme (MDDP) with</b><br>
the support from Irish Aid introduced<br>
<b>Contour Farming Project in East</b><br>
<b>Usambara mountains. The objectives</b><br>
were to control runoff and soil loss from<br>
the agricultural lands and to improve<br>
soil fertility, by using land management<br>
practices and locally available nutrients<br>
sources such as farm yard manure and<br>
crop residues. The broad target was<br>
to increase land productivity thereby<br>
improve household food security and<br>
income. Other country initiatives include<br>
soil conservation in Dodoma that aimed<br>
at saving the region from severe erosion.<br>
<b>In this region the country in collaboration</b><br>

with various institutions it implemented<br>
soil conservation initiative known as<br>
<b>Hifadhi Ardhi Dodoma (HADO) since</b><br>
1973. The main objectives of HADO,<br>
among others, was to protect land from<br>
degradation and to rehabilitate the<br>
already depleted soils. The activities<br>
carried out include reclamation of land<br>
already destroyed by soil and gully<br>
erosion, raising and distribution of tree<br>
seedlings, and planting of demonstration<br>
woodlots and protection. The land<br>
reclamation methods used involved<br>
distocking and removal of people from<br>
non-agricultural areas.<br>
<b>Other methods involved introducing</b><br>
zero grazing; construction of contour<br>
ridges and planting of vegetation such<br>
as Agave, Eucalyptus, and Leucaena on<br>
the ridges; construction of the stone-<br>
check dams to control gully erosion;<br>
and planting of vegetation in the gully<br>
bottoms. Moreover, included protection<br>
of grazing, settlement, establishment of<br>
new agricultural farms, discouraging<br>
illegal tree cutting, and bush fires. This<br>
was achieved by carrying out regular<br>
patrols and labourers and control fire<br>
outbreak. HADO was successfully<br>
in many ways including substantial<br>
vegetation recovery, take off of the<br>
gully healing processes, rivers now<br>
follow more defined water courses, and<br>
arable land were accessible. Despite the<br>
above achievements of the project, it<br>
was generally challenged for not being<br>
participatory in nature rather it was<br>
considered as top-down. This is revealed<br>
in the local people dislikes that have<br>
led to many court cases and fines. Such<br>
a situation brought strain and conflicts<br>
between the HADO staff and the local<br>
communities that the project is supposed<br>
to serve.<br>
<b>Soil conservation case study</b><br>
<b>Farmers at farm level play great role in</b><br>
soil conservation and management. For<br>
example, in 1990s northern Tanzania’s<br>
<b>Arusha region was encountering serious</b><br>
threats to farming and food security.<br>
<b>To overcome this problem, farmers in</b><br>
<b>Likamba launched their own self-help</b><br>
organization, the Eotulelo Farmer Field<br>
<b>School (FFS). Eotulelo translates to</b><br>
“come and join us” in the local Maasai<br>
language. The original motivation to start<br>
the Eotulelo FFS came from experiences<br>
of the neighboring village, Ngorbob,<br>
where the Regional Land Management<br>
<b>Unit (RELMA) had started working</b><br>
with the community on conservation<br>
agriculture. Conservation agriculture<br>
carried out in the area entails a system<br>
of farming that conserves, improves,<br>
and makes more efficient use of<br>
natural resources, through integrated<br>
management of available soil, water, and<br>
biological resources. It combines three<br>
principles: which are disturb the soil as<br>
little as possible rather than plowing and<br>
keep the soil covered with cover crops,<br>
crop residues, or mulch and rotate or mix<br>
crops (planting a cereal such as maize and<br>
a legume such as pigeonpea or lablab).<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   328</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

329<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>The Eotulelo Farmer Field School</b><br>
participants strive in increasing yields<br>
dramatically and improved resource<br>
management through erosion-control<br>
technologies, sustainability measures,<br>
improved cattle and goat sheds, fodder<br>
planting, and new income sources.<br>
<b>Extension officers at various</b><br>
administrative levels deliver extension<br>
education to famers on soil conservation<br>
and conservation agriculture in general.<br>
<b>The ministry of agriculture has 14</b><br>
agricultural training institutes in the<br>
country to mention few are MATI-<br>
Tumbi-Tabora, Hort Tengeru- Arusha,<br>
<b>Inyala and Uyole-Mbeya. These institute</b><br>
offer certificates and diplomas in general<br>
agriculture. Besides that, the institutes<br>
conduct research and offer extension<br>
services to farmers. In addition, the<br>
<b>Department of Soil and Geological</b><br>
<b>Sciences of the College of Agriculture</b><br>
at the Sokoine University of Agriculture<br>
(SUA) offers undergraduate and graduate<br>
degree programmes in soil science. Its<br>
mission is to train and provide advisory<br>
services to farmers on soil and geological<br>
sciences. Furthermore, the ministry<br>
has established the Tanzania fertilizer<br>
<b>Regulatory Authority (TFRA). The</b><br>
authority ensures that farmers obtain<br>
fertilizers in time through provision<br>
of subsidies such as the input voucher<br>
system.<br>
<b>Another important national institution</b><br>
in soil conservation and management<br>
is the National Environmental<br>
<b>Management Council (NEMC). The</b><br>
council is the principle institution for<br>
the implementation of the National<br>
Environmental Management Act (2004).<br>
The Act prescribes among other things,<br>
the establishment of the National<br>
<b>Environmental Standards Committee</b><br>
and formulation of environmental<br>
regulations that ensure soil protection<br>
in agriculture and habitat. The main<br>
objectives of the soil quality standards<br>
are to set limits for soil contaminants<br>
in agriculture and habitat, and enforce<br>
minimum soil quality standards.<br>
<b>The Ministry of Land Housing and</b><br>
<b>Settlement is concerned with the</b><br>
preparation of   village land use plans,<br>
urban planning, and land surveying and<br>
mapping. For example, village land use<br>
planning is prepared by national land use<br>
planning commission (NLUPC), district<br>
participatory land use management team<br>
and village level land use management<br>
committee. The plans are prepared<br>
for the sake of minimizing conflicts,<br>
enhancement of land tenure security,<br>
increased agricultural productivity<br>
through identification of areas for<br>
commercial agricultural investment and<br>
to ensure land allocation for investment<br>
and facilitate conservation of water and<br>
soil. Proper implementation of these<br>
plans will enhance soil management<br>
and conservation both in the rural and<br>
urban areas.<br>
