<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Six</b><br>
<b>Atmospheric dynamics</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Weather and climate amongst other factors influence people’s lifestyles and the<br>
activities carried out on one hand, and on the other influence their decision<br>
over time. Also, understanding of weather and climate of a particular place can<br>
be a good indicator of understanding peoples’ way of living in that place. In<br>
this chapter, you will learn about weather and climate, and thier dynamics, the<br>
world’s climate regions and climate change altogether linked with the human<br>
activities. The competencies obtained from this chapter will enable you to plan<br>
and carry out various economic activities such as agriculture, business and<br>
other production enterprises based on climatic element such as precipitation,<br>
clouds, sunshine, humidity, wind and temperature.<br>
<b>Weather and climate</b><br>
<b>Weather refers to the state of the</b><br>
atmosphere at a particular geographical<br>
area for a short period of time, it can<br>
be hourly, daily, weekly, monthly<br>
or<br>
seasonally.<br>
<b>Weather</b><br>
describes<br>
atmospheric conditions that principally<br>
guide<br>
individual<br>
decision<br>
when<br>
planning for outdoor activities. For<br>
instance, knowing when it is sunny or<br>
rainy, warm or cold, helps an individual<br>
(s) to decide what type of clothes<br>
to wear, and re-organize domestic<br>
activities.<br>
<b>Climate</b><br>
is<br>
the<br>
average<br>
weather<br>
conditions of the geographical area<br>
recorded for a long period of time.<br>
<b>Usually, thirty to fourty years are</b><br>
considered to be the minimum period<br>
of time for establishing an adequate<br>
summary of a particular climate of an<br>
area. Climate constantly varies with<br>
varying geographical locations and<br>
time. Although weather and climate<br>
are related, yet they are neither,<br>
synonimous nor be interchangeably<br>
used. Weather is ‘what you get’ while<br>
climate ‘is what you expect’.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   331</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
332<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Elements of weather and climate</b><br>
<b>Although weather and climate are not</b><br>
identical, they share the same elements<br>
which are temperature, humidity, clouds,<br>
precipitation, air pressure, wind, and<br>
sunshine. These elements are very much<br>
interrelated in terms of variation as a<br>
change in one of them will bring about<br>
changes in others. At the weather station,<br>
each of the elements is recorded by a<br>
specific instrument.<br>
<b>Atmospheric temperature</b><br>
<b>Atmospheric temperature originates</b><br>
from the Sun whose interaction affect the<br>
incoming and outgoing solar radiation.<br>
<b>Atmospheric temperature refers to the</b><br>
degree of hotness or coldness of the<br>
atmosphere. Temperature is measured by<br>
a thermometer and expressed in Celsius<br>
(oC) or Fahrenheit (oF) or Kelvin (K).<br>
<b>Emission of solar radiation into the</b><br>
atmosphere leads to scattering, reflecting<br>
and/or absorbing of solar radiation which<br>
is needed in energy balance. This means<br>
that, when short-waves are emitted by<br>
the Sun, as they pass the atmosphere,<br>
three processes may occur which are<br>
absorption, reflection, or scattering.<br>
<b>Absorption refers to the process in</b><br>
which solar radiation is retained by a<br>
substance and converted into heat. It<br>
is the consumption of solar energy by<br>
various atmospheric components such as<br>
water vapour, clouds cover, and carbon<br>
dioxide. As a result, the heat gained by<br>
those substances, will enable them to<br>
emit their own radiation (infrared). In<br>
general, the absorption of solar radiation<br>
by substances in the earth’s atmosphere<br>
results in temperatures of utmost<br>
1800° celsius. About 19% of the solar<br>
radiation is absorbed by the atmospheric<br>
contents particularly, carbon dioxide,<br>
water vapour, ozone, and particles of<br>
dusts and ice. The absorbed proportion<br>
of solar radiation is so small (low)<br>
because they are forms of short waves<br>
which pass with little check through the<br>
atmosphere. An object or substance that<br>
is good radiator is also a good absorber,<br>
and the vice versa is true. For example,<br>
mineral materials like rocks and soils<br>
are generally excellent absorbers;<br>
snow and ice are poor absorbers, and<br>
dark coloured surface are much more<br>
efficient absorbers of radiation in the<br>
visible portion of the spectrum than light<br>
coloured surface.<br>
<b>Reflection is a process whereby sunlight</b><br>
is redirected back after it strikes an<br>
atmospheric particle (Figure 6.1). It<br>
occurs when the radiation hits the<br>
particles like clouds that are available in<br>
the atmosphere. Redirection of sunlight<br>
causes a 25% loss of the insolation. The<br>
ratio of the incoming radiation to the<br>
amount of reflected sunlight is known as<br>
albedo and is expressed in percentage.<br>
<b>The albedo varies with cloud type from</b><br>
30 – 40% in thicker stratus clouds and<br>
90% in cumulo-nimbus clouds, only<br>
10% reaches the atmosphere below<br>
cloud level. Also, albedo varies over<br>
different land surfaces. The ocean, and<br>
dark soil have an albedo of less than<br>
10%, coniferous forest and urban areas<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   332</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
333<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
15%, grassland and deciduous forest<br>
25%, light-coloured deserts 40% and<br>
fresh snow 85%. The occurance of<br>
deforestation and overgrazing practices<br>
result to the increase of the albedo, this<br>
reduces the chance of cloud formation<br>
and precipitation triggering the risk of<br>
desertification.<br>
<b>Insolation</b><br>
<b>Longwave</b><br>
emission<br>
<b>Atmospheric</b><br>
particle<br>
<b>Figure 6.1: Reflection of solar radiation</b><br>
<b>Scattering is a process whereby sunlight</b><br>
is diverted from its straight path on<br>
striking obstacles like dusts, gases<br>
molecules and water vapour. Scattering<br>
takes place in all directions, and some of<br>
the solar radiations reaching the earth’s<br>
surface as diffused radiations. About<br>
8% of the incoming solar radiation is<br>
lost by scattering. Scattering therefore,<br>
reduces the amount of incoming solar<br>
radiation reaching the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>A significant proportion of scattered</b><br>
shortwave is redirected back to space<br>
(Figure 6.2). The scattering effects<br>
always depend on two factors which<br>
are the wavelength of the incoming<br>
radiation, and the size of the scattering<br>
particle or gas molecule. Basing on<br>
such grounds, scattering is categorized<br>
as Rayleigh, Mie, and Non-selective<br>
scattering. Rayleigh scattering occurs<br>
when the particles causing scattering<br>
are less or smaller than the wave lengths<br>
of radiation in contact. It is therefore,<br>
wave length dependent. Mie scattering is<br>
caused by solid particles such as pollens,<br>
dust, and smoke in lower atmosphere. It<br>
occurs when the size of the particles that<br>
cause scattering is similar to the wave<br>
lengths of the incoming radiation. Non-<br>
selective scattering occurs when the size<br>
of the particles are much bigger than<br>
the incident radiation, usually in lower<br>
atmosphere. Scattering is a non-wave<br>
length dependent. Particles responsible<br>
for this effect are atmospheric water<br>
droplets and larger dust particles. The<br>
common example of non-selective<br>
scattering is the appearance of clouds<br>
as white.<br>
<b>The presence of a large number of</b><br>
particles with a size of about 0.5 microns<br>
in the earth’s atmosphere, results in<br>
shorter wavelengths being preferentially<br>
scattered. This factor causes the sky to<br>
appear blue as the colour corresponds to<br>
the wavelengths that are best diffused. If<br>
scattering was absent in our atmosphere<br>
the daylight sky would appear black.<br>
<b>Atmospheric particles</b><br>
<b>Figure 6.2: Scattering of solar radiation</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   333</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
334<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>It should be noted that, the presence</b><br>
of  absorption, reflection and scattering<br>
processes of solar radiation, results<br>
to only about 46% of incoming solar<br>
radiation to reach the earth’s surface<br>
directly, and as diffused radiation<br>
(Figure 6.3). The incoming solar<br>
radiation is converted into heat energy<br>
when it reaches the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>As the ground warms up, it radiates</b><br>
energy in form of long waves back into<br>
the atmosphere where 6% is absorbed<br>
mainly by water vapour and carbon<br>
dioxide which are greenhouse gases<br>
that mostly cause greenhouse effect.<br>
<b>Without greenhouse effect, much of</b><br>
the outgoing radiation would have<br>
been lost, consequently, the world<br>
temperature would be colder than it<br>
is at present. Therefore, the existing<br>
mechanisms between the incoming<br>
and outgoing radiation creates a natural<br>
balance which is called heat budget or<br>
heat balance.<br>
<b>Solar energy and radiation balance</b><br>
<b>The sun is the main source of energy</b><br>
to the planet Earth. The solar radiation<br>
also known as short wave or insolation<br>
travels through space from the sun<br>
in form of electro-magnetic energy.<br>
<b>Insolation in this regard, refers to the</b><br>
solar energy that reaches the earth’s<br>
atmosphere and surface which can be<br>
reflected, scattered or absorbed and<br>
often the occurance of all processes.<br>
<b>Energy from the sun controls weather</b><br>
and<br>
climate<br>
aids<br>
photosynthesis<br>
process. This energy is converted into<br>
food substances that supports all forms<br>
of life on Earth. The amount of energy<br>
received by the Earth is determined<br>
by astronomical factors including<br>
incidence angle of the sun rays,<br>
length of the day, transparency of the<br>
atmosphere, variations in the distance<br>
between the earth and the Sun, and the<br>
solar constant (the quantity of radiant).<br>
<b>Solar energy accounts for 46% of the</b><br>
solar radiation that passes through the<br>
atmosphere to become available on the<br>
earth’s surface. This energy is used for<br>
three main purposes which are heating<br>
the earth’s surface and the lower<br>
atmosphere, evaporating and melting<br>
of ice, snow, and glacier, as well as<br>
running photosynthesis processes in<br>
plants.<br>
<b>At the outer limit of the atmosphere the</b><br>
solar radiation consists of visible rays<br>
of about 41% in total, gamma rays,<br>
alpha rays, x-rays and ultraviolet rays<br>
of about 9% and the longer infrared<br>
and heat rays of about 50%.<br>
<b>During a clear day, there is net gain</b><br>
of heat from the Sun and temperature<br>
rises, and reaching a maximum in the<br>
early afternoon. During  the cloudy day,<br>
incoming solar radiation is minimal,<br>
and during the night incoming solar<br>
radiation<br>
ceases<br>
while<br>
outgoing<br>
radiation continues.<br>

<b>As shown in Figure 6.4 net gain of</b><br>

to the larger land masses of the pole<br>
ward sides of those latitudes shown in<br>
<b>Curve C. This means that, there is a</b><br>
positive heat balance within the tropics<br>
and a negative heat balance both at<br>
high latitudes and altitude.<br>
<b>Heat transfer</b><br>
<b>Heat transfer refers to the vertical and</b><br>
horizontal flow of heat energy along the<br>
thermal gradients normally resulting<br>
from differential heating. The amount<br>
of heat contained in the atmosphere<br>
can always be transferred from one<br>
location to another as well as from one<br>
atmospheric component to another.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   335</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
336<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Heat transfer occurs in order to avoid</b><br>
overheating and overcooling of parts of<br>
the surface of the Earth.<br>
<b>Mechanisms of heat transfer in the</b><br>
atmosphere<br>
<b>Mechanisms of heat transfer are</b><br>
basically the means by which heat<br>
energy is carried from either one<br>
molecule to another or one geographical<br>
area to another. Heat transfer in the<br>
atmosphere can either be horizontal or<br>
vertical.<br>
<b>Horizontal heat transfer</b><br>
<b>The heat is transferred horizontally</b><br>
away from the tropics preventing the<br>
<b>Equator and polar belts to become</b><br>
hotter and colder respectively. It<br>
involves transfer of heat from mid-<br>
latitudes area where there is heat<br>
surplus to high latitudes area where<br>
there is heat deficit in order to avoid<br>
overheating and overcooling of mid<br>
and high latitude areas respectively.<br>
<b>Generally, winds are responsible for</b><br>
about 80% of this kind of heat transfer<br>
while the ocean currents account for<br>
only 20%.<br>
<b>Vertical heat transfer</b><br>
<b>Heat can be transferred upward to prevent</b><br>
the earth’s surface from becoming very<br>
warm and the atmosphere to become<br>
colder. It involves transportation of<br>
heat from low altitude to high altitude<br>
areas in order to avoid overheating and<br>
overcooling of low and high altitude<br>
areas respectively. There are three<br>
processes of vertical heat transfer<br>
namely radiation, conduction, and<br>
convection.<br>
<b>Radiation</b><br>
<b>Radiation is the transfer of heat</b><br>
energy between two objects by<br>
electromagnetic waves. Solar radiation<br>
in the atmosphere is absorbed by gases,<br>
water vapour, particles, while on the<br>
earth’s surface it is absorbed by trees,<br>
flowers, soils and water bodies which<br>
then warm up. Dark objects on the<br>
surface absorb and warm faster than<br>
light coloured objects which reflect the<br>
radiation back to space. All substances<br>
tend to emit radiation (infrared).<br>
<b>However, a substance’s temperature</b><br>
will determine which wave length<br>
of radiation the substance will emit<br>
and the emission rate. Therefore, the<br>
higher the substance’s temperature, the<br>
shorter the wave length of the emitted<br>
radiation. Similarly, the higher the<br>
substance’s temperature, the greater<br>
the emission rate of the radiation.<br>
<b>Conduction</b><br>
<b>Conduction is a mechanism of heat</b><br>
transfer in the atmosphere whereby<br>
the heat moves from areas of much<br>
more heat to areas of less heat by<br>
direct contact of molecules. Warmer<br>
molecules vibrate rapidly and collide<br>
with other nearby molecules leading<br>
to the transfer of their energy. Heat<br>
transfer through conduction can be<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   336</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
337<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
demostrated by holding a metal bar<br>
between fingers and place it to a flame,<br>
the bar will absorb energy from the<br>
flame and the molecules inside it will<br>
begin to move, and the fingers will heat<br>
up, indicating that heat is transfered<br>
from the bar to fingers. This is one<br>
of the good examples of heat transfer<br>
through conduction. When heat is<br>
transferred through conduction, it<br>
flows from warmer to colder regions<br>
through air molecules and dusts. The<br>
transfer becomes more rapid when<br>
temperature differences increase. The<br>
rate of heat transfer through conduction<br>
depends on the nature of substances<br>
which can be good or bad conductor of<br>
heat. In the atmosphere, conduction is<br>
more effective at lower altitude where<br>
air density is relatively higher, and<br>
transfered upward to low density areas<br>
in which the molecules are spread<br>
further apart.<br>
<b>Convection</b><br>
<b>Convection is the transfer of heat</b><br>
through movement of water or air<br>
molecules. This type of heat transfer<br>
can occur in liquids and gases because<br>
their free movement of the molecules,<br>
making it possible to set up warm or cold<br>
currents. Convection occurs naturally<br>
in the atmosphere on a sunny day. As<br>
the earth’s surface absorbs sunlight,<br>
proportionally certain portions of the<br>
surface absorb more of it than other<br>
portions. The earth’s surface and the<br>
air near the surface heat unevenly. The<br>
warmest air expands and becomes less<br>
dense than the surrounding cooler air<br>
making them rise and become buoyant.<br>
These rising “bubbles” of warm air,<br>
called thermals transfers heat up into<br>
the atmosphere. Cooler, heavier air<br>
then flows onto the ground surface to<br>
replace the warm air that has risen.<br>
When the cooler air reaches the surface,<br>
is warmed as well, eventually rises as a<br>
thermal. This circulation is referred to<br>
as a convective circulation or thermal<br>
cell.<br>
<b>Variations in the radiation balance</b><br>
<b>The variations in radiation balance</b><br>
occur at different spatial and temporal<br>
scales. Regional differences are caused<br>
by uneven distribution of land, sea,<br>
altitude and the direction of prevailing<br>
winds. Local variations can be caused<br>
by aspect and the amounts of cloud cover<br>
while seasonal and diurnal variations are<br>
related to the altitude of the sun and the<br>
length of night and day.<br>
<b>Global factors affecting temperature</b><br>
<b>Factors affecting amount of temperature</b><br>
received at a given place and its<br>
radioactive balance vary considerably<br>
over time and space. This means that<br>
the temperature of a place depends on<br>
both long term, short term and local<br>
factors.<br>
<b>Long-term factors</b><br>
<b>Nature of the surface</b><br>
<b>Amount</b><br>
of<br>
temperature<br>
is<br>
not<br>
homogeneous all over the surface. The<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   337</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
338<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
nature of the surface includes both land<br>
and sea. Land and sea differ in their<br>
ability to absorb, transfer and radiate<br>
heat energy. The water surface is more<br>
transparent than the land, it is capable<br>
of absorbing heat down to a depth of 10<br>
metres. It can then transfer this heat to<br>
greater depth through the movements<br>
of waves and currents. Concerning<br>
land, it reflects high proportion of the<br>
solar radiation. For example, burnished<br>
silver reflects 95% of the solar<br>
radiation, while darker earth substances<br>
absorb more solar radiation. Water has<br>
a greater specific heat capacity than the<br>
land, therefore, land surfaces heats up<br>
more rapidly and more intensively than<br>
water surfaces, and cools more quickly<br>
compared to the water surface. As a<br>
result of differential thermal response,<br>
the land is hotter during the summer<br>
but cold in winter, while the sea is<br>
warmer during the winter but cold in<br>
summer. Similarly, the land is hotter in<br>
the day but cold in night while the sea<br>
is warmer in the night but cold in the<br>
day.<br>
<b>Distance from the sea</b><br>
<b>The continents tend to be warmer</b><br>
in summer than oceans in the same<br>
latitudes, but appreciably colder in<br>
winter. Therefore, the larger the land-<br>
mass the greater the contrast. During<br>
summer, the sea heats up more slowly<br>
than the land, while during winter, the<br>
opposite is the case. This causes areas<br>
near larger water bodies to be warmer<br>
in winter compared to those areas<br>
which are in the continental, the reason<br>
is that, the winds (on-shore winds) lose<br>
their moisture content before reaching<br>
continents. Instead they reach as dry<br>
cool winds.<br>
<b>Oceans, act as efficient ‘thermal</b><br>
reservoirs’. This explains why coastal<br>
environments have smaller annual range<br>
of temperature than locations at the<br>
centre of the continents. The climates<br>
whose temperatures are influenced<br>
greatly by the sea are called Maritime<br>
or Oceanic or insular climates. These<br>
climates occur on coastal regions<br>
which lie under prevailing wind (on<br>
shore wind). The climates whose<br>
temperatures are greatly influenced<br>
by remoteness from the sea are called<br>
continental climates. These occur most<br>
frequently in the interior of continents.<br>
<b>This can be well understood through</b><br>
the concept of land and sea breezes<br>
which will be discussed in the next<br>
section.<br>
<b>Altitude</b><br>
<b>Altitude has an immediate influence</b><br>
on temperature. Water vapour and<br>
dust in the air prevent heat formed at<br>
the surface of the Earth from rapidly<br>
passing back into space. But at high<br>
altitudes, for example on the tops of<br>
high mountains, the air is rarefied and<br>
it contains very little dust or water<br>
vapour. The heat from the tops of high<br>
mountains rapidly escapes and the air<br>
remains cold. As altitude of an area<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   338</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
339<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
increases, temperature decreases, and<br>
so does the density or pressure of the<br>
air. Air temperature in the atmosphere<br>
decrease at the rate of 0.6oC in every<br>
100 m of rising. This is because the<br>
molecules in the air which receive and<br>
retain heat become fewer and more<br>
widely spaced as height increases.<br>
<b>The effect of altitude on temperature</b><br>
can be well understood through the<br>
concept known as ‘lapse rate’ (Figure.<br>
6.5). Generally dropping tendency<br>
of temperature with the increase in<br>
height is caused by the changes in the<br>
concentration of dust and soot particles,<br>
and water vapour molecules which<br>
are carriers of heat and are usually<br>
more concentrated in lower elevation<br>
level, due to gravitational attraction.<br>
<b>Therefore, the higher the concentration</b><br>
of particles, the higher the temperature<br>
and vice versa. Highly elevated areas<br>
like mounteneous regions normally<br>
experiences their own climatic patterns<br>
compared to low land areas.<br>
1000m = 6oC<br>
500m = 3oC<br>
100m = 0.6oC<br>
<b>Figure 6.5: Effect of altitude on</b><br>
temperature<br>
<b>Temperature lapse rate</b><br>
<b>It expresses the physical condition</b><br>
whereby atmospheric temperature<br>
changes in response to the change in<br>
altitude which is a height from the mean<br>
sea level. It is the process decribing<br>
decrease or increase in temperature<br>
as the altitude increases or decreases<br>
respectively. The temperature in lapse<br>
rate is caused by the expansion and<br>
compression of air as it either descends<br>
or ascends at an altitude. Therefore, the<br>
loss of temperature is brought about<br>
by air expansion as it ascends into the<br>
upper levels of the atmosphere, while the<br>
increase of temperature is brought about<br>
by air compression (contracted) to high<br>
pressure down the slope, descending as<br>
it losses altitude.<br>
<b>Types of temperature lapse rates</b><br>
<b>The temperature lapse rates are</b><br>
categorized into environmental and<br>
adiabatic.<br>
<b>Environmental lapse rate (ELR)</b><br>
<b>This is the average decrease of</b><br>
temperature in a still air (stationary<br>
air) with change in altitude (increase in<br>
altitude) without being affected by the<br>
surrounding air or water vapour. It is a<br>
vertical distribution of temperatures at<br>
a given geographical location and time.<br>
<b>The ELR can also be understood as the</b><br>
decrease in temperature experienced<br>
with gain in height through the<br>
troposphere at an approximate rate of<br>
0.6ºC for every 100 m depending on<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   339</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
340<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
the local conditions. Factors affecting<br>
ELR are;<br>
(i)<br>
Height: ELR is lower at the<br>
ground level.<br>
(ii)<br>
Season of the year:  ELR is lower<br>
during winter and rain seasons<br>
than during summer.<br>
(iii)	 Surface characteristics: ELR is<br>
lower over mainland areas than<br>
over the oceans, seas, lakes and<br>
dense forests.<br>
(iv)	 Air-mass:<br>
<b>ELR</b><br>
varies<br>
with<br>
differences in the characteristics<br>
of air masses. ELR decreases<br>
with the increase in the amount<br>
of water vapour (humid air) and<br>
increase with decrease in the<br>
amount of water vapour (dry air).<br>
<b>Adiabatic lapse rate (ALR)</b><br>
<b>This is the rate at which temperature of</b><br>
an air parcel changes in response to the<br>
compression or expansion associated<br>
with elevation change without gaining<br>
or losing any heat to the environment<br>
surrounding the parcel. This describes<br>
what happens when a parcel of air<br>
(air mass) rises. The temperature<br>
decrease with an increase in altitude<br>
either below or above normal. When<br>
an air mass rises, increases its volume<br>
while decreasing its pressure and<br>
temperature. The ALR also describes a<br>
situation where the descending air mass<br>
decreases its volume while increasing<br>
its pressure and temperature down the<br>
slope with the decrease in altitude.<br>
<b>In either of the two processes of air</b><br>
mass (descending or ascending) the<br>
mixing with the surrounding air mass<br>
is negligible. Adiabatic lapse rate can<br>
be categorised into the following types:<br>
<b>Warm adiabatic lapse rate: This is a type</b><br>
of adiabatic lapse rate which occurs as<br>
a result of dynamic compression which<br>
usually takes place due to descending<br>
air masses in an area with high pressure.<br>
<b>Cool adiabatic lapse rate: This occurs</b><br>
due to dynamic expansion of air which<br>
tends to scatter air molecules away from<br>
each other, hence reducing its pressure<br>
and temperature. Cool adiabatic lapse<br>
rate includes dry and wet adiabatic<br>
lapse rate.<br>
<b>Dry adiabatic lapse rate (DALR): It</b><br>
occurs when an unsaturated air mass<br>
ascends vertically and expands together<br>
with losing its pressure, which results<br>
in cooling and loss of its temperature<br>
adiabatically.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
meteorological<br>
constant loss of temperature is 1ºC for<br>
every 100 m of ascent. In this process,<br>
the upward movement of air does not<br>
lead to the condensation. Therefore,<br>
the energy used by expansion will<br>
cause the temperature of the air parcel<br>
to fall.<br>
<b>Wet or saturated adiabatic lapse rate</b><br>
(SALR): It is the one which occurs<br>
when upward movement of air mass is<br>
sufficiently prolonged to enable the air<br>
to cool to its dew-point temperature,<br>
allowing condensation to take place.<br>
<b>At this point the loss of temperature</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   340</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
341<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
with the gain in height (altitude) is<br>
compensated by the release of latent<br>
heat. The saturated air mass which<br>
therefore cools at a slower rate than<br>
unsaturated air mass, loses heat at<br>
the saturated adiabatic lapse rate. The<br>
released latent heat reduces the rate at<br>
which the ascending air mass cools.<br>
<b>The saturated or wet adiabatic lapse</b><br>
rate can take place anywhere between<br>
0.3ºC and 0.9ºC per 100 m of ascent<br>
depending on the amount of water<br>
vapour present and the temperature of<br>
the air.<br>
<b>Temperature inversion</b><br>
<b>This is an increase in temperature with</b><br>
increase in altitude.  It is the reverse<br>
of the normal temperature lapse rate<br>
where temperature decreases with<br>
increase of altitude. On the earth’s<br>
surface, temperature inversion lasts<br>
only for a short period of time but<br>
below and within the ozone layer<br>
the condition may be permanent.<br>
Sometime due to differences in density,<br>
the warm air overlies the cool air mass<br>
at different levels of the atmosphere.<br>
<b>Temperature inversion can occur in the</b><br>
stratosphere and troposphere, that is,<br>
near the ground-level and high in the<br>
troposphere. High level temperature<br>
inversion occurs in the stratosphere<br>
due to presence of the ozone layer. It<br>
also occurs in the thermosphere as a<br>
result of presence of atomic oxygen<br>
and ionized particles. It also involves<br>
temperature<br>
inversion<br>
in<br>
upper<br>
troposphere. High level inversions are<br>
found in the depressions where a warm<br>
air-mass overrides a cold air mass at the<br>
warm front, or is undercut by colder air<br>
at the cold front.<br>
<b>Low level or ground inversions usually</b><br>
occur under anti-cyclonic conditions<br>
when there is a rapid loss of heat from<br>
the ground due to radiation at night<br>
or when warm air is advanced over a<br>
cold surface. This explains why fogs<br>
and frosts form along the valleys and<br>
hollows or depressions, especially<br>
during early morning.<br>
<b>The increase in temperature is</b><br>
experienced from about 15ºC to<br>
29ºC (60º – 80ºF) at about 50 km in<br>
the atmosphere, especially in the<br>
stratosphere due to the presence of the<br>
<b>Ozone which causes the absorption of</b><br>
shorter ultra violet rays. The ultra violet<br>
rays constitutes the radiant energy from<br>
the sun which result in heating the thin<br>
layer of the atmosphere, leading to the<br>
formation of a hot layer. Inversion also<br>
occurs below the Ozone layer due to<br>
accumulated greenhouse gases which act<br>
as a blanket that absorbs heat normally,<br>
the long waves. As the absorption of long<br>
waves takes place, heat waves below<br>
the ozone layer increase, causing the<br>
lower part temperature to increase as<br>
well. Hence, when some one moves from<br>
the ground towards the troposphere, will<br>
start experiencing a temperature lapse<br>
rate of about -80ºC (-112ºF) and then the<br>
temperature inversion as you approach<br>
the Ozone to about 20ºC (68ºF).<br>

09/03/2022   17:08<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

342<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Temperature inversion in the atmosphere</b><br>
is generally caused by number of factors<br>
including the following;<br>
(i)<br>
Convergence of air masses. When<br>
two<br>
heterogeneous<br>
air<br>
mass<br>
with contrasting humidity and<br>
temperature converge, the warm<br>
humid air mass is forced to rise<br>
over a cold dry air mass causing<br>
the upper part of the atmosphere<br>
to be warmer than the lower parts.<br>
<b>This also results into the formation</b><br>
of frontal rainfall and frontal fog<br>
among others.<br>
(ii)	 Land breezes. Due to differential<br>
cooling between the land and<br>
the sea, land experiences high<br>
pressure in the night due to low<br>
temperature while the sea develops<br>
low atmospheric pressure since<br>
it is warm. This results into<br>
movement of winds from the land<br>
towards the sea. The cold air from<br>
the land then replaces warm air<br>
(less dense) over the surface of<br>
the sea, forcing it to rise, leading<br>
to temperature inversion.<br>
(iii)	 Terrestrial radiation. The cloudy<br>
atmosphere<br>
especially<br>
during<br>
the night tends to absorb the<br>
outgoing long wave radiation<br>
and progressively getting warmer<br>
than the ground since the ground<br>
keeps on losing heat. This creates<br>
thermal inversion since the ground<br>
gets cold while the atmosphere<br>
gets warmer (Figure 6.6).<br>

by clouds<br>

(iv)	 Air subsidence. When the large mass of air especially in high pressure<br>
parts of upper atmosphere is down lifted, it normally becomes compressed<br>
making its air molecules to rub against each other leading to generation of<br>
heat and hence warming (Figure 6.7).<br>

(v)	 Advection. Temperature inversion is experienced when a warm air mass<br>
moves over the cold surface of the ground or sea. Such a horizontal<br>
movement of air result to the transfer of heat to the surface cold winds. This<br>
will eventually make the upper part of the atmosphere to appear warmer<br>
than the lower parts (Figure 6.8).<br>

<b>Ocean currents</b><br>
Ocean currents are the continuous,<br>
predictable, directional movement of<br>
seawater driven by gravity, wind, and<br>
water density. Wind tends to drag water<br>
along the barometric gradient leading to<br>
the movement of surface water of the<br>
ocean or sea. The coldness or hotness<br>
of the ocean currents depends on the<br>
source region. Density and gravity<br>
determine the upwelling and down<br>
welling of ocean or sea water.  Basing<br>
on the origin, ocean currents can be<br>
warm or cold which rises or lowers the<br>
temperature of an area subjected to them<br>
respectively. The surface temperature<br>
of water affects the temperature of the<br>
air above it, in the sense that cool water<br>
cools the air while warm water warms it.<br>
<b>Warm and cold ocean currents often raise</b><br>
and lower the temperature of coastlands<br>
respectively, especially if the winds are<br>
onshore.<br>
<b>Ocean currents and winds have the</b><br>
ability to transfer temperatures because<br>
ocean currents travelling away from the<br>
equator contain warm water, while those<br>
travelling towards the equator contain<br>
cool water. Cold ocean currents have less<br>
effect on the temperatures of coastlands<br>
because they are mainly located in areas<br>
where the winds are off-shore. But there<br>
are exceptions, for example, during<br>
summer, winds along the east coast of<br>
<b>Canada are onshore and they are cold</b><br>
because they have crossed “the cold<br>
<b>Labrador current”. When this current</b><br>
moves, it usually lowers the summer<br>
temperature. Similarly, along the coasts<br>
of California and Peru where onshore<br>
winds cross the cold Californian and<br>
<b>Peruvian currents, summer temperatures</b><br>
are lowered. Moreover, the cooling<br>
influence of the Southward-flowing cold<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   344</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>

<b>Canaries current can be felt as far as</b><br>
<b>South Cape Verde, on the West Coast</b><br>
of Africa. Warm ocean currents moving<br>
towards the poles carry tropical warmth<br>
into the high latitudes, and this warming<br>
influence is marked between latitudes 40º<br>
and 65º on the West sides of continents.<br>
<b>For instance, the warmth of the North</b><br>
<b>Atlantic Drift is carried to the land by</b><br>
the prevailing Westerly Winds, mainly<br>
in the winter (Figure 6.7).<br>

<b>Exercise 6.2</b><br>
1.<br>
What do you understand by<br>
the term ocean current?<br>
2.<br>
With examples, explain the<br>
effects of ocean currents.<br>
<b>Prevailing winds</b><br>
<b>Onshore winds in the tropics blowing</b><br>
from the cooler ocean tend to modify<br>
temperatures on the coast margins. The<br>
onshore winds such as the Westerlies<br>
may carry mild temperatures from<br>
the oceans to the continental margins<br>
in winter. Local winds such as the<br>
warm Sirocco, the cold Mistral, Bora,<br>
and Föhn or Chinook on the leeward<br>
side of mountains may produce rapid<br>
temperature changes. Usually, onshore<br>
winds influence coastal temperatures<br>
when the winds have crossed ocean<br>
currents. But even if there are no ocean<br>
currents, onshore winds tend to lower<br>
summer temperatures and raise winter<br>
temperatures in temperate latitudes.<br>
<b>Similarly, such winds have a cooling</b><br>
influence on day temperatures in<br>
tropical latitudes. This is noticeable<br>
along the coast of West Africa when<br>
the South West monsoon winds blow.<br>
<b>Sometimes, this influence extends up</b><br>
to 100km inland. During the northern<br>
winter, such as from November to<br>
<b>February, the cool Harmattan blows out</b><br>
of the Sahara towards the coast of West<br>
Africa, and this lowers the temperature,<br>
especially in those regions bordering<br>
the Sahara. During the hot seasons, the<br>
Harmattan winds are hot dry and dusty,<br>
often causing the temperature to rise<br>
sharply.<br>
<b>Length of the day and night</b><br>
<b>Insolation is only received during</b><br>
daylight hours and reaches its peak at<br>
noon. The axis of the Earth about which<br>

it rotates is tilted at an angle of 66½º<br>
to the plane of the path it traces during<br>

of the overhead midday sun varies<br>
throughout the year. The sun appears to<br>
“decline South” in the northern winter<br>
and to return to North in summer. The<br>
angle of incidence at which the sun’s<br>
rays fall upon the surface of the earth<br>
influences their heating effect.  During<br>
spring and autumn equinoxes, around<br>
21st March and 22nd September every<br>
parallel is half in daylight and half in<br>
darkness. This makes day and night to<br>
be almost equal throughout the world<br>
and almost equal amount of sunshine<br>
over all areas.<br>
<b>During summer and winter solstices</b><br>
(21st June and 22nd December) when the<br>
sun is overhead at the tropics due to the<br>
<b>Earth’s tilt, the hemisphere experiencing</b><br>
“summer” will receive maximum<br>
insolation (Figure. 6.9). All places<br>
nearby the equator experience equal day<br>
and night all the year. Northwards or<br>
<b>Southwards towards the poles, the length</b><br>
of day and night vary with latitude. For<br>
example, in the northern hemisphere<br>
when Earth is inclined toward the sun<br>
days are longer than night and southern<br>
hemisphere when the earth inclined<br>
towards the pole nights are longer than<br>
days (Table 6. 1).<br>

<b>Latitudes</b><br>
The curved nature of the earth’s surface causes the distance from the Sun to the<br>
earth’s surface and the angle of incidence of sun rays to the Earth to vary. The<br>
variation results into differential in heating between the tropics as compared to<br>
the polar areas. Relatively, there is long distance between the Sun and the earth’s<br>
polar areas in which radiation reaches at acute angle leading to relatively low<br>
temperature, which may be reflected by pressure of the ice sheets over there.<br>

<b>Elliptical orbit of the Earth on revolution</b><br>
Elliptical orbit of the Earth affects the temperature of the earth’s surface and<br>
atmosphere mainly by earth’s revolution. Through revolution, the earth has an<br>
elliptical orbit around the sun, this causes the earth to tilt or incline towards the<br>
sun. Its farthest position from the sun is on July 4th (Aphelion – 152 million km<br>
away from the sun) and its nearest position is on January 3rd (Perihelion – 147.3<br>
million km away from the sun). Therefore, the amount of solar energy received is<br>
6% more at the perihelion as compared during aphelion (Figure. 6.10).<br>

<b>Local factors</b><br>
<b>Cloud cover</b><br>
Clouds reduce the amount of solar radiation reaching on the earth’s surface and<br>
the amount of radiation leaving the earth’s surface. When there are no clouds,<br>

both types of radiation are at the<br>

<b>This is because heavy cloud cover</b><br>
reduces the amount of solar radiation<br>
reaching the earth’s surface. The heavy<br>
cloud cover in the equatorial regions<br>
explains why the day temperature<br>
rarely exceed 30oC. Normally, cloud<br>
act as a blanket thus prevents excessive<br>
loss of earth radiation. Clouds absorb<br>
and reflect a significant amount of<br>
solar radiation whereas loss of earth’s<br>
radiation is checked by clouds (Figures<br>
6.11). The absence of clouds in desert<br>
regions results into extremely high<br>
day temperature of about 44oC and<br>
low night temperature of about 21oC.<br>
<b>The absence of clouds during the day</b><br>
results in the maximum solar radiaton<br>
reaching the earth’s surfaces. By night<br>
clear sky allows the earth’s heat to<br>
escape freely (Figure 6.12). Variations<br>
in cloud cover between the desert areas<br>
and equatorial regions in particular<br>
explain why there is high temperature<br>
range in desert and low temperature<br>
range in equatorial regions.<br>

<b>Aspect</b><br>
<b>Is the side of the mountain facing the</b><br>
sun rays direction. The hill sides usually<br>
affect the angle at which the sun’s<br>
rays reach the ground. In the northern<br>
hemisphere<br>
for<br>
instance,<br>
slopes<br>
facing to the North, being in shadow<br>
for most or all of the year are cooler<br>
than those facing South. The steeper<br>
the South-facing slope, the higher the<br>
angle of sun’s rays to it and therefore<br>
the higher the temperature. Radiation<br>
involves vertical movement of air from<br>
the earth’s surface. However, in the<br>
southern hemisphere, slopes facing to<br>
the South, being in shadow for most<br>
or all of the year are cooler than those<br>
facing North. The steeper the North-<br>
facing slope, the higher the angle of<br>
sun’s rays to it and therefore the higher<br>
the temperature. North and south facing<br>
slopes are referred to, respectively, as<br>
the adret and ubac (Figure 6.13).<br>

<b>Urbanization</b><br>
<b>Narrow streets with tall buildings</b><br>
are more likely to develop micro-<br>
climates than those that are wider with<br>
lower buildings. Tower blocks cast<br>
more shadow, and normal building<br>
materials tend to be non-reflective and<br>
so absorb much heat during the day.<br>
<b>Dark coloured roofs and tarmac roads</b><br>
have a high thermal capacity which<br>
means they are capable of storing heat<br>
during the day and releasing it slowly<br>
during the night. Furthermore, heat in<br>
urban areas is obtained from car fumes,<br>
factories, power stations, and central<br>
heating, all these result in “urban heat<br>
island”. Temperatures are the highest in<br>
more built-up city centres and tend to<br>
decrease towards the suburbs and open<br>
countryside. In that regard, temperatures<br>
are on average 0.6ºC warmer. Night<br>
temperature may be 3oC or 4oC warmer<br>
due to presence of atmospheric dust and<br>
cloud that act like a blanket to reduce<br>
radiation. Buildings have a tendency of<br>
giving out heat like storage radiators.<br>
<b>Altitude of the sun often influences</b><br>
spacing between tall buildings such as<br>
blocks of flats (Figure 6.14).<br>

(b)<br>

<b>Humidity</b><br>
<b>Humidity refers to the amount of water</b><br>
vapour present in the atmosphere. It<br>
is measured by an instrument called<br>
hygrometer. The amount of water vapour<br>
present in the air varies widely from<br>
place to place and from time to time.<br>
<b>Warm air can hold much more water</b><br>
vapour than cold air. For example, air<br>
at room temperature (20oC) can hold<br>
almost three times as much water vapour<br>
as air at freezing (0o) point. Humidity can<br>
be expressed as absolute, specific or as<br>
relative humidity. The main sources of<br>
atmospheric humidity are water bodies,<br>
and vegetation. Humidity is taken into<br>
the atmosphere through evaporation and<br>
transpiration by the process known as<br>
evapotranspiration.<br>
<b>Absolute humidity</b><br>
<b>This is the mass of water vapour in</b><br>
a given volume of air measured in<br>
grams per cubic metre (g/m3). For<br>
example, if the absolute humidity of<br>
air is 10 grams, it means that 1 cubic<br>
metre of that air holds 10 grams of<br>
moisture in the form of water vapour.<br>
<b>The capacity of holding water vapour</b><br>
of an air increases with the increase<br>
in its temperature. For example, at<br>
100C, one cubic metre of an air can<br>
hold 11.4 grams of water vapour. If the<br>
temperature of the same air increases to<br>
210C, the same volume of air can hold<br>
<b>22.2 grams of water vapour. Change in</b><br>
temperature and pressure conditions of<br>
an air results in the change of its volume<br>
and consequently there is change in its<br>
absolute humidity.<br>
<b>Specific humidity</b><br>
<b>Refers to the actual quantity of water</b><br>
vapour held by a parcel of air. It can<br>
also be defined as the mass of water<br>
vapour contained in a given mass of<br>
air and is expressed as grams of water<br>
per kilogram of air (g/kg). Specific<br>
humidity is important in describing<br>
how much water vapour is available<br>
for precipitation. Generally, humidity<br>
depends upon the temperature of the air<br>
and changes from place to place, and<br>
with change in time. However, there is a<br>
limit in the amount of moisture that the<br>
air can hold. When this limit is reached,<br>
the air is said to be saturated.<br>
<b>Relative humidity</b><br>
<b>Relative humidity (RH) is the amount</b><br>
of water vapour in the air at a specific<br>
temperature compared to the maximum<br>
water vapour that the air is able to hold at<br>
that given temperature. When the relative<br>
humidity is 100%, the air is saturated,<br>
when the air lies between 80-99%, it is<br>
said to be ‘moist’, and the weather is<br>
humid or clammy, and when it drops to<br>
50% the air is dry. If unsaturated air is<br>
cooled and atmospheric pressure remains<br>
constant, a critical temperature will be<br>
reached when the air becomes saturated<br>
(RH = 100%) which is known as dew<br>
point or saturation point. Any further<br>
cooling will result in the condensation of<br>
excess vapour, either into water droplets<br>
where condensation nuclei are present,<br>
or into ice crystals if the air temperature<br>
is below 0oC. Relative humidity of the<br>
atmosphere changes in two ways namely<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   351</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
352<br>

direct gain or loss and temperature<br>
changes. The direct gain or loss of<br>
water vapour that is the additional water<br>
vapour has implication in raising specific<br>
humidity and in turn lowers relative<br>
humidity. The changes of temperature<br>
holds that even though no water vapour<br>
is added, a lowering of temperature<br>
results in the rise of relative humidity.<br>
<b>Significance of humidity</b><br>
<b>Firstly, climatologists often use specific</b><br>
humidity to describe the moisture<br>
characteristics of a large mass of air.<br>
<b>For instance, extremely cold, dry air</b><br>
over arctic regions in winter may have<br>
a specific humidity as low as 0.2 g/kg<br>
in comparison with the extreme warm,<br>
moist air of equatorial region which often<br>
holds as much as 18 g/kg. Secondly, it<br>
is a geographer’s yardstick for a basic<br>
natural resource. It is a measure of the<br>
quantity of water in the atmosphere that<br>
can be extracted as precipitation. Thirdly,<br>
cold, moist air can supply only a little<br>
quantity of rain or snow, but warm moist<br>
air is capable of supplying a considerable<br>
amount.<br>
<b>Importance of humidity to human life</b><br>
<b>Water vapour is an important heat</b><br>
absorber in the air and thus affects<br>
the energy balance of the atmosphere<br>
as it is a key agent in both weather<br>
and climate. The release of the latent<br>
heat of condensation is an important<br>
source of energy for the maintenance<br>
of the atmospheric processes. During<br>
evaporation and transpiration, latent<br>
heat is removed from surface liquid and<br>
results in cooling of the earth’s surface. It<br>
indicates the likelihood of precipitation,<br>
dew or fog. A higher water vapour<br>
content is necessary for producing<br>
clouds and ultmately precipitation. It<br>
brings human comfort. Human beings<br>
regulate their body temperature mainly<br>
by sweating and shivering. It is one<br>
of the fundamentals of biotic factors<br>
that define any habitat. It determines<br>
the kind of animals and plants that can<br>
thrive in a given environment which are<br>
useful to human life. Humidity is useful<br>
in industrial production and storage of<br>
goods. Humidity measurements are<br>
used to prevent condensation, corrosion,<br>
moulding, warping and other spoilage<br>
that are highly relevant for foods,<br>
pharmaceuticals, fuels, wood, papers<br>
and many other products. Humidity<br>
measurements contribute to achievement<br>
of correct environmental conditions.<br>
<b>Clouds</b><br>
<b>Clouds are frequent features of the</b><br>
atmosphere formed when air cools to<br>
dew point and vapour condenses into<br>
water droplets or ice crystals. About a<br>
half of the earth is covered by clouds at<br>
any given time. Major sources of clouds<br>
are the moisture evaporated from land<br>
surface, water bodies, as well as moisture<br>
from vegetation through transpiration.<br>
<b>Clouds can appear in varying shapes</b><br>
and sizes; from small, white puffy clouds<br>
often seen in summer to the dark layers<br>
that produce rain. They are often difficult<br>
to distinguish as their form constantly<br>

changes. The general classification of<br>
clouds was proposed by Luke Howard in<br>
1803. His classification was a descriptive<br>
classification based on clouds shapes<br>
and height. He used four Latin words<br>
which are; Cirrus (a lock of curly hair),<br>
<b>Cumulus (a heap or pile), Stratus (a</b><br>
layer), and Nimbus (the rain-bearing)<br>
(Table 6.2).<br>
<b>Howard compiled composite names</b><br>
using these four terms, such as<br>
cumulonimbus and cirrostratus, and<br>
added a prefix “alto” for middle level<br>
clouds. However, according to the<br>
<b>International Clouds Atlas of the</b><br>
<b>World Meteorological Organization</b><br>
(ICAWMO), there are ten types of<br>
clouds which are grouped into four (4)<br>
families as presented below.<br>

clouds<br>

<b>On the basis of their form, clouds</b><br>
are grouped into two major classes:<br>
stratiform (layered) clouds, and<br>
cumuliform (globular) clouds. These<br>
are described below:<br>
<b>Stratiform clouds</b><br>
<b>These are blanket-like clouds which</b><br>
cover a large area. Stratus may cover<br>
the entire sky. They occur when the moist<br>
and rising air continue to rise, leading<br>
to the formation of dense thick clouds<br>
which produce abundant rain or snow.<br>
<b>Cumuliform clouds</b><br>
<b>They are globular masses of clouds</b><br>
that are associated with small and large<br>
parcels of rising air. They occur when<br>
warmed rising air cools adiabatically,<br>
leading to condensation. Sometimes<br>
the upward movement yields dense tall<br>
clouds that produce thunderstorms.<br>
<b>Condensation</b><br>
<b>This is the process by which water</b><br>
vapour in the atmosphere is changed into<br>
a liquid after being cooled to and beyond<br>
dew point. Condensation is determined<br>
by conditions such as presence of<br>
water vapour, presence of hygroscopic<br>
nuclei (particles) and cooling process.<br>
<b>Therefore, condensation occurs in the</b><br>
following ways;<br>
<b>Radiation cooling occurs when the</b><br>
ground loses heat rapidly through<br>
terrestrial radiation and the air in contact<br>
with it is then cooled by conduction. If<br>
the air is moist, some vapour condenses<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   353</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
354<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
to form radiation fog and dew. If the<br>
temperature is below freezing point, frost<br>
is formed.<br>
Advection cooling occurs when warm,<br>
moist air moves over a cooler land or<br>
sea surface. Whereas, advection involves<br>
horizontal movement of air mass from<br>
one region to another, thus, the amount<br>
of condensation created is limited.<br>
Orographic uplift occurs when warm,<br>
moist air is forced to rise as it crosses<br>
a mountain barrier. As warm air rises<br>
it quickly cools down raise relative<br>
humidity and clouds.<br>
<b>Convective or adiabatic cooling occurs</b><br>
when air is warmed during the daytime<br>
and rises in pockets as thermals. As the<br>
air expands, it uses energy and loses heat,<br>
and consequently, the temperature drops.<br>
<b>Because air is cooled by the reduction</b><br>
of pressure with height rather than by<br>
loss of heat to the surrounding air, it<br>
is said to be adiabatically cooled. This<br>
kind of cooling results into formation<br>
of thunderstorms and convectional<br>
rainfall especially in equatorial and<br>
tropical regions. Since both orographic<br>
and adiabatic cooling involve vertical<br>
movement of air, they are more effective<br>
mechanism of condensation.<br>
<b>Frontal cooling. This occurs when two air</b><br>
masses with contrasting characteristics<br>
of humidity and temperature meet.<br>
<b>Normally the cold dry air is forced to</b><br>
pass below the warm humid air and<br>
exchange of heat occurs whereby the<br>
warm air mass loses heat and cools.<br>
<b>Condensation manifestation</b><br>
Condensation manifests itself as dew,<br>
frost, fog or as clouds. Dew is water<br>
in the form of droplets that appear on<br>
thin, exposed objects in the morning<br>
or evening due to condensation. It<br>
constitutes tiny drops of water that<br>
form on cool surfaces at night, when<br>
atmospheric vapour condenses. Dew<br>
forms when the temperature of the<br>
surfaces falls below the dew point of the<br>
surrounding air, usually due to radiation<br>
cooling. Dew point is the temperature<br>
at which the water vapour in the air at a<br>
constant pressure condense into liquid.<br>
<b>The higher the dew point rise the greater</b><br>
the amount of moisture in the air. Frost<br>
is a deposit of small white ice crystals<br>
formed on the ground or other surfaces<br>
when the temperature falls below<br>
freezing point. In temperate climate,<br>
it commonly appears as fragile white<br>
crystals of frozen dew drops near the<br>
ground, but in cold climates it occurs<br>
in a wider variety of forms.<br>
<b>Fog and mist are collection of liquid</b><br>
water droplets or ice crystals suspended<br>
in the air at or near the earth’s surface.<br>
<b>They constitute a cloud layer at or</b><br>
very close to the earth’s surface. They<br>
form when the difference between air<br>
temperature and dew point is generally<br>
less than 2.5o C. They mostly begin to<br>
form when water vapour condenses into<br>
tinny liquid water droplet suspended<br>
in the air. The term fog is used when<br>
visibility is less than 1 km, while mist<br>
is used when visibility extends from 1<br>
to 2 km. Fogs are formed under different<br>

atmospheric conditions near the ground.<br>
<b>Based on the genesis fog can be classified</b><br>
as; Radiation fog (ground fog) formed<br>
at night when the temperature of the air<br>
layer at the ground level falls below the<br>
dew point. It is associated with a low<br>
level temperature inversion. Advection<br>
fog which occurs when a warm moist air<br>
current is cooled as it moves horizontally<br>
over a cold land or sea surface. As the<br>
warm air layer loses heat to the surface,<br>
its temperature drops below the dew<br>
point and the condensation sets in. It<br>
commonly occurs over oceans where<br>
warm and cold currents occur side by side.<br>
<b>Steam fog (sea fog) which is generally</b><br>
more localized than the two main types.<br>
<b>It usually develops in situations where</b><br>
cold air blows over much warmer<br>
waters. Evaporated air from the water<br>
body quickly cools and the resulting<br>
condensation is seen as a steaming.<br>
<b>Frontal fog (precipitation fog) forms</b><br>
when two air masses of contrasting<br>
temperature and humidity converge<br>
cooling process of the warm moist air<br>
which causes fog formation among<br>
other things. It mostly occurs when<br>
precipitation falls into drier air below<br>
the cloud as the liquid droplets evaporate<br>
into water vapour. The water vapour<br>
cools, and at the dew point it condenses<br>
and fog forms. Hill fog occurs when<br>
the passage of a front cloud extends<br>
down to the surface. This is especially<br>
the case over higher ground. Smog fog<br>
is a yellowish or blackish fog formed<br>
mainly by a mixture of pollutants in the<br>
atmosphere, consisting of fine particles<br>
and gases such as CO2 and SO2. Smog<br>
fog is commonly found in cities and<br>
industrial areas, such as the Ruhr<br>
industrial region in Germany.<br>
<b>Major environmental hazards caused</b><br>
by fog<br>
<b>Fog may cause significant environmental</b><br>
hazards. In industrialized countries,<br>
dense fog on high speed highways<br>
can cause chain reaction accidents,<br>
injury or death. Fog brings travelling<br>
inconvenience to thousands of travellers<br>
in a single day, as it makes them move<br>
in very low speed. Sometimes fog leads<br>
to vehicle collision along the road and<br>
ship collision in the sea due to very poor<br>
visibility. Moreover, polluted fog has side<br>
effects to the human health for example,<br>
it is one among factors that increases the<br>
incidences of asthma and allergies cases.<br>
<b>Smog also affects the climate through its</b><br>
cooling effect. Since fog contains SO2<br>
as one of its components, it causes acid<br>
rain. The aviation industry is affected by<br>
the severity of an existing fog condition.<br>
<b>During extreme fog conditions, safe</b><br>
landings are impossible and airports<br>
are closed until fog conditions have<br>
improved.<br>
<b>Precipitation</b><br>
<b>Precipitation is any liquid or solid</b><br>
condensation of water vapour falling<br>
from the atmosphere to the surface<br>
of the Earth. Precipitation in warmer<br>
clouds is formed when fine water<br>
droplets condense, collide and coalesce<br>
into larger droplets that fall as rain.<br>

09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
356<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>In colder clouds, ice crystals form</b><br>
and grow in a cloud that contains a<br>
mixture of both ice crystals and water<br>
droplets.<br>
<b>Formation of precipitation</b><br>
<b>Precipitation forms in the clouds when</b><br>
water vapour condenses and grow<br>
bigger enough to form water droplets.<br>
<b>When the drops grow in size, they</b><br>
become heavier enough and drop to<br>
the ground as precipitation. If a cloud<br>
is colder, like it would be at higher<br>
altitudes, the water droplets may freeze<br>
to form ice. Precipitation includes all<br>
forms of water particles that follow<br>
onto the ground. These particles<br>
then fall to the ground as snow, sleet,<br>
drizzle, hail or rain depending on the<br>
temperature within the cloud and at the<br>
earth’s surface.<br>
<b>Forms of Precipitation</b><br>
<b>Rain is a liquid in the form of droplets</b><br>
whose size is greater than 0.50 mm.<br>
<b>It occurs when condensation process</b><br>
takes place at or just above the freezing<br>
point. Snow is a form of precipitation<br>
which is formed when water vapour<br>
condenses at a temperature below<br>
freezing point, passing directly from<br>
gaseous to the solid state forming<br>
minute particles of ice.  These particles<br>
are of hexagonal plates or prism like,<br>
and when condensation continues,<br>
they can unite to form snowflakes and<br>
reach the ground without melting. The<br>
formation of snow is similar to that of a<br>
hail but they differ in their appearance<br>
and occurrence.  For snow to fall, there<br>
must be both plentiful vapour in the<br>
atmosphere and low temperature to<br>
prevent melting of ice crystals. Snow<br>
may fall in any latitudes even on the<br>
equatorial mountains of East Africa<br>
and in the Andes. However, snow is<br>
rarely formed at the sea level about 30º<br>
<b>North and South of the Equator. The</b><br>
snow line indicates the lowest edge of<br>
snow cover and its position depends on<br>
the climatic and terrain factors.<br>
<b>Hail is formed when water droplets</b><br>
are carried upwards by the rising air<br>
currents to great height where they<br>
are super cooled and turned into ice<br>
crystals. Hails fall in the form of<br>
small spherical pellets of ice with a<br>
diameter of about 5 – 50 mm. Hails are<br>
associated with conditions of extreme<br>
atmospheric instability, and very low<br>
temperature leading to supper cooling<br>
and coalescence of water into ice or<br>
hail stones. When these ice crystals or<br>
hail stones fall, water vapour around<br>
them condenses into ice and becomes<br>
bigger. Sometimes these ice crystals<br>
are carried upward again by convection<br>
currents and the process is repeated to<br>
make them acquire more layers of ice.<br>
<b>Hails are almost always accompanied</b><br>
by thunderstorms.  They occur widely<br>
on the earth’s surface except in the<br>
polar regions where thunderstorms<br>
are rare, in the equatorial zones where<br>
the air temperature is so high, causing<br>
the hails to melt before they reach the<br>
ground, and in hot deserts.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   356</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
357<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Sleet forms in cold weather when</b><br>
rain enters a layer of very cold air<br>
close to the ground and then freezes.<br>
<b>They are a mixture of ice and snow</b><br>
formed when the upper air temperature<br>
is below freezing point, allowing<br>
snowflakes to form. They are formed in<br>
cumulonimbus clouds when the lower<br>
atmospheric temperature is around 2ºC<br>
to 4ºC. There are slight differences in<br>
the conceptualization of sleets between<br>
the American and the British. Whereas<br>
in Britain sleets are viewed as a mixture<br>
of snow and rain, in America they are<br>
regarded as the raindrops which have<br>
frozen and partially melted again.<br>
<b>Generally, sleets are the intermediate</b><br>
forms of precipitation.<br>
<b>Glaze is the reverse of the sleet and</b><br>
occurs when water droplets form in the<br>
upper air but turn into ice on contact with<br>
freezing surface. When glazed frost<br>
forms on roads, it is called “black ice”.<br>
<b>Dew is formed on cold surfaces during</b><br>
early morning hours, from moisture<br>
in air. This is one of the forms of<br>
condensation which occurs when water<br>
vapour (the invisible form of water) in<br>
the atmosphere changes into visible<br>
water droplets on cooling. Drizzle is a<br>
very fine rain with very small droplets.<br>
<b>It originates from layers of clouds with</b><br>
very limited vertical development,<br>
such as stratus and stratocumulus.<br>
<b>Mist is tiny liquid droplets dropping</b><br>
from low strata, whose intervisibility<br>
extends from 1 km to 2 km, usually<br>
formed when warm humid air cools<br>
suddenly forming small water droplets<br>
suspended in air. Unlike fogs, mist<br>
results in moderate visibility usually<br>
occurs in the lower atmosphere.<br>
<b>Rainfall</b><br>
<b>Rainfall is formed when water droplets</b><br>
condense from water vapour in the<br>
atmosphere on a nuclei and then float as<br>
clouds. When these droplets coalesce,<br>
they normally form larger droplets<br>
which later fall as rain after overcoming<br>
ascending air by gravity. Rainfall is<br>
measured by using an instrument called<br>
raingauge. It is expressed in millimeters<br>
(mm) or centimeters (cm). Most air<br>
in the atmosphere contains minute<br>
particles of matter called nuclei which<br>
are made of salts, dust, ice and soot<br>
(carbon derived from burnt vegetable<br>
matter). When raindrops continue to<br>
grow around these nuclei, they become<br>
heavy and fall on the ground as drizzle<br>
or rain. Although the temperature at the<br>
level of clouds formation in atmosphere<br>
is below 0ºC, the droplets do not turn<br>
into ice crystals. In cumulonimbus, for<br>
example, raindrops often rise and fall<br>
several times in the convection currents<br>
before they have sufficient weight to<br>
fall to the ground. Sometimes the super<br>
cooled water droplets crystalize into<br>
ice crystals and then fall to the ground<br>
before melting into raindrops. When<br>
they reach the ground in form of drops<br>
of ice they are called hail.<br>
<b>Types of rainfall</b><br>
<b>Rainfall is classified into three main</b><br>
types based on the origin. These<br>

includes convectional, orographic and<br>
cyclonic rainfall.<br>
<b>Convectional rainfall</b><br>
<b>Convectional rainfall is a type of rainfall</b><br>
which is formed by vertical moving<br>
warm air, which has been heated by the<br>
ground through conduction. As air rises<br>
from the heated surface, it expands<br>
and thus cools adiabatically. Thus,<br>
forms stratocumulus or cumulonimbus<br>
clouds with very powerful turbulent<br>
currents. This results in a very strong<br>
convectional rainfall accompanied by<br>
thunder and lightning. Convectional<br>
rainfall occurs throughout the year<br>
near the Equator, due to constant<br>
high<br>
temperatures<br>
and<br>
humidity.<br>
convectional rainfall is usually in the<br>
summer or the hotter part of the day.<br>
<b>Such rainfall is main associated  with</b><br>
the hail and graupel. The equitorial<br>
region and internal part of continents<br>
mainly in northern hemisphere receive<br>
convectional rainfall (Figure 6.15).<br>

<b>Orographic rainfall</b><br>
<b>This type of rainfall is also known</b><br>
as relief rainfall.  It develops as a<br>
result of the influence of topography,<br>
specifically mountain range. It occurs<br>
when air mass carrying moisture<br>
is forced to rise on the side of the<br>
mountain range. Orographic rainfall is<br>
common where onshore winds rise-up<br>
over hilly or in mountain regions that<br>
are at right angles to the direction of<br>
the winds. This kind of rainfall occurs<br>
in areas where the surface of the Earth<br>
is greatly heated by the solar radiation<br>
in all latitudes.<br>
<b>Orographic rainfall is common in areas</b><br>
where hills lie parallel to the coast over<br>
which moist winds from the sea blow.<br>
<b>Because this rainfall is caused by the</b><br>
rising air over one side of the mountain,<br>
it makes only one side of the mountain<br>
to receive relatively much rainfall. The<br>
side receiving orographic rainfall is<br>
called the windward side, while the side<br>
of the mountain that receive relatively<br>
little rainfall is called the leeward side<br>
or rain-shadow. As the air ascends the<br>
mountain ranges it is cooled at dry<br>
adiabatic rate. This cooling air mass<br>
contains moisture from water bodies<br>
and evapotranspiration from down<br>
mountain vegetation. The air continues<br>
to rise at the saturated adiabatic lapse<br>
rate until dew point is reached. This<br>
cooling releases latent heat which<br>
makes the atmosphere more unstable,<br>
forcing the air to continue rising until<br>
condensation takes place, leading to<br>
the formation of stratocumulus and<br>

further rising cumulonimbus clouds<br>
(Figure 6.16).<br>

<b>Cyclonic rainfall</b><br>
<b>This type of rainfall occurs along the</b><br>
frontal zones of convergence, notably<br>
at the Inter-Tropical Convergence<br>
Zone (ITCZ) and at the polar front.<br>
<b>This type of rainfall develops when</b><br>
patterns of conveying air at ITCZ under<br>
prevailing warm and humid conditions<br>
cause the formation of massive<br>
cumulonimbus clouds with torrential<br>
rain and thunderstorms. Normally,<br>
cyclonic rainfall occurs when air<br>
masses of different characteristics<br>
and strength meet and mix their air.<br>
<b>It develops when warm moist air of</b><br>
a maritime (Tm) tropical air mass is<br>
forced to rise over the cold air of polar<br>
air mass (Pm) when two air masses<br>
meets (Figure 6.17). Cyclonic rainfall<br>
is sometimes called depression rainfall<br>
because it occurs where different air<br>
masses of different characteristics<br>
meet and form a depression wind (low<br>
pressure cell). Depression rainfall in<br>
temperate latitudes is usually lighter<br>
than convection rain and it takes much<br>
longer duration of up to several days.<br>
<b>Frontal rainfall occurs in the mid and</b><br>
high latitudes between 40º – 60º North<br>
and South of the Equator. These areas<br>
are convergence zones of both warm<br>
moist and cold dense air.<br>
<b>Condensation</b><br>
<b>Warm moist air rises</b><br>
expands and cools<br>
<b>Cold</b><br>
dry air<br>
descends<br>
earth’s surface<br>

<b>Front</b><br>

<b>Thunderstorm</b><br>
<b>Thunderstorm is an event in which the</b><br>
intense rain and violent winds coupled<br>
with lightning and thunder occur. It refers<br>
to sound produced when two different air<br>
masses meet abruptly. Before the sound<br>
is head, light is seen first because it travel<br>
first than sound. Therefore, thunderstorm<br>
is an intense local storm associated with a<br>
tall, dense cumulonimbus cloud in which<br>
there are very strong updrafts of air. It<br>
is accompanied with lightning (great<br>
electric) with a series of gigantic sparks<br>
passing between different charged parts<br>
of the cloud mass or between clouds<br>
and the ground. The development of<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   359</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

360<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
convectional overturning under extreme<br>
condition of instability may produce<br>
thunderstorms (Figure 6.18). This results<br>
as the towering cumulonimbus clouds<br>
advance across the sky characterized<br>
by rapid fall in pressure producing a<br>
very turbulent wind before storms<br>
develop. This leads to heavy rain or<br>
hail falling as clouds passing overhead<br>
accompanied by lightning and thunder.<br>
<b>As the hot and expanding air rises from</b><br>
the surface, it absorbs a large amount of<br>
moisture. Huge amounts of energy are<br>
released when condensation of this warm<br>
moist air takes place. This provides the<br>
energy to power thunderstorms that are<br>
frequently associated with convectional<br>
rainfalls and hails.<br>
<b>Thunderstorms can occur whenever land</b><br>
surfaces are greatly overheated. They are<br>
common in humid tropical regions. They<br>
usually occur in the afternoon when the<br>
land is heated by the overhead solar<br>
radiation. Thunders are caused by the<br>
rapid expansion and contraction of the<br>
air resulting from electrical discharges<br>
that generate intense heat and pressure, a<br>
common feature of convectional rainfall.<br>
<b>During a lightning discharge, a current</b><br>
of as much as 60 000-100 000 amperes<br>
may develop. The current heats the air<br>
intensely, causing it to expand very<br>
rapidly like an explosion which sends out<br>
sound waves, recognized as thunderclap.<br>
<b>Most lightning discharges occur within</b><br>
the cloud but a significant proportion<br>
strike the ground. For example, in USA<br>
alone, lightning causes a yearly average<br>
of about 150 human deaths and property<br>
damage of hundreds of millions of<br>
dollars. It also causes forest fires.<br>

<b>Factors affecting the distribution of</b><br>
rainfall<br>
<b>Rainfall distribution across regions</b><br>
varies considerably due to a number<br>
of factors including; the distance from<br>
the equator, altitude, ocean currents,<br>
the prevailing winds both onshore and<br>
offshore winds, the nature and shape<br>
of the continent, as well as lack or<br>
presence of water bodies which are<br>
evaporating surfaces.<br>
