<b>Atmospheric pressure</b><br>
<b>Air has weight and therefore, exerts</b><br>
pressure on the earth’s surface called<br>
atmospheric pressure. The atmospheric<br>
pressure is measured by an instrument<br>
called barometer. Actually, 1 bar = 1000<br>
millibars.  The line that joins different<br>
places with the same atmospheric<br>
pressure in the map is called an<br>
isobar. The isobar indicates the<br>
horizontal distribution of the pressure<br>
in a certain region. The pressure of a<br>
particular place is indicated as high<br>
or low, depending on the pressure of<br>
the adjoining areas. The atmospheric<br>
pressure is not the same to all regions,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   361</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
362<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
nor the same to one region all the<br>
time. This means that in some regions,<br>
the pressure is higher for one part of<br>
the year than it is for another part of<br>
the year. The variation of pressure in<br>
different regions is a result of a number<br>
of factors which include;<br>
<b>Altitude</b><br>
<b>Air pressure at sea level is higher than</b><br>
it is at the top of a mountain.  This<br>
is because at the sea level air has to<br>
support a greater weight of air than it<br>
does on the top of a mountain (Figure<br>
6.19). The earth’s atmosphere consists<br>
of different materials in all states of<br>
matter such as gaseous, water vapour<br>
and solid. These materials exert a<br>
downforce (atmospheric pressure) in<br>
the strength of which depends on the<br>
elevation. Normally, highly elevated<br>
parts of the Earth are with high<br>
atmospheric pressure as opposed to<br>
lowly elevated areas where the pressure<br>
is low. Pressure tends to develop at a<br>
rate of 10 mb to every 100 m of rising.<br>
<b>Molecules get</b><br>
closer together<br>
and outward<br>
pressure increase<br>
<b>High pressure</b><br>
<b>Low pressure</b><br>
<b>High pressure</b><br>
<b>Low pressure</b><br>
<b>Molecules get</b><br>
further apart and<br>
outward pressure<br>
increases<br>
<b>Figure 6.19: Influence of altitude on pressure</b><br>
<b>The molecules of the air at sea level</b><br>
push outwards with a force equal<br>
to that exerted by the air above it,<br>
whereas the molecules of the air at the<br>
top of mountain push outwards, with<br>
much less force because the weight of<br>
the air above it is less. This explains<br>
why air pressure increases when air<br>
descends. When air descends, its<br>
volume decreases but the number of<br>
molecules in it remains the same. The<br>
outward pressure of these molecules is<br>
spread over a smaller area and pressure<br>
increases. Similarly, when air rises,<br>
its volume increases and the outward<br>
pressure of its molecules is spread over<br>
a larger area eventually, its pressure<br>
decreases.<br>
<b>Temperature</b><br>
<b>When air sinks, its pressure increases</b><br>
because it becomes compressed. When<br>
air becomes compressed its molecules<br>
move more quickly and heat is<br>
produced. Apparently, temperature of<br>
air rises its pressure falls as well. When<br>
air rises, its pressure decreases because<br>
it expands, but when air expands, its<br>
molecules move more slowly, and<br>
heat is used up. The temperature of<br>
air falls when its pressure falls. When<br>
air is heated, it expands and when this<br>
happens the outward pressure of its<br>
molecules is spread over a larger area.<br>
<b>This means that the pressure of the air</b><br>
decreases. The pressure of the air falls<br>
when its temperature rises. When air<br>
is cooled it contracts and when this<br>
happens the outward pressure of its<br>
molecules is spread over a smaller<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   362</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
363<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
area. This means that the pressure of<br>
the air increases. The pressure of the<br>
air rises when its temperature falls.<br>
<b>Low temperature at the poles causes</b><br>
the air to contract, hence high pressure<br>
develops. High temperature along the<br>
equator causes the air to expand, hence<br>
low pressure called the doldrums low<br>
pressure, develops.<br>
<b>Rotation</b><br>
<b>The rotation of the Earth causes the</b><br>
air mass at the poles to be thrown<br>
away towards the equator, occupying<br>
large area in the mid latitudes (sub-<br>
polar) which develop a low pressure.<br>
<b>However, pressure remains high in</b><br>
the polar areas due to extremely low<br>
temperature. Again, the rotation of the<br>
<b>Earth throws air mass from equator</b><br>
polarwards, which then descends in<br>
the horse latitude leading to creation of<br>
high pressure as air is forced to occupy a<br>
small area compared to its original area.<br>
<b>Nature of the surface</b><br>
<b>The surface of the Earth is normally</b><br>
uneven with different landforms and<br>
water bodies. This causes the behaviour<br>
of temperature to vary from one area to<br>
another. Normally, land and water heat<br>
and cool at different rates, affecting the<br>
temperature of air masses occupying<br>
them. The variation in heating and<br>
cooling rates of the land and water<br>
masses on the earth surface cause<br>
variations in pressure. This causes<br>
some areas to have high pressure or low<br>
pressure. This is because temperature<br>
change causes either expansion or<br>
contraction of air-mass, which in turn<br>
affects air pressure. The variation of<br>
heating and cooling between land<br>
surface and water surface leads to local<br>
pressure variation which in turn results<br>
in land breeze and sea breeze.<br>
<b>Latitude</b><br>
<b>The Earth is not a perfect sphere and</b><br>
therefore the force of gravity varies<br>
according to latitudes, attaining its<br>
maximum gravity at the poles and<br>
its minimum gravity at the Equator.<br>
<b>Atmospheric pressure is therefore</b><br>
lower at the equator and higher at<br>
the poles because temperature at the<br>
<b>Equator is high causing low pressure</b><br>
cells while at the poles temperature is<br>
low causing high pressure cells. Also,<br>
high gravitational pull at polar regions<br>
results into high atmospheric pressure<br>
because the weight of atmospheric<br>
contents increases with the increase in<br>
gravitational pull.<br>
<b>Planetary pressure systems</b><br>
<b>The planetary pressure system is</b><br>
a theoretical concept which helps<br>
to understand the distribution and<br>
patterns of pressure on the surface.<br>
<b>When the pressure of the air mass</b><br>
is low at the centre and increases<br>
outward, it is called depression. This<br>
is common in temperate latitude<br>
areas. Normally, anticyclones form<br>
the opposite of depression pressure<br>
cells in which the pressure increases<br>
towards the centre. The atmospheric<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   363</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
364<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
pressure varies with temperature: it is<br>
low in high temperature and high in<br>
low temperature. It also varies with<br>
altitude and latitude: it is low in high<br>
altitudes and latitudes, and high in low<br>
altitudes and latitudes. Due to the above<br>
variation, the atmospheric pressure can<br>
be categorized into four pressure zones<br>
or belts (Figure 6.20):<br>
<b>Equatorial trough</b><br>
<b>The equatorial trough also known as low</b><br>
pressure belts is an area lying between<br>
0º and 10º North and South of the<br>
<b>Equator. This is a region of low pressure</b><br>
zone with less than 1013 millibars. It is<br>
at the Equator where the northern and<br>
southern trade winds converge to form<br>
a zone called intertropical convergence<br>
zone (ITCZ). This area is characterized<br>
by high temperature and humidity, and<br>
the air near the sea level is stationary<br>
or moving very slowly.  This belt is<br>
commonly known as the Doldrums.<br>
<b>The low pressure in this area is</b><br>
due to heating as the area receives<br>
high insolation which causes the<br>
temperature to increase. The increase<br>
in temperature causes the volume of<br>
air pressure to decrease.  This change<br>
in pressuring along the equatorial belt<br>
is actually a generalization since there<br>
are also local variations.<br>
<b>Sub-tropical high pressure belt</b><br>
<b>Sub-tropical high pressure belt also</b><br>
known as horse latitudes occurs along<br>
the calm and descending air currents.<br>
<b>It is a zone or belt of descending air</b><br>
mass produced dynamically by wind<br>
of the upper atmosphere lying between<br>
30º North and South of the Equator.<br>
<b>In the northern hemisphere it is called</b><br>
<b>North sub-tropical high pressure belt</b><br>
and in the southern hemisphere it<br>
is known a South sub-tropical high<br>
pressure belt. It is difficult to describe<br>
these belts but they can generally be<br>
caused by two factors. Firstly, they can<br>
be caused by general circulation of air<br>
towards the poles in the upper part of<br>
the troposphere from the Equator. They<br>
are influenced by the earth’s rotation<br>
whereby the centrifugal force throws<br>
the air back, causing the currents to<br>
accumulate along these latitudes.<br>
<b>Secondly, they can be caused by</b><br>
movement of air from high latitudes<br>
in the upper part of the troposphere<br>
towards the Equator. These air masses<br>
descend in the horse latitude and they<br>
increase air accumulation along the<br>
lower latitude areas.<br>
<b>Sub-polar low pressure belts</b><br>
<b>Sub-polar low pressure belts also known</b><br>
as temperate low pressure belts are<br>
found between 40º and 70º North and<br>
<b>South of the Equator where the rotation</b><br>
of the Earth causes a polar whirl which<br>
moves towards the low pressure zones<br>
at the poles. However, the intensity of<br>
the coldness around the poles causes<br>
the thermal effects to overcome these<br>
air masses. Consequently, this places<br>
these pressure belts around or near the<br>
polar circles.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   364</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
365<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Polar high pressure belts</b><br>
<b>This occurs along the Antarctic ice cap</b><br>
above which the intensely cold dense<br>
air with a shallow high pressure system<br>
over it lies. This belt lies towards the<br>
pole between 70º North and South<br>
of the Equator. It is produced by the<br>
constant low temperature over the polar<br>
ice caps with low temperature causing<br>
high polar pressure cells. However,<br>
this region is complicated since it<br>
is an ocean basin with its landmass<br>
grouped in about two thirds of its<br>
perimeter. Generally, the distribution<br>
of the pressure zones over the earth<br>
surface has a considerable effect on the<br>
formation of winds and wind patterns.<br>
<b>Winds normally move from high</b><br>
pressure areas to low pressure areas<br>
in an attempt to balance the pressure.<br>
<b>This process is called gradient pressure</b><br>
winds.  The wind that blows towards the<br>
centre of low pressure cell, especially<br>
in mid latitude is called depression<br>
wind (Figure 6.20).<br>

<b>Exercise 6.5</b><br>
1.	 Explain the factors affecting<br>
atmospheric pressure.<br>
2.	 Using<br>
annotated<br>
diagram,<br>
describe the planetary pressure<br>
systems.<br>
3.	 The distribution of the pressure<br>
zones over the earth’s surface<br>
has a considerable effect on the<br>
formation of wind. Discuss.<br>
<b>Wind</b><br>
<b>Wind is the air in motion which blows</b><br>
from an area of high pressure to the<br>
area of low pressure. Wind results<br>
from differences in air pressure caused<br>
by differences in temperature and force<br>
exerted by gravity as pressure decrease<br>
rapidly with height. An increase<br>
in temperature causes air to warm,<br>
expand, become less dense and rise,<br>
creating an area of low pressure below.<br>
<b>Conversely, a drop of temperature</b><br>
produces an area of high pressure.<br>
<b>Winds are characterized with specific</b><br>
origin, direction, and velocity.<br>
<b>Factors affecting speed, direction and</b><br>
the strength of wind<br>
<b>Pressure gradient</b><br>
<b>Pressure gradient is also known as</b><br>
barometric gradient. Regions of high<br>
pressure form centres from which<br>
winds tend to blow, that is, they are<br>
areas<br>
of<br>
divergence.<br>
Conversely,<br>
regions of low pressure are foci of<br>
winds, which means that they are areas<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   365</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
366<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
of convergence. However, when the<br>
difference in pressure between two<br>
neighbouring points on the earth’s<br>
surface is great, it is said to be a steep<br>
pressure gradient, and on the pressure<br>
map, the isobars appear close to each<br>
other. In such a case, the resulting<br>
wind is strong. Isobars and pressure<br>
maps are thus analogous to contours<br>
and relief maps. Conversely, when the<br>
barometric gradient is gentle, winds<br>
are light, causing air to move from<br>
areas of high pressure to areas of low<br>
pressure. The succeeding factors,<br>
however, considerably modify this<br>
generalization (Figure 6.21).<br>

<b>Coriolis force</b><br>
<b>The effect of Coriolis force is produced</b><br>
by the rotation of the earth. It may be<br>
clearly stated in terms of Ferrell’s<br>
<b>Law. The Ferrell’s Law states that a</b><br>
free body moving on the surface of<br>
the earth is deflected to its own right<br>
in the Northern hemisphere and to its<br>
left in the Southern hemisphere as a<br>
result of the earth’s rotation. Deflection<br>
is stronger near the poles and decreases<br>
to zero at the Equator. When a balanced<br>
condition develops between the forces<br>
exerted by the pressure gradient in<br>
one direction and the Coriolis force in<br>
the opposite direction, a steady wind<br>
will blow. This is known as a state<br>
of geostrophic balance, and the air<br>
movement is known as geostrophic flow<br>
or geostrophic wind. The wind blows<br>
in the direction parallel to the straight<br>
isobars. The Coriolis force affects both<br>
direction, speed, and strength of winds<br>
(Figure. 6.22).<br>

1400 km/hr<br>

<b>Wind deflected</b><br>

<b>Centrifugal forces</b><br>
<b>Centrifugal force is the force acting</b><br>
outwards from the centre of curvature.<br>
<b>When an air-stream moves on a curved</b><br>
course, as in a pressure system with<br>
closed or curved isobars, it is subjected<br>
to centrifugal force. The air thus,<br>
tends to travel along the isobars, in<br>
the Northern hemisphere clockwise,<br>
around the highs and anti-clockwise<br>
around the lows, while in the Southern<br>
hemisphere it would tend to move<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   366</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
367<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
anticlockwise around the highs and<br>
clockwise around the lows. This<br>
relationship of wind and atmospheric<br>
pressure was put-forward by a Dutch<br>
<b>Scientist, Buys Ballot in the middle of</b><br>
the 19thC.<br>
<b>Frictional forces</b><br>
In the lowest parts of the atmosphere,<br>
normally below 750m, the frictional<br>
drag exerted by the ground on the<br>
airflow above, has an effect on the<br>
balance of the other wind forces. This<br>
allows the pressure gradient to assert<br>
its greater strength by causing the air to<br>
flow more towards low pressure. Thus,<br>
the usual situation is that surface winds<br>
flow at a slight angle to the isobars near<br>
the ground. However, friction tends to<br>
reduce the power of the deflection due<br>
to rotation, and thus its effect is greater<br>
over land than over the oceans and less<br>
in increasing altitude.<br>
<b>Air masses</b><br>
<b>An air mass is a large body of air (parcel</b><br>
of air) with fairly uniform characteristics<br>
of temperature and moisture covering<br>
an extensive surface area moving<br>
horizontally over several kilometers.<br>
<b>Air masses only develop over an area</b><br>
which is extensive and which is uniform<br>
in size and shape, for example a desert<br>
surface or an ocean surface.<br>
<b>General characteristics of air masses</b><br>
<b>The characteristics of an air mass</b><br>
are obtained from the region where<br>
it forms. These characteristics are<br>
retained by the air mass when it moves<br>
away, even to considerable distances<br>
from its origin. The following are the<br>
characteristics of air mass;<br>
<b>Firstly, air masses are born from the</b><br>
extended homogeneous earth’s surface<br>
like ocean, desert surface, and over<br>
the forest. Secondly, air masses are<br>
always mobile, which accounts for<br>
their thermal and hydromodification.<br>
<b>Thirdly, air masses are carriers of</b><br>
amount of heat, which depends on the<br>
nature of their source regions. Lastly,<br>
direction of air masses is dependent of<br>
barometric gradient.<br>
<b>Major types and classification of air</b><br>
masses<br>
<b>Air masses are classified basing on two</b><br>
main approaches which are geographical<br>
classification (according to their origin),<br>
and thermodynamic classification.<br>
<b>Geographical classification considers</b><br>
features of the source regions. Basing<br>
on the geographical classification, there<br>
are two broad categories of air mass.<br>
<b>The first category is Polar air mass (P)</b><br>
which originates in Polar areas and<br>
<b>Arctic air masses which originates from</b><br>
arctic region. The second category is<br>
<b>Tropical air mass (T) which originates</b><br>
in tropical areas. Equatorial air masses<br>
are also included in this category. They<br>
are further divided into two types on<br>
the basis of the nature of the surface of<br>
the source regions (whether continental<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   367</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
368<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
or oceanic areas). Basing on the above<br>
fact, air masses are classified into four<br>
principal types, namely Polar continental<br>
air mass, Polar maritime air masses,<br>
<b>Tropical continental air mass, and</b><br>
Tropical maritime air mass.<br>
<b>Polar Maritime (Pm)</b><br>
<b>This is a type of cold moist air mass</b><br>
originating at high latitude in Polar<br>
region. It is characterized by cold and<br>
moist air especially in winter, low<br>
temperature and high moisture content.<br>
It is also featured by light clouds, snow,<br>
and moderate rainfall. It is heated by<br>
warm water bodies. The heating makes<br>
it unstable and consequently causes<br>
blustery showers over the oceans.<br>
<b>Polar Continental (Pc)</b><br>
<b>This is a type of cold dry air mass</b><br>
originating at high latitude over land<br>
area. It includes cold and dry air masses<br>
with low moisture content. It is stable<br>
due to low temperature but dry and<br>
warm in summer. It brings the cold,<br>
dry, and clear weather on a winter day.<br>
<b>Tropical Maritime (Tm)</b><br>
<b>This type of warm moist air mass</b><br>
originates in warm waters of the tropics.<br>
<b>It is characterized by high temperature</b><br>
and high moisture content. It is unstable<br>
due to high temperature, and moisture<br>
content. It brings overcast skies with<br>
prolonged rains, thunderstorms, and<br>
haze of dust. Sometimes it produces<br>
warm weather with abundant sunshine.<br>
<b>Tropical Continental (Tc)</b><br>
<b>This is a type of warm dry air mass</b><br>
originating from sub-tropical and<br>
desert regions, especially in summer. It<br>
is warm and very dry (high temperature<br>
and low moisture contents). It is also<br>
unstable due to high temperature,<br>
resulting in thick clouds and thundery<br>
showers. It causes poor visibility<br>
due to picking of air pollutants in<br>
<b>Europe or dust storms in the Saharan</b><br>
deserts.<br>
<b>Equatorial maritime air mass is</b><br>
sometimes regarded to be very moist<br>
and warm air masses originating within<br>
a few degrees of the Equator. According<br>
to thermal dynamic characteristics, air<br>
masses are categorized basing on heating<br>
or cooling from below while passing<br>
through different surfaces away from<br>
the source region. It considers initial<br>
characteristics of the air mass which<br>
includes; temperature and moisture<br>
content; nature of land or water surface of<br>
over which a particular air mass moves;<br>
path followed by the air mass from the<br>
source region; and time taken by air<br>
mass to reach a particular destination.<br>
<b>Therefore, this classification gives out</b><br>
two types of air masses which are cold<br>
air mass which originates in the polar<br>
and arctic regions and warm air mass<br>
which originates in the subtropical<br>
regions characterized by anticyclonic<br>
conditions.<br>

<b>Fronts</b><br>
<b>When air masses meet, they do not mix</b><br>
readily due to differences in temperature<br>
and density.  The point at which they<br>
meet is called a front. Therefore, a<br>
front can be defined as a point at which<br>
two air masses of different temperature<br>
and density meet. Based on differences<br>
in temperature and density, fronts can<br>
be grouped into two major types which<br>
are; cold front and warm front (Figure<br>
6.23), others include occluded front<br>
and stationary front.<br>
<b>Cold front is formed when cold air</b><br>
invades warm air. Since the colder air<br>
mass is denser, it remains in contact<br>
with the ground as it moves forward,<br>
it forces the warm air mass to rise<br>
above it. If the warm air is unstable,<br>
severe thunderstorms may develop.<br>
<b>Thunderstorms near a cold front often</b><br>
form a long line of massive clouds<br>
stretching out tens of kilometres.<br>
<b>Something to be noted is that, cold air</b><br>
is always dense than warm air, so when<br>
a warm air mass meets a cold air mass,<br>
the warm air will always ascend while<br>
the cold one will sink. Once the warm<br>
air has risen, it cools and clouds can<br>
form.<br>
<b>Warm front is formed when warm air</b><br>
is advancing and replaces the cold<br>
air, where a warm air mass is forced<br>
to rise over the cold air and the cold<br>
air masses remain in contact with the<br>
ground because it is denser. The rising<br>
motion of air causes stratus clouds to<br>
form and often precipitation follows. If<br>
the warm air is stable, the precipitation<br>
will be steady, but if the warm air is<br>
unstable, convection cells can develop,<br>
producing<br>
cumulonimbus<br>
clouds<br>
with heavy showers or thunderstorm.<br>
<b>Also warm front can be explained as</b><br>
boundaries between air masses when<br>
warm air invades cold air.<br>
<b>Warm air</b><br>
<b>Warm air</b><br>

<b>Occluded front occurs when a warm front</b><br>
is overtaken by a cold front, the warm air<br>
is pushed away and it is unable to contact<br>
the ground. When this happens, abrupt<br>
lifting by the denser cold air produces<br>
precipitation (Figure 6.24).<br>

<b>Stationary front occurs when two air</b><br>
masses are in contact but with little or<br>
no relative motion. Often a stationary<br>
front arises when a cold or warm front<br>
stalls and ceases forward motion.<br>
<b>Clouds and precipitation often remain</b><br>
in the vicinity of the front. The most<br>
notable front is the polar front which<br>
occurs when warm moist (Tm) air<br>
meets colder drier (Pm) air and it is at<br>
the polar front depression or cyclones<br>
(Figure 6.25).<br>

<b>Planetary wind systems</b><br>
<b>Wind is the movement of air mass from</b><br>
high to low pressure areas in a horizontal<br>
plane. It is principally influenced by<br>
unevenly heating of the earth’s surface<br>
by the sun which causes the pressure<br>
differences and the difference in the<br>
atmospheric pressure. Although the<br>
distribution of pressure zones over the<br>
earth’s surface has the considerable<br>
effects on the formation of winds on<br>
the earth’s surface, its direction and<br>
strength depends on pressure gradient,<br>
<b>Coriolis</b><br>
force,<br>
centrifugal<br>
force,<br>
and friction as discussed earlier. The<br>
measurement and recording of winds<br>
involves both the direction from which<br>
they blow and their velocity. A wind-<br>
vane is used to measure the direction of<br>
the wind while an anemometer is used<br>
to measure wind velocity.<br>
<b>Observation of wind direction and</b><br>
velocity is usually calculated on<br>
percentage basis, and a detailed table is<br>
used to provide information of wind’s<br>
direction, velocity and strength. Wind<br>
maps are produced, indicating wind<br>
directions by means of arrows and<br>
symbols. The distribution of winds<br>
on the earth surface is similar to<br>
that of the pressure belts. Therefore,<br>
planetary wind patterns are a result of<br>
global distribution of pressure patterns<br>
(Figure. 6.26).<br>

<b>Types of planetary wind systems</b><br>
<b>Trade winds</b><br>
<b>These are winds that blow from sub-</b><br>
tropical highs towards the Equatorial<br>
low pressure belts, but they are deflected<br>
by the rotation of the earth eastward and<br>
blow from North easterly direction in<br>
the Northern – hemisphere, commonly<br>
known as the North – East Trade winds.<br>
<b>Also, they blow from South easterly</b><br>
directly in the Southern hemisphere and<br>
are commonly called South–Easterly<br>
<b>Trade Winds. Trade winds occur between</b><br>
30º North and South of the Equator.<br>
<b>The name “trade winds” comes from</b><br>
the phrase “to blow trade”, that is, to<br>
blow steady in a constant direction.<br>
<b>The name originally had nothing to do</b><br>
with trading, although during sailing<br>
ships for trade, these winds were of<br>
significance. Between the two trade<br>
wind systems there is a low pressure<br>
belt through which trade winds blow.<br>
<b>This area is called the Inter-Tropical</b><br>
<b>Convergence Zone (ITCZ). During</b><br>
summer, especially over the continents<br>
where thermal heating is high, a zone of<br>
<b>Westerly-winds intervenes between the</b><br>
<b>Trade winds. These intervening winds</b><br>
are called Equatorial Westerlies.<br>
<b>Characteristics of trade winds</b><br>
(i)<br>
They blow from a high pressure<br>
belt near 30º latitude in each<br>
hemisphere called the sub-tropical<br>
high towards the low pressure<br>
belt near the Equator called the<br>
Equatorial low pressure belt.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   371</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
372<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
(ii)<br>
They blow from North–east in the<br>
northern hemisphere and South-<br>
east in the southern hemisphere<br>
towards the equatorial low-<br>
pressure belts.<br>
(iii)	  They follow a regular path.<br>
<b>Though</b><br>
their<br>
strength<br>
and<br>
directions are changed with their<br>
location and nature of surface<br>
which determine local pressure<br>
cells, they are marked to be<br>
stronger in the summer than the<br>
winter season.<br>
(iv)	  They are affected by deflection<br>
force of the South’s rotation<br>
called Coriolis force which<br>
causes them to blow North-east<br>
and South –east of the southern<br>
and<br>
northern<br>
hemispheres<br>
respectively.<br>
(v)<br>
They are affected by the local<br>
pressure disturbances in the<br>
western side and near the<br>
equators. Additionally, they are<br>
dominated by rare calmness and<br>
the clear sunny conditions.<br>
(vi)	 In the Northern hemisphere<br>
during winter season, the North<br>
– east trade winds cover the zone<br>
between the Tropic of Cancer<br>
and few degrees North of the<br>
<b>Equator while during summer</b><br>
they occupy a belt further North<br>
from 30ºN to about 10ºN.  Also,<br>
during this period, the system<br>
of the South-east Trade-winds<br>
moves northwards.<br>
(vii)	 Trade winds are less regular over<br>
the land than over the oceans.<br>
<b>They are normally constant in</b><br>
motion and direction, especially<br>
over the oceans where they are<br>
minimally subjected to frictions<br>
of the underlying surface.<br>
(viii)	They pick-up latent heat as they<br>
pass through warm tropical<br>
oceans and they are forced<br>
to rise by violent convection<br>
currents. These unstable, warm<br>
moist winds are rapidly cooled<br>
adiabatically to produce the<br>
towering cumulonimbus clouds<br>
which<br>
are<br>
characterized<br>
by<br>
frequent afternoon thunderstorms<br>
and low pressure traits of the<br>
equatorial climate.<br>
(ix)	 They are the major drivers of the<br>
global circulation system since<br>
they turn latent heat into sensible<br>
heat, turning it later into potential<br>
energy.<br>
<b>Westerlies</b><br>
<b>These are the air-masses flowing from</b><br>
the subtropical highs to the sub-polar<br>
lows from about 35º to 60º North<br>
and South of the Equator. These belts<br>
extends North and South with the<br>
seasonal change of the pressure belts.<br>
<b>At about 30º from the Equator in both</b><br>
hemispheres lies the Horse latitude with<br>
high pressure belts.  In the polar side<br>
of this Horse latitude, the atmospheric<br>
pressure<br>
diminishes<br>
towards<br>
low<br>
pressure centres in the middle and high<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   372</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
373<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
latitude. As the wind is set in motion<br>
towards the poles by these pressure<br>
systems, they are deflected eastward by<br>
earth’s rotation.<br>
<b>Characteristics of the westerlies</b><br>
(i)<br>
They consist of two separate<br>
circum-polar vortices increasing<br>
in both speed and constancy with<br>
altitude. As they move towards<br>
the poles, they are disrupted by Jet<br>
streams in the upper troposphere.<br>
(ii)	 Within these belts, both wind<br>
direction and velocity are far from<br>
constant due to the influence of<br>
local pressure systems.<br>
(iii)	 They form the most frequent<br>
winds in the northern hemisphere<br>
where a westerly component is<br>
predominant from the south-<br>
west. Due to this, they are<br>
commonly known as “South-<br>
<b>Westerly variables or Disturbed</b><br>
Westerlies”.<br>
(iv)	 They normally blow with strength<br>
and constancy between 40º – 60º<br>
<b>South in the Southern hemisphere</b><br>
where there is a continuous open<br>
ocean.<br>
(v)	 They normally blow from Horse<br>
latitude<br>
or<br>
sub-tropical<br>
high<br>
pressure belts to sub-polar or<br>
temperate-low-pressure belts and<br>
they are deflected to the right to<br>
become South westerlies in the<br>
<b>Northern hemisphere and to the</b><br>
left to become North westerlies in<br>
the Southern hemisphere.<br>
(vi)	 They are extremely variable<br>
both in direction and velocity,<br>
giving rise to both cyclones and<br>
anticyclones, which are strong<br>
enough to overcome the main<br>
direction and velocity.<br>
<b>Polar easterly winds</b><br>
<b>Polar easterly wind also known as polar</b><br>
easterlies are the cold air masses that<br>
move towards the equator from the polar<br>
high-pressure areas to low pressure<br>
temperate regions. They are better<br>
developed in the southern hemisphere<br>
than in the northern hemisphere. Their<br>
movement is pronounced much in the<br>
southern hemisphere where both the<br>
high over the Antarctic plateau and the<br>
sub-polar low over the Southern Ocean<br>
are clearly defined.<br>
<b>Characteristics of the easterly winds</b><br>
(i)<br>
They are deflected to the left<br>
as the air moves towards the<br>
equator, developing a belt of<br>
winds.  These wind belts develop<br>
spirals from the polar region in<br>
an easterly direction forming a<br>
polar vortex.<br>
(ii)<br>
They are generally weak.<br>
(iii)	 These winds are irregular in the<br>
northern hemisphere because<br>
they blow over the land and<br>
they are known as continental<br>
winds, while they are regular in<br>
the southern hemisphere because<br>
they blow over the surface water<br>
and they are known as oceanic<br>
winds.<br>

<b>Exercise 6.6</b><br>
1.	 Elaborate the determinants of<br>
strength and direction of wind.<br>
2.	 The origin of planetary wind<br>
systems is an interplay of<br>
several conditions. Discuss<br>
3.	 ‘Each air mass is unique’.<br>
<b>Discuss</b><br>
4.	 Describe the major types of<br>
fronts.<br>
5.	 Using a well labelled diagram,<br>
describe the planetary wind<br>
systems.<br>
<b>Cyclones and anticyclones</b><br>
<b>Cyclones</b><br>
<b>Cyclones are areas of low pressure</b><br>
or convergence centre. They are<br>
also called ‘depressions’. Cyclones<br>
are mostly formed over the ocean<br>
mid-latitudes and spread eastwards,<br>
bringing cloud and rain to western<br>
margins of continents (Figure 6.27).<br>
<b>Figure 6.27: Cyclone movement</b><br>
<b>Cyclones or depressions are usually</b><br>
associated with high winds, warm air,<br>
and atmospheric lifting which produces<br>
clouds cover, storms and precipitation.<br>
<b>Types of cyclones</b><br>
There are three types of cyclones,<br>
namely wave cyclones, tropical cyclones<br>
and tornadoes. These are described as<br>
follows;<br>
<b>Wave cyclones</b><br>
<b>Wave cyclones are common in mid</b><br>
latitude areas, including Arctic and<br>
<b>Antarctic zones. Their intensity ranges</b><br>
from a weak disturbance to a powerful<br>
storm. They constitute a large spiral of<br>
air that repeatedly forms, intensifies<br>
and then dissolves along the polar front.<br>
<b>Tropical cyclones</b><br>
<b>A tropical cyclone is the most powerful</b><br>
and destructive type of cyclone.<br>
<b>Tropical cyclones are common in</b><br>
tropical and sub-tropical areas. They<br>
are known by various names in different<br>
regions, including hurricane in western<br>
hemispheres (America), typhoons and<br>
willy-willies in warm Pacific (Asia),<br>
and cyclones in the Indian Ocean. They<br>
travel between 5º and 20º North and<br>
<b>South of the Equator. The areas that are</b><br>
much affected by tropical cyclones are<br>
Southern and Eastern USA.<br>
<b>Stages of tropical cyclones</b><br>
development<br>
<b>There are three main stages of cyclones</b><br>
development, these are; embryo,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   374</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
375<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
maturity and decay depression. An<br>
embryo depression begins as a small<br>
wave on the polar front. Here warm<br>
moist tropical air masses meet with<br>
colder and drier polar (Pm) air. The<br>
convergence of the two air masses<br>
results in the warmer, less dense air being<br>
forced to rise in a spiral movement. The<br>
upward movement results in less air at<br>
the earth’s surface, creating an area of<br>
below-average or Low Pressure (LP). A<br>
mature depression is recognized by the<br>
increasing amplitude of the initial wave<br>
and the pressure continues to fall as more<br>
warm air, in the warm sector is forced<br>
to rise. As pressure falls, the pressure<br>
gradient steepens, that is, the inward<br>
blowing winds increases in strength.<br>
<b>Therefore, the rise of warm air results</b><br>
into cooling to dew point and some of the<br>
water vapour condenses to form clouds<br>
which eventually develop precipitation.<br>
<b>The decay depression begins to decline</b><br>
when the cold front catches up the warm<br>
front to form an occlusion or occluded<br>
front.<br>
<b>Characteristics of tropical cyclones</b><br>
(i)<br>
They always move in circular<br>
motion due to the strong pressure<br>
gradient and winds that spiral<br>
inward at high speed.<br>
(ii)	 Convergence<br>
and<br>
uplift<br>
are<br>
intense producing very heavy<br>
rainfall.<br>
(iii)	 Air descends from high altitudes<br>
and adiabatically gets warmed.<br>
(iv)	 As they pass over a site, calm<br>
prevails and the sky clears.<br>
(v)	 The storm diameter can range<br>
from 150 – 500 km and its speed<br>
may be 65 – 135 miles per hour.<br>
<b>Conditions necessary for the</b><br>
development of tropical cyclones<br>
<b>Tropical cyclones tend to develop</b><br>
over warm tropical oceans, where<br>
temperature exceeds 26ºC and where<br>
there is considerable depth of warm<br>
water. They develop in autumn, when<br>
sea temperatures are at their highest. They<br>
also develop in the trade wind belt, where<br>
the surface winds warm as they blow<br>
towards the Equator and between latitude<br>
5o – 20o North and South of the Equator<br>
where Coriolis force is insufficient to<br>
enable the future to spin.<br>
<b>Impacts of tropical cyclones</b><br>
<b>Tropical cyclones are one of the major</b><br>
natural hazards which often cause<br>
considerable loss of assets and life.<br>
<b>There are four main causes of damage</b><br>
associated with cyclones. Tropical<br>
cyclones produce high winds which<br>
often exceeding 160 km / hour or in<br>
extreme cases 300 km/hour. With this<br>
wind, the whole village, town or city<br>
can be destroyed and thus a country<br>
may suffer serious economic problems<br>
such as damage of transport and<br>
communication infrastructure. Ocean<br>
storms (tidal) surges resulting from<br>
cyclones destroy coastal areas which<br>
are in most cases densely populated.<br>
<b>Good example is in Bangladesh where</b><br>
about 40,000 people were washed out<br>
to sea at the Bay of Bengal in 1994.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   375</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
376<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Flooding caused by storm surge or</b><br>
torrential rain causes a lot of damage.<br>
<b>For example, in 1974, about 800,000</b><br>
people died in Honduras as their homes<br>
were washed away.<br>
<b>Landslides. Due to heavy rainfall, the</b><br>
buildings which have been constructed<br>
on steep and unstable slopes, such as<br>
those in Hong Kong, get destroyed<br>
easily.<br>
<b>Hurricanes</b><br>
<b>Hurricanes are the tropical cyclones</b><br>
of the Atlantic, and most powerful and<br>
destructive form of cyclone storm, with<br>
wind speeds ranging from 160 to 300<br>
km per hour. These are formed after the<br>
<b>ITCZ has moved to its most northerly</b><br>
enabling air to converge at low levels<br>
and can have a diameter of up to 650<br>
km. ITCZ is the area encircling the<br>
<b>Earth near the Equator, where the</b><br>
northwest and southeast trade winds<br>
come together.<br>
<b>Conditions for development of</b><br>
hurricanes<br>
<b>Hurricanes develop near the earth’s</b><br>
surface due to intense low pressure,<br>
strong winds and continuous source<br>
of heat to maintain rising air currents.<br>
<b>There should be enough supply of</b><br>
moisture to provide the latent heat<br>
released by condensation to drive the<br>
storm and provide the heavy rainfall.<br>
<b>It is estimated that in a single day</b><br>
a hurricane releases an amount of<br>
energy equivalent to that released by<br>
500000 atomic bombs. Therefore,<br>
it is the area of air subsiding which<br>
increases instability by warming and<br>
exaggerating the storm’s intensity.<br>
<b>However, hurricanes rapidly decline</b><br>
once the source of heavy air is removed.<br>
<b>When air moves over colder water on</b><br>
land surface, it increases friction and<br>
cannot supply sufficient moisture. The<br>
average life span of tropical cyclone is<br>
7 – 14 days.<br>
<b>Tornadoes</b><br>
<b>A tornado is a small-diameter of</b><br>
violently rotating column of air that<br>
is in contact with both the surface of<br>
the earth and a cumulonimbus cloud.<br>
<b>Tornadoes are often referred to as</b><br>
‘twister’ in the USA. Thus, they are<br>
intense and enormously powerful winds<br>
in which air spirals at tremendous speed.<br>
<b>They are smaller in size compared</b><br>
to other cyclones, commonly related<br>
to convectional activity. They occur<br>
within cumulonimbus clouds travelling<br>
in advance for a cold front and are<br>
mostly common in spring and summer.<br>
<b>They are associated with thunderstorms</b><br>
and can occur over both land and sea.<br>
<b>They usually appear as a dark funnel</b><br>
cloud hanging from the base of a dense<br>
cumulonimbus clouds, travelling at the<br>
speed of 225 km per hour. USA is the<br>
country mostly affected by Tornadoes.<br>
<b>Other countries or regions frequently</b><br>
hit by tornadoes include South Africa,<br>
<b>Uruguay, Brazil, Netherlands and Far</b><br>
Eastern Asia (Figure 6.28).<br>

<b>Figure 6.28: A tornado</b><br>
<b>Anticyclones</b><br>
<b>These are areas of high pressure which</b><br>
develop in a region where the air is<br>
descending. They are associated with<br>
outward blow in a clockwise direction in<br>
northern hemisphere and an anticlockwise<br>
direction in the southern hemisphere.<br>
<b>In an anticyclone, a downward motion</b><br>
causes air to descend and be warmed<br>
adiabatically hence condensation does<br>
not occur (Figure 6.29).<br>
<b>Cool, sinking air</b><br>
1 000 – 5 000 miles<br>
<b>Anticyclone</b><br>
<b>High</b><br>
<b>Figure 6.29: Anticyclone</b><br>
Anticyclones have low pressure gradients that is the air pressure in it does not change<br>
rapidly. This means that the winds are gentle. As air sinks, it warms, leading to<br>
warm and dry weather. Anticyclones are much larger than depression and therefore<br>
can lead to many days or weeks of settled and calm weather. They often block<br>
the path of depressions either slowing down bad weather or forcing it round the<br>
outside of the high pressure system. They are then called blocking highs. Thus, as<br>
air descends, air pressure increases. Therefore, the earth’s rotation makes the air<br>
change direction. In the Northern Hemisphere, the air is pushed clockwise while<br>
in the Southern Hemisphere, it is pushed anticlockwise.<br>
<b>Weather conditions associated with anticyclones</b><br>
Weather conditions can vary between summer and winter, thus causing formation<br>
of weather related anticyclones namely summer and winter anticyclones are<br>
formed. Summer anticyclones are characterized by hot days with few or no clouds,<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   377</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
378<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
and very light winds. They have cooled<br>
grounds which leads to morning mist.<br>
<b>Warm moist air rising from the ground</b><br>
forms thunderstorms while cloud cover<br>
over eastern England caused by light<br>
winds blow over the cooler North Sea.<br>
<b>Winter anticyclones are characterized</b><br>
by cloudless skies but less radiation<br>
due to the low angle of the sun. Their<br>
temperature drops, making the days cold<br>
and the nights even colder due to lack of<br>
cloud cover. Due to the coldness during<br>
night fogs and frost are formed at night.<br>
<b>The weather conditions associated either</b><br>
by cyclones or anticyclones may differ<br>
depending on among other reasons, the<br>
season of the year.<br>
<b>Local winds</b><br>
<b>Local winds are types of winds which</b><br>
occur in the small spatial scale. They<br>
occur either regularly or periodically,<br>
affecting only limited areas, and blow<br>
for short periods of time. Local winds<br>
have distinctive characteristics and<br>
they produce important effects on<br>
the weather of the areas over which<br>
they blow. They include land and sea<br>
breezes, mountain and valley winds,<br>
descending winds (föhn), monsoon<br>
winds, depression winds, and convection<br>
winds. Such winds affect small area on<br>
the earth’s surface and they lack specific<br>
direction.<br>
<b>Land and sea breezes</b><br>
<b>Land and sea breezes constitute one</b><br>
of the diurnal circulation systems<br>
resulting from differential heating<br>
and cooling between land surfaces<br>
and adjacent sea areas. The resultant<br>
pressure differences, although small<br>
and localised, produce gentle breezes<br>
which affect coastal areas. They are<br>
also called the coastal winds as they<br>
normally affect coastal areas both at<br>
night and during day hours.<br>
<b>Land breeze is the local wind which</b><br>
moves from the land to the see during<br>
the night when the sea retains heat<br>
longer than the land. During land<br>
breeze, there is a reversal of the pressure<br>
gradient which affects wind direction.<br>
<b>During night, low pressure develops in</b><br>
the sea, resulting in the movement of<br>
wind towards the land (Figure 6.30).<br>
<b>High pressure</b><br>
<b>Low</b><br>
pressure<br>
<b>Land breeze</b><br>
<b>Hot</b><br>
<b>Ocean</b><br>
<b>Cold</b><br>
<b>Land</b><br>
<b>Low specific heat</b><br>
capacity (Temperature<br>
drops faster)<br>
<b>Cloud</b><br>
<b>Moon</b><br>
<b>Figure 6.30: Land breeze</b><br>
<b>Sea breeze is the local wind which</b><br>
moves from the sea to the land during<br>
the day time when the land heats up<br>
rapidly than the sea. When the land is<br>
hot, lower pressure forms and a gentle<br>
breeze begins to blow from the sea<br>
(Figure 6.31). Sea breeze is capable of<br>
lowering coastal temperatures.<br>

<b>Mountain and valley winds</b><br>
<b>Mountain and valley winds are special</b><br>
category of local or tertially winds that<br>
are directly related to the topography of<br>
the area. These winds are categorised<br>
into the following:<br>
<b>Anabatic winds</b><br>
<b>A term anabatic is derived from the</b><br>
<b>Greek</b><br>
word<br>
‘Anabatos’<br>
meaning<br>
moving upward. Anabatic wind are the<br>
local winds which blow in highland<br>
regions caused by ascending air masses<br>
along the mountain valley. During the<br>
day time, when the slope is heated by the<br>
sun, the air in contact with it becomes<br>
warmer than the air at the same level<br>
in a free atmosphere. The heated air<br>
mass expands and becomes less dense<br>
(rarefied) and thus rises up. The rising<br>
warm air mass is replaced by the cool<br>
air mass down the slope (Figure 6.32).<br>
<b>Anabatic winds develop in areas where</b><br>
the general global wind circulation is<br>
not strong enough to obscure these<br>
local winds. The mountain or up-valley<br>
winds blow during the daytime as the<br>
result of differential heating by the<br>
sun. Anabatic winds develop near the<br>
mountain slopes where the air is heated<br>
by conduction to a greater extent than<br>
air at the same level above the valley.<br>
<b>This causes the convectional rising</b><br>
of air from above the slopes which<br>
in turn causes the down slope air<br>
from the valley to take place. These<br>
winds sometimes rise to the level of<br>
condensation, which later leads to<br>
mountain rainfall.<br>
<b>Day breeze</b><br>
<b>Ascending air</b><br>
<b>Highland</b><br>
<b>Sun</b><br>
<b>Highland</b><br>
<b>Figure 6.32: Anabatic winds</b><br>

09/03/2022   17:09<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

380<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Katabatic winds</b><br>
<b>The term Katabatic is derived from the</b><br>
<b>Greek Word ‘Katabatikos’ meaning</b><br>
flow downhill. Therefore, katabatic<br>
winds are the valley winds that blow<br>
down a valley during night where the<br>
general circulation of winds is not strong<br>
enough to obscure local influences.<br>
<b>These winds develop when the upper</b><br>
slopes are chilled rapidly at night by<br>
radiation and the cool dense air flows<br>
down the hill by gravity, following the<br>
valley because of less resistance. These<br>
winds are more common in areas where<br>
cold air blows off an ice cap over which<br>
chilling is high. Katabatic winds blow<br>
cold, dense air down the slope into the<br>
valley, leading to cold temperature at<br>
night. The cold temperature brought<br>
about by the cold dense air results in<br>
the condensation and formation of<br>
mists and fogs (Figure 6.33).<br>
<b>Night breeze</b><br>
<b>Descending air</b><br>
<b>Moon</b><br>
<b>Highland</b><br>
<b>Highland</b><br>
<b>Figure. 6.33: Katabatic winds</b><br>
<b>Föhn wind</b><br>
Föhn is a strong, warm and dry wind which blows periodically to the leeward<br>
side of a mountain range. It is a rain shadow wind that results from the adiabatic<br>
warming of air that has dropped most of its moisture on windward slopes. It<br>
is common in the Alps when a depression (LP) passes to the north of the<br>
mountainous area and draws in warm, moist air from the Mediterranean. As the<br>
air rises, it cools at the dry adiabatic lapse rate of 1oC per every 100 m. This wind<br>
is also known as the Chinook in Alberta, USA, on the American prairies, and it<br>
has considerable effects on human activity. It causes snow to melt, especially in<br>
spring. It is very notorious in the Alps where the winds add difficulty in ascending<br>
an already difficult peak. Also, it is associated with the rapid spread of wildfires<br>
in fire prone regions as it blows (Figure 6.34).<br>

09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
381<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Ascending moist air</b><br>
<b>Descending dry air</b><br>
<b>Dew point for drier air</b><br>
<b>Dew point for moist air</b><br>
<b>Direction of wind</b><br>
<b>Mountain</b><br>
<b>Clouds</b><br>
<b>Figure 6.34: Föhn wind effect</b><br>
<b>Monsoons winds</b><br>
<b>The word ‘monsoon’ is derived from</b><br>
the Arabic word ‘Mausim’ meaning<br>
season. These are winds which develop<br>
due to seasonal changes in temperature,<br>
which cause reversal changes in<br>
pressure, especially in Central Asia.<br>
<b>Monsoon winds are common in</b><br>
<b>Central Asia because Indian Ocean has</b><br>
no meridional extent (does not extend<br>
from North to South) as it is interrupted<br>
by Asia continent which cause extreme<br>
thermal contrast. In summer (between<br>
<b>May and October), a low pressure belt</b><br>
develops over north western India,<br>
which is so intense that it becomes<br>
more effective and pronounced than<br>
those of the equatorial low pressure<br>
belt. This phenomenon leads to the<br>
development of an interrupted pressure<br>
gradient from the subtropical high<br>
in the Southern hemisphere across<br>
the Equator to north Western India.<br>
<b>The wind blows from the southern</b><br>
hemisphere (Australia) high pressure<br>
cell as the South east (SE) but yet<br>
deflected to the right which then flows<br>
as South west (SW) winds towards the<br>
<b>Indian sub-continent. The time taken to</b><br>
reverse the direction of these winds is<br>
six months. These winds are prominent<br>
within the tropics on the eastern side<br>
of the landmass and Indian Ocean<br>
(Figure 6.35). Normally, the south –<br>
east trade winds cross the equator, but<br>
on the way, they are affected by the<br>
rotation of the Earth, and consequently,<br>
turn into the south-west monsoon in<br>
most parts of Asia, blowing towards<br>
the heated continent. Due to changes<br>
in the overhead sun at different points<br>
and seasons, the surface temperature<br>
and pressure change seasonally. This<br>
causes a reversal movement of winds<br>
following the seasonal change in the<br>
temperature and pressure between<br>
northern and southern hemispheres.<br>
<b>In winter, the intense high pressure</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   381</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
382<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
develops over central Asia, while the southern hemisphere experiences low<br>
pressure with its centre over the Australian landmass. Then, wind blow along<br>
thermos barometric gradient from central Asia as North east (NE) wind and get<br>
deflected to the North-westery direction after crossing the equation. For instance,<br>
along the East African coast, monsoon winds cause moderate rainfall in Mombasa,<br>
Kilwa, Malindi and Dar es Salaam.<br>
<b>Figure 6.35: Summer monsoon winds</b><br>
During winter, an intense low pressure develops over the Australian continent<br>
and high pressure in Asia minor, leading to the reversed direction of winds<br>
moving from Asia high pressure belt to Australian continent as North-<br>
East winds which then, flow as North-West wind after crossing the Equator<br>
(Figure 6.36).<br>

1.<br>
Write short notes on the<br>
following concepts;<br>
(i)<br>
Cyclones and<br>
<b>Anticyclones</b><br>
(ii)	 Anabatic and Katabatic<br>
winds<br>

<b>Atmospheric stability and instability</b><br>
<b>A parcel of warm air rises through the</b><br>
lower atmosphere cools adiabatically.<br>
<b>The rate and maintenance of any vertical</b><br>
uplift depends upon temperature –<br>
density balance between the rising<br>
parcel and the surrounding air. In a<br>
simplified form, this balance is the<br>
relationship between the environmental<br>
lapse rate, and the dry and saturated<br>
adiabatic lapse rates.<br>
<b>Stability</b><br>
<b>The stability of the atmosphere</b><br>
occurs when a rising parcel of dry air<br>
(unsaturated air) cools more rapidly<br>
than the air surrounding it. It occurs<br>
when adiabatic lapse rate is dominant<br>
than environmental lapse rate that<br>
result to limited rising of warm<br>
airmass vertically that cause stable<br>
atmospheric condition. For example,<br>
the environmental lapse rate is 6.5ºC<br>
per 1000 m and the dry adiabatic lapse<br>
rate is 1oC per 1000 m.  By the time<br>
the rising air has reached 1000 m, it<br>
has cooled to 10ºC, which leaves it<br>
colder and denser than the surrounding<br>
air, which has cooled to 14ºC. If there<br>
is nothing to force the parcel of air<br>
to rise, such as mountains or fronts,<br>
it will sink back to its starting point,<br>
resulting into the stable atmosphere.<br>
<b>A stable atmosphere is characterized</b><br>
by the clear atmosphere and sunny<br>
days. This is due to the limited cloud<br>
formation prevented by inability of<br>
air parcel to reach condensation point<br>
(the dew point), low atmospheric<br>
dampness<br>
resulting<br>
from<br>
limited<br>
or no condensation process. High<br>
temperature range due to extreme day<br>
and night temperature, since absence of<br>
clouds cause maximum day temperature<br>
and very low night temperature.<br>
<b>Presence of calm atmosphere due to</b><br>
absence of thunderstorms lightening<br>
and precipitation, and temperature<br>
inversion resulting from descending<br>
of air parcels that are associated with<br>
dynamic compression. Pockets of air<br>
are forced to rise in fair ways:<br>
<b>Firstly, when the earth’s surface is</b><br>
heated up, the overlying air mass is<br>
warmed by conduction, hence air rises.<br>
<b>Secondly, when wind blows against</b><br>
a mountain side, the pocket of air is<br>
forced to rise up mechanically. Thirdly,<br>
when a cold air mass meets a warm air<br>
mass, the cold air, because it is denser<br>
than the warm air, descends, forcing<br>
the warm air to ascend. Lastly, vertical<br>
ascent may take place as in a tropical<br>
cyclone.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   384</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
385<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>

<b>Instability</b><br>
<b>Atmospheric instability is a condition</b><br>
where the earth’s atmosphere is<br>
generally considered to be unstable<br>
and as a result the weather is subjected<br>
to high degree of variability through<br>
distance and time. The instability of<br>
the atmosphere occurs when a rising<br>
parcel of dry air cools less rapidly than<br>
the surrounding air. It is common on<br>
hot days when localized heating of<br>
the ground warms the adjacent air by<br>
conduction, hence creating a higher<br>
lapse rate. Instability is common in<br>
saturated air than unsaturated air, since<br>
the rising air cools much less rapidly<br>
and so remains warmer and lighter than<br>
the surrounding air. A warm damp air<br>
mass may therefore rise to great heights<br>
and cause very unstable atmospheric<br>
conditions, building up great clouds<br>
and possibly causing heavy rainfall,<br>
hail and thunderstorms. When the<br>
air-mass reaches a height at which<br>
it has the same temperature as the<br>
surrounding air, the vertical rise ceases<br>
and it becomes neutral or indifferent<br>
equilibrium state. Weather conditions<br>
associated with atmospheric instability<br>
include: deep convective current which<br>
leads to thunderstorm development<br>
(when enough moisture is present),<br>
over warm oceans which can lead to<br>
tropical cyclone and turbulence. Again,<br>
in dry atmosphere inferior mirages,<br>
dust devils, steam devils, and fire whirls<br>
can form. For example, fire whirls can<br>
spread fire beyond its previous bounds.<br>
<b>Furthermore, small scale tornado like</b><br>
circulations can occur over or near<br>
any intense surface heat source, which<br>
would have significant instability<br>
in its vicinity, and cumulonimbus<br>
clouds and heavy rainfall may occur<br>
when sufficient moisture reaches dew<br>
point.<br>
<b>Conditional instability</b><br>
<b>Conditional instability occurs when</b><br>
<b>Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR) is</b><br>
lower than the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate<br>
(DALR) but higher than the Saturated<br>
<b>Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR). If a mass</b><br>
of dry air rises as a wind up a mountain<br>
slope, it cools at the dry adiabatic lapse<br>
rate until it reaches its dew point. Then<br>
condensation begins, and it now cools at<br>
the saturated adiabatic lapse rate.  This<br>
may mean that even though the initial<br>
cause of the uplift was mechanical,<br>
the lower rate of cooling results in the<br>
air mass remaining relatively warmer<br>
than its surrounding and so it will<br>
continue ascending on its own accord.<br>
<b>It is a conditional instability because</b><br>
instability depends on the presence of<br>
sufficient water vapour. Conditional<br>
instability is a common cause of storms<br>
and heavy rain.<br>

09/03/2022   17:09<br>

<b>Sunshine</b><br>
<b>This is the visible light from the Sun to</b><br>
the surface of the Earth. The duration of<br>
sunshine is partly a function of latitudes<br>
as hours of light vary with season in<br>
different latitudes. It is also a function of<br>
daytime cloudiness as cloud cover limits<br>
sun rays from penetrating and reaching<br>
the surface of the Earth. For example,<br>
equatorial climate is characterized<br>
by rainfall and considerable cloud<br>
throughout the year. However, there is<br>
usually bright sunshine in the mornings,<br>
and the sky becomes overcast in the<br>
afternoons and early evenings. In<br>
cool temperate latitudes, the skies are<br>
frequently cloudy and drizzle or rain<br>
is common, thus less bright sunshine<br>
or sunshine duration. In the interior<br>
of continents, the sky is dry and less<br>
cloudy, experiencing more sunshine.<br>
<b>Sunshine duration is the length of the</b><br>
time that the ground surface is irradiated<br>
by direct solar radiation. The World<br>
<b>Meteorological Organization (WMO)</b><br>
defines sunshine duration as the period<br>
during which direct solar irradiance<br>
exceeds a threshold value of 120 watts<br>
per square metre (W/m2). Normally<br>
it is expressed in hours per year or in<br>
average hours per day. Sunshine duration<br>
influences other elements of weather and<br>
climate including temperature, humidity<br>
as well as air pressure. Geographic pattern<br>
and cloudiness are among other factors<br>
affecting sunshine duration. Sunshine<br>
duration is recorded by Campbell-Stokes<br>
sunshine recorder.<br>
<b>Weather forecasting</b><br>
<b>Weather forecasting is the application</b><br>
of science and technology to predict<br>
the state of the atmosphere for a given<br>
location and time. It entails predicting<br>
how the present state of the atmosphere<br>
will change. Besides the prevalence of<br>
modern weather forecasting, eversince<br>
nineteenth century, human being<br>
has attempted to predict the weather<br>
informally for several millennia. The<br>
informal weather forecasting is termed<br>
traditional weather forecasting which<br>
uses indigenous knowledge in weather<br>
forecasting.<br>
<b>Types of weather forecasting</b><br>
<b>There are two major types of weather</b><br>
forecasting, which are traditional and<br>
modern weather forecasting.<br>
<b>Traditional weather forecasting</b><br>
<b>The traditional weather forecasting</b><br>
predicts weather through such methods<br>
as observation of types of birds’ voices,<br>
crocking of frogs, rainbow, migration of<br>
birds, insects’ voices, changes of wind<br>
direction, position of the moon and<br>
general body feeling. These behaviours<br>
of plants and animals and changes of<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   386</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
387<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
environmental elements are associated<br>
with weather messages and meanings<br>
such as onset of rainfall, drought, and<br>
spring seasons. The methods are still<br>
widely used in predicting weather among<br>
the indigenous communities.However,<br>
the weaknesses of traditional weather<br>
forecasting largely lies on its limited<br>
coverage and reliability. Also, its short fall<br>
on being too narrative and context specific.<br>
<b>Modern weather forecasting</b><br>
<b>The modern weather forecasting involves</b><br>
the use of modern equipment such as<br>
satellites, electronic computers, radar,<br>
aircraft, balloons, ship, sub-marines,<br>
rockets and daily recordings from weather<br>
stations. These methods  of weather<br>
forecasting are most reliable compared<br>
to traditional weather forecasting.<br>
<b>Importance of weather forecasting</b><br>
<b>Provides early warning information to the</b><br>
population. This helps the government<br>
and other stakeholders to prepare and<br>
respond to extreme weather events more<br>
effectively and efficiently and reduce<br>
damage to property and loss of lives,<br>
such as warnings against hurricanes and<br>
storms. Forecasting based on temperature<br>
and precipitation are important for various<br>
planning purposes including agricultural<br>
activities. This is especially important<br>
in countries where agriculture is the<br>
main livelihood activity for the majority<br>
of the population. Planning outdoor<br>
activities: Since outdoor activities are<br>
severely interrupted by heavy rain, snow<br>
and wind chill, forecast can be used to<br>
inform appropriate planning of activities<br>
or measures in relation to predicted<br>
weather. Navigation and air transport:<br>
<b>Weather forecast is crucial for water and</b><br>
air transport as their safety depends on<br>
the state of weather on a given route.<br>
<b>Weather forecasts also help to determine</b><br>
the type of cloth people wear in a given<br>
day. This is more important in the Arctic<br>
or Equatorial regions where temperature<br>
is highly variable. Fishing activities:<br>
<b>Both local and modern fishing activities</b><br>
largely depend on the state of weather<br>
of a given day because it affects the<br>
movements of sea water. Geographic<br>
expeditions and military operations both<br>
largely rely on weather in conducting<br>
their activities. for example, flying<br>
drones can be prevented by strong winds.<br>
<b>Contractors and engineers are very keen</b><br>
on weather for successful construction<br>
activities. For example, construction of<br>
roads and bridges can be prevented by<br>
heavy rain.<br>

<b>World climate regions</b><br>
<b>Although it is possible to study</b><br>
climatic phenomenon in isolation, an<br>
understanding of the development of<br>
soil and vegetation necessitates an<br>
appreciation of the interrelationship<br>
between soil, vegetation and climate. The<br>
integration and interaction of climate,<br>
soils and vegetation gives the world its<br>
major ecosystems or biomes.<br>
<b>Classification of climate</b><br>
<b>The classification of climate represents</b><br>
targets to distinguish various<br>
combinations of climatic elements<br>
throughout the world. By studying<br>
the weather, it is possible to make<br>
generalizations about the climate of a<br>
given place. Generally, there are two<br>
ways or methods of classifying climates<br>
namely; empirical classification and<br>
genetic classification.<br>
<b>Empirical Classification</b><br>
<b>This is based on statistics, experiments</b><br>
or physical characteristics related<br>
to climate. It is, therefore, based on<br>
observation and hence it is more stable<br>
overtime than genetic classification.<br>
<b>It focuses on temperature and</b><br>
precipitation.<br>
<b>Genetic classification</b><br>
<b>This approach is based on the causes</b><br>
or genesis of climatic variations.  In<br>
this scheme of classification, climatic<br>
conditions or phenomena as they are<br>
obtained in different areas on the earth’s<br>
surface, are organized according to their<br>
causes or origin. Basing on knowledge<br>
of the causes and relationship between<br>
different climatic variables, this type of<br>
classification is easy to use. However,<br>
genetic classification suffers from the<br>
uncertainty of our understanding of<br>
causes and origins of climatic conditions<br>
or behaviours as they may change at any<br>
time. It is done for a specific purpose, or<br>
for certain climatological activities. The<br>
earliest classification of climate types<br>
was made by the Greek philosophers<br>
who distinguished three latitudinal<br>
temperature zones namely; Torrid<br>
(within the tropics), Frigid (within the<br>
polar circles) and Temperate (between<br>
the two).<br>

09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Köppen’s climates classification system</b><br>
The widely used classification system of climates is the empirical system<br>
developed by Vladimir Köppen in 1884 which uses statistical data of temperature,<br>
precipitation, and vegetation distribution. It is based on the identification of the<br>
close relationship between vegetation and climate distribution. The system uses<br>
values of temperature and precipitation in relation to vegetation distribution<br>
on the surface of the Earth as criteria for climate classification. The system is<br>
an empirical because it is based on the mean annual and monthly temperature<br>
and precipitation respectively. It uses capital and small letters to designate<br>
climate groups and types. Table 6.2 indicates Köppen’s climate groups and their<br>
characteristics.<br>
<b>Table 6.3: Climates Groups by Köppen</b><br>
<b>Group</b><br>
<b>Characteristics</b><br>
<b>Tropical climates (A)</b><br>
<b>Average temperature of the coldest month is</b><br>
18oC or higher.<br>
<b>Dry climate (B)</b><br>
Potential evaporation exceeds precipitation.<br>
<b>Temperate climates (C)</b><br>
<b>The average temperature of the coldest</b><br>
month of the (Mid-latitude) climate years is<br>
higher than -3oC but below 18oC.<br>
<b>Cold snow forest climates (D)</b><br>
<b>The average temperature of the coldest</b><br>
month is -3oC.<br>
<b>Cold climates (E)</b><br>
<b>Average temperature for all months is</b><br>
belows 10oC.<br>
<b>Highland climates (H)</b><br>
<b>Cold due to elevation</b><br>
The capital letters A, C, D and E represent humid climate while the letter<br>
‘B’ represents dry climates. The climatic groups are further sub-divided and<br>
represented by small letters based on seasonality of precipitations and temperature<br>
characteristics.<br>
The seasons of dryness are indicated by small letters f, m, w and s: where f<br>
corresponds to no dry season, m-for monsoon climate, w-for winter dry season,<br>
and s-for summer dry season. The small letters a, b, c and d refer to the degree of<br>
severity of temperature (Table 6.3). The B-Dry climates are sub-divided using the<br>
capital letters S-for steppe or semi-arid and W-for desert.<br>

<b>Significance of the Köppen climates classification system</b><br>
This classification provides an efficient way to describe climatic conditions<br>
defined by temperature and precipitation and their seasonality. Being<br>
ecologically relevant, it has been widely used to map geological distribution<br>
of long term climate and associated ecosystem conditions. It helps to predict<br>
the dominant vegetation type based on the climatic data. It provides reasonable<br>
correlation globally with major vegetation regions. It requires a minimum<br>
amount of calculation, and it is widely used in educational circles throughout<br>
the world.<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   390</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
391<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Limitation</b><br>
<b>Despite its significance, yet it is not</b><br>
universally applicable as it only relies on<br>
data or mean monthly temperature and<br>
precipitation to classify climate. Again,<br>
there is no provision for variations in<br>
the strength or constancy of winds,<br>
temperature extremes, precipitation<br>
intensity and range, amount of clouds<br>
cover, and net radiation balance.<br>
<b>Furthermore, its great inadequacy</b><br>
perhaps lies in its application to humid<br>
dry boundaries and impractical to land<br>
management and planning purposes<br>
where more precise and varied factors<br>
need to be utilized.<br>
<b>Miller’s classification</b><br>
<b>In 1946 A. A. Miller developed</b><br>
workable classification of climate.<br>
<b>Such the climate classification is most</b><br>
applicable to geographers because of its<br>
applicability to regional description by<br>
combining a map of temperature zones<br>
with that of the seasonal distribution<br>
of rainfall. Miller thus, developed<br>
a five temperature zones of climate<br>
classification that is hot climate,<br>
warm temperate (sub-tropical), cool<br>
temperate, cold climate, and Arctic<br>
climate. He also added desert climate<br>
and mountain climates to these zones<br>
based on temperature (Table 6.4).<br>
<b>Table 6.5: Miller’s classification of</b><br>
climates<br>
<b>Tempera-</b><br>

type<br>
<b>Below (-10) Very cold</b><br>
<b>Arctic</b><br>
(-10) to 0<br>
<b>Cold</b><br>
<b>Cold</b><br>
climate<br>
0 to 10<br>
<b>Cool</b><br>

<b>Activity 6.5</b><br>
In a group;<br>
1.	 Read books and other sources<br>
to find out the basis for each<br>
climates classification systems<br>
and the specific type of climates<br>
proposed.<br>
2.	 Present findings to the class for<br>
discussion.<br>

<b>Exercise 6.10</b><br>
1.	 Discuss<br>
the<br>

2.	 Examine the strength and<br>
weakness of Köppen’s climates<br>
classification system.<br>

and<br>
contrast<br>
<b>Köppen’s and Miller climates</b><br>
classification systems.<br>
<b>Types of climate</b><br>
<b>Generally, the integration of Köppen</b><br>
and Miller’s climate classification<br>
has resulted to the emergence of nine<br>
types of climate along their respective<br>
characteristics which are widely accepted<br>
climates in the world as follows.<br>
<b>Equatorial climate</b><br>
<b>This climate is usually found within</b><br>
latitudes 0o and 5o North and South<br>
of the equator. This type of climate<br>
covers the Amazon basin, the Congo<br>
basin, southern Ivory Coast, South<br>
western and South central Ghana,<br>
<b>Western coastal Nigeria, and Eastern</b><br>
coastal Madagascar. Highlands located<br>
between these latitudes are known<br>
as modified equatorial climate, for<br>
example the East African  Highlands.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>The equatorial climate lies under the</b><br>
doldrums of low pressure system<br>
with high monthly mean temperature<br>
from 26o to 28oC, while annual range<br>
of temperature is between 2o to 3oC.<br>
<b>It has no seasonal rainfall as rain is</b><br>
almost throughout the year. The area<br>
experiences high humidity and heavy<br>
rainfall accompanied by lightning<br>
and thunderstorms. The area receives<br>
abundant rainfall with annual rainfall<br>
of about 2000 mm, with two maxima.<br>
<b>Also, it is the zone where wind</b><br>
converge at the Equator, popularly<br>
known as Inter Tropical Convergence<br>
Zone (ITCZ).<br>
<b>Equatorial monsoon climate</b><br>
<b>This type of climate is found within</b><br>
latitudes 0o and 5o North and South<br>
of the equator. This type of climate is<br>
common in South East Asia especially<br>
in Malaysia, Singapore and Indonesia.<br>
<b>It also occurs on a smaller scale in</b><br>
western Cameroon and Southeastern<br>
Nigeria.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
It is characterised by high temperature,<br>
between 26o - 28oC throughout the year.<br>
<b>Humidity is very high accompanied</b><br>
with heavy rainfall over 2500 mm.<br>
<b>The region experiences no dry season</b><br>
almost throughout the year, but has<br>
a well-developed double maximum<br>
rainfall in May and October.<br>
<b>Tropical marine climate</b><br>
<b>This climate is found within latitudes</b><br>
5o and 15o North and South of the<br>
equator, particularly on the East<br>
coast regions which come under the<br>

influence of onshore Trade Winds.<br>
<b>This type of climate is found on the</b><br>
<b>East coast of Brazil, the eastern coast</b><br>
of Madagascar, the lowlands of Central<br>
<b>America and West Indies, the coast of</b><br>
<b>Queensland, and the southern Islands</b><br>
of the Philippines.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>It is characterized by high temperature of</b><br>
29oC, high rainfall between 1000-2000<br>
mm with no dry season, high humidity<br>
throughout the year, and onshore Trade<br>
Wind blowing throughout the year.<br>
<b>Tropical cyclones, and hurricanes or</b><br>
typhoons often affect the region.<br>
<b>Vegetation</b><br>
<b>The uniform climatic characteristics</b><br>
developed in Equatorial, Equatorial<br>
<b>Monsoon and Tropical Marine regions</b><br>
are abundant rainfall, lack of a dry<br>
season and uniformly high temperature<br>
which create excellent conditions for<br>
vegitation growth. The lowlands have<br>
a natural vegetation of tall, broad<br>
leafed evergreen trees which make up<br>
a forest complex known as rain forest<br>
or selvas (Figure 6.37). The main<br>
features of rainforest or selvas include<br>
the following;<br>
(a)	 The forest consists of three<br>
layers, namely top layer, middle<br>
layer and bottom layer: The top<br>
layer consists of tall trees with<br>
buttress roots. The height of the<br>
trees ranges between 32- 45 m.<br>
<b>The middle layer consists of</b><br>
tree ferns, lianas (thick stemmed<br>
creepers), for example, rattan and<br>
epiphytes (which use the trees for<br>
support). The height of the trees<br>
is between 19-34 m. The bottom<br>
layer consists of ferns, herbaceous<br>
plants and saprophytes (plants<br>
which live on dead plants), with<br>
trees up to 17 m tall.<br>
(b)	 Nearly all plants are broad<br>
leaved<br>
evergreens<br>
because<br>
high temperature and evenly<br>
distributed rainfall permit growth<br>
throughout the year.<br>
(b)	 Absence of climatic season results<br>
in some plants being in flower,<br>
others in fruit, and others in leaf-<br>
fall at once.<br>
(d)	 The leaves of the tall trees form<br>
almost continuous cover which<br>
shuts out most of the sunlight from<br>
the ground, thereby preventing<br>
the growth of many plants on the<br>
forest floor. But where the forest<br>
is more open, along river banks,<br>
and in forest clearings, sunlight<br>
reaches the ground and a thick<br>
undergrowth of ferns, bushes and<br>
creepers develops.<br>
(e)	 Mangrove<br>
trees<br>
form<br>
dense<br>
forests in coastal swamps and in<br>
the lower valleys of tidal rivers.<br>
(f)	 Most of the trees are hardwoods,<br>
such<br>
as<br>
mahogany,<br>
ebony<br>
ironwood,<br>
rosewood<br>
and<br>
greenheart. Trees, ferns and palms<br>
are also common in equatorial<br>
forests.<br>

(g)	 The climates contain a large<br>
variety of plants which usually<br>
grow close together.<br>
(h)	 When a part of a tropical evergreen<br>
forest is cleared, either for shifting<br>
cultivation or for lumbering, a<br>
less luxuriant forest growth takes<br>
place. This is called secondary<br>
forest and it consists of short trees<br>
and dense undergrowth.<br>
