<b>Tropical monsoon climate</b><br>
<b>The tropical monsoon climate is found</b><br>
along the coastal regions of south-<br>
east Asia, south China, the Indian<br>
sub-continent, and the northern parts<br>
of Australia. It occurs in small scale,<br>
particularly in coastal areas. This<br>
climate covers areas such as southern<br>
<b>Senegal, Guinea-Bissau, Guinea, and</b><br>
Sierra-Leone in the West Africa.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
(i)<br>
The seasonal and ocean winds<br>
operate on a very large scale<br>
affecting both continents and<br>
oceans. These seasonal winds are<br>
called monsoon winds.<br>
(ii)	 Seasonal reversal of winds is the<br>
main characteristic of this climate.<br>
<b>For one season, the winds blow</b><br>
from the sea to the land, bringing<br>
heavy rain to coastal regions and<br>
for another season they blow from<br>
the land to the sea, giving little or<br>
no rain except where they cross a<br>
wide stretch of sea.<br>
(iii)	 Wind reversal is caused by<br>
pressure changes over the land<br>
masses.<br>
(iv)	 Temperatures range from 250C in<br>
cool season to 320C in hot season.<br>
(v)	 Annual rainfall varies greatly due<br>
to relief and the angle at which<br>
onshore winds meet. Annual<br>
rainfall varies greatly, for example<br>
about 620 mm around Delhi, and as<br>
much as 1250 mm on south-facing<br>
slopes of Khari Hills, in Assam.<br>
<b>A typical tropical monsoon climate has</b><br>
three seasons which are;<br>
<b>Cool, almost dry season (November-</b><br>
<b>February) which is characterized by</b><br>
no rain due to offshore winds, with<br>
temperature ranging between 120C and<br>
<b>250C.</b><br>
<b>Hot dry season (March-May) which</b><br>
is characterized by high temperature<br>
reaching 280C or higher, and dry<br>
condition. Hot, wet season (June –<br>
<b>October). This season is characterized</b><br>
by high rain because of onshore wind.<br>
<b>It is dominated by cloudy skies, high</b><br>
humidity, and temperature between<br>
25ºC and 31º C.<br>

09/03/2022   17:09<br>

<b>Main features of Monsoon forest</b><br>
<b>Most of the trees are deciduous and</b><br>
leaves fall off during the hot, dry season.<br>
<b>There is rapid growth of trees in the</b><br>
hot and wet season, and the trees soon<br>
become covered with leaves. Trees are<br>
tall, often as high as 30 m, but they are<br>
not as close as in tropical evergreen<br>
forests. On the other hand, undergrowth<br>
is denser, bamboo thickets are common.<br>
<b>The number of species is smaller than</b><br>
in the rain forest. Stands of one type of<br>
tree are very common, for example, teak<br>
forest of Thailand and Burma, the Sal<br>
of India and the eucalyptus of northern<br>
<b>Australia. In West Africa, this type</b><br>
of vegetation is known as casamance<br>
woodland, the name derived from the<br>
<b>Casamance district of Senegal where it</b><br>
is well developed (Figure 6.38).<br>
<b>Figure 6.38: Tropical monsoon forest</b><br>
<b>Tropical continental climate</b><br>
<b>This type of climate is located between 5º</b><br>
and 15º North and South of the Equator.<br>
<b>Areas covered by this climate include</b><br>
<b>West, East and Central Africa, South</b><br>
America (especially Guiana Highlands,<br>
Orinoco Basin and Brazilian Highlands),<br>
<b>India (around Deccan plateau), and the</b><br>
areas neighbouring the North and East<br>
of the Australian Desert.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>The major feature of this type of climate is</b><br>
the existence of high temperature during<br>
summer (32ºC) and winter (21ºC). The<br>
annual range of temperatures is about<br>
11ºC. The wet season may be as short<br>
as three months in some areas and as<br>
long as seven or eight months in others.<br>
<b>The area closer to the Equator tends</b><br>
to have longer wet seasons and may<br>
experience over 1000 mm of rainfall per<br>
year. The climate experiences the trade<br>
wind. Sometimes experience strong, hot<br>
and dusty winds, such as the Harmattan<br>
of West Africa.<br>
<b>Vegetation</b><br>
<b>In areas which experience a tropical</b><br>
continental climate, the seasonal rainfall<br>
tends to give rise to a distinct type of<br>
vegetation known as tropical grassland<br>
or savannah. The term savannah refers<br>
to open grassland, usually with some<br>
scattered trees or bushes. It contains tall<br>
grasses often reaching 2 m in height and<br>
scattered trees mainly located near to<br>
water courses. Trees are more common<br>
than grasses in the areas bordering the<br>
equatorial forest. The dry season in this<br>
type of climate lasts for three months,<br>
and the vegetation is known as Savannah<br>
woodland. In the areas bordering the<br>
tropical desert, the vegetation consists<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   395</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
mainly of thorn bushes, low shrubs and<br>
thorn trees with dry season lasting for<br>
nine months. High temperatures and<br>
heavy rainfall in the summer result in<br>
rapid and luxuriant plant growth (Figure<br>
6.39). Most of the grasses wither and<br>
turn brown in the dry season, and fires<br>
are common. Roots remain dormant and<br>
when the rains come, they produce new<br>
shoots and leaves. Tropical grasslands<br>
have different names depending on their<br>
location, such as Campos (Brazil), Llanos<br>
(Guiana), Highlands and Savannah<br>
(Africa and Australia).<br>
<b>Trees in this type of climate survive</b><br>
the dry season in various ways, which<br>
include, storing water in their trunks, like<br>
baobab trees, some store water through<br>
loss of leaves, most of the trees have long<br>
roots and develop an umbrella shaped<br>
crown which provides shade around the<br>
trunk and roots which help to conserve<br>
soil moisture.<br>
<b>Figure 6.39: Tropical continental</b><br>
vegetation<br>
<b>Tropical Desert</b><br>
<b>This type of climate is located in the</b><br>
area between the latitudinal belt of<br>
15º and 30º North and South of the<br>
<b>Equator. This region is the driest and</b><br>
hottest place on Earth. Rainfall is very<br>
minimal and in some years there is no<br>
rainfall at all. Deserts are typically found<br>
in continental interiors of the sub tropics.<br>
<b>Tropical dessert climate occurs on the</b><br>
<b>Western side of land masses in the belt</b><br>
of permanent trade winds. The principal<br>
tropical deserts include: The Sahara,<br>
Kalahari and Namib deserts in Africa;<br>
the Jordan, Syria, Iran, Iraq, Saudi Arabia<br>
and Israel deserts in South West Asia;<br>
the Thar Desert in Indian subcontinent;<br>
the Mohave, Colourado and Mexican<br>
deserts in North and Central America;<br>
the Atacama Desert in South America;<br>
the Great Australia desert in Australia;<br>
and the Somalia desert in the Horn of<br>
Africa.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>Trade winds blow towards the sea across</b><br>
tropical deserts for most of the year (off-<br>
shore winds). Likewise, the high and<br>
low-sun monsoon winds blow parallel<br>
to the coast, so moisture laden maritime<br>
can penetrate over land infrequently. In<br>
most low-latitude deserts, clouds cover<br>
is uncommon, resulting in low rainfall,<br>
rarely exceeding 120 mm (often no rain<br>
at all). Sudden downpour of rainfall may<br>
give rise to flash floods. Temperature<br>
is high with a monthly mean range of<br>
21º-32ºC. Daily temperature variations<br>
are extreme, ranging from 35º-40ºC<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   396</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
397<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
and above. The region experiences high<br>
humidity during night.<br>
<b>Vegetation</b><br>
<b>The region is characterized by very poor</b><br>
vegetation cover. Cactus, thorny plants<br>
and grasses (Figure 6.40) are the major<br>
natural vegetation found in desert region.<br>
<b>The scarcity or absence of vegetation</b><br>
is due to high temperature, excessive<br>
evaporation, and insufficient rainfall. All<br>
plants are adapted to high temperatures,<br>
high evaporation, and long periods of<br>
dryness. Most of the desert vegetation<br>
have long taproots which go far deeper<br>
to the water table to collect water from<br>
the deep surface. They have capacity<br>
to store water in their stem and leaves.<br>
<b>Other plants grow waxy or needle shaped</b><br>
leaves which reduce transpiration (loss<br>
of water from plant through stomata)<br>
rate. Most of the plants have strong<br>
water storage organs, hence help them to<br>
survive. Most of the plants are deciduous<br>
plants, thus they have a tendency of<br>
shading off their leaves during the dry<br>
season in order to cut off the rate of<br>
transpiration, while flourishing back<br>
during the wet season. Most of the plants<br>
have narrow shaped leaves that reduce<br>
the rate of transpiration. Some of the<br>
plants have spines which usually store<br>
water and thorn shaped structure which<br>
cut off the rate of transpiration. They<br>
have sunken stomata that reduce the<br>
loss of water through leaves. Sunken<br>
stomata are the stomata that are found<br>
below the leaves which reduce the rate<br>
of transpiration. The thorns found in<br>
most of the vegetation in arid and semi-<br>
arid deserts help them to fight parasite<br>
vegetation.<br>

<b>Warm temperate climate</b><br>
<b>This type of climate is also known as</b><br>
<b>Mediterranean climate or West margin</b><br>
climate. It occurs between latitudes<br>
30ºN – 45oN and 30ºS– 40oS of the<br>
equator on the western sides of the<br>
continents. They are mainly found on<br>
the lands bordering the Mediterranean,<br>
south West Africa (at Cape Province),<br>
central Chile, central California, and<br>
South west and southern Australia.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>The temperature in these regions is</b><br>
generally moderate, and the changes<br>
between summer and winter are also<br>
moderate. The temperature ranges<br>
from 10ºC in winter to 21º C during<br>
summer. They have high rainfall in<br>
winter due to onshore winds and low<br>
rainfall in summer due to offshore<br>
winds. Winter rain ranges between<br>
500-900 mm. They are characterised<br>
by local winds, such as Sirocco which<br>
blows across the Sahara Desert towards<br>
the Mediterranean.<br>
<b>Vegetation</b><br>
<b>This climate has warm temperate</b><br>
forests. It occurs where summers<br>
are warm, winters are mild without<br>
extended periods of snow cover or<br>
severe frost, and where there is no<br>
marked dry season. In these regions,<br>
much of forest and woodland have been<br>
cleared for agriculture, settlement and<br>
industries; only scattered woodland<br>
of trees and shrubs, remain. Common<br>
trees in these regions include evergreen<br>
oak, cork, sweet chestnut, beech, cedar<br>
and pines, cypress, and eucalyptus.<br>
<b>Xerophytes manage to adapt to the</b><br>
very hot, dry summer conditions by<br>
storing water from the winter rain in<br>
their leaves or barks. Such leaves have<br>
a waxy surface to reduce transpiration<br>
and a thick bark. Some plants have very<br>
small, spiny leaves again to reduce<br>
transpiration and long roots which can<br>
tap underground moisture.<br>
<b>Tundra climate</b><br>
<b>It occurs on the coastlands bordering</b><br>
the Arctic Sea in North America, and<br>
the coast lands of Eurasia.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>Temperature ranges from -29º C to -40º</b><br>
C in winter, which is usually very long.<br>
<b>The sun never rises above the horizon</b><br>
for several weeks. Summers are short<br>
and cool, with about 10º C, but during<br>
summer the sun remains above the<br>
horizon for several weeks. The annual<br>
precipitation is about 250 mm, some<br>
of which fall as rain in summer, and as<br>
snow in winter.<br>
<b>Vegetation</b><br>
<b>The natural vegetation consists of</b><br>
mosses, lichens and dwarf trees and<br>
shrubs. This vegetation is called ‘tundra<br>
vegetation’. The characteristics of this<br>
vegetation include small flowering<br>
shrubs such as bilberry and crowberry,<br>
flower and fruit in the short summer<br>
and their stems are twisted and woody.<br>
<b>Mosses, sedges and lichens occupy</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   398</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
399<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
much of the low-lying semi-swamp<br>
areas and have a height of only few<br>
centimetres. Dwarf and twisted trees<br>
such as willow and birch grow in the<br>
more sheltered area, especially on<br>
south-facing slopes. A large variety of<br>
small flowering plants, such as poppy<br>
and anemone are able to compete their<br>
life cycle in the short summer.<br>
<b>Polar climates</b><br>
It is found in the interiors of Iceland,<br>
<b>Greenland and in Antarctica. This type</b><br>
of climate is permanently characterized<br>
by temperatures below 0ºC. Snow<br>
storms are very frequent. The winters<br>
consist of continuous night and the<br>
summers of continuous day. Surface<br>
vegetation is almost non-existent.<br>
<b>Mountain climate</b><br>
<b>This type of climate is also known</b><br>
as Alpine climate, occurs in high<br>
mountain areas. Examples of regions<br>
where it is found include Ethiopian<br>
highlands, East African mountains,<br>
the mountains and plateaus of central<br>
<b>Asia, the Alps of Europe, the Andes of</b><br>
<b>South America, the Rockies of North</b><br>
America, and the plateau of Tibet.<br>
<b>Climate characteristics</b><br>
<b>Mountain climate is featured by</b><br>
decrease in pressure and temperature<br>
with<br>
increase<br>
in<br>
altitude<br>
while<br>
precipitation increases. If mountains<br>
are high enough there is a height at<br>
which maximum precipitation occurs<br>
and above which it decreases. There<br>
is usually a succession of temperature<br>
belts particularly in high mountains,<br>
such as the Ander which lies within<br>
the tropics. Local winds often develop<br>
in most high mountains. These tend<br>
to blow up the valley during the day<br>
(anabatic) but down the valleys and<br>
mountain slopes during the night<br>
(katabatic). Other local winds such as<br>
<b>Chinook in the Rockies and the Föhn</b><br>
in the Alps are caused by air crossing<br>
the mountains and descending down<br>
the leeward slopes.<br>
<b>Exercise 6.11</b><br>
1.	 Explain the characteristics of<br>
tropical rainforest.<br>
2.	 Explain how trees adapt to<br>
the dry season in tropical<br>
continental climate.<br>
3.	 Mountains maintain their own<br>
climate. Discuss.<br>
<b>Climate change</b><br>
<b>Climate change is a statistical change in</b><br>
the state of the climate that persists for<br>
an extended period typically decades<br>
or longer, whether due to natural<br>
variability or as a result of human<br>
activity. Climate change explains the<br>
invariability of elements of climate<br>
over time and space.<br>

<b>Evidences of the past climate change</b><br>
<b>Climates have changed and keep on</b><br>
constantly changing at all scales, and<br>
over varying time spans (both long<br>
and short terms). In understanding the<br>
current change in climate, there is a<br>
need to understand the past climates.<br>
<b>Meteorologists and earth scientists are</b><br>
continually providing a list of evidences<br>
of the past climates through;<br>
<b>Rocks</b><br>
<b>Rocks that are found today were</b><br>
formed under climatic conditions and<br>
environments that are no longer existing.<br>
<b>A good example is the climate condition</b><br>
which led to the formation of the coal in<br>
<b>Britain. Scientific investigation proves</b><br>
the existence of some rocks which were<br>
formed in climatic conditions which no<br>
longer exist. This is a strong implication<br>
that climate has changed and is still<br>
changing.<br>
<b>Fossils landscapes</b><br>
<b>Fossils landscapes that exist today have</b><br>
been produced by geomorphologic<br>
processes which is no longer operating.<br>
<b>For instance, glacially eroded highlands</b><br>
in the North and West Britain, granite<br>
tors on Dartmoor, and wadis formed<br>
during wetter periods (fluvial) in<br>
deserts. From a climate morphological<br>
point of view, land forms on the earth’s<br>
surface are shaped and reshaped<br>
by prevailing weather and climatic<br>
condition operating in a particular<br>
area. These land forms are different<br>
depending among other things on the<br>
agent involve. Therefore, land formed<br>
due to wind, is different from landforms<br>
formed due to glaciation. Therefore,<br>
presence of glacial landform in a region<br>
with no glaciers is an indication that<br>
formerly the area was under glaciation.<br>
<b>Changes in sea level</b><br>
<b>There are also evidences of changes in</b><br>
the sea level isostatically and eustatically<br>
in various areas of the world including<br>
<b>Maldives Island. Rising of the sea level</b><br>
is an indicator of melting of ice resulted<br>
from high temperature. Also, there are<br>
a number of islands whose coastal lines<br>
and shores have been submerged.<br>
<b>Vegetation belts</b><br>
<b>Vegetation belts have shifted through</b><br>
some 10o of latitude. A good example<br>
can be drawn from the Sahara Desert in<br>
<b>Africa. Desert margins are progressively</b><br>
extending engulfing more land and<br>
changing it into bare land. This indicates<br>
progressively increase in global<br>
temperature.<br>
<b>Pollen analysis</b><br>
<b>Pollen analysis shows which plants</b><br>
were dominant at the specific area<br>
and time. Each plant species has a<br>
distinctively-shaped<br>
pollen<br>
grain.<br>
<b>Although pollen can be transported</b><br>
by the action of winds or wildlife, it<br>
is assumed that grains trapped in peat<br>
form a representative sample of the<br>
vegetation that grew in the surrounding<br>
area at a given time. They show that<br>
this vegetation was a response to the<br>

climate conditions which prevailed<br>
at that time. Normally pollens of one<br>
plant species are completely unique<br>
from those of another plant species. In<br>
oxygen free environments deposited<br>
pollens may survive for a long period<br>
of time. Therefore, through scientific<br>
analysis, it is possible to identify the<br>
vegetation and climate type which<br>
prevailed at that time.<br>
<b>Dendrochronology</b><br>
<b>Dendrochronology also known as tree</b><br>
ring dating is a technique of obtaining<br>
a core from a tree trunk and using it<br>
to determine the age of the tree. Tree<br>
growth is rapid in spring, slower in<br>
autumn, and stops in winter in temperate<br>
latitudes. Each year’s growth is shown<br>
by as single ring. However, when the<br>
year is warm and wet, the ring will be<br>
larger because the tree grows more<br>
quickly than when the year is cold and<br>
dry. Tree-rings therefore are analysed<br>
to reflect the changes in climates.<br>
<b>Chemical methods</b><br>
<b>Chemical methods include studying</b><br>
of oxygen and carbon isotopes. An<br>
isotope is one of two or more forms<br>
of an element which differ from each<br>
other in atomic weight. This means that<br>
they have the same number of protons<br>
in a nucleus with different number<br>
of neutrons. For instance, the most<br>
accurate form of dating is based on<br>
C-14, a radioactive isotope of Carbon.<br>
<b>Carbon is taken in by plants during</b><br>
the carbon cycle. Carbon-14 decays<br>
radioactively at a known rate and can<br>
be compared with C-12, which does<br>
not decay and can be applied in dating<br>
process. Using C-12 and C-14 from<br>
a dead plant, scientists can determine<br>
the date of death to a standard error<br>
of ±5%, the techniques can accurately<br>
date organic matter up to 50,000 years.<br>
<b>Historical records</b><br>
<b>Historical records of climatic change</b><br>
include cave paintings of elephants in<br>
central Sahara and giraffes in Jordan.<br>
Since, elephants are not desert animals,<br>
it indicates that in past, the region<br>
(Sahara) was an another climatic region<br>
which favoured the life of animals like<br>
elephants. Another good example is that,<br>
graves for human burial in Greenland<br>
which were dug to a depth of 2 m in<br>
the 13th century, could be dug by only<br>
1m in the 14th century, and could not be<br>
dug at all in the 15th century due to the<br>
extension of permafrost – in contrast to<br>
its retreat in the 2000’s.<br>
<b>Recent changes in climate</b><br>
<b>Over the last century, glaciers and ice</b><br>
caps have been receding, snow cover has<br>
reduced and sea levels have been rising.<br>
The Earth has warmed by about 0.7°C,<br>
particularly over the last 15 years. The<br>
concentrations of greenhouse gases,<br>
such as carbon dioxide and methane<br>
in the atmosphere are greater than at<br>
any time in roughly the last 500,000<br>
years and their concentrations have<br>
risen sharply in recent decades. The<br>
current concentration of atmospheric<br>

greenhouse gases is equivalent to<br>
carbon dioxide levels of around 430<br>
parts per million compared with<br>
only 280 parts per million before the<br>
<b>Industrial Revolution. The rate of</b><br>
increase in temperature is faster now<br>
than previously, even at the end of<br>
glacial periods. The Intergovernmental<br>
<b>Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) fourth</b><br>
assessment report of 2007, emphasizes<br>
that “warming of the climate system is<br>
unequivocal, as is now evident from<br>
observations of increases in global<br>
average air and ocean temperatures,<br>
widespread melting of snow and ice,<br>
and rising global average sea level”.<br>
<b>The temperature increase is widespread</b><br>
over the globe but more concentrated<br>
at higher northern latitudes. For the<br>
<b>Arctic, the temperatures over the last</b><br>
100 years have increased twice the<br>
rate of the global average. The oceans<br>
have warmed down to depths of at least<br>
3 000 metres. Numerous other long-<br>
term changes in climate have been<br>
observed. For example, there have<br>
been an increase in rainfall in northern<br>
Europe, northern and central Asia,<br>
and eastern, northern and southern<br>
America between 1900 and 2005.<br>
Rainfall has declined in the Sahel,<br>
the Mediterranean, southern Africa,<br>
and parts of southern Asia. The IPCC<br>
fourth assessment report noted that it is<br>
very likely that cold days, cold nights<br>
and frosts have become less frequent<br>
over most land areas, while hot days<br>
and hot nights have become more<br>
frequent. It is also likely that both heat-<br>
waves and heavy precipitation events<br>
have become more frequent over most<br>
land areas.<br>
<b>Theories of climate change</b><br>
<b>Several arguments have been put</b><br>
forward to explain climate change over<br>
different time scales and epochs. Most<br>
of the climatologists now accept that<br>
each of the causes of climate change<br>
described below has a role to play in<br>
explaining change in the past, whether<br>
over long or short periods of time.<br>
<b>Climate change is attributed to natural</b><br>
and anthropogenic factors as follows;<br>
<b>Variations in solar energy</b><br>
<b>Although it was initially believed</b><br>
that solar energy output does not vary<br>
overtime, increasing evidence suggests<br>
that sunspot activity, which occurs in<br>
cycles, may significantly affect our<br>
climate. High annual temperatures<br>
on Earth appear to correspond to<br>
periods of maximum sunspot activity.<br>
<b>Whereas, low annual temperature on</b><br>
the earth’s surroundings corresponds to<br>
minimal support output phase, which is<br>
responsible for global climate change.<br>
<b>Most of the proponents of the theory</b><br>
believe that solar variability drives<br>
changes in earth’s climate and positive<br>
feedback occurs either by a process<br>
involving the influence of solar wind<br>
on cosmic rays which affects cloud<br>
formation, or on the ocean’s thermohaline<br>
circulation (T.H.C), which affects sea<br>
surface temperatures and wind patterns.<br>

<b>Astronomical relationships between</b><br>
the sun and the Earth<br>
<b>There is increasing evidence supporting</b><br>
<b>Milankovitch’s cycles of change in the</b><br>
earth’s orbit, tilt and wobble, which<br>
would account for changes in the<br>
amount of solar radiation reaching the<br>
earth’s surface. According to the theory,<br>
the earth’s orbit changes from being<br>
less to more elliptical within a cycle<br>
of 100 000 making the Earth to appear<br>
near or far from the Sun, affecting the<br>
annual thermal output, hence climatic<br>
change.<br>
<b>Changes in oceanic circulation</b><br>
<b>These affect the exchange of heat</b><br>
between the oceans and the atmosphere.<br>
<b>This can have both, long term effects</b><br>
on world climate where currents at the<br>
onset of the Quaternary ice age flow in<br>
directions opposite to those at the end<br>
of the ice age, and short term effects (El<br>
<b>Niño). The latest theory compares the</b><br>
<b>North Atlantic Drift that brings water</b><br>
to North West Europe. If, for example,<br>
the conveyor belt was closed possibly<br>
by a huge influx of fresh water into the<br>
sea, then the climate would become<br>
dramatically colder.<br>
<b>Meteorites</b><br>
<b>Meteorites is one of the considered</b><br>
theories in explaining the climate<br>
change. It explains a collision of one<br>
or more meteorites falling on the earth<br>
and attributed to the reduction of<br>
incoming solar radiation, a depletion<br>
of ozone layer and lowering of global<br>
temperature. Consequently, this event<br>
was associated with the extinction of a<br>
dinosaurs among other effects about 65<br>
million years ago.<br>
<b>Volcanic activity</b><br>
<b>It has been accepted for some time</b><br>
that volcanic activity has influenced<br>
climate in the past, and continues to<br>
do so. World temperatures are lowered<br>
after every single eruption, such as the<br>
ones in Mount Krakatoa and Pinatubo.<br>
<b>This is due to the increase in volcanic</b><br>
dust particles in the lower atmosphere<br>
which absorb and scatter more of<br>
incoming radiation, and offset the<br>
greenhouse effect. Precipitation also<br>
increases due to the greater number of<br>
hygroscopic nuclei (dust particles) in<br>
the atmosphere. Volcanic dust particles<br>
also act as inhabitant to the direct<br>
incoming solar radiation, therefore<br>
lowering the global temperature, hence<br>
climate change.<br>
<b>Plate tectonics</b><br>
<b>The</b><br>
movements<br>
have<br>
led<br>
to<br>
redistributions of land masses causing<br>
long-term effects on climate. These<br>
effects may result from a land mass<br>
drifting into different latitudes or<br>
uplift forming high fold mountains.<br>
<b>Mountains can lead to colder climate</b><br>
as they act as barriers to atmospheric<br>
circulation. For example, the Asian<br>
<b>Monsoon were established by the</b><br>
creation of the Tibetan Plateau.<br>

<b>Composition of atmosphere</b><br>
<b>The concertation of the atmospheric</b><br>
gases can be altered in various ways.<br>
<b>For example, the concertation of the</b><br>
atmospheric gases can be increased<br>
by volcanic eruptions. At present,<br>
increasing concern is being expressed<br>
at build-up of CO2 gases in the<br>
atmosphere and the resultant green-<br>
house effect, along with the use of<br>
aerosols.<br>
<b>The influence of human activities on</b><br>
climate change<br>
<b>The Earth is warmed during the day by</b><br>
incoming solar radiation and cooled at<br>
night by outgoing radiation (infrared<br>
radiation). Normally, the incoming<br>
solar radiation is able to pass through<br>
the atmosphere, some of the outgoing<br>
radiation is trapped by trace gases.<br>
Without these natural greenhouse gases,<br>
the earth’s average temperature would<br>
have been 330C colder than it is today,<br>
from which life of any form would have<br>
been impossible. Water vapour provide<br>
the majority of the natural greenhouse<br>
gases effect with lesser contributions<br>
from carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous<br>
oxide and ozone. During the last 150<br>
years there has been, with exception<br>
of water vapour which remains a<br>
constant in the system, a rise in<br>
greenhouse gas concentrations. This<br>
has been due largely to the increase in<br>
world population and a corresponding<br>
growth of human activities especially<br>
agriculture and industries.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
increased<br>
concentration<br>
of<br>
these gases into the atmosphere, is<br>
increasing its ability to trap heat.<br>
<b>Due to this, it is widely accepted that</b><br>
the  greenhouse effect is associated<br>
with global warming (Figure 6.41).<br>
<b>World temperature, which have risen</b><br>
by 0.5oC since the middle of the last<br>
century, increased rapidly in the 1980s<br>
and it is at this time when warmest<br>
years were recorded, and prediction<br>
suggest that there could be a further<br>
rise between 1.50C and 4.50C by the<br>
year 2100. Though warming has not<br>
been uniform across the planet, the<br>
upward trend in the globally averaged<br>
temperature shows that more areas<br>
are warming than cooling. According<br>
to National Oceanic and Atmospheric<br>
<b>Administration (NOAA) annual climate</b><br>
report of 2020, the combined land and<br>
ocean temperature has increased at an<br>
average rate of 0.13 degrees Fahrenheit<br>
(0.08 degrees Celsius) per decade since<br>
1880, however, the average rate of<br>
increase since 1981 (0.18°C / 0.32°F)<br>
has been more than twice that rate.<br>
<b>Some sunlight</b><br>
that hits the<br>
earth is reflected<br>
<b>Sun</b><br>
<b>CO2 and other</b><br>
gases in the<br>
atmosphere trap<br>
heat, keeping<br>
the earth warm<br>

<b>Sources of greenhouse gas</b><br>
<b>It is absolutely certain that humans</b><br>
have contributed large quantities of<br>
carbon dioxide by burning fossil fuels<br>
and vegetation. Similarly, the increase<br>
in methane concentrations which are<br>
currently rising at fast rates as compared<br>
to carbon dioxide can be linked to such<br>
factors as energy production, food<br>
production (from rice fields or ruminant<br>
livestock), landfills, waste treatment and<br>
vegetation burning. Recent estimates<br>
suggest that one-third of nitrous oxide<br>
emissions is caused by human actions;<br>
the increased generation rate of nitrous<br>
oxide is associated with the growing<br>
application of nitrogen fertilizers in<br>
agricultural production. Humans have<br>
also been emitting fumes and smoke<br>
from industrial processes and transport.<br>
<b>These aerosols partly shield the planet</b><br>
from the sun’s energy, scattering and<br>
reflecting it. However, not all aerosols<br>
behave in the same way. Aerosols from<br>
vegetation burning, ‘black carbon’, and<br>
the ‘brown clouds’ that come from urban<br>
sources may have the opposite effect.<br>
<b>The former seems to cause cooling while</b><br>
the later result into warming (Table 6.5).<br>

<b>Future trend of climate change</b><br>
<b>Recent</b><br>
climate<br>
change<br>
models<br>
predicted a 2° to 4°C increase in global<br>
temperature over the next century<br>
and a 50 per cent chance that average<br>
global temperatures could rise by 5°C.<br>
<b>Substantial impacts from such increase</b><br>
are anticipated. Such changes would<br>
transform the physical geography of<br>
the world with serious implications to<br>
where people live and how they live.<br>
<b>The following are the notable potential</b><br>
impacts of climate change:<br>
(i)<br>
There<br>
may be more severe<br>
weather events like storms,<br>
floods, hurricanes, heat-waves,<br>
and droughts as a warmer Earth<br>
means there is more energy<br>
available for atmospheric and<br>
oceanic transfers. For example,<br>
there could be a 10 per cent<br>
increase in average hurricane<br>
wind speed.<br>
(ii)<br>
Warming may induce sudden<br>
shifts in regional weather patterns,<br>
such as the monsoons or the El<br>
<b>Niño Southern Oscillation. This</b><br>
could have major consequences<br>
for water availability, crops,<br>
flooding and wildfire in tropical<br>
regions. Some climate models<br>
predict a more frequent and<br>
intense El Niño, perhaps even<br>
causing<br>
replacement<br>
of<br>
the<br>
rainforest of Brazil by savannah.<br>
(iii)	 Melting glaciers will at first<br>
increase flood risk and then,<br>
once depleted, reduce water<br>
supplies. Large parts of the<br>
<b>Indian subcontinent, China and</b><br>
the Andes rely on summer glacial<br>
melted water to provide water for<br>
crops and for drinking. Without<br>
this regular supply, new ways<br>
will have to be found to capture<br>
water for around one billion<br>
people.<br>
(iv)	 About 40 per cent of the world’s<br>
population<br>
live<br>
within<br>
100<br>
kilometres of the coast and two-<br>
thirds of the world’s largest cities<br>
are found there. Rising sea levels<br>
from ice melt and the expansion<br>
of the water upon warming could<br>
result in hundreds of millions<br>
of more people enduring floods<br>
each year. More than a fifth of<br>
<b>Bangladesh could be submerged</b><br>
by 2100.<br>
(v)<br>
Some cold areas such as Canada,<br>
<b>Russia and Scandinavia may</b><br>
enjoy an increase in agricultural<br>
productivity as a result of<br>
warming, at least in the short<br>
term, while many of the world’s<br>
main food- producing regions<br>
may become too hot and dry<br>
for crops to grow for example,<br>
central and southern Europe<br>
and the Great Plains of North<br>
America. Declining crop yields,<br>
especially in Africa, could leave<br>
hundreds of millions without the<br>
ability to produce or purchase<br>
sufficient food.<br>

(vi)	 The area that is burnt by wildfires<br>
may increase warming condition<br>
substantially.<br>
<b>It</b><br>
has<br>
been<br>
estimated that the area burnt<br>
by wildfire in western United<br>
<b>States may increase by 200 to</b><br>
400 per cent for every degree of<br>
warming.<br>
(vii)	 Deaths	 from malnutrition and<br>
heat stress will increase and<br>
diseases such as malaria could<br>
become<br>
more<br>
widespread.<br>
<b>Diseases are likely to spread</b><br>
from the tropics to the mid-<br>
latitudes as the climate warms<br>
and outbreaks of pests may<br>
become more extreme because<br>
natural<br>
biological<br>
controls<br>
may be lacking in these newly<br>
colonised pest areas.<br>
(viii)	Plant and animal extinction rates<br>
will increase as many species’<br>
migration will mismatch with the<br>
temperature changes. Up to 37<br>
per cent of species may be on the<br>
road to extinction by 2050.<br>
(ix)	 Ocean acidification is occurring<br>
and will increase as the oceans<br>
absorb carbon dioxide from the<br>
atmosphere. This buffers global<br>
warming but it can have major<br>
effects on marine ecosystems.<br>
<b>For example, it reduces the</b><br>
ability<br>
of<br>
species<br>
such<br>
as<br>
molluscs, crustaceans, coccoliths<br>
and corals from forming the<br>
calcium carbonate needed for<br>
their structures and this would<br>
compromise the food chain.<br>
(x)<br>
Global	 economic output could<br>
be reduced by 3 to 10 per cent,<br>
with the poorest countries being<br>
mostly affected.<br>
<b>Short-term climate change</b><br>
<b>El-Niño and La-Niña Atmospheric -</b><br>
<b>Oceanic Circulations (ENSO)</b><br>
<b>El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO)</b><br>
is an inter-annual fluctuation of the<br>
atmosphere-ocean<br>
system<br>
in<br>
the<br>
equatorial Pacific ocean with three<br>
circulation phases of Warm (El Niño),<br>
<b>Cold (La Niña) and Neutral.  Although</b><br>
<b>El Niño is considered the warm phase</b><br>
of ENSO and La Niña as a cold phase<br>
of ENSO, they are not considered<br>
opposite because they occur at different<br>
magnitudes,<br>
spatial<br>
extent,<br>
and<br>
duration. During an El Niño, usually,<br>
warm water appears in the Eastern<br>
<b>Pacific Ocean of the coasts of Peru and</b><br>
<b>Ecuador. Because the warm current</b><br>
usually appears around the Christmas<br>
time (December), the fishermen called<br>
it El Niño, a Spanish word (name) for<br>
“the Christ child”.  During La Niña, a<br>
<b>Spanish world for “Little girl” usually</b><br>
cold water is present in these locations<br>
(Peru and Ecuador) causing contrasting<br>
shift in local weather patterns as well as<br>
the global climates. The name El Niño<br>
nowadays, contrary to its origin, is<br>
widely used specifically to refer to the<br>
anomalous warming of the sea surface<br>
that occurs every few years, typically<br>
concentrated in the East Central<br>
equatorial Pacific. Moreover, La Niña<br>
is contrary to its original meaning, as<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   407</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
408<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
it is used to refer the adopted episode<br>
of cooler-than-normal sea surface<br>
temperature in the tropical Pacific that<br>
in many ways are opposite to El Niño.<br>
<b>Anomalous weather patterns in La</b><br>
<b>Niña seasons are generally opposite</b><br>
from those in El Niño. These changes<br>
in the surface water temperature are<br>
linked to the changes in the strength<br>
of the Trade Winds (Tw) blowing from<br>
<b>East to West across the region.  Neutral</b><br>
conditions occur when neither El Niño<br>
nor La Niña is present.<br>
<b>El Niños and La Niñas occur semi</b><br>
regularly at intervals of 2-5 years and<br>
they usually last for 9 - 12 months.<br>
<b>The large pours of warm or cold water</b><br>
produced in these events change the<br>
atmospheric pressure patterns in the<br>
<b>Tropical Pacific region and the global</b><br>
wind patterns far from the tropics.<br>
<b>This leads to observable changes in</b><br>
temperature and rainfall patterns that<br>
vary by ENSO phase in many areas of the<br>
world. El Niño and La Niña events are<br>
associated with the wide spread changes<br>
in the climate system and can lead to<br>
significant socio-economic impacts<br>
affecting infrastructure, agriculture,<br>
health, and energy facilities. They are<br>
also associated with extreme weather<br>
events often with significant physical<br>
and financial impacts. However, they<br>
can vary greatly from year to year with<br>
the variation of the prevailing El Niño<br>
and La Niño magnitude.<br>
<b>Causes of El Niño and La Niña</b><br>
<b>El Niño and La Niña are the episodes</b><br>
that alternate in irregular inter-annual<br>
cycles called ENSO. The “Southern<br>
<b>Oscillation” is the historical term for</b><br>
the atmospheric changes in the tropical<br>
<b>Pacific that accompany both El Niño</b><br>
and La Niño episodes in the ocean. The<br>
name “ENSO” is a reminder that close<br>
interaction between the atmosphere<br>
and ocean is an essential part of the<br>
process. Normally, the global climate<br>
system contains many climate and<br>
weather processes, the ENSO is by far<br>
the dominant feature of Inter-annual<br>
time scale.<br>
<b>The cycle is the consequence of slow</b><br>
feedbacks in the ocean atmospheric<br>
system acting alongside the strong<br>
air-sea interaction processes in the<br>
tropics that allow the growth of small<br>
disturbances to the large scale ocean<br>
state.<br>
<b>Normally, the equatorial Pacific Ocean</b><br>
has a pool of relatively warm water in<br>
the upper part of the ocean in the West<br>
and a shallower layer or relatively cool<br>
water in the East maintained by and in<br>
balance with easterly surface winds.<br>
<b>The well-mixed upper ocean layer lies</b><br>
above a thin thermocline layer with<br>
cold water below which causes the<br>
outburst of winds and, consequently,<br>
the disturbance on the balance between<br>
the upper warmer water with the lower<br>
cooler water.  Through the action of<br>
<b>Westerly wind-burst, a short-lived</b><br>
storm like the event in the West Pacific<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   408</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
409<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
affects the balanced upper surface<br>
ocean air-mass.<br>
<b>The increased sea surface temperature</b><br>
influences the atmospheric winds<br>
which in turn influence the upper part<br>
of the ocean and thermocline such that<br>
the sea surface temperature is increased<br>
more as a positive feedback. When<br>
these conditions are favourable and<br>
continuous, they generate El Niño as a<br>
feedback. A La Niña event can arise in<br>
a similar way. Normally, El Niño and<br>
La Niña events are self-controlling,<br>
evolving and stabilizing in such a way<br>
that the equatorial surface temperature<br>
gradually returns to its normal and<br>
the event ends after several months,<br>
normally 9 to 12 months. However,<br>
most of the time, the system does not<br>
return to normal at 100% but rather it<br>
overshoots to start the opposite phase<br>
of another cycle.<br>
<b>Impact of El Niño and La Niña on the</b><br>
global climate and livelihoods<br>
(a)  Impacts on global weather and<br>
climate<br>
<b>El Niño and La Niña events change</b><br>
the sea surface temperature, which in<br>
turn causes the changes in atmospheric<br>
circulation<br>
of<br>
wind,<br>
pressure,<br>
temperature, and rainfall patterns.<br>
Through these atmospheric dynamics,<br>
the atmospheric changes extend much<br>
beyond the tropic Pacific region.  At<br>
this extension, the Atlantic and Indian<br>
<b>Oceans are also affected. This further</b><br>
prolongs the impacts of ENSO.<br>
<b>Normally, the effects of ENSO vary</b><br>
considerably with location and extent<br>
of the event. For example, during<br>
<b>El Niño event, the eastward shift of</b><br>
rainfall in West Pacific region tends to<br>
cause rainfall deficits in the Philippines,<br>
<b>Indonesia and Australia while Central</b><br>
<b>Pacific Islands experience excess</b><br>
rainfall. However, as explained above,<br>
each individual El Niño or La Niña<br>
event has got its different severity and<br>
timing with its different impacts on a<br>
given area. Sometimes the impacts of<br>
<b>ENSO are modified by the state of the</b><br>
climate at that time.  Even the sign of the<br>
anomaly, such as excess or insufficient<br>
rainfall may vary from one event to<br>
another for a particular location. By<br>
average, ENSO causes variation in<br>
extreme global precipitation at about<br>
15%-20% of precipitation extremes<br>
while the remaining 80% is caused by<br>
other factors of climatic system.<br>
<b>The occurrence of both wetter and</b><br>
drier regions on the same latitudinal<br>
location reflects the tendency of ENSO<br>
events to displace rainfall patterns<br>
geographically and to enhance or<br>
decrease local precipitation.  The<br>
effects of this is more pronounced<br>
around the Pacific and tropical regions.<br>
<b>Along with changes in rainfall, El</b><br>
<b>Niño and La Niña cycles, substantially</b><br>
affect other weather conditions, such as<br>
temperature, with regional and seasonal<br>
variations. However, priority is given<br>
to rainfall and tropical storms as these<br>
have more impacts on properties and<br>
people’s livelihoods.<br>

(b)  Social and economic impacts<br>
The changes in regional temperature,<br>
rainfall and wind patterns in the tropics<br>
and extra-tropics caused by El Nino<br>
and La Nina events affect countries’<br>
economies. The direct impacts include<br>
property damage by floods or storms;<br>
failure in agriculture production due to<br>
either heavy rainfall, floods or drought<br>
during La Niña; forest damage due<br>
to fire outbreaks during La Niña dry<br>
periods; damage of infrastructure due<br>
to floods; and impact on market due to<br>
price fluctuation, especially high costs<br>
of products and goods during flooding<br>
events.<br>
<b>El Niño and La Niña events have</b><br>
very enormous impacts on people’s<br>
livelihoods and social well-being.  The<br>
most severe documented impacts of<br>
<b>ENSO were those associated with a very</b><br>
strong El Niño event of 1997/98 and<br>
2010/11 which followed by La Niña that<br>
extended over two years. For example,<br>
during El Niño event of 1997/98 in East<br>
<b>Africa, Kenya experienced the damage of</b><br>
about 11% of its GDP. Over 90% of the<br>
calculated flood losses were associated<br>
with transport, infrastructure damage and<br>
water supply and sanitation.  This was<br>
followed by two consecutive years of La<br>
<b>Niña which caused loss of about 16%</b><br>
of GDP in both 1998/99 and 1999/2000<br>
financial years. These two events were<br>
serious and affected the whole part of<br>
<b>Eastern Africa, causing more than 11,000</b><br>
deaths of people in Ethiopia, Somalia<br>
and Eritrea.<br>
<b>Impact of climate change</b><br>
<b>The impacts that scientists predicted</b><br>
in the past would result from global<br>
climate change are now occurring.<br>
The impacts include melting of ice,<br>
rise of sea level, negative impacts on<br>
ecosystems, hydrological cycle, water<br>
resources,<br>
agriculture,<br>
settlements<br>
and other economic activities. These<br>
impacts are further explained below.<br>
<b>Ice and snow melting</b><br>
<b>The rise of temperature has caused</b><br>
the melting of ice and snow in the<br>
<b>Antarctic, Greenland, Alaska, Arctic</b><br>
and Siberia to an alarming speed.<br>
<b>Melting ice and snow has at first</b><br>
increased flood risk and reduced water<br>
supplies. For example, large parts of<br>
the Indian subcontinent, China and the<br>
<b>Andes rely on summer glacial melted</b><br>
water to provide water for crops and<br>
for drinking. Without glaciers, they<br>
will have and have already started to<br>
exprience critical water shortage.<br>
<b>Increased severe weather events</b><br>
<b>Due to climate change, severe weather</b><br>
events have intensified. Such events<br>
include storms, hurricanes, heat waves,<br>
droughts and floods. The events<br>
have increased in both severity and<br>
frequency of occurrence. A warmer<br>
<b>Earth means there is more energy</b><br>
available for atmospheric and oceanic<br>
transfers.<br>
<b>For</b><br>
instance,<br>
scientists<br>
predict that there could be a 10 per<br>
cent increase in average hurricane<br>
<b>Physical Geography Form 5&6 (2022).indd   410</b><br>
09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
411<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
wind<br>
speed.<br>
Likewise,<br>
climatic<br>
change<br>
intensifies<br>
the<br>
circulation<br>
of water on, above and below the<br>
surface of the Earth. Consequently,<br>
floods and droughts have become<br>
more severe, causing huge losses to<br>
property and livelihoods in developing<br>
countries.<br>
<b>Disturbance on ecosystems</b><br>
<b>Rising temperatures have changed</b><br>
weather and vegetation patterns across<br>
the globe, has been causing unrest<br>
and in turn is forcing animal species<br>
to migrate to new, cooler areas in<br>
order to survive. The rapid nature of<br>
climate change is likely to exceed the<br>
ability of many species to migrate.<br>
<b>Due to this it is predicted that about 37</b><br>
percent of the earth’s species will be<br>
headed for extinction by 2050, a good<br>
example of which being the bears of<br>
the Artic. Conservation managers will<br>
have to cope with changing biomes<br>
as the changing climate would result<br>
in changing the current conservation<br>
status quo.<br>
<b>Food insecurity</b><br>
<b>Drought may lead to serious shortage</b><br>
of food especially in the Sub-Saharan<br>
region because a large population<br>
in this region depends on rain fed<br>
agriculture<br>
for<br>
its<br>
livelihoods.<br>
<b>Prolonged droughts and an increase in</b><br>
the number and severity of floods and<br>
pests have seriously compromise food<br>
security.<br>
<b>Disruption of settlement and</b><br>
economic activities<br>
<b>Some coastlines and river deltas of</b><br>
<b>Africa which are densely populated are</b><br>
currently being affected by the rise in<br>
sea levels. Other coastal settlements<br>
will be subjected to increased coastal<br>
erosion. For example, recent flooding<br>
in<br>
<b>East</b><br>
<b>Africa</b><br>
highlighted<br>
the<br>
vulnerability of flood plain settlements,<br>
such as the Jangwani valley settlements<br>
in Dar es Salaam, and the need to<br>
develop adaptive strategies for extreme<br>
weather events management, thus<br>
mitigation. In the semi-arid and arid<br>
zones, however, many settlements<br>
were associated with inland drainage<br>
water sources, on the contrary, the<br>
increase in drought, in such areas may<br>
enhance water supply vulnerabilities.<br>
<b>Moreover, there will be an increase</b><br>
in the prevalence of vector-borne<br>
diseases (such as malaria) as a result of<br>
increased temperatures and increased<br>
mobility of people from such areas to<br>
safer places. Similarly, water supply<br>
and irrigation reservoirs in seasonal<br>
river catchments might fail, leading to<br>
poor sanitation in urban areas, as well<br>
as food shortage.<br>
<b>Disturbing oceans and marine</b><br>
ecosystems<br>
<b>Climate change will increase ocean</b><br>
acidification. This is because, as the<br>
oceans absorb carbon dioxide from the<br>
atmosphere, it buffers global warming<br>
but it can have a major effect on marine<br>

09/03/2022   17:09<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
412<br>
<b>Student’s Book  Form Five and Six</b><br>
ecosystems. Increased acidification<br>
will reduce the ability of species such<br>
as molluscs, crustaceans, coccoliths<br>
and corals from forming the calcium<br>
carbonate needed for their structures<br>
and this would have knock on effects<br>
on the food chain.<br>
<b>Energy insecurity</b><br>
<b>Hydroelectric power generation, for</b><br>
example in Mtera Dam in Tanzania,<br>
could be restricted in drought periods,<br>
which could in turn lead to a multiplicity<br>
of other impacts. As a precaution, states<br>
are advised to develop alternative<br>
sources of renewable energy.<br>
<b>Adaptive measures</b><br>
<b>Adaptive</b><br>
measures<br>
will<br>
involve<br>
methods of coastal defence (where<br>
applicable), a critical review of the<br>
energy sector, both regionally and<br>
nationally, and a rigorous adherence to<br>
city hygiene procedures.  The measures<br>
will also involve the agricultural<br>
industry adapting to the changing<br>
climate in terms of cropping strategies<br>
and<br>
innovations<br>
in<br>
environment<br>
design to maximize human comfort<br>
at minimum energy expenditure. In<br>
the Savannah and arid areas, water<br>
resources management systems will<br>
be needed to optimize the use of water<br>
resources and inter-state cooperation<br>
where such resources are shared. Since<br>
the environment is a shared resource,<br>
likewise the effects of pollution resulting<br>
from emission of greenhouse gases is<br>
a transboundary problem. Thus, there<br>
is a need for all countries in the world<br>
to join together in implementing the<br>
environmental protection polices. Such<br>
polices includes those for reduction of<br>
greenhouse gases which are associated<br>
with the increased warming of the<br>
globe. This can done by localizing<br>
global polices and revising the existing<br>
polices on environmental protection.<br>

<b>Physical Geography for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Aquifer</b><br>
A permeable rock which stores and transfers water.<br>
<b>Atoll</b><br>
A ring-shaped coral reef.<br>
<b>Barysphere</b><br>
The inner most part of the earth.<br>
<b>Bedding plane</b><br>
<b>The boundary between adjacent layers or strata in a sedimentary</b><br>
rock.<br>
<b>Biosphere</b><br>
<b>The whole of the region of the earth’s surface, the sea, and the</b><br>
air that is inhabited by living organisms.<br>
<b>Cliff</b><br>
A tall, vertical, or near vertical, rock face.<br>
<b>Climate</b><br>
<b>Aggregate weather conditions of an area over a long period of</b><br>
time which allow for the designation of seasonal patterns and<br>
expected future weather.<br>
<b>Climate change</b><br>
<b>Long term variations in climate, particularly related to average</b><br>
annual temperatures and annual rainfall.<br>
<b>Clint</b><br>
<b>Flat-topped block that forms the ‘paving stone’ in a limestone</b><br>
pavement.<br>
<b>Deforestation</b><br>
<b>Removal of forest covers due to cutting or burning, or a</b><br>
combination of the two.<br>
<b>Denudation</b><br>
<b>Stripping of surface cover. Can apply to both vegetation and</b><br>
soils.<br>
<b>Escarpment</b><br>
<b>An elongated, steep slope at the edge of an upland area such</b><br>
as a plateau or cuesta.<br>
<b>Geology</b><br>
<b>The science that deals with the earth’s physical structure and</b><br>
substance, its history, and the processes that act on it.<br>
<b>Humus</b><br>
<b>Layer of the soil profile that is composed of decomposed</b><br>
organic matter normally from dead and decaying plants and<br>
animals and is usually dark coloured.<br>
<b>Lithosphere</b><br>
A rock layer forming the outermost part of the earth.<br>
<b>Loam</b><br>
A soil having roughly equal proportions of clay, sand and silt.<br>
Nitrogen fixation A process carried out by certain algae and soil bacteria whereby<br>
atmospheric nitrogen is incorporated to form nitrogen-based<br>
organic compounds.<br>

<b>Oasis</b><br>
A wet-point site in an arid area.<br>
<b>Organic matter</b><br>
<b>Decomposed animal or plant material that is added to the soil</b><br>
and becomes a part of soil profile. After fully decomposition<br>
becomes integral part of the soil characterised by dark and<br>
moist substance called humus which is rich in nutrients.<br>
<b>Pedosphere</b><br>
<b>Thin outer layer of earth’s crust made up of soil. It acts as an</b><br>
integrator between the atmosphere, biosphere, lithosphere and<br>
hydrosphere of the Earth.<br>
<b>Peds</b><br>
<b>Natural unit of the soil structure that have been formed by</b><br>
tendency of the soil to fracture along planes of weakness.<br>
Pervious rocks        Rocks that allow water to flow along cracks or joints.<br>
<b>Plucking</b><br>
<b>A form of glacial erosion in which ice freezes on to rock and</b><br>
pulls it away when the glacier moves on.<br>
<b>Regolith</b><br>
<b>The assemblage of unconsolidated rock materials that have</b><br>
been broken down, modified and altered by weathering; and<br>
soil materials on the earth’s surface, sometimes considered to<br>
be loose earth’s material above the solid bedrock.<br>
<b>Ridge</b><br>
A long, narrow crest of a hill or mountain.<br>
<b>Scarp</b><br>
The steep slope of an escarpment.<br>
<b>Scarp slope</b><br>
A slope in the land that cuts across the underlying strata,<br>
especially the steeper slope of a cuesta.<br>
<b>Soil horizon</b><br>
<b>Individual soil layer which has distinctive characteristics such</b><br>
as colour structure, texture or other properties that make it<br>
different from other layers in the soil profile.<br>
<b>Soil profile</b><br>
<b>Vertical section of the soil from the soil surface down through</b><br>
the horizons including the parent material. It consists of two<br>
parts, the solum and parent material.<br>
<b>Suspension</b><br>
The transport of load in the body of the water   in a river i.e.,<br>
being carried along in the flow.<br>
<b>Vent</b><br>
<b>A pipe-like gap in the ground which allows volcanic material</b><br>
to pass through to the surface.<br>
<b>Ventifacts</b><br>
<b>A stone or pebble which has been shaped   by wind-blown</b><br>
sand, usually in the desert, so that its surface consists of flat<br>
facets with sharp edges.<br>
